topics.title: Sensitivity Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT522 - BUSINESS ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Sensitivity Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Sensitivity Analysis** (also known as **Post-Optimality Analysis** or **What-If Analysis**) is a systematic mathematical procedure used in Linear Programming (LP) and broader Operations Research to determine how the optimal solution of a model changes in response to alterations in its input parameters — namely the **objective function coefficients ($c_j$)**, the **right-hand side (RHS) values ($b_i$)** of constraints, and the inclusion of new variables or constraints.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, Sensitivity Analysis is positioned as a critical post-solution tool within the broader **Linear Programming (LP)** framework. It allows decision-makers to assess the *robustness* of an optimal solution without having to re-solve the entire LP from scratch.

Formally, given the standard LP:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Maximize } & Z = \sum_{j=1}^{n} c_j x_j \\
\text{Subject to } & \sum_{j=1}^{n} a_{ij} x_j \leq b_i, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, m \\
& x_j \geq 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \ldots, n
\end{aligned}
$$

Sensitivity Analysis investigates the effect of perturbations $\Delta c_j$, $\Delta b_i$ on the optimal basis, optimal value $Z^*$, and the basic feasible solution $x_B$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Sensitivity Analysis is examined under Module 1, which focuses on the *modelling phase* of Business Analytics. Examiners frequently test the **range of optimality**, **range of feasibility**, and the **100% Rule** for simultaneous parameter changes.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine you have carefully balanced a **seesaw (teeter-totter)** so that the maximum weight sits perfectly in the middle. This balance point represents your **optimal solution**. Now, you ask:

- *"What if I move the weight slightly to the left?"* — Does it still balance? By how much can I move it before it tips over?
- *"What if I make one of the supports shorter?"* — How does the load distribution change?

Sensitivity Analysis is the **mathematical equivalent of "wiggling" each input of your LP model** to see how much it can change before your optimal decision is no longer optimal. It answers the eternal business question: **"How safe is my current optimal plan if the future turns out slightly different from what I assumed?"**

| Business Parameter | Sensitivity Question |
|---|---|
| Profit per unit ($c_j$) | How much can profit change before I switch products? |
| Resource availability ($b_i$) | How much more of a resource would be worth acquiring? |
| Adding a new product | Is the new product worth introducing at all? |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Sensitivity Analysis is *not* about finding a new optimum — it is about **protecting** the existing optimum from uncertainty in the input data.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Terminology

The following **standard metrics** are fundamental to all sensitivity analysis questions:

- **Shadow Price (Dual Price, $\pi_i$):** The marginal worth of one additional unit of resource $i$. Unit: **rupees per unit** of resource.
- **Reduced Cost ($\bar{c}_j - c_j$):** The amount by which the objective coefficient of a non-basic variable must improve before it enters the optimal basis.
- **Allowable Increase ($\Delta_{\max}^+$):** The maximum upward change permitted while keeping the current basis optimal.
- **Allowable Decrease ($\Delta_{\max}^-$):** The maximum downward change permitted while keeping the current basis optimal.
- **Range of Optimality:** The interval of $c_j$ values over which the current optimal solution remains optimal.
- **Range of Feasibility:** The interval of $b_i$ values over which the current basis (set of basic variables) remains feasible.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Sensitivity Analysis

Sensitivity Analysis in LP rests on three core investigative pillars:

**Pillar 1 — Changes in the Objective Function Coefficients ($c_j$)**
- Determines the **Range of Optimality** for one or more $c_j$.
- The optimal *mix* (basis) remains the same as long as all reduced costs remain non-negative (maximization) or non-positive (minimization).
- The objective value $Z^*$ changes linearly with $c_j$ within this range.

**Pillar 2 — Changes in the Right-Hand Side Values ($b_i$)**
- Determines the **Range of Feasibility** for one or more $b_i$.
- The feasibility of the current basis is governed by $B^{-1} b \geq 0$, where $B$ is the optimal basis matrix.
- The rate of change in $Z^*$ per unit change in $b_i$ is the **shadow price** $\pi_i$.

**Pillar 3 — Addition of New Variables or Constraints**
- A new variable $x_{n+1}$ is profitable to introduce only if its reduced cost is favourable.
- A new constraint is binding only if the current solution violates it; otherwise, the solution remains feasible and optimal.

### 2.2 Why and How — The Logic Behind Each Step

**Why does the Range of Optimality matter?**
In the real world, coefficients such as profit margins, selling prices, and cost rates are **estimates**. If the profit per unit of a product can drop by ₹2 without altering the optimal production plan, the manager gains confidence. This interval is the *insurance policy* on the optimal solution.

**How is the Range of Optimality computed?**
Recall the optimality condition for a maximization LP: all reduced costs $\bar{c}_j - c_j \geq 0$. Substituting $c_j \rightarrow c_j + \Delta c_j$ and solving for the range of $\Delta c_j$ that preserves non-negativity yields the allowable increase and decrease.

**Why does the Range of Feasibility matter?**
Resources (labour hours, raw material, machine time) are subject to supply fluctuations. The Range of Feasibility tells the manager **how much a resource can shrink or grow** before the current production schedule becomes infeasible.

**How is the Range of Feasibility computed?**
Feasibility requires $x_B = B^{-1} b \geq 0$. With $b \rightarrow b + \Delta b$, we solve $B^{-1}(b + \Delta b) \geq 0$ for $\Delta b$ to obtain the allowable range.

### 2.3 The 100% Rule for Simultaneous Changes

When **two or more parameters** are estimated incorrectly and all change simultaneously, the **100% Rule** provides a sufficient condition under which the current optimal solution *or* basis remains valid.

**For Objective Function Coefficients (Optimality Test):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{j \in \text{changing}} \frac{\vert \Delta c_j \vert}{\vert \Delta c_j^{\max} \vert} \leq 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**For RHS Values (Feasibility Test):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{i \in \text{changing}} \frac{\vert \Delta b_i \vert}{\vert \Delta b_i^{\max} \vert} \leq 1
\end{aligned}
$$

If the sum does not exceed **1 (i.e., 100%)**, the current optimal solution (or basis) is guaranteed to remain valid.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often forget that the 100% Rule provides a *sufficient* condition — failure of the rule does not automatically mean the basis changes; it simply means a re-solve is required.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula / Condition** | **Interpretation** |
|---|---|---|
| Optimality Condition (Max) | $\bar{c}_j = c_j - c_B^T B^{-1} a_j \geq 0$ for all non-basic $j$ | Ensures no profitable variable exists outside the basis |
| Reduced Cost | $\bar{c}_j - c_j$ | Amount by which $c_j$ must improve for variable to enter |
| Shadow Price | $\pi_i = c_B^T B^{-1} e_i = y_i$ | Marginal value of one extra unit of resource $i$ |
| Basic Variables | $x_B = B^{-1} b$ | Values of basic variables at optimality |
| Range of Optimality | $c_j + \Delta c_j \in [c_j - \Delta_{\max}^-, \ c_j + \Delta_{\max}^+]$ | Interval where basis remains optimal |
| Range of Feasibility | $b_i + \Delta b_i \in [b_i - \Delta_{\max}^-, \ b_i + \Delta_{\max}^+]$ | Interval where basis remains feasible |
| 100% Rule (Objective) | $\sum \frac{\vert \Delta c_j \vert}{\vert \Delta_j^{\max} \vert} \leq 1$ | Sufficient for basis optimality |
| 100% Rule (RHS) | $\sum \frac{\vert \Delta b_i \vert}{\vert \Delta_i^{\max} \vert} \leq 1$ | Sufficient for basis feasibility |
| New Variable Test | Reduced cost $\bar{c}_{n+1} > 0$ (Max) | Introduce if profitable |
| New Constraint Test | $a_{\text{new}} x^* \leq b_{\text{new}}$? | If yes, current solution still optimal |

> [!NOTE]
> All absolute values in the table above use $\vert \cdot \vert$ notation to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering and Business

Sensitivity Analysis is the **cornerstone of robust decision-making** in:

- **Manufacturing:** Determining how sensitive the production plan is to fluctuations in raw material costs or machine availability.
- **Finance:** Portfolio optimization under uncertain returns.
- **Logistics:** Evaluating the impact of fuel price changes on distribution network design.
- **Healthcare:** Resource allocation under uncertain patient inflow rates.
- **Energy:** Optimal power generation mix under fluctuating demand and fuel costs.

In every domain, the principle is the same: **understand which parameters matter most, and quantify the cost of being wrong.**

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Range of Optimality for a Basic Variable

Consider the standard LP in standard form with optimal basis $B$ and basis variables indexed by $B$. Let $x_k \in B$ be a basic variable with current objective coefficient $c_k$. We wish to find the allowable change $\Delta c_k$ such that optimality is preserved.

**Step 1 — Express the optimality condition.**
For maximization, the reduced cost of every non-basic variable $x_j$ must satisfy:
$$
\bar{c}_j = c_j - c_B^T B^{-1} a_j \geq 0
$$

**Step 2 — Isolate the dependency on $c_k$.**
Let $e_k$ be the unit column vector selecting row $k$ in $c_B$. The vector $c_B^T B^{-1}$ (the simplex multipliers, $y^T$) depends on $c_B$ linearly. Specifically, if $c_k \to c_k + \Delta c_k$, then:
$$
y^T(\Delta c_k) = y^T(0) + \Delta c_k \cdot e_k^T B^{-1}
$$

The change in the $j$-th reduced cost is therefore:
$$
\Delta \bar{c}_j = -\Delta c_k \cdot (B^{-1})_{kj}
$$

**Step 3 — Enforce the optimality condition post-change.**
We require $\bar{c}_j + \Delta \bar{c}_j \geq 0$ for all non-basic $j$:
$$
\bar{c}_j - \Delta c_k \cdot (B^{-1})_{kj} \geq 0
$$

**Step 4 — Solve the two-sided inequality.**
This produces:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{If } (B^{-1})_{kj} > 0: & \quad \Delta c_k \leq \frac{\bar{c}_j}{(B^{-1})_{kj}} \\
\text{If } (B^{-1})_{kj} < 0: & \quad \Delta c_k \geq \frac{\bar{c}_j}{(B^{-1})_{kj}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Take the tightest bounds across all non-basic $j$.**
$$
\Delta c_k^{\max+} = \min_{j: (B^{-1})_{kj} > 0} \left\{ \frac{\bar{c}_j}{(B^{-1})_{kj}} \right\}
$$
$$
\Delta c_k^{\max-} = \min_{j: (B^{-1})_{kj} < 0} \left\{ -\frac{\bar{c}_j}{(B^{-1})_{kj}} \right\}
$$

The Range of Optimality for $c_k$ is:
$$
c_k \in \left[ c_k - \Delta c_k^{\max-}, \ c_k + \Delta c_k^{\max+} \right]
$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Range of Feasibility for RHS

**Step 1 — Current basic solution.**
At optimality, the basic variables take the value $x_B = B^{-1} b$.

**Step 2 — Perturb the RHS.**
Let $b_i \to b_i + \Delta b_i$. Denote the change vector as $\Delta b = \Delta b_i \cdot e_i$. The new basic solution is:
$$
x_B(\Delta b_i) = B^{-1} b + \Delta b_i \cdot B^{-1} e_i = x_B + \Delta b_i \cdot \bar{a}_i
$$
where $\bar{a}_i$ is the $i$-th column of $B^{-1}$.

**Step 3 — Enforce feasibility.**
We require every component of $x_B(\Delta b_i)$ to be non-negative:
$$
x_{B,r} + \Delta b_i \cdot (B^{-1})_{ri} \geq 0 \quad \text{for all basic rows } r
$$

**Step 4 — Solve the inequality.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{If } (B^{-1})_{ri} > 0: & \quad \Delta b_i \geq -\frac{x_{B,r}}{(B^{-1})_{ri}} \\
\text{If } (B^{-1})_{ri} < 0: & \quad \Delta b_i \leq -\frac{x_{B,r}}{(B^{-1})_{ri}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Take the tightest bounds.**
$$
\Delta b_i^{\max+} = \min_{r: (B^{-1})_{ri} < 0} \left\{ -\frac{x_{B,r}}{(B^{-1})_{ri}} \right\}
$$
$$
\Delta b_i^{\max-} = \min_{r: (B^{-1})_{ri} > 0} \left\{ \frac{x_{B,r}}{(B^{-1})_{ri}} \right\}
$$

The Range of Feasibility for $b_i$ is:
$$
b_i \in \left[ b_i - \Delta b_i^{\max-}, \ b_i + \Delta b_i^{\max+} \right]
$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Analytical Matrix)

**Problem:** A furniture company produces Tables ($x_1$) and Chairs ($x_2$).
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Max } Z &= 40 x_1 + 30 x_2 \\
\text{s.t. } & 2x_1 + x_2 \leq 100 \quad \text{(Wood)} \\
& x_1 + 2x_2 \leq 80 \quad \text{(Labour)} \\
& x_1, x_2 \geq 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Optimal Tableau (final simplex):**

| Basis | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $s_1$ | $s_2$ | RHS |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $Z$ | 0 | 0 | 14 | 8 | 3200 |
| $x_1$ | 1 | 0 | 2/3 | -1/3 | 40 |
| $x_2$ | 0 | 1 | -1/3 | 2/3 | 20 |

**Reading the Tableau:**

- Optimal solution: $x_1 = 40$, $x_2 = 20$, $Z^* = 3200$.
- Shadow prices: $\pi_1 = 14$ (Wood), $\pi_2 = 8$ (Labour).
- Reduced costs: $s_1, s_2$ are slacks with $\bar{c}_{s_1} = 14$, $\bar{c}_{s_2} = 8$ (maximization; both $\geq 0$ confirms optimality).
- Inverse basis matrix:
$$
B^{-1} = \begin{pmatrix} 2/3 & -1/3 \\ -1/3 & 2/3 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Question (a):** What is the range of optimality for the profit coefficient of Tables ($c_1 = 40$)?

Using the formula with $B^{-1}$ column for $x_1$ — but $x_1$ is basic, so we examine the effect on the slacks' reduced costs.

The change in reduced cost of $s_1$: $\Delta \bar{c}_{s_1} = -\Delta c_1 \cdot (B^{-1})_{1, s_1 \text{ position}}$. For $c_1$ change, the multipliers $y^T$ change in row 1 of $B^{-1}$, i.e., $(2/3, -1/3)$.

Constraint: For each non-basic $j$,
$$
\bar{c}_j - \Delta c_1 \cdot (B^{-1})_{kj} \geq 0
$$

For $s_1$: $14 - \Delta c_1 \cdot (2/3) \geq 0 \Rightarrow \Delta c_1 \leq 21$.

For $s_2$: $8 - \Delta c_1 \cdot (-1/3) \geq 0 \Rightarrow \Delta c_1 \leq 24$.

Tightest bound: $\Delta c_1^{\max+} = 21$. By symmetry, $\Delta c_1^{\max-} = 12$.

**Range of Optimality:** $c_1 \in [40 - 12, 40 + 21] = [28, 61]$.

**Question (b):** What is the range of feasibility for Wood ($b_1 = 100$)?

The $B^{-1}$ column corresponding to $b_1$ perturbation: we need $B^{-1} e_1 = (2/3, -1/3)^T$.

Current $x_B = (40, 20)^T$. Apply:
$$
x_{B,r} + \Delta b_1 \cdot (B^{-1})_{r1} \geq 0
$$

Row 1 ($x_1$): $40 + \Delta b_1 \cdot (2/3) \geq 0 \Rightarrow \Delta b_1 \geq -60$.

Row 2 ($x_2$): $20 + \Delta b_1 \cdot (-1/3) \geq 0 \Rightarrow \Delta b_1 \leq 60$.

**Range of Feasibility:** $b_1 \in [100 - 60, 100 + 60] = [40, 160]$.

**Verification of Shadow Price:** If Wood increases from 100 to 101, $Z^*$ should increase by $\pi_1 = 14$. Using the formula:
$$
\Delta Z = y^T \Delta b = (14, 8) \cdot (1, 0)^T = 14 \checkmark
$$

### 3.4 The 100% Rule — Worked Illustration

Suppose simultaneously $c_1$ drops by 5 and $c_2$ drops by 4. Allowable changes: $c_1^- = 12$, $c_2^- = 8$ (assume).

$$
\text{Percentage} = \frac{5}{12} + \frac{4}{8} = 0.4167 + 0.5 = 0.9167 \leq 1
$$

Since the sum is **less than 100%**, the current optimal basis remains optimal. The new optimal value changes by:
$$
\Delta Z = \Delta c_1 x_1^* + \Delta c_2 x_2^* = (-5)(40) + (-4)(20) = -280
$$
$$
Z_{\text{new}}^* = 3200 - 280 = 2920
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Sensitivity Analysis Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Optimal LP Solution Obtained] --> B{What Type of Change?}
    
    B -->|Change in cj| C[Objective Coefficient Analysis]
    B -->|Change in bi| D[RHS Resource Analysis]
    B -->|Add New Variable| E[New Product Evaluation]
    B -->|Add New Constraint| F[New Requirement Check]
    
    C --> C1[Compute New Reduced Costs]
    C1 --> C2{All Reduced Costs >= 0?}
    C2 -->|Yes| C3[Basis Remains Optimal]
    C2 -->|No| C4[Re-solve LP]
    
    D --> D1[Compute B-inverse times b]
    D1 --> D2{All Basic Variables >= 0?}
    D2 -->|Yes| D3[Basis Remains Feasible]
    D2 -->|No| D4[Re-solve LP Dual]
    
    E --> E1[Compute Reduced Cost of New Variable]
    E1 --> E2{Reduced Cost Favorable?}
    E2 -->|Yes| E3[Introduce Variable and Re-solve]
    E2 -->|No| E4[Do Not Introduce]
    
    F --> F1[Evaluate Constraint at Current Solution]
    F1 --> F2{Constraint Satisfied?}
    F2 -->|Yes| F3[Current Solution Still Optimal]
    F2 -->|No| F4[Add to LP and Re-solve]
    
    C3 --> G[Update Z Value and Report]
    D3 --> G
    E4 --> G
    F3 --> G
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Sensitivity Analysis Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Layer]
        A1[Optimal Basis B]
        A2[Current cj Values]
        A3[Current bi Values]
        A4[Proposed Changes]
    end
    
    subgraph PROCESS[Processing Engine]
        B1[Compute B-inverse]
        B2[Reduced Cost Calculator]
        B3[Feasibility Checker]
        B4[100 Percent Rule Validator]
        B5[Shadow Price Engine]
    end
    
    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Layer]
        C1[Range of Optimality]
        C2[Range of Feasibility]
        C3[New Optimal Value Z]
        C4[Decision Recommendation]
    end
    
    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B2
    A3 --> B3
    A4 --> B4
    
    B1 --> B2
    B1 --> B3
    B1 --> B5
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C2
    B4 --> C1
    B4 --> C2
    B5 --> C3
    B1 --> C3
    C1 --> C4
    C2 --> C4
    C3 --> C4
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Sensitivity Analysis. Mention its significance in linear programming.**

**Model Answer:**
Sensitivity Analysis, also called **post-optimality analysis**, is the study of how the optimal solution of a linear programming problem changes when the model parameters (objective coefficients, RHS values, or constraint coefficients) are varied. Its significance lies in:

1. **Validating the solution** under uncertain or estimated input data.
2. **Identifying critical parameters** that most influence the optimal decision.
3. **Avoiding repeated re-solves** by providing allowable ranges of change.
4. **Supporting managerial insight** through shadow prices and reduced costs.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is a shadow price? Explain with an example.**

**Model Answer:**
A **shadow price** ($\pi_i$) of a resource constraint is the rate of change in the optimal objective function value $Z^*$ per unit change in the RHS value $b_i$, assuming all other parameters remain constant.

**Example:** If the shadow price of a raw material constraint is ₹15, then an additional unit of that raw material increases the optimal profit by ₹15, provided the increase lies within the range of feasibility.

Mathematically:
$$
\pi_i = \frac{\partial Z^*}{\partial b_i} = c_B^T B^{-1} e_i
$$

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

A manufacturing firm produces two products A and B. The final optimal simplex tableau is given below:

| Basis | $x_1$ (A) | $x_2$ (B) | $s_1$ | $s_2$ | $s_3$ | RHS |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $Z$ | 0 | 0 | 5 | 3 | 2 | 280 |
| $x_1$ | 1 | 0 | 2 | -1 | 0 | 20 |
| $x_2$ | 0 | 1 | -1 | 2 | 1 | 30 |

**Answer the following:**

**(a)** Determine the **range of optimality** for the profit coefficient of product A. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Determine the **range of feasibility** for the second constraint. If the right-hand side of this constraint increases by 10 units, find the new optimal value of $Z$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Range of Optimality for $c_1$ (Product A):**

The current optimal basis consists of $x_1$ and $x_2$. The columns of the inverse basis matrix are read from the slack columns $s_1$ and $s_2$ (or $s_3$):

$$
B^{-1} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & -1 & 0 \\ -1 & 2 & 1 \end{pmatrix}
$$

For a change $\Delta c_1$ in the coefficient of $x_1$ (basic variable), the simplex multiplier vector changes as:
$$
\Delta y^T = (\Delta c_1, 0) B^{-1} = (2\Delta c_1, -\Delta c_1, 0)
$$

The new reduced costs of non-basic variables $s_1, s_2, s_3$ must remain $\geq 0$:

- $\bar{c}_{s_1}$: $5 - 2\Delta c_1 \geq 0 \Rightarrow \Delta c_1 \leq 2.5$
- $\bar{c}_{s_2}$: $3 - (-\Delta c_1) \geq 0 \Rightarrow \Delta c_1 \geq -3$
- $\bar{c}_{s_3}$: $2 - 0 \geq 0$ (no constraint)

**Allowable Increase:** $\Delta c_1^{\max+} = 2.5$
**Allowable Decrease:** $\Delta c_1^{\max-} = 3$

**Range of Optimality:** $c_1 \in [c_1 - 3, \ c_1 + 2.5]$

If $c_1 = 50$ (assumed from problem), then $c_1 \in [47, 52.5]$.

**(b) Range of Feasibility for Constraint 2 and New $Z$:**

The shadow price for constraint 2 is read from row $Z$: $\pi_2 = 3$.

The $B^{-1}$ column corresponding to constraint 2 is the second column:
$$
(B^{-1})_{\cdot, 2} = \begin{pmatrix} -1 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix}
$$

Apply the feasibility condition $x_{B,r} + \Delta b_2 \cdot (B^{-1})_{r,2} \geq 0$:

- Row 1: $20 + \Delta b_2 \cdot (-1) \geq 0 \Rightarrow \Delta b_2 \leq 20$
- Row 2: $30 + \Delta b_2 \cdot 2 \geq 0 \Rightarrow \Delta b_2 \geq -15$

**Range of Feasibility:** $b_2 \in [b_2 - 15, \ b_2 + 20]$.

If $b_2 = 100$, then $b_2 \in [85, 120]$.

For an increase of $\Delta b_2 = 10$ (within the range):
$$
\Delta Z = \pi_2 \cdot \Delta b_2 = 3 \times 10 = 30
$$
$$
Z_{\text{new}}^* = 280 + 30 = 310
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

Consider the following LP problem and its optimal tableau:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Max } Z &= 5x_1 + 4x_2 \\
\text{s.t. } & 6x_1 + 4x_2 \leq 24 \\
& x_1 + 2x_2 \leq 6 \\
& x_1, x_2 \geq 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Optimal Tableau:**

| Basis | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $s_1$ | $s_2$ | RHS |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $Z$ | 0 | 0 | 3/4 | 1/2 | 21 |
| $x_1$ | 1 | 0 | 1/2 | -1/2 | 3 |
| $x_2$ | 0 | 1 | -1/4 | 3/4 | 3/2 |

**Answer the following:**

**(a)** Find the **shadow prices** of both resources and interpret them. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Suppose the profit coefficient $c_1$ drops from 5 to 3 and $c_2$ increases from 4 to 6 simultaneously. Using the **100% Rule**, determine whether the current basis remains optimal. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Shadow Prices and Interpretation:**

The shadow prices are the coefficients of slack variables in the $Z$ row:
- $\pi_1 = 3/4 = 0.75$ (Resource 1: Wood-like)
- $\pi_2 = 1/2 = 0.50$ (Resource 2: Labour-like)

**Interpretation:**
- If we obtain **one additional unit of Resource 1**, the optimal profit $Z^*$ will increase by ₹0.75, provided the increase stays within the range of feasibility.
- If we obtain **one additional unit of Resource 2**, the optimal profit $Z^*$ will increase by ₹0.50, within the feasibility range.
- Resource 1 is **more valuable** per unit than Resource 2; the firm should prioritize acquiring more of Resource 1 first.

**(b) Applying the 100% Rule:**

We need the allowable changes for $c_1$ and $c_2$.

For $c_1$ (basic variable): Using the $B^{-1}$ matrix from the slack columns:
$$
B^{-1} = \begin{pmatrix} 1/2 & -1/2 \\ -1/4 & 3/4 \end{pmatrix}
$$

Effect on reduced costs of $s_1$ and $s_2$:
- $\bar{c}_{s_1}$: $3/4 - \Delta c_1 \cdot (1/2) \geq 0 \Rightarrow \Delta c_1 \leq 3/2$
- $\bar{c}_{s_2}$: $1/2 - \Delta c_1 \cdot (-1/2) \geq 0 \Rightarrow \Delta c_1 \geq -1$

**Allowable:** $\Delta c_1^{\max+} = 1.5$, $\Delta c_1^{\max-} = 1$

For $c_2$ (basic variable): similar analysis yields $\Delta c_2^{\max+} = 1$ and $\Delta c_2^{\max-} = 2$ (after calculation; assumed for illustration).

**Given Changes:**
- $\Delta c_1 = 3 - 5 = -2$ (decrease of 2)
- $\Delta c_2 = 6 - 4 = +2$ (increase of 2)

**Applying the 100% Rule:**
$$
\frac{\vert \Delta c_1 \vert}{\Delta c_1^{\max-}} + \frac{\vert \Delta c_2 \vert}{\Delta c_2^{\max+}} = \frac{2}{1} + \frac{2}{1} = 2 + 2 = 4
$$

Since $4 > 1$ (i.e., **400%**), the 100% Rule is **violated**. We **cannot conclude** the basis remains optimal from this rule alone. A re-solve of the LP is required to determine the new optimal solution.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting absolute values** in the 100% Rule. Both decreases and increases in $c_j$ contribute positively to the percentage sum.
> 2. **Confusing Range of Optimality with Range of Feasibility.** The former applies to objective coefficients; the latter to RHS values. Examiners frequently test this distinction.
> 3. **Omitting the interpretation of shadow prices.** Shadow prices are not just numbers — they must be **interpreted in business terms** (e.g., "worth of an extra unit of resource").
> 4. **Misapplying the 100% Rule direction.** It tests the **optimality** of the basis for $c_j$ changes and the **feasibility** of the basis for $b_i$ changes — not vice versa.
> 5. **Skipping units in final answers.** Always state the unit (₹, units of resource) to gain full marks in KTU valuation.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

**Quick-Fire Revision Checklist:**

- **Sensitivity Analysis** = study of how optimal LP solutions respond to parameter changes.
- Three pillars: **$c_j$ changes, $b_i$ changes, new variables/constraints.**
- **Shadow price** = marginal value of a resource; read from the $Z$-row coefficient of the corresponding slack/surplus.
- **Reduced cost** = amount by which a non-basic variable's $c_j$ must improve to enter the basis.
- **Range of Optimality** = interval of $c_j$ over which the current basis is optimal.
- **Range of Feasibility** = interval of $b_i$ over which the current basis is feasible.
- **100% Rule (Objective):** $\sum \frac{\vert \Delta c_j \vert}{\Delta_j^{\max}} \leq 1 \Rightarrow$ basis remains optimal.
- **100% Rule (RHS):** $\sum \frac{\vert \Delta b_i \vert}{\Delta_i^{\max}} \leq 1 \Rightarrow$ basis remains feasible.
- New variable is **worth introducing** only if its reduced cost is favourable (positive for max, negative for min).
- New constraint is **binding** only if current solution violates it; otherwise, current solution stands.
- $B^{-1}$ is the **key computational tool** — read its columns from the slack columns of the optimal tableau.
- Always **interpret results in business context** — KTU values managerial insight, not just numerical answers.
- The 100% Rule gives a **sufficient** condition; its failure does not automatically mean re-solving is needed, but it cannot be ruled out.
- For maximization: reduced costs must be $\geq 0$; for minimization: reduced costs must be $\leq 0$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "SCORE":**
> - **S**hadow price = $Z$-row slack coefficient
> - **C**hange in $Z$ = $\sum (\text{change in } c_j \times x_j^*)$ or $\sum (\pi_i \times \Delta b_i)$
> - **O**ptimality range for $c_j$; **R**HS range for $b_i$
> - **E**valuate new variables by reduced cost; new constraints by feasibility check
> - **100% Rule** = "Add percentages; if under 100, safe."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Human Resource Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT522 - BUSINESS ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Human Resource Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Human Resource Management (HRM) — Formal Definition

**Human Resource Management (HRM)** is the strategic, integrated, and systematic process of planning, recruiting, selecting, developing, compensating, and retaining the human capital of an organization to achieve its short-term operational goals and long-term strategic objectives.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** (PEADT522 – Business Analytics), HRM is treated not merely as an administrative personnel function, but as a **data-driven strategic discipline** that intersects with workforce analytics, organizational behavior, and predictive modelling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 1:**
> The KTU Board treats HRM under the umbrella of *Business Analytics* because modern HR departments use descriptive, diagnostic, predictive, and prescriptive analytics to make evidence-based decisions on talent, performance, compensation, and retention.

### 1.2 Human Resource Analytics (HR Analytics) — Formal Definition

**Human Resource Analytics (HR Analytics)** is the application of data science, statistical modelling, and visualization techniques to Human Resource Management data in order to improve employee performance, optimize workforce planning, and enhance organizational effectiveness.

It is formally defined by the **Society for Human Resource Management (SHRM)** as:

> *"HR Analytics is a data-driven, goal-oriented approach to improving decisions about talent and the organization through people-data analysis."*

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Hospital ICU Monitor"**
>
> Imagine a hospital's Intensive Care Unit (ICU). Doctors do not just observe patients; they attach **monitors** that continuously track Heart Rate (BPM), Oxygen Saturation ($\text{SpO}_2$), and Blood Pressure (mmHg). If a reading crosses a threshold, an **alarm** rings.
>
> In exactly the same way, **HRM** is the "doctor" managing employee health (satisfaction, performance, retention), while **HR Analytics** is the "monitor + alarm system." It tracks metrics such as:
> - **Employee Engagement Score** (like BPM)
> - **Attrition Rate** (like fever temperature)
> - **Time-to-Hire** (like patient recovery time)
>
> Without analytics, HRM is *reactive* (treats illness after it occurs). With analytics, HRM becomes *proactive and predictive* (prevents illness before it occurs).

### 1.4 Standard HR Metrics Reference Values (Industry Benchmarks)

The following metrics are standardized across industries and frequently tested in **KTU ESE (End Semester Evaluation)**:

| Metric | Industry Standard Benchmark | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Voluntary Turnover Rate | **10%–15%** annually | Percentage (%) |
| Absenteeism Rate | **1.5%–3.0%** of total workdays | Percentage (%) |
| Revenue per Employee | **\$150,000–\$300,000** | USD |
| Time-to-Fill (TTF) | **36–42** working days | Days |
| Offer Acceptance Rate | **85%–90%** | Percentage (%) |
| Training Hours per Employee | **24–40** hours/year | Hours |
| Engagement Score | **70%–80%** (Gallup norm) | Percentage (%) |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for HRM Functions:** **"PROMOCS"**
> **P** – Procurement (Recruitment & Selection)
> **R** – Retention
> **O** – Orientation & Onboarding
> **M** – Motivation & Compensation
> **O** – Opportunity (Training & Development)
> **C** – Compliance (Legal & Labour Laws)
> **S** – Strategic Alignment
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Core Functions of HRM (Operational Logic)

The functions of HRM are classified into two broad categories in KTU 2024 Scheme:

#### A. Managerial Functions (performed by all managers)
1. **Planning** — Forecasting manpower requirements (both quantitative and qualitative).
2. **Organizing** — Designing the organizational structure, jobs, roles, and reporting hierarchy.
3. **Directing** — Leading, motivating, communicating, and supervising the workforce.
4. **Controlling** — Monitoring performance, comparing with standards, and initiating corrective action.

#### B. Operative Functions (performed specifically by the HR department)
1. **Procurement (Manpower Planning)** — Job analysis, recruitment, selection, placement, induction.
2. **Development** — Training, executive development, career planning, succession planning.
3. **Compensation** — Wage/salary administration, incentives, fringe benefits, performance-linked pay.
4. **Integration** — Grievance handling, collective bargaining, employee welfare, discipline.
5. **Maintenance** — Health & safety, social security, labour law compliance, separation/retirement.

### 2.2 The Four Types of HR Analytics (KTU High-Yield Topic)

This is one of the most frequently asked topics in **KTU ESE Module 1**.

| Type of Analytics | Core Question Answered | Technique Used | Example in HRM |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Descriptive Analytics** | *"What happened?"* | Dashboards, KPI reports, histograms | Monthly attrition report showing **12%** turnover in Q3 |
| **Diagnostic Analytics** | *"Why did it happen?"* | Drill-down, correlation, root cause analysis | Identifying that attrition is highest in the **21–25 age bracket** with **<2 years tenure** |
| **Predictive Analytics** | *"What will happen?"* | Regression, classification, time-series forecasting | Predicting that **23 employees** are likely to resign in the next 6 months |
| **Prescriptive Analytics** | *"What should we do?"* | Optimization, simulation, decision trees | Recommending a **counter-offer strategy** with 8% salary hike to retain the top 5% high-risk talent |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:**
> Students frequently confuse *Diagnostic* with *Predictive* analytics. Remember:
> - *Diagnostic* looks **backward** (root cause of past events).
> - *Predictive* looks **forward** (likelihood of future events).
> - *Prescriptive* suggests the **best action**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All the following formulas are mathematically derivable and form the **core calculation toolkit** for KTU numerical problems in Module 1.

#### HR Metric Formulas

Let $N$ denote the number of employees at a given reference point. All rates are expressed as percentages.

$$
\text{Turnover Rate (\%)} = \left(\frac{\text{Number of Separations during Period}}{\text{Average Number of Employees}}\right) \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Absenteeism Rate (\%)} = \left(\frac{\text{Total Days Lost due to Absence}}{\text{Total Available Workdays}}\right) \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Time-to-Fill (Days)} = \text{Date of Offer Acceptance} - \text{Date of Requisition Approval}
$$

$$
\text{Offer Acceptance Rate (\%)} = \left(\frac{\text{Offers Accepted}}{\text{Offers Extended}}\right) \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Revenue per Employee (USD)} = \frac{\text{Total Annual Revenue}}{\text{Average Headcount}}
$$

$$
\text{Training ROI (\%)} = \left(\frac{\text{Monetary Benefits of Training} - \text{Training Cost}}{\text{Training Cost}}\right) \times 100
$$

#### HR Predictive Model Formulas

$$
\text{Linear Regression (Engagement Score Prediction):}
$$

$$
y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \beta_2 x_2 + \dots + \beta_n x_n + \epsilon
$$

Where:
- $y$ = predicted employee outcome (e.g., attrition probability)
- $\beta_0$ = intercept (baseline value)
- $\beta_i$ = coefficient of the $i^{\text{th}}$ predictor
- $x_i$ = $i^{\text{th}}$ predictor variable (e.g., salary, tenure, engagement)
- $\epsilon$ = error term

$$
\text{Attrition Probability (Logistic Regression):}
$$

$$
P(\text{Attrition} = 1) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(\beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \dots + \beta_n x_n)}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Pitfall Avoidance:** When computing *Average Number of Employees*, do **not** use only the year-end headcount. The correct formula is:
>
> $$\text{Average Headcount} = \frac{\text{Headcount at Start} + \text{Headcount at End}}{2}$$
>
> Many students use just one value and lose **2 marks**.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility of HR Analytics in Engineering Industries

- **IT/Software Companies (TCS, Infosys, Wipro):** Predict project-resource mismatches, optimize bench-strength utilization.
- **Manufacturing (L&T, Tata Steel):** Predict machine-operator attrition on critical production lines; reduce safety incidents.
- **Healthcare Engineering (GE Healthcare, Siemens Healthineers):** Optimize hospital staffing using demand-forecasting models.
- **BPO/KPO Industry:** Reduce absenteeism using shift-rotation optimization and engagement prediction.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example — Attrition Rate Calculation (Descriptive Analytics)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement [KTU University Exam Style]:**
> TechVista Solutions Pvt. Ltd., a software firm, had the following employee data for the financial year 2024–25:
>
> - Employees at the **start of the year** (1 April 2024): **1,200**
> - Employees at the **end of the year** (31 March 2025): **1,350**
> - Total **separations** during the year: **180**
> - Total **new hires** during the year: **330**
>
> Compute:
> 1. The Average Headcount
> 2. The Turnover Rate
> 3. The Net Change in Workforce
> 4. Comment on whether the turnover is "healthy" by industry benchmarks.

#### Step 1: Compute the Average Headcount

The Average Headcount is the arithmetic mean of the starting and ending headcount values.

$$
\text{Average Headcount} = \frac{\text{Headcount}_{\text{start}} + \text{Headcount}_{\text{end}}}{2}
$$

Substitute the given values:

$$
\text{Average Headcount} = \frac{1200 + 1350}{2}
$$

$$
\text{Average Headcount} = \frac{2550}{2} = 1275 \text{ employees}
$$

#### Step 2: Compute the Turnover Rate

$$
\text{Turnover Rate} = \left(\frac{\text{Separations}}{\text{Average Headcount}}\right) \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Turnover Rate} = \left(\frac{180}{1275}\right) \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Turnover Rate} = 0.14117 \times 100 \approx 14.12\%
$$

#### Step 3: Compute the Net Change in Workforce

$$
\text{Net Change} = \text{New Hires} - \text{Separations}
$$

$$
\text{Net Change} = 330 - 180 = +150 \text{ employees}
$$

This is also verifiable as $1350 - 1200 = 150$.

#### Step 4: Analytical Commentary

Comparing $14.12\%$ to the **KTU/industry benchmark of 10%–15%**:

> The turnover rate is **within the healthy benchmark range**, but on the **higher side**. The positive net change of 150 employees indicates the firm is in a **growth phase**, but HR analytics should be deployed in **diagnostic mode** to identify which department, age bracket, or tenure band is contributing most to the 180 separations.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example — Absenteeism Rate with Multi-Group Analysis (Diagnostic Analytics)

> **Problem Statement:**
> An engineering college has **3 departments**: CSE, ECE, and MECH, each with **100 students/staff members**. The total available working days in a semester is **120 days**. The absenteeism data (in total days lost) is:
>
> - CSE: **240 days**
> - ECE: **360 days**
> - MECH: **150 days**
>
> Compute:
> 1. Department-wise absenteeism rates.
> 2. Overall absenteeism rate.
> 3. Identify the department needing **urgent intervention** (the highest rate).
> 4. Suggest a **prescriptive analytics** solution.

#### Step 1: Department-wise Absenteeism Rate

Total available person-days for each department:

$$
\text{Available Person-Days} = 100 \times 120 = 12{,}000 \text{ days}
$$

For CSE:

$$
\text{Absenteeism}_{\text{CSE}} = \left(\frac{240}{12000}\right) \times 100 = 2.00\%
$$

For ECE:

$$
\text{Absenteeism}_{\text{ECE}} = \left(\frac{360}{12000}\right) \times 100 = 3.00\%
$$

For MECH:

$$
\text{Absenteeism}_{\text{MECH}} = \left(\frac{150}{12000}\right) \times 100 = 1.25\%
$$

#### Step 2: Overall Absenteeism Rate

$$
\text{Absenteeism}_{\text{Overall}} = \frac{240 + 360 + 150}{3 \times 12000} \times 100
$$

$$
= \frac{750}{36000} \times 100 = 2.083\%
$$

#### Step 3: Diagnostic Insight

ECE has the **highest absenteeism rate (3.00%)**, which is **double** the MECH rate. Diagnostic analytics should investigate:
- Subject difficulty perception
- Faculty-student ratio
- Class scheduling (early morning vs afternoon slots)

#### Step 4: Prescriptive Recommendation

Deploy **predictive analytics** using a logistic regression model on past attendance data, then use **prescriptive optimization** to:
- Reschedule high-difficulty subjects to mid-week.
- Introduce mentorship programs.
- Implement attendance-linked gamification.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example — Logistic Regression for Attrition Prediction (Predictive Analytics)

> **Problem Statement:**
> A logistic regression model was built to predict employee attrition using two predictors:
> - $x_1$ = Monthly Salary (in ₹ thousands)
> - $x_2$ = Years of Tenure
>
> The fitted model is:
> $$\beta_0 = 2.5, \quad \beta_1 = -0.04, \quad \beta_2 = -0.15$$
>
> For an employee with **Salary = ₹60,000** and **Tenure = 4 years**, compute the probability of attrition.

#### Step 1: Substitute into the Logistic Regression Equation

$$
P(\text{Attrition}) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(\beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \beta_2 x_2)}}
$$

With $x_1 = 60$ (since salary is in ₹ thousands: ₹60,000 / 1000 = 60) and $x_2 = 4$:

$$
z = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \beta_2 x_2
$$

$$
z = 2.5 + (-0.04)(60) + (-0.15)(4)
$$

$$
z = 2.5 - 2.4 - 0.6 = -0.5
$$

#### Step 2: Compute the Probability

$$
P(\text{Attrition}) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(-0.5)}} = \frac{1}{1 + e^{0.5}}
$$

$$
e^{0.5} \approx 1.6487
$$

$$
P(\text{Attrition}) = \frac{1}{1 + 1.6487} = \frac{1}{2.6487} \approx 0.3775
$$

$$
P(\text{Attrition}) \approx 37.75\%
$$

#### Step 3: Interpretation

There is approximately a **37.75% probability** that this employee will leave the organization. Since this exceeds the typical 30% threshold, the company should consider a **retention intervention** (prescriptive analytics) such as a salary revision, promotion, or lateral role change.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mark Allocation Tip:**
> For such derivations, marks are typically awarded as:
> - Correct substitution: **3 marks**
> - Correct evaluation of $z$: **3 marks**
> - Correct exponential calculation: **3 marks**
> - Correct final probability + interpretation: **3 marks**
> - Neat presentation: **2 marks**
> Total = **14 marks** (Part B).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of Human Resource Management

The following Mermaid block illustrates the integrated functional architecture of HRM, mapping how each function interacts with HR Analytics.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[HR Planning] --> A2[Job Analysis]
    A2 --> A3[Recruitment]

    B2[Diagnostic Analytics] --> A10

    A10 --> A2
    A10 --> A6
    A10 --> A8
```

> - This cyclical architecture represents the **continuous improvement model** mandated by KTU 2024 OBE framework.

---

### 4.2 HR Analytics Maturity Model (Block-Level Architecture Flow)

The following diagram depicts the maturity progression of HR Analytics adoption in organizations — a high-yield KTU 2024 topic.

```mermaid
    U1[Intuition Based Decisions] --> S1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Conceptual Note:**
> Most Indian IT firms today operate at **Stage 3 (Dashboarding)**. Global tech giants like Google and Microsoft operate at **Stage 5**. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to identify the maturity stage of a given case study scenario.

```mermaid
    P1 --> P1A[Example: Reduce Attrition by 20%]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define Human Resource Management. List any four operative functions of HRM.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (2 marks):** Human Resource Management (HRM) is the strategic and systematic process of planning, recruiting, developing, and retaining human capital to achieve organizational objectives. It integrates people-management practices with the organization's strategic goals.
>
> **Four Operative Functions (½ mark each):**
> 1. **Procurement** — Recruitment, selection, placement, induction.
> 2. **Development** — Training, executive development, career planning.
> 3. **Compensation** — Salary administration, incentives, fringe benefits.
> 4. **Integration** — Grievance handling, employee welfare, collective bargaining.

---

#### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Differentiate between HR Metrics and HR Analytics with suitable examples.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | HR Metrics | HR Analytics |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Nature** | Quantitative measurement of simple HR data | Interpretation and modelling of HR data |
| **Question Answered** | *"What is the number/percentage?"* | *"Why is it happening? What will happen? What should we do?"* |
| **Complexity** | Low — uses simple arithmetic | High — uses statistics, ML, and optimization |
| **Example** | Turnover rate = **14%** | Logistic regression predicting the probability of attrition for each employee |
| **Output** | A single number | Insights, forecasts, and recommendations |

> **[Closing remark for 3rd mark]:** HR Metrics are the *raw inputs*; HR Analytics is the *intelligence layer* that converts them into decisions.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, **any ONE of the two questions** must be attempted. Each contains two sub-parts of **7 marks each**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
**(a)** Explain in detail the **four types of HR Analytics** (Descriptive, Diagnostic, Predictive, Prescriptive) with one real-world HR example for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The HR department of BetaTech Pvt. Ltd. collected the following data for the calendar year 2024:
- Headcount on **1 Jan 2024**: **500**
- Headcount on **31 Dec 2024**: **560**
- Total separations during the year: **80**
- Total new hires: **140**
- Total training expenditure: **₹15,00,000**
- Estimated monetary benefit from training (productivity gain): **₹60,00,000**

Compute:
1. Average Headcount
2. Turnover Rate
3. Net Workforce Change
4. Training ROI

Comment on whether the turnover rate is healthy by industry standards. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Four Types of HR Analytics (7 Marks):**

For each type, **1 mark for definition**, **½ mark for technique**, and **½ mark for HR example** (Total: 7 marks).

**1. Descriptive Analytics (What happened?)**
It summarizes historical HR data through KPIs, dashboards, and reports. *Technique:* Bar charts, pie charts, average, percentages. *HR Example:* "Quarterly attrition report showing that 80 employees left in 2024."

**2. Diagnostic Analytics (Why did it happen?)**
It investigates the root causes of past HR outcomes using drill-down, correlation, and statistical testing. *Technique:* Root cause analysis, fishbone diagrams, chi-square test. *HR Example:* "Diagnostic analysis revealed that 65% of attrition occurred in the 0–2 year tenure bracket, primarily due to compensation mismatch."

**3. Predictive Analytics (What will happen?)**
It uses statistical models and machine learning to forecast future HR events. *Technique:* Logistic regression, random forest, time-series forecasting. *HR Example:* "Predicting that 95 employees are likely to resign in 2025 based on engagement score and tenure."

**4. Prescriptive Analytics (What should we do?)**
It recommends optimal actions using optimization and simulation techniques. *Technique:* Linear programming, decision trees, scenario simulation. *HR Example:* "Recommending a 12% salary revision for the top 20% high-risk performers to retain them, with an estimated cost saving of ₹2 crore."

---

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Average Headcount (1 Mark):**

$$
\text{Average Headcount} = \frac{500 + 560}{2} = \frac{1060}{2} = 530 \text{ employees}
$$

**Step 2 — Turnover Rate (2 Marks):**

$$
\text{Turnover Rate} = \left(\frac{80}{530}\right) \times 100 = 15.094\% \approx 15.09\%
$$

**Step 3 — Net Workforce Change (1 Mark):**

$$
\text{Net Change} = 140 - 80 = +60 \text{ employees}
$$

**Step 4 — Training ROI (2 Marks):**

$$
\text{Training ROI} = \left(\frac{60{,}00{,}000 - 15{,}00{,}000}{15{,}00{,}000}\right) \times 100
$$

$$
= \left(\frac{45{,}00{,}000}{15{,}00{,}000}\right) \times 100 = 300\%
$$

**Step 5 — Comment (1 Mark):**

The turnover rate of **15.09%** is **at the upper limit** of the healthy industry benchmark (10%–15%). While the positive net change indicates growth, the company should employ **diagnostic analytics** to identify departments with high attrition and deploy **predictive analytics** for proactive retention.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Alternative]**
**(a)** What is **HR Analytics**? Discuss its **scope and significance** in modern organizations with reference to talent acquisition, performance management, and employee retention. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A logistic regression model for attrition prediction yields the following coefficients:
$\beta_0 = 1.8$, $\beta_1 = -0.03$ (Salary in ₹ thousands), $\beta_2 = -0.25$ (Tenure in years).

For an employee earning **₹50,000/month** with **3 years of tenure**, calculate:
1. The logit value $z$
2. The probability of attrition
3. Interpret the result and suggest a **prescriptive action** if the probability exceeds **30%**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) HR Analytics — Scope and Significance (7 Marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** HR Analytics is the data-driven discipline of collecting, analysing, and interpreting HR data using statistical and computational techniques to improve workforce decisions and organizational outcomes.

**Scope (3 marks):**
1. **Talent Acquisition:** Optimizing recruitment channels, reducing time-to-hire, improving quality-of-hire using predictive scoring models.
2. **Performance Management:** Identifying high-performers and skill gaps; linking compensation to data-driven performance metrics.
3. **Employee Retention:** Predicting flight risk, analysing engagement drivers, designing targeted retention programs.

**Significance (2 marks):** HR Analytics transforms HR from a *cost centre* into a *strategic value driver*, enabling evidence-based decisions, reducing bias, improving employee experience, and aligning workforce strategy with business goals.

---

**(b) Logistic Regression Computation (7 Marks):**

**Given Data:** $x_1 = 50$ (₹50,000 / 1000), $x_2 = 3$, $\beta_0 = 1.8$, $\beta_1 = -0.03$, $\beta_2 = -0.25$.

**Step 1 — Compute the Logit $z$ (2 Marks):**

$$
z = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \beta_2 x_2
$$

$$
z = 1.8 + (-0.03)(50) + (-0.25)(3)
$$

$$
z = 1.8 - 1.5 - 0.75 = -0.45
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Probability of Attrition (3 Marks):**

$$
P(\text{Attrition}) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}} = \frac{1}{1 + e^{0.45}}
$$

Since $e^{0.45} \approx 1.5683$:

$$
P(\text{Attrition}) = \frac{1}{1 + 1.5683} = \frac{1}{2.5683} \approx 0.3894
$$

$$
P(\text{Attrition}) \approx 38.94\%
$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation and Prescriptive Action (2 Marks):**

Since the probability (38.94%) exceeds the 30% threshold, the employee is classified as a **high attrition risk**. **Prescriptive action:** Offer a structured retention package — a salary hike of 10–15%, designation upgrade, or lateral role change. Additionally, conduct a stay-interview to identify non-monetary concerns (workload, manager relationship, growth path).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in KTU ESE for HRM / HR Analytics Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Diagnostic with Predictive analytics** — Costs up to **3 marks**. Remember: Diagnostic = *backward-looking root cause*; Predictive = *forward-looking forecast*.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert salary to thousands** before applying logistic regression — Costs **1 mark** per occurrence.
> 3. **Using only year-end headcount** instead of Average Headcount — Costs **2 marks** in numerical questions.
> 4. **Writing the logistic equation as a linear equation** ($y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x$) instead of using the sigmoid form — Costs **3 marks**.
> 5. **Not interpreting numerical results** — KTU awards **1–2 marks** specifically for the interpretive/commentary step in 14-mark questions.
> 6. **Missing the "units" in the final answer** (e.g., writing 14 instead of 14%) — Minor deduction of **½ mark**, but examiners are strict.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — HRM & HR Analytics (Module 1)**
>
> **A. Core Concepts**
> - HRM is a **strategic, not merely administrative**, function.
> - The **four operative functions** are: Procurement, Development, Compensation, Integration.
> - The **four managerial functions** are: Planning, Organizing, Directing, Controlling.
>
> **B. HR Analytics Hierarchy (Most Asked)**
> - Descriptive → *"What happened?"* (Past)
> - Diagnostic → *"Why?"* (Past causes)
> - Predictive → *"What will happen?"* (Future forecast)
> - Prescriptive → *"What to do?"* (Optimization)
>
> **C. Essential Formulas (Must Memorize)**
> - Turnover Rate = (Separations / Average Headcount) × 100
> - Absenteeism Rate = (Days Lost / Available Workdays) × 100
> - Revenue per Employee = Total Revenue / Average Headcount
> - Training ROI = [(Benefits − Cost) / Cost] × 100
> - Logistic Regression: $P = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(\beta_0 + \sum \beta_i x_i)}}$
>
> **D. KTU-Specific Numerical Skills**
> - Always compute Average Headcount as the **mean of start and end** values.
> - For logistic regression, **substitute values carefully** and remember to take $e^{-z}$, not $e^{z}$.
> - Provide a **business interpretation** for every numerical result.
>
> **E. Process Models**
> - HR Analytics follows the **CRISP-DM** cycle (Business Understanding → Data Understanding → Data Prep → Modelling → Evaluation → Deployment).
> - The **HR Analytics Maturity Model** has 5 stages: Reactive → Standard Reporting → Dashboarding → Predictive → Prescriptive.
>
> **F. Industry Benchmarks (Quick Recall)**
> - Healthy Turnover: **10–15%**
> - Absenteeism: **1.5–3.0%**
> - Offer Acceptance Rate: **85–90%**
> - Engagement Score (Gallup): **70–80%**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Market Share Estimation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT522 - BUSINESS ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Market Share Estimation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Market Share** is defined as the proportion of a firm's sales revenue (or unit volume) of a particular product or product line, expressed as a percentage of the total industry or market sales for that same category, over a specified time period. It is the most fundamental competitive performance metric used in strategic marketing analytics.

$$MS_i = \frac{S_i}{T} \times 100\%$$

Where:
- $MS_i$ = Market Share of firm *i*
- $S_i$ = Sales (revenue or units) of firm *i*
- $T$ = Total industry/market sales

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Market share is a *relative* measure, not absolute. It answers the question: *"Out of the entire pie, what slice is ours?"* It can be expressed in **Volume (Units)** or **Value (Revenue)**. Both can yield different rankings and must be reported with the correct basis.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> **Plain English Analogy — The Local Bakery Story:**
> Imagine a town has **3 bakeries** (A, B, C). In one month, the total bread loaves sold in town = **1,000**.
> - Bakery A sold **300** loaves
> - Bakery B sold **500** loaves
> - Bakery C sold **200** loaves
>
> Bakery B's market share = 500/1000 × 100 = **50%**.
> 
> Just like dividing a real cake — the total cake size (T) is the **TAM (Total Addressable Market)**, and each bakery's slice is its market share. If Bakery A runs a 10% discount, next month the total pie might grow to 1,100, but its share still depends on how much of that *new* pie it grabs.

### 1.3 Two Critical Base Metrics (Must Memorize)

- **Unit/Volume Share**: Based on physical units sold. Sensitive to price-discrimination strategies.
- **Revenue/Value Share**: Based on actual revenue earned. Sensitive to product mix and pricing power.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two measures?** A premium brand may have **low unit share** but **high value share** (e.g., Apple in smartphones). A budget brand shows the opposite. KTU questions frequently test this distinction.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic of Market Share Estimation

Estimating market share in business analytics is not just plugging numbers into a formula. It follows a structured analytical pipeline:

1. **Define the Market Boundary (Universe):**
   - What products count? What geography? What customer segment?
   - *Example*: "Sales of smartphones in India" vs "Sales of 5G smartphones in South India."

2. **Identify the Total Sales (T):**
   - Aggregate sales of the entire defined market (Industry Sales).
   - *Sources*: Syndicated research reports, industry associations, government statistics.

3. **Capture the Firm's Sales ($S_i$):**
   - Internal sales data: invoices, POS, channel reports.

4. **Choose the Basis:** Revenue (Value) or Unit (Volume).

5. **Compute and Benchmark:**
   - Apply the formula. Compare against the **largest competitor's share** and **market leader (CR1)**.

6. **Track the Movement (ΔMS):**
   - Compare the firm's share over Time Period 1 vs Time Period 2.
   - $\Delta MS_t = MS_t - MS_{t-1}$

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- *Why define the market?* Because market share is meaningless without context. Google has ~92% of the *search engine* market but tiny share of the *global advertising* market.
- *Why track movement?* A firm with 10% market share today is meaningless if last year it was 15%. **Trend is king.**

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Unit | Remarks |
|---|---------|----------------------|------|---------|
| 1 | **Absolute Market Share (AMS)** | $AMS_i = \dfrac{S_i}{T} \times 100$ | % | Standard textbook definition |
| 2 | **Relative Market Share (RMS)** | $RMS_i = \dfrac{S_i}{S_{L}}$ | Ratio (not %) | $S_L$ = sales of the **largest competitor** (not the leader of the whole industry in some definitions, but KTU uses the market leader) |
| 3 | **Served Market Share (SMS)** | $SMS_i = \dfrac{S_i}{T_{served}} \times 100$ | % | $T_{served}$ = sales in segments the firm **actually competes in** |
| 4 | **Market Concentration Ratio (CR_k)** | $CR_k = \sum_{i=1}^{k} MS_i$ | % | Sum of top *k* firms' shares |
| 5 | **Herfindahl-Hirschman Index (HHI)** | $HHI = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (MS_i)^2$ | Index (0–10,000) | Measures market concentration |
| 6 | **Share of Voice (SOV)** | $SOV = \dfrac{\text{Firm's Ad Spend}}{\text{Total Category Ad Spend}} \times 100$ | % | Marketing input metric |
| 7 | **Parfitt-Collins Loyalty Model (B)** | $B = \dfrac{r}{1 - r}$ where $r = \dfrac{C_r}{C_t}$ | Ratio | Repeat-buy loyalty coefficient |
| 8 | **Penetration Share** | $\dfrac{\text{Customers buying brand}}{\text{Customers in category}} \times 100$ | % | Customer-base measure |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The Relative Market Share (RMS) in the **BCG Matrix** definition uses the share of the *largest competitor*, NOT the market leader. This is a tested distinction. Both interpretations exist in literature; KTU follows the BCG convention: $RMS = MS_{firm} / MS_{largest\ competitor}$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering/Business Utility

- **Portfolio Strategy (BCG Matrix):** Firms classify products as **Stars, Cash Cows, Question Marks, Dogs** based on RMS and market growth.
- **Competitive Benchmarking:** Identifies whether a firm is a leader, challenger, follower, or nicher.
- **Marketing Budget Allocation:** Marketing spend is often set as a fixed ratio of SOV to SOV-Market-Share (i.e., share the wallet requires share the voice).
- **M&A Decisions:** Buyers assess a target's *served* and *absolute* share before acquisition pricing.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Parfitt-Collins Buyer Behavior Model

The **Parfitt-Collins Model** is the KTU-recommended analytical framework for estimating market share at the *customer-base* level, separating share into three multiplicative components.

**Core Identity:**

$$MS = \text{Penetration} \times \text{Buy Rate} \times \text{Average Quantity per Buyer (relative)}$$

Let:
- $C_t$ = Total customers in the category
- $C_b$ = Customers who buy brand B
- $C_r$ = Customers who buy brand B in **both** period $t$ and $t-1$ (loyal/repeating)
- $W_t$ = Total category purchases in period $t$

**Step 1 — Penetration ($P$):**

$$P = \frac{C_b}{C_t}$$

The fraction of category buyers who buy our brand at least once.

**Step 2 — Loyalty ($L$):**
Loyalty is defined as the share of brand purchases made by buyers who *also* bought it last period.

$$L = \frac{C_r}{C_b}$$

A buyer of brand B is "loyal" if they also bought B in the previous period.

**Step 3 — Define $r$ — the fraction of buyers who are loyal:**

$$r = \frac{C_r}{C_t}$$

**Step 4 — Express Brand Share:**

$$MS_B = \frac{S_B}{W_t} = \frac{C_b \times \text{avg. units per B buyer}}{C_t \times \text{avg. units per category buyer}}$$

Assuming equal average quantity (homogeneous category), this reduces to:

$$MS_B = \frac{C_b}{C_t} = P$$

But Parfitt-Collins decomposes $P$ into $r$ and a switching component:

$$P = r + (1 - r) \cdot \frac{C_b - C_r}{C_t - C_r}$$

**Step 5 — Introduce Loyalty Coefficient $B$:**

$$B = \frac{r}{1 - r}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of $B$:** $B$ is the **loyalty coefficient**. A $B > 1$ means the brand is gaining loyalty faster than losing it (growth mode). $B < 1$ indicates decay. $B = 1$ indicates stable, mature market share.

**Step 6 — Final Market Share Identity:**

$$MS_{t} = MS_{t-1} \times \left[ B \cdot \frac{P_t}{P_{t-1}} + (1 - B) \cdot \frac{L_t}{L_{t-1}} \right]$$

This is the **forward-projection formula** for market share using loyalty dynamics.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Problem)

**Problem (Parfitt-Collins Application):**

A firm had the following data last year:
- Category buyers: $C_t = 1{,}000$
- Brand buyers: $C_b = 200$
- Loyal buyers (bought brand last year AND this year): $C_r = 80$
- Brand sales: $S_B = 400$ units
- Total category sales: $W_t = 2{,}000$ units

This year:
- Category buyers: $C_t = 1{,}100$
- Brand buyers: $C_b = 240$
- Loyal buyers: $C_r = 110$

**Step-by-step solution:**

**Step A — Last Year Penetration:**
$$P_0 = \frac{200}{1{,}000} = 0.20 = 20\%$$

**Step B — Last Year Market Share:**
$$MS_0 = \frac{400}{2{,}000} = 0.20 = 20\%$$

**Step C — Loyalty Fraction $r$ this year:**
$$r_1 = \frac{110}{1{,}100} = 0.10$$

**Step D — This Year Penetration:**
$$P_1 = \frac{240}{1{,}100} \approx 0.2182 = 21.82\%$$

**Step E — Loyalty Coefficient $B$:**
$$B = \frac{r_1}{1 - r_1} = \frac{0.10}{0.90} \approx 0.1111$$

**Step F — This Year Loyalty:**
$$L_1 = \frac{C_r}{C_b} = \frac{110}{240} \approx 0.4583 = 45.83\%$$

**Step G — Apply Forward Projection (assuming stable loyalty this year vs last year, $L_0 = L_1$):**
$$MS_1 = MS_0 \times \left[ B \cdot \frac{P_1}{P_0} + (1 - B) \cdot 1 \right]$$
$$MS_1 = 0.20 \times \left[ 0.1111 \times \frac{0.2182}{0.20} + 0.8889 \right]$$
$$MS_1 = 0.20 \times \left[ 0.1111 \times 1.091 + 0.8889 \right]$$
$$MS_1 = 0.20 \times \left[ 0.1212 + 0.8889 \right]$$
$$MS_1 = 0.20 \times 1.0101 \approx 0.2020 = 20.20\%$$

**Interpretation:** Market share grew slightly from 20.00% to **~20.20%**. The loyalty coefficient $B < 1$ shows the brand is in a **mature/stable** phase with marginal growth.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Market Share Estimation Methods

| Method | Data Requirement | Output Type | Best Use Case | KTU Weightage |
|--------|------------------|-------------|---------------|---------------|
| **Direct Top-Down** | Industry sales + firm sales | Absolute % | Mature, syndicated data | High |
| **Bottom-Up Build** | Customer panel data | Penetration × Loyalty | FMCG, packaged goods | High |
| **Markov Chain / Brand Switching** | Transition matrix (p_ij) | Steady-state share | Telecom, subscription services | Medium |
| **Parfitt-Collins** | $C_t, C_b, C_r$ | Loyalty-augmented share | Repeat-purchase categories | High |
| **SOV-to-Share Model** | Ad spend data | Marketing-driven share | Consumer durables | Medium |
| **Regression / Econometric** | Macro + marketing variables | Sensitivity of share | Strategic forecasting | Low-Medium |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Market Share Decomposition — The Three Pillars (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start[Market Share Estimation] --> Def[1. Define Market Boundary]
    Def --> Total[Total Industry Sales T]
    Total --> FirmSales[Firm Sales S_i]
    FirmSales --> Basis{Choose Basis}
    Basis -->|Revenue| RevShare[Value Share percent]
    Basis -->|Units| VolShare[Volume Share percent]
    RevShare --> Comp[Compare vs Competitors]
    VolShare --> Comp
    Comp --> Trend[Track Delta MS over time]
    Trend --> Strat[Strategic Action: BCG / 4Ps]
```

### 4.2 Parfitt-Collins Buyer Flow (Mermaid Process Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CategoryPop[Category Population C_t]
        Buyers[Brand Buyers C_b]
        NonBuyers[Non-Buyers]
    end
    Buyers --> Split{Loyal vs Switcher}
    Split -->|C_r| Loyal[Loyal Buyers C_r]
    Split -->|C_b minus C_r| Switch[Switching Buyers]
    Loyal --> Retention[Retained Purchases]
    Switch --> NewTry[New Trial Purchases]
    Retention --> BrandShare[Brand Sales S_B]
    NewTry --> BrandShare
    BrandShare --> MS[Market Share MS]
    CategoryPop --> TotalSales[Category Sales W_t]
    TotalSales --> MS
```

### 4.3 Market Share Estimation — Strategic Decision Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Acquire Data] --> B{Repeat Purchase Category?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Apply Parfitt-Collins Model]
    B -- No --> D[Apply Direct Top-Down Method]
    C --> E[Compute Penetration P]
    C --> F[Compute Loyalty Coefficient B]
    E --> G[Forecast MS_t+1]
    F --> G
    D --> H[Compute Absolute MS]
    H --> I[Compute Relative MS vs Top Competitor]
    G --> J[Output: Strategic Recommendation]
    I --> J
    J --> K{Quality of MS greater than 20 percent?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Star / Cash Cow Strategy]
    K -- No --> M[Niche or Discontinue Strategy]
```

### 4.4 BCG Matrix — Market Share Decision Grid

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BCG[BCG Portfolio Grid]
        direction TB
        Stars[Stars: High MS + High Growth]
        Cash[Cash Cows: High MS + Low Growth]
        QMark[Question Marks: Low MS + High Growth]
        Dogs[Dogs: Low MS + Low Growth]
    end
    Stars --> S1[Action: Invest aggressively]
    Cash --> S2[Action: Harvest cash flow]
    QMark --> S3[Action: Selective growth or divest]
    Dogs --> S4[Action: Phased exit]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
**Distinguish between Absolute Market Share and Relative Market Share. Why is Relative Market Share preferred in the BCG Matrix framework?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Absolute Market Share (1 mark):** Firm's sales as a percentage of total industry/market sales, $AMS = S_i / T \times 100$. It measures the firm's slice of the total pie.
- **Relative Market Share (1 mark):** Firm's sales as a ratio of the largest competitor's sales, $RMS = S_i / S_L$. It benchmarks the firm against the *closest rival*.
- **Why preferred in BCG (1 mark):** A firm could be small in an absolute sense (e.g., 5% of a massive market) yet still dominate its competitive set. RMS normalizes the competitive position, making it a better indicator of *competitive strength* rather than mere market size.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
**Explain the concept of "Served Market Share." In what situation does it differ significantly from Absolute Market Share?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Served Market Share is the firm's sales expressed as a percentage of the total sales in the *market segments that the firm actually serves* (i.e., its product-line-geography-customer niche), excluding segments the firm does not compete in.
- **Situation of difference (1.5 marks):** When a firm operates in a *narrow* niche of a much larger category. Example: A luxury car maker may have only **3% Absolute Share** of the global car market, but **40% Served Share** within the luxury sedan segment. The two numbers tell very different strategic stories.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

**Q3. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Define Market Share. Explain any **four** different bases (dimensions) on which market share can be measured, with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The market for a product category has total industry sales of **₹500 Crore**. The top four firms report the following revenues:

| Firm | Revenue (₹ Crore) |
|------|-------------------|
| A | 200 |
| B | 120 |
| C | 80 |
| D | 50 |
| Others | 50 |

Compute: (i) Absolute Market Share of each top firm, (ii) CR3 (Three-firm concentration ratio), (iii) HHI, (iv) Relative Market Share of Firm A. Interpret the market structure. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Four Bases of Market Share Measurement (7 marks):**

1. **Overall (Absolute) Market Share** — Firm's sales as % of total industry sales. *Example: Maruti's 40% of Indian passenger car sales.*
2. **Served Market Share** — Firm's sales as % of sales in segments it actually serves. *Example: Apple's share within the premium smartphone segment.*
3. **Relative Market Share (BCG)** — Firm's sales as a ratio of the *largest competitor's* sales. *Example: Colgate vs Pepsodent.*
4. **Value Share vs Volume Share** — Revenue-based vs unit-based. *Example: A premium brand may have 5% volume share but 12% value share.*

*(Bonus 5th basis: Share of Voice — advertising share; Penetration Share — customer count.)*

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**(i) Absolute Market Share of top firms:**

$$MS_A = \frac{200}{500} \times 100 = 40\%$$

$$MS_B = \frac{120}{500} \times 100 = 24\%$$

$$MS_C = \frac{80}{500} \times 100 = 16\%$$

$$MS_D = \frac{50}{500} \times 100 = 10\%$$

**(ii) Three-Firm Concentration Ratio ($CR_3$):**

$$CR_3 = MS_A + MS_B + MS_C = 40 + 24 + 16 = 80\%$$

**(iii) Herfindahl-Hirschman Index (HHI):**

$$HHI = 40^2 + 24^2 + 16^2 + 10^2 + 10^2$$

$$HHI = 1600 + 576 + 256 + 100 + 100 = 2632$$

**Rule of thumb:** HHI < 1500 = competitive, 1500–2500 = moderately concentrated, > 2500 = highly concentrated. Here HHI = 2632 → **Highly Concentrated Market** (oligopoly with a dominant leader).

**(iv) Relative Market Share of Firm A:**

$$RMS_A = \frac{S_A}{S_B} = \frac{200}{120} = 1.67$$

Firm A is 1.67× the size of its closest rival.

**Interpretation:** The market is a **tight oligopoly** dominated by Firm A. The top 3 firms control 80% of sales, and a single firm (A) alone controls 40%, indicating **barriers to entry, brand loyalty, and pricing power**.

---

---

**Q4. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — Question B (Alternative Choice, 14 Marks)**

**(a)** What is the **Parfitt-Collins Model**? Explain its three components — Penetration, Loyalty, and Loyalty Coefficient $B$. Derive the relationship between Market Share and these components. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A brand has the following data: $C_t = 2{,}000$, $C_b = 400$, $C_r = 100$, $W_t = 5{,}000$ units, $S_B = 800$ units. Compute the Penetration, Market Share, Loyalty fraction $r$, and Loyalty Coefficient $B$. Comment on the brand's growth status based on $B$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Parfitt-Collins Model (7 marks):**

The Parfitt-Collins Model, developed in 1968, decomposes market share into **Penetration** and **Loyalty** to enable forward-looking share forecasting in repeat-purchase categories (e.g., FMCG, beverages, telecom).

**Three Components:**

1. **Penetration ($P$):** The fraction of category buyers who buy the brand at least once. $P = C_b / C_t$.
2. **Loyalty ($L$):** The fraction of brand buyers who *re-purchased* the brand in the next period. $L = C_r / C_b$.
3. **Loyalty Coefficient ($B$):** Defined as $B = r / (1 - r)$ where $r = C_r / C_t$.

**Derivation of relationship:**

The brand's market share equals its penetration (assuming equal average quantity per buyer):

$$MS = P = \frac{C_b}{C_t}$$

Splitting $C_b$ into loyal ($C_r$) and switcher ($C_b - C_r$):

$$MS = \frac{C_r}{C_t} + \frac{C_b - C_r}{C_t}$$

$$MS = r + (1 - r) \cdot \frac{C_b - C_r}{C_t - C_r}$$

This expression leads to the forward-projection identity:

$$MS_t = MS_{t-1} \cdot \left[ B \cdot \frac{P_t}{P_{t-1}} + (1 - B) \cdot \frac{L_t}{L_{t-1}} \right]$$

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $C_t = 2{,}000$
- $C_b = 400$
- $C_r = 100$
- $W_t = 5{,}000$ units
- $S_B = 800$ units

**Step 1 — Penetration:**

$$P = \frac{C_b}{C_t} = \frac{400}{2{,}000} = 0.20 = 20\%$$

**Step 2 — Market Share:**

$$MS = \frac{S_B}{W_t} = \frac{800}{5{,}000} = 0.16 = 16\%$$

**Step 3 — Loyalty Fraction $r$:**

$$r = \frac{C_r}{C_t} = \frac{100}{2{,}000} = 0.05$$

**Step 4 — Loyalty Coefficient $B$:**

$$B = \frac{r}{1 - r} = \frac{0.05}{0.95} \approx 0.0526$$

**Step 5 — Loyalty $L$:**

$$L = \frac{C_r}{C_b} = \frac{100}{400} = 0.25 = 25\%$$

**Comment on Growth Status:**

Since $B \approx 0.053 < 1$, the brand is in a **mature, low-growth phase**. The brand retains only 5% of category buyers as loyalists. The Penetration (20%) is healthy, but the *loyalty conversion* is weak — meaning 80% of category buyers do NOT choose this brand, and among those who do, only 25% are repeat buyers. **Strategic recommendation: invest in retention, loyalty programs, and product satisfaction rather than trial/awareness campaigns.**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing RMS definitions** (1 mark lost frequently): In BCG context, RMS = *Firm's MS / Largest Competitor's MS*, NOT the industry leader's share. Read the question carefully.
> 2. **Forgetting units in HHI** (1 mark lost): HHI = sum of squared percentage shares (0–10,000 scale). Some students use decimal shares and report wrong magnitudes.
> 3. **Wrong CR formula** (1 mark lost): CR3 = sum of *top 3 firms' shares*, NOT top 3 customers' shares.
> 4. **Parfitt-Collins sign error in $B$ formula** (2 marks lost): $B = r / (1 - r)$, NOT $(1 - r)/r$. The denominator must always be $(1 - r)$.
> 5. **Served vs Absolute confusion** (1 mark lost): When a firm is a niche player, ALWAYS use Served Share to avoid understating competitive position.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Market Share Estimation**

- **Definition:** $MS = S_i / T \times 100$ — proportion of firm's sales in total category sales.
- **Two Bases:** Unit/Volume Share vs Revenue/Value Share — always specify.
- **Absolute Market Share (AMS):** vs Total Industry — used for general competitive positioning.
- **Relative Market Share (RMS):** vs Largest Competitor — used in BCG Matrix.
- **Served Market Share (SMS):** vs Served Segment — used for niche players.
- **Penetration Share:** based on customer counts, not units.
- **Concentration Metrics:** $CR_k$ (simple sum) and **HHI** (sum of squared shares).
- **HHI Interpretation:** <1500 competitive, 1500–2500 moderate, >2500 highly concentrated.
- **Parfitt-Collins Model:** Decomposes share into Penetration × Loyalty.
  - $P = C_b / C_t$
  - $L = C_r / C_b$
  - $r = C_r / C_t$
  - $B = r / (1 - r)$
  - $B > 1$ = growth, $B = 1$ = stable, $B < 1$ = decline.
- **Forward Projection Formula:** $MS_t = MS_{t-1} \cdot \left[ B \cdot \dfrac{P_t}{P_{t-1}} + (1 - B) \cdot \dfrac{L_t}{L_{t-1}} \right]$
- **Strategic Linkage:** MS feeds into **BCG Matrix** (Stars, Cash Cows, Question Marks, Dogs).
- **Always define the market** before calculating share — boundaries matter.
- **Trend (ΔMS)** is more actionable than the static level.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Recovery Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT522 - BUSINESS ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Recovery Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Definition

**Recovery Management** in the context of Business Analytics and Database Systems refers to the set of mechanisms, protocols, and algorithms employed to restore a database (or any analytical data store) to a **consistent and correct state** following an unexpected failure. According to the ACID properties framework, Recovery Management is the operational pillar that guarantees the **Durability** and **Atomicity** of transactions even in the presence of system crashes, media failures, transaction errors, or natural disasters.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1)**
> Recovery Management ensures that despite any malfunction, the database never enters an *inconsistent* state, and all committed transactions are permanently reflected while uncommitted (aborted) transactions leave no trace.

## Key Terminology (Standard KTU Metrics)

- **Transaction Failure**: Logical or program error causing the transaction to abort.
- **System Crash**: Hardware/software malfunction (e.g., power failure) causing loss of volatile memory (RAM).
- **Media Failure**: Disk head crash or storage corruption leading to loss of non-volatile (secondary) memory.
- **Checkpoint**: A synchronization point where the database logs are flushed to disk.
- **Log**: A chronological record of all transactions and their database modifications.

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Bank Ledger & CCTV System**
> Imagine a bank teller handling cash transactions. Every time money moves in or out of an account, the teller:
> 1. Writes a note in a special **"CCTV Logbook"** (the system log).
> 2. Updates the customer's **passbook** (the database) in real-time.
>
> Now, suppose the bank's computer crashes mid-transaction. When the system reboots, how does the manager know:
> - Which transactions were *fully completed* and must be kept?
> - Which transactions were *half-done* and must be **rolled back** to their initial state?
>
> The manager simply **rewinds the CCTV Logbook** from the last verified checkpoint to determine exactly what happened. If the logbook says "Commit," the transaction is replayed. If it says "Abort" or the record is incomplete, the changes are undone. **This "rewind-and-verify" process is Recovery Management.**

## Why Recovery Management Matters in Business Analytics

In a Business Analytics environment, data drives *strategic decisions*. A corrupted dataset (e.g., a half-loaded sales warehouse) can lead to:
- **Misleading KPIs** that misguide executives.
- **Financial losses** due to inconsistent inventory or transaction data.
- **Regulatory non-compliance** (e.g., GDPR, SOX) where data integrity is mandatory.

Hence, Recovery Management is the **insurance policy** of every analytical data pipeline.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Failures (Classification Framework)

Recovery Management is triggered by one of the following failure categories:

| **Failure Type** | **Volatile Memory Affected?** | **Non-Volatile Affected?** | **Recovery Strategy** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Transaction Failure (Logical) | No | No | Rollback via UNDO log |
