<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

This section is structured to mirror the **KTU 2024 ESE pattern**: Part A (3 marks), Part B (14 marks with internal choice), and a rapid-revision checklist.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]`
**Q: Define the importance of Business Analytics. List any four dimensions of its importance.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Business Analytics importance refers to the strategic, operational, financial, and customer value derived from data-driven decision-making. The four dimensions of importance are:

1. **Evidence-based decision making** — replaces intuition with statistical evidence.
2. **Competitive advantage** — enables dynamic pricing and faster market response.
3. **Operational efficiency** — optimizes supply chain and reduces waste.
4. **Customer personalization** — tailors offerings and improves customer lifetime value.

> **Marking Note:** 1 mark for the definition; ½ mark each for the four dimensions; ½ mark reserved for the closing strategic statement.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]`
**Q: "Data is the new oil." Critically comment on this statement in the context of Business Analytics importance.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The phrase "Data is the new oil" highlights that raw data, like crude oil, must be **refined** through analytics to extract value. Just as oil fuels economies, refined data fuels decisions, products, and revenue. The comparison underscores the importance of Business Analytics in **transforming inert data into actionable insights**. However, unlike oil, data is **non-depleting** and grows in value when shared and recombined — making analytics an even more important enabler in the digital economy.

> **Marking Note:** 1 mark for the analogy explanation; 1 mark for the importance linkage; 1 mark for the contrasting/critical insight.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each** (typically part (a) and part (b)). Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **seven dimensions of importance** of Business Analytics in modern enterprises. Support your answer with at least **two real-world corporate examples**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, illustrate how the **four types of analytics** (descriptive, diagnostic, predictive, prescriptive) collectively contribute to the **strategic importance** of Business Analytics. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part A(a) [7 Marks]:**

The seven dimensions of importance are:

1. **Evidence-based decision making:** Analytics replaces intuition with statistically validated insights. *Example:* Google runs over **1,000 A/B tests** annually to refine its search algorithms.
2. **Competitive advantage:** Real-time pricing models allow firms to outmaneuver rivals. *Example:* **Uber's surge pricing** algorithm adjusts fares based on demand-supply in real time.
3. **Operational efficiency:** Predictive maintenance in manufacturing reduces downtime. *Example:* **Siemens** uses sensor analytics to predict machine failures before they occur.
4. **Customer personalization:** Recommendation engines drive sales. *Example:* **Amazon's recommendation engine** drives **35% of total revenue**.
5. **Risk and fraud management:** Anomaly detection models safeguard transactions. *Example:* **PayPal** uses machine learning to flag fraudulent transactions within milliseconds.
6. **Innovation and new revenue streams:** Data itself becomes a product. *Example:* **Bloomberg Terminal** monetizes financial datasets.
7. **Real-time agility:** Streaming analytics enables rapid response. *Example:* **Coca-Cola's social media dashboards** track viral trends within minutes.

#### **Model Solution — Part A(b) [7 Marks]:**

The block diagram (refer to Section 4.2) shows the four analytics layers feeding into business value:

- **Descriptive Analytics** answers "What happened?" → operational dashboards.
- **Diagnostic Analytics** answers "Why did it happen?" → root cause insights.
- **Predictive Analytics** answers "What will happen?" → forecasting models.
- **Prescriptive Analytics** answers "What should we do?" → optimization recommendations.

Collectively, these four layers create a **closed-loop decision system** that drives cost reduction, revenue uplift, risk mitigation, and strategic agility — the four pillars of analytics importance.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Part (b):** Students frequently draw the four analytics types as **independent boxes** without showing how they **cascade** into business value. Marks are lost if the diagram does not depict the **output → outcome → value** chain. Always include an arrow connecting analytics outputs to **quantified business outcomes** (revenue, cost, risk, agility).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – 2024 Model — Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** Discuss the **strategic importance of Business Analytics** in supporting **evidence-based decision making** and **competitive advantage**. Cite **two industry examples** in your answer. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** "Without analytics, modern enterprises are flying blind." Justify this statement with reference to **risk management, customer personalization, and operational efficiency**. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part B(a) [7 Marks]:**

**Evidence-based decision making** is the most fundamental importance of Business Analytics. Senior managers historically relied on intuition, which is biased and inconsistent. Analytics introduces **hypothesis testing, regression analysis, and A/B testing** to validate business decisions before committing resources. This reduces the cost of wrong decisions and accelerates the learning cycle.

**Competitive advantage** emerges from the speed and precision of analytical insights. Firms that adopt analytics can:
- React to market changes in **hours, not months**.
- Personalize offerings at an individual level.
- Optimize pricing dynamically based on demand elasticity.

*Industry Examples:*
- **Netflix** uses viewing analytics to decide which original series to produce — saving over **$1 billion** annually in content investment risk.
- **Walmart** uses predictive analytics for inventory replenishment, achieving a **99% in-stock rate** and outperforming competitors during seasonal demand spikes.

#### **Model Solution — Part B(b) [7 Marks]:**

The statement is justified as follows:

- **Risk Management:** Analytics models such as credit scoring (FICO), fraud detection (PayPal), and cyber-threat detection (FireEye) protect enterprises from catastrophic losses. Without analytics, risk goes undetected until it materializes.
- **Customer Personalization:** Modern customers expect tailored experiences. Amazon's recommendation engine, Spotify's "Discover Weekly," and Starbucks' personalized offers demonstrate that analytics is **no longer optional** — it is the baseline expectation.
- **Operational Efficiency:** Predictive maintenance (Siemens), supply chain optimization (Walmart), and workforce analytics (Accenture) demonstrate that analytics directly converts data into measurable cost savings.

Without analytics, enterprises operate on **stale data, delayed reports, and reactive strategies** — effectively "flying blind" in a data-rich, hyper-competitive market.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Part (a):** A common mistake is to **list examples without quantifying them**. A statement like "Netflix uses analytics" earns only **½ mark**, whereas "Netflix's analytics-driven content decisions save **$1 billion annually**" earns **1.5 marks**. Always quantify the importance with metrics.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Revision — Topic: Importance of Business Analytics**

- **Definition (Must Memorize):** Business Analytics importance = the strategic, operational, financial, and customer value derived from data-driven decision-making.
- **Seven Dimensions (Enumerate in Any Order):**
  1. Evidence-based decision making
  2. Competitive advantage
  3. Operational efficiency
  4. Customer personalization
  5. Risk and fraud management
  6. Innovation and new revenue streams
  7. Real-time agility
- **Four Pillars of Business Value:** Cost reduction, revenue uplift, risk mitigation, strategic agility.
- **Four Analytics Types (Cascade):** Descriptive → Diagnostic → Predictive → Prescriptive.
- **Must-Cite Companies (for ESE examples):** Amazon, Netflix, Uber, Walmart, PayPal, Google, Siemens, Spotify.
- **Must-Cite Metrics:** ROI, CLV, NPS, Churn Rate, Conversion Rate, A/B Test Uplift.
- **Strategic Insight to Include:** Data is the "new oil" — but **refined** by analytics into actionable insights.
- **Diagram to Draw:** A **four-layer block diagram** showing analytics types cascading into business value.
- **Quantitative Close (for 14-mark answers):** Always end with a **quantified outcome** (e.g., ROI, % uplift, ₹ savings).
- **Avoid These Traps:** Generic statements without examples, no diagrams, no quantification, mixing up the four analytics types.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU 2024 Tip:** The "Importance" topic is **high-yield** and appears in nearly every ESE cycle. Mastering the seven dimensions with **two corporate examples each** and the four-pillar value framework guarantees **full marks** in both Part A and Part B.

topics.title: Components
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT522 - BUSINESS ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Components

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Business Analytics (BA)**, the term **"Components"** refers to the **fundamental, interdependent building blocks** that together constitute a complete, functional analytics ecosystem within an organization. These components collectively transform raw data into actionable business insights, enabling data-driven decision-making at strategic, tactical, and operational levels.

As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PEADT522**, the core components of Business Analytics are broadly classified into **six pillars**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Construct)**
> The components of Business Analytics comprise the **Data Layer, Technology Infrastructure, Analytical Techniques (Models & Algorithms), People & Expertise, Business Processes, and the Insight/Visualization Layer**. Each component is a necessary but not sufficient condition; only their integration delivers sustainable analytical value.

The formal taxonomy can be represented as:

$$
C_{BA} = \{ D, T, M, P, Pr, V \}
$$

where:
- $D$ = **Data** (raw, processed, big, small, structured, unstructured)
- $T$ = **Technology** (hardware, software, platforms, cloud infrastructure)
- $M$ = **Models/Methods** (statistical, ML, optimization, simulation models)
- $P$ = **People** (data scientists, analysts, domain experts, leadership)
- $Pr$ = **Process** (CRISP-DM, SEMMA, governance, lifecycle management)
- $V$ = **Visualization/Insight Delivery** (dashboards, reports, narratives)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hospital Analogy"

Imagine a modern hospital treating patients. The hospital does not run on doctors alone. It needs:

| Hospital Element | Business Analytics Equivalent | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|
| Patients & medical records | **Data** | Raw input — symptoms, history, lab results |
| MRI machines, labs, computers | **Technology** | Tools that collect and process |
| Diagnostic procedures, treatment protocols | **Models & Methods** | Algorithms that derive meaning |
| Doctors, nurses, lab technicians | **People** | Skilled human expertise |
| Hospital workflow (admit → diagnose → treat → discharge) | **Process** | Structured sequence of operations |
| Discharge summary, patient advice | **Visualization/Insight** | Final output to decision-makers (patient/family) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Hook for Students:** Just as a hospital cannot treat patients with only MRI machines (no doctors, no records, no workflow), an organization cannot perform analytics with only software or only data scientists. **All six components must function in harmony.** This is the central thesis of Module 2.

## 1.3 Why "Components" is a Standalone Module Topic

The KTU curriculum isolates this topic because organizations routinely fail not due to lack of data or lack of money, but due to **component imbalance** — e.g., buying expensive tools ($T$) without skilled people ($P$), or having talented data scientists but no process governance ($Pr$). The "Components" framework gives the future manager a **diagnostic lens** to evaluate any analytics initiative.

## 1.4 Core Terminology (Bolded Glossary)

- **Data**: A collection of facts, observations, or values that can be processed by a computer.
- **Structured Data**: Data organized in a defined schema (e.g., RDBMS tables). Typically **20%** of enterprise data.
- **Unstructured Data**: Data without a predefined model (text, images, video). Typically **80%** of enterprise data.
- **ETL**: **Extract, Transform, Load** — the data pipeline process.
- **CRISP-DM**: **Cross-Industry Standard Process for Data Mining** — the dominant analytics lifecycle.
- **KPI**: **Key Performance Indicator** — measurable value showing effectiveness.
- **Data Lake vs Data Warehouse**: A Data Lake stores raw data in native format; a Data Warehouse stores cleaned, structured, query-optimized data.
- **Self-Service Analytics**: Empowering business users (non-coders) to perform their own analysis.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Components — Exhaustive Theoretical Breakdown

### Component 1: The DATA Layer ($D$)

Data is the **lifeblood** of analytics. Without data, no model has substance. The data component is sub-divided into:

- **Internal Data**: Generated by the organization (sales transactions, CRM logs, ERP records, IoT sensor streams).
- **External Data**: Acquired from third parties (social media feeds, government open data, market research, weather APIs).
- **Big Data Characteristics (The 5 V's)**: The standard metric framework, required by the KTU 2024 scheme:
  - **Volume** — Terabytes to Petabytes scale.
  - **Velocity** — Speed of generation (real-time streams vs. batch).
  - **Variety** — Structured, semi-structured, unstructured.
  - **Veracity** — Trustworthiness, data quality, lineage.
  - **Value** — Business worth extracted from data.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** In the 2024 scheme, the **5 V's of Big Data** is a confirmed high-weightage topic. Students must memorize all five Vs with one-line definitions.

### Component 2: The TECHNOLOGY Layer ($T$)

The technology stack provides the computational engine. It is layered as:

- **Infrastructure Layer**: Servers, storage, networking — on-premise, cloud (AWS, Azure, GCP), or hybrid.
- **Data Storage Layer**: RDBMS (MySQL, PostgreSQL), NoSQL (MongoDB, Cassandra), Data Lakes (HDFS, S3), Data Warehouses (Snowflake, Redshift).
- **Processing Layer**: Hadoop, Spark, Flink for distributed processing.
- **Analytics Layer**: Python (Pandas, Scikit-learn), R, SAS, SPSS, specialized tools (Tableau, Power BI, Qlik).
- **Deployment Layer**: Docker, Kubernetes, MLflow for model productionization.

### Component 3: The MODELS & METHODS Layer ($M$)

Models are the **mathematical heart** of analytics. The hierarchy is:

- **Descriptive Models**: "What happened?" — Mean, median, mode, variance, clustering, association rules.
- **Predictive Models**: "What will happen?" — Linear regression, logistic regression, decision trees, neural networks, time-series forecasting (ARIMA, Exponential Smoothing).
- **Prescriptive Models**: "What should we do?" — Linear programming, integer programming, simulation, reinforcement learning.

### Component 4: The PEOPLE Layer ($P$)

The human capital component includes:

- **Data Engineers**: Build and maintain data pipelines.
- **Data Scientists**: Build predictive and prescriptive models.
- **Data/Business Analysts**: Translate business questions into data questions, create dashboards.
- **Domain Experts / SMEs**: Provide business context.
- **CDO (Chief Data Officer) / Leadership**: Set analytics strategy and governance.
- **End Users / Decision Makers**: Consume the insights.

### Component 5: The PROCESS Layer ($Pr$)

Process ensures repeatability and governance. The dominant frameworks are:

- **CRISP-DM** (most widely adopted): Business Understanding → Data Understanding → Data Preparation → Modeling → Evaluation → Deployment.
- **SEMMA** (SAS Institute): Sample → Explore → Modify → Model → Assess.
- **KDD** (Knowledge Discovery in Databases): Selection → Preprocessing → Transformation → Data Mining → Interpretation.

### Component 6: The VISUALIZATION & INSIGHT Layer ($V$)

The final mile — where analysis becomes action. It includes:

- **Reports**: Static, periodic (daily/weekly/monthly).
- **Dashboards**: Interactive, real-time KPI monitoring.
- **Storytelling with Data**: Narrative-driven presentations.
- **Alerts & Notifications**: Triggered by thresholds (e.g., fraud detection).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Framework | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Components of BA | $C_{BA} = \{D, T, M, P, Pr, V\}$ | Six-pillar taxonomy |
| 2 | 5 V's of Big Data | $\{V_{vol}, V_{vel}, V_{var}, V_{ver}, V_{val}\}$ | Standard big data dimensions |
| 3 | Structured vs Unstructured | $S : U \approx 20 : 80$ | Enterprise data ratio (Gartner estimate) |
| 4 | Mean (Descriptive) | $\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$ | Central tendency measure |
| 5 | Variance | $\sigma^2 = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})^2$ | Spread measure |
| 6 | Standard Deviation | $\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^2}$ | Same unit as data |
| 7 | Linear Regression (Predictive) | $y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x + \varepsilon$ | Predictive model form |
| 8 | Logistic Regression | $P(y=1 \mid x) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(\beta_0 + \beta_1 x)}}$ | Classification model |
| 9 | Mean Squared Error (Model Eval.) | $MSE = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Predictive accuracy |
| 10 | R-Squared | $R^2 = 1 - \frac{SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}}$ | Variance explained |
| 11 | ROI of Analytics | $ROI = \frac{\text{Benefit} - \text{Cost}}{\text{Cost}} \times 100\%$ | Business value |
| 12 | ETL Pipeline Order | $E \rightarrow T \rightarrow L$ | Extract, Transform, Load sequence |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution Hint (KTU 2024):** Questions on "components" typically test the **5 V's of Big Data**, the **CRISP-DM phases**, and the **Data → Insight pipeline** — these three together account for nearly **60% of marks** in Module 2 according to past KTU university exam trends.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Business

In real engineering and business settings, the components framework is used as a **maturity assessment tool**. For example, a manufacturing firm may use this framework to audit itself:

- Do we have **Data** from IoT sensors on the shop floor? (D)
- Do we have **Technology** (cloud storage, edge computing)? (T)
- Do we have **Models** for predictive maintenance? (M)
- Do we have **People** trained in ML? (P)
- Do we have a **Process** (predictive maintenance SOP)? (Pr)
- Do we have **Visualization** (operator dashboards)? (V)

A "yes" to all six indicates **analytics maturity**; any "no" indicates the specific gap to be closed.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — How the Components Interact

The components do not exist in isolation. They form a **closed-loop value chain** as derived below:

$$
\text{Value Chain} = D \xrightarrow{\text{ETL}} T \xrightarrow{\text{Apply}} M \xrightarrow{\text{Insight}} V \xrightarrow{\text{Feedback}} Pr \xrightarrow{\text{Governs}} P \xrightarrow{\text{Operates}} D
$$

**Step-by-step logic of the value chain:**

- **Step 1 ($D \rightarrow T$):** Raw data is ingested into the technology stack via ETL pipelines.
- **Step 2 ($T \rightarrow M$):** Cleaned, stored data is fed into analytical models (regression, classification, clustering).
- **Step 3 ($M \rightarrow V$):** Model outputs (predictions, clusters, scores) are rendered into visual dashboards.
- **Step 4 ($V \rightarrow Pr$):** Business users consume the visualization and trigger business processes (e.g., restock, campaign launch).
- **Step 5 ($Pr \rightarrow P$):** Process governance defines roles, data quality standards, and SLAs for the people.
- **Step 6 ($P \rightarrow D$):** Skilled people design what data to collect next, closing the loop.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Data-to-Decision Pipeline

A rigorous derivation of how raw data becomes a decision, using a simple business example:

**Given:** A retail store has $n = 1000$ transactions, each with revenue $x_i$ (in ₹).

**Step 1: Compute the Mean Revenue**

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i
$$

**Step 2: Compute Variance (spread around the mean)**

$$
\sigma^2 = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \bar{x})^2
$$

**Step 3: Compute the Coefficient of Variation (normalized spread)**

$$
CV = \frac{\sigma}{\bar{x}} \times 100\%
$$

This $CV$ is the **business metric** that tells the manager: "Revenue is fluctuating by $CV$% around the average." A high $CV$ means unstable revenue → triggers a **predictive** model to forecast next quarter.

**Step 4: Predictive Model Fit (Linear Regression)**

$$
\hat{y} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x
$$

where $\hat{y}$ is predicted revenue, and the coefficients are derived via **Ordinary Least Squares (OLS)**:

$$
\beta_1 = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})^2}, \quad \beta_0 = \bar{y} - \beta_1 \bar{x}
$$

**Step 5: Model Quality (R-Squared)**

$$
R^2 = 1 - \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \bar{y})^2}
$$

**Step 6: Decision Trigger**

$$
\text{If } R^2 \geq 0.75, \text{ then model is reliable} \Rightarrow \text{Use } \hat{y} \text{ for inventory planning}
$$

This end-to-end derivation demonstrates the **seamless integration of all six components**: $D$ (transaction data) → $T$ (Excel/Python) → $M$ (regression) → $V$ (dashboard) → $Pr$ (inventory SOP) → $P$ (store manager's action).

## 3.3 Component Maturity Score (Analytical Metric)

A useful analytical construct for KTU exams — the **Analytics Maturity Score (AMS)**:

$$
AMS = \frac{1}{6} \sum_{i=1}^{6} s_i, \quad s_i \in \{0, 1, 2, 3\}
$$

where $s_i$ is the maturity score (0 = absent, 1 = ad-hoc, 2 = defined, 3 = optimized) for each of the 6 components $i \in \{D, T, M, P, Pr, V\}$.

- $AMS \in [0, 0.5]$ → **Beginner** (analytics is reactive)
- $AMS \in (0.5, 1.5]$ → **Developing** (some ad-hoc analytics)
- $AMS \in (1.5, 2.5]$ → **Advanced** (data-driven culture forming)
- $AMS \in (2.5, 3.0]$ → **Optimized** (analytics is a competitive advantage)

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** This AMS framework is a strong self-scoring answer for "Evaluate the analytics maturity of an organization" — a 14-mark question.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

A company evaluates its analytics maturity. The CEO scores each component out of 3:
- Data: $s_D = 2$ (defined)
- Technology: $s_T = 1$ (ad-hoc)
- Models: $s_M = 2$ (defined)
- People: $s_P = 1$ (ad-hoc)
- Process: $s_{Pr} = 0$ (absent)
- Visualization: $s_V = 2$ (defined)

Compute the AMS:

$$
AMS = \frac{2 + 1 + 2 + 1 + 0 + 2}{6} = \frac{8}{6} = 1.33
$$

Since $1.33 \in (0.5, 1.5]$, the company is at the **"Developing"** maturity stage. The diagnosis: invest first in **Process ($Pr$)** and **People ($P$)** — the weakest pillars.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Six Components of Business Analytics — Hub-and-Spoke Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the six components of Business Analytics as a central hub (Decision/Insight) surrounded by the six supporting pillars, with explicit interconnections showing that this is **not a linear chain but an integrated ecosystem**.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Hub["BUSINESS DECISION AND INSIGHT"]:::hub

    D["COMPONENT 1: DATA LAYER\nInternal Data, External Data, Big Data 5Vs"]:::compD
    T["COMPONENT 2: TECHNOLOGY LAYER\nInfrastructure, Storage, Processing, Tools"]:::compT
    M["COMPONENT 3: MODELS AND METHODS\nDescriptive, Predictive, Prescriptive"]:::compM
    P["COMPONENT 4: PEOPLE LAYER\nEngineers, Scientists, Analysts, Leaders"]:::compP
    Pr["COMPONENT 5: PROCESS LAYER\nCRISP-DM, SEMMA, KDD, Governance"]:::compPr
    V["COMPONENT 6: VISUALIZATION LAYER\nReports, Dashboards, Storytelling"]:::compV

    D --> Hub
    T --> Hub
    M --> Hub
    P --> Hub
    Pr --> Hub
    V --> Hub

    Hub -.Feedback.-> D
    Hub -.Feedback.-> Pr

    D <--> T
    T <--> M
    M <--> V
    P <--> Pr
    Pr --> M

    classDef hub fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef compD fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,color:#000
    classDef compT fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000
    classDef compM fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
    classDef compP fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A,color:#000
    classDef compPr fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#880E4F,color:#000
    classDef compV fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#006064,color:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The solid arrows show **direct contribution** to business decision-making. The dotted feedback arrows show that the decision itself generates new data and new process requirements. The cross-arrows (D↔T, T↔M, etc.) show **horizontal interdependence** — the components cannot function in silos.

## 4.2 CRISP-DM Process Flow — Block-Level Functional Architecture

Since CRISP-DM is a six-phase circular lifecycle, the following block diagram shows how the **Process Component ($Pr$)** integrates with the other five components at each phase.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["PHASE 1: BUSINESS UNDERSTANDING\nComponent P drives this phase"]:::phase1
    P2["PHASE 2: DATA UNDERSTANDING\nComponent D is the primary focus"]:::phase2
    P3["PHASE 3: DATA PREPARATION\nComponent T and Component P collaborate"]:::phase3
    P4["PHASE 4: MODELING\nComponent M is the primary focus"]:::phase4
    P5["PHASE 5: EVALUATION\nComponent P and Component M evaluate"]:::phase5
    P6["PHASE 6: DEPLOYMENT\nComponent V delivers the insight"]:::phase6

    P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5 --> P6
    P6 -.Revisit.-> P1

    classDef phase1 fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000
    classDef phase2 fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
    classDef phase3 fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000
    classDef phase4 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    classDef phase5 fill:#B3E5FC,stroke:#01579B,color:#000
    classDef phase6 fill:#D1C4E9,stroke:#311B92,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Data-to-Insight Pipeline — Sequential Block Architecture

This is the **functional architecture flow** of how a single data point travels through the analytics stack from raw sensor to executive dashboard.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["DATA SOURCE\nPOS, CRM, IoT, Web Logs"]:::src
    B["INGEST LAYER\nKafka, APIs, Batch Loads"]:::ingest
    C["STORAGE LAYER\nData Lake, Data Warehouse"]:::store
    D["PROCESSING LAYER\nSpark, Python ETL"]:::proc
    E["ANALYTICS LAYER\nRegression, Classification, Clustering"]:::anal
    F["VISUALIZATION LAYER\nTableau, Power BI, Custom Dashboard"]:::viz
    G["DECISION MAKER\nManager, Executive, Operator"]:::dec

    A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G
    G -.Action.-> A

    classDef src fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C,color:#000
    classDef ingest fill:#FFE082,stroke:#FF6F00,color:#000
    classDef store fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    classDef proc fill:#80DEEA,stroke:#006064,color:#000
    classDef anal fill:#CE93D8,stroke:#4A148C,color:#000
    classDef viz fill:#90CAF9,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000
    classDef dec fill:#F48FB1,stroke:#880E4F,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these diagrams matter for exams:** KTU 2024 scheme Part B questions frequently ask students to "draw the architecture of an analytics system" or "explain the data-to-decision pipeline." These block diagrams are exam-ready and score full marks when labelled with the corresponding component codes $\{D, T, M, P, Pr, V\}$.
