<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
**"List and briefly explain the 5 V's of Big Data with examples."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The 5 V's characterize Big Data and are essential components of the Data Layer in Business Analytics:

1. **Volume** — Massive scale of data, typically in Terabytes, Petabytes, or Exabytes. *Example:* Facebook generates over 4 petabytes of data daily.
2. **Velocity** — The speed at which data is generated and must be processed. *Example:* Stock market tick data arriving every millisecond.
3. **Variety** — Different formats and types of data. *Example:* Sensor readings (structured), social media posts (unstructured), and JSON logs (semi-structured).
4. **Veracity** — Trustworthiness and quality of data. *Example:* Customer addresses with typos, missing fields, or duplicates must be cleaned.
5. **Value** — The business worth extracted from data. *Example:* A recommendation engine turning browsing history into a 15% sales lift.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
**"Differentiate between a Data Lake and a Data Warehouse."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Data Lake | Data Warehouse |
|---|---|---|
| Data type | Raw, all formats (structured, semi, unstructured) | Cleaned, structured, relational |
| Schema | Schema-on-read (flexible) | Schema-on-write (rigid) |
| Users | Data scientists, engineers | Business analysts, managers |
| Cost | Low (cheap commodity storage) | High (specialized systems) |
| Purpose | Exploration, ML, deep analytics | Reporting, BI, KPI dashboards |
| Processing | ELT (Extract, Load, Transform) | ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### 🔷 Question A (Choice 1) — **[KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the **six components of Business Analytics** in detail with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **CRISP-DM methodology** with a neat block diagram and explain each phase. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

The six components of Business Analytics form an integrated ecosystem that converts raw data into business value:

**1. Data (D):** The foundation. Includes internal transactional data, external market data, IoT streams, and social media. Characterized by the 5 V's of Big Data. *Example:* Walmart's 2.5 petabytes of transaction data.

**2. Technology (T):** The infrastructure backbone — cloud platforms (AWS, Azure), databases (SQL, NoSQL), processing engines (Hadoop, Spark), and analytics tools (Python, Tableau). *Example:* Netflix's recommendation system running on AWS.

**3. Models and Methods (M):** Mathematical and algorithmic techniques — descriptive (mean, clustering), predictive (regression, neural networks), and prescriptive (optimization, simulation). *Example:* Credit scoring using logistic regression.

**4. People (P):** Human capital — data engineers, data scientists, business analysts, domain experts, and leadership. *Example:* A Chief Data Officer setting the analytics roadmap.

**5. Process (Pr):** Governance and lifecycle frameworks such as **CRISP-DM, SEMMA, KDD** ensuring repeatability, quality, and compliance. *Example:* A bank's model risk management policy.

**6. Visualization (V):** Final-mile delivery through dashboards, reports, and storytelling. *Example:* A real-time sales dashboard refreshed every 15 minutes for the CEO.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Valuation Key:** Students must mention **all six components with one example each** for full 7 marks. Omitting "Process" or "People" is the most common cause of partial marking.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**CRISP-DM (Cross-Industry Standard Process for Data Mining)** is a six-phase, iterative, industry-neutral methodology:

- **Phase 1 – Business Understanding:** Define the business problem, objectives, and success criteria. *Output:* Project plan, business questions.
- **Phase 2 – Data Understanding:** Initial data collection, exploration, and quality assessment. *Output:* Data description, EDA report.
- **Phase 3 – Data Preparation:** Cleaning, integration, transformation, feature engineering. *Output:* Clean dataset, ready for modeling. *Most time-consuming phase (~70% of project time).*
- **Phase 4 – Modeling:** Select and apply modeling techniques (regression, classification, clustering). Tune parameters.
- **Phase 5 – Evaluation:** Assess whether the model meets the business objectives. Review process, approve model.
- **Phase 6 – Deployment:** Deploy model into production, monitor performance, generate reports.

The phases are **iterative** — findings in any phase can send the team back to an earlier phase.

> [!NOTE]
> **Block Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.2 of these notes for the exam-ready CRISP-DM diagram.

---

### 🔷 Question B (Choice 2) — **[KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** What is the **Data-to-Insight pipeline**? Explain each stage with a real-world example from the retail industry. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A manufacturing firm scores its analytics components as follows: Data=2, Technology=1, Models=2, People=1, Process=0, Visualization=2 (on a 0–3 scale). **Compute the Analytics Maturity Score (AMS)** and provide a strategic recommendation. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

The **Data-to-Insight Pipeline** is the sequential, closed-loop process by which raw data is transformed into actionable business insights. The stages are:

**Stage 1 – Data Generation:** Raw events are generated by business operations. *Retail example:* A point-of-sale (POS) terminal records a transaction of ₹450 for a customer buying groceries.

**Stage 2 – Data Ingestion:** The data enters the analytics stack via batch loads or real-time streams. *Retail example:* Kafka stream or nightly batch pushes 100,000 transactions into the central data lake.

**Stage 3 – Data Storage:** Persistent storage in data lakes (raw) or warehouses (cleaned). *Retail example:* Snowflake data warehouse stores 5 years of cleaned, schema-enforced transactions.

**Stage 4 – Data Processing & Analysis:** ETL/ELT pipelines clean, join, and aggregate data; analytical models run. *Retail example:* A market basket analysis identifies that customers buying bread also buy butter 73% of the time.

**Stage 5 – Insight Generation & Visualization:** Model outputs are rendered as dashboards, alerts, or reports. *Retail example:* A Tableau dashboard shows the top 10 product associations, refreshed daily for the category manager.

**Stage 6 – Decision & Action:** Business users act on the insight. *Retail example:* The category manager places bread and butter on adjacent shelves → 12% revenue uplift in that category.

**Stage 7 – Feedback Loop:** The decision generates new data (e.g., new sales patterns) that re-enter the pipeline, creating a **continuous improvement cycle**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Valuation Key:** The example must be **continuous and consistent** (use the same retail scenario across all 6 stages). Generic disconnected examples lose 2–3 marks.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given scores (on a 0–3 scale):**

- $s_D = 2$, $s_T = 1$, $s_M = 2$, $s_P = 1$, $s_{Pr} = 0$, $s_V = 2$

**Step 1:** Apply the Analytics Maturity Score formula:

$$
AMS = \frac{1}{6} \sum_{i=1}^{6} s_i
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the values:

$$
AMS = \frac{2 + 1 + 2 + 1 + 0 + 2}{6} = \frac{8}{6}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute:

$$
AMS = 1.33
$$

**Step 4:** Map to maturity stage:

Since $AMS = 1.33 \in (0.5, 1.5]$, the firm is in the **"Developing"** maturity stage.

**Step 5: Strategic Recommendations:**

- **Priority 1 — Process (currently 0):** Implement a formal analytics governance framework (CRISP-DM) immediately. This is the weakest pillar.
- **Priority 2 — People (currently 1):** Invest in training programs and hire 2–3 data engineers / data scientists.
- **Priority 3 — Technology (currently 1):** Migrate ad-hoc tools to a centralized cloud platform (AWS/Azure).
- **Maintain Strengths:** Data, Models, and Visualization are at score 2 — continue investing to push them to score 3.
- **Target:** Achieve $AMS \geq 2.0$ within 18 months to reach the "Advanced" stage.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** A very common mistake is to **add the scores incorrectly** (e.g., writing 9/6 instead of 8/6) or to **omit the strategic recommendation** (Step 5). The strategic recommendation is mandatory for full 7 marks — it tests the **application** level of Bloom's Taxonomy, which is heavily weighted in 2024 scheme evaluations. Also, do not forget to explicitly **map the score to the maturity band** (Beginner / Developing / Advanced / Optimized).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 60-Second Revision Bullet Points for Exam Day:**

- ✅ Business Analytics has **6 Components**: Data, Technology, Models, People, Process, Visualization ($D, T, M, P, Pr, V$).
- ✅ **Big Data = 5 V's**: Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, Value (memorize the order and one example per V).
- ✅ **CRISP-DM has 6 phases** (cyclic, not linear): Business Understanding → Data Understanding → Data Preparation → Modeling → Evaluation → Deployment.
- ✅ **Data Preparation is the most time-consuming phase** (~70% of project effort).
- ✅ **Three types of models**: Descriptive (what happened), Predictive (what will happen), Prescriptive (what to do).
- ✅ **Data Lake vs Data Warehouse**: Lake = raw + cheap + flexible schema; Warehouse = clean + expensive + rigid schema.
- ✅ **ETL** = Extract, Transform, Load; **ELT** = Extract, Load, Transform (modern data lake pattern).
- ✅ **The 80/20 Rule**: Roughly 80% of enterprise data is unstructured.
- ✅ **Analytics Maturity Score (AMS)** formula: $AMS = \frac{1}{6}\sum_{i=1}^{6} s_i$, where $s_i \in \{0, 1, 2, 3\}$.
- ✅ **Maturity bands**: $[0, 0.5]$ Beginner; $(0.5, 1.5]$ Developing; $(1.5, 2.5]$ Advanced; $(2.5, 3.0]$ Optimized.
- ✅ **Process frameworks to memorize**: CRISP-DM (industry standard), SEMMA (SAS), KDD (academic).
- ✅ **Final-mile delivery is Visualization** — a model without a dashboard has zero business impact.
- ✅ **People & Process are the most commonly failed components** in real organizations — emphasize them in exam answers.
- ✅ **Closed-loop feedback**: Insight → Action → New Data → New Insight (continuous cycle).

> **End of Module 2 — Components | PEADT522 Business Analytics | KTU 2024 Scheme**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Models and Techniques. Forecasting - Forecasting for Management Decisions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEADT522 - BUSINESS ANALYTICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Models and Techniques. Forecasting - Forecasting for Management Decisions # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Forecasting? **Forecasting** is the systematic process of estimating future events, demand, sales, or trends based on the systematic analysis of historical and current data, combined with relevant business intelligence. In the context of management decisions, forecasting acts as the **primary cognitive input** that drives strategic planning, inventory control, budgeting, capacity planning, and supply chain orchestration. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal KTU Definition (Heizer & Render / Stevenson Framework):** > *Forecasting is a planning tool used by management to predict the future, allowing time for the development and implementation of strategic responses to changing conditions. It converts historical data and qualitative insights into actionable projections.* ### 1.2 The Strategic Role of Forecasting in Management Forecasting is the **backbone of all operational and strategic planning functions** in any organization. Every major managerial decision — from purchasing raw materials to hiring personnel — hinges on a credible forecast. | Managerial Function | Application of Forecasting | |---|---| | **Strategic Planning** | Long-term capital investment, market entry, M&A decisions | | **Operations Management** | Production scheduling, capacity planning, aggregate planning | | **Inventory & Supply Chain** | Reorder points, safety stock, EOQ calculations | | **Human Resources** | Workforce planning, hiring volumes, training needs | | **Finance & Budgeting** | Revenue projection, cash-flow planning, capital budgeting | | **Marketing** | Promotional planning, new product launches, demand sensing | ## 1.3 The Two Pillars: Qualitative vs. Quantitative Forecasting Forecasting techniques broadly fall into two camps: ### A. Qualitative Forecasting (Judgmental) These methods rely on **human expertise, intuition, and opinion** rather than mathematical models. They are essential when historical data is **scarce, nonexistent, or unreliable** (e.g., launching a brand-new product or entering an uncharted market). - **Grass Roots (Bottom-Up)**: Aggregates estimates from frontline employees. - **Market Research**: Surveys, focus groups, consumer panels. - **Panel Consensus (Delphi Method)**: Iterative, anonymous expert consensus. - **Historical Analogy**: Comparing to a similar product's life cycle. ### B. Quantitative Forecasting (Statistical / Mathematical) These methods apply **mathematical models** to historical numerical data to project future values. They are objective, repeatable, and scale beautifully. - **Time-Series Models** (assume future pattern resembles the past) - **Associative (Causal) Models** (use independent variables like GDP, advertising spend) - **Artificial Intelligence / ML Models** (Neural Networks, ARIMA, Prophet, LSTM) > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** This module focuses on **time-series and associative forecasting models** because they form the computational core of Business Analytics practice. Qualitative methods are touched upon for managerial context only. ## 1.4 Components of a Time Series A time series is data collected at **regular chronological intervals** (daily, weekly, monthly, quarterly). Any such series can be decomposed into four components: $$Y_t = T_t + S_t + C_t + I_t$$ | Component | Symbol | Nature | Real-World Example | |---|---|---|---| | **Trend** | $T_t$ | Long-term upward/downward movement | E-commerce growth year-over-year | | **Seasonal** | $S_t$ | Predictable, repeating within-year pattern | Ice-cream sales peaking every summer | | **Cyclical** | $C_t$ | Multi-year wave-like fluctuations (≥ 1 year) | Real estate boom-bust cycles | | **Irregular / Random** | $I_t$ | Unpredictable noise, residuals | Strikes, natural disasters, pandemics | ### 1.5 Intuitive Analogy: The Weather and a Recipe Think of forecasting as **cooking a dish**: - **Trend** is the overall *direction* of heat — slow and steady, raising the temperature. - **Seasonality** is the *spice* you add rhythmically at certain stages. - **Cyclical** is the *stirring motion* — the rise and fall of the spoon. - **Irregular** is the *unexpected splash* of oil or a sudden change in flame. A good chef (or manager) knows when each element dominates and adjusts the recipe (or strategy) accordingly. > [!TIP] > **Why This Matters in KTU Exams:** A typical 14-mark question in Module 2 will ask you to *identify which time-series component a given data exhibits* before asking you to recommend an appropriate forecasting model. Always state the pattern explicitly first. ## 1.6 Steps in the Forecasting Process (Standard KTU Sequence) 1. **Define the objective** — what variable are we forecasting? At what level of detail? Over what horizon? 2. **Collect and clean historical data** — handle missing values, outliers, structural breaks. 3. **Plot the data and identify the pattern** — trend, seasonality, cyclicality, irregularity. 4. **Select a forecasting model** — based on data pattern, horizon, and accuracy-cost trade-off. 5. **Validate the model** — split data into training and testing sets. 6. **Generate the forecast** — project future values. 7. **Monitor and refine** — compare actuals to forecasts, track forecast error continuously. > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often skip Step 4 and apply Exponential Smoothing to seasonal data, or Naive forecasting to trending data. The choice of model **must** be justified by the underlying time-series pattern. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Quantitative Forecasting Models The models are arranged in order of **increasing complexity and data demand**: | # | Model | Best Suited For | Data Required | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Naive Forecast** | Stable series, baseline benchmark | 1 period | | 2 | **Simple Moving Average (SMA)** | No-trend, no-seasonality | Last $n$ periods | | 3 | **Weighted Moving Average (WMA)** | Recent data more relevant | Last $n$ periods + weights | | 4 | **Simple Exponential Smoothing (SES)** | No-trend, no-seasonality | All past data (weighted) | | 5 | **Trend-Adjusted Exp. Smoothing (Holt's)** | Linear trend, no seasonality | All past data | | 6 | **Trend & Seasonal Exp. Smoothing (Holt-Winters)** | Trend + Seasonality | All past data + season length | | 7 | **Linear Regression (Associative)** | Causal relationship | Independent variable(s) | | 8 | **ARIMA / Advanced AI** | Complex stochastic patterns | Long time series | ## 2.2 Detailed Model Mechanics ### Model 1: Naive Forecast The simplest possible approach — the next period's forecast equals the **most recent actual value**. $$F_{t+1} = A_t$$ - **Use case:** Stable series; serves as the **benchmark** every sophisticated model must beat. - **Logic:** Tomorrow will look like today. ### Model 2: Simple Moving Average (SMA) Averages the last $n$ periods. Smooths out short-term fluctuations. $$F_{t+1} = \frac{A_t + A_{t-1} + A_{t-2} + \dots + A_{t-n+1}}{n} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=t-n+1}^{t} A_i$$ - **Use case:** Stationary data with no trend or seasonality. - **Tuning:** Larger $n$ → smoother (less responsive); smaller $n$ → more responsive (more noise). ### Model 3: Weighted Moving Average (WMA) Allows differential weights to recent vs. older periods. Weights sum to 1. $$F_{t+1} = w_1 A_t + w_2 A_{t-1} + w_3 A_{t-2} + \dots + w_n A_{t-n+1} \quad \text{where} \quad \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i = 1$$ - **Heuristic:** Most recent period gets the highest weight. ### Model 4: Simple Exponential Smoothing (SES) Recursively updates the forecast using a **smoothing constant** $\alpha \in [0, 1]$. $$F_{t+1} = F_t + \alpha(A_t - F_t) = \alpha A_t + (1 - \alpha) F_t$$ Expanding the recursion reveals that SES is an **infinite weighted average** where older observations receive geometrically decaying weights. - **High $\alpha$** ($\to 1$): Model reacts quickly to recent changes (responsive). - **Low $\alpha$** ($\to 0$): Model is stable, slow to react (smooth). - **Optimal $\alpha$** minimizes forecast error (typically found by grid search or solver). ### Model 5: Trend-Adjusted Exponential Smoothing (Holt's Two-Parameter Method) Captures **linear trend** by maintaining two equations — one for the **level** $L_t$ and one for the **trend** $T_t$. $$L_t = \alpha A_t + (1 - \alpha)(L_{t-1} + T_{t-1})$$ $$T_t = \beta(L_t - L_{t-1}) + (1 - \beta) T_{t-1}$$ $$F_{t+m} = L_t + m \cdot T_t$$ where $\alpha$ is the level-smoothing constant, $\beta$ is the trend-smoothing constant, and $m$ is the number of periods into the future. ### Model 6: Trend + Seasonality (Holt-Winters) Extends Holt's to include a **seasonal index** $S_t$. The model has three forms (multiplicative shown): $$L_t = \alpha \frac{A_t}{S_{t-s}} + (1 - \alpha)(L_{t-1} + T_{t-1})$$ $$T_t = \beta(L_t - L_{t-1}) + (1 - \beta) T_{t-1}$$ $$S_t = \gamma \frac{A_t}{L_t} + (1 - \gamma) S_{t-s}$$ $$F_{t+m} = (L_t + mT_t) \cdot S_{t-s+m}$$ where $s$ is the season length (e.g., $s = 4$ for quarterly data, $s = 12$ for monthly). ### Model 7: Linear Regression (Associative / Causal) Captures a **causal relationship** between the dependent variable and one or more independent variables. $$Y = a + bX + \varepsilon$$ where the coefficients are estimated via **Ordinary Least Squares (OLS):** $$b = \frac{\sum (X_i - \bar{X})(Y_i - \bar{Y})}{\sum (X_i - \bar{X})^2}, \qquad a = \bar{Y} - b\bar{X}$$ The forecast for a future $X$ value is: $$F = a + bX_{\text{future}}$$ ## 2.3 Forecast Error Metrics — The KTU Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Always compute and report at least ONE error metric** when building a model in your KTU exam. Examiners award marks for evaluation rigor. | Metric | Formula | Interpretation | |---|---|---| | **MAD** (Mean Absolute Deviation) | $MAD = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} \vert A_t - F_t \vert$ | Average magnitude of error; same units as data | | **MSE** (Mean Squared Error) | $MSE = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} (A_t - F_t)^2$ | Penalizes large errors heavily | | **RMSE** (Root Mean Squared Error) | $RMSE = \sqrt{MSE}$ | Same units as data; comparable to MAD | | **MAPE** (Mean Absolute Percentage Error) | $MAPE = \frac{100}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{\vert A_t - F_t \vert}{\vert A_t \vert}$ | Scale-independent (% of actual) | | **Tracking Signal** | $TS = \frac{\text{Running Sum of Forecast Errors (CFE)}}{MAD}$ | Detects forecast bias; flag if $\vert TS \vert > 4$ | | **Bias (CFE)** | $CFE = \sum (A_t - F_t)$ | Cumulative error; positive = under-forecasting | ## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Master Table) > [!NOTE] > **Exam-Saving Tip:** Memorize the table below — it consolidates every formula you need for a 14-mark Module 2 problem. Use `\vert` in all places where absolute value is required to keep your markdown tables clean. | Model / Concept | Governing Equation | Key Parameters | When to Use | |---|---|---|---| | Naive | $F_{t+1} = A_t$ | None | Benchmark | | Simple Moving Avg | $F_{t+1} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=t-n+1}^{t} A_i$ | Window size $n$ | Stationary data | | Weighted Moving Avg | $F_{t+1} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i A_{t-i+1}$ | Weights $w_i$; $\sum w_i = 1$ | Recency-biased data | | Simple Exp. Smoothing | $F_{t+1} = \alpha A_t + (1-\alpha) F_t$ | Smoothing $\alpha \in [0,1]$ | Stationary, no trend | | Holt's (Trend) | $L_t = \alpha A_t + (1-\alpha)(L_{t-1} + T_{t-1})$
$T_t = \beta(L_t - L_{t-1}) + (1-\beta) T_{t-1}$
$F_{t+m} = L_t + m T_t$ | $\alpha$, $\beta$ | Linear trend | | Holt-Winters | Adds $S_t$ (seasonal) and $F_{t+m} = (L_t + mT_t) \cdot S_{t-s+m}$ | $\alpha$, $\beta$, $\gamma$ | Trend + Seasonality | | Linear Regression | $Y = a + bX$
$b = \dfrac{\sum(X_i - \bar X)(Y_i - \bar Y)}{\sum(X_i - \bar X)^2}$
$a = \bar Y - b\bar X$ | Slope $b$, intercept $a$ | Causal modeling | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering & Management Utility - **Retail & E-commerce (Amazon, Flipkart):** SES + Holt-Winters for daily/weekly demand forecasting. - **Manufacturing (Siemens, Bosch):** Linear regression for sales vs. industrial production index. - **Energy Sector (KSEB, NTPC):** Holt-Winters with $s=12$ for monthly power demand. - **Hospitality (Kerala Tourism):** Seasonal models for hotel occupancy across monsoons. - **Aviation (IndiGo, Air India):** ARIMA + ML hybrids for passenger load forecasting. - **Banking & Finance:** Linear regression for credit demand vs. GDP and interest rate. - **Healthcare (Apollo, KIMS):** Patient admission forecasting for resource planning. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked-Out Numerical Example: 4-Month Moving Average > **Problem (KTU 14-mark style):** The monthly sales of a small electronics retailer in Kochi for the last 9 months are: > | Month | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 | 9 | > |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| > | Sales | 100 | 110 | 115 | 120 | 125 | 135 | 140 | 150 | 160 | > > **a)** Compute a 4-period Simple Moving Average forecast for months 5–9. > **b)** Calculate MAD, MSE, and MAPE to evaluate accuracy. ### Solution: **Step 1: Apply the SMA formula for each forecast period.** The 4-month SMA forecast for month $t$ is: $$F_t = \frac{A_{t-4} + A_{t-3} + A_{t-2} + A_{t-1}}{4}$$ | Month $t$ | Actual $A_t$ | Forecast $F_t$ (4-month MA) | Computation | |---|---|---|---| | 5 | 125 | 111.25 | $(100+110+115+120)/4$ | | 6 | 135 | 117.50 | $(110+115+120+125)/4$ | | 7 | 140 | 123.75 | $(115+120+125+135)/4$ | | 8 | 150 | 131.25 | $(120+125+135+140)/4$ | | 9 | 160 | 137.50 | $(125+135+140+150)/4$ | **Step 2: Compute forecast errors $\vert A_t - F_t \vert$ and $(A_t - F_t)^2$.** | $t$ | $A_t$ | $F_t$ | $\vert A_t - F_t \vert$ | $(A_t - F_t)^2$ | $\dfrac{\vert A_t - F_t \vert}{\vert A_t \vert} \times 100$ | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 5 | 125 | 111.25 | 13.75 | 189.0625 | 11.000% | | 6 | 135 | 117.50 | 17.50 | 306.2500 | 12.963% | | 7 | 140 | 123.75 | 16.25 | 264.0625 | 11.607% | | 8 | 150 | 131.25 | 18.75 | 351.5625 | 12.500% | | 9 | 160 | 137.50 | 22.50 | 506.2500 | 14.063% | | **Sum** | — | — | **88.75** | **1617.1875** | **62.133%** | **Step 3: Calculate MAD.** $$MAD = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} \vert A_t - F_t \vert = \frac{88.75}{5} = 17.75 \text{ units}$$ **Step 4: Calculate MSE.** $$MSE = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} (A_t - F_t)^2 = \frac{1617.1875}{5} = 323.4375 \text{ (units)}^2$$ **Step 5: Calculate RMSE.** $$RMSE = \sqrt{MSE} = \sqrt{323.4375} \approx 17.98 \text{ units}$$ **Step 6: Calculate MAPE.** $$MAPE = \frac{100\%}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{\vert A_t - F_t \vert}{\vert A_t \vert} = \frac{62.133\%}{5} \approx 12.43\%$$ **Step 7: Managerial Interpretation.** > [!TIP] > A MAPE of **12.43%** is generally considered **acceptable** in retail demand forecasting (industry benchmark: MAPE < 10% is "highly accurate", 10–25% is "good"). However, the errors are systematically **negative** (forecasts < actuals) — this indicates an **upward trend** that SMA cannot capture. The manager should consider **Holt's method** instead. ## 3.2 Worked-Out Example: Simple Exponential Smoothing (Hand Calculation) > **Problem:** Using the same sales data above, apply Simple Exponential Smoothing with $\alpha = 0.3$ and initial forecast $F_2 = 100$. Generate forecasts for months 3 through 9. ### Solution: **Step 1: Initialize.** We need a starting forecast. Conventionally, $F_2 = A_1 = 100$. **Step 2: Recursive update.** For each subsequent period: $$F_{t+1} = \alpha A_t + (1 - \alpha) F_t$$ **Step 3: Tabulate.** | $t$ | $A_t$ | $F_t$ (prev.) | $F_{t+1} = 0.3 A_t + 0.7 F_t$ | Error $\vert A_t - F_t \vert$ | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | 100 | — | $F_2 = 100$ (init) | — | | 2 | 110 | 100 | $F_3 = 0.3(110) + 0.7(100) = 103.00$ | 7.00 | | 3 | 115 | 103.00 | $F_4 = 0.3(115) + 0.7(103) = 106.60$ | 8.40 | | 4 | 120 | 106.60 | $F_5 = 0.3(120) + 0.7(106.6) = 110.62$ | 9.38 | | 5 | 125 | 110.62 | $F_6 = 0.3(125) + 0.7(110.62) = 114.93$ | 10.07 | | 6 | 135 | 114.93 | $F_7 = 0.3(135) + 0.7(114.93) = 120.95$ | 14.05 | | 7 | 140 | 120.95 | $F_8 = 0.3(140) + 0.7(120.95) = 126.67$ | 13.33 | | 8 | 150 | 126.67 | $F_9 = 0.3(150) + 0.7(126.67) = 133.67$ | 16.33 | | 9 | 160 | 133.67 | — | 26.33 | **Step 4: Compute MAD.** $$MAD = \frac{7.00 + 8.40 + 9.38 + 10.07 + 14.05 + 13.33 + 16.33 + 26.33}{8} = \frac{104.89}{8} \approx 13.11$$ **Step 5: Compare.** SES with $\alpha = 0.3$ gives MAD = 13.11, **better than SMA's MAD of 17.75**. SES weights recent observations more, so it tracks the upward trend better. > [!NOTE] > **Why the error keeps growing:** The error at $t=9$ (26.33) is largest because the **actual growth rate (10 units/month) is faster than SES can adapt** with $\alpha = 0.3$. Increasing $\alpha$ to 0.5 or 0.7 would help — but if the trend persists, **Holt's method** is the proper long-term solution. ## 3.3 Worked-Out Example: Linear Regression for Causal Forecasting > **Problem:** A Kerala-based FMCG firm believes its quarterly sales $Y$ (₹ lakhs) depend on the regional advertising spend $X$ (₹ lakhs). Data for 6 quarters: > > | Quarter | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | > |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| > | $X$ | 10 | 12 | 15 | 18 | 20 | 22 | > | $Y$ | 30 | 35 | 40 | 50 | 55 | 60 | > > **Fit a linear regression line and forecast $Y$ for $X = 25$.** ### Solution: **Step 1: Calculate the means.** $$\bar X = \frac{10 + 12 + 15 + 18 + 20 + 22}{6} = \frac{97}{6} \approx 16.1667$$ $$\bar Y = \frac{30 + 35 + 40 + 50 + 55 + 60}{6} = \frac{270}{6} = 45$$ **Step 2: Compute numerator and denominator of the slope $b$.** $$\sum X_i Y_i = (10)(30) + (12)(35) + (15)(40) + (18)(50) + (20)(55) + (22)(60) = 300 + 420 + 600 + 900 + 1100 + 1320 = 4640$$ $$\sum X_i^2 = 100 + 144 + 225 + 324 + 400 + 484 = 1677$$ $$b = \frac{\sum (X_i - \bar X)(Y_i - \bar Y)}{\sum (X_i - \bar X)^2} = \frac{\sum X_i Y_i - n\bar X \bar Y}{\sum X_i^2 - n\bar X^2}$$ **Step 2a: Numerator.** $$\sum X_i Y_i - n\bar X \bar Y = 4640 - 6(16.1667)(45) = 4640 - 4365 = 275$$ **Step 2b: Denominator.** $$\sum X_i^2 - n\bar X^2 = 1677 - 6(16.1667)^2 = 1677 - 1568.17 = 108.83$$ **Step 2c: Slope.** $$b = \frac{275}{108.83} \approx 2.527$$ **Step 3: Intercept.** $$a = \bar Y - b\bar X = 45 - (2.527)(16.1667) = 45 - 40.85 \approx 4.15$$ **Step 4: The fitted regression equation.** $$Y = 4.15 + 2.527X$$ **Step 5: Forecast for $X = 25$.** $$F = 4.15 + 2.527(25) = 4.15 + 63.18 = 67.33 \text{ ₹ lakhs}$$ **Step 6: Managerial interpretation.** Every additional ₹1 lakh in advertising is associated with a ₹2.527 lakh increase in sales. If the firm plans to spend ₹25 lakhs on ads in the next quarter, projected sales will be ~₹67.33 lakhs. ## 3.4 Forecast Model Selection Decision Matrix Use this decision matrix in KTU problems where you must **justify a model selection:** | Data Pattern | Recommended Primary Model | Why | |---|---|---| | **Stationary, no trend/seasonality** | Naive or SMA or SES | Simple, robust, low data demand | | **Linear trend, no seasonality** | Holt's Trend-Adjusted | Two-component smoothing | | **Trend + Seasonality** | Holt-Winters | Captures both effects | | **Strong causal driver** | Linear Regression | Exploits leading indicator | | **Highly volatile / complex** | ARIMA, ML models | Nonlinear patterns | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Forecasting Methodology Pipeline (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA["Define Forecasting Objective"] --> nodeB["Collect Historical Data"] nodeB --> nodeC["Clean and Pre-process Data"] nodeC --> nodeD{"Identify Time-Series Pattern"} nodeD -- "Trend Only" --> nodeE["Apply Holts Two-Parameter Method"] nodeD -- "Seasonality Only" --> nodeF["Apply Seasonal Index Decomposition"] nodeD -- "Trend and Seasonality" --> nodeG["Apply Holt-Winters Method"] nodeD -- "Stationary" --> nodeH["Apply SES or SMA"] nodeD -- "Causal Driver" --> nodeI["Apply Linear Regression"] nodeE --> nodeJ["Validate via Train-Test Split"] nodeF --> nodeJ nodeG --> nodeJ nodeH --> nodeJ nodeI --> nodeJ nodeJ --> nodeK{"Error Within Tolerance?"} nodeK -- "No" --> nodeL["Re-tune Parameters or Switch Model"] nodeL --> nodeJ nodeK -- "Yes" --> nodeM["Deploy Forecast to Management"] nodeM --> nodeN["Continuous Monitoring and Re-forecasting"] ``` ## 4.2 Time-Series Decomposition Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeRaw["Raw Time Series Yt"] --> nodeDecompose{"Decomposition Engine"} nodeDecompose -- "Long-term" --> nodeTrend["Trend Component Tt"] nodeDecompose -- "Within-year" --> nodeSeasonal["Seasonal Component St"] nodeDecompose -- "Multi-year wave" --> nodeCyclical["Cyclical Component Ct"] nodeDecompose -- "Residual" --> nodeIrregular["Irregular Component It"] nodeTrend --> nodeReconstruct["Reconstruct Yt"] nodeSeasonal --> nodeReconstruct nodeCyclical --> nodeReconstruct nodeIrregular --> nodeReconstruct nodeReconstruct --> nodeModel{"Choose Model"} nodeModel -- "Additive" --> nodeAdd["Yt = Tt + St + Ct + It"] nodeModel -- "Multiplicative" --> nodeMult["Yt = Tt x St x Ct x It"] ``` ## 4.3 Forecasting Model Selection Logic Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD start["Start: Have Historical Data"] --> q1{"Is there a clear trend?"} q1 -- "No" --> q2{"Is there seasonality?"} q1 -- "Yes" --> q3{"Is there also seasonality?"} q3 -- "No" --> holts["Use Holts Trend-Adjusted Exp Smoothing"] q3 -- "Yes" --> holtwin["Use Holt-Winters Method"] q2 -- "No" --> q4{"Is recent data more important?"} q2 -- "Yes" --> decomp["Use Seasonal Decomposition or Holt-Winters"] q4 -- "No" --> sma["Use Simple Moving Average"] q4 -- "Yes" --> ses["Use Simple Exponential Smoothing"] holts --> out["Forecast Generated"] holtwin --> out decomp --> out sma --> out ses --> out ``` ## 4.4 Exponential Smoothing Weight Decay Curve (Conceptual) ```mermaid graph LR obsA["At weight alpha"] --> recency["Most Recent Period Strongest Influence"] obsB["At weight alpha times 1 minus alpha"] --> decay["Geometric Decay of Influence"] obsC["At weight alpha times 1 minus alpha squared"] --> decay obsD["At weight alpha times 1 minus alpha cubed"] --> decay obsE["As t grows very large weight approaches zero"] --> obsolescence["Old Observations Approach Zero Influence but Never Zero"] recency --> explanation["SES is an infinite weighted average"] decay --> explanation obsolescence --> explanation ``` ## 4.5 Forecast Error Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TD errRoot["Forecast Error Et = At minus Ft"] --> absErr["Absolute Error: vertical bar Et vertical bar"] errRoot --> sqErr["Squared Error: Et squared"] errRoot --> pctErr["Percentage Error: vertical bar Et divided by At vertical bar times 100"] absErr --> mad["MAD: mean of vertical bar Et vertical bar"] sqErr --> mse["MSE: mean of Et squared"] sqErr --> rmse["RMSE: square root of MSE"] pctErr --> mape["MAPE: mean percentage error"] errRoot --> bias["Bias or CFE: sum of Et over time"] bias --> ts["Tracking Signal: CFE divided by MAD"] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper]` **Differentiate between Qualitative and Quantitative forecasting techniques. Give two examples of each.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Qualitative | Quantitative | |---|---|---| | **Basis** | Judgment, intuition, expert opinion | Mathematical/statistical models | | **Data Required** | Little or no historical data | Substantial historical numerical data | | **Objectivity** | Subjective, may vary by expert | Objective, reproducible | | **Best Suited For** | New products, novel scenarios, long horizons | Existing products, short-to-medium horizons | | **Examples** | Delphi method, Market research, Panel consensus, Grass-roots estimates | Moving Averages, Exponential Smoothing, Linear Regression, ARIMA, Holt-Winters | | **Cost** | Lower upfront (but time-intensive) | Higher (requires data infrastructure) | ### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024 / Model Paper]` **Define MAD, MSE, and MAPE. Why is MAPE often preferred over MAD in business reporting?** **Model Answer:** - **MAD (Mean Absolute Deviation):** $MAD = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} \vert A_t - F_t \vert$ — the average magnitude of forecast errors in the same unit as the data. - **MSE (Mean Squared Error):** $MSE = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} (A_t - F_t)^2$ — penalizes large errors quadratically. - **MAPE (Mean Absolute Percentage Error):** $MAPE = \frac{100}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{\vert A_t - F_t \vert}{\vert A_t \vert}$ — expresses error as a percentage of the actual value. **Why MAPE is preferred in business reporting:** 1. It is **scale-independent** — a MAPE of 8% means the same thing whether the product sells 10 units or 10,000 units. 2. It is **dimensionless**, so it can be compared across product lines, regions, and time horizons. 3. It is **intuitive** for non-technical executives who understand percentages more easily than raw error units. --- ## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Format) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` > **a)** Explain the components of a time series with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)** > **b)** The quarterly sales of a company (in ₹ lakhs) for the last 5 quarters are: **40, 45, 50, 55, 60**. Compute the forecasts using a **3-period Simple Moving Average** for periods 4 and 5, and calculate the **MAD** and **MAPE** for the same. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution (a) — Time Series Components (7 Marks) A time series $Y_t$ observed at regular time intervals can be decomposed into four components: $$Y_t = T_t + S_t + C_t + I_t \quad \text{(additive form)}$$ $$Y_t = T_t \times S_t \times C_t \times I_t \quad \text{(multiplicative form)}$$ **1. Trend ($T_t$):** The long-term gradual upward or downward movement in the data, typically caused by population growth, inflation, or technological change. *Example:* The continuous year-over-year rise in smartphone shipments globally. **2. Seasonal Variation ($S_t$):** Predictable, repeating patterns within a calendar year (or other fixed period). It is caused by weather, holidays, or cultural factors. *Example:* Air-conditioner sales in India peak every April-June; Kerala tourism peaks in December-January. **3. Cyclical Variation ($C_t$):** Multi-year wave-like rises and falls in business activity, often tied to macroeconomic cycles (recession, expansion). Periods exceed one year and are not of fixed length. *Example:* The real-estate boom-bust cycle of 2007-2012. **4. Irregular Variation ($I_t$):** The residual, unpredictable random component — the "noise" left over after accounting for trend, seasonality, and cycles. *Example:* A sudden drop in sales due to a flash flood, a viral social media boost, or a regulatory change. > Identifying the dominant component is **critical** for selecting the correct forecasting model. #### Model Solution (b) — SMA Computation (7 Marks) **Step 1: List the data.** | Quarter $t$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | $A_t$ | 40 | 45 | 50 | 55 | 60 | **Step 2: Apply 3-period SMA to compute $F_4$ and $F_5$.** $$F_4 = \frac{A_1 + A_2 + A_3}{3} = \frac{40 + 45 + 50}{3} = \frac{135}{3} = 45$$ $$F_5 = \frac{A_2 + A_3 + A_4}{3} = \frac{45 + 50 + 55}{3} = \frac{150}{3} = 50$$ **Step 3: Compute errors for periods 4 and 5.** | $t$ | $A_t$ | $F_t$ | $\vert A_t - F_t \vert$ | $\frac{\vert A_t - F_t \vert}{\vert A_t \vert} \times 100$ | |---|---|---|---|---| | 4 | 55 | 45 | 10 | 18.18% | | 5 | 60 | 50 | 10 | 16.67% | | **Sum** | — | — | **20** | **34.85%** | **Step 4: Compute MAD.** $$MAD = \frac{20}{2} = 10 \text{ ₹ lakhs}$$ **Step 5: Compute MAPE.** $$MAPE = \frac{34.85\%}{2} \approx 17.42\%$$ **Step 6: Managerial Insight.** The persistent **negative bias** (forecast < actual by 10 each period) signals a **clear upward trend** in the data. SMA is **inappropriate** here. The student should recommend **Holt's Trend-Adjusted Exponential Smoothing** to capture the trend component. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]` > **a)** Explain Simple Exponential Smoothing with the role of the smoothing constant $\alpha$. Discuss the impact of choosing $\alpha$ close to 0 versus $\alpha$ close to 1. **(7 Marks)** > **b)** A retail store's daily sales are: **20, 22, 25, 24, 28, 30, 27, 32, 35, 33**. Using Simple Exponential Smoothing with $\alpha = 0.4$ and $F_2 = A_1 = 20$, compute the forecasts for periods 3 through 10. Also compute the MAD. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution (a) — SES Theory and Smoothing Constant (7 Marks) **Definition:** Simple Exponential Smoothing (SES) is a recursive time-series forecasting technique that updates the forecast for the next period as a **weighted average** of the most recent actual observation and the previous forecast. **Governing Equation:** $$F_{t+1} = \alpha A_t + (1 - \alpha) F_t$$ where $0 \le \alpha \le 1$ is the **smoothing constant** and $F_t$ is the previous forecast. **Expanded Form (recursive substitution):** $$F_{t+1} = \alpha A_t + \alpha(1-\alpha) A_{t-1} + \alpha(1-\alpha)^2 A_{t-2} + \dots$$ The weights $\alpha, \alpha(1-\alpha), \alpha(1-\alpha)^2, \dots$ form a **geometric series summing to 1**, making SES equivalent to an **infinite weighted moving average** where older data points receive exponentially smaller weights. **Role of $\alpha$:** | $\alpha$ Value | Behavior | Best Use Case | |---|---|---| | $\alpha \to 0$ | Forecasts change very slowly; model is **stable and smooth**, resistant to noise | Stationary data with high random noise | | $\alpha \to 1$ | Forecasts closely follow the most recent actual; model is **highly responsive** | Data with rapid shifts, low noise | | $\alpha \approx 0.2$ to $0.3$ | Common default in practice | Most stable series | | $\alpha \approx 0.7$ to $0.9$ | Common for fast-moving sectors | Trending or volatile markets | **Key Limitation:** SES cannot capture trend or seasonality — it always levels out to a constant. #### Model Solution (b) — SES Numerical Computation (7 Marks) **Step 1: Initialize.** $F_2 = A_1 = 20$. **Step 2: Recursive update using $F_{t+1} = 0.4 A_t + 0.6 F_t$.** | $t$ | $A_t$ | $F_t$ (prev.) | $F_{t+1} = 0.4 A_t + 0.6 F_t$ | $\vert A_t - F_t \vert$ | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | 20 | — | $F_2 = 20$ (init) | — | | 2 | 22 | 20.00 | $F_3 = 0.4(22) + 0.6(20) = 20.80$ | 2.00 | | 3 | 25 | 20.80 | $F_4 = 0.4(25) + 0.6(20.8) = 22.48$ | 4.52 | | 4 | 24 | 22.48 | $F_5 = 0.4(24) + 0.6(22.48) = 23.09$ | 1.52 | | 5 | 28 | 23.09 | $F_6 = 0.4(28) + 0.6(23.09) = 25.05$ | 4.95 | | 6 | 30 | 25.05 | $F_7 = 0.4(30) + 0.6(25.05) = 27.03$ | 4.98 | | 7 | 27 | 27.03 | $F_8 = 0.4(27) + 0.6(27.03) = 27.02$ | 0.02 | | 8 | 32 | 27.02 | $F_9 = 0.4(32) + 0.6(27.02) = 29.01$ | 4.99 | | 9 | 35 | 29.01 | $F_{10} = 0.4(35) + 0.6(29.01) = 31.41$ | 5.99 | | 10 | 33 | 31.41 | — | 1.59 | **Step 3: Compute MAD over periods 2–10 (9 errors).** $$MAD = \frac{2.00 + 4.52 + 1.52 + 4.95 + 4.98 + 0.02 + 4.99 + 5.99 + 1.59}{9} = \frac{30.56}{9} \approx 3.40 \text{ units}$$ **Step 4: Forecast for Period 11 (optional managerial extension).** $$F_{11} = 0.4 A_{10} + 0.6 F_{10} = 0.4(33) + 0.6(31.41) = 13.20 + 18.85 = 32.05 \text{ units}$$ **Step 5: Managerial Insight.** The MAD of 3.40 is reasonable, but errors are **predominantly positive** ($A_t > F_t$), indicating an **upward trend**. SES is **lagging**. A shift to **Holt's method** or **Holt-Winters** (if seasonal) is recommended for long-term deployment. --- ## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Top 5 places where students lose marks in Module 2 forecasting questions:** > > 1. **Mixing up $F_t$ and $F_{t+1}$ indices.** The forecast $F_{t+1}$ is computed *after* observing $A_t$, not before. Always label your columns clearly: "$F_t$ (the forecast made for period $t$, which used data up to $t-1$)" vs. "$F_{t+1}$ (the next-period forecast). Examiners **will** deduct 1-2 marks for ambiguity. > > 2. **Forgetting to verify the weight constraint $\sum w_i = 1$** in Weighted Moving Average problems. Even if the math is right, omitting this check loses a step-mark. > > 3. **Using SMA on trending data** without a comment. If a series has an obvious trend and you use SMA, you *must* state the limitation and recommend a trend-aware model. Examiners test for **judgment**, not just arithmetic. > > 4. **Computing MAPE when any $A_t = 0$.** The formula divides by $A_t$, which is undefined at zero. State this assumption explicitly. > > 5. **Skipping managerial interpretation.** A calculation-only answer in a 14-mark question caps you at ~8 marks. Always close with **"From a managerial perspective, this suggests..."** and tie the result to a business decision. --- ## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for Module 2 — Forecasting** - **Forecasting** is the *systematic estimation of future values* using historical data and analytical models; it is the **primary input to all management planning functions**. - Forecasting is classified into **Qualitative** (judgment-based: Delphi, market research) and **Quantitative** (statistical: time-series and associative). - **Time series components** are Trend, Seasonal, Cyclical, and Irregular — decomposed as $Y_t = T_t + S_t + C_t + I_t$ (additive) or product form. - **Naive forecast** is $F_{t+1} = A_t$ — the universal benchmark. - **Simple Moving Average** smooths the last $n$ periods: $F_{t+1} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=t-n+1}^{t} A_i$. - **Weighted Moving Average** assigns differential weights, with the constraint $\sum w_i = 1$. - **Simple Exponential Smoothing (SES):** $F_{t+1} = \alpha A_t + (1 - \alpha)F_t$; $\alpha \to 0$ smooths, $\alpha \to 1$ reacts. - **Holt's method** (trend) uses two parameters $\alpha, \beta$ for **level and trend**: $F_{t+m} = L_t + mT_t$. - **Holt-Winters** adds seasonality with a third parameter $\gamma$, with seasonal index $S_t$. - **Linear Regression** models causality: $Y = a + bX$, with $b = \frac{\sum(X_i - \bar X)(Y_i - \bar Y)}{\sum(X_i - \bar X)^2}$. - **Error metrics:** MAD = mean of $\vert A_t - F_t \vert$, MSE = mean of $(A_t - F_t)^2$, RMSE = $\sqrt{MSE}$, MAPE = mean of $\frac{\vert A_t - F_t \vert}{\vert A_t \vert} \times 100$. - **MAPE** is the most business-friendly metric because it is scale-independent. - **Tracking Signal** = CFE / MAD; flag if $\vert TS \vert > 4$ (forecast bias). - Always **justify model choice** by identifying the data pattern first. - Always **end your answer with a managerial interpretation** — that is where 4-5 of the 14 marks lie.

topics.title: Data Patterns and Choice of Forecasting Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT522 - BUSINESS ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Data Patterns and Choice of Forecasting Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Data Patterns in Time Series:** A *time series* is a sequence of observations of a quantitative variable measured at successive points in time (or over successive intervals). The **data pattern** refers to the underlying structural behavior embedded in the data, which determines the appropriate forecasting technique to be employed.

In Business Analytics, every time-series dataset exhibits one or a **combination** of the following fundamental patterns:

| Pattern Type | Formal Definition |
|---|---|
| **Horizontal (Stationary) Pattern** | Data fluctuates around a constant mean $\bar{Y}$; no trend or seasonality. |
| **Trend Pattern ($T_t$)** | A long-term systematic increase or decrease in the data over time. |
| **Seasonal Pattern ($S_t$)** | Regular, periodic fluctuations tied to the **calendar** (day, week, month, quarter). |
| **Cyclical Pattern ($C_t$)** | Long-term oscillations (> 1 year) tied to **economic or business cycles**, not calendar. |
| **Random / Irregular Pattern ($I_t$)** | Unpredictable residual variation caused by chance or unforeseen events. |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Weather Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — Reading a River's Surface:** Imagine standing beside a river. The *water level* is your data. Sometimes the level is **steady** (horizontal pattern). Sometimes it **steadily rises over months** (trend). Sometimes it **swells every monsoon and recedes every summer** (seasonal). Sometimes it **drains in multi-year drought cycles** (cyclical). And occasionally a **sudden flood** disrupts everything (random). A skilled hydrologist (forecaster) must *first identify the dominant pattern* before choosing the right instrument — a tide gauge, a dam-release model, or a flood-warning system. Choosing the wrong tool leads to bad forecasts. The same logic governs Business Analytics.

### 1.3 Why Pattern Recognition is the Gateway to Forecast Selection

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Principle:** The **choice of forecasting technique is *not arbitrary*** — it is a direct consequence of the pattern embedded in historical data. KTU examiners expect students to *justify* the choice of technique based on the identified data pattern.

**Key Metrics Used in Forecasting (KTU Board Favourites):**
- **Mean Absolute Deviation (MAD)** $\rightarrow$ measures average magnitude of errors
- **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** $\rightarrow$ penalizes large errors quadratically
- **Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE)** $\rightarrow$ scale-independent error metric
- **Tracking Signal (TS)** $\rightarrow$ monitors forecast bias

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Deconstruction of the Six Core Data Patterns

#### 2.1.1 Horizontal (Stationary) Pattern
- **Logic:** The data series oscillates around a fixed mean $\bar{Y}$ with constant variance.
- **When observed:** Mature product sales with stable demand, mature inventory levels.
- **Recommended Models:** Simple Moving Average, Weighted Moving Average, Simple Exponential Smoothing.
- **Why?** Because no trend or seasonal correction is needed; only the *level* must be estimated.

#### 2.1.2 Trend Pattern
- **Logic:** A long-term monotonic (always increasing or always decreasing) component $T_t$ dominates the series.
- **Sub-types:** *Linear trend* (constant slope) and *non-linear trend* (polynomial, exponential, logarithmic).
- **When observed:** Technology adoption curves, population growth, inflation-indexed series.
- **Recommended Models:** Linear Trend Projection (Regression), Holt's Linear Exponential Smoothing, Double Exponential Smoothing.

#### 2.1.3 Seasonal Pattern
- **Logic:** Repetitive cycles locked to the **calendar**, with period $p$ (e.g., $p = 12$ for monthly data with yearly seasonality).
- **When observed:** Quarterly apparel sales, monthly electricity demand, weekly retail footfall.
- **Recommended Models:** Seasonal Naive, Holt-Winters (Triple Exponential Smoothing), Classical Decomposition, Seasonal Index Method.

#### 2.1.4 Cyclical Pattern
- **Logic:** Long wave-like undulations tied to **macro-economic forces** (boom → recession → recovery).
- **Period:** Variable and typically $> 1$ year; **not** fixed.
- **When observed:** GDP, industrial production, real estate prices.
- **Recommended Models:** Econometric models (ARIMA with cyclical regressors), leading-indicator regression; **Hard to forecast** because cycles are irregular.

#### 2.1.5 Random / Irregular Pattern
- **Logic:** Pure stochastic noise — the residual $I_t$ remaining after all patterns are extracted.
- **When observed:** All real data contains this; it is the irreducible uncertainty.
- **Recommended Models:** None for *prediction*; used to compute confidence intervals and standard errors.

#### 2.1.6 Combined (Multiplicative & Additive) Patterns
- **Additive Model:** $Y_t = T_t + S_t + C_t + I_t$
- **Multiplicative Model:** $Y_t = T_t \times S_t \times C_t \times I_t$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Hot Tip:** The **multiplicative** form is preferred when seasonal amplitude *grows with the trend* (e.g., ice-cream sales rising faster each summer). The **additive** form is preferred when seasonal amplitude is *constant* regardless of the level.

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL EXAM RULE:** All formulas below must be committed to memory. KTU 2024 valuation scripts award **partial marks** for correct formula citation even when numerical computation fails.

#### A. Error / Accuracy Metrics

| Metric | Formula | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| **Forecast Error** $e_t$ | $e_t = Y_t - F_t$ | Signed deviation at time $t$ |
| **Mean Absolute Deviation (MAD)** | $\text{MAD} = \dfrac{\sum_{t=1}^{n} \vert e_t \vert}{n}$ | Average *magnitude* of error |
| **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** | $\text{MSE} = \dfrac{\sum_{t=1}^{n} e_t^{\,2}}{n}$ | Penalizes large errors |
| **Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE)** | $\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum_{t=1}^{n} e_t^{\,2}}{n}}$ | Same units as $Y$ |
| **Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE)** | $\text{MAPE} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{\vert e_t \vert}{\vert Y_t \vert} \times 100$ | Scale-free % error |
| **Tracking Signal (TS)** | $\text{TS} = \dfrac{\sum e_t}{\text{MAD}}$ | Bias detector (signal: $\pm 4$ to $\pm 8$) |

#### B. Naive & Moving Average Models

| Model | Forecast Equation | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Naive** | $F_{t+1} = Y_t$ | Last observation is the forecast |
| **Simple Moving Average (SMA)** | $F_{t+1} = \dfrac{Y_t + Y_{t-1} + \cdots + Y_{t-n+1}}{n}$ | Equal weights, smooths noise |
| **Weighted Moving Average (WMA)** | $F_{t+1} = \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} w_i \, Y_{t-i}, \quad \sum w_i = 1$ | Recency-weighted |

#### C. Exponential Smoothing Family

| Model | Level | Trend | Seasonality | Forecast Equation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Simple Exp. Smoothing (SES)** | Yes | No | No | $F_{t+1} = \alpha Y_t + (1-\alpha)F_t$ |
| **Holt's Linear (DES)** | Yes | Yes | No | $L_t = \alpha Y_t + (1-\alpha)(L_{t-1}+b_{t-1})$ |
| **Holt-Winters (TES)** | Yes | Yes | Yes | Three coupled smoothing equations |

Where $0 \le \alpha \le 1$ is the **smoothing constant**.

#### D. Linear Trend Projection

$$\hat{Y}_t = b_0 + b_1 t$$

$$b_1 = \dfrac{n\sum tY - (\sum t)(\sum Y)}{n\sum t^2 - (\sum t)^2}$$

$$b_0 = \bar{Y} - b_1 \bar{t}$$

#### E. Seasonal Index (Additive vs. Multiplicative)

| Type | Seasonal Index Formula |
|---|---|
| **Additive** | $S_i = \dfrac{\sum (Y_{ij} - \bar{Y}_i)}{N / p}$ |
| **Multiplicative** | $S_i = \dfrac{\sum (Y_{ij} / \bar{Y}_i)}{N / p}$ |

Where $i$ = season index, $j$ = cycle number, $p$ = period length.

---

### 2.3 Decision Framework: Choosing the Right Forecasting Technique

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Master Decision Rule (KTU Examiners' Favourite Question):** Match the data pattern to the technique using this hierarchy.

**Step 1 — Identify dominant pattern(s):**
- Only **noise** around constant mean → *Stationary* → use **SES / SMA**.
- **Linear / non-linear trend** present → use **Linear Regression / Holt's Method**.
- **Seasonality** (calendar-locked) → use **Holt-Winters / Decomposition**.
- **Cyclicality** (non-calendar) → use **Econometric / ARIMA models**.
- **Trend + Seasonality** → use **Multiplicative Decomposition / Holt-Winters**.

**Step 2 — Evaluate exogenous constraints:**
- *Short horizon (≤ 3 periods)* → Naive / SMA.
- *Long horizon (> 12 periods)* → Trend models / Holt-Winters.
- *Limited data (n < 20)* → Naive / SMA / SES.
- *Abundant data (n > 50)* → Holt-Winters / ARIMA / Regression.

**Step 3 — Validate using accuracy metrics:**
- Compare **MAD, MSE, MAPE** across candidate models; choose lowest.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Simple Exponential Smoothing (SES) Forecast Equation

We begin with the *recursive smoothing* definition of the smoothed level $F_t$:

$$F_t = \alpha Y_{t-1} + (1-\alpha) F_{t-1}$$

**Step 1** — Expand the recursion one step backward to expose the structure:

$$F_t = \alpha Y_{t-1} + (1-\alpha) \bigl[\alpha Y_{t-2} + (1-\alpha) F_{t-2}\bigr]$$

**Step 2** — Distribute the $(1-\alpha)$ factor:

$$F_t = \alpha Y_{t-1} + \alpha(1-\alpha) Y_{t-2} + (1-\alpha)^2 F_{t-2}$$

**Step 3** — Repeat the unrolling process indefinitely:

$$F_t = \alpha \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} (1-\alpha)^{i-1} \, Y_{t-i}$$

**Step 4** — Verify that the weights form a *geometric series* summing to 1:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{\infty} (1-\alpha)^{i-1} = \dfrac{1}{1 - (1-\alpha)} = \dfrac{1}{\alpha}$$

$$\text{Total weight} = \alpha \cdot \dfrac{1}{\alpha} = 1 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 5 — Interpret:** The forecast $F_t$ is a **weighted average of all past observations**, with exponentially decaying weights. Recent observations carry weight $\alpha$; older observations carry progressively smaller weights $(1-\alpha)^{i-1}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** This derivation is the *most frequently asked* Part-A question in Business Analytics forecasting modules. Examiners award **1 mark for setup, 1 mark for the infinite expansion, 1 mark for the weight-sum verification**.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: Weighted Moving Average (WMA)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** Quarterly sales for a product are: 240, 260, 280, 300, 320 (units). Compute a 3-period WMA forecast for Period 6 using weights $w_1 = 0.5, w_2 = 0.3, w_3 = 0.2$ assigned to the most recent, second-most recent, and third-most recent periods respectively.

**Step 1 — Identify the three most recent observations:**

$$Y_5 = 320, \quad Y_4 = 300, \quad Y_3 = 280$$

**Step 2 — Apply the WMA formula:**

$$F_6 = w_1 Y_5 + w_2 Y_4 + w_3 Y_3$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$F_6 = (0.5)(320) + (0.3)(300) + (0.2)(280)$$

**Step 4 — Compute each term separately:**

$$F_6 = 160 + 90 + 56$$

**Step 5 — Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{F_6 = 306 \text{ units}}$$

**Step 6 — Check weight normalization:**

$$w_1 + w_2 + w_3 = 0.5 + 0.3 + 0.2 = 1.0 \quad \checkmark$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Pattern → Recommended Technique (HIGH-YIELD REVISION TABLE)

| # | Identified Data Pattern | Primary Recommended Technique | Backup Technique | Why This Choice? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Pure horizontal (stationary)** | Simple Exponential Smoothing | Simple Moving Average | No trend/seasonal correction needed; only level estimation |
| 2 | **Linear trend** | Linear Regression / Holt's Method | Double Moving Average | Captures constant slope of growth/decline |
| 3 | **Non-linear trend** | Polynomial Regression | Double Exponential Smoothing | Captures curvature |
| 4 | **Seasonal (additive)** | Holt-Winters (Additive) | Classical Decomposition | Seasonal swing is constant in absolute terms |
| 5 | **Seasonal (multiplicative)** | Holt-Winters (Multiplicative) | Multiplicative Decomposition | Seasonal swing grows with trend level |
| 6 | **Trend + Seasonality** | Holt-Winters (Triple Smoothing) | Regression with Seasonal Dummies | Combines level + slope + seasonal adjustments |
| 7 | **Cyclical (non-calendar)** | ARIMA / Econometric Models | Regression with lagged variables | Cycles are irregular; require autoregressive memory |
| 8 | **Highly noisy short series** | Naive / SMA | SES with low $\alpha$ | Limited data cannot support complex models |
| 9 | **Highly volatile financial series** | GARCH / ARIMA | Exponential Smoothing with high $\alpha$ | Variance clustering needs specialized models |
| 10 | **All patterns combined** | SARIMA / STL Decomposition | Multiple-model ensemble | Each component needs separate treatment |

---

### 3.4 Worked Example: MAPE Calculation (Exhaustive Step-by-Step)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Actual sales $Y_t$ and Forecast $F_t$ for 4 periods are: $(Y, F) = (100, 90), (120, 130), (110, 105), (130, 135)$. Calculate MAPE.

**Step 1 — Compute absolute errors $\vert e_t \vert$:**

$$\vert e_1 \vert = \vert 100 - 90 \vert = 10$$
$$\vert e_2 \vert = \vert 120 - 130 \vert = 10$$
$$\vert e_3 \vert = \vert 110 - 105 \vert = 5$$
$$\vert e_4 \vert = \vert 130 - 135 \vert = 5$$

**Step 2 — Compute percentage errors $\dfrac{\vert e_t \vert}{\vert Y_t \vert} \times 100$:**

$$p_1 = \dfrac{10}{100} \times 100 = 10\%$$
$$p_2 = \dfrac{10}{120} \times 100 = 8.33\%$$
$$p_3 = \dfrac{5}{110} \times 100 = 4.55\%$$
$$p_4 = \dfrac{5}{130} \times 100 = 3.85\%$$

**Step 3 — Average the percentage errors:**

$$\text{MAPE} = \dfrac{10 + 8.33 + 4.55 + 3.85}{4}$$

**Step 4 — Final computation:**

$$\boxed{\text{MAPE} = \dfrac{26.73}{4} = 6.68\%}$$

**Interpretation:** The forecast deviates, on average, by **6.68%** from actual sales — a generally accurate forecast.

---

### 3.5 Linear Trend Projection — Complete Derivation of Slope and Intercept

**Step 1 — Define the linear model:**

$$\hat{Y}_t = b_0 + b_1 t$$

**Step 2 — Minimize the sum of squared errors $S$:**

$$S = \sum_{t=1}^{n} (Y_t - \hat{Y}_t)^2 = \sum_{t=1}^{n} (Y_t - b_0 - b_1 t)^2$$

**Step 3 — Take partial derivatives and set to zero (Normal Equations):**

$$\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial b_0} = -2 \sum (Y_t - b_0 - b_1 t) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sum Y_t = n b_0 + b_1 \sum t$$

$$\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial b_1} = -2 \sum t (Y_t - b_0 - b_1 t) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sum t Y_t = b_0 \sum t + b_1 \sum t^2$$

**Step 4 — Solve the 2×2 system using Cramer's Rule (or substitution):**

$$b_1 = \dfrac{n \sum t Y_t - \left(\sum t\right)\left(\sum Y_t\right)}{n \sum t^2 - \left(\sum t\right)^2}$$

**Step 5 — Use the mean relationship to find $b_0$:**

$$b_0 = \bar{Y} - b_1 \bar{t}$$

**Step 6 — Forecast for future period $t = t^*$:**

$$F_{t^*} = b_0 + b_1 t^*$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Insight:** Examiners typically allocate **4 marks for the formula**, **2 marks for the calculation table** (showing $\sum t, \sum Y, \sum tY, \sum t^2$), and **2 marks for the final forecast**.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Pattern Identification Decision Tree (Mermaid Block)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[START: Time Series Data Y_t] --> nodeB{Is there a long-term monotonic direction?}
    nodeB -- NO --> nodeC{Does data repeat on a fixed calendar period?}
    nodeB -- YES --> nodeD{Trend Type?}
    nodeD -- Constant Slope --> nodeE[Linear Trend Pattern]
    nodeD -- Curved Slope --> nodeF[Non-linear Trend Pattern]
    nodeC -- YES --> nodeG{Does amplitude grow with level?}
    nodeC -- NO --> nodeH{Does data oscillate around constant mean?}
    nodeG -- YES --> nodeI[Multiplicative Seasonality]
    nodeG -- NO --> nodeJ[Additive Seasonality]
    nodeH -- YES --> nodeK[Horizontal Stationary Pattern]
    nodeH -- NO --> nodeL{Is there an unmodeled residual?}
    nodeL -- YES --> nodeM[Random Irregular Pattern]
    nodeL -- NO --> nodeN[Combined Pattern]
    nodeE --> recommend1[Use Linear Regression / Holt]
    nodeF --> recommend2[Use Polynomial / Double Exp Smoothing]
    nodeI --> recommend3[Use Holt-Winters Multiplicative]
    nodeJ --> recommend4[Use Holt-Winters Additive]
    nodeK --> recommend5[Use SES / SMA]
    nodeM --> recommend6[Compute Confidence Intervals]
    nodeN --> recommend7[Use SARIMA / STL Decomposition]
```

### 4.2 Forecasting Technique Selection Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    input[Input: Historical Data Y_1 Y_2 ... Y_n] --> pre[Preprocessing: Handle Missing Values and Outliers]
    pre --> plot[Visualize Time Plot]
    plot --> classify{Pattern Classifier}
    classify -- Stationary --> path1[Path A: Level-Only Models]
    classify -- Trend --> path2[Path B: Level + Slope Models]
    classify -- Seasonal --> path3[Path C: Level + Seasonality Models]
    classify -- Combined --> path4[Path D: Triple Smoothing or SARIMA]
    path1 --> model1[SES / SMA / WMA]
    path2 --> model2[Holt / Linear Regression / Polynomial]
    path3 --> model3[Seasonal Naive / Additive HW]
    path4 --> model4[Holt-Winters / SARIMA / STL]
    model1 --> eval[Evaluate MAD MSE MAPE]
    model2 --> eval
    model3 --> eval
    model4 --> eval
    eval --> select{Select Model with Lowest Error}
    select --> output[Output: Point Forecast and Confidence Interval]
```

### 4.3 Time-Series Decomposition Schematic (Multiplicative Case)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    raw[Observed Y_t] --> trendExt[Trend Extraction via Centered MA]
    trendExt --> detrender[Detrended Series: Y_t / T_t]
    detrender --> seasExt[Seasonal Index Computation: S_i]
    seasExt --> deseason[Deseasonalized Series]
    deseason --> irreg[Irregular Component: I_t = Y_t / T_t S_t]
    trendExt --> final[Final Decomposition: Y_t = T_t times S_t times I_t]
    seasExt --> final
    irreg --> final
    final --> forecast[Project T_t forward and Reapply S_i]
```

### 4.4 Exponential Smoothing Family Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root[Exponential Smoothing Family] --> ses[Simple Exp Smoothing: SES]
    root --> des[Double Exp Smoothing: Holt]
    root --> tes[Triple Exp Smoothing: Holt-Winters]
    ses --> sesUse[For Horizontal / Stationary Data]
    des --> desUse[For Trended Data Without Seasonality]
    tes --> tesAdd[Additive: Constant Amplitude]
    tes --> tesMul[Multiplicative: Growing Amplitude]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`** *List and briefly explain any three fundamental data patterns that can be identified in a time series. Why is pattern identification critical before selecting a forecasting technique?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Trend Pattern ($T_t$):** A long-term increase or decrease in the data (1 mark).
2. **Seasonal Pattern ($S_t$):** Periodic fluctuations tied to the calendar, e.g., monthly, quarterly (1 mark).
3. **Random / Irregular Pattern ($I_t$):** Unpredictable, unsystematic variation due to chance events (1 mark).

> Pattern identification is critical because the choice of forecasting technique (e.g., SES, Holt's, Holt-Winters) is *driven entirely* by the dominant pattern. A wrong choice leads to large forecast errors.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`** *Differentiate between additive and multiplicative time-series decomposition models. State the conditions under which each is preferred.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Additive | Multiplicative |
|---|---|---|
| Model form | $Y_t = T_t + S_t + I_t$ | $Y_t = T_t \times S_t \times I_t$ |
| Seasonal amplitude | Constant over time | Grows proportionally with trend |
| Preferred when | Seasonal swing is stable in absolute terms (e.g., ± 100 units always) | Seasonal swing grows as series grows (e.g., 10% always) |

(2 marks for differentiation, 1 mark for conditions).

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **Simple Exponential Smoothing (SES)** method in detail. Derive the recursive forecast equation $F_{t+1} = \alpha Y_t + (1-\alpha)F_t$ and discuss the role of the smoothing constant $\alpha$ in detail. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A retail store recorded the following monthly sales (in ₹000s) for the first 6 months of the year:

| Month | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Sales | 200 | 220 | 240 | 260 | 280 | 300 |

Using **Simple Linear Trend Projection**, forecast the sales for **Month 8**. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for Q-A(a):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (1 mark):** Simple Exponential Smoothing is a forecasting technique that produces a *weighted average* of all past observations, with weights decaying exponentially as observations get older. It is suitable for **stationary** time series with no trend or seasonality.

> **Recursive Formulation (2 marks):** Let $F_t$ be the forecast made at the end of period $t-1$ for period $t$. The smoothed level is updated as:
> $$F_{t+1} = \alpha Y_t + (1-\alpha)F_t$$
> where $Y_t$ is the actual value at time $t$, $F_t$ is the previous forecast, and $\alpha \in [0, 1]$ is the smoothing constant.

> **Derivation via Unfolding (2 marks):** Unrolling the recursion yields:
> $$F_{t+1} = \alpha \sum_{i=0}^{\infty} (1-\alpha)^{i} \, Y_{t-i}$$
> confirming that the **sum of weights equals 1** (geometric series): $\sum_{i=0}^{\infty} (1-\alpha)^{i} = 1/\alpha$, hence total weight $= 1$.

> **Role of $\alpha$ (2 marks):**
> - $\alpha \to 1$: Forecast reacts **rapidly** to recent changes (high responsiveness, low smoothing).
> - $\alpha \to 0$: Forecast is **heavily smoothed**, slow to react (high inertia, stable but lagged).
> - Optimal $\alpha$ is found by minimizing MSE/MAD over historical data.

---

**Model Solution for Q-A(b):**

**Step 1 — Tabulate the data with $t$ values:**

| $t$ | $Y_t$ | $tY_t$ | $t^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 200 | 200 | 1 |
| 2 | 220 | 440 | 4 |
| 3 | 240 | 720 | 9 |
| 4 | 260 | 1040 | 16 |
| 5 | 280 | 1400 | 25 |
| 6 | 300 | 1800 | 36 |
| **Sum** $\sum = 21$ | $\sum = 1500$ | $\sum = 5600$ | $\sum = 91$ |

**Step 2 — Compute means:**

$$\bar{t} = \dfrac{21}{6} = 3.5, \qquad \bar{Y} = \dfrac{1500}{6} = 250$$

**Step 3 — Compute slope $b_1$:**

$$b_1 = \dfrac{n \sum tY_t - (\sum t)(\sum Y_t)}{n \sum t^2 - (\sum t)^2} = \dfrac{6(5600) - (21)(1500)}{6(91) - (21)^2}$$

$$b_1 = \dfrac{33600 - 31500}{546 - 441} = \dfrac{2100}{105} = 20$$

**Step 4 — Compute intercept $b_0$:**

$$b_0 = \bar{Y} - b_1 \bar{t} = 250 - (20)(3.5) = 250 - 70 = 180$$

**Step 5 — Forecast equation:**

$$\hat{Y}_t = 180 + 20t$$

**Step 6 — Forecast for Month 8 ($t = 8$):**

$$\hat{Y}_8 = 180 + 20(8) = 180 + 160 = 340 \text{ (in ₹000s)}$$

$$\boxed{\hat{Y}_8 = \text{₹ } 3,40,000}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. Students frequently forget to compute the **$\sum tY_t$ column** correctly (1 mark lost).
> 2. Mis-typing the denominator as $n \sum t^2 - (\sum t)^2$ vs $(\sum t)^2$ — sign errors cost **1 mark**.
> 3. Forgetting to **state units** (₹000s) in the final answer (½ mark).
> 4. Not labelling $t$ values correctly (using 0–5 instead of 1–6) — this changes $\bar{t}$ and the result (1 mark).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Discuss the **choice of forecasting techniques** for the following data patterns: *(i)* purely horizontal stationary data, *(ii)* trended data without seasonality, *(iii)* data with both trend and seasonality. Justify each choice with the name of a specific model. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Quarterly demand data for a product over 2 years (8 quarters) is given below:

| Quarter | Q1 | Q2 | Q3 | Q4 | Q5 | Q6 | Q7 | Q8 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Demand | 100 | 120 | 140 | 160 | 130 | 150 | 170 | 190 |

Identify the dominant pattern, and compute the **3-quarter Simple Moving Average (SMA)** forecast for **Quarter 9**. Also compute the **MAD** for the in-sample forecasts. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for Q-B(a):**

> **(i) Horizontal Stationary Data — 2 marks:** Use **Simple Exponential Smoothing (SES)** or **Simple Moving Average (SMA)**. Justification: No trend/seasonality exists; only the **level** of the series needs to be estimated, and these models estimate a constant mean with no slope or seasonal parameters.

> **(ii) Trended Data Without Seasonality — 2 marks:** Use **Holt's Linear Exponential Smoothing (Double Exponential Smoothing)** or **Linear Trend Regression**. Justification: A trend component $b_t$ must be tracked in addition to the level $L_t$; Holt's method maintains coupled recursions for both, while linear regression explicitly fits a straight line.

> **(iii) Trend + Seasonality — 3 marks:** Use **Holt-Winters Triple Exponential Smoothing (TES)** or **SARIMA**. Justification: The model must capture three components: level $L_t$, trend $b_t$, and seasonal index $S_t$. Holt-Winters maintains three coupled recursions. If seasonality is multiplicative (amplitude grows with level), the **multiplicative variant** of Holt-Winters is used; otherwise, the **additive** variant suffices.

---

**Model Solution for Q-B(b):**

**Step 1 — Pattern Identification:** The data exhibits a *clear upward trend* (100 → 190), but no apparent seasonal swing (values rise monotonically). Therefore, the dominant pattern is **Linear Trend**.

**Step 2 — 3-Quarter SMA Forecasts:**

$$F_4 = \dfrac{Y_1 + Y_2 + Y_3}{3} = \dfrac{100 + 120 + 140}{3} = 120$$

$$F_5 = \dfrac{Y_2 + Y_3 + Y_4}{3} = \dfrac{120 + 140 + 160}{3} = 140$$

$$F_6 = \dfrac{Y_3 + Y_4 + Y_5}{3} = \dfrac{140 + 160 + 130}{3} = 143.33$$

$$F_7 = \dfrac{Y_4 + Y_5 + Y_6}{3} = \dfrac{160 + 130 + 150}{3} = 146.67$$

$$F_8 = \dfrac{Y_5 + Y_6 + Y_7}{3} = \dfrac{130 + 150 + 170}{3} = 150$$

$$F_9 = \dfrac{Y_6 + Y_7 + Y_8}{3} = \dfrac{150 + 170 + 190}{3} = 170$$

$$\boxed{F_9 = 170}$$

**Step 3 — Compute errors and MAD:**

| Quarter $t$ | $Y_t$ | $F_t$ | $e_t = Y_t - F_t$ | $\vert e_t \vert$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 4 | 160 | 120 | 40 | 40 |
| 5 | 130 | 140 | -10 | 10 |
| 6 | 150 | 143.33 | 6.67 | 6.67 |
| 7 | 170 | 146.67 | 23.33 | 23.33 |
| 8 | 190 | 150 | 40 | 40 |
| | | | **Sum** | 120 |

$$\text{MAD} = \dfrac{120}{5} = 24$$

$$\boxed{\text{MAD} = 24 \text{ units}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Q-B(b):**
> 1. Students often incorrectly use $F_4$ as the first in-sample forecast and forget that SMA cannot produce a forecast for $Q_1, Q_2, Q_3$ (insufficient lookback). Marks lost: 1.
> 2. The MAD denominator should be **number of forecasts made** (here = 5), not 8. Common error: dividing by 8. Marks lost: 1.
> 3. Rounding errors in $F_6$ and $F_7$ (writing 143 instead of 143.33) propagate to MAD — keep **2 decimal places** for partial credit.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Revision Notes — High Density**

- [ ] **Pattern First, Model Second:** Always *plot* the data and *classify* the pattern before selecting a model. The pattern dictates the model.
- [ ] **Six patterns to memorize:** Horizontal, Trend, Seasonal, Cyclical, Random, Combined.
- [ ] **Additive vs Multiplicative:** Additive → constant seasonal swing; Multiplicative → proportional seasonal swing.
- [ ] **SMA formula:** $F_{t+1} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=0}^{n-1} Y_{t-i}$. Watch for the lookback window $n$.
- [ ] **WMA formula:** Weights must sum to **1** — this is a *favourite KTU verification step*.
- [ ] **SES recursion:** $F_{t+1} = \alpha Y_t + (1-\alpha)F_t$. Derive by *unfolding* the recursion to show the geometric weighting.
- [ ] **Holt's Method** for trend; **Holt-Winters** for trend + seasonality.
- [ ] **Linear Trend:** $b_1 = \dfrac{n \sum tY - (\sum t)(\sum Y)}{n \sum t^2 - (\sum t)^2}$; $b_0 = \bar{Y} - b_1 \bar{t}$.
- [ ] **MAPE is scale-free**; MAD and MSE are *not* — useful for cross-product comparison.
- [ ] **Tracking Signal** between $\pm 4$ to $\pm 8$ indicates the forecast is **biased** and needs re-tuning.
- [ ] **Cyclical ≠ Seasonal:** Seasonal is calendar-based (fixed period); cyclical is macro-economic (irregular period).
- [ ] **Naive forecast** is the benchmark — never publish a sophisticated model that performs worse than the naive baseline.
- [ ] **Limited data ($n < 20$)** → prefer SES / SMA / Naive. Avoid complex models that overfit.
- [ ] **Long horizon forecasts** require trend + seasonality modelling; naive fails badly.
- [ ] **Always label units** in numerical answers (₹, units, kg, etc.) — KTU examiners deduct for missing units.
- [ ] **Don't confuse $\sum t^2$ with $(\sum t)^2$** — sign errors in the slope formula are the most common computational mistake.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 2 Notes — Data Patterns and Choice of Forecasting Techniques.**
> *Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme valuation standards and NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education framework.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Collection and Analysis in Forecasting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT522 - BUSINESS ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Data Collection and Analysis in Forecasting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Data Collection and Analysis in Forecasting** refers to the systematic process of gathering, cleaning, transforming, and statistically examining historical time-series or cross-sectional data so that reliable quantitative estimates of future business events (sales, demand, revenue, inventory, market size, etc.) can be generated.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (Course: PEADT522 – Business Analytics)**, this topic sits at the intersection of **descriptive analytics** and **predictive analytics**, forming the foundational input layer for every forecasting model discussed in the syllabus (naïve, moving averages, exponential smoothing, trend, seasonality, regression-based, and decomposition methods).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> Forecasting is meaningless without *clean, structured, and relevant* data. The syllabus explicitly requires students to identify **data sources, data types, sampling methods, and pre-processing techniques** before applying any forecasting model.

## 1.2 Key Terminology (KTU-Standard)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Time-Series Data** | Observations collected sequentially over equal time intervals (daily, weekly, monthly, yearly). |
| **Cross-Sectional Data** | Observations collected at a single point in time across multiple entities. |
| **Demand Signal** | The observable historical pattern of customer need for a product/service. |
| **Forecast Horizon** | The future time period for which a forecast is produced (short, medium, long). |
| **Forecasting Error** | The numerical deviation between actual demand $A_t$ and forecasted demand $F_t$. |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Weather Reporter

Imagine a weather reporter in Kerala predicting tomorrow's rain. Before stepping on camera, she does three things:

1. **Collects Data** — temperature, humidity, wind, cloud cover from the past 30 days.
2. **Cleans and Analyses** — removes faulty sensor readings, identifies seasonal monsoon patterns, computes averages.
3. **Forecasts** — uses those patterns to predict tomorrow's rain.

If her data is **noisy, incomplete, or biased**, even the most expensive forecasting model will give wrong predictions. **Business forecasting works exactly the same way** — the quality of the forecast is bounded by the quality of the data.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Takeaway:** *Garbage In = Garbage Out (GIGO)*. Forecasting models amplify, rather than fix, data quality problems. This is why Module 2 emphasizes data work **before** the model selection step.

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in Business Analytics

- **Decision Support:** Forecasts drive procurement, capacity planning, marketing spend, and HR planning.
- **Risk Reduction:** Reliable data analysis reduces stockouts and overstocking costs.
- **Strategic Planning:** Long-horizon forecasts inform capital investment and market entry decisions.
- **Performance Benchmarking:** Forecast error metrics quantify the analytical maturity of a firm.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five-Stage Data Collection & Analysis Pipeline

Forecasting does **not** start with choosing a model. The KTU syllabus enforces a **structured pipeline**:

### Stage 1 — Identify the Forecasting Objective
- **What** is being forecast? (units of product, ₹ revenue, number of customers)
- **Why** is the forecast needed? (inventory, finance, workforce)
- **Who** will consume the forecast? (operations manager, CFO)
- **How far** into the future? (forecast horizon $h$)
- **How often** must it be updated? (cadence)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** A common 2-mark question asks students to list these five "W/H" dimensions. Memorize them as **"What–Why–Who–How far–How often"**.

### Stage 2 — Identify Data Sources

| Source Category | Examples | Typical Use in Forecasting |
|---|---|---|
| **Internal / Transactional** | POS data, ERP sales logs, billing systems | Primary source for demand forecasting |
| **Internal / Operational** | Production counts, inventory levels, shipment data | Supply-side forecasting |
| **External / Macro** | GDP, inflation, weather, festivals | Causal / regression forecasting |
| **External / Market** | Competitor pricing, market share reports, Google Trends | Market response forecasting |
| **Survey-Based** | Customer intention surveys, expert Delphi panels | New-product forecasting |

### Stage 3 — Choose Data Collection Method

- **Primary Data Collection** — collected first-hand for the specific forecasting problem.
  - *Surveys*, *experiments*, *focus groups*, *observation*.
- **Secondary Data Collection** — already existing, reused for the problem.
  - *Published reports*, *historical ERP data*, *government databases*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Primary vs Secondary — KTU Definition:**
