**Sub-part (b) [7 Marks]:** Forecast deliveries for $X = 25$ vehicles. Construct a 95% prediction interval, given that $s_e = 0.82$ thousand and $\sum (X_i - \bar{X})^2 = 112$. The critical $t$-value at 95% confidence and 5 degrees of freedom is **2.571**.

---

### MODEL SOLUTION — QUESTION B

#### Part (a) — Model Development [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Required Sums (2 Marks)**

$$n = 7, \quad \sum X = 112, \quad \sum Y = 232$$

$$\sum X^2 = 100+144+196+256+324+400+484 = 1904$$

$$\sum XY = 220+312+420+528+666+800+968 = 3914$$

**Step 2: Means (1 Mark)**

$$\bar{X} = \frac{112}{7} = 16, \quad \bar{Y} = \frac{232}{7} = 33.143$$

**Step 3: Slope Coefficient (2 Marks)**

$$b_1 = \frac{7(3914) - (112)(232)}{7(1904) - (112)^2} = \frac{27398 - 25984}{13328 - 12544} = \frac{1414}{784} = 1.8036$$

**Step 4: Intercept (1 Mark)**

$$b_0 = 33.143 - (1.8036)(16) = 33.143 - 28.858 = 4.286$$

**Step 5: Business Interpretation (1 Mark)**

$$\hat{Y} = 4.286 + 1.8036 X$$

**Interpretation:** Each additional vehicle deployed generates an average of **1,803.6 additional deliveries per month**. This helps the operations manager evaluate fleet expansion ROI.

#### Part (b) — Forecast and Prediction Interval [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Point Forecast at $X_p = 25$ (1 Mark)**

$$\hat{Y}_p = 4.286 + 1.8036(25) = 4.286 + 45.090 = 49.376 \text{ thousand deliveries}$$

**Step 2: Standard Error of the Forecast (2 Marks)**

$$SE(\hat{Y}_p) = s_e \sqrt{1 + \frac{1}{n} + \frac{(X_p - \bar{X})^2}{\sum (X_i - \bar{X})^2}}$$

$$SE(\hat{Y}_p) = 0.82 \sqrt{1 + \frac{1}{7} + \frac{(25-16)^2}{112}}$$

$$SE(\hat{Y}_p) = 0.82 \sqrt{1 + 0.1429 + 0.7232} = 0.82 \sqrt{1.8661} = 0.82 \times 1.3661 = 1.1202$$

**Step 3: 95% Prediction Interval (2 Marks)**

$$PI = \hat{Y}_p \pm t_{0.025, n-2} \cdot SE(\hat{Y}_p)$$

$$PI = 49.376 \pm (2.571)(1.1202) = 49.376 \pm 2.880$$

$$PI = [46.496, \; 52.256] \text{ thousand deliveries}$$

**Step 4: Interpretation (2 Marks)**

With 95% confidence, deploying **25 vehicles** will result in monthly deliveries between approximately **46,496** and **52,256 units**. The relatively narrow interval (width ≈ 5,760 deliveries) reflects the model's high precision. Management can use this interval for capacity planning and SLA commitments to customers.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Forecasting with Regression:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to use the deviation form or raw form correctly** — Students often mix numerator and denominator of the slope formula, losing 2-3 marks immediately. Memorize: $b_1 = \dfrac{n\sum XY - \sum X \sum Y}{n\sum X^2 - (\sum X)^2}$.
>
> 2. **Not interpreting coefficients in business context** — A purely numerical answer without "every ₹1,000 increase in ad spend raises sales by ₹2.5 lakhs" loses the **interpretation marks** (typically 1-2 marks in 7-mark sub-parts).
>
> 3. **Confusing Prediction Interval (PI) with Confidence Interval (CI)** — The PI has the "+1" term inside the square root and is wider than CI. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
>
> 4. **Using wrong degrees of freedom** — For $s_e$, use $n-2$, not $n$. For prediction interval $t$-value, use $t_{\alpha/2, n-2}$.
>
> 5. **Skipping residual calculation** — Many KTU part-(b) questions require SSE and residuals. Skipping this step forfeits 2 marks.
>
> 6. **Mixing up $R^2$ and $r$** — $R^2$ is for regression (always non-negative); $r$ is for correlation (can be negative). The sign of $r$ matches the sign of $b_1$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Forecasting with Regression**

- ✅ **Forecasting with Regression** fits a mathematical line/curve to historical data to extrapolate future values of the dependent variable.
- ✅ The **population model** is $Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_i + \varepsilon_i$; the **sample model** (used for forecasting) is $\hat{Y}_i = b_0 + b_1 X_i$.
- ✅ **OLS criterion:** Minimize $SSE = \sum (Y_i - \hat{Y}_i)^2$.
- ✅ **Slope formula** (raw form): $b_1 = \dfrac{n\sum X_i Y_i - \sum X_i \sum Y_i}{n\sum X_i^2 - (\sum X_i)^2}$.
- ✅ **Intercept:** $b_0 = \bar{Y} - b_1 \bar{X}$.
- ✅ **$R^2$** ranges from **0 to 1**; higher is better; measures explained variation proportion.
- ✅ **$s_e$** = standard error of estimate = $\sqrt{\dfrac{SSE}{n-2}}$; measures typical prediction error.
- ✅ **Five CLRM assumptions:** Linearity, Independence, Homoscedasticity, Normality, No Multicollinearity.
- ✅ **Forecast point estimate:** Plug $X_p$ into the fitted equation.
- ✅ **Prediction Interval (95%):** $\hat{Y}_p \pm t_{0.025, n-2} \cdot SE(\hat{Y}_p)$ where $SE(\hat{Y}_p) = s_e \sqrt{1 + \frac{1}{n} + \frac{(X_p - \bar{X})^2}{\sum (X_i - \bar{X})^2}}$.
- ✅ **Multiple Linear Regression** generalizes SLR to $k$ predictors; use **Adjusted $R^2$** for model comparison.
- ✅ **Interpretation is mandatory** — always state what coefficients mean in business terms.
- ✅ **SLR vs MLR:** SLR has 1 predictor (2 parameters), MLR has $k$ predictors ($k+1$ parameters).
- ✅ **Key relation:** $SST = SSR + SSE$ — total variation decomposes into explained plus unexplained.
- ✅ **MLR coefficients** are obtained via matrix formula $\mathbf{b} = (\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{Y}$.
- ✅ **Outliers, non-linearity, autocorrelation (Durbin-Watson), and multicollinearity (VIF)** are common model-violation issues to check in exam answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Modelling with Tableau - Extracting Data into Tableau - Design Flow
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT522 - BUSINESS ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Data Modelling with Tableau - Extracting Data into Tableau - Design Flow

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 3 — Data Modelling with Tableau: Extracting Data & Design Flow

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Data Modelling in Tableau

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)**
> *Data Modelling* in Tableau is the structured process of **defining logical and physical relationships** between heterogeneous datasets using joins, blends, and unions, and exposing them through logical and physical layers so that the analyst can perform unified, contextual analysis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** A Tableau data model is built in **two tiers** — the *Logical Layer* (semantic/relationship view) and the *Physical Layer* (actual tables, joins, and columns). Modern Tableau (v2020.2+) uses a **semantic layer** with *Relationships* rather than traditional joins, enabling row-level multi-table analysis at varying aggregation levels.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition — The "Recipe Book" Analogy**

Imagine you are a chef preparing a complex dish. You don't want to run to 10 different markets for every ingredient. Instead, you maintain a **master recipe book** that lists:
- **Which ingredients (tables)** you need
- **How they relate to each other** (e.g., flour + water = dough)
- **How much of each (joins/blends)** to use

Tableau's data model is your *master recipe book*. **Source tables are the raw ingredients**, **joins are the recipes**, and the **semantic layer is your chef's intuition** that decides which ingredients to combine for which dish. With this book, you can whip up any number of "dishes" (visualizations) without ever reorganizing your pantry.

---

### 1.2 Extracting Data into Tableau

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition**
> A **Tableau Data Extract (.hyper file)** is a columnar, in-memory, compressed snapshot of an underlying data source that is optimized for fast analytical querying and offline exploration within Tableau.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Metric:** Tableau extracts use the **.hyper** engine (since v10.5) — capable of handling **billions of rows** with sub-second query response. Default compression ratios typically achieve **10:1 to 100:1** depending on data cardinality.

**Conceptual Analogy — The "Frozen Meal Prep" Analogy**

Think of data extraction like **meal-prepping on a Sunday**:
- You buy fresh ingredients from various sources throughout the week (live database connections)
- On Sunday, you **cook and freeze portions** (extract) so you can quickly heat and eat any day (analyze fast)
- The frozen meal **doesn't change** unless you re-cook (refresh) it
- It **saves time** and works even when the grocery store (source database) is closed

A Tableau extract is your **"frozen data meal"** — pre-processed, optimized, and ready to be reheated (analyzed) at any moment.

---

### 1.3 Design Flow in Tableau

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition**
> *Design Flow* in Tableau is the **end-to-end, sequential methodology** that an analyst follows — from requirement elicitation, data acquisition, modelling, transformation, visualization design, dashboard construction, and final storyboarding — to deliver actionable business intelligence.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 7-Stage Tableau Design Flow:** Connect → Prepare/Model → Visualize → Analyze → Format → Publish → Storyboard. KTU examiners expect students to articulate *why* each stage exists, not just *what* it does.

**Conceptual Analogy — The "Architect's Blueprint" Analogy**

A civil engineer doesn't start pouring concrete without a **blueprint**:
- First, she **understands the client's needs** (Connect)
- Then, she **studies the soil and terrain** (Prepare/Model)
- She drafts **initial sketches** (Visualize)
- She **stress-tests the structure** (Analyze)
- She adds **finishing touches** (Format)
- She **hands it to construction crew** (Publish)
- Finally, she shows the client a **3D walkthrough** (Storyboard)

Tableau's Design Flow is the **blueprint** of your analytical project — it ensures you don't end up with a beautiful dashboard that answers the *wrong question*.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Tableau Data Model

A modern Tableau data model comprises **three logical zones**:

1. **Semantic Layer (Relationships)** — declared using N-cardinality rules (Many-to-One, One-to-One, Many-to-Many)
2. **Logical Layer (Tables & Named Sets)** — joined or unioned tables
3. **Physical Layer (Columns & Data Types)** — actual columns with data types and aggregations

### Why Two Layers? — The "Why" Behind the Design

- **Physical Layer:** Maps 1:1 to the source schema (it is what it is)
- **Logical Layer:** Lets you create *aliases*, *custom fields*, and *folders* without touching source data
- **Semantic Layer:** Solves the *join explosion* problem of classic joins — Relationships automatically use the right aggregation context

---

## 2.2 Data Extraction — Why, How, and What

### Why Extract?
- **Performance:** Sub-second response on billion-row datasets
- **Offline access:** Work without a live connection
- **Data protection:** Mask sensitive source systems
- **Aggregation:** Pre-compute heavy aggregations

### How Extraction Works Internally

| Stage | Description | Tableau Component |
|-------|-------------|-------------------|
| **Stage 1: Connection** | Tableau initiates ODBC/JDBC handshake | Tableau Data Server |
| **Stage 2: Query Generation** | Tableau translates user filters into SQL-like queries | VizQL Engine |
| **Stage 3: Columnar Storage** | Data written to **.hyper** file in columnar format | Hyper Data Engine |
| **Stage 4: Compression** | Dictionary encoding, run-length encoding applied | Hyper Compression Module |
| **Stage 5: Indexing** | Min/Max indexes on each column built | Hyper Indexer |

### Extract File Lifecycle

| Phase | Operation | Frequency |
|-------|-----------|-----------|
| **Initial Extract** | Full snapshot of source | One-time |
| **Incremental Refresh** | Append new rows only (using `last_updated` column) | Scheduled |
| **Full Refresh** | Replace entire extract | Scheduled / Manual |
| **Append** | Add data from another extract to current | Manual |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Reference Tables

### Table 2.1 — Tableau Connection Types

| Connection Type | Live | Extract | Use Case |
|-----------------|:----:|:-------:|----------|
| **Relational DB (MySQL, PostgreSQL)** | ✅ | ✅ | Transactional analytics |
| **Cloud (Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift)** | ✅ | ✅ | Enterprise data warehousing |
| **Flat Files (CSV, Excel, JSON)** | ❌ | ✅ | Quick prototyping |
| **Web Data Connector (WDC)** | ✅ | ✅ | Custom API sources |
| **Salesforce / Google Analytics** | ✅ | ✅ | SaaS integrations |
| **Tableau Server / Published Data Source** | ✅ | ✅ | Enterprise reusability |

### Table 2.2 — Tableau File Format Reference

| Extension | Name | Contains | Use |
|-----------|------|----------|-----|
| **`.twb`** | Tableau Workbook | XML pointers to data + worksheets | Editable, lightweight |
| **`.twbx`** | Packaged Workbook | `.twb` + extract data | Portable, shareable |
| **`.tds`** | Tableau Data Source | Connection + metadata, no data | Reusable data definition |
| **`.tdsx`** | Packaged Data Source | `.tds` + data extract | Offline data sharing |
| **`.hyper`** | Extract File | Columnar, compressed data | High-performance analytics |

### Table 2.3 — Relationship vs Join vs Blend

| Feature | Relationship (v2020.2+) | Join | Blend |
|---------|:----------------------:|:----:|:-----:|
| **Cardinality** | Implicit, context-aware | Explicit (Inner, Left, Right, Full Outer) | Aggregated only |
| **Multi-grain** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (causes row duplication) | ✅ Yes (LOD-aware) |
| **Performance** | Optimized by engine | Pre-filtered | Slower for large data |
| **Data Sources** | Single source | Single source | Multiple sources |
| **KTU Weightage** | ⭐⭐⭐ High | ⭐⭐ Medium | ⭐⭐ Medium |

### Table 2.4 — Design Flow Stages (7-Stage Model)

| Stage | Activity | KTU-Mapped Outcome |
|-------|----------|--------------------|
| **1. Connect** | Identify & link to data source | CO1: Data acquisition |
| **2. Prepare** | Clean, pivot, union, filter | CO2: Data wrangling |
| **3. Model** | Define relationships, hierarchies, sets | CO2: Logical modelling |
| **4. Visualize** | Choose chart types, build worksheets | CO3: Visual design |
| **5. Analyze** | Apply calculations, parameters, forecasts | CO4: Analytical reasoning |
| **6. Format** | Refine aesthetics, tooltips, titles | CO3: Communication |
| **7. Publish & Story** | Build dashboards, publish to server | CO5: Business storytelling |

### Table 2.5 — Key Extract Configuration Parameters

| Parameter | Description | Default Value |
|-----------|-------------|:-------------:|
| **Number of Rows** | Rows to extract (All vs N) | All Rows |
| **Aggregation** | Aggregate visible dimensions | Not aggregated |
| **Filters** | Pre-filter data before extraction | None |
| **Hidden Columns** | Exclude unused columns | All included |
| **Data Encryption** | Password-protect extract | Disabled |
| **Incremental Refresh Column** | Identify new rows | None |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** In industry, the Design Flow + Extract strategy is the **backbone of self-service BI**. Companies like Airbnb, Netflix, and Tesco use exactly this pipeline to deliver **real-time executive dashboards** to thousands of decision-makers.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Workflow Analysis

## 3.1 Exhaustive Step-by-Step: Extracting Data into Tableau

This is the **canonical KTU-style procedural derivation** of building a Tableau extract.

### **STEP 1: Establish Source Connection**

Open Tableau Desktop → *Data → Connect to Data Source*

Select appropriate connector (Excel / SQL / Cloud / etc.)

**Logic:** Tableau loads the schema metadata (column names, types) without pulling actual rows yet.

> **Outcome:** A connection node appears in the *Data Source* tab.

---

### **STEP 2: Drag Tables to the Canvas**

Drag the relevant tables (e.g., `Orders`, `Customers`, `Products`) onto the canvas area.

**Logic:** Tableau creates a *physical layer* node for each table. The engine now knows which tables will participate in the model.

> **Outcome:** Multiple table boxes appear side by side on the canvas.

---

### **STEP 3: Define Relationships (or Joins)**

Drag a field from one table onto a matching field in another (e.g., `Customers.CustomerID → Orders.CustomerID`).

**Logic:** Tableau creates a semantic *Relationship* (v2020.2+) rather than a hard join. Relationships preserve the **native aggregation level** of each table.

> **Outcome:** A relationship line (dotted in Tableau 2020.2+, solid in newer versions) appears between tables.

---

### **STEP 4: Switch to Extract Mode**

In the Data Source tab, top-right corner, select **"Extract"** instead of "Live".

Click *Edit* to open the *Extract Data* dialog box.

**Logic:** Switching mode tells Tableau to materialize the data into a local `.hyper` file rather than issue live queries to the source.

> **Outcome:** A "Extract" radio button is now active.

---

### **STEP 5: Apply Extract Filters (Optional but Recommended)**

In the Extract dialog:

- **Filters tab:** Add dimension filters (e.g., `Order Date >= 2023-01-01`)
- **Aggregation:** Choose to aggregate data for visible dimensions
- **Number of Rows:** Choose *All Rows* or specify a limit (e.g., for sampling)

**Logic:** Pre-filtering reduces extract size, improves performance, and removes PII before storage.

> **Outcome:** Filter summary appears (e.g., "5 columns, 1 filter applied").

---

### **STEP 6: Add Hidden Columns / Configure Encryption**

Decide whether to:
- *Hide unused columns* (reduces extract size)
- *Encrypt with password* (regulatory compliance for sensitive data)

**Logic:** Column hiding removes non-essential fields; encryption protects data at rest.

> **Outcome:** A `.hyper` file begins writing to disk.

---

### **STEP 7: Save the Extract**

Click *Extract* button → Choose save location → The `.hyper` file is created.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical Insight:** A typical 1 GB source database often compresses to a **50–100 MB .hyper extract** due to columnar compression and dictionary encoding.

---

### **STEP 8: Configure Refresh Schedule**

Go to *Data → Extract → Refresh*:

- *Full Refresh:* Replaces the entire extract
- *Incremental Refresh:* Only adds rows where the *Last Update* column exceeds the last extracted value
- *Schedule:* Set automatic refresh (e.g., daily at 2 AM)

**Logic:** Incremental refresh is mathematically defined as:

$$
R_{\text{new}} = R_{\text{old}} \cup \{r_i \mid r_i \in S, \; r_i \text{.LastUpdate} > L_{\text{last}}\}
$$

Where:
- $R_{\text{new}}$ = new extract rows
- $R_{\text{old}}$ = previous extract rows
- $S$ = source dataset
- $L_{\text{last}}$ = timestamp of last successful extract

> **Outcome:** The extract stays current without rebuilding the whole file.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Decision Logic for Live vs Extract

| Decision Criterion | Use **Live** When | Use **Extract** When |
|--------------------|-------------------|----------------------|
| **Data Volume** | Small to medium (< 1M rows) | Large (> 1M rows) |
| **Refresh Need** | Real-time required | Daily/Weekly acceptable |
| **Network** | Stable, low latency | Unstable / offline work |
| **Security** | Source has row-level security | Data needs to be shared offline |
| **Performance** | Database is fast & indexed | Database is slow / legacy |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Examiners frequently test the **incremental refresh formula**. Memorize the row-set notation: $R_{\text{new}} = R_{\text{old}} \cup \{r_i \mid r_i \in S \wedge r_i \text{.TS} > L_{\text{last}}\}$.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Tableau Data Model — Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    srcA["Source A: Orders Table"]:::src
    srcB["Source B: Customers Table"]:::src
    srcC["Source C: Products Table"]:::src
    
    phy["PHYSICAL LAYER\nColumns, Data Types, Joins"]:::phy
    log["LOGICAL LAYER\nAliases, Folders, Sets"]:::log
    sem["SEMANTIC LAYER\nRelationships, N-to-1 Rules"]:::sem
    
    viz["Worksheets & Dashboards"]:::viz
    
    srcA --> phy
    srcB --> phy
    srcC --> phy
    phy --> log
    log --> sem
    sem --> viz
    
    classDef src fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,color:#000
    classDef phy fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    classDef log fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c,color:#000
    classDef sem fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    classDef viz fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#880e4f,color:#000
```

> **Reading Guide:** Data flows upward — from raw sources through the three modelling layers, ultimately feeding the visualization layer. Each layer progressively adds *semantic meaning* to raw data.

---

## 4.2 Data Extraction Flow — Process Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Step1: Connect to Source"]:::step --> B["Step2: Select Tables and Fields"]:::step
    B --> C["Step3: Apply Extract Filters"]:::step
    C --> D["Step4: Choose Aggregation Mode"]:::step
    D --> E["Step5: Generate .hyper File"]:::file
    E --> F["Step6: Configure Refresh Schedule"]:::step
    F --> G["Step7: Publish to Tableau Server"]:::final
    
    E -.-> E1["Columnar Compression\nDictionary Encoding\nMin/Max Indexing"]:::tech
    
    classDef step fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#1565c0,color:#000
    classDef file fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    classDef final fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000
    classDef tech fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#ad1457,color:#000
```

---

## 4.3 Complete Tableau Design Flow — End-to-End

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    s1["Stage1: Connect\nIdentify Data Sources"]:::s --> s2["Stage2: Prepare\nClean, Pivot, Filter"]:::s
    s2 --> s3["Stage3: Model\nBuild Relationships"]:::s
    s3 --> s4["Stage4: Visualize\nSelect Chart Types"]:::s
    s4 --> s5["Stage5: Analyze\nCalculations, Parameters"]:::s
    s5 --> s6["Stage6: Format\nAesthetics, Tooltips"]:::s
    s6 --> s7["Stage7: Publish and Story\nDashboard, Narrative"]:::s
    
    s7 --> outcome["Business Decision Made"]:::out
    
    s1 -.feedback.- s3
    s4 -.feedback.- s2
    s5 -.feedback.- s3
    
    classDef s fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#311b92,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
```

> **Reading Guide:** Solid arrows show the *forward flow*; dashed arrows show *feedback loops* — a hallmark of agile analytics where insights from one stage often require revisiting earlier decisions.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between Tableau Live Connection and Data Extract. State any three scenarios where an extract is preferred over a live connection.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Live Connection | Data Extract |
|--------|---------------|--------------|
| **Storage** | Query goes to source in real time | Local `.hyper` file snapshot |
| **Performance** | Depends on source DB performance | Optimized for sub-second queries |
| **Network Dependency** | Required always | Not required after extract |

**Three scenarios where Extract is preferred (1 mark each):**

1. **Large datasets** (millions of rows) where source DB is slow
2. **Offline analysis** when network is unstable or unavailable
3. **Data sharing** with stakeholders who lack source access rights

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Explain the concept of a "Relationship" in Tableau's data model. How is it different from a traditional Join?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Relationship** is a semantic-layer declaration in Tableau (v2020.2+) that defines how two tables relate *contextually*, without merging them at the row level. Unlike a Join, a Relationship:

- Does **not** force a single row-level cardinality (avoids *join explosion*)
- **Preserves** the native aggregation level of each table
- Allows **multi-grain** analysis — each table can aggregate at its own level
- Is **performance-optimized** by the Tableau engine, generating queries on-the-fly

A **Join**, in contrast, physically merges rows into a single output table, which can cause:
- Row duplication (especially with Many-to-Many joins)
- Loss of original aggregation context
- Need for LOD (Level of Detail) expressions to recover original grain

---

## 5.2 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **OPTION A — Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**Q.A. (a)** *Explain the step-by-step process of creating a Tableau Data Extract from a relational database. Include configuration parameters and the formula for incremental refresh. (7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Connect to Source (1 mark)**
- Open Tableau Desktop → Connect → select RDBMS (e.g., MySQL)
- Enter credentials, test connection

**Step 2: Drag Tables to Canvas (0.5 mark)**
- Drag `Orders` and `Customers` tables to the canvas

**Step 3: Define Relationship (0.5 mark)**
- Link `Orders.CustomerID` with `Customers.CustomerID`
- Set cardinality (assumed Many-to-One by default)

**Step 4: Switch to Extract Mode (0.5 mark)**
- Click "Extract" radio button in top-right
- Click "Edit" to open Extract dialog

**Step 5: Configure Parameters (2 marks)**

| Parameter | Recommended Setting |
|-----------|---------------------|
| Number of Rows | All Rows |
| Aggregation | Not aggregated (for full-fidelity) |
| Filters | `Order Date >= 2023-01-01` |
| Hidden Columns | Hide unnecessary audit fields |
| Encryption | Password-protect for PII compliance |

**Step 6: Save and Refresh Setup (1 mark)**
- Save `.hyper` file
- Configure *Incremental Refresh* using `LastModified` column

**Step 7: Incremental Refresh Formula (1.5 marks)**

$$
R_{\text{new}} = R_{\text{old}} \cup \left\{ r_i \;\middle|\; r_i \in S, \; r_i\text{.LastModified} > L_{\text{last}} \right\}
$$

Where $L_{\text{last}}$ is the maximum timestamp stored in the previous extract, $S$ is the source table, and $R_{\text{old}}$ is the previously extracted row set. Only rows newer than the last refresh are appended.

---

**Q.A. (b)** *Describe the 7-stage Design Flow of Tableau. For each stage, state the primary activity and one KTU Course Outcome it maps to. (7 marks)*

**Model Answer (1 mark per stage):**

| Stage | Primary Activity | KTU CO Mapping |
|-------|------------------|----------------|
| **1. Connect** | Establish data source connection | CO1: Data acquisition |
| **2. Prepare** | Clean, filter, pivot, union data | CO2: Data preparation |
| **3. Model** | Define relationships, hierarchies | CO2: Data modelling |
| **4. Visualize** | Build worksheets, select chart types | CO3: Visual communication |
| **5. Analyze** | Apply calculations, parameters, forecasting | CO4: Analytical reasoning |
| **6. Format** | Refine tooltips, titles, color, fonts | CO3: Design aesthetics |
| **7. Publish & Story** | Assemble dashboards, publish to server | CO5: Business storytelling |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The Design Flow is iterative — insights from *Analyze* often trigger re-preparation in *Stage 2*, making it a feedback-driven, agile methodology rather than a linear waterfall.

---

### **OPTION B — Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q.B. (a)** *Compare and contrast Relationship, Join, and Blend in Tableau. Present your answer in a tabular format with at least 5 differentiating parameters. (7 marks)*

**Model Answer (5 parameters × 1.2 marks each = 6 marks + 1 mark for conclusion):**

| Parameter | Relationship | Join | Blend |
|-----------|:------------:|:----:|:-----:|
| **Cardinality** | Implicit, auto-detected | Explicit (Inner/Left/Right/Full) | Aggregated linkage |
| **Multi-Grain Support** | ✅ Yes (native) | ❌ No (causes duplication) | ✅ Yes (via LOD) |
| **Data Source Scope** | Single source | Single source | Multiple sources |
| **Aggregation Level** | Preserved per table | Forced into one | Source-aggregated |
| **Performance** | Engine-optimized | Pre-merged, fast for small data | Slower, query-time joins |
| **Created At** | Semantic Layer | Physical Layer | Data Window |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Relationships are the *modern* Tableau recommendation for multi-table modeling. Joins remain useful for tightly coupled transactional tables. Blends solve the *cross-database* problem where joins are infeasible.

---

**Q.B. (b)** *An e-commerce company has 50 million rows of transaction data in a MySQL database. Network latency is 200 ms per query. Recommend whether to use Live or Extract. Justify with quantitative analysis. (7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- Rows: $N = 50{,}000{,}000$
- Latency: $L = 200$ ms/query
- Typical dashboard: $Q = 15$ queries (filters + aggregations)

**Step 1: Live Connection Analysis (2 marks)**

$$
T_{\text{live}} = Q \times L = 15 \times 200 \text{ ms} = 3000 \text{ ms} = 3 \text{ seconds}
$$

Plus MySQL query execution time (varies, typically 1–10 seconds for 50M rows).

$$
T_{\text{live, total}} \approx 3 \text{ s (latency)} + 8 \text{ s (execution)} = 11 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 2: Extract Analysis (2 marks)**

- After extract, queries run against in-memory `.hyper` engine
- Average query time on extract: $T_{\text{extract}} \approx 200$ ms
- Latency becomes irrelevant (no network calls)

$$
T_{\text{extract, total}} \approx Q \times T_{\text{extract}} = 15 \times 200 \text{ ms} = 3 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 3: Compression Estimate (1.5 marks)**

$$
\text{Extract Size} \approx \frac{\text{Source Size}}{\text{Compression Ratio}} = \frac{S}{10 \text{ to } 100}
$$

For 50M rows of order data (~5 GB raw), expected extract size is **50 MB – 500 MB**, easily handled by modern workstations.

**Step 4: Recommendation (1.5 marks)**

✅ **Recommendation: Use Extract**

**Justification:**
- Performance gain: $\frac{11 \text{ s}}{3 \text{ s}} \approx 3.7\times$ faster
- Offline capability enables executive review without network
- Incremental refresh keeps extract current without full rebuild
- Network cost: $0$ per query (vs 200 ms × N queries/day in Live)

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall**
> - **Do NOT** mix up *Relationship* with *Join* — this is the #1 source of lost marks. Remember: **Relationship = Semantic Layer**, **Join = Physical Layer**.
> - **Do NOT** state "Extract is always faster" — it depends on *data volume*, *network latency*, and *refresh frequency*. Always justify with a calculation.
> - **Do NOT** forget the **incremental refresh formula** in extract questions. Examiners allocate 1.5–2 marks specifically for $R_{\text{new}} = R_{\text{old}} \cup \{r_i \mid r_i\text{.TS} > L_{\text{last}}\}$.
> - **Do NOT** confuse `.twb` (workbook) with `.twbx` (packaged workbook). The `x` suffix means **data is included**.
> - **Do NOT** answer 14-mark questions in bullet-only format. KTU 2024 scheme requires **explanation + table/diagram + conclusion** structure for full marks.

---

## 5.3 🎯 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Read this 5 minutes before the exam):**

- ✅ Tableau data model has **3 layers**: Physical → Logical → Semantic
- ✅ **Relationship** (v2020.2+) replaced classical Joins for multi-table modeling
- ✅ **Extract = `.hyper` file** (columnar, compressed, in-memory)
- ✅ **Live = real-time query** to source database
- ✅ **7-Stage Design Flow:** Connect → Prepare → Model → Visualize → Analyze → Format → Publish
- ✅ **File types:** `.twb` (workbook), `.twbx` (packaged), `.tds` (data source), `.tdsx` (packaged DS), `.hyper` (extract)
- ✅ **Incremental Refresh Formula:** $R_{\text{new}} = R_{\text{old}} \cup \{r_i \mid r_i \in S, \; r_i\text{.TS} > L_{\text{last}}\}$
- ✅ **Compression ratio** of extracts: typically **10:1 to 100:1**
- ✅ **Hyper Engine** can handle **billions of rows** with sub-second response
- ✅ **Refresh types:** Full Refresh, Incremental Refresh, Append
- ✅ **Data Source scope:** Relationship/Join = single source, Blend = multiple sources
- ✅ **Aggregation in extract** can be *visible dimensions only* or *full data*
- ✅ **Encryption** is optional but recommended for PII compliance
- ✅ **KTU expects** process diagrams (mermaid/flowchart) in 14-mark answers

---

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Data Modelling with Tableau: Extracting Data & Design Flow** ✅
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: File Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT522 - BUSINESS ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** File Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Business Analytics** and Data Engineering, a **File Type** (also referred to as a **File Format** or **Data Format**) is a standardized way in which information is encoded and stored on a digital medium, enabling systematic retrieval, processing, and interpretation by software systems, analytics platforms, and downstream consumers. File types dictate how bytes are organized, how delimiters separate records, how metadata is described, and how data schemas are enforced.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** A file type is defined by two components — (1) the **MIME type / extension** (e.g., `.csv`, `.json`, `.parquet`) and (2) the **internal structural schema** (e.g., row-based, columnar, hierarchical, binary). Both are mandatory identification criteria in KTU examination contexts.

### 1.2 The Three Universal Classes of Data Files

KTU 2024 Scheme categorizes file types under three primary data paradigms:

| Class | Definition | Typical File Types | Storage Strategy |
|-------|-----------|--------------------|-----------------|
| **Structured** | Data with a rigid, predefined schema (rows & columns) | CSV, TSV, XLSX, Parquet, ORC, Avro, RDBMS dumps | Row/Columnar warehouses |
| **Semi-Structured** | Data with partial schema, often hierarchical or tag-based | JSON, XML, YAML, HTML, EDI | Document stores, Data Lakes |
| **Unstructured** | Data without inherent schema (binary or free-form) | TXT, MP4, JPG, WAV, PDF, LOG files | Object stores (S3, HDFS) |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Filing Cabinet" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box — The Office Cabinet Analogy**
> Think of your computer as a giant **filing cabinet**:
> - A **CSV file** is like a **printed spreadsheet** — neat rows and columns, easy for anyone to read, but takes up a lot of paper.
> - A **JSON file** is like a **nested folder system** — folders inside folders, flexible but harder to flatten.
> - A **Parquet file** is like a **compressed archive** — you cannot read it with naked eyes, but it stores the same spreadsheet in a fraction of the space, optimized for analytics.
> - A **text/log file** is like a **diary** — free-flowing sentences with no rules, but full of valuable signals.

This analogy maps directly to the *3 Vs* of Big Data (Volume, Velocity, Variety) that KTU emphasizes.

### 1.4 Why File Types Matter in Business Analytics

- **Interoperability:** Different analytics tools (Python, R, Power BI, Tableau, Spark) accept different file types.
- **Storage Efficiency:** Columnar formats (Parquet, ORC) can yield **up to 75% storage compression**.
- **Query Performance:** Choosing the right file format can accelerate queries by **10x–100x**.
- **Data Integrity:** Some formats enforce schemas (Avro, Parquet), preventing "dirty data" corruption.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In ESE questions, candidates are often asked to *justify the selection* of a file type for a given business scenario (e.g., "Which format is best for streaming IoT data?"). Selection rationale carries full marks — not just naming the format.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of File Types — Operational Breakdown

#### 2.1.1 Row-Oriented File Formats
- Data is stored **record-by-record**.
- Best for: **OLTP (Online Transaction Processing)** workloads.
- Examples: CSV, TSV, Avro, SequenceFile.
- **Operational Logic:** When a query asks for one full row, reading is sequential and fast. But when the query asks for one column from a billion rows, the system must read the *entire dataset* and discard irrelevant fields.

#### 2.1.2 Column-Oriented File Formats
- Data is stored **column-by-column** within row groups.
- Best for: **OLAP (Online Analytical Processing)** workloads.
- Examples: **Apache Parquet**, **Apache ORC (Optimized Row Columnar)**.
- **Operational Logic:** The system reads *only* the columns referenced in the query, dramatically reducing I/O.

#### 2.1.3 Hierarchical File Formats
- Data is nested using tags or key-value pairs.
- Best for: **APIs, Web Services, Configuration files**.
- Examples: JSON, XML, YAML.
- **Operational Logic:** Schemas are self-describing and flexible, but parsing is more CPU-intensive than flat files.

#### 2.1.4 Binary File Formats
- Data is stored in machine-readable, non-human-readable form.
- Best for: **High-performance, type-safe operations**.
- Examples: Avro, Parquet, ORC, Protocol Buffers.
- **Operational Logic:** Smaller size, faster I/O, but requires schema-aware readers.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates the **high-yield, exam-ready formulas and metrics** for File Types. Memorize this — it appears in nearly every KTU Module 3 question paper.

| Metric / Concept | Formula or Rule | Application Context |
|------------------|-----------------|---------------------|
| **Compression Ratio (CR)** | $CR = \dfrac{\text{Original Size}}{\text{Compressed Size}}$ | Comparing Parquet vs CSV |
| **Space Savings %** | $S\% = \left(1 - \dfrac{\text{Compressed}}{\text{Original}}\right) \times 100$ | Storage cost optimization |
| **Read Time Reduction** | $T_{new} = T_{old} \times \left(1 - \dfrac{\text{Columns Read}}{\text{Total Columns}}\right)$ | Columnar format benefit |
| **Schema Evolution Cost** | $C_{evolve} = \alpha \cdot B_{breaks} + \beta \cdot B_{adds}$ | Where $B$ = backward/forward breaks, $\alpha, \beta$ = weights |
| **I/O Efficiency (Columnar)** | $E_{col} = \dfrac{1}{N_c} \sum_{i=1}^{N_c} \mathbb{1}[\text{col}_i \in \text{Query}]$ | Probability of column being queried |
| **Throughput (rows/sec)** | $\Theta = \dfrac{R}{T_{elapsed}}$ | Benchmarking file ingestion pipelines |
| **JSON Nesting Depth Penalty** | $P_{depth} = O(d)$ where $d$ = nesting depth | Parsing complexity bound |
| **Parquet Row Group Size** | $RG \in [128 \text{ MB}, 1 \text{ GB}]$ | Recommended Spark/Hive tuning |
| **CSV Delimiter Rule** | $\text{Field} \in \text{Line} \iff \text{char} \neq D$ | $D$ = delimiter (comma, tab, pipe) |
| **Big Data Format Selection Heuristic** | $\text{Format} = \begin{cases} \text{CSV} & \text{if data} < 1 \text{ GB, schema stable} \\ \text{Parquet} & \text{if analytics-heavy, column reads} \\ \text{AVRO} & \text{if streaming, schema evolution} \\ \text{ORC} & \text{if Hive/Hadoop stack} \\ \text{JSON} & \text{if API/web, semi-structured} \end{cases}$ | KTU Decision Matrix |

### 2.3 The 'Why' Behind the Engineering

- **CSV exists for human readability** — it sacrifices performance for transparency.
- **Parquet exists for analytical speed** — it sacrifices human readability for compression and columnar pruning.
- **Avro exists for streaming and schema evolution** — it embeds the schema in the file header.
- **ORC exists for the Hadoop ecosystem** — it is the Hive-native equivalent of Parquet with built-in indexes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** Companies like **Netflix** use Parquet on Amazon S3 to power their analytics on petabytes of viewing data. **Uber** uses Avro for Kafka-based event streaming. **Banks** use fixed-width flat files for legacy mainframe interchanges.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Storage Savings Using Columnar Compression

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
A retail analytics team stores **1 TB (Terabyte)** of sales transaction data in CSV format. Each row contains 20 columns. An analyst typically queries only **4 specific columns** (Product_ID, Region, Revenue, Date). After migrating to Parquet, the file size shrinks to **250 GB** and the query engine reads only the queried columns. Compute: (a) Compression Ratio, (b) Space Savings %, and (c) the theoretical Read Time Reduction.

---

#### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Identify the Given Variables

Let:
- $S_{orig} = 1 \text{ TB} = 1024 \text{ GB}$ (original CSV size)
- $S_{comp} = 250 \text{ GB}$ (compressed Parquet size)
- $N_{total} = 20$ (total columns)
- $N_{query} = 4$ (queried columns)

---

#### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Compute the Compression Ratio (CR)

The compression ratio is defined as the ratio of the original size to the compressed size.

$$
CR = \frac{S_{orig}}{S_{comp}}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
CR = \frac{1024 \text{ GB}}{250 \text{ GB}}
$$

$$
CR = 4.096
$$

**Interpretation:** Parquet compresses the data to roughly **1/4th** of its original size. The data is **~4x more compact** than CSV.

---

#### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Compute the Space Savings Percentage (S%)

The percentage of storage space saved by the migration is:

$$
S\% = \left(1 - \frac{S_{comp}}{S_{orig}}\right) \times 100
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
S\% = \left(1 - \frac{250 \text{ GB}}{1024 \text{ GB}}\right) \times 100
$$

$$
S\% = \left(1 - 0.2441\right) \times 100
$$

$$
S\% = 0.7559 \times 100
$$

$$
S\% \approx 75.59\%
$$

**Interpretation:** Migration to Parquet saves approximately **75.59%** of the original storage cost. For a cloud bill, this is a massive operational saving.

---

#### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Compute the Theoretical Read Time Reduction

For columnar formats, only the queried columns are read from disk. The reduction in I/O is proportional to the fraction of columns *not* read.

$$
T_{reduction} = \frac{N_{query}}{N_{total}} \times T_{old}
$$

Here, $T_{old}$ is the time taken to read the full CSV. The new time $T_{new}$ reads only 4 of 20 columns.

$$
T_{new} = T_{old} \times \frac{N_{query}}{N_{total}}
$$

$$
T_{new} = T_{old} \times \frac{4}{20}
$$

$$
T_{new} = T_{old} \times 0.20
$$

Thus, the *reduction* in read time is:

$$
\text{Reduction}\% = \left(1 - \frac{N_{query}}{N_{total}}\right) \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Reduction}\% = \left(1 - \frac{4}{20}\right) \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Reduction}\% = (1 - 0.20) \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Reduction}\% = 80\%
$$

**Interpretation:** Columnar storage theoretically reduces the disk I/O required for the query by **80%**, since only 4 of 20 columns must be touched. Combined with compression, the actual speedup is even greater.

---

#### 3.1.5 Step 5 — Final Analytical Summary Table

| Parameter | Symbol | Computed Value | Engineering Insight |
|-----------|--------|----------------|---------------------|
| Original Size | $S_{orig}$ | **1024 GB** | Baseline |
| Compressed Size | $S_{comp}$ | **250 GB** | Post-migration |
| Compression Ratio | $CR$ | **4.096** | Parquet is ~4x denser |
| Space Savings | $S\%$ | **75.59%** | Direct cloud cost cut |
| Read Time Reduction | $T_{red}$ | **80%** | I/O efficiency gain |

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: File Type Selection for Business Scenarios

The following decision matrix is a **high-yield KTU exam artifact** — students are routinely asked to justify a choice from this matrix.

| Business Scenario | Recommended Format | Justification |
|-------------------|--------------------|---------------|
| Daily sales export to Excel for managers | **XLSX / CSV** | Human-readable, no technical skill needed |
| Streaming clickstream from a mobile app | **Avro / JSON** | Schema evolution, Kafka compatibility |
| Historical analytics on 5 years of transactions | **Parquet** | Columnar, compressed, query-optimized |
| Web API payload between microservices | **JSON** | Lightweight, native to JavaScript/Node.js |
| Legacy banking data exchange (fixed-width) | **Fixed-width TXT** | Mainframe compatibility, predictable parsing |
| Hadoop/Hive data warehouse storage | **ORC** | Native Hive format, ACID support, indexes |
| Configuration files for DevOps pipelines | **YAML / JSON** | Human-readable, supports comments (YAML) |
| Machine Learning feature store | **Parquet** | Fast column access for feature engineering |
| Real-time log ingestion into ELK stack | **JSON / NDJSON** | Logstash native, line-delimited for streaming |
| Image and video archival for analytics | **Binary (JPG, MP4)** | Unstructured, loss-tolerant compression |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** Notice the pattern — *readable formats* (CSV, XLSX) are chosen for **human consumption**, while *binary/columnar formats* (Parquet, ORC, Avro) are chosen for **machine consumption**. This is the foundational decision rule in KTU's analytics architecture.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**  
> *Define the term "File Type" as used in Business Analytics. Differentiate between structured and semi-structured data with one example each.* (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

A **File Type** in Business Analytics refers to the standardized format in which data is encoded, stored, and exchanged between systems, identified by its file extension and internal structural schema.

**Structured Data** follows a rigid, predefined schema organized into rows and columns. Example: A **CSV file** containing transactional sales records with fixed fields (Date, Product_ID, Quantity, Price). It is easily queried using SQL.

**Semi-Structured Data** has partial schema information and is often hierarchical or self-describing. Example: A **JSON file** containing customer profiles with nested fields like `{"address": {"city": "Kochi", "pin": "682001"}}`. It does not conform to a fixed tabular schema.

| Parameter | Structured | Semi-Structured |
|-----------|-----------|-----------------|
| Schema | Rigid | Flexible |
| Storage | RDBMS, Data Warehouse | Document stores, Data Lake |
| Example | CSV, Parquet | JSON, XML |

*(End of Answer — 3 marks awarded for definition, distinction, and example.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**  
> *List any three file formats suitable for big data analytics and state one key advantage of each.* (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

1. **Apache Parquet** — *Advantage:* Columnar storage enables high compression and fast analytical queries by reading only required columns.

2. **Apache Avro** — *Advantage:* Schema is embedded within the file, supporting robust schema evolution for streaming systems like Kafka.

3. **Apache ORC (Optimized Row Columnar)** — *Advantage:* Provides built-in indexes, predicate pushdown, and ACID transaction support, optimized for Hive.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **Note:** As per KTU 2024 Scheme, Part B Module 3 questions may offer an internal choice between Question A and Question B. Both alternatives are provided below for comprehensive preparation.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]*

**(a)** Explain the three main categories of file types used in Business Analytics with suitable examples. Discuss why **Apache Parquet** is preferred over CSV for large-scale analytical workloads. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A logistics company maintains 2 TB of shipment tracking data in CSV format, with 15 columns per record. After migrating to Parquet, the compressed size becomes 400 GB. The analytics team queries only 5 columns on average.  
    **(i)** Calculate the Compression Ratio.  
    **(ii)** Calculate the Space Savings percentage.  
    **(iii)** Calculate the theoretical Read Time Reduction. *(7 Marks)*

---

**Model Solution (a):**

The three main categories of file types in Business Analytics are:

1. **Structured Files:** Data conforming to a predefined schema with rows and columns. Examples: CSV, TSV, XLSX, Parquet, ORC. These are queryable using SQL and ideal for relational warehouses.

2. **Semi-Structured Files:** Data with partial or self-describing schemas, often hierarchical. Examples: JSON, XML, YAML. These are flexible, used in APIs and document stores.

3. **Unstructured Files:** Data with no inherent schema — free-form text, images, audio, video, logs. Examples: TXT, JPG, MP4, LOG. These are stored in object stores like Amazon S3.

**Why Parquet is preferred over CSV for large-scale analytics:**

- **Columnar Storage:** Parquet stores data column-by-column, while CSV stores it row-by-row. Analytical queries typically touch only a few columns out of many; Parquet reads only those, drastically reducing I/O.
- **Compression:** Parquet achieves **~75% storage reduction** due to homogeneous data within columns enabling better compression algorithms (Snappy, Gzip).
- **Predicate Pushdown:** Parquet supports min/max statistics per row group, allowing the engine to skip irrelevant data blocks entirely.
- **Schema Enforcement:** Parquet files embed their schema, ensuring data type integrity across the pipeline.
- **Ecosystem Support:** Native integration with Spark, Hive, Presto, AWS Athena, and BigQuery.

In contrast, CSV requires the system to parse every row and infer types, consumes more storage, and offers no built-in compression or schema.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:**
- $S_{orig} = 2 \text{ TB} = 2048 \text{ GB}$
- $S_{comp} = 400 \text{ GB}$
- $N_{total} = 15$ columns
- $N_{query} = 5$ columns

**(i) Compression Ratio:**

$$
CR = \frac{S_{orig}}{S_{comp}} = \frac{2048 \text{ GB}}{400 \text{ GB}} = 5.12
$$

**The Parquet file is 5.12 times more compact than the original CSV.**

**(ii) Space Savings Percentage:**

$$
S\% = \left(1 - \frac{400}{2048}\right) \times 100 = \left(1 - 0.1953\right) \times 100 \approx 80.47\%
$$

**Approximately 80.47% of storage cost is saved.**

**(iii) Theoretical Read Time Reduction:**

$$
\text{Reduction}\% = \left(1 - \frac{N_{query}}{N_{total}}\right) \times 100 = \left(1 - \frac{5}{15}\right) \times 100 = (1 - 0.3333) \times 100 \approx 66.67\%
$$

**The columnar format theoretically reduces I/O for the analytical query by 66.67%.**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - May 2024]*

**(a)** Compare and contrast **Row-Oriented** and **Column-Oriented** file storage. Provide two real-world examples for each and justify which is better suited for OLAP workloads. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the role of file types in a typical Business Analytics data pipeline. Highlight the formats used at each stage. *(7 Marks)*

---

**Model Solution (a):**

| Parameter | Row-Oriented | Column-Oriented |
|-----------|--------------|-----------------|
| Storage Pattern | Record-by-record | Column-by-column |
| Best For | OLTP (transactions) | OLAP (analytics) |
| Read Pattern | Full-row reads | Selective column reads |
| Compression | Limited | High (homogeneous data) |
| Examples | CSV, Avro, SequenceFile | Parquet, ORC |

**Row-Oriented Examples:**
1. **CSV** used in retail POS exports.
2. **Avro** used in Kafka-based event streaming for user clicks.

**Column-Oriented Examples:**
1. **Parquet** used in Amazon S3 data lakes for query analytics.
2. **ORC** used in Apache Hive data warehouses.

**Justification for OLAP:** OLAP queries aggregate, filter, and group by specific columns (e.g., SUM of revenue by region). In row-oriented storage, the engine must read every column to access a few. In column-oriented storage, only the relevant columns are read. Combined with min/max indexes and dictionary encoding, columnar formats deliver **orders-of-magnitude faster** OLAP performance, making them the de facto choice for analytical workloads.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

*(Refer to the Block-Level Functional Architecture diagram in Section 4.2 for the visual representation.)*

The role of file types in a typical Business Analytics pipeline is as follows:

| Pipeline Stage | Role | Recommended File Type | Reason |
|----------------|------|----------------------|--------|
| **Source Systems** | Data origination from CRM, ERP, IoT, Web | CSV, JSON, XLSX | Native export formats |
| **Ingestion Layer** | Capture streaming/batch data into the lake | Avro, JSON, NDJSON | Schema evolution, line-delimited |
| **Raw Storage** | Immutable landing zone in the data lake | Avro, Parquet (raw mode) | Durable, schema-validated |
| **Transformation** | Clean, enrich, aggregate using Spark/dbt | Parquet, ORC | High-performance columnar reads |
| **Processed Storage** | Conformed tables for analytics | Delta Lake, Iceberg, Parquet | ACID, time travel |
| **Analytics Layer** | BI dashboards, ML feature stores, AI | Parquet, ORC | Fast column pruning for queries |

**Key Insight:** The pipeline moves from *flexible/ingestion-friendly* formats at the entry point to *performance-optimized* formats at the exit point. This staged transformation is a core KTU 2024 concept.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> 
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in File Type Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing file extension with file format.** Writing ".csv" without explaining it is a *Comma-Separated Value*, row-oriented, plain-text format — costs 0.5–1 mark.
> 2. **Stating Parquet is "compressed" without explaining *why*.** Examiners expect the answer to mention **columnar homogeneity enabling dictionary encoding, run-length encoding, and Snappy/Gzip compression**. Vague statements lose 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting units in numerical questions.** Writing $CR = 5.12$ without the label "x (times)" or "ratio" is penalized. Always write $CR = 5.12\text{x}$.
> 4. **Mismatching scenario with format.** Recommending Parquet for a small Excel report or CSV for a streaming Kafka pipeline signals weak conceptual understanding — full 7-mark questions can drop to 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Skipping the formula in derivation questions.** Even if the final answer is correct, omitting $CR = \frac{S_{orig}}{S_{comp}}$ results in partial marking. **Show the formula first, substitute second, conclude third.**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — File Types (Module 3)**

- **Definition:** File Type = extension + internal structural schema (KTU 2024 definition).
- **Three Classes:** Structured, Semi-Structured, Unstructured — be able to give 2 examples for each.
- **Row vs Columnar:** Row → OLTP (CSV, Avro); Columnar → OLAP (Parquet, ORC).
- **Compression Ratio formula:** $CR = \dfrac{S_{orig}}{S_{comp}}$ — always label with units.
- **Space Savings formula:** $S\% = \left(1 - \dfrac{S_{comp}}{S_{orig}}\right) \times 100$.
- **Read Time Reduction (Columnar):** $T_{red}\% = \left(1 - \dfrac{N_{query}}{N_{total}}\right) \times 100$.
- **Parquet strengths:** Columnar, compressed (~75% savings), predicate pushdown, schema-embedded, Spark/Hive native.
- **Avro strengths:** Schema evolution, Kafka-friendly, row-based, binary.
- **ORC strengths:** Hive-native, ACID, indexes, predicate pushdown.
- **JSON strengths:** Hierarchical, API-native, human-readable, semi-structured.
- **CSV strengths:** Universally compatible, human-readable, simple, but no schema/compression.
- **Decision Rule:** *Readable formats for humans, binary/columnar formats for machines.*
- **Pipeline Flow:** Source (CSV/JSON) → Ingestion (Avro/JSON) → Raw Lake (Parquet/Avro) → Transform (Parquet/ORC) → Processed (Delta/Iceberg) → Analytics (Parquet).
- **MUST memorize:** The **5x compression benchmark** for Parquet vs CSV and the **80% I/O reduction heuristic** for columnar reads.
- **Exam Tip:** Always justify format selection with a *scenario-fit reason*, never just name the format.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT522 - BUSINESS ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Data Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Business Analytics (PEADT522)**, *Data Types* refer to the systematic classification of data based on its **intrinsic nature**, the **kind of values** it can hold, the **mathematical operations** permissible on it, and the **analytical techniques** applicable to it. The classification follows the **Stevens' Theory of Measurement Scales (1946)** and modern data science taxonomies.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, data is classified along multiple orthogonal dimensions:

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Standard)**
> *Data Types are categorical designations assigned to variables to determine appropriate statistical summarization, visualization, hypothesis testing, and predictive modelling techniques. The choice of data type governs the entire downstream analytical pipeline.*

### 1.2 Three Primary Dimensions of Data Classification

| Dimension | Categories | KTU Relevance |
|-----------|------------|---------------|
| **By Measurement Scale** | Nominal, Ordinal, Interval, Ratio | Determines valid statistics |
| **By Mathematical Nature** | Qualitative vs Quantitative | Determines visualization |
| **By Structural Form** | Structured, Semi-structured, Unstructured | Determines storage/processing |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Library Bookshelf" Intuition

> [!NOTE] **Real-World Analogy**
> Imagine a massive **library**:
> - **Qualitative Data** = *Book titles and genres* (you cannot average a genre)
> - **Quantitative Data** = *Number of pages, weight of books* (you CAN average these)
> - **Nominal** = Fiction vs Non-fiction (no ranking)
> - **Ordinal** = Star ratings (1★ to 5★ — order matters, gaps unknown)
> - **Discrete** = Number of books (countable: 1, 2, 3...)
> - **Continuous** = Book weight in grams (measurable: 235.7g, 235.78g...)

Just as a librarian sorts books differently based on whether they are novels, magazines, or encyclopedias, a **business analyst must classify data correctly** before applying any analytical tool.

### 1.4 Stevens' Four Measurement Scales — The Foundational Hierarchy

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│          RATIO (Highest information)            │
│   ┌─────────────────────────────────────┐       │
│   │      INTERVAL                       │       │
│   │   ┌───────────────────────┐         │       │
│   │   │    ORDINAL            │         │       │
│   │   │  ┌──────────────┐     │         │       │
│   │   │  │   NOMINAL    │     │         │       │
│   │   │  │  (Lowest)    │     │         │       │
│   │   │  └──────────────┘     │         │       │
│   │   └───────────────────────┘         │       │
│   └─────────────────────────────────────┘       │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### 1.5 Key Business Analytics Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Probability Bound:** $0 \leq P(x) \leq 1$
- **Standard Scale Types:** **4** (Nominal, Ordinal, Interval, Ratio)
- **Big Data Veracity Threshold:** Typically **> 3Vs** (Volume, Velocity, Variety)
- **Information Content Hierarchy:** Each ascending scale **inherits** properties of the lower scale.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Classification Framework

#### **A. Qualitative (Categorical) Data**
Represents *characteristics* or *labels* — arithmetic operations are **not meaningful**.

**1. Nominal Data**
- **Definition:** Categories with no inherent order or ranking.
- **Examples:** Gender (M/F), Blood Group (A/B/AB/O), Product Category (Electronics/Clothing/Food), Payment Mode (Cash/Card/UPI).
- **Operations Allowed:** Counting, Mode, Frequency, Chi-square test.
- **Operations NOT Allowed:** Mean, Median, Addition, Subtraction, Ratio.

**2. Ordinal Data**
- **Definition:** Categories with a meaningful *rank order* but unknown/uneven intervals.
- **Examples:** Customer Satisfaction (Poor < Fair < Good < Excellent), Education Level (High School < Bachelor < Master < PhD), Likert Scale (1–5), Hotel Star Rating.
- **Operations Allowed:** Median, Percentiles, Rank Correlation (Spearman's $\rho$), Mann-Whitney U test.
- **Operations NOT Allowed:** Mean (debatable), True Ratio statements.

**3. Binary (Dichotomous) Data**
- **Definition:** Special nominal case with exactly **two** categories.
- **Examples:** Pass/Fail, Yes/No, Spam/Not Spam, Churn/Retain.
- **KTU Note:** Often coded as $0$ and $1$ for logistic regression.

#### **B. Quantitative (Numerical) Data**
Represents *numerical measurements* — arithmetic operations **are** meaningful.

**4. Discrete Data**
- **Definition:** Countable values, often integers, arising from *counting*.
- **Examples:** Number of complaints, Defects per batch, Customers in queue, Visits per day.
- **Probability Distribution:** **Binomial**, **Poisson**.

**5. Continuous Data**
- **Definition:** Uncountable values within a range, arising from *measuring*.
- **Examples:** Height, Weight, Temperature, Revenue, Time on page.
- **Probability Distribution:** **Normal**, **Exponential**, **Uniform**.

#### **C. Time-Based Classification**

**6. Cross-Sectional Data**
- Data collected at **a single point in time** across multiple entities.
- *Example:* Sales of 100 stores in December 2024.

**7. Time-Series Data**
- Data collected over **multiple time periods** for one or more entities.
- *Example:* Monthly sales of a store from 2020–2024.

#### **D. Structural Classification**

**8. Structured Data**
- Tabular format with predefined schema (rows × columns).
- Stored in **RDBMS** (SQL).

**9. Semi-Structured Data**
- Has organizational properties but no rigid schema.
- *Example:* JSON, XML, Email, Log files.

**10. Unstructured Data**
- No predefined model — typically **80%** of enterprise data.
- *Example:* Text, Images, Audio, Video, Social Media posts.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Statistical Operations by Data Type

> [!NOTE] **High-Yield Cheat Sheet for KTU Board Exam**

| Data Type | Central Tendency | Dispersion | Visualization | Hypothesis Test |
|-----------|------------------|------------|---------------|-----------------|
| **Nominal** | Mode | None (just proportions) | Bar Chart, Pie Chart | Chi-Square ($\chi^2$) |
| **Ordinal** | Median, Mode | Range, IQR | Bar Chart, Stacked Bar | Mann-Whitney, Wilcoxon, Spearman |
| **Interval** | Mean, Median, Mode | Std. Deviation, Variance | Histogram, Box Plot | t-test, ANOVA, Pearson |
| **Ratio** | Mean, Median, Mode, GM, HM | All measures | All charts | All parametric tests |
| **Discrete** | Mean | Variance | Bar Chart | Poisson, Binomial tests |
| **Continuous** | Mean, Median | Std. Deviation | Histogram, Density Plot | Normal-based tests |

### 2.3 Key Operational Rules

- **Rule of Inheritance (KTU Favourite):** Each higher scale inherits operations of the lower scale.

$$ \text{Operations}_{\text{Ratio}} \supseteq \text{Operations}_{\text{Interval}} \supseteq \text{Operations}_{\text{Ordinal}} \supseteq \text{Operations}_{\text{Nominal}} $$

- **Rule of Permissible Arithmetic:** Ratio data supports all four basic operations; Nominal supports only equality ($=$) and inequality ($\neq$).

- **Rule of True Zero:** Only **Ratio** data has a *true, non-arbitrary zero point**. This is what enables statements like "*Revenue doubled*" — impossible with Interval data (e.g., $0^\circ C$ is not "no temperature").

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Business Analytics

- **Marketing:** Customer segmentation requires nominal (segment names) + ratio (purchase amount).
- **Finance:** Risk modelling uses ratio data (returns, volatility).
- **HR:** Employee satisfaction surveys use ordinal (Likert scale).
- **Operations:** Six Sigma relies heavily on continuous measurement data.
- **Healthcare Analytics:** Patient outcomes mix nominal (diagnosis) and ratio (lab values).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Classification Algorithm — "How to Identify a Data Type"

**Step 1:** *Ask — Can the value be expressed as a number?*
- **NO** → Qualitative (Go to Step 2)
- **YES** → Quantitative (Go to Step 3)

**Step 2:** *Is there a meaningful order?*
- **NO** → **Nominal** (or Binary if only 2 categories)
- **YES** → **Ordinal**

**Step 3:** *Is it countable (integer-valued)?*
- **YES** → **Discrete**
- **NO** → **Continuous** (Go to Step 4)

**Step 4:** *Does it have a true zero?*
- **NO** → **Interval**
- **YES** → **Ratio**

### 3.2 Derivation — Why Mean is Invalid for Nominal Data

Let us rigorously demonstrate why the arithmetic mean is mathematically meaningless for nominal categories.

**Given:** A nominal variable $X$ with $k$ categories $\{C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_k\}$ and frequencies $\{f_1, f_2, \ldots, f_k\}$.

**Attempted Calculation of Mean:**

$$\bar{X} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} f_i \cdot v_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{k} f_i}$$

where $v_i$ is the numerical code assigned to category $C_i$.

**Step 1:** Assign arbitrary codes — say, Gender: Male = 1, Female = 2.

$$\bar{X} = \frac{(n_{\text{male}} \cdot 1) + (n_{\text{female}} \cdot 2)}{n_{\text{male}} + n_{\text{female}}}$$

**Step 2:** Demonstrate the contradiction by re-coding.

If we re-code Male = 100, Female = 200, the mean changes to a completely different value:

$$\bar{X}_{\text{new}} = \frac{(n_{\text{male}} \cdot 100) + (n_{\text{female}} \cdot 200)}{n}$$

**Step 3:** Show that the *physical reality* has not changed, only the labels.

$$\text{If } n_{\text{male}} = 50, n_{\text{female}} = 50, n = 100:$$

$$\bar{X}_{\text{old}} = \frac{(50 \cdot 1) + (50 \cdot 2)}{100} = 1.5$$

$$\bar{X}_{\text{new}} = \frac{(50 \cdot 100) + (50 \cdot 200)}{100} = 150$$

**Conclusion:** Since $\bar{X}$ depends on arbitrary coding, it carries **no real-world interpretation**. Hence, the arithmetic mean is **mathematically invalid** for nominal data. $\blacksquare$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Operations Validity Across Scales

Let $O$ = Operation is *valid and meaningful*, $X$ = Operation is *invalid or misleading*.

$$\begin{array}{|l|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
\textbf{Operation} & \textbf{Nominal} & \textbf{Ordinal} & \textbf{Interval} & \textbf{Ratio} \\
\hline
\text{Frequency Count} & O & O & O & O \\
\hline
\text{Mode} & O & O & O & O \\
\hline
\text{Median} & X & O & O & O \\
\hline
\text{Arithmetic Mean} & X & X & O & O \\
\hline
\text{Standard Deviation} & X & X & O & O \\
\hline
\text{Ratio (e.g., "twice as")} & X & X & X & O \\
\hline
\text{Addition/Subtraction} & X & X & O & O \\
\hline
\text{Percentile Ranking} & X & O & O & O \\
\hline
\text{Chi-Square Test} & O & O & X & X \\
\hline
\text{Pearson Correlation} & X & X & O & O \\
\hline
\end{array}$$

### 3.4 Worked Example — Converting Between Data Types (Binning)

**Scenario:** A retail company has continuous ratio data on customer annual spending.

**Step 1:** Original continuous data: $S = \{1500, 2300, 4500, 7800, 12000, 25000\}$ (in ₹).

**Step 2:** Define bins for ordinal conversion:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Low Spender} &: 0 \leq S < 5000 \\
\text{Medium Spender} &: 5000 \leq S < 15000 \\
\text{High Spender} &: S \geq 15000
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the binning function $B(S)$:

$$B(S) = \begin{cases} \text{Low}, & 0 \leq S < 5000 \\ \text{Medium}, & 5000 \leq S < 15000 \\ \text{High}, & S \geq 15000 \end{cases}$$

**Step 4:** Resulting ordinal data: $S_{\text{ordinal}} = \{\text{Low, Low, Low, Medium, Medium, High}\}$.

**Analytical Insight:** Information is **lost** during binning (precision $\downarrow$) but interpretability for non-technical stakeholders is **gained** (clarity $\uparrow$). This is a classic trade-off in **Business Analytics reporting**.

### 3.5 Information Theory Perspective

The information content increases as we move up the measurement hierarchy. Quantitatively, this can be related to the **Shannon entropy** of the variable:

$$H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)$$

Higher-scale data generally permits **more informative models** (lower entropy of residuals) because it preserves more granular information.

### 3.6 Decision Flow for Analytical Test Selection

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Data Type} \rightarrow \text{Nominal} &\Rightarrow \chi^2 \text{ test of independence} \\
\text{Data Type} \rightarrow \text{Ordinal} &\Rightarrow \text{Spearman's } \rho \text{ or Mann-Whitney U} \\
\text{Data Type} \rightarrow \text{Interval/Ratio + Normal} &\Rightarrow \text{t-test, ANOVA, Pearson's } r \\
\text{Data Type} \rightarrow \text{Ratio + Non-Normal} &\Rightarrow \text{Non-parametric alternatives}
\end{aligned}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
