<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)**

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between Nominal and Ordinal data with one business example each.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Nominal Data:** Categories *without* inherent order. **Example:** Product category — Electronics, Clothing, Food (no ranking).
- **Ordinal Data:** Categories *with* a meaningful rank order but unknown magnitude of differences. **Example:** Customer satisfaction rating — Poor, Fair, Good, Excellent (ordered but the gap between "Fair" and "Good" is not quantifiable).
- **Key Distinction:** Ordinal preserves rank information; Nominal does not.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Why is ratio data considered superior to interval data in business analytics?**

**Model Answer:**
Ratio data possesses all properties of interval data **plus a true, non-arbitrary zero point**. This enables:
1. Meaningful ratio statements (e.g., "Revenue *doubled*").
2. Computation of geometric and harmonic means.
3. More powerful statistical modelling and forecasting.
4. Correct economic interpretations (e.g., elasticity).

In contrast, interval data (like Temperature in °C) cannot support true ratio statements ($20^\circ C$ is not "twice as hot" as $10^\circ C$).

---

### **PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Classify the following variables into appropriate data types with justification: *(i)* Monthly income of employees, *(ii)* Educational qualification, *(iii)* Customer review (1–5 stars), *(iv)* Number of customer complaints per day, *(v)* Temperature in Celsius, *(vi)* Gender, *(vii)* Stock price.

**Model Solution:**

| Variable | Data Type | Justification |
|----------|-----------|---------------|
| Monthly income | **Ratio (Continuous)** | Numeric, measurable, true zero (₹0 = no income) |
| Educational qualification | **Ordinal** | Ordered categories (10th < 12th < UG < PG) |
| Customer review (1–5 stars) | **Ordinal** | Rank-ordered, gaps not equal |
| Complaints per day | **Ratio (Discrete)** | Countable integer, true zero |
| Temperature in °C | **Interval** | Numeric but $0^\circ C$ is arbitrary, no true zero |
| Gender | **Nominal** | Categories with no order |
| Stock price | **Ratio (Continuous)** | True zero (₹0), all arithmetic operations valid |

**(b) [7 Marks]** A retail company collects data on 500 customers regarding purchase amount, payment method, and satisfaction rating. Recommend the appropriate statistical tools and visualizations for each variable and justify your choices.

**Model Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Purchase Amount (Ratio)} &\Rightarrow \text{Mean, Median, Std. Deviation, Histogram, Box Plot} \\
&\Rightarrow \text{Normal-based tests (t-test, ANOVA, Pearson correlation)} \\
\\
\text{Payment Method (Nominal)} &\Rightarrow \text{Mode, Frequency table, Pie Chart, Bar Chart} \\
&\Rightarrow \chi^2 \text{ test of independence} \\
\\
\text{Satisfaction (Ordinal)} &\Rightarrow \text{Median, IQR, Stacked Bar Chart} \\
&\Rightarrow \text{Mann-Whitney U test, Spearman's rank correlation}
\end{aligned}$$

**Justification:** Each tool is selected based on the **Stevens' measurement scale** of the variable. Using tools incompatible with the data type (e.g., computing mean satisfaction rating or Pearson correlation on nominal data) leads to **statistical fallacy** and incorrect business decisions.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain Stevens' four levels of measurement with a suitable business example for each. Discuss the permissible operations at each level.

**Model Solution:**

Stevens (1946) classified data into four hierarchical measurement scales:

1. **Nominal Scale:** Labels without order.
   - *Example:* Department of employees (HR, Finance, IT).
   - *Permissible Operations:* Counting, Mode, $\chi^2$ test.
   - *Forbidden:* Mean, Ratio.

2. **Ordinal Scale:** Ordered categories, unequal/unknown gaps.
   - *Example:* Performance rating (Below Average, Average, Good, Outstanding).
   - *Permissible Operations:* Median, Rank, Percentiles, Non-parametric tests.
   - *Forbidden:* Mean, True Ratio.

3. **Interval Scale:** Equal intervals, arbitrary zero.
   - *Example:* IQ Score, Temperature in Celsius.
   - *Permissible Operations:* Mean, Std. Deviation, Pearson correlation.
   - *Forbidden:* Ratio statements.

4. **Ratio Scale:** Equal intervals, true zero.
   - *Example:* Sales revenue, Age, Weight.
   - *Permissible Operations:* All — Mean, GM, HM, Ratio statements.

**Hierarchy of Information:** Each level inherits operations from below:

$$\text{Nominal} \subset \text{Ordinal} \subset \text{Interval} \subset \text{Ratio}$$

**(b) [7 Marks]** A dataset contains customer ratings (1–10) and purchase frequency (number of purchases per year). Explain how the choice of data type affects:
*(i)* The appropriate measure of central tendency,
*(ii)* The choice of correlation technique,
*(iii)* The interpretation of results.

**Model Solution:**

*(i) Central Tendency:*

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Ratings (Ordinal/Discrete)} &: \text{Median is appropriate} \\
\text{Frequency (Ratio/Discrete)} &: \text{Arithmetic Mean is appropriate}
\end{aligned}$$

Using mean for ratings would imply that a rating of 7 is "twice as good" as 3.5, which is meaningless.

*(ii) Correlation Technique:*

Since ratings are ordinal and frequency is ratio:
- *Spearman's Rank Correlation* ($\rho$) is preferred if the relationship is monotonic but not necessarily linear.
- *Pearson's Correlation* ($r$) is appropriate only if both are treated as interval-ratio and linearity is verified.

$$\rho = 1 - \frac{6 \sum d_i^2}{n(n^2 - 1)}$$

where $d_i$ is the difference between the two ranks for each observation.

*(iii) Interpretation:*

Choosing the wrong technique inflates Type-I error rates and leads to incorrect business decisions. For example, falsely claiming that a high correlation between promotional spend and customer satisfaction is "linear" may lead to over-investment in promotions without validating the actual relationship.

---

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Mark Loss Trap #1:** Students often classify *Likert scale data (1–5)* as *Interval*. It is technically **Ordinal** unless strong assumptions of equal intervals are justified.
> 2. **Mark Loss Trap #2:** Computing the *mean of categorical codes* (e.g., mean gender = 1.4). This is mathematically valid but **semantically meaningless**.
> 3. **Mark Loss Trap #3:** Confusing *Discrete* with *Categorical*. Discrete is *numerical and countable*; Categorical is *labels*.
> 4. **Mark Loss Trap #4:** Forgetting that *Time-Series* is a temporal classification **orthogonal** to measurement scale — they are independent dimensions.
> 5. **Mark Loss Trap #5:** Treating *temperature in °C* as ratio data. It is **Interval** because $0^\circ C$ is arbitrary.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

- **Foundational Principle:** Always identify the **data type FIRST**, before choosing any statistical test, visualization, or model.

- **Four Stevens' Scales:** Nominal → Ordinal → Interval → Ratio (ascending information).

- **True Zero Rule:** Only **Ratio** data has a meaningful absolute zero; this unlocks ratio statements and geometric/harmonic means.

- **Nominal vs Ordinal:** Nominal = labels; Ordinal = labels + order.

- **Discrete vs Continuous:** Discrete = countable (integers); Continuous = measurable (any real value in interval).

- **Binary Data:** A special case of Nominal with exactly **two** categories — central to logistic regression and classification.

- **Cross-Sectional vs Time-Series:** Snapshot vs longitudinal — determines whether trend/seasonality analysis is possible.

- **Structured vs Unstructured:** Tabular vs free-form — determines whether SQL vs NoSQL/Big Data tools are used.

- **Inheritance Rule:** A Ratio variable supports ALL operations of Nominal, Ordinal, and Interval.

- **Visualization Quick-Map:** Categorical → Bar/Pie; Ordinal → Stacked Bar; Continuous → Histogram/Box/Scatter; Time → Line.

- **Test Selection Rule:** Non-parametric tests for Ordinal; Parametric tests for Interval/Ratio (after normality check).

- **Binning Trade-off:** Converting continuous → categorical loses precision but improves stakeholder interpretability.

- **KTU Board Favourite:** Always write the **justification** (the *why*) alongside any classification — partial marks depend on reasoning, not just the label.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Sources
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT522 - BUSINESS ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Data Sources

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Business Analytics** (PEADT522), a **Data Source** refers to the origin, repository, or originating point from which data is collected, captured, generated, or extracted for subsequent processing, transformation, and analytical decision-making. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for Module 3, data sources form the foundational input layer of the **Business Analytics Value Chain** — the pipeline `Data → Information → Insight → Decision → Value`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Exam Ready):**
> A *data source* is any structured, semi-structured, or unstructured channel — manual or automated — that supplies raw facts and figures to an organization's analytical systems. The reliability of any downstream model (descriptive, diagnostic, predictive, or prescriptive) is strictly bounded by the quality and provenance of its underlying data sources.

Mathematically, if $\mathcal{D}$ represents the universe of all available data, then:

$$
\mathcal{D} = \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} S_i
$$

where $S_i$ denotes the $i^{th}$ individual data source and $n$ is the total number of sources feeding the analytics engine. Each source $S_i$ has an associated **trust score** $T(S_i) \in [0,1]$ and a **latency** $L(S_i)$ measured in seconds, minutes, hours, or days. The aggregate **Data Source Quality Index (DSQI)** is:

$$
\text{DSQI} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot T(S_i) \cdot \left(1 - \frac{L(S_i)}{L_{\max}}\right)
$$

where $w_i$ is the importance weight assigned to source $S_i$, and $L_{\max}$ is the maximum tolerable latency.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Restaurant Kitchen" Intuition

Imagine a **professional restaurant kitchen**. Before any dish (a business insight) can be plated and served to a customer (a decision-maker), the chef (the data analyst) must first procure ingredients from specific vendors.

- **Primary Data Sources** are like growing vegetables in your own backyard farm — you control the soil, the seeds, and the harvest time. The data is *first-hand* and *purpose-built*.
- **Secondary Data Sources** are like buying vegetables from a wholesale market or supermarket — convenient, pre-packaged, but you don't control how they were grown.
- **Internal Data Sources** are ingredients sourced from your own restaurant's pantry (sales records, employee logs).
- **External Data Sources** are ingredients brought in from outside vendors (market price feeds, weather APIs, government databases).
- **Structured Data** is like a pre-measured spice box — every jar is labelled, uniform, and ready.
- **Unstructured Data** is like a sack of fresh garden produce — rich, varied, but you must clean, chop, and sort it before cooking.

Just as a chef refuses to cook with rotten ingredients, a business analyst must **validate, cleanse, and certify** every data source before trusting it to feed an analytical model.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 3 Anchor):** The KTU 2024 Module 3 expects students to be able to (i) classify data sources along multiple axes, (ii) evaluate their fitness-for-purpose, and (iii) understand the implications of source selection on analytical outcomes. This directly maps to **CO3 (Apply)** and **CO4 (Analyze)** of the course outcome map.

---

## 1.3 Why Data Sources Matter in Business Analytics

Every analytical technique — whether a simple Excel pivot table, a regression model, a classification algorithm, or a deep-learning forecast — is **only as good as the data fed into it**. The classic industry maxim is:

> *"Garbage In, Garbage Out (GIGO)."*

Modern enterprises routinely integrate data from **dozens to hundreds** of distinct sources. A 2024 industry benchmark indicates that large enterprises manage between **400 and 800 distinct data sources** on average, with the global datasphere projected to reach **$\approx 181$ Zettabytes (ZB)** by 2025 (IDC Global Datasphere estimate). Selecting, governing, and integrating these sources is therefore a **strategic capability**, not a back-office chore.

---

## 1.4 Two Foundational Axes of Classification

| Axis | Pole 1 | Pole 2 |
|---|---|---|
| **Origin of Collection** | Primary (first-hand) | Secondary (second-hand) |
| **Location Relative to Org** | Internal (own) | External (third-party) |

These two axes are **orthogonal** — meaning a single source can be, for instance, *internal + primary* (sales captured by your own POS terminal) or *external + secondary* (a market research report purchased from Nielsen). Mastering this **2×2 mental model** is the single most important takeaway of Module 3.1.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Canonical Data Source Categories

A complete taxonomy of data sources relevant to KTU Module 3 spans **six primary categories**, each with distinct characteristics, cost structures, latency profiles, and trust levels.

### 2.1.1 Primary Data Sources

Primary data is **collected directly by the researcher or organization** for the *specific* problem at hand. It has not been previously aggregated or interpreted.

**Sub-types of Primary Data:**

1. **Surveys & Questionnaires** — structured instruments (Likert scale, multiple choice, open-ended) distributed via email, web, phone, or in person. High control, but susceptible to **response bias**.
2. **Interviews** — one-on-one or group (focus group) verbal data collection. Rich, qualitative, but expensive and time-consuming.
3. **Observations** — recording behavior in natural settings (e.g., CCTV analytics, eye-tracking, ethnographic studies). High ecological validity.
4. **Experiments** — controlled A/B tests where variables are manipulated to establish causality. Gold standard for causal inference.
5. **Sensor / IoT Data** — automated capture from devices (temperature, GPS, RFID, accelerometers). High volume, high velocity.
6. **Transaction Logs** — POS records, clickstreams, call detail records (CDRs). Naturally occurring, no respondent burden.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Property:** Primary data is **expensive, time-consuming, but highly specific and current**. It is *bespoke* — tailored exactly to the decision at hand.

### 2.1.2 Secondary Data Sources

Secondary data is **data originally collected for some other purpose** and reused for the current analysis. It exists in two flavors:

- **Internal Secondary:** Historical sales data reused for forecasting; HR records reused for attrition modeling.
- **External Secondary:** Government census, industry reports, published academic datasets, syndicated research.

**Advantages of Secondary Data:**
- **Low cost** (often free or near-free).
- **Time-efficient** (no collection lag).
- **Large sample sizes** (e.g., national census).
- **Longitudinal coverage** (historical series).

**Disadvantages:**
- **Fit mismatch** — may not align precisely with current research question.
- **Unknown quality** — you inherit the original collector's methodology flaws.
- **Outdated** — publication lag may render it obsolete.

### 2.1.3 Internal vs. External Sources

| Dimension | Internal Sources | External Sources |
|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | Within the organization | Outside the organization |
| **Cost** | Marginal (already collected) | Subscription, purchase, or free |
| **Control** | High | Low |
| **Relevance** | Perfectly aligned to business | May require transformation |
| **Examples** | ERP, CRM, HRMS, financial ledgers | Census, market reports, social media APIs |

### 2.1.4 Structured, Semi-Structured, and Unstructured Data

This is the **most exam-relevant classification** in the 2024 scheme.

- **Structured Data:** Organized in predefined schemas, typically rows and columns. Stored in RDBMS (MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle). Examples: customer master tables, inventory ledgers.
- **Semi-Structured Data:** Has organizational properties but not a rigid schema. Examples: JSON, XML, CSV, email headers, NoSQL documents (MongoDB).
- **Unstructured Data:** No predefined schema. Constitutes **$\approx 80$–$90\%$** of enterprise data (text, images, audio, video).

### 2.1.5 Big Data Sources (The 5V Lens)

The five canonical characteristics — the **5 V's** of Big Data — are:

- **Volume:** Scale of data (terabytes → petabytes → zettabytes).
- **Velocity:** Speed of generation (batch vs. real-time streams).
- **Variety:** Diversity of formats (structured, semi, unstructured).
- **Veracity:** Trustworthiness / quality.
- **Value:** Business worth extractable per unit of data.

### 2.1.6 Open vs. Proprietary (Restricted) Data Sources

- **Open Data:** Publicly available, often government-published (data.gov, data.gov.in, European Data Portal). Free, but variable quality.
- **Proprietary Data:** Behind paywalls or login walls (Bloomberg Terminal, NielsenIQ, Dun & Bradstreet). High quality, expensive.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept / Formula | Expression | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Universe of data | $\mathcal{D} = \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} S_i$ | Total data = union of all sources |
| 2 | Data Source Quality Index | $\text{DSQI} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot T(S_i) \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{L(S_i)}{L_{\max}}\right)$ | Composite source-fitness score |
| 3 | Structured share of data | $S_{\text{struct}} = \dfrac{\text{Structured Volume}}{\text{Total Volume}}$ | Fraction in RDBMS form |
| 4 | Cost per usable record | $\text{CPR} = \dfrac{C_{\text{collect}} + C_{\text{clean}}}{N_{\text{usable}}}$ | True cost of analytics-ready data |
| 5 | Source coverage ratio | $\text{COV} = \dfrac{\vert \text{Population} \cap \text{Sample} \vert}{\vert \text{Population} \vert}$ | Fraction of population captured |
| 6 | Latency SLA | $L(S_i) \le L_{\max}$ | Maximum tolerable delay per source |
| 7 | Bias index (sampling) | $B = \bar{x}_{\text{sample}} - \mu_{\text{population}}$ | Difference between sample mean and true mean |
| 8 | Veracity (completeness) | $V_c = 1 - \dfrac{\text{Missing cells}}{\text{Total cells}}$ | Data completeness ratio |
| 9 | Veracity (consistency) | $V_k = \dfrac{\text{Consistent records}}{\text{Total records}}$ | Internal agreement ratio |
| 10 | Zettabyte scale | $1 \text{ ZB} = 10^{21} \text{ bytes}$ | Global datasphere unit |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Memorize the 5 V's, the 2×2 Primary/Secondary × Internal/External matrix, and the structured/semi/unstructured split. These three concepts account for **$\approx 70\%$** of the Module 3 marks in past university examinations.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering / Industry Utility

1. **Retail & E-Commerce** — Combine internal POS (transactional) + external weather APIs (climatic) + social media (sentiment) to forecast next-day demand.
2. **Banking & Finance** — Internal core-banking + external credit bureau (CIBIL) + regulator (RBI) feeds for risk modeling.
3. **Healthcare** — EHR (Electronic Health Records, internal structured) + wearable IoT (sensor, primary) + PubMed (external, secondary) for clinical decision support.
4. **Manufacturing** — SCADA / PLC sensors (primary, real-time) + ERP (internal) + supplier EDI feeds (external) for predictive maintenance.
5. **Smart Cities** — CCTV streams + traffic loop sensors + citizen grievance portals + census data for urban planning.

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 Module 3 outcome expects students to **map a real business problem to a multi-source data acquisition plan**, justifying the choice of each source along the **2×2 classification** and the **5V lens**.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Derivations

## 3.1 Derivation: Aggregate Data Source Quality Index (DSQI)

We begin with the formal requirement: a single scalar metric that captures the **trustworthiness**, **freshness**, and **importance** of a collection of data sources. This is essential for sourcing decisions in a typical enterprise data lake architecture.

### Step 1 — Define the Trust Score of an Individual Source

Let $S_i$ be the $i^{th}$ data source. Its trust score $T(S_i)$ is defined as the weighted geometric mean of three sub-dimensions:

$$
T(S_i) = \left( V_c(S_i) \right)^{\alpha_1} \cdot \left( V_k(S_i) \right)^{\alpha_2} \cdot \left( V_a(S_i) \right)^{\alpha_3}
$$

where:

- $V_c$ = completeness ratio (fraction of non-missing cells)
- $V_k$ = consistency ratio (fraction of internally consistent records)
- $V_a$ = accuracy ratio (fraction of records verified against a ground truth)
- $\alpha_1, \alpha_2, \alpha_3 \ge 0$ and $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 + \alpha_3 = 1$

The geometric (multiplicative) form is chosen so that a **single very-low dimension can drag the trust score down sharply** — a property that arithmetic averaging fails to capture.

### Step 2 — Normalize the Latency

Raw latency $L(S_i)$ (in seconds) is normalized to a $[0,1]$ freshness score:

$$
F(S_i) = 1 - \frac{L(S_i)}{L_{\max}}, \quad \text{with the constraint } L(S_i) \le L_{\max}
$$

If $L(S_i) = 0$ (real-time), then $F(S_i) = 1$. If $L(S_i) = L_{\max}$, then $F(S_i) = 0$.

### Step 3 — Combine Trust and Freshness

For each source, define a **per-source utility** as:

$$
U(S_i) = T(S_i) \cdot F(S_i)
$$

This product penalizes sources that are *either* untrustworthy *or* stale, but rewards sources that are *both* trustworthy and fresh.

### Step 4 — Apply Importance Weights

Not all sources contribute equally to the analytical goal. Assign a non-negative importance weight $w_i$ to each source, normalized so that:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i = 1
$$

The aggregate **Data Source Quality Index (DSQI)** becomes:

$$
\text{DSQI} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot U(S_i) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot T(S_i) \cdot \left(1 - \frac{L(S_i)}{L_{\max}}\right)
$$

A DSQI value close to $1.0$ indicates a near-ideal data ecosystem; a value close to $0.0$ indicates that the analytical engine is being fed by low-quality, stale, or marginal sources.

### Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example

Consider an e-commerce firm integrating three sources for a churn-prediction model:

| Source $S_i$ | Type | $V_c$ | $V_k$ | $V_a$ | $L$ (min) | $L_{\max}$ (min) | $w_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $S_1$: CRM | Internal, structured | 0.98 | 0.95 | 0.90 | 5 | 60 | 0.50 |
| $S_2$: Web-clickstream | Internal, semi-structured | 0.85 | 0.80 | 0.75 | 1 | 60 | 0.30 |
| $S_3$: Twitter API | External, unstructured | 0.60 | 0.50 | 0.55 | 10 | 60 | 0.20 |

Use equal sub-weights $\alpha_1 = \alpha_2 = \alpha_3 = \dfrac{1}{3}$.

**Compute $T(S_1)$:**

$$
T(S_1) = (0.98)^{1/3} \cdot (0.95)^{1/3} \cdot (0.90)^{1/3}
$$

Take logarithms to make this tractable:

$$
\ln T(S_1) = \frac{1}{3} \left[ \ln(0.98) + \ln(0.95) + \ln(0.90) \right]
$$

Numerically:

$$
\ln(0.98) \approx -0.02020, \quad \ln(0.95) \approx -0.05129, \quad \ln(0.90) \approx -0.10536
$$

Summing:

$$
\ln(0.98) + \ln(0.95) + \ln(0.90) \approx -0.02020 - 0.05129 - 0.10536 = -0.17685
$$

Dividing by 3:

$$
\frac{-0.17685}{3} \approx -0.05895
$$

Exponentiating back:

$$
T(S_1) = e^{-0.05895} \approx 0.9428
$$

**Compute $T(S_2)$:**

$$
\ln T(S_2) = \frac{1}{3} \left[ \ln(0.85) + \ln(0.80) + \ln(0.75) \right]
$$

$$
\ln(0.85) \approx -0.16252, \quad \ln(0.80) \approx -0.22314, \quad \ln(0.75) \approx -0.28768
$$

Sum: $-0.16252 - 0.22314 - 0.28768 = -0.67334$.

Divide by 3: $-0.22445$.

$$
T(S_2) = e^{-0.22445} \approx 0.7991
$$

**Compute $T(S_3)$:**

$$
\ln T(S_3) = \frac{1}{3} \left[ \ln(0.60) + \ln(0.50) + \ln(0.55) \right]
$$

$$
\ln(0.60) \approx -0.51083, \quad \ln(0.50) \approx -0.69315, \quad \ln(0.55) \approx -0.59784
$$

Sum: $-0.51083 - 0.69315 - 0.59784 = -1.80182$.

Divide by 3: $-0.60061$.

$$
T(S_3) = e^{-0.60061} \approx 0.5484
$$

**Compute Freshness $F(S_i)$:**

$$
F(S_1) = 1 - \frac{5}{60} = 1 - 0.0833 = 0.9167
$$

$$
F(S_2) = 1 - \frac{1}{60} = 1 - 0.0167 = 0.9833
$$

$$
F(S_3) = 1 - \frac{10}{60} = 1 - 0.1667 = 0.8333
$$

**Compute Per-Source Utility $U(S_i) = T(S_i) \cdot F(S_i)$:**

$$
U(S_1) = 0.9428 \times 0.9167 \approx 0.8641
$$

$$
U(S_2) = 0.7991 \times 0.9833 \approx 0.7857
$$

$$
U(S_3) = 0.5484 \times 0.8333 \approx 0.4570
$$

**Compute DSQI:**

$$
\text{DSQI} = (0.50)(0.8641) + (0.30)(0.7857) + (0.20)(0.4570)
$$

$$
\text{DSQI} = 0.43205 + 0.23571 + 0.09140 = 0.7592
$$

**Interpretation:** A DSQI of $0.76$ indicates a **moderately healthy** data ecosystem. The weakest link is $S_3$ (Twitter API), with utility only $0.457$ — the firm should either invest in better social-media cleaning or reduce $w_3$ (the importance weight) below $0.20$.

---

## 3.2 Decision Framework — When to Choose Primary vs. Secondary

Use the following three-question test (a **decision tree** in spirit):

1. **Is the data publicly available with adequate quality?** → If YES, prefer *secondary* (lower cost, faster).
2. **Is the time-to-decision less than the secondary collection lag?** → If YES, you must commission *primary* collection.
3. **Is the analytical precision worth the marginal cost?** → If YES, supplement secondary with primary.

A formal cost-benefit inequality can be written as:

$$
C_{\text{primary}} + C_{\text{clean}} \le C_{\text{secondary}} + C_{\text{error}} \cdot P(\text{decision error})
$$

where $C_{\text{error}}$ is the expected monetary loss from a wrong decision, and $P(\text{decision error})$ is its probability. If the inequality holds, primary collection is economically justified.

---

## 3.3 Source Integration Patterns

| Pattern | Description | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Federation** | Query multiple sources in place, no copying | Real-time dashboards |
| **Warehousing (ETL)** | Extract → Transform → Load into central store | Historical BI |
| **Data Lake** | Store raw, schema-on-read | ML, exploratory analytics |
| **Data Mesh** | Domain-owned, decentralized sources | Large enterprises |
| **Streaming (Kafka)** | Continuous ingestion from event sources | IoT, clickstream |

---

## 3.4 Ethical and Legal Considerations (2024 Emphasis)

KTU 2024 explicitly highlights **data ethics** as a transversal theme. Key principles:

- **Consent:** Primary data must be collected with informed consent.
- **Privacy:** Comply with the **Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023 (DPDPA)** in India, and **GDPR** for any EU-resident data.
- **Provenance:** Document the origin and chain of custody of every secondary source.
- **Bias:** Be alert to historical biases embedded in secondary data (e.g., census under-counting of marginalized groups).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Valuation Trap:** Examiners frequently deduct 1–2 marks for **failing to mention consent, privacy, and provenance** when describing a primary data source. Always append an ethical note, even in a 3-mark question.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
Differentiate clearly between **Primary** and **Secondary** data sources. State one advantage and one limitation of each.

**Model Answer (Board-Exam Standard):**

**Primary Data:** Data collected *for the first time* by the researcher, specifically tailored to the problem at hand. It is original, current, and highly relevant.

- *Advantage:* Maximum relevance and accuracy for the specific research objective.
- *Limitation:* Expensive, time-consuming, and limited in geographical/temporal scope.

**Secondary Data:** Data *already collected* by someone else for a different purpose and reused for the current analysis.

- *Advantage:* Low cost, time-efficient, and often available in large volumes (e.g., census).
- *Limitation:* May be outdated, may not perfectly match the current research question, and inherits the methodology flaws of the original collector.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Split:** Definition (1) + Advantage (1) + Limitation (1) = **3 marks**.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
List and briefly define the **Five V's of Big Data** as data source characteristics.

**Model Answer:**

1. **Volume:** The sheer quantity of data generated and stored, typically measured in Terabytes, Petabytes, or Zettabytes ($1 \text{ ZB} = 10^{21}$ bytes).
2. **Velocity:** The speed at which data is generated, captured, and processed — ranging from batch (daily) to real-time (millisecond) streams.
3. **Variety:** The diversity of data types and formats — structured (RDBMS), semi-structured (JSON, XML), and unstructured (text, image, audio, video).
4. **Veracity:** The trustworthiness, accuracy, and reliability of the data — including completeness and consistency.
5. **Value:** The measurable business worth that can be extracted from a given dataset, often via analytical or ML models.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Split:** Five V's × 0.5 mark for naming + 0.5 mark for definition = **3 marks**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A — Choice 1 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3, Adapted]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
Classify data sources along the **Primary/Secondary** and **Internal/External** axes, producing a **2 × 2 matrix**. For each of the four cells, give one **real-world business example** and explain its strategic implication.

#### Model Answer — Part (a)

**The 2 × 2 Classification Matrix:**

|  | **Internal** | **External** |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary** | **Cell I:** Data collected by the firm, from inside, for a specific purpose. *Example:* A retail chain runs a customer-exit survey at its own outlets to study basket abandonment. *Implication:* High control, perfect fit, but high per-record cost. | **Cell II:** Data collected by the firm from outside sources, for a specific purpose. *Example:* A FMCG company commissions a bespoke market-research study in Tier-3 Indian cities to gauge rural demand. *Implication:* Solves information gap that secondary data cannot fill; high cost, long lead time. |
| **Secondary** | **Cell III:** Data already collected by the firm, reused. *Example:* Five years of historical sales from the ERP are reused to train a demand-forecasting model. *Implication:* Near-zero marginal cost, high reliability, but bounded by past data quality. | **Cell IV:** Data already collected by others, reused. *Example:* Census of India 2011 / 2021 demographic data is reused for store-location optimization. *Implication:* Cheap, fast, large sample, but potential fit mismatch and outdated. |

**Strategic Rule of Thumb:**
- Cells I and III are the "core" data assets of the firm.
- Cells II and IV are acquired/auxiliary sources.
- A balanced analytics strategy combines *at least one Internal + one External* source to gain both operational depth and market context.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Split (7 marks):** Matrix structure (2) + Four examples (4 × 1) + Strategic synthesis (1) = **7 marks**.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
A bank is building a **credit-default prediction model**. It has access to four data sources:

| $S_i$ | Description | $V_c$ | $V_k$ | $V_a$ | $L$ (hours) | $L_{\max}$ (hours) | $w_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $S_1$ | Internal loan ledger | 0.99 | 0.98 | 0.97 | 2 | 24 | 0.40 |
| $S_2$ | Internal mobile-app logs | 0.92 | 0.88 | 0.85 | 1 | 24 | 0.20 |
| $S_3$ | External CIBIL bureau | 0.95 | 0.96 | 0.94 | 12 | 24 | 0.30 |
| $S_4$ | External social-media sentiment | 0.65 | 0.55 | 0.60 | 6 | 24 | 0.10 |

Using equal sub-weights $\alpha_1 = \alpha_2 = \alpha_3 = \dfrac{1}{3}$, compute the **Data Source Quality Index (DSQI)** and recommend whether the bank should proceed to modelling.

#### Model Answer — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Trust scores $T(S_i) = (V_c \cdot V_k \cdot V_a)^{1/3}$:**

For $S_1$:

$$
T(S_1) = (0.99 \times 0.98 \times 0.97)^{1/3} = (0.9413)^{1/3} \approx 0.9801
$$

For $S_2$:

$$
T(S_2) = (0.92 \times 0.88 \times 0.85)^{1/3} = (0.6882)^{1/3} \approx 0.8814
$$

For $S_3$:

$$
T(S_3) = (0.95 \times 0.96 \times 0.94)^{1/3} = (0.8573)^{1/3} \approx 0.9507
$$

For $S_4$:

$$
T(S_4) = (0.65 \times 0.55 \times 0.60)^{1/3} = (0.2145)^{1/3} \approx 0.5984
$$

**Step 2 — Freshness $F(S_i) = 1 - L(S_i) / L_{\max}$:**

$$
F(S_1) = 1 - \frac{2}{24} = 0.9167
$$

$$
F(S_2) = 1 - \frac{1}{24} = 0.9583
$$

$$
F(S_3) = 1 - \frac{12}{24} = 0.5000
$$

$$
F(S_4) = 1 - \frac{6}{24} = 0.7500
$$

**Step 3 — Per-source utility $U(S_i) = T(S_i) \cdot F(S_i)$:**

$$
U(S_1) = 0.9801 \times 0.9167 \approx 0.8984
$$

$$
U(S_2) = 0.8814 \times 0.9583 \approx 0.8447
$$

$$
U(S_3) = 0.9507 \times 0.5000 \approx 0.4754
$$

$$
U(S_4) = 0.5984 \times 0.7500 \approx 0.4488
$$

**Step 4 — Weighted sum (DSQI):**

$$
\text{DSQI} = (0.40)(0.8984) + (0.20)(0.8447) + (0.30)(0.4754) + (0.10)(0.4488)
$$

$$
\text{DSQI} = 0.3594 + 0.1689 + 0.1426 + 0.0449
$$

$$
\text{DSQI} \approx 0.7158
$$

**Step 5 — Recommendation:**

Since $\text{DSQI} = 0.7158 < 0.80$ (the recommended threshold), the bank should **not proceed to modelling in its current state**. Two remediation actions are advised:

1. **Improve CIBIL integration** — move from a 12-hour batch feed to a near-real-time API to raise $F(S_3)$ from $0.50$ to $0.95+$, which would lift DSQI to approximately $0.815$.
2. **Re-weight or drop $S_4$** — social-media sentiment is dragging down the average. Either reduce $w_4$ to $0.05$ or replace it with a higher-quality bureau feed.

After remediation, $\text{DSQI}$ can comfortably exceed the $0.80$ threshold, and modelling can proceed with confidence.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Split (7 marks):** Trust computation (2) + Freshness (1) + Utility (1) + DSQI weighted sum (2) + Recommendation with threshold (1) = **7 marks**.

---

### Question B — Choice 2 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3, Adapted]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
Explain the **structured, semi-structured, and unstructured** data classification with **two examples each**. Discuss the technical challenges of analysing unstructured data and the modern toolchain used to address them.

#### Model Answer — Part (a)

**1. Structured Data** — Data conforming to a predefined schema, typically tabular, with fixed rows, columns, and data types. Stored in RDBMS.

- *Example 1:* Customer master table in a bank: `cust_id (INT), name (VARCHAR), dob (DATE), balance (DECIMAL)`.
- *Example 2:* Daily sales ledger in a POS system: `txn_id, sku, qty, price, timestamp`.

**2. Semi-Structured Data** — Data with organizational tags or markers but no rigid relational schema. Hierarchical or key-value in nature.

- *Example 1:* A JSON payload from a payment gateway: `{"txn_id":"T001","amount":500,"status":"SUCCESS","card_type":"VISA"}`.
- *Example 2:* Email files with headers (`From`, `To`, `Subject`) but free-form body.

**3. Unstructured Data** — Data with no predefined schema, accounting for **$\approx 80$–$90\%$** of enterprise data volumes.

- *Example 1:* Free-text customer reviews on an e-commerce site.
- *Example 2:* CCTV footage from a manufacturing plant's assembly line.

**Challenges of Unstructured Data Analysis:**

- **Storage:** Cannot fit neatly into RDBMS rows; needs object stores (Hadoop HDFS, AWS S3).
- **Search/Retrieval:** Lacks indexing fields; requires full-text search engines (Elasticsearch).
- **Semantic Understanding:** Text requires NLP (tokenization, lemmatization, embeddings); images require CV (CNNs); audio requires ASR.
- **Integration:** Hard to join with structured tables; needs entity resolution and schema mapping.
- **Quality:** Noisy, redundant, and ambiguous; needs cleaning pipelines.

**Modern Toolchain:**

- **Storage:** HDFS, Amazon S3, Azure Data Lake.
- **Processing:** Apache Spark, Apache Flink.
- **NLP:** NLTK, spaCy, HuggingFace Transformers, BERT embeddings.
- **Computer Vision:** OpenCV, YOLO, ResNet, vision transformers.
- **Search:** Elasticsearch, Solr.
- **Orchestration:** Apache Airflow, Databricks Workflows.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Split (7 marks):** Three-class definition (3 × 1) + Six examples (2 marks, bonus awarded for richness) + Toolchain (2) = **7 marks**.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Scenario:** An Indian insurance company wants to build an analytical model to detect fraudulent claims. Identify **at least four data sources** (mixing primary, secondary, internal, and external) that the company should integrate. Justify the inclusion of each source in terms of the **5V's of Big Data** and the **DSQI** improvement it brings.

#### Model Answer — Part (b)

**Recommended Data Sources:**

1. **Internal Claims Database (Primary + Internal, Structured):**
   Captures historical claim records, including `claim_id`, `amount`, `policy_id`, `incident_date`, `status`.
   - **5V Mapping:** High Veracity (own validated data), moderate Volume, low Velocity (batch).
   - **DSQI Contribution:** Trust ≈ 0.95, Freshness = 0.90 → Utility ≈ 0.86 — the backbone of the model.

2. **Internal Customer-Interaction Logs (Primary + Internal, Semi-Structured):**
   Call-centre transcripts, chatbot logs, and email correspondence in JSON/text.
   - **5V Mapping:** High Variety (text + structured), high Velocity.
   - **DSQI Contribution:** Trust ≈ 0.75, Freshness = 0.95 → Utility ≈ 0.71 — adds behavioural signal.

3. **External Credit Bureau Feed (Secondary + External, Structured):**
   CIBIL/Credit Information Bureau scores for the claimant.
   - **5V Mapping:** Strong Veracity, moderate Volume, low Velocity.
   - **DSQI Contribution:** Trust ≈ 0.92, Freshness = 0.70 → Utility ≈ 0.64 — third-party validation lift.

4. **External Police / FIR Records (Secondary + External, Semi-Structured):**
   Government open-data portals and state police FIR databases.
   - **5V Mapping:** High Volume, moderate Veracity (manual entry prone to errors), low Velocity.
   - **DSQI Contribution:** Trust ≈ 0.65, Freshness = 0.50 → Utility ≈ 0.33 — useful but noisy.

5. *(Optional — bonus)* **IoT Telematics from Insured Vehicles (Primary + Internal, Unstructured Stream):**
   Accelerometer and GPS streams from company-issued devices.
   - **5V Mapping:** Extreme Volume + Velocity, high Veracity (sensor-grade), high Variety.
   - **DSQI Contribution:** Trust ≈ 0.85, Freshness = 0.99 → Utility ≈ 0.84 — the modern "killer" source for auto-fraud.

**Composite Reasoning:**

The combined DSQI after integration should comfortably exceed the **$0.80$** threshold if sources 1, 2, and 3 carry weights summing to $\ge 0.70$, and source 5 (telematics) is added as a high-utility real-time stream. Sources 4 (FIR) and 3 (CIBIL) act as **external validation** — they raise the **Veracity** dimension disproportionately because they cross-check the firm's own data against third-party ground truth.

**Strategic Justification:**
- The 5V lens forces the firm to deliberately balance Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, and Value.
- Without internal primary data (sources 1, 2), the model is context-blind.
- Without external secondary data (sources 3, 4), the model is echo-chamber-biased.
- IoT telematics (source 5) demonstrates how a *single high-utility source* can lift the entire DSQI and unlock predictive power that structured data alone cannot.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Split (7 marks):** Four sources with type labels (2) + 5V mapping (2) + DSQI justification (2) + Strategic synthesis (1) = **7 marks**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Internal" with "Primary"** — They are *orthogonal* axes. A customer survey conducted by an external agency is *primary* but *external*. Most students mistakenly call it "internal" because they perceive "survey" as a proprietary activity. **Lose 1 mark per misuse.**
> 2. **Omitting the 5V's** — Any 7-mark or 14-mark question on data sources that does not mention the **5V's** is automatically penalized **2–3 marks** under the 2024 scheme.
> 3. **No quantitative working** — In DSQI-style numerical questions, showing only the formula and the final answer without intermediate computations (logarithms, products, weighted sum) is treated as "incomplete" and **1–2 marks are docked**.
> 4. **Forgetting ethics** — A primary-data answer without a mention of **informed consent, DPDPA/GDPR, or data provenance** is treated as a 1-mark deduction by strict evaluators.
> 5. **Single-source bias** — A common answer trap: students list *only one* data source for a real-world problem. The KTU 2024 scheme explicitly rewards **multi-source integration** answers. Always propose *at least 3–4 sources* spanning internal/external and primary/secondary.
> 6. **Spelling "Zettabyte" as "Zettabite" / "Zetabyte"** — Examiners deduct **0.5 mark** for incorrect technical spelling of key data-scale units (Petabyte, Exabyte, Zettabyte, Yottabyte).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** A data source is the origin point of raw facts feeding an analytics pipeline — its quality bounds every downstream model.
- **2 × 2 Matrix (Most Important!):** Primary/Secondary × Internal/External — must be reproducible from memory.
- **Six Canonical Categories:** Primary, Secondary, Internal, External, Structured/Unstructured, Big Data (5V's), Open/Proprietary.
- **5 V's of Big Data:** **V**olume, **V**elocity, **V**ariety, **V**eracity, **V**alue — listed and defined in this order.
- **Structured vs. Unstructured:** $\approx 80$–$90\%$ of enterprise data is unstructured; structured fits RDBMS rows/columns.
- **DSQI Formula (Recall without notes):**
  $$
  \text{DSQI} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot T(S_i) \cdot \left(1 - \frac{L(S_i)}{L_{\max}}\right)
  $$
- **Trust Score Sub-dimensions:** $V_c$ (completeness), $V_k$ (consistency), $V_a$ (accuracy) — combined via geometric mean with sub-weights $\alpha_1, \alpha_2, \alpha_3$ summing to 1.
- **Cost-per-Record:** $\text{CPR} = (C_{\text{collect}} + C_{\text{clean}}) / N_{\text{usable}}$ — true cost of analytics-ready data.
- **Latency Normalization:** $F(S_i) = 1 - L(S_i) / L_{\max}$ — real-time sources score $F = 1$.
- **Primary Data Collection Methods:** Surveys, Interviews, Observations, Experiments, IoT/Sensors, Transaction Logs — six in total.
- **Secondary Data Sources (External):** Government census, industry reports (Nielsen, Gartner), published academic datasets, syndicated research.
- **Big Data Scale Units:** $1 \text{ KB} = 10^3$, $1 \text{ MB} = 10^6$, $1 \text{ GB} = 10^9$, $1 \text{ TB} = 10^{12}$, $1 \text{ PB} = 10^{15}$, $1 \text{ EB} = 10^{18}$, $1 \text{ ZB} = 10^{21}$, $1 \text{ YB} = 10^{24}$ bytes.
- **Integration Patterns:** Federation, Warehousing (ETL), Data Lake, Data Mesh, Streaming (Kafka).
- **Ethics (2024 Emphasis):** Consent, Privacy (DPDPA 2023, GDPR), Provenance, Bias-awareness — must appear in any primary-data answer.
- **DSQI Threshold Rule of Thumb:** $\text{DSQI} \ge 0.80$ → proceed to modelling; $\text{DSQI} < 0.80$ → source remediation required.
- **"Garbage In, Garbage Out" (GIGO):** Mention this principle in any introductory question on data quality.
- **Always propose multi-source answers** — single-source answers are penalized under the 2024 scheme.
- **Practical Takeaway:** For any business problem, the *right* data-source mix is *at least one internal primary + one external secondary* for triangulation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Preparations
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 3: DATA PREPARATIONS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Data Preparation** (also termed *Data Preprocessing* or *Data Wrangling*) is the foundational phase in the Business Analytics (BA) and Data Mining (DM) lifecycle wherein raw, heterogeneous, and noisy datasets are systematically cleaned, integrated, transformed, reduced, and discretized to produce a refined, analysis-ready dataset that is suitable for downstream modeling, statistical inference, and machine learning algorithms.

In the context of the **CRISP-DM (Cross-Industry Standard Process for Data Mining)** and **KTU 2024 BA Curriculum**, Data Preparation is formally defined as the process of converting raw enterprise data (from OLTP systems, IoT streams, surveys, social media, and web logs) into a consolidated, consistent, and quality-assured analytical base table (ABT).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Standard):**
> *"Data Preparation is the iterative and investigative process of transforming raw, real-world data into a clean, integrated, and analytically valuable format through techniques such as data cleaning, integration, transformation, reduction, and discretization, thereby ensuring data quality, consistency, and compatibility with selected analytical models."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **chef preparing a multi-course meal** for a VIP guest.

- The **raw ingredients** (vegetables from the market, spices, meat) arrive dirty, uncut, inconsistent in size, and may contain some rotten pieces.
- Before you can cook, you must:
  1. **Wash** the vegetables (removing dirt and impurities) → equivalent to **Data Cleaning**
  2. **Peel and cut** them into uniform pieces (standardizing format) → equivalent to **Data Transformation**
  3. **Discard the rotten** ones and the inedible parts → equivalent to **Handling Missing Values & Outliers**
  4. **Combine ingredients** from different sources (vegetables from one shop, spices from another) into one consistent recipe → equivalent to **Data Integration**
  5. **Reduce excess** by trimming fat or pre-cooking to concentrate flavors → equivalent to **Data Reduction**
  6. **Categorize** ingredients by type (spices, vegetables, proteins) for easy retrieval → equivalent to **Data Discretization**

Without this preparation, even the best recipe (your analytics algorithm) will fail. **Garbage In = Garbage Out (GIGO)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The GIGO Principle in KTU Context:**
> Industry studies indicate that data scientists spend approximately **60–80%** of their time on data preparation, and the quality of the predictive model is **directly proportional** to the quality of the prepared data. KTU examiners frequently test this principle.

### 1.3 Why Data Preparation is Critical

| Reason | Description |
|---|---|
| **Real-world data is dirty** | Incomplete, noisy, inconsistent, and contains outliers |
| **Algorithm sensitivity** | Distance-based algorithms (KNN, K-Means) and gradient descent are highly sensitive to feature scales |
| **Storage & Computation** | Cleaned and reduced data saves computational resources |
| **Model accuracy** | Properly prepared data improves model performance metrics (Accuracy, F1-Score, RMSE) |
| **Regulatory compliance** | GDPR, HIPAA demand clean, anonymized, and consistent data |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Insight:** A question worth 7 marks often asks students to list and explain the major steps of data preparation. Memorize the **5 Tasks of Data Preparation** as per Han, Kamber & Pei (Data Mining textbook standard referenced in KTU 2024 scheme).

### 1.4 The Five Major Tasks of Data Preparation

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly identifies the following five tasks:

1. **Data Cleaning** – Fill missing values, smooth noisy data, identify/remove outliers, resolve inconsistencies.
2. **Data Integration** – Merge data from multiple sources, handle schema conflicts, and remove redundancy.
3. **Data Transformation** – Normalize, aggregate, smooth, and construct new attributes.
4. **Data Reduction** – Reduce dimensionality, numerosity, and data compression.
5. **Data Discretization** – Convert continuous attributes into categorical (bins/intervals).

---

### 1.5 Data Quality Dimensions (KTU Highlight)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Six Key Dimensions of Data Quality** (these are recurring 3-mark questions):

| Dimension | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Accuracy** | Correctness of values | Age = 250 is inaccurate |
| **Completeness** | Presence of all required values | Missing customer phone number |
| **Consistency** | Agreement across datasets | DOB 01/05/1990 vs. Age declared as 30 in 2020 (conflict) |
| **Timeliness** | Data is up-to-date | Last login 5 years ago |
| **Uniqueness** | No duplicate records | Same customer listed twice |
| **Validity** | Conforms to defined rules/constraints | Salary field contains a string |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Task 1: Data Cleaning

Data cleaning addresses four sub-problems:

### 2.1.1 Handling Missing Values

**Causes of Missing Data:**
- Equipment malfunction (sensor failure)
- Inconsistent data entry
- Data not provided by customers
- Data deletion due to system upgrades
- Historical data not preserved

**Types of Missingness (Rubin's Classification — KTU expects awareness):**

| Type | Notation | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **MCAR** (Missing Completely At Random) | $P(M \mid Y, X) = P(M)$ | Missingness is unrelated to any data | Sensor randomly fails |
| **MAR** (Missing At Random) | $P(M \mid Y, X) = P(M \mid X)$ | Missingness depends on observed variables | Men less likely to fill health survey |
| **MNAR** (Missing Not At Random) | Non-random | Missingness depends on the missing value itself | High earners hide income |

**Methods to Handle Missing Values:**

| Method | Description | Best Used When |
|---|---|---|
| **Ignore / Discard Tuple** | Remove the entire record | Missingness is minimal (<5%) and dataset is large |
| **Manual Imputation** | Domain expert fills values | Small dataset, critical domain |
| **Constant/Class Imputation** | Replace with a fixed value (e.g., "Unknown", 0) | Categorical data |
| **Mean Imputation** | Replace with attribute mean | Numeric, MCAR data, no outliers |
| **Median Imputation** | Replace with attribute median | Numeric data with outliers |
| **Mode Imputation** | Replace with attribute mode | Categorical data |
| **Hot-Deck Imputation** | Use value from similar record | Survey data |
| **Regression Imputation** | Predict missing value from other attributes | When correlation exists |
| **KNN Imputation** | Use $k$ nearest neighbors to impute | Strong pattern-based data |
| **Multiple Imputation (MI)** | Generate multiple plausible values, pool results | Statistical inference tasks |

### 2.1.2 Noisy Data & Smoothing Techniques

**Noise** = random error or variance in a measured variable. Examples: sensor jitter, transcription errors.

**Binning Methods** (Local Smoothing — sorted-data based):

- **Equal-Width Binning:** Divide range into $k$ equal intervals.
- **Equal-Frequency Binning:** Each bin has approximately the same number of values.
- **Binning Means:** Replace values with the bin mean.
- **Binning Boundaries:** Replace with the nearest boundary (min or max).
- **Binning Medians:** Replace with the bin median.

**Other Smoothing Techniques:**
- **Regression:** Fit a regression line/curve to smooth the data.
- **Outlier Analysis:** Detect and remove anomalies using clustering or Z-score.

### 2.1.3 Outlier Detection Methods

An **outlier** is a data point that deviates so significantly from other observations as to arouse suspicion that it was generated by a different mechanism.

| Method | Formula/Approach |
|---|---|
| **Z-Score Method** | $Z = \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$; if $\vert Z \vert > 3$, it is an outlier |
| **IQR Method** | Outlier if $x < Q_1 - 1.5 \cdot IQR$ or $x > Q_3 + 1.5 \cdot IQR$ |
| **Modified Z-Score (MAD)** | $M_i = \dfrac{0.6745 \cdot (x_i - \tilde{x})}{MAD}$ |
| **Clustering-based** | Points that do not belong to any cluster (DBSCAN) |
| **Isolation Forest** | Anomalies are isolated closer to the root of random trees |

### 2.1.4 Data Cleaning as a Process

Steps in real-world data cleaning:
1. **Define and determine error types** (audit data using statistical methods).
2. **Search and identify error instances** (use scripts, queries).
3. **Correct identified errors** (replace, delete, impute).
4. **Document error types and transformations** (audit trail for compliance).

---

## 2.2 Task 2: Data Integration

Data Integration combines data from **multiple heterogeneous sources** into a coherent data store (e.g., data warehouse or analytics-ready table).

### 2.2.1 Major Issues in Data Integration

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Key Integration Issues (Board-Favorite 7-Mark Question):**

1. **Schema Integration & Object Matching**
   - Same attribute with different names across sources (e.g., `customer_id` vs. `cust_id`).
   - Metadata inspection is required.

2. **Redundancy & Correlation Analysis**
   - An attribute may be redundant if it can be derived from another.
   - **Pearson's Chi-Square Test** for categorical redundancy:

$$
\chi^2 = \sum \dfrac{(O_{ij} - E_{ij})^2}{E_{ij}}
$$

   where $O_{ij}$ is the observed frequency and $E_{ij}$ is the expected frequency.

   - **Pearson's Correlation Coefficient** for numeric redundancy:

$$
r_{A,B} = \dfrac{\sum (A_i - \bar{A})(B_i - \bar{B})}{(n-1)\sigma_A \sigma_B}
$$

   If $r_{A,B} > 0$ (strong positive), one attribute may be redundant.

3. **Tuple Duplication**
   - Same data values appear in multiple sources, increasing redundancy.

4. **Data Value Conflicts**
   - Different representations: "M" vs. "Male", "1 kg" vs. "1000 g", currency differences, scale differences.

---

## 2.3 Task 3: Data Transformation

Data transformation applies **functional mappings** to consolidate data into forms appropriate for mining.

### 2.3.1 Smoothing

Apply binning, regression, or clustering to remove noise.

### 2.3.2 Aggregation

Combine daily sales → monthly or yearly sales (data cube operations like roll-up).

### 2.3.3 Normalization (Critical — KTU High-Yield Topic)

> [!NOTE]
> **Normalization** scales attribute values to a small specified range, typically $[-1, 1]$ or $[0, 1]$. It is essential for **distance-based algorithms** (KNN, K-Means, Neural Networks, PCA).

**Three Primary Normalization Methods:**

**(a) Min-Max Normalization**

$$
v' = \dfrac{v - \min(A)}{\max(A) - \min(A)} \cdot (new\_max - new\_min) + new\_min
$$

For range $[0, 1]$:

$$
v' = \dfrac{v - \min(A)}{\max(A) - \min(A)}
$$

**(b) Z-Score Normalization (Standardization)**

$$
v' = \dfrac{v - \mu}{\sigma}
$$

Result has mean = 0 and standard deviation = 1.

**(c) Decimal Scaling Normalization**

$$
v' = \dfrac{v}{10^j}
$$

where $j$ is the smallest integer such that $\max(\vert v' \vert) < 1$.

### 2.3.4 Attribute Construction (Feature Engineering)

New attributes are constructed from existing ones to enhance mining accuracy.
- Example: Construct `BMI = weight / height^2` from `weight` and `height` attributes.

---

## 2.4 Task 4: Data Reduction

Data reduction obtains a **reduced representation** of the dataset that is much smaller in volume yet produces the same (or nearly the same) analytical results.

### 2.4.1 Dimensionality Reduction

| Technique | Description |
|---|---|
| **PCA (Principal Component Analysis)** | Linear projection to top $k$ eigenvectors of covariance matrix |
| **LDA (Linear Discriminant Analysis)** | Supervised dimensionality reduction |
| **t-SNE / UMAP** | Non-linear dimensionality reduction for visualization |

### 2.4.2 Numerosity Reduction

- **Parametric Methods:** Regression (linear/logistic) and log-linear models.
- **Non-Parametric Methods:**
  - **Histograms** (equal-width, equal-frequency, max-diff).
  - **Clustering** (data grouped into clusters; cluster representatives stored).
  - **Sampling** (random, stratified, cluster).

### 2.4.3 Data Compression

- **Lossless Compression:** Huffman coding, run-length encoding.
- **Lossy Compression:** Wavelet transforms (DWT), PCA, JPEG/MP3 style compression.

---

## 2.5 Task 5: Data Discretization & Concept Hierarchy Generation

Discretization converts **continuous attributes** into **categorical (interval-based) attributes**.

### 2.5.1 Why Discretize?

- Required by some algorithms (e.g., Naive Bayes, association rule mining with categorical data).
- Makes data more compact and easier to interpret.
- Enables concept hierarchy generation.

### 2.5.2 Discretization Methods

| Category | Method |
|---|---|
| **Unsupervised** | Binning (equal-width, equal-frequency, K-means), CAIM |
| **Supervised** | Entropy-based (ID3-like), ChiMerge |
| **Top-Down (Splitting)** | Start with one interval → recursively split (e.g., ChiMerge, Entropy) |
| **Bottom-Up (Merging)** | Start with each value in its own interval → merge (e.g., ChiMerge bottom-up variant) |

### 2.5.3 Concept Hierarchy Generation

For nominal (categorical) data, partial/total orderings create hierarchies:

- Example: `City < State < Country` (geographical hierarchy).
- Example: `Manager < Senior Manager < Director < VP < CEO` (organizational hierarchy).

---

## 2.6 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Min-Max Normalization | $v' = \dfrac{v - \min_A}{\max_A - \min_A}$ | Scale to $[0, 1]$ |
| 2 | Z-Score Normalization | $v' = \dfrac{v - \mu}{\sigma}$ | Standardize (mean 0, std 1) |
| 3 | Decimal Scaling | $v' = \dfrac{v}{10^j}$ | Scale to $[-1, 1]$ |
| 4 | Z-Score Outlier | $Z = \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$, threshold $\vert Z \vert > 3$ | Detect outliers |
| 5 | IQR Outlier | $x < Q_1 - 1.5 \cdot IQR$ or $x > Q_3 + 1.5 \cdot IQR$ | Robust outlier detection |
| 6 | MAD-based Z | $M_i = \dfrac{0.6745(x_i - \tilde{x})}{MAD}$ | Robust for small samples |
| 7 | Pearson Correlation | $r_{A,B} = \dfrac{\sum (A_i - \bar{A})(B_i - \bar{B})}{(n-1)\sigma_A \sigma_B}$ | Detect redundancy |
| 8 | Chi-Square | $\chi^2 = \sum \dfrac{(O_{ij} - E_{ij})^2}{E_{ij}}$ | Categorical redundancy |
| 9 | Expected Frequency | $E_{ij} = \dfrac{(\text{row total}_i)(\text{col total}_j)}{n}$ | Chi-Square prerequisite |
| 10 | BMI (Example) | $BMI = \dfrac{w}{h^2}$ | Feature construction |
| 11 | Covariance | $\text{Cov}(A,B) = \dfrac{1}{n-1}\sum (A_i - \bar{A})(B_i - \bar{B})$ | PCA prerequisite |
| 12 | Cosine Similarity | $\cos(\theta) = \dfrac{A \cdot B}{\vert \vert A \vert \vert \cdot \vert \vert B \vert \vert}$ | Text normalization |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always quote the **range** when describing Min-Max normalization and the **resulting mean/standard deviation** when describing Z-Score. Do not leave the answer ambiguous.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Utility in Engineering

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Healthcare** | Cleaning EHR (Electronic Health Record) data before disease prediction |
| **Finance** | Fraud detection via outlier analysis; z-score on transactions |
| **IoT** | Sensor noise smoothing; discretization of temperature |
| **E-Commerce** | Customer 360° view via data integration; recommendation engines |
| **Manufacturing** | PCA for quality control with thousands of sensor variables |
| **Telecom** | Churn prediction after handling missing call detail records |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Min-Max Normalization

**Problem:** A dataset of annual incomes (in ₹ Lakhs) is: $\{45, 60, 35, 72, 55, 40, 100\}$. Apply **Min-Max normalization** to transform the values into the range $[0, 1]$.

### Step 1: Identify the min and max

$$
\min(A) = 35, \quad \max(A) = 100
$$

### Step 2: Apply the formula

$$
v' = \dfrac{v - 35}{100 - 35} = \dfrac{v - 35}{65}
$$

### Step 3: Compute each transformed value

$$
\begin{aligned}
v'_{45} &= \dfrac{45 - 35}{65} = \dfrac{10}{65} = 0.1538 \\
v'_{60} &= \dfrac{60 - 35}{65} = \dfrac{25}{65} = 0.3846 \\
v'_{35} &= \dfrac{35 - 35}{65} = 0 \\
v'_{72} &= \dfrac{72 - 35}{65} = \dfrac{37}{65} = 0.5692 \\
v'_{55} &= \dfrac{55 - 35}{65} = \dfrac{20}{65} = 0.3077 \\
v'_{40} &= \dfrac{40 - 35}{65} = \dfrac{5}{65} = 0.0769 \\
v'_{100} &= \dfrac{100 - 35}{65} = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Verification

After transformation, $\min(v') = 0$ and $\max(v') = 1$. ✓

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Z-Score Standardization

**Problem:** For the same dataset $\{45, 60, 35, 72, 55, 40, 100\}$, apply **Z-score normalization**.

### Step 1: Compute the mean

$$
\mu = \dfrac{45 + 60 + 35 + 72 + 55 + 40 + 100}{7} = \dfrac{407}{7} = 58.14
$$

### Step 2: Compute the squared deviations

$$
\begin{aligned}
(45 - 58.14)^2 &= 172.10 \\
(60 - 58.14)^2 &= 3.46 \\
(35 - 58.14)^2 &= 534.74 \\
(72 - 58.14)^2 &= 192.10 \\
(55 - 58.14)^2 &= 9.86 \\
(40 - 58.14)^2 &= 329.30 \\
(100 - 58.14)^2 &= 1750.10
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Sum the squared deviations and compute variance

$$
\sum (x_i - \mu)^2 = 172.10 + 3.46 + 534.74 + 192.10 + 9.86 + 329.30 + 1750.10 = 2991.66
$$

$$
\sigma^2 = \dfrac{2991.66}{7 - 1} = 498.61
$$

### Step 4: Compute standard deviation

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{498.61} = 22.33
$$

### Step 5: Apply Z-Score formula

$$
v' = \dfrac{v - 58.14}{22.33}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
z_{45} &= \dfrac{45 - 58.14}{22.33} = -0.588 \\
z_{60} &= \dfrac{60 - 58.14}{22.33} = 0.083 \\
z_{35} &= \dfrac{35 - 58.14}{22.33} = -1.035 \\
z_{72} &= \dfrac{72 - 58.14}{22.33} = 1.243 \\
z_{55} &= \dfrac{55 - 58.14}{22.33} = -0.140 \\
z_{40} &= \dfrac{40 - 58.14}{22.33} = -0.813 \\
z_{100} &= \dfrac{100 - 58.14}{22.33} = 1.869
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6: Verification

After transformation, the new mean $\approx 0$ and the new standard deviation $\approx 1$. ✓

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Z-Score Outlier Detection

**Problem:** A class of students has scores: $\{45, 50, 55, 48, 52, 49, 51, 53, 47, 95\}$. Identify the outlier using the Z-Score method (threshold $\vert Z \vert > 2$).

### Step 1: Compute mean and standard deviation

$$
\mu = \dfrac{45 + 50 + 55 + 48 + 52 + 49 + 51 + 53 + 47 + 95}{10} = \dfrac{545}{10} = 54.5
$$

### Step 2: Compute squared deviations

$$
\begin{aligned}
(45 - 54.5)^2 &= 90.25 \\
(50 - 54.5)^2 &= 20.25 \\
(55 - 54.5)^2 &= 0.25 \\
(48 - 54.5)^2 &= 42.25 \\
(52 - 54.5)^2 &= 6.25 \\
(49 - 54.5)^2 &= 30.25 \\
(51 - 54.5)^2 &= 12.25 \\
(53 - 54.5)^2 &= 2.25 \\
(47 - 54.5)^2 &= 56.25 \\
(95 - 54.5)^2 &= 1640.25
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Sum and compute $\sigma$

$$
\sum (x_i - \mu)^2 = 1900.50
$$

$$
\sigma^2 = \dfrac{1900.50}{9} = 211.17
$$

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{211.17} = 14.53
$$

### Step 4: Compute Z-scores

$$
\begin{aligned}
z_{45} &= \dfrac{45 - 54.5}{14.53} = -0.65 \\
z_{50} &= \dfrac{50 - 54.5}{14.53} = -0.31 \\
z_{55} &= \dfrac{55 - 54.5}{14.53} = 0.03 \\
z_{95} &= \dfrac{95 - 54.5}{14.53} = 2.79
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Identify the outlier

Since $\vert z_{95} \vert = 2.79 > 2$, the score **95** is an outlier.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4: IQR-based Outlier Detection

**Problem:** For the sorted dataset $\{10, 12, 14, 15, 16, 17, 18, 19, 20, 60\}$, identify outliers using the IQR method.

### Step 1: Find $Q_1$ and $Q_3$

For $n = 10$, split into two halves: lower = $\{10, 12, 14, 15, 16\}$ and upper = $\{17, 18, 19, 20, 60\}$.

$$
Q_1 = 14, \quad Q_3 = 19
$$

### Step 2: Compute IQR

$$
IQR = Q_3 - Q_1 = 19 - 14 = 5
$$

### Step 3: Compute the lower and upper fences

$$
\text{Lower Fence} = Q_1 - 1.5 \cdot IQR = 14 - 1.5 \cdot 5 = 6.5
$$

$$
\text{Upper Fence} = Q_3 + 1.5 \cdot IQR = 19 + 1.5 \cdot 5 = 26.5
$$

### Step 4: Identify outliers

Any value $< 6.5$ or $> 26.5$ is an outlier.

Since $60 > 26.5$, the value **60** is an outlier.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 5: Chi-Square Test for Redundancy

**Problem:** A dataset has 200 customer records classified by `Payment_Mode` (Cash, Card, UPI) and `Receipt_Issued` (Yes, No). Observed frequencies:

| | Receipt_Yes | Receipt_No | Total |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cash** | 30 | 20 | 50 |
| **Card** | 60 | 40 | 100 |
| **UPI** | 40 | 10 | 50 |
| **Total** | 130 | 70 | 200 |

Determine if `Payment_Mode` and `Receipt_Issued` are correlated using $\alpha = 0.05$.

### Step 1: State hypotheses

- $H_0$: The two variables are independent (no redundancy).
- $H_1$: The two variables are dependent (redundancy exists).

### Step 2: Compute expected frequencies

$$
E_{ij} = \dfrac{(\text{Row Total}_i)(\text{Col Total}_j)}{n}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{Cash,Yes}} &= \dfrac{50 \cdot 130}{200} = 32.5 \\
E_{\text{Cash,No}} &= \dfrac{50 \cdot 70}{200} = 17.5 \\
E_{\text{Card,Yes}} &= \dfrac{100 \cdot 130}{200} = 65 \\
E_{\text{Card,No}} &= \dfrac{100 \cdot 70}{200} = 35 \\
E_{\text{UPI,Yes}} &= \dfrac{50 \cdot 130}{200} = 32.5 \\
E_{\text{UPI,No}} &= \dfrac{50 \cdot 70}{200} = 17.5
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Apply the Chi-Square formula

$$
\chi^2 = \sum \dfrac{(O_{ij} - E_{ij})^2}{E_{ij}}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\chi^2_{\text{Cash,Yes}} &= \dfrac{(30 - 32.5)^2}{32.5} = 0.192 \\
\chi^2_{\text{Cash,No}} &= \dfrac{(20 - 17.5)^2}{17.5} = 0.357 \\
\chi^2_{\text{Card,Yes}} &= \dfrac{(60 - 65)^2}{65} = 0.385 \\
\chi^2_{\text{Card,No}} &= \dfrac{(40 - 35)^2}{35} = 0.714 \\
\chi^2_{\text{UPI,Yes}} &= \dfrac{(40 - 32.5)^2}{32.5} = 1.731 \\
\chi^2_{\text{UPI,No}} &= \dfrac{(10 - 17.5)^2}{17.5} = 3.214
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\chi^2_{\text{total}} = 0.192 + 0.357 + 0.385 + 0.714 + 1.731 + 3.214 = 6.593
$$

### Step 4: Determine degrees of freedom

$$
df = (r - 1)(c - 1) = (3 - 1)(2 - 1) = 2
$$

### Step 5: Compare with critical value

Critical value at $\alpha = 0.05$ and $df = 2$ is $\chi^2_{\text{crit}} = 5.991$.

Since $\chi^2_{\text{calc}} = 6.593 > 5.991$, we **reject $H_0$**. The variables are dependent → potential redundancy.

---

## 3.6 Worked Example 6: Decimal Scaling

**Problem:** Normalize the values $\{-128, 50, 12, 4789\}$ using decimal scaling.

### Step 1: Identify $j$

The maximum absolute value is $\vert -4789 \vert = 4789$. We need $j$ such that $\dfrac{4789}{10^j} < 1$.

For $j = 4$: $\dfrac{4789}{10000} = 0.4789 < 1$. ✓

### Step 2: Apply decimal scaling

$$
\begin{aligned}
v'_{-128} &= \dfrac{-128}{10000} = -0.0128 \\
v'_{50} &= \dfrac{50}{10000} = 0.0050 \\
v'_{12} &= \dfrac{12}{10000} = 0.0012 \\
v'_{4789} &= \dfrac{4789}{10000} = 0.4789
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.7 Worked Example 7: Binning by Mean Smoothing

**Problem:** Sorted data: $\{4, 8, 9, 15, 21, 21, 24, 25, 26, 28, 29, 34\}$. Apply equal-frequency binning (4 values per bin) and replace with bin means.

### Step 1: Divide into 3 equal-frequency bins

- **Bin 1:** $\{4, 8, 9, 15\}$
- **Bin 2:** $\{21, 21, 24, 25\}$
- **Bin 3:** $\{26, 28, 29, 34\}$

### Step 2: Compute bin means

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mean}_1 &= \dfrac{4 + 8 + 9 + 15}{4} = \dfrac{36}{4} = 9 \\
\text{Mean}_2 &= \dfrac{21 + 21 + 24 + 25}{4} = \dfrac{91}{4} = 22.75 \\
\text{Mean}_3 &= \dfrac{26 + 28 + 29 + 34}{4} = \dfrac{117}{4} = 29.25
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Smoothing by bin means

$$
\text{Smoothed Data} = \{9, 9, 9, 9, 22.75, 22.75, 22.75, 22.75, 29.25, 29.25, 29.25, 29.25\}
$$

---

## 3.8 Worked Example 8: Binning by Boundary Smoothing

Using the same data, apply **boundary smoothing** (replace with nearest bin boundary).

### Step 1: Bin boundaries

- **Bin 1:** $\{4, 8, 9, 15\}$ → boundaries: $\min = 4$, $\max = 15$
- **Bin 2:** $\{21, 21, 24, 25\}$ → boundaries: $\min = 21$, $\max = 25$
- **Bin 3:** $\{26, 28, 29, 34\}$ → boundaries: $\min = 26$, $\max = 34$

### Step 2: Replace each value with the nearest boundary

$$
\begin{aligned}
4 &\to 4, & 8 &\to 4, & 9 &\to 15, & 15 &\to 15 \\
21 &\to 21, & 21 &\to 21, & 24 &\to 25, & 25 &\to 25 \\
26 &\to 26, & 28 &\to 34, & 29 &\to 34, & 34 &\to 34
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.9 Analytical Matrix: When to Use What?

| Problem | Recommended Technique | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Outliers in small dataset | MAD-based Z-Score | Robust to outliers in small samples |
| Outliers in normal distribution | Z-Score | Assumes normality |
| Outliers in skewed distribution | IQR | Non-parametric |
| Min/Max known, no outliers | Min-Max Normalization | Preserves relationships |
| Outliers present | Z-Score Normalization | Min-Max is sensitive to outliers |
| Different units (kg, cm) | Z-Score or Min-Max | Standardize scale |
| Very large range | Decimal Scaling | Quick and simple |
| Categorical redundancy | Chi-Square | Non-parametric |
| Numeric redundancy | Pearson Correlation | Parametric, linear |
| Compression required | Wavelet / PCA | Reduces dimensionality |
| Need to bin continuous | Entropy / ChiMerge | Supervised splitting |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Data Preparation Pipeline (End-to-End Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Data Sources] --> B[Data Cleaning]
    B --> C[Data Integration]
    C --> D[Data Transformation]
    D --> E[Data Reduction]
    E --> F[Data Discretization]
    F --> G[Analytics Ready Dataset]

    A1[OLTP Databases] --> A
    A2[IoT Sensors] --> A
    A3[Surveys and Forms] --> A
    A4[Web and Social Logs] --> A

    B --> B1[Handle Missing Values]
    B --> B2[Smooth Noisy Data]
    B --> B3[Detect Outliers]
    B --> B4[Correct Inconsistencies]

    C --> C1[Schema Matching]
    C --> C2[Resolve Conflicts]
    C --> C3[Remove Redundancy]

    D --> D1[Normalization]
    D --> D2[Aggregation]
    D --> D3[Attribute Construction]

    E --> E1[Dimensionality Reduction]
    E --> E2[Numerosity Reduction]
    E --> E3[Data Compression]

    F --> F1[Binning]
    F --> F2[Concept Hierarchy]
    F --> F3[Cluster Analysis]
```

## 4.2 Data Cleaning Sub-Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Data] --> B{Is Data Missing?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Imputation or Deletion]
    B -- No --> D{Is Data Noisy?}
    C --> D
    D -- Yes --> E[Smoothing Binning, Regression]
    D -- No --> F{Outliers Present?}
    E --> F
    F -- Yes --> G[Z-Score or IQR Detection]
    F -- No --> H[Cleaned Data]
    G --> H
```

## 4.3 Normalization Methods Comparison Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Choose Normalization] --> B{Is Data Bounded?}
    B -- Yes and No Outliers --> C[Min-Max]
    B -- No Outliers Unknown --> D[Z-Score Standardization]
    B -- Very Large Range --> E[Decimal Scaling]
    B -- Text or Sparse Data --> F[Cosine Normalization]

    C --> C1[Range 0 to 1]
    D --> D1[Mean 0 Std 1]
    E --> E1[Divide by Power of 10]
    F --> F1[Unit Vector Projection]
```

## 4.4 Outlier Detection Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Outlier Analysis] --> B{Data Distribution?}
    B -- Normal --> C[Z-Score Method]
    B -- Skewed --> D[IQR Method]
    B -- Small Sample --> E[MAD Z-Score]
    B -- Unsupervised High Dim --> F[Isolation Forest]

    C --> C1[Threshold Z greater than 3]
    D --> D1[Fence Q1 minus 1.5 IQR to Q3 plus 1.5 IQR]
    E --> E1[Threshold M greater than 3.5]
    F --> F1[Short Path Length Anomaly]
```

## 4.5 CRISP-DM Positioning of Data Preparation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[1 Business Understanding] --> P2[2 Data Understanding]
    P2 --> P3[3 Data Preparation]
    P3 --> P4[4 Modeling]
    P4 --> P5[5 Evaluation]
    P5 --> P6[6 Deployment]
    P6 -. Feedback .-> P3
    P6 -. Feedback .-> P2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Insight:** The CRISP-DM diagram above frequently appears in KTU module questions. The two-way feedback loops from Deployment to Data Understanding and Data Preparation highlight the **iterative** nature of analytics.
