# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Data Preparation. List its major tasks.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Data Preparation** is the process of cleaning, integrating, transforming, reducing, and discretizing raw data to make it suitable for analytics and mining.
>
> **Major Tasks:**
> 1. **Data Cleaning** – Fill missing values, smooth noise, remove outliers.
> 2. **Data Integration** – Combine multiple sources, handle schema conflicts.
> 3. **Data Transformation** – Normalize, aggregate, construct attributes.
> 4. **Data Reduction** – Reduce dimensionality, numerosity, compress data.
> 5. **Data Discretization** – Convert continuous values to categorical bins.

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Explain the GIGO principle. Why is data quality critical in Business Analytics?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **GIGO (Garbage In, Garbage Out)** is a fundamental principle stating that the quality of analytical output is directly determined by the quality of input data. If raw data is noisy, incomplete, or inconsistent, the resulting models, predictions, and business decisions will be unreliable.
>
> Data quality is critical because:
> 1. **Model performance degrades** with poor-quality inputs (e.g., low accuracy, high RMSE).
> 2. **Business decisions** based on flawed analytics can lead to financial loss.
> 3. **Regulatory compliance** (GDPR, HIPAA) requires clean, auditable data.
> 4. Studies show that **60–80%** of analytics effort goes into data preparation alone.

---

## 📝 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the different methods of handling **missing values** in a dataset. Discuss the scenarios in which each method is appropriate. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable example, explain the **Min-Max Normalization** and **Z-Score Normalization** techniques. Compare their advantages and limitations. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Part (a):

**Methods of Handling Missing Values:**

1. **Ignore the Tuple (Listwise Deletion):**
   - Simply discard the record with missing value.
   - **Suitable when:** Missingness is very small (<5%) and the dataset is large.
   - **Limitation:** Can introduce bias if data is not MCAR.

2. **Manual Imputation:**
   - Domain expert fills the missing value manually.
   - **Suitable when:** Dataset is small and the missing attribute is critical.
   - **Limitation:** Time-consuming, subjective.

3. **Constant / Class Imputation:**
   - Replace with a constant such as "Unknown" or 0.
   - **Suitable when:** Categorical data with rare missingness.

4. **Mean / Median / Mode Imputation:**
   - Replace with central tendency measure.
   - **Mean** for symmetric numeric data, **Median** for skewed numeric data with outliers, **Mode** for categorical data.
   - **Limitation:** Reduces variance; ignores correlation.

5. **Hot-Deck Imputation:**
   - Use the value of a similar (donor) record.
   - **Suitable when:** Survey data and imputation must preserve distribution.

6. **Regression Imputation:**
   - Predict the missing value using a regression model on other attributes.
   - **Suitable when:** Strong correlation exists between attributes.

7. **K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN) Imputation:**
   - Use the values of $k$ most similar records.
   - **Suitable when:** Pattern-based data; preserves local structure.

8. **Multiple Imputation (MI):**
   - Generate $m > 1$ plausible values, run analysis on each, pool the results.
   - **Suitable when:** Statistical inference where uncertainty must be quantified.

**Conclusion:** Choice depends on missingness type (MCAR, MAR, MNAR), percentage of missingness, and downstream task.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Part (b):

**Min-Max Normalization:**

Performs a linear transformation on the original data to map it into a specified range, typically $[0, 1]$.

$$
v' = \dfrac{v - \min_A}{\max_A - \min_A}
$$

**Example:** Income dataset $\{45, 60, 35, 72, 55, 40, 100\}$.
- $\min = 35$, $\max = 100$, range $= 65$.
- For $v = 60$: $v' = \dfrac{60 - 35}{65} = 0.3846$.

**Advantages:**
- Preserves original distribution shape.
- All values are bounded within a known range.

**Limitations:**
- Highly sensitive to **outliers** (since $\min$ and $\max$ are used).
- Requires knowledge of $\min$ and $\max$, which may not be available in streaming data.

**Z-Score Normalization:**

Transforms data to have mean $\mu = 0$ and standard deviation $\sigma = 1$.

$$
v' = \dfrac{v - \mu}{\sigma}
$$

**Example (same dataset):**
- $\mu = 58.14$, $\sigma = 22.33$.
- For $v = 60$: $v' = \dfrac{60 - 58.14}{22.33} = 0.083$.

**Advantages:**
- Robust to outliers (uses mean and std, not min/max).
- Useful when distribution is approximately Gaussian.

**Limitations:**
- Does not bound values to a specific range (e.g., output may be $-2$ or $+5$).
- Assumes approximate normality of the data.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Min-Max | Z-Score |
|---|---|---|
| Range | Bounded $[0, 1]$ | Unbounded (typically $[-3, 3]$) |
| Outlier Sensitivity | High | Low |
| Use Case | Neural networks, image processing | PCA, regression, K-Means |
| Knowledge Required | $\min$, $\max$ | $\mu$, $\sigma$ |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – May 2024 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** What is **Data Discretization**? Explain any two unsupervised discretization techniques with examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the major issues in **Data Integration**. Explain how correlation analysis is used to detect redundancy between two numerical attributes. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Part (a):

**Data Discretization** is the process of converting continuous-valued attributes into discrete categorical attributes (bins or intervals). It is essential for algorithms like Naive Bayes, association rule mining, and decision trees, which require categorical input.

**Techniques:**

1. **Binning (Equal-Width Binning):**
   - The range $[\min, \max]$ is divided into $k$ equal-width intervals.
   - Each interval width = $\dfrac{\max - \min}{k}$.
   - **Example:** For $\{4, 8, 15, 21, 24, 28, 34\}$ with $k = 3$:
     - Width = $\dfrac{34 - 4}{3} = 10$.
     - Bin 1: $[4, 14]$, Bin 2: $[14, 24]$, Bin 3: $[24, 34]$.
   - **Limitation:** Sensitive to outliers; may produce empty bins.

2. **Equal-Frequency Binning:**
   - Each bin contains approximately the same number of data points.
   - **Example:** Same dataset with $k = 3$, $\approx 2$–3 values per bin.
     - Bin 1: $\{4, 8, 15\}$ (3 values)
     - Bin 2: $\{21, 24, 28\}$ (3 values)
     - Bin 3: $\{34\}$ (1 value) — adjusted in practice.
   - **Advantage:** Better handling of skew; bins represent distribution.
   - **Limitation:** Different bin widths may result.

3. **K-Means Clustering-Based Discretization (Bonus):**
   - Apply K-Means on the continuous attribute; cluster centers form the bin boundaries.

**Conclusion:** Unsupervised discretization does not use class labels and is purely based on the distribution of the data.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Part (b):

**Major Issues in Data Integration:**

1. **Schema Integration & Object Identification:**
   - Same real-world entity represented by different attribute names across sources (e.g., `cust_id` vs. `customer_id`).
   - Requires metadata inspection.

2. **Redundant Data:**
   - An attribute that can be derived from another (e.g., `total_price = unit_price \times quantity$`).

3. **Tuple Duplication:**
   - Same record present in multiple source systems.

4. **Data Value Conflicts:**
   - Different representations, units, or scales (e.g., "M" vs. "Male"; "kg" vs. "lb"; different currencies).

**Correlation Analysis for Numerical Attributes (Redundancy Detection):**

**Pearson's Correlation Coefficient** measures the strength and direction of a linear relationship between two numerical attributes $A$ and $B$:

$$
r_{A,B} = \dfrac{\sum (A_i - \bar{A})(B_i - \bar{B})}{(n-1)\sigma_A \sigma_B}
$$

- $r_{A,B}$ ranges from $-1$ to $+1$.
- If $\vert r_{A,B} \vert > 0.7$ (or as per domain threshold), one attribute may be redundant.

**Example:** Suppose for $n = 5$ customers, `Annual_Income` and `Monthly_Income` are perfectly correlated ($r = 1$).

| Customer | Annual_Income (A) | Monthly_Income (B) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 600000 | 50000 |
| 2 | 1200000 | 100000 |
| 3 | 360000 | 30000 |
| 4 | 900000 | 75000 |
| 5 | 480000 | 40000 |

Since `Annual_Income = 12 \times Monthly_Income`, one attribute is mathematically redundant and can be removed during dimensionality reduction.

**For Categorical Attributes:** Use **Chi-Square Test** of independence:

$$
\chi^2 = \sum \dfrac{(O_{ij} - E_{ij})^2}{E_{ij}}
$$

If the null hypothesis (independence) is rejected, the attributes are dependent → potential redundancy.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Min-Max with Z-Score** — many students write the Z-Score formula when asked for Min-Max. Always re-read the question and quote the formula accurately.
> 2. **Forgetting the Range in Min-Max** — if asked to normalize to $[0, 1]$, the formula is $\dfrac{v - \min}{\max - \min}$, not $\dfrac{v}{\max}$.
> 3. **Skipping the "Why"** — KTU expects justification, not just definition. Always state *why* a method is chosen (e.g., "Z-Score is robust to outliers because it uses mean and standard deviation, not min and max").
> 4. **Missing the units/range in answers** — losing 0.5–1 mark per slip.
> 5. **Confusing Data Reduction with Data Cleaning** — reduction is about *volume* (rows/columns), cleaning is about *quality*.
> 6. **Writing Chi-Square formula without defining $O$ and $E$** — examiners expect $\chi^2 = \sum \dfrac{(O - E)^2}{E}$ with explicit explanation of $O$ and $E$.
> 7. **Forgetting MCAR/MAR/MNAR classification** — a 3-mark question may ask for the three types; missing one loses a mark.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- **Data Preparation** is the process of converting raw data into analytics-ready data.
- **Five Major Tasks:** Cleaning, Integration, Transformation, Reduction, Discretization.
- **GIGO Principle** underpins all data quality discussions.
- **Data Quality Dimensions:** Accuracy, Completeness, Consistency, Timeliness, Uniqueness, Validity.
- **Missing Data Types:** MCAR, MAR, MNAR.
- **Missing Value Methods:** Ignore, Manual, Constant, Mean/Median/Mode, Hot-Deck, Regression, KNN, Multiple Imputation.
- **Binning Methods:** Equal-Width, Equal-Frequency, Mean Smoothing, Boundary Smoothing, Median Smoothing.
- **Outlier Detection:** Z-Score (threshold > 3), IQR (1.5 × IQR rule), MAD-based, Isolation Forest, DBSCAN.
- **Normalization Methods:**
  - Min-Max: $v' = \dfrac{v - \min}{\max - \min}$ → range $[0, 1]$.
  - Z-Score: $v' = \dfrac{v - \mu}{\sigma}$ → mean 0, std 1.
  - Decimal Scaling: $v' = \dfrac{v}{10^j}$.
- **Data Integration Issues:** Schema mismatch, redundancy, tuple duplication, value conflicts.
- **Redundancy Detection:**
  - Numeric: Pearson's $r_{A,B} = \dfrac{\sum (A_i - \bar{A})(B_i - \bar{B})}{(n-1)\sigma_A \sigma_B}$.
  - Categorical: Chi-Square $\chi^2 = \sum \dfrac{(O_{ij} - E_{ij})^2}{E_{ij}}$.
- **Data Reduction Categories:** Dimensionality Reduction (PCA), Numerosity Reduction (Sampling, Histograms, Clustering), Data Compression (Wavelet, Huffman).
- **Data Discretization:** Binning, Concept Hierarchy Generation (e.g., City < State < Country).
- **CRISP-DM:** Data Preparation is the **third phase** of the standard data mining methodology, and is highly iterative.
- **Real-World Impact:** Data preparation consumes **60–80%** of analytics project time.
- **Algorithm Sensitivity:** Distance-based algorithms (KNN, K-Means, SVM) are most affected by unscaled data.
- **Always justify your method choice** in KTU answers with at least one line of reasoning.
- **Memorize formulas** — KTU frequently asks for direct computation in Part A and Part B.

> **End of Module 3: Data Preparations Notes**

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In **Dimensional Modeling** — the foundational data design technique for **Business Analytics, Data Warehousing, and OLAP systems** — a **Dimension** is a categorical (qualitative) attribute or entity that provides the *contextual "who, what, where, when, why, and how"* surrounding a numeric business event. Dimensions are stored in separate **Dimension Tables**, which are joined to a central **Fact Table** containing measurable numeric facts (measures).

A Dimension is therefore best understood as a **descriptive axis of analysis** along which a business process can be sliced, aggregated, filtered, or pivoted.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (Board-Exam Standard)**
> A **Dimension** is a structural attribute (or table) in a dimensional model that categorizes the data stored in a fact table, enabling users to perform multi-dimensional analysis (Slice, Dice, Drill-Down, Roll-Up, Pivot) on business measures such as Sales Amount, Profit, Quantity, or Cost.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are analyzing the **Sales of a supermarket chain** over the last 5 years.

The *raw number* "₹45,000" by itself is meaningless. To make sense of it, you need to ask:

- **When** did it happen? → **Time** Dimension (Date, Month, Quarter, Year)
- **Where** did it happen? → **Geography** Dimension (City, State, Country, Store)
- **What** was sold? → **Product** Dimension (Category, Sub-Category, Brand, SKU)
- **Who** bought it? → **Customer** Dimension (Gender, Age-Group, Segment)
- **How** was it sold? → **Promotion / Channel** Dimension (Online, In-Store, Discount)

Without these contextual "lenses", the number is a dead statistic. With them, it becomes **business intelligence**. The dimensions turn raw transactions into a navigable **multi-dimensional cube** that executives can pivot, slice, and explore.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Cube" Mental Model** — A Fact Table sits at the **intersection point** (the cell) of a multi-dimensional cube. Each **edge** of the cube represents a Dimension. The numeric value at the cell is the **Measure**.

## 1.3 Dimensions in the KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Framework

| KTU Course Outcome (CO) Tag | Cognitive Level (Bloom's) | Coverage in this Module Topic |
|---|---|---|
| **CO3** — Design Dimensional Models | **Apply / Analyze** | Identifying & classifying dimensions |
| **CO4** — Implement OLAP Operations | **Apply** | Slice, Dice, Roll-up, Drill-down |
| **CO5** — Evaluate Slowly Changing Data | **Evaluate** | SCD Types 1, 2, 3 |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Anatomy of a Dimension Table

A dimension table is typically:
- **Wide** (many descriptive columns) — also called *denormalized* / *flat*.
- **Short** (relatively few rows compared to fact tables).
- **Surrogate-keyed** (uses an artificial integer key, not the operational PK).
- **Slowly changing** (attributes evolve over time — the basis of SCDs).

A standard dimension table contains:

| Column Type | Purpose | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Surrogate Key (PK)** | Joins to Fact Table | `customer_id = 101` |
| **Natural Key** | Operational system ID | `cust_ssn = "X-9982"` |
| **Descriptive Attributes** | Business-readable text | `name`, `city`, `gender` |
| **Hierarchical Attributes** | Multi-level grouping | `country → state → city` |
| **Outrigger / Mini-dimension** | High-cardinality flags | `age_band`, `income_bracket` |
| **Effective Date / Expiry Date** | Tracks history (SCD Type 2) | `valid_from`, `valid_to` |
| **Current Flag** | Optimized current-row filter | `is_current = 1` |
| **Audit Metadata** | ETL / governance | `loaded_at`, `source_system` |

## 2.2 Anatomy of a Fact Table (Companion Concept)

A Fact Table contains:
- **Foreign Keys** to each dimension (forms the star's points).
- **Measures / Metrics** — additive, semi-additive, or non-additive numeric facts.
- **Degenerate Dimensions** (transaction IDs that act as dimensions but live in the fact).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two foundational design patterns** in Business Analytics dimensional modeling:
> 1. **Star Schema** — One fact table directly joined to flat dimension tables. Optimized for query speed (fewer JOINs). Preferred in Kimball-style warehouses.
> 2. **Snowflake Schema** — Dimensions are **normalized** into multiple related tables. Saves storage but increases JOIN cost. Rarely used in practice; usually only for very large, slowly-changing hierarchies (e.g., Geography).

## 2.3 Types of Dimensions (HIGH-YIELD — Board Favourite)

| Dimension Type | Definition (Board-Exam Ready) | Real-World Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Conformed Dimension** | A dimension shared across **multiple fact tables / data marts** with consistent meaning. | `Date` dim shared by Sales, Inventory, and Finance facts. |
| **Role-Playing Dimension** | A single physical dimension used **multiple times in one fact** under different roles (aliased). | `Date` dim used as *Order Date*, *Ship Date*, *Delivery Date* in Sales fact. |
| **Junk Dimension** | A consolidation of **low-cardinality flags & indicators** (yes/no, type codes) into one dimension to avoid clutter. | `Payment_Type` (Cash, Card, UPI) + `Order_Status` (Pending, Shipped) combined. |
| **Degenerate Dimension** | A dimension attribute stored **in the fact table itself** because it has no other descriptive content. | `Invoice_Number`, `Transaction_ID` in Sales fact. |
| **Slowly Changing Dimension (SCD)** | A dimension whose attributes **change over time**; tracked using SCD Types 1, 2, or 3. | Customer moves from `Mumbai` to `Pune` — how do we record it? |
| **Rapidly Changing Dimension** | Very high-frequency changes — handled via **mini-dimensions** or **outrigger tables**. | Customer *clickstream preferences*. |
| **Static Dimension** | Attributes **never change** after loading. | `Date` dimension, `Currency_Code`. |
| **Derived Dimension** | Attributes computed from **other dimensional columns**. | `Profit_Margin` = `(Revenue - Cost) / Revenue` (usually a fact, not a dim — distinction matters). |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Property / Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Business Analytics dimensional modeling is **concept-driven, not formula-driven**. The following table is the "formula-equivalent" knowledge that KTU examiners expect in numerical/short-answer questions.

| Symbol / Concept | Expression / Rule | Engineering / Business Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Cardinality** of Dim `D` | $\vert D \vert$ = number of unique member rows | Drives fact-table width; high cardinality → big JOINs |
| **Granularity** of a Fact | = combined PK of all its dimension FKs | Lowest level of detail captured (e.g., one row per line item) |
| **# Dimension Tables in Star** | $\ge 1$ | Every star has ≥ 3 (typically 4–12) dims |
| **Multi-dimensional Cell (Cube)** | $\text{Measure}(d_1, d_2, \ldots, d_n)$ | The numeric value at intersection of `n` dimension members |
| **SCD Type 2 — Record Insertion Trigger** | If new attribute value ≠ current value → INSERT new row | Preserves full history |
| **SCD Type 2 — Time Validity** | $\text{valid\_to} = \text{NULL}$ for current row | NULL = "still active" |
| **OLAP Slice** | Fixes one dimension member; reduces cube by 1 dimension | $\text{Cube}_{n} \rightarrow \text{Slice}_{n-1}$ |
| **OLAP Dice** | Picks a sub-cube by selecting ranges on 2+ dims | Subset of original cube |
| **OLAP Roll-Up** | Aggregates upward along hierarchy | Day → Month → Quarter → Year |
| **OLAP Drill-Down** | De-aggregates downward along hierarchy | Year → Quarter → Month → Day |
| **OLAP Pivot (Rotate)** | Reorients cube view (swap rows ↔ columns) | Visual re-arrangement only |

## 2.5 Why Dimensions Matter — Real-World Business Utility

- **Retail Analytics** — Product, Store, Time, Promotion dims drive sales dashboards.
- **Banking & Finance** — Customer, Account, Branch, Date dims support risk, fraud, and profitability analysis.
- **Healthcare** — Patient, Doctor, Diagnosis, Date dims support outcome and cost analytics.
- **Telecom** — Subscriber, Tower, Plan, Date dims drive churn modelling and network analytics.
- **E-Commerce / EdTech** — User, Product, Session, Channel, Date dims drive funnel and recommendation systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway:** Without well-designed dimensions, a data warehouse is a **graveyard of numbers**. With them, it becomes a **decision-support engine**.

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough — From Business Scenario to Dimensional Model

> [!NOTE]
> Since "Dimensions" is a design/analytical topic (not a physics derivation), this section provides a **rigorous, step-by-step logical derivation** of (a) how a real business scenario is converted into a dimensional model, and (b) how OLAP operations act upon dimensions.

## 3.1 Worked Example: Designing Dimensions for a "Retail Sales" Business Process

**Business Requirement:** A Keralite retail chain "GreenMart" wants to analyze sales to identify top products, peak seasons, profitable stores, and promotional effectiveness.

### Step 1 — Identify the Business Process
The process is **Sales Transaction Point-of-Sale (POS) Events**. This becomes the **Fact Table** name: `FACT_SALES`.

### Step 2 — Declare the Grain (Granularity)
Grain = **one row per sales line item per transaction**.
Without fixing grain, the entire design collapses. (A common 7-mark question setter trap.)

### Step 3 — Identify the Measures (Numeric Facts)
These are the additive / semi-additive numbers in the fact:

$$
\text{Measures} = \{\ \text{sales\_amount},\ \text{quantity\_sold},\ \text{discount\_amount},\ \text{cost\_amount},\ \text{profit}\ \}
$$

### Step 4 — Identify the Dimensions (Contextual "Lenses")

$$
\text{Dimensions} = \{\ \text{Date},\ \text{Product},\ \text{Store},\ \text{Customer},\ \text{Promotion},\ \text{Employee}\ \}
$$

### Step 5 — Resolve Special Dimension Cases

- **Degenerate Dimension** → `transaction_id`, `line_item_number` → live in FACT_SALES itself.
- **Junk Dimension** → Combine `payment_method` (Cash, Card, UPI, Wallet) and `order_channel` (Online, In-Store, App) into a single `DIM_PAYMENT_CHANNEL` to avoid sparse low-cardinality flags.
- **Role-Playing Dimension** → `DIM_DATE` is used three times: as `order_date_key`, `ship_date_key`, `delivery_date_key` — aliased in views.
- **Conformed Dimension** → `DIM_DATE`, `DIM_PRODUCT`, `DIM_STORE` are conformed across the future `FACT_INVENTORY` and `FACT_PROMOTION` data marts.

### Step 6 — Build the Star Schema Structure
The center is `FACT_SALES`; each surrounding table is a dimension.

### Step 7 — Apply SCD Strategy
- `DIM_CUSTOMER` → **SCD Type 2** (customer city, marital status, income bracket change over years — must preserve history).
- `DIM_PRODUCT` → **SCD Type 1** (product name correction is overwrite; no historical interest).
- `DIM_STORE` → **SCD Type 2** (store relocations, manager changes).
- `DIM_DATE` → **Static** (a date is a date forever).

## 3.2 Step-by-Step OLAP Operations on Dimensions

Given a 3-D cube with axes **(Time, Product, Geography)** and measure **Sales**:

### Operation 1 — Slice
Fix `Product = "Rice"`. Result is a 2-D plane (Time × Geography). Mathematically:

$$
\text{Cube}_{\text{3D}} \xrightarrow{\text{Slice on } d_1 = \text{"Rice"}} \text{Plane}_{\text{2D}}
$$

### Operation 2 — Dice
Pick `Year ∈ {2023, 2024}` AND `Geography ∈ {"Kerala", "Karnataka"}`. Result is a sub-cube (3-D) smaller in scope.

### Operation 3 — Roll-Up (Aggregation Upward)
Collapse `Day → Month → Quarter → Year`. Example:

$$
\text{Sales}_{\text{Yearly}} = \sum_{d=1}^{365} \text{Sales}_{\text{Daily}}(d)
$$

### Operation 4 — Drill-Down (Disaggregation Downward)
Reverse of Roll-Up. Expand `Year → Quarter → Month → Day`. Useful for root-cause analysis.

### Operation 5 — Pivot (Rotate)
Swap axes, e.g., `Rows = Product`, `Columns = Time` becomes `Rows = Time`, `Columns = Product`. Visualization only — no data change.

## 3.3 Slowly Changing Dimensions — Step-by-Step Resolution

**Scenario:** Customer `C101` lives in `Kochi` in 2022. In 2024, they move to `Bangalore`. How do we record this in `DIM_CUSTOMER`?

### SCD Type 1 — Overwrite (No History)
| customer_key | name | city | valid_from |
|---|---|---|---|
| 101 | Anil | **Bangalore** (overwritten) | 2022-01-01 |

- ✅ Simple. ❌ Loses history — we never knew he was from Kochi.

### SCD Type 2 — Add New Row (Full History)
| customer_key | name | city | valid_from | valid_to | is_current |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 101 | Anil | Kochi | 2022-01-01 | 2024-03-31 | 0 |
| 101 | Anil | **Bangalore** | 2024-04-01 | NULL | 1 |

- ✅ Preserves history. ❌ Dimension grows; need surrogate key strategy.

### SCD Type 3 — Add New Column (Limited History)
| customer_key | name | **old\_city** | **current\_city** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 101 | Anil | Kochi | Bangalore |

- ✅ Simple & preserves 1 prior value. ❌ Cannot track >1 historical change.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board-Exam Rule of Thumb:** Type 2 is the **default industry choice** (Kimball). Type 1 is for corrections. Type 3 is rare and limited.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Star Schema — Fact & Dimension Layout

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    factSales["FACT_SALES\nMeasures:\nsales_amount, quantity,\nprofit, discount"]

    dimDate["DIM_DATE\ndate_key PK\nday, month, quarter,\nyear, day_of_week"]
    dimProd["DIM_PRODUCT\nproduct_key PK\nsku, name, brand,\ncategory, sub_category"]
    dimStore["DIM_STORE\nstore_key PK\nstore_name, city,\nstate, region"]
    dimCust["DIM_CUSTOMER\ncustomer_key PK\nname, gender,\nage_band, city"]
    dimPromo["DIM_PROMOTION\npromo_key PK\npromo_type,\noffer_name"]
    dimPay["DIM_PAYMENT_CHANNEL\npayment_key PK\nmethod, channel\n(JUNK DIM)"]
    dimDeg["DEGENERATE DIM\ntransaction_id, line_no\n(lives inside FACT)"]

    dimDate --- factSales
    dimProd --- factSales
    dimStore --- factSales
    dimCust --- factSales
    dimPromo --- factSales
    dimPay --- factSales
    dimDeg -.- factSales
```

## 4.2 Snowflake Schema (Normalized) — Comparative View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    factSales2["FACT_SALES"]

    dimDate2["DIM_DATE"]
    dimMonth["DIM_MONTH"]
    dimQuarter["DIM_QUARTER"]
    dimYear["DIM_YEAR"]

    dimProd2["DIM_PRODUCT"]
    dimSubCat["DIM_SUB_CATEGORY"]
    dimCategory["DIM_CATEGORY"]
    dimDept["DIM_DEPARTMENT"]

    dimDate2 --> dimMonth
    dimMonth --> dimQuarter
    dimQuarter --> dimYear

    dimProd2 --> dimSubCat
    dimSubCat --> dimCategory
    dimCategory --> dimDept

    factSales2 --- dimDate2
    factSales2 --- dimProd2
```

## 4.3 SCD Resolution — Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    start(["New Source Record\nArrives"]) --> compare{"Compare with\nCurrent Row\nin Dimension?"}
    compare -- "Identical" --> skip["Skip / No-Action"]
    compare -- "Changed" --> policy{"Business\nSCD Policy?"}
    policy -- "Type 1" --> t1["OVERWRITE\n(loses history)"]
    policy -- "Type 2" --> t2["INSERT new row\n+ close old row\n(preserves full history)"]
    policy -- "Type 3" --> t3["UPDATE old_city column\n+ write new_city\n(preserves 1 prior value)"]
    t1 --> audit["Audit trail\nlogged"]
    t2 --> audit
    t3 --> audit
    audit --> end1(["Dimension Updated"])
```

## 4.4 OLAP Cube & Dimension Axes — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph cube["MULTI-DIMENSIONAL CUBE - SALES"]
        cell["MEASURE CELL\nsales_amount = Rs.45,000\nquantity = 12 units\nprofit = Rs.7,200"]
    end

    dimT["DIM_TIME\nYear, Quarter, Month, Day"]
    dimP["DIM_PRODUCT\nCategory, Sub-Cat, Brand, SKU"]
    dimG["DIM_GEOGRAPHY\nCountry, State, City, Store"]

    dimT --- cube
    dimP --- cube
    dimG --- cube

    slice["SLICE\nFix: Product = Rice"] -.-> cube
    dice["DICE\nYear 2023-24 +\nKerala + Karnataka"] -.-> cube
    rollup["ROLL-UP\nDay -> Month -> Year"] -.-> cube
    drill["DRILL-DOWN\nYear -> Quarter -> Day"] -.-> cube
    pivot["PIVOT\nSwap Rows/Cols"] -.-> cube
```

## 4.5 Role-Playing Dimension (Date) — Aliased Usage

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    dimDateR["DIM_DATE\n(Physical Table)"]

    factSalesR["FACT_SALES"]

    v1["View: v_order_date\nAliases date_key as\norder_date_key"]
    v2["View: v_ship_date\nAliases date_key as\nship_date_key"]
    v3["View: v_delivery_date\nAliases date_key as\ndelivery_date_key"]

    dimDateR --> v1
    dimDateR --> v2
    dimDateR --> v3

    v1 --> factSalesR
    v2 --> factSalesR
    v3 --> factSalesR
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Mermaid Block Diagrams?** In Business Analytics, the "physical drawing" is a schema. Mermaid's flowchart syntax is the industry-preferred way to render star/snowflake schemas in documentation, Confluence pages, and academic notes (this same style is accepted in KTU digital answer sheets).

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define a Dimension in the context of dimensional modeling. Give two examples. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Dimension** in dimensional modeling is a categorical attribute or table that provides the descriptive context (who, what, where, when, how) for the numeric measures stored in a fact table, enabling multi-dimensional business analysis.

**Two Examples (1 mark each):**
- **Time Dimension** — `date_key`, `day`, `month`, `quarter`, `year` — supports temporal analysis of sales.
- **Product Dimension** — `product_key`, `sku`, `brand`, `category` — supports product mix and category analysis.

*(1 mark for clean definition + 1 mark each for examples = 3 marks)*

---

### Q2. Differentiate between a Fact Table and a Dimension Table. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Fact Table | Dimension Table |
|---|---|---|
| **Content** | Numeric measures (sales, profit, qty) | Descriptive text attributes (name, city, date) |
| **Structure** | Narrow & long (millions of rows) | Wide & short (thousands of rows) |
| **Keys** | Composite FK of all dim keys | Single surrogate PK |
| **Role in Cube** | Cell of the cube (the value) | Edge of the cube (the axis) |
| **Change Rate** | Append-only (immutable facts) | Slowly / rapidly changing attributes |
| **Example** | `FACT_SALES` | `DIM_PRODUCT`, `DIM_CUSTOMER` |

*(1 mark per meaningful row × 3 selected rows = 3 marks)*

---

## 📗 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks)

> **`(a)` Explain Dimensional Modeling with the Star Schema. Illustrate with a Retail Sales example. (7 marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**1. Definition (2 marks):**
Dimensional Modeling, popularized by **Ralph Kimball**, is a logical design technique for data warehouses that structures data into **Fact Tables** (numeric business events) and **Dimension Tables** (descriptive context). The most common physical implementation is the **Star Schema**, so named because the fact table sits at the center with dimension tables radiating outward like points of a star.

**2. Components of a Star (2 marks):**
- **Central Fact Table** — holds additive measures (`sales_amount`, `quantity`, `profit`) and foreign keys to all dimensions.
- **Surrounding Dimension Tables** — denormalized, wide tables with surrogate primary keys, descriptive text attributes, and hierarchical columns (e.g., `category → sub_category → brand`).

**3. Retail Sales Example (2 marks):**
For a chain like "GreenMart", the star contains:
- **FACT_SALES** (center) with measures `sales_amount`, `quantity_sold`, `discount`, `profit`.
- **DIM_DATE**, **DIM_PRODUCT**, **DIM_STORE**, **DIM_CUSTOMER**, **DIM_PROMOTION** (5 points of the star).
- The **grain** of FACT_SALES is "one row per sales line item per transaction".

**4. Advantages (1 mark):**
- Fewer JOINs → faster OLAP queries.
- Business-user friendly — tables map directly to report axes.
- Conformed dimensions enable cross-data-mart analysis.

---

> **`(b)` Explain any four types of dimensions with suitable examples. (7 marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**

**Model Answer (7 marks — 1.75 marks per type):**

1. **Conformed Dimension** — A dimension shared across multiple fact tables with the same meaning. *Example:* `DIM_DATE` shared by `FACT_SALES` and `FACT_INVENTORY` enables consistent time-based analysis.

2. **Role-Playing Dimension** — A single physical dimension used multiple times in the same fact under different roles. *Example:* `DIM_DATE` acting as `order_date`, `ship_date`, and `delivery_date` in `FACT_SALES`.

3. **Junk Dimension** — Combines low-cardinality flags and indicators into one dimension to avoid cluttering the fact. *Example:* `DIM_TRANSACTION_FLAGS` with attributes `payment_method` (Cash/Card/UPI), `order_status` (Pending/Shipped), `gift_wrap` (Y/N).

4. **Degenerate Dimension** — A dimension attribute stored in the fact table itself because it has no further descriptive content. *Example:* `invoice_number`, `transaction_id` living inside `FACT_SALES`.

*(Optional extra: SCD, Rapidly Changing, Static, Derived — but only 4 needed.)*

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **`(a)` Explain the concept of Slowly Changing Dimensions. Compare SCD Type 1, Type 2, and Type 3 with a customer-address-change example. (7 marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**1. Concept (2 marks):**
**Slowly Changing Dimensions (SCDs)** are dimensions whose attribute values change over time (e.g., customer address, marital status, job title). The warehouse must decide *how* to record these changes — this decision defines the SCD Type.

**Customer Example Setup (0.5 marks):**
`Customer C101` = Anil, lives in **Kochi** (2022). In **March 2024**, Anil moves to **Bangalore**. The three SCD strategies handle this differently.

**2. SCD Type 1 — Overwrite (1.5 marks):**
- **Action:** UPDATE the existing row; old value is permanently lost.
- **Resulting Row:** `customer_key=101, city=Bangalore` (Kochi gone forever).
- **Use Case:** Corrections, low analytical value of history.
- **Pros:** Simple, no row growth. **Cons:** No audit trail.

**3. SCD Type 2 — New Row with Effective Dates (2 marks):**
- **Action:** Close the old row (`valid_to = 2024-03-31, is_current = 0`), INSERT a new row (`valid_from = 2024-04-01, is_current = 1, city = Bangalore`).
- **Resulting Rows:** Two rows for `customer_key=101` — one historic (Kochi) and one current (Bangalore).
- **Use Case:** Full historical tracking of customer behaviour changes.
- **Pros:** Preserves all history. **Cons:** Dimension grows; surrogate key strategy needed.

**4. SCD Type 3 — Previous-Value Column (1 mark):**
- **Action:** Add `previous_city` column; update `current_city`.
- **Resulting Row:** `customer_key=101, previous_city=Kochi, current_city=Bangalore`.
- **Use Case:** Compare current vs. only one prior value.
- **Pros:** Simple, fast access. **Cons:** Limited to 1 history; cannot track multiple moves.

> *(Total: 2 + 0.5 + 1.5 + 2 + 1 = 7 marks)*

---

> **`(b)` A Kerala-based online food delivery company "SpiceBite" wants to build a data warehouse to analyse orders. Design a star schema identifying the grain, fact table, measures, and at least four dimensions. Justify your choice of SCD type for the Customer dimension. (7 marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**1. Business Process & Grain (1 mark):**
- **Process:** Order Fulfillment / Delivery.
- **Grain:** *One row per order line item per order.*

**2. Fact Table: `FACT_ORDERS` (1.5 marks):**
**Measures (numeric facts):**

$$
\text{Measures} = \{\ \text{order\_amount},\ \text{discount\_amount},\ \text{delivery\_fee},\ \text{commission},\ \text{preparation\_time\_min}\ \}
$$

**Foreign Keys (to dimensions):** `order_date_key`, `customer_key`, `restaurant_key`, `delivery_partner_key`, `cuisine_key`.
**Degenerate Dim:** `order_id`, `invoice_number` (lives inside the fact).

**3. Dimensions (3 marks):**

| Dimension | Sample Attributes | Type |
|---|---|---|
| `DIM_DATE` | date_key, day, month, quarter, year, day_of_week, is_holiday | **Static** |
| `DIM_CUSTOMER` | customer_key, name, age_band, gender, city, registration_date, segment | **SCD Type 2** |
| `DIM_RESTAURANT` | restaurant_key, name, city, cuisine_type, rating_band, onboarded_date | **SCD Type 2** |
| `DIM_DELIVERY_PARTNER` | partner_key, name, vehicle_type, city, join_date, rating_band | **SCD Type 2** |
| `DIM_CUISINE` | cuisine_key, cuisine_name, parent_category | **SCD Type 1** |
| `DIM_PROMOTION` (Junk dim) | promo_key, coupon_type, payment_mode, offer_source | **Slowly changing** |

**4. Justification: SCD Type 2 for `DIM_CUSTOMER` (1.5 marks):**
- Customer attributes such as **city, marital status, income bracket, and segment** change over time and these changes **directly affect order patterns** (e.g., a customer shifting to a new city changes which restaurants they order from).
- Marketing & analytics teams must answer: *"What was the customer's segment at the time of THIS order?"* — this requires historical accuracy.
- SCD Type 2 preserves **full history** via `valid_from`, `valid_to`, and `is_current` columns, enabling point-in-time joins with the fact table using the dimension surrogate key effective on the order date.
- Type 1 is rejected because it would erase history; Type 3 is rejected because customers may move multiple times and Type 3 stores only 1 prior value.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Examiner-Verified):**
>
> 1. **Grain confusion (–2 marks):** Students write "Sales fact" but **forget to fix the grain** (e.g., "one row per transaction" vs. "one row per line item"). Always declare the grain explicitly.
>
> 2. **Confusing Fact vs. Dimension attributes (–1 to 2 marks):** Putting `sales_amount` in a dimension, or `product_name` in a fact. Rule of thumb: **Numbers that you SUM = Fact; Text that you GROUP BY = Dimension.**
>
> 3. **Mixing up SCD types (–2 marks):** Writing "SCD Type 2 overwrites the row" — this is **Type 1**. Type 2 = **new row inserted** with effective dates.
>
> 4. **Drawing the Star Schema incorrectly (–1 mark):** Drawing a Snowflake when asked for Star (or vice-versa). Star = flat dimensions; Snowflake = normalized hierarchies.
>
> 5. **Skipping the Junk / Degenerate / Role-Playing dim examples (–1 mark each in 7-mark sub-parts):** Examiners specifically look for these three "edge-case" dimensions because they are board favourites.
>
> 6. **No justification for SCD type (–1.5 marks):** When asked to "justify", simply naming the type is insufficient — give a **business reason** (e.g., "we need historical segment to attribute past revenue correctly").
>
> 7. **Forgetting surrogate keys (–1 mark):** Operational natural keys (like Aadhaar, phone) should NOT be the dimension PK. Always introduce a **surrogate integer key**.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this checklist and revise 30 minutes before the exam.**

- [ ] **Dimension** = descriptive context table; **Fact** = numeric measurement table.
- [ ] **Grain** must be fixed *first*, before any design — it controls everything downstream.
- [ ] **Star Schema** (denormalized) is the default; **Snowflake** is the rare exception.
- [ ] **Surrogate Key** (integer) is mandatory for dimension PKs; natural keys stay as attributes.
- [ ] **Conformed Dimension** = shared across multiple facts (Date, Customer, Product).
- [ ] **Role-Playing Dimension** = one physical dim used multiple times in one fact (Date as order/ship/delivery).
- [ ] **Junk Dimension** = low-cardinality flags consolidated into one dim.
- [ ] **Degenerate Dimension** = transactional ID living in the fact table.
- [ ] **SCD Type 1** = overwrite (no history).
- [ ] **SCD Type 2** = new row + `valid_from` / `valid_to` / `is_current` (full history). **Default industry choice.**
- [ ] **SCD Type 3** = `previous_value` + `current_value` columns (1 prior value only).
- [ ] **OLAP Slice** = fix one dim; **Dice** = sub-cube; **Roll-Up** = aggregate up hierarchy; **Drill-Down** = disaggregate down; **Pivot** = rotate axes.
- [ ] Numbers you **SUM** go in **Fact**; Text you **GROUP BY** goes in **Dimension**.
- [ ] Always state **business justification** when choosing an SCD type — examiners reward it.
- [ ] Star schema queries are faster because of fewer JOINs — remember this for "compare Star vs Snowflake" questions.
- [ ] Mini-dimensions handle **rapidly changing** high-cardinality attributes (e.g., customer preferences).
- [ ] Conformed dimensions enable **enterprise-wide data marts** to talk to each other.
- [ ] A **Role-Playing dim** is implemented using **view aliases** in the warehouse, not as separate physical tables.

---

> ✅ **End of Module 3 — Topic: Dimensions | KTU 2024 Scheme | PEADT522 Business Analytics**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Custom Data View
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT522 - BUSINESS ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Custom Data View

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Custom Data View (CDV)** in the context of Business Analytics refers to a *user-defined, interactive, and personalized presentation layer* built on top of an underlying tabular or relational data model. It allows analysts to slice, project, aggregate, and visualize data from a single canonical dataset into multiple purpose-specific perspectives (e.g., Sales View, Inventory View, HR View) without modifying the source data structure.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology (mapped to tools like **Microsoft Power BI**, **Power View**, and **Power Pivot Tabular Models**), a Custom Data View is the bridge between *raw tabular data* and *decision-ready insights*. It is constructed by combining:

1. **Custom Columns** — DAX-computed fields extending the data model.
2. **Custom Measures** — Dynamic aggregate expressions evaluated at query time.
3. **Custom Perspectives** — Filtered metadata layers defining what a role/group of users can see.
4. **Custom Sort, Filter, and Hierarchical Drill Paths** — for the analytical canvas.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight (KTU PEADT522 – Module 3):**
> The Custom Data View topic enables learners to design *self-service BI reports* where business users (not data engineers) tailor the view to their analytical question while respecting centralized governance.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **Custom Data View** like the **menu card of a multi-cuisine restaurant**:

- The **kitchen (data warehouse)** prepares all possible ingredients: rice, pasta, spices, dough, vegetables, meats.
- The **menu card (Custom Data View)** is *not* the kitchen itself — it is a curated presentation. The chef (data engineer) decides what raw ingredients exist; the manager (analyst) decides how to display them as "Today's Specials," "Vegetarian Combos," or "Family Packs."
- A vegetarian customer (a Sales Manager role) sees a *different filtered menu* than a bulk caterer (an Operations role), even though they are served from the *same kitchen*.

In BI terms:
- The **same sales dataset** powers a *Regional Sales View*, a *Product Category View*, and a *Time-Series Forecast View*.
- Each view is a **Custom Data View** — same source, different projection, different purpose.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook:** *Same Data → Many Lenses.* A Custom Data View is one such lens.

---

### 1.3 Why Custom Data View Matters

| Reason | Business Impact |
|---|---|
| **Personalization** | Each stakeholder sees KPIs relevant to their role. |
| **Governance** | Perspectives enforce row-/column-level security. |
| **Reusability** | One data model serves dozens of dashboards. |
| **Performance** | Pre-modeled measures avoid runtime computation overhead. |
| **Self-Service BI** | Reduces dependency on IT for report creation. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Standard Metric — **BI Adoption Rate****: Enterprises achieving a Custom Data View–driven BI model report **20–35%** faster decision-making cycles (per Gartner's 2024 BI maturity benchmarks).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architecture of a Custom Data View

A Custom Data View sits on a **three-tier architecture**:

1. **Data Source Tier** — Databases (SQL Server, Azure, Excel), APIs, flat files.
2. **Semantic Model Tier (Tabular Model)** — Relationships, hierarchies, DAX measures.
3. **Presentation Tier (Custom View Layer)** — Visuals, filters, slicers, perspectives.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A Custom Data View operates strictly at the **Presentation Tier**, but it pulls computational logic from the **Semantic Model Tier** via DAX expressions.

---

### 2.2 Core Components (Step-by-Step Logic)

**Step 1 — Custom Columns**
- Created using Power Query (M language) or DAX `ADDCOLUMNS()`.
- Static (row-level) computation; evaluated during data refresh.
- *Why?* To derive new attributes like `ProfitMargin = Profit / Sales`.

**Step 2 — Custom Measures**
- DAX expressions that aggregate over multiple rows.
- Evaluated dynamically based on filter context.
- *Why?* To compute KPIs like `YTD Sales = TOTALYTD(SUM(Sales[Amount]), DateTable[Date])`.

**Step 3 — Custom Hierarchies**
- Drill-down paths (e.g., Year → Quarter → Month → Day).
- Built on dimension tables; enable pivoting across granularity levels.

**Step 4 — Custom Perspectives**
- Subset of tables/columns/measures exposed to a user role.
- *Why?* Role-based security and reduced cognitive load.

**Step 5 — Custom Sort Orders & Conditional Formatting Rules**
- Override default alphabetic sorting using `SORTBY()` or static sort columns.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Rule:** All formulas below are *DAX expressions* unless explicitly labeled M (Power Query M Language). Units apply to monetary KPIs unless contextually stated.

| # | Operation | Formula (DAX / M) | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Custom Column (DAX) | `ProfitMargin = DIVIDE(Sales[Profit], Sales[Sales], 0)` | Row-level ratio with safe division. |
| 2 | Custom Measure (Sum) | `Total Sales = SUM(Sales[Amount])` | Basic aggregation. |
| 3 | Custom Measure (Iter.) | `AvgTicket = AVERAGEX(Sales, Sales[Qty] * Sales[Price])` | Per-row compute, then average. |
| 4 | YTD Measure | `Sales YTD = TOTALYTD(SUM(Sales[Amount]), 'Date'[Date])` | Year-to-date aggregation. |
| 5 | YoY Growth | `YoY % = DIVIDE([ThisYear] - [LastYear], [LastYear], 0)` | Period-over-period growth. |
| 6 | Custom Sort (M) | `= Table.Sort(Source, {"Region", Order.Ascending})` | Pre-sort in Power Query. |
| 7 | Conditional Column (M) | `= Table.AddColumn(Source, "Bucket", each if [Amount] > 1000 then "High" else "Low")` | Bin creation. |
| 8 | Perspective (XMLA) | Defined in Tabular Editor / TMSL | Role-restricted object visibility. |
| 9 | Filter Context Override | `CALCULATE([Sales], ALL(Sales[Region]))` | Remove filter context. |
| 10 | Rank Measure | `Rank = RANKX(ALL(Product), [Total Sales])` | Cross-category ranking. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Never use bare `|` inside table cells** — always use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX-style math within Markdown tables.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering / Business Utility

- **Finance:** CFO View — P&L, cash flow, variance against budget.
- **Operations:** Plant Manager View — OEE, downtime, throughput.
- **Marketing:** CMO View — CAC, LTV, campaign ROI.
- **Supply Chain:** Inventory Aging, Lead Time Variance, Stockout %.
- **HR:** Attrition Rate, Time-to-Hire, Diversity Ratios.

A single ERP-backed data warehouse can fan out into **20+ Custom Data Views**, each tuned to a department.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Building a YoY Growth Custom Measure

> **Problem Context:** You are given a `Sales` fact table and a `Date` dimension table in a star schema. Derive a Custom Measure `YoY Growth %` that compares current year's total sales to the previous year's, displayed inside a Custom Data View (Card Visual).

**Given:**
- `Sales[Amount]` — line-item sales value.
- `Date[Date]` — calendar date.
- `Date[Year]` — extracted year attribute.

**Required:**
- `YoY Growth % = ((Sales_CY − Sales_PY) / Sales_PY) * 100`

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define This Year Sales Measure**

$$
\text{Sales\_CY} \;=\; \text{CALCULATE}\bigl(\,\text{SUM(Sales[Amount])},\,\text{'Date'[Year]} = \text{YEAR(TODAY())}\,\bigr)
$$

*Logic:* We compute the sum of `Sales[Amount]` but **filter** the `Date[Year]` column to equal the current year. `CALCULATE` overrides the existing filter context with a new one.

---

**Step 2 — Define Last Year Sales Measure**

$$
\text{Sales\_PY} \;=\; \text{CALCULATE}\bigl(\,\text{SUM(Sales[Amount])},\,\text{'Date'[Year]} = \text{YEAR(TODAY())} - 1\,\bigr)
$$

*Logic:* Identical structure, but the filter is set to `Year(TODAY()) − 1`, i.e., the previous calendar year.

---

**Step 3 — Compose the YoY Growth Formula**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{YoY Growth \%} \; & = \; \text{DIVIDE}\bigl(\,\text{Sales\_CY} - \text{Sales\_PY},\; \text{Sales\_PY},\; 0\,\bigr) \\
 & = \; \frac{\text{Sales\_CY} - \text{Sales\_PY}}{\text{Sales\_PY}} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* `DIVIDE` performs safe division; the third argument `0` is returned when the denominator is zero (e.g., when the prior year had no sales — a startup's first year).

---

**Step 4 — Wire the Measure into a Custom View (Card Visual)**

In the report canvas, drag `YoY Growth %` onto a **Card** visual. The Custom Data View will dynamically compute this measure per the slicer selections (e.g., Region, Product Category). The Card updates in real time as the user toggles filters.

---

**Step 5 — Verify with a Worked Numerical Example**

Assume:
- `Sales_CY = 1,200,000`
- `Sales_PY = 1,000,000`

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{YoY Growth \%} \; & = \; \frac{1{,}200{,}000 - 1{,}000{,}000}{1{,}000{,}000} \times 100 \\
 & = \; \frac{200{,}000}{1{,}000{,}000} \times 100 \\
 & = \; 0.20 \times 100 \\
 & = \; 20\%
\end{aligned}
$$

✅ Sales grew **20%** Year-over-Year.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: When to Use What

| Scenario | Recommended Component | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Need a static new attribute | Custom Column (M) | Computed once at refresh; lowest cost. |
| Need dynamic ratio changing with slicers | Custom Measure (DAX) | Evaluates per filter context. |
| Need row-by-row conditional flag | Custom Column with `IF` | Operates at row granularity. |
| Need aggregation across many rows | Measure with `SUMX`, `AVERAGEX` | Iterators handle row + aggregate. |
| Restrict user to subset of fields | Custom Perspective | Enforces governance. |
| Build a drill-down report | Custom Hierarchy + Field Parameters | Enables user-driven exploration. |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Custom Data View — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Data Sources: SQL, Excel, APIs] --> B[ETL Layer: Power Query M]
    B --> C[Tabular Semantic Model]
    C --> C1[Fact Tables: Sales, Inventory]
    C --> C2[Dimension Tables: Date, Product, Region]
    C --> C3[Relationships: Star Schema]
    C --> D[DAX Computation Engine]
    D --> D1[Custom Columns]
    D --> D2[Custom Measures]
    D --> D3[Custom Hierarchies]
    C3 --> E[Custom Data View Layer]
    D1 --> E
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> E1[Sales Manager View]
    E --> E2[Operations View]
    E --> E3[Finance View]
    E --> E4[Marketing View]
    E1 --> F1[Dashboard with KPIs]
    E2 --> F2[Real-time Monitoring Board]
    E3 --> F3[Card Visuals and P&L]
    E4 --> F4[Campaign ROI Report]
    F1 --> G[End User Insights]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
```

---

### 4.2 Custom Data View — Creation Lifecycle Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Step 1: Identify Business Question] --> P2[Step 2: Locate Source Tables]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Build or Modify Data Model]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: Author DAX Measures]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5: Define Hierarchies]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6: Apply Perspectives and RLS]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7: Design Visual Canvas]
    P7 --> P8[Step 8: Publish and Share]
    P8 --> P9[Step 9: Monitor Usage and Iterate]
    P9 --> P1
```

---

### 4.3 Component Relationship Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SRCLayer[Data Source Layer]
        S1[SQL Server]
        S2[Excel Workbook]
        S3[REST API]
    end
    subgraph MDL[Semantic Model]
        FT[Fact Table: Sales]
        DT[Dim Table: Date]
        DP[Dim Table: Product]
        DR[Dim Table: Region]
    end
    subgraph CDVLayer[Custom Data View]
        CC[Custom Columns]
        CM[Custom Measures]
        CH[Custom Hierarchies]
        CP[Custom Perspectives]
    end
    S1 --> FT
    S2 --> FT
    S3 --> FT
    FT --- DT
    FT --- DP
    FT --- DR
    FT --> CC
    FT --> CM
    DT --> CH
    CP --> CC
    CP --> CM
    CP --> CH
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q:** Define a **Custom Data View** in the context of Business Intelligence. Mention any two of its components.

**Model Answer:**
> A Custom Data View is a user-defined, role-specific presentation layer built atop a tabular data model in BI tools like Power BI. It projects a single underlying dataset into multiple analytical perspectives tailored to business roles.
>
> **Two components:**
> 1. **Custom Columns** — derived attributes computed via DAX or Power Query.
> 2. **Custom Measures** — dynamic aggregate expressions evaluated at query time based on filter context.

*(Expected length: 4–6 lines; 3 marks.)*

---

#### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q:** Differentiate between a **Custom Column** and a **Custom Measure** with one example each.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Custom Column | Custom Measure |
|---|---|---|
| Granularity | Row-level | Aggregate-level |
| Evaluation Time | At refresh | At query time |
| Storage | Consumes memory in model | Computed on-the-fly |
| Example | `ProfitMargin = Profit / Sales` | `Total Sales = SUM(Sales[Amount])` |

*(3 marks: 1 for definition clarity + 1 for each example.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3]`

**Q:** A retail company maintains a star schema with `Sales[Amount]`, `Sales[Quantity]`, `Sales[Date]`, and a `Date` dimension table.
**(a)** Build a Custom Measure to compute **Average Transaction Value (ATV)** using DAX. Explain each argument. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Design a Custom Data View titled *Regional Sales Performance* by listing any **five visuals** you would include and justifying each. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) — ATV Custom Measure (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Identify the logic:**
ATV = Total Sales Value ÷ Number of Transactions.

**Step 2 — DAX Expression:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ATV} \; & = \; \text{DIVIDE}\bigl(\,\text{SUM(Sales[Amount])},\; \text{DISTINCTCOUNT(Sales[TransactionID])},\; 0\,\bigr)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Argument Explanation:**
- `SUM(Sales[Amount])` — Numerator: total monetary value of all sales line items in the current filter context.
- `DISTINCTCOUNT(Sales[TransactionID])` — Denominator: counts *unique* transactions (a transaction may have multiple line items).
- `0` — Safe-divide fallback returned when denominator is zero.

**Step 4 — Verification:**
Suppose 100 transactions worth ₹5,00,000 total.
$$
\text{ATV} = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{100} = ₹5{,}000 \text{ per transaction}.
$$

*(Marks: 2 for formula, 3 for argument explanation, 2 for numerical verification.)*

---

**Part (b) — Regional Sales Performance Custom Data View (7 marks)**

| Visual | Justification |
|---|---|
| **1. Map Visual** | Plots sales by geographic region, giving instant geographic insight. |
| **2. Bar Chart (Sales by Region)** | Compares regional totals at a glance. |
| **3. KPI Card (Total Sales)** | Headline metric for executive summary. |
| **4. Line Chart (Monthly Trend)** | Shows seasonality per region over time. |
| **5. Slicer (Region / Product)** | Enables user-driven interactivity. |

*(Marks: 1 per visual + 1 for justifying role alignment = 7 marks total.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3]`

**Q:** An e-commerce firm wants to track **Year-over-Year (YoY) Revenue Growth** and **Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)**.
**(a)** Derive the DAX measure for YoY Revenue Growth and compute it given CY = ₹8 Cr, PY = ₹5 Cr. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Explain the concept of **Custom Perspectives** and how they enforce governance in a Custom Data View. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) — YoY Revenue Growth Derivation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Define variables:**

Let `R_CY` = current year revenue, `R_PY` = previous year revenue.

**Step 2 — Generic Formula:**

$$
\text{YoY Growth \%} = \frac{R_{CY} - R_{PY}}{R_{PY}} \times 100
$$

**Step 3 — DAX Implementation:**

$$
\text{YoY \%} = \text{DIVIDE}\bigl(\text{[Revenue CY]} - \text{[Revenue PY]},\; \text{[Revenue PY]},\; 0\bigr)
$$

where
$$
\text{[Revenue CY]} = \text{CALCULATE}\bigl(\text{SUM(Sales[Amount])},\; \text{'Date'[Year]} = \text{YEAR(TODAY())}\bigr)
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Computation:**

Given `R_CY = ₹8,00,00,000` and `R_PY = ₹5,00,00,000`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{YoY \%} \; & = \; \frac{8{,}00{,}00{,}000 - 5{,}00{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}00{,}000} \times 100 \\
 & = \; \frac{3{,}00{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}00{,}000} \times 100 \\
 & = \; 0.60 \times 100 \\
 & = \; 60\%
\end{aligned}
$$

✅ Revenue grew **60%** YoY.

*(Marks: 2 formula, 3 DAX mapping, 2 computation.)*

---

**Part (b) — Custom Perspectives (7 marks)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A Custom Perspective is a *subset* of the tables, columns, hierarchies, and measures of a tabular model that is exposed to a specific user role.

**Governance Functions:**

1. **Role-Based Visibility** — A "Sales Manager" perspective may show only Sales and Customer tables, hiding sensitive HR cost data.
2. **Cognitive Load Reduction** — End users see only relevant fields, preventing confusion.
3. **Security Layer** — Complements Row-Level Security (RLS); together they enforce *column-level* and *row-level* segregation.
4. **Auditability** — A clear scope of "who can see what" simplifies compliance with data-protection regulations (e.g., DPDP Act, GDPR).
5. **Maintainability** — Multiple perspectives can share one underlying model — single source of truth, multiple presentations.

**Real-World Mapping:** A pharmaceutical Custom Data View may have a *Clinician Perspective*, a *Finance Perspective*, and a *Supply Chain Perspective*, each defined by a Tabular Editor script.

*(Marks: 2 definition + 5 governance points × 1 each = 7 marks.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Custom Data View Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Custom Column with Custom Measure** — Examiners allocate **1 mark** specifically for correct identification. Mixing them up costs easy marks.
> 2. **Forgetting `DIVIDE` safe-denominator argument** — Always include the third argument (`0`) in DAX division; bare `/` operator returns `BLANK()` and may be marked down.
> 3. **Skipping argument-level explanation** — Part (a) of 14-mark questions expects **explicit justification** of every argument, not just code.
> 4. **No numerical verification** — Where numerical data is given, failing to substitute and compute costs 2 marks.
> 5. **Misnaming Perspective vs. RLS** — *Perspective* hides objects; *RLS* hides rows. Conflating them is a frequent error.
> 6. **Using `|` inside markdown tables in answer sheets** — Use `\vert` or `mod` notation instead.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Custom Data View**

- ✅ **Definition:** Personalized presentation layer over a tabular data model.
- ✅ **Three Tiers:** Data Source → Semantic Model → Presentation (Custom View).
- ✅ **Custom Column** = static, row-level, computed at refresh.
- ✅ **Custom Measure** = dynamic, aggregate-level, computed at query.
- ✅ **Custom Hierarchy** = drill-down path (e.g., Year → Quarter → Month).
- ✅ **Custom Perspective** = role-based subset of model objects.
- ✅ **Key DAX Functions:** `CALCULATE`, `DIVIDE`, `TOTALYTD`, `AVERAGEX`, `RANKX`, `ALL`, `FILTER`.
- ✅ **Safe Division:** Always use `DIVIDE(numerator, denominator, alternate)`.
- ✅ **YoY Formula:** `(CY − PY) / PY × 100`.
- ✅ **ATV Formula:** `Total Sales ÷ DISTINCTCOUNT(TransactionID)`.
- ✅ **Governance:** Combine Perspective + RLS for full security stack.
- ✅ **Always verify numerically** when problem supplies values.
- ✅ **Avoid** `|` in Markdown tables — use `\vert`.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Extracting and Editing Data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT522 - BUSINESS ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Extracting and Editing Data

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Data Extraction** is the systematic process of retrieving, retrieving, and consolidating data from heterogeneous source systems (structured databases, semi-structured files, unstructured web content, APIs, and legacy systems) into a unified, intermediate staging environment for subsequent analytical processing.

**Data Editing (Data Cleansing / Data Wrangling)** is the disciplined sequence of operations performed on extracted raw data to identify, correct, standardize, validate, and enrich inconsistencies, errors, missing values, duplicates, and structural anomalies, transforming it into a reliable, analysis-ready dataset.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Business Analytics syllabus**, these two activities form the foundational layer of the **ETL (Extract, Transform, Load)** pipeline and are critical pre-requisites for downstream descriptive, diagnostic, predictive, and prescriptive analytics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 explicitly tests a student's ability to *differentiate* between extraction and editing operations, *apply* appropriate SQL/Excel techniques, and *evaluate* data quality frameworks. Marks are routinely awarded for correctly identifying **staging areas**, **transformation logic**, and **data quality dimensions**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **gold refinery**:

- **Extraction** is analogous to **mining raw ore** from multiple distant quarries (databases, websites, sensors, files) and transporting it to a central processing plant (the staging area). The ore is messy, mixed with dirt, and unusable as-is.
- **Editing** is analogous to the **refining process** — crushing, washing, filtering, removing impurities (missing values), melting duplicates together, and casting the pure gold bars (clean dataset) ready for the jeweler (the analyst) to craft a finished product (the business insight).

Just as no jeweler can work with raw ore, no data scientist can build a reliable predictive model on raw, uncleaned data. The **"Garbage In, Garbage Out (GIGO)"** principle is the bedrock of business analytics.

### 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Data Source** | The origin system from which data is harvested (RDBMS, NoSQL, CSV, JSON, XML, Web API, IoT stream). |
| **Staging Area** | An intermediate, isolated storage zone where raw extracted data is temporarily held *unaltered* before transformation. |
| **Data Quality (DQ)** | The fitness of data for its intended use, measured across **accuracy, completeness, consistency, timeliness, uniqueness, and validity** dimensions. |
| **Schema Mapping** | The process of aligning fields from source schema to target schema. |
| **Data Lineage** | A documented trail tracing the origin, transformations, and destination of every data element. |
| **Master Data** | The consistent, shared, authoritative version of core business entities (customer, product, employee). |

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Standard Metrics:** KTU examiners expect familiarity with the **six core data quality dimensions** defined by **DAMA (Data Management Association)** and the **ISO 8000** standard. The benchmark acceptable error rate in cleaned datasets is typically **< 0.5%**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Data Extraction

Data extraction is classified along two primary axes: **Source Type** and **Trigger Mechanism**.

**A. By Source Type:**

1. **Structured Extraction** — From RDBMS (MySQL, Oracle, PostgreSQL) using **SQL SELECT** statements, views, and stored procedures.
2. **Semi-Structured Extraction** — From JSON, XML, CSV, Parquet files using parsers and XPath/XQuery.
3. **Unstructured Extraction** — From text documents, images, PDFs, social media feeds, web pages using **Web Scraping** (BeautifulSoup, Scrapy), **OCR**, and **NLP pipelines**.
4. **Streaming Extraction** — Real-time extraction from IoT sensors, clickstreams, and message queues (Apache Kafka, AWS Kinesis).

**B. By Trigger Mechanism:**

1. **Full Extraction** — Complete dump of source data. Resource-intensive, used for initial loads.
2. **Incremental Extraction** — Only records changed since the last extraction, identified via **timestamps**, **change-data-capture (CDC) flags**, or **database triggers**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Two Stages?** KTU examiners frequently ask: *"Why not extract and edit simultaneously?"* The answer: separating extraction (preserving raw, auditable source data in the staging area) from editing (creating reproducible transformation logic) enables **rollback, auditability, and regulatory compliance** (e.g., GDPR, RBI guidelines).

### 2.2 The Data Editing Workflow (Step-by-Step Logic)

Data editing follows a canonical six-stage pipeline:

1. **Profiling** — Statistical analysis of fields (min, max, mean, mode, distribution, null percentage) to understand anomalies.
2. **Standardization** — Converting heterogeneous formats to a unified format (e.g., `12/Jan/2024`, `2024-01-12`, `January 12, 2024` → `2024-01-12`).
3. **Deduplication** — Identifying and merging/eliminating duplicate records using **exact matching** or **fuzzy matching** (Levenshtein distance, Soundex).
4. **Missing Value Treatment** — Imputation strategies:
   - *Deletion* (listwise/pairwise)
   - *Mean/Median/Mode imputation*
   - *Regression imputation*
   - *K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) imputation*
   - *Forward/Backward fill* (time series)
5. **Outlier Detection & Treatment** — Using **Z-score** $\vert z \vert > 3$, **IQR method**, or **Isolation Forest**.
6. **Validation** — Enforcing business rules, referential integrity, and domain constraints.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Metric | Expression | Purpose / Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Completeness Ratio** | $C = \dfrac{\text{Non-null values}}{\text{Total values}} \times 100\%$ | Measures % of populated fields. |
| **Accuracy Ratio** | $A = \dfrac{\text{Correct entries}}{\text{Total entries}} \times 100\%$ | Measures correctness against ground truth. |
| **Uniqueness Ratio** | $U = \dfrac{\text{Distinct records}}{\text{Total records}} \times 100\%$ | Detects duplicate prevalence. |
| **Z-Score (Outlier)** | $z_i = \dfrac{x_i - \mu}{\sigma}$ | Flags values with $\vert z \vert > 3$. |
| **IQR Bounds** | $[\,Q_1 - 1.5 \cdot IQR,\ Q_3 + 1.5 \cdot IQR\,]$ | Box-plot outlier fences. |
| **Levenshtein Distance** | $L(a,b) = \vert a \vert + \vert b \vert - 2 \cdot LCS(a,b)$ *(simplified)* | Fuzzy string matching for dedup. |
| **ETL Throughput** | $T = \dfrac{N_{records}}{t_{elapsed}}$ (rows/sec) | Performance benchmarking. |
| **Data Quality Index (DQI)** | $DQI = \dfrac{w_1 C + w_2 A + w_3 U + w_4 V}{\sum w_i}$ | Composite quality score. |

> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used in **data warehousing** (Inmon/Kimball methodologies), **regulatory reporting** (Basel III, IFRS), **customer data platforms (CDPs)**, and **ML model preprocessing pipelines**.

### 2.4 The ETL vs. ELT Paradigm

| Feature | ETL (Traditional) | ELT (Modern Cloud) |
|---|---|---|
| **Order** | Extract → Transform → Load | Extract → Load → Transform |
| **Transformation Site** | Staging server (mid-tier) | Target data warehouse (Snowflake, BigQuery) |
| **Best For** | On-premise, regulated data | Cloud-scale, unstructured data |
| **Latency** | High (batch) | Low (real-time capable) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** The shift from **ETL to ELT** (enabled by cloud data warehouses like Snowflake, Redshift, BigQuery) is a recurring 14-mark question. Be prepared to contrast the two with respect to *scalability, cost, latency, and skill requirements*.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Walkthrough: SQL-Based Data Extraction

Extraction from a relational source is best demonstrated via a multi-step analytical query. Consider a **retail database** with tables: `Customers`, `Orders`, `Products`, `OrderItems`.

**Objective:** Extract a denormalized sales dataset for the year **2024** for customers in **Kerala**, with editing already partially applied (joining, filtering, aliasing).

**Step 1 — Base Selection (Extraction Layer):**

```sql
SELECT
    c.customer_id,
    c.customer_name,
    c.city,
    o.order_id,
    o.order_date,
    p.product_name,
    p.category,
    oi.quantity,
    oi.unit_price,
    (oi.quantity * oi.unit_price) AS line_revenue
FROM Customers c
INNER JOIN Orders o
    ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id
INNER JOIN OrderItems oi
    ON o.order_id = oi.order_id
INNER JOIN Products p
    ON oi.product_id = p.product_id
WHERE c.state = 'Kerala'
  AND YEAR(o.order_date) = 2024
  AND o.order_status = 'Completed';
```

**Step 2 — Editing via Conditional Logic:**

```sql
SELECT
    c.customer_id,
    UPPER(TRIM(c.customer_name)) AS clean_customer_name,
    CASE
        WHEN c.email IS NULL THEN 'UNKNOWN'
        WHEN c.email NOT LIKE '%_@__%.__%' THEN 'INVALID'
        ELSE LOWER(c.email)
    END AS validated_email,
    COALESCE(c.phone, 'N/A') AS contact_phone,
    o.order_date
FROM Customers c
LEFT JOIN Orders o ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id;
```

**Step 3 — Deduplication Using Window Function:**

```sql
WITH ranked AS (
    SELECT
        *,
        ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
            PARTITION BY customer_id, order_id
            ORDER BY last_modified DESC
        ) AS rn
    FROM staging_customer_orders
)
SELECT * FROM ranked WHERE rn = 1;
```

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation: Outlier Detection Using Z-Score

**Problem Statement:** A column `transaction_amount` has the following 5 sample values (₹): 100, 105, 98, 102, 5000. Determine whether 5000 is an outlier.

**Step 1 — Compute the Mean ($\mu$):**

$$
\mu = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i}{n}
$$

$$
\mu = \frac{100 + 105 + 98 + 102 + 5000}{5} = \frac{5405}{5} = 1081
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Standard Deviation ($\sigma$):**

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \mu)^2}{n}}
$$

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{(100-1081)^2 + (105-1081)^2 + (98-1081)^2 + (102-1081)^2 + (5000-1081)^2}{5}}
$$

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{961{,}881 + 952{,}576 + 967{,}009 + 958{,}849 + 15{,}370{,}881}{5}}
$$

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{19{,}211{,}196}{5}} = \sqrt{3{,}842{,}239.2} \approx 1960.16
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Z-Score for $x = 5000$:**

$$
z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma} = \frac{5000 - 1081}{1960.16} = \frac{3919}{1960.16} \approx 2.00
$$

**Step 4 — Decision Rule:**

$$
|z| > 3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Flagged as outlier}
$$

$$
|z| = 2.00 < 3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Not flagged (marginal case)}
$$

**Step 5 — Refinement using IQR for Robustness:**

The mean itself is skewed by the suspected outlier, demonstrating **masking**. The robust alternative is the **IQR method**:

$$
\text{Sorted: } [98, 100, 102, 105, 5000]
$$

$$
Q_1 = 100, \quad Q_3 = 105, \quad IQR = 5
$$

$$
\text{Upper Fence} = Q_3 + 1.5 \times IQR = 105 + 7.5 = 112.5
$$

$$
5000 \gg 112.5 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Clearly an outlier}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner Insight:** KTU evaluators *prefer* showing awareness that **mean-based methods are sensitive to outliers** (masking effect) and that **median/IQR-based methods are robust**. Mentioning this earns bonus marks.

### 3.3 Imputation Strategy Selection Matrix

| Missing Data Pattern | Best Strategy | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| **MCAR** (Missing Completely at Random) | Listwise deletion | No bias introduced by removal. |
| **MAR** (Missing at Random) | Mean/Mode/Regression imputation | Conditional probability can be modeled. |
| **MNAR** (Missing Not at Random) | KNN imputation, domain modeling | Requires auxiliary information. |
| **Time Series Missing** | Forward/Backward fill, Interpolation | Preserves temporal autocorrelation. |
| **High Cardinality Categorical** | Mode imputation + "Missing" flag | Avoids creating artificial ordering. |

### 3.4 Worked Example: Data Quality Index Computation

A dataset has 10,000 customer records. After editing, the following observations are made:

- **8,000** have valid (non-null) email addresses
- **9,500** have correct gender entries (verified against ground truth)
- **9,800** are unique (no duplicates)
- **9,900** pass business rule validation (e.g., age between 18 and 100)

Using equal weights ($w_1 = w_2 = w_3 = w_4 = 1$):

$$
C = \frac{8000}{10000} \times 100\% = 80.0\%
$$

$$
A = \frac{9500}{10000} \times 100\% = 95.0\%
$$

$$
U = \frac{9800}{10000} \times 100\% = 98.0\%
$$

$$
V = \frac{9900}{10000} \times 100\% = 99.0\%
$$

$$
DQI = \frac{80.0 + 95.0 + 98.0 + 99.0}{4} = \frac{372.0}{4} = 93.0\%
$$

A **DQI of 93%** indicates high-quality data, suitable for downstream business analytics.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End ETL Pipeline Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src1[(RDBMS)]
    src2[(NoSQL DB)]
    src3[(CSV Files)]
    src4[(Web API)]
    src5[(IoT Stream)]
    ext1[Extraction Engine]
    ext2[CDC Tracker]
    ext3[Web Scraper]
    stage[(Staging Area)]
    prof[Data Profiling]
    std[Standardization]
    dedup[Deduplication]
    imp[Imputation]
    out[Outlier Detection]
    val[Validation]
    dw[(Data Warehouse)]
    viz[BI Dashboard]
    ml[ML Pipeline]

    src1 --> ext1
    src2 --> ext1
    src3 --> ext1
    src4 --> ext3
    src5 --> ext2
    ext1 --> stage
    ext2 --> stage
    ext3 --> stage
    stage --> prof
    prof --> std
    std --> dedup
    dedup --> imp
    imp --> out
    out --> val
    val --> dw
    dw --> viz
    dw --> ml
```

### 4.2 Data Editing Sub-Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Extracted Data] --> B[Data Profiling]
    B --> C{Issues Found?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Standardize Formats]
    C -- No --> H[Validated Data]
    D --> E[Deduplicate Records]
    E --> F{Missing Values?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Apply Imputation]
    F -- No --> I[Outlier Check]
    G --> I
    I --> J{Outliers Detected?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Treat or Cap]
    J -- No --> L[Business Rule Validation]
    K --> L
    L --> M{Valid?}
    M -- Yes --> H
    M -- No --> N[Quarantine / Reject]
    H --> O[Analysis-Ready Dataset]
    N --> P[Error Log]
```

### 4.3 Data Source Classification Matrix

```mermaid
graph TB
    DS[Data Sources]
    STR[Structured]
    SEMI[Semi-Structured]
    UNS[Unstructured]

    DS --> STR
    DS --> SEMI
    DS --> UNS

    STR --> S1[MySQL]
    STR --> S2[PostgreSQL]
    STR --> S3[Oracle]
```
