```mermaid
    SEMI --> M1[JSON Files]
    SEMI --> M2[XML Docs]
    SEMI --> M3[CSV Parquet]
    SEMI --> M4[MongoDB]

    UNS --> U1[Web Pages]
    UNS --> U2[PDFs Images]
    UNS --> U3[Social Media]
    UNS --> U4[Email Archives]
```

### 4.4 Extraction vs. Editing Responsibility Matrix (RACI)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    role1[Data Engineer]
    role2[Data Analyst]
    role3[Data Steward]
    role4[Business User]

    act1[Extraction]
    act2[Profiling]
    act3[Cleansing]
    act4[Validation]
    act5[Consumption]

    role1 -- R --> act1
    role1 -- R --> act2
    role2 -- C --> act2
    role3 -- A --> act3
    role3 -- R --> act4
    role4 -- I --> act5
    role2 -- R --> act5
```

> **Legend:** R = Responsible, A = Accountable, C = Consulted, I = Informed

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Distinguish between Data Extraction and Data Editing. Why are they performed in separate stages?*

**Model Answer:**
Data Extraction is the act of retrieving raw data from heterogeneous source systems (databases, files, APIs) and loading it into a staging area without modification. Data Editing is the subsequent process of cleansing, transforming, and validating this raw data to make it analysis-ready. They are performed separately to preserve the **immutable audit copy** of source data, enable **rollback** in case of faulty transformations, ensure **regulatory compliance**, and allow **parallel processing**. This separation is the foundation of the traditional **ETL** architecture.

*(Expected Answer Length: 80–100 words | 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List any six data quality dimensions as per the DAMA framework with one-line definitions.*

**Model Answer:**
1. **Accuracy** — Degree to which data correctly represents the real-world entity.
2. **Completeness** — Proportion of stored data against the 100% expected population.
3. **Consistency** — Absence of contradictions across systems.
4. **Timeliness** — Degree to which data is up-to-date for the task.
5. **Uniqueness** — Absence of duplicate records for the same entity.
6. **Validity** — Conformance to defined business rules and formats.
*(Bonus: Validity, Integrity, Reasonableness)*

*(Expected Answer Length: 70–90 words | 3 marks)*

---

### Part B — Extended Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Note:** Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, answer **ONE** of the two alternatives. Each contains two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

**Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the complete **ETL pipeline** in detail. With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the role of the **staging area** and the **data warehouse**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare and contrast **ETL** with **ELT**. Discuss at least four parameters and explain why cloud-based analytics platforms prefer ELT. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution for (a):**

The **ETL pipeline** consists of three sequential stages:

1. **Extract** — Data is pulled from multiple source systems (RDBMS, NoSQL, flat files, APIs, IoT streams). Extraction can be **full** (complete dump) or **incremental** (CDC-based delta).
2. **Transform** — The extracted data undergoes editing operations: profiling, standardization, deduplication, imputation, outlier treatment, and validation against business rules.
3. **Load** — The cleaned, transformed data is loaded into a **data warehouse** using **bulk loading** utilities for performance.

The **staging area** is an intermediate storage zone that:
- Preserves the raw extracted data in its original form (immutable).
- Isolates source systems from the analytical load.
- Enables parallel editing operations and rollback capability.

The **data warehouse** is a **subject-oriented, integrated, non-volatile, time-variant** collection of data (per Bill Inmon's definition) designed for analytical queries and decision support.

*(Block diagram from Section 4.1 may be reproduced for full 7 marks.)*

**Model Solution for (b):**

| Parameter | ETL | ELT |
|---|---|---|
| **Transformation Location** | Mid-tier server (staging) | Target warehouse (in-database) |
| **Order** | Extract → Transform → Load | Extract → Load → Transform |
| **Scalability** | Limited by server capacity | Elastic, cloud-native |
| **Latency** | High (batch-oriented) | Low (near real-time) |
| **Best For** | Sensitive, regulated, on-premise data | Petabyte-scale, semi/unstructured data |
| **Tools** | Informatica, Talend, SSIS | Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift + dbt |

**Why ELT is preferred on cloud platforms:** Cloud data warehouses leverage **massively parallel processing (MPP)** and **columnar storage**, making in-warehouse transformations extremely fast. Decoupling transformation from extraction allows data engineers to **re-run transformations** (using tools like **dbt**) without re-extracting data, improving agility and reducing cost.

---

**Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** Discuss the various **data quality issues** encountered during the editing phase. For each issue, suggest an appropriate **editing technique** with justification. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A column `daily_sales` contains 7 observations (in ₹000s): 45, 48, 50, 47, 49, 46, 850. Using the **IQR method**, determine if 850 is an outlier. If yes, suggest a treatment strategy. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution for (a):**

| Data Quality Issue | Example | Editing Technique | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Missing Values** | NULL in `email` | Mean/Median/Mode imputation; KNN | Preserves sample size; choice depends on MCAR/MAR/MNAR. |
| **Duplicates** | Same customer twice | ROW_NUMBER() window function; fuzzy match | Ensures entity uniqueness. |
| **Inconsistent Formats** | `12/01/2024` vs `2024-01-12` | Standardization with `TO_DATE()`, regex | Enables correct temporal sorting. |
| **Outliers** | Salary = ₹999 crore | IQR / Z-score capping | Prevents skewing of statistical models. |
| **Invalid Values** | Age = 250 | Business rule validation | Enforces domain constraints. |
| **Inconsistent Categoricals** | "M"/"Male"/"male" | Lookup table mapping | Achieves semantic consistency. |
| **Type Mismatches** | String `123` in integer field | CAST / data type enforcement | Prevents downstream computation errors. |

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:** Data = [45, 48, 50, 47, 49, 46, 850]

**Step 1 — Sort the data:**
[45, 46, 47, 48, 49, 50, 850]

**Step 2 — Identify Quartiles:**

For $n = 7$ (odd), the median is the 4th value:

$$
Q_2 = 48
$$

Lower half: [45, 46, 47] → Median = $Q_1 = 46$

Upper half: [49, 50, 850] → Median = $Q_3 = 50$

**Step 3 — Compute IQR:**

$$
IQR = Q_3 - Q_1 = 50 - 46 = 4
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Fences:**

$$
\text{Lower Fence} = Q_1 - 1.5 \times IQR = 46 - 6 = 40
$$

$$
\text{Upper Fence} = Q_3 + 1.5 \times IQR = 50 + 6 = 56
$$

**Step 5 — Decision:**

$$
850 \gg 56 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 850 \text{ is an EXTREME OUTLIER}
$$

**Step 6 — Treatment Strategies:**

1. **Winsorization** — Cap the value at the upper fence (₹56,000).
2. **Log Transformation** — Apply $\log(850) = 2.93$ to compress scale.
3. **Removal** — Delete the record if data volume permits and the outlier is a data-entry error.
4. **Imputation** — Replace with $Q_3 = 50$ (robust to outliers).
5. **Separate Modeling** — Build two models, one with and one without the outlier, and compare.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing ETL with ELT** — Students often write generic definitions without specifying the *order* of operations.
> 2. **Forgetting the staging area** — A 14-mark ETL question without mentioning the staging area loses **at least 2 marks**.
> 3. **Skipping computation steps in IQR/Z-score** — Examiners award **step marks**. Always show sorted data, quartile identification, and final fence calculation explicitly.
> 4. **Recommending deletion for missing values blindly** — Always state the *missingness mechanism* (MCAR/MAR/MNAR) before choosing a strategy.
> 5. **Ignoring data lineage** — Modern KTU questions reward answers that mention **auditability, lineage, and reproducibility** of the editing process.
> 6. **Using Python code blocks** — Business Analytics in KTU 2024 expects **SQL, Excel, and conceptual** answers. Avoid full Python scripts; use SQL snippets only.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Extraction ≠ Editing**: Extraction is *retrieval*; Editing is *cleansing + transformation*.
- ✅ **Staging Area** is *non-negotiable* in any KTU ETL answer — it preserves raw data.
- ✅ **Six Data Quality Dimensions**: Accuracy, Completeness, Consistency, Timeliness, Uniqueness, Validity (DAMA).
- ✅ **Missingness Mechanisms**: MCAR (delete) → MAR (mean/regression impute) → MNAR (KNN/domain).
- ✅ **Outlier Detection**: Use **Z-score** for normal data; use **IQR** for skewed data (robust).
- ✅ **ETL vs ELT**: ETL = transform in middle tier; ELT = transform in warehouse (cloud-native).
- ✅ **Deduplication**: SQL `ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY ...)` or fuzzy matching (Levenshtein).
- ✅ **Standardization Examples**: Date formats, casing (UPPER/LOWER), trimming whitespace, currency symbols.
- ✅ **Incremental Extraction**: Uses CDC flags or `last_modified` timestamps to pull only deltas.
- ✅ **Audit & Lineage**: Always document transformation rules for **regulatory compliance** (GDPR, RBI).
- ✅ **DQI Formula**: Weighted average of completeness, accuracy, uniqueness, validity.
- ✅ **Tools to mention**: SQL, Excel Power Query, Talend, Informatica, Alteryx, dbt, Apache Airflow.
- ✅ **Always show computation steps** in numerical questions — KTU awards step marks, not just final answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transformation of Variables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT522 - BUSINESS ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Transformation of Variables

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transformation of Variables** is a statistical technique in which a random variable $X$ is mathematically converted into a new random variable $Y$ through a function $Y = g(X)$ (or equivalently $X = h(Y)$), in order to simplify analysis, satisfy distributional assumptions, stabilize variance, or improve interpretability in business analytics models.

In the KTU 2024 Business Analytics context, transformation of variables is a critical **data preprocessing and statistical modeling tool** that allows analysts to:

- **Linearize** non-linear relationships (e.g., exponential, power-law).
- **Normalize** skewed data distributions.
- **Stabilize** the variance across groups.
- **Convert** multiplicative relationships into additive ones.
- **Improve** the validity of inferential tests (e.g., t-tests, ANOVA, regression) that assume normality and homoscedasticity.

Mathematically, if $X$ is a continuous random variable with probability density function (PDF) $f_X(x)$ and $Y = g(X)$ is a **monotonic, differentiable** transformation, then the PDF of $Y$ is given by the **change-of-variables formula**:

$$f_Y(y) = f_X(x) \cdot \left| \frac{dx}{dy} \right| = f_X\bigl(g^{-1}(y)\bigr) \cdot \left| \frac{d}{dy}g^{-1}(y) \right|$$

where the absolute value (Jacobian term) ensures the PDF remains non-negative.

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU 2024 Scheme Business Analytics, this topic directly maps to **CO3: Apply appropriate data transformation techniques to prepare data for analysis and modeling.**

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **currency trader** in Kerala who earns income in **US Dollars (USD)** but spends in **Indian Rupees (INR)**. The raw data on your income (in USD) cannot be directly compared to expenditure data (in INR). To make sense of your finances, you **transform** every USD figure using the function:

$$\text{INR} = 84.50 \times \text{USD}$$

This is a **linear transformation** — it changes the *scale* of every value but preserves the *shape* of the distribution. Similarly, in business analytics, we transform variables to make them **comparable, interpretable, and statistically well-behaved**.

Another analogy: Consider **movie box-office revenue** that is highly right-skewed (a few blockbusters dwarf the rest). Applying a **logarithmic transformation** $Y = \log(X)$ "compresses" the large values and "stretches" the small ones, producing a more symmetric, bell-shaped distribution that is suitable for regression and hypothesis testing.

> [!TIP]
> Think of transformation as **putting on a different pair of glasses** — the underlying data hasn't changed, but you can now see patterns that were previously hidden by skewness, outliers, or scale differences.

---

## 1.3 Why Transformations Matter in Business Analytics

| Business Scenario | Raw Variable | Recommended Transformation | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| Customer income data | Annual income in ₹ | $\log(X)$ | Highly right-skewed |
| Comparing departments | Sales in different currencies | Linear scaling | Scale harmonization |
| Regression with curves | $X$ vs $Y$ (exponential) | $\log(Y)$ on $Y$ | Linearize relationship |
| Machine learning preprocessing | Features at different scales | $Z = (X-\mu)/\sigma$ | Standardization for ML |
| Multiplicative effects | Revenue = Price × Volume | $\log(\text{Revenue}) = \log(\text{Price}) + \log(\text{Volume})$ | Additive decomposition |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **two foundational transformation families** in business analytics are:
> 1. **Mathematical Transformations** — algebraic functions (log, square root, reciprocal, Box-Cox).
> 2. **Standardization / Normalization Transformations** — rescaling operations (Z-score, min-max scaling, robust scaling).

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

When performing transformations, the following standard metrics are commonly used:

- **Mean** ($\mu$ or $\bar{X}$): The arithmetic average of the data, equal to $\mu = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$.
- **Standard Deviation** ($\sigma$ or $s$): The square root of variance, measuring spread.
- **Variance** ($\sigma^2$ or $s^2$): The average of squared deviations from the mean.
- **Median**: The middle value of an ordered dataset.
- **Interquartile Range (IQR)**: The difference between the 75th and 25th percentiles, used in robust scaling.
- **Skewness** ($\gamma_1$): A measure of asymmetry, where $\gamma_1 = 0$ indicates perfect symmetry.
- **Kurtosis** ($\gamma_2$): A measure of tail-heaviness relative to a normal distribution.

> [!NOTE]
> All transformations preserve the **rank order** of data (for monotonic functions) but can dramatically alter the **mean, variance, skewness, and kurtosis**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Variable Transformations

Transformations are classified along two primary dimensions:

### A. By Mathematical Form

1. **Linear Transformations** — $Y = aX + b$ where $a \neq 0$.
2. **Logarithmic Transformations** — $Y = \log_b(X)$ (commonly $b = e$ or $b = 10$).
3. **Power Transformations** — $Y = X^{\lambda}$ for various $\lambda$.
4. **Reciprocal Transformations** — $Y = 1/X$.
5. **Exponential Transformations** — $Y = e^{X}$.
6. **Box-Cox Transformation** — A generalized power transformation family.
7. **Rank-Based Transformations** — $Y = \text{rank}(X)$.

### B. By Statistical Purpose

1. **Shape Transformations** — To normalize the distribution shape.
2. **Scale Transformations** — To change the unit or magnitude.
3. **Location Transformations** — To shift the center of the distribution.
4. **Variance-Stabilizing Transformations** — To make variance independent of the mean.

---

## 2.2 Linear Transformations — Theory

Given $Y = aX + b$ where $a$ and $b$ are real constants with $a \neq 0$:

- The transformation is **affine** (shift + scale).
- It is a **monotonic** function, so rank order is preserved.
- The shape of the distribution remains **unchanged** — only its location and scale change.

### Why and How:
- **Why**: To convert units (e.g., Celsius to Fahrenheit, INR to USD), to center data around zero, or to rescale to a standard range.
- **How**: Choose $a$ as the scaling factor (slope) and $b$ as the shift (intercept).

---

## 2.3 Logarithmic Transformations — Theory

Given $Y = \log(X)$ (natural log) or $Y = \log_{10}(X)$:

- Compresses large values and expands small values.
- **Strongly effective** for right-skewed data (common in business: income, sales, waiting times).
- Converts **multiplicative** relationships into **additive** ones.
- For $X > 0$; zero and negative values must be shifted first: $Y = \log(X + c)$ for some constant $c > 0$.

### Why and How:
- **Why**: Income distributions, sales figures, and customer counts are typically right-skewed. Log transformation makes them approximately normal.
- **How**: Apply $\log$ to every observation; verify skewness reduction; proceed with parametric tests.

---

## 2.4 Power & Box-Cox Transformations — Theory

The **Box-Cox transformation** is a parameterized family defined as:

$$Y^{(\lambda)} = \begin{cases} \dfrac{X^{\lambda} - 1}{\lambda} & \text{if } \lambda \neq 0 \\[10pt] \ln(X) & \text{if } \lambda = 0 \end{cases}$$

where $\lambda$ is the transformation parameter, typically estimated by **maximum likelihood** to maximize normality of the transformed variable.

### Special Cases of $\lambda$:

| $\lambda$ | Transformation | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| $-1$ | $Y = 1/X$ | Reciprocal, for left-skewed data |
| $-0.5$ | $Y = 1/\sqrt{X}$ | Moderate reciprocal |
| $0$ | $Y = \log(X)$ | Standard log (limit as $\lambda \to 0$) |
| $0.5$ | $Y = \sqrt{X}$ | Square root, for count data |
| $1$ | $Y = X$ | No transformation |
| $2$ | $Y = X^2$ | Square, for left-skewed data |
| $3$ | $Y = X^3$ | Cube, for strongly left-skewed data |

---

## 2.5 Standardization (Z-Score Normalization) — Theory

$$Z = \frac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$$

- Centers the data at **mean = 0** and scales to **standard deviation = 1**.
- Does not change the **shape** of the distribution.
- Essential for algorithms sensitive to feature scaling (e.g., K-Nearest Neighbors, Support Vector Machines, Principal Component Analysis, Gradient Descent-based models).
- The transformed variable $Z$ always has $\mu_Z = 0$ and $\sigma_Z = 1$.

---

## 2.6 Min-Max Normalization (Feature Scaling) — Theory

$$X_{\text{norm}} = \frac{X - X_{\min}}{X_{\max} - X_{\min}}$$

- Rescales data to the interval $[0, 1]$.
- Preserves the **shape** of the distribution.
- **Sensitive to outliers** (one extreme value compresses the rest of the data).
- Commonly used in neural networks and image processing.

---

## 2.7 Robust Scaling — Theory

$$X_{\text{robust}} = \frac{X - \text{Median}(X)}{\text{IQR}(X)}$$$

- Uses **median** and **IQR** instead of mean and standard deviation.
- **Resistant to outliers** — ideal for business data with extreme values.
- Common in financial analytics where outliers are frequent and meaningful.

---

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Transformation | Formula | Effect on Mean | Effect on Variance | Effect on Shape | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Linear | $Y = aX + b$ | $\mu_Y = a\mu_X + b$ | $\sigma^2_Y = a^2 \sigma^2_X$ | **None** | Unit conversion, centering |
| 2 | Z-Score Standardization | $Z = (X-\mu)/\sigma$ | $\mu_Z = 0$ | $\sigma^2_Z = 1$ | **None** | ML preprocessing, standardization |
| 3 | Min-Max Normalization | $X^* = (X-X_{\min})/(X_{\max}-X_{\min})$ | $0 \leq X^* \leq 1$ | Reduced | **None** | Neural networks, bounded scaling |
| 4 | Logarithmic | $Y = \log(X)$ | Complex (no simple formula) | Complex | Reduces right skew | Skewed income/sales data |
| 5 | Square Root | $Y = \sqrt{X}$ | Complex | Complex | Mildly reduces right skew | Count data (Poisson) |
| 6 | Reciprocal | $Y = 1/X$ | Complex | Complex | Reverses skew direction | Left-skewed or rate data |
| 7 | Square | $Y = X^2$ | Complex | Complex | Increases right skew | Left-skewed data |
| 8 | Box-Cox | $Y^{(\lambda)} = (X^{\lambda}-1)/\lambda$ | Depends on $\lambda$ | Depends on $\lambda$ | Optimized for normality | Automated normality seeking |
| 9 | Robust Scaling | $X' = (X - \text{med})/\text{IQR}$ | Median-centered | IQR-scaled | **None** | Data with outliers |
| 10 | Log of (X+1) | $Y = \log(X+1)$ | Defined for $X \geq 0$ | Complex | Reduces right skew | Data with zeros |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Invariant**: For ANY monotonic transformation, the **rank order** of the data is preserved. This means Spearman's rank correlation is unaffected by monotonic transformations.

---

## 2.9 Real-World Utility in Business Analytics

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Marketing Analytics** | Log-transforming customer lifetime value (CLV) for regression modeling |
| **Financial Analytics** | Standardizing stock returns for portfolio optimization |
| **Operations Research** | Square-root transforming wait times to apply ANOVA |
| **Human Resources** | Box-Cox transforming salary data to study compensation equity |
| **Machine Learning** | Min-max scaling features for neural network training |
| **Econometrics** | Log-log models to estimate price elasticities: $\log(Q) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \log(P)$ |
| **Customer Segmentation** | Z-score normalization for clustering (K-Means) |
| **Risk Management** | Robust scaling of loss data to handle extreme events |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Effect of Linear Transformation on Mean and Variance

**Given:** $Y = aX + b$, where $a$ and $b$ are constants and $X$ is a random variable with mean $\mu_X$ and variance $\sigma^2_X$.

**Goal:** Derive $\mu_Y$ and $\sigma^2_Y$ in terms of $\mu_X$ and $\sigma^2_X$.

### Step 1: Write the definition of expected value for $Y$.

$$\mu_Y = E[Y] = E[aX + b]$$

### Step 2: Apply the linearity of expectation.

Since expectation is a linear operator, $E[aX + b] = a \cdot E[X] + b$.

$$\mu_Y = a \cdot E[X] + b = a\mu_X + b$$

### Step 3: Write the definition of variance for $Y$.

$$\sigma^2_Y = \text{Var}(Y) = E[(Y - \mu_Y)^2]$$

### Step 4: Substitute $Y = aX + b$ and $\mu_Y = a\mu_X + b$.

$$\sigma^2_Y = E\bigl[(aX + b - a\mu_X - b)^2\bigr]$$

### Step 5: Simplify the expression inside the expectation.

The constant $b$ cancels out, leaving:

$$\sigma^2_Y = E\bigl[(aX - a\mu_X)^2\bigr] = E\bigl[a^2(X - \mu_X)^2\bigr]$$

### Step 6: Factor out the constant $a^2$.

$$\sigma^2_Y = a^2 \cdot E[(X - \mu_X)^2] = a^2 \cdot \text{Var}(X) = a^2 \sigma^2_X$$

### Final Result:

$$\boxed{\mu_Y = a\mu_X + b \quad \text{and} \quad \sigma^2_Y = a^2 \sigma^2_X}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The shift $b$ does **not** affect variance. Only the scale factor $a$ matters. This is why standardization (setting $a = 1/\sigma$ and $b = -\mu/\sigma$) reduces variance to exactly 1.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Change-of-Variables Formula for a PDF

**Given:** $X$ is a continuous random variable with PDF $f_X(x)$, and $Y = g(X)$ is a strictly monotonic, differentiable transformation.

**Goal:** Derive the PDF $f_Y(y)$ of $Y$.

### Step 1: Use the definition of the cumulative distribution function (CDF).

$$F_Y(y) = P(Y \leq y) = P(g(X) \leq y)$$

### Step 2: Apply the inverse transformation.

Since $g$ is strictly monotonic, $g(X) \leq y$ is equivalent to $X \leq g^{-1}(y)$ (if $g$ is increasing) or $X \geq g^{-1}(y)$ (if $g$ is decreasing).

$$F_Y(y) = P\bigl(X \leq g^{-1}(y)\bigr) = F_X\bigl(g^{-1}(y)\bigr)$$

### Step 3: Differentiate both sides with respect to $y$ to get the PDF.

$$\frac{d}{dy}F_Y(y) = \frac{d}{dy}F_X\bigl(g^{-1}(y)\bigr)$$

### Step 4: Apply the chain rule.

$$f_Y(y) = f_X\bigl(g^{-1}(y)\bigr) \cdot \frac{d}{dy}g^{-1}(y)$$

### Step 5: Take the absolute value to ensure non-negativity (works for both increasing and decreasing $g$).

$$f_Y(y) = f_X\bigl(g^{-1}(y)\bigr) \cdot \left| \frac{d}{dy}g^{-1}(y) \right|$$

### Final Result (Change-of-Variables Formula):

$$\boxed{f_Y(y) = f_X\bigl(g^{-1}(y)\bigr) \cdot \left| \frac{d}{dy}g^{-1}(y) \right|}$$

Equivalently, since $x = g^{-1}(y)$:

$$f_Y(y) = f_X(x) \cdot \left| \frac{dx}{dy} \right|$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The factor $\left| \frac{dx}{dy} \right|$ is called the **Jacobian** of the transformation. It is the absolute value of the derivative of the inverse transformation evaluated at $y$.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 1: Linear Transformation of a Uniform Distribution

**Problem:** Let $X \sim \text{Uniform}(0, 10)$ so that $f_X(x) = 1/10$ for $0 \leq x \leq 10$. Find the PDF of $Y = 3X + 5$.

### Step 1: Identify the transformation and its inverse.

$$Y = 3X + 5 \implies X = \frac{Y - 5}{3} = g^{-1}(y)$$

### Step 2: Compute the Jacobian.

$$\frac{dx}{dy} = \frac{d}{dy}\left(\frac{y-5}{3}\right) = \frac{1}{3}$$

$$\left| \frac{dx}{dy} \right| = \frac{1}{3}$$

### Step 3: Determine the support of $Y$.

When $x = 0$, $y = 5$. When $x = 10$, $y = 35$. So $5 \leq y \leq 35$.

### Step 4: Apply the change-of-variables formula.

$$f_Y(y) = f_X\!\left(\frac{y-5}{3}\right) \cdot \frac{1}{3}$$

Since $f_X(x) = 1/10$ for $x \in [0, 10]$:

$$f_Y(y) = \frac{1}{10} \cdot \frac{1}{3} = \frac{1}{30} \quad \text{for } 5 \leq y \leq 35$$

### Verification: The total probability should be 1.

$$\int_5^{35} \frac{1}{30}\,dy = \frac{1}{30}(35-5) = \frac{30}{30} = 1 \quad \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice that $Y$ is also uniformly distributed, just on a different interval: $Y \sim \text{Uniform}(5, 35)$. This confirms our theoretical result: **linear transformations preserve the shape** of a uniform distribution.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 2: Logarithmic Transformation of an Exponential Distribution

**Problem:** Let $X \sim \text{Exponential}(\lambda = 2)$ so that $f_X(x) = 2e^{-2x}$ for $x \geq 0$. Find the PDF of $Y = \log(X)$.

### Step 1: Identify the transformation and its inverse.

$$Y = \log(X) \implies X = e^Y = g^{-1}(y)$$

### Step 2: Compute the Jacobian.

$$\frac{dx}{dy} = \frac{d}{dy}(e^y) = e^y$$

$$\left| \frac{dx}{dy} \right| = e^y$$

### Step 3: Determine the support of $Y$.

When $x \in (0, \infty)$, $y = \log(x) \in (-\infty, \infty)$.

### Step 4: Apply the change-of-variables formula.

$$f_Y(y) = f_X(e^y) \cdot e^y = 2e^{-2e^y} \cdot e^y = 2e^{y - 2e^y}$$

### Final Answer:

$$f_Y(y) = 2e^{y - 2e^y} \quad \text{for } y \in (-\infty, \infty)$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **double-exponential-type** distribution. It is not a standard named distribution, but it illustrates how a nonlinear transformation fundamentally changes the distribution shape.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 3: Box-Cox Transformation for Skewness Correction

**Problem:** A business analyst has the following monthly sales data (in ₹ thousands): $15, 18, 22, 25, 30, 35, 45, 60, 90, 200$. The data is right-skewed. Apply the Box-Cox transformation with $\lambda = 0$ (i.e., log transformation) and verify the skewness reduction.

### Step 1: Compute the original skewness.

The mean is $\bar{X} = 54.0$ and the standard deviation is $s \approx 56.7$.

The skewness (using the standard formula) is:

$$\gamma_1 = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(\frac{x_i - \bar{X}}{s}\right)^3 \approx 2.34$$

This is **highly right-skewed** ($\gamma_1 > 0$).

### Step 2: Apply the log transformation: $Y_i = \log(X_i)$.

| $X_i$ | $Y_i = \log(X_i)$ |
|---|---|
| 15 | 2.708 |
| 18 | 2.890 |
| 22 | 3.091 |
| 25 | 3.219 |
| 30 | 3.401 |
| 35 | 3.555 |
| 45 | 3.807 |
| 60 | 4.094 |
| 90 | 4.500 |
| 200 | 5.298 |

### Step 3: Compute the skewness of the transformed data.

The mean of $Y$ is $\bar{Y} = 3.656$ and the standard deviation is $s_Y \approx 0.781$.

The skewness of $Y$ is approximately:

$$\gamma_1(Y) \approx 0.12$$

### Step 4: Interpret the result.

The skewness dropped from **2.34** to **0.12** — a near-perfect normalization. The log transformation was highly effective for this business data.

> [!TIP]
> In practice, the optimal $\lambda$ for Box-Cox is found by maximum likelihood. In Python's `scipy.stats.boxcox`, the function returns both the transformed data and the optimal $\lambda$ value. However, as per KTU guidelines, we present the manual analytical workflow here.

---

## 3.6 Worked Example 4: Z-Score Standardization with Practical Computation

**Problem:** A company's five sales representatives have monthly sales (in ₹ lakhs): $4, 6, 8, 10, 12$. Standardize the data using the Z-score formula.

### Step 1: Compute the mean.

$$\bar{X} = \frac{4 + 6 + 8 + 10 + 12}{5} = \frac{40}{5} = 8$$

### Step 2: Compute the variance.

$$\sigma^2 = \frac{1}{5}\left[(4-8)^2 + (6-8)^2 + (8-8)^2 + (10-8)^2 + (12-8)^2\right]$$

$$\sigma^2 = \frac{1}{5}\left[16 + 4 + 0 + 4 + 16\right] = \frac{40}{5} = 8$$

### Step 3: Compute the standard deviation.

$$\sigma = \sqrt{8} = 2\sqrt{2} \approx 2.828$$

### Step 4: Apply $Z = (X - \bar{X})/\sigma$.

| $X_i$ | $Z_i = (X_i - 8)/2.828$ |
|---|---|
| 4 | $-1.414$ |
| 6 | $-0.707$ |
| 8 | $0.000$ |
| 10 | $0.707$ |
| 12 | $1.414$ |

### Step 5: Verify $\mu_Z = 0$ and $\sigma_Z = 1$.

$$\bar{Z} = \frac{-1.414 - 0.707 + 0 + 0.707 + 1.414}{5} = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

$$s_Z^2 = \frac{1}{5}\left[(-1.414)^2 + (-0.707)^2 + 0^2 + (0.707)^2 + (1.414)^2\right] = \frac{1}{5}[2 + 0.5 + 0 + 0.5 + 2] = 1 \quad \checkmark$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The verification step is critical. Always confirm that standardization yields mean = 0 and variance = 1.

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Transformation

| Diagnostic Finding on Raw Data | Recommended Transformation | Expected Outcome |
|---|---|---|
| Moderate right skew ($0.5 < \gamma_1 < 1$) | Square root: $Y = \sqrt{X}$ | Skewness approaches 0 |
| Strong right skew ($\gamma_1 > 1$) | Log: $Y = \log(X)$ or $Y = \log(X+1)$ | Skewness approaches 0 |
| Left skew ($\gamma_1 < -0.5$) | Square: $Y = X^2$ or reflect-and-log | Skewness approaches 0 |
| Heteroscedasticity in regression | Log of $Y$: $Y' = \log(Y)$ | Constant variance across $X$ levels |
| Different measurement units (e.g., ₹ vs $) | Z-score standardization | Comparable scales (mean=0, SD=1) |
| Features with different ranges for ML | Min-max normalization | All features in $[0, 1]$ |
| Data with extreme outliers | Robust scaling | Outlier-resistant rescaling |
| Optimal normality sought | Box-Cox with MLE $\lambda$ | Best possible normality |
| Percentage/proportion data | Logit: $Y = \log(p/(1-p))$ | Unbounded, approximately normal |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Transformation Workflow Diagram

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **decision workflow** for choosing the appropriate variable transformation in a business analytics pipeline.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Raw Variable X] --> B{Data Type?}
    B --> C[Numerical Continuous]
    B --> D[Numerical Count]
    B --> E[Proportion / Percentage]
    C --> F{Inspect Distribution}
    F --> G{Skewness Check}
    G --> H[Skewness = 0]
    G --> I[Right Skewed gamma1 greater than 0.5]
    G --> J[Left Skewed gamma1 less than negative 0.5]
    H --> K[No transformation needed or Z-score standardization for ML]
    I --> L{Strength of Skew}
    L --> M[Mild: Square Root Y = sqrt X]
    L --> N[Strong: Log Y = log X or Y = log X plus 1]
    L --> O[Unknown: Box-Cox with MLE lambda]
    J --> P[Reflect and Log: Y = log max X plus 1 minus X]
    D --> Q[Count Data: Square Root or Log X plus 1]
    E --> R[Proportion Data: Logit Y = log p divided by 1 minus p]
    K --> S[End: Use Transformed or Original Variable]
    M --> S
    N --> S
    O --> S
    P --> S
    Q --> S
    R --> S
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the diagram above, the notation `gamma1 greater than 0.5` replaces the mathematical symbol $\gamma_1 > 0.5$ to comply with the Mermaid label formatting restriction (no LaTeX or markdown formatting inside double-quoted labels).

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Transformation Pipeline

The following diagram shows the **end-to-end functional architecture** of a transformation pipeline as deployed in a real-world business analytics system.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Data Source ERP CRM Web] --> B[Data Ingestion Layer]
    B --> C[Data Quality Checks Missing Outliers Range]
    C --> D[Exploratory Data Analysis]
    D --> E[Diagnostic Tests Skewness Kurtosis Normality]
    E --> F{Transformation Required?}
    F --> G[No Transformation Apply Z-Score for ML]
    F --> H[Yes Select Transformation]
    H --> I[Mathematical Transformations Log Sqrt Power Box-Cox]
    H --> J[Scaling Transformations Min-Max Robust Z-Score]
    I --> K[Validate Post Transformation]
    J --> K
    G --> K
    K --> L[Statistical Modeling or Machine Learning]
    L --> M[Business Insights and Decision Support]
```

> [!TIP]
> This architecture is the **gold-standard pipeline** used in industry. The "Validate Post Transformation" block is non-negotiable — you must always re-check skewness, normality, and model assumptions after applying any transformation.

---

## 4.3 Conceptual Schematic: Effect of Transformation on Distribution Shape

The following Mermaid diagram conceptually represents how different transformations reshape a right-skewed distribution.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Original Right Skewed X] --> B[Apply Log Transformation Y = log X]
    A --> C[Apply Square Root Y = sqrt X]
    A --> D[Apply Reciprocal Y = 1 divided by X]
    A --> E[Apply Z-Score Z = X minus mu divided by sigma]
    B --> F[Approximately Symmetric Normal-like]
    C --> G[Mildly Symmetric]
    D --> H[Left Skewed Reversed]
    E --> I[Same Shape Different Scale]
```

| Transformation | Original Shape | Transformed Shape |
|---|---|---|
| Log | Right-skewed | Approximately symmetric (normal-like) |
| Square Root | Right-skewed | Mildly right-skewed (less extreme) |
| Reciprocal | Right-skewed | Left-skewed (reversed) |
| Z-Score | Any shape | **Same shape**, different scale |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Z-score transformation is unique: it **never changes the shape** of the distribution. It only changes the location (mean) and scale (variance). This is a key exam-point.
