# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define "Transformation of Variables" in the context of business analytics. List any four commonly used transformations.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** Transformation of variables is a statistical technique in which a raw variable $X$ is converted into a new variable $Y = g(X)$ through a mathematical function, in order to satisfy statistical assumptions, improve model performance, or enhance interpretability.

**Four commonly used transformations are:**

1. **Logarithmic Transformation** — $Y = \log(X)$ for right-skewed data.
2. **Z-Score Standardization** — $Z = (X - \mu)/\sigma$ for ML preprocessing.
3. **Min-Max Normalization** — $X^* = (X - X_{\min})/(X_{\max} - X_{\min})$ for bounded scaling.
4. **Box-Cox Transformation** — $Y^{(\lambda)} = (X^{\lambda} - 1)/\lambda$ for optimal normality.

> [!NOTE]
> Marks Distribution (3 marks): 1 mark for definition + 1 mark for purpose + 1 mark for listing four transformations with brief descriptions.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: What is the Jacobian in the change-of-variables formula? Why is the absolute value taken?**

**Model Answer:**

> The **Jacobian** in the change-of-variables formula is the factor $\left| \frac{dx}{dy} \right|$, which is the absolute value of the derivative of the inverse transformation with respect to the new variable $y$.

> The **absolute value** is taken to ensure that the resulting PDF $f_Y(y)$ remains **non-negative**, regardless of whether the original transformation $g$ is increasing or decreasing. Since probabilities and densities cannot be negative, the absolute value is mathematically necessary.

$$f_Y(y) = f_X\bigl(g^{-1}(y)\bigr) \cdot \left| \frac{dx}{dy} \right|$$

> Marks Distribution (3 marks): 1 mark for defining Jacobian + 1 mark for writing the formula + 1 mark for explaining the absolute value.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme regulations, Part B questions carry **14 marks** with internal choice. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**. Students must answer ONE full question (both sub-parts).

---

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the concept of the Box-Cox transformation in detail. Derive the special case when $\lambda = 0$ and show that it reduces to a natural logarithmic transformation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The monthly electricity bills (in ₹) for 8 households in a Kerala locality are: 1200, 1500, 1800, 2100, 2500, 3000, 4000, 8500. Apply the log transformation to this data and compute the mean, variance, and skewness of the original and transformed data. Comment on the effectiveness of the transformation. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(a):

**Step 1: Definition of Box-Cox Transformation.**

The Box-Cox transformation is a generalized power transformation family defined as:

$$Y^{(\lambda)} = \begin{cases} \dfrac{X^{\lambda} - 1}{\lambda} & \text{if } \lambda \neq 0 \\[10pt] \ln(X) & \text{if } \lambda = 0 \end{cases}$$

where $X > 0$ is the original variable and $\lambda$ is the transformation parameter estimated by maximum likelihood.

**Step 2: Deriving the $\lambda = 0$ case using L'Hôpital's Rule.**

When $\lambda \to 0$, we need to evaluate $\lim_{\lambda \to 0} \frac{X^{\lambda} - 1}{\lambda}$.

This is an indeterminate form $\frac{0}{0}$. Apply L'Hôpital's Rule by differentiating the numerator and denominator with respect to $\lambda$:

$$\lim_{\lambda \to 0} \frac{X^{\lambda} - 1}{\lambda} = \lim_{\lambda \to 0} \frac{\frac{d}{d\lambda}(X^{\lambda} - 1)}{\frac{d}{d\lambda}(\lambda)} = \lim_{\lambda \to 0} \frac{X^{\lambda} \cdot \ln(X)}{1}$$

**Step 3: Evaluate the limit at $\lambda = 0$.**

Since $X^0 = 1$:

$$\lim_{\lambda \to 0} \frac{X^{\lambda} \cdot \ln(X)}{1} = 1 \cdot \ln(X) = \ln(X)$$

**Step 4: Conclude.**

$$\boxed{\lim_{\lambda \to 0} Y^{(\lambda)} = \ln(X)}$$

Hence, the Box-Cox transformation with $\lambda = 0$ reduces to the **natural logarithmic transformation**.

> [!NOTE]
> Marks Distribution (7 marks): 2 marks for Box-Cox definition + 2 marks for applying L'Hôpital's Rule + 2 marks for limit evaluation + 1 mark for final conclusion.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(b):

**Step 1: Organize the data and compute the log-transformed values.**

Let $Y_i = \ln(X_i)$.

| $X_i$ | $Y_i = \ln(X_i)$ |
|---|---|
| 1200 | 7.0901 |
| 1500 | 7.3132 |
| 1800 | 7.4955 |
| 2100 | 7.6497 |
| 2500 | 7.8240 |
| 3000 | 8.0064 |
| 4000 | 8.2941 |
| 8500 | 9.0478 |

**Step 2: Compute the mean of original data $X$.**

$$\bar{X} = \frac{1200 + 1500 + 1800 + 2100 + 2500 + 3000 + 4000 + 8500}{8} = \frac{24600}{8} = 3075$$

**Step 3: Compute the variance of $X$.**

$$\sigma^2_X = \frac{1}{8}\sum_{i=1}^{8}(X_i - 3075)^2$$

Computing each squared deviation:

$$\sum (X_i - 3075)^2 = (-1875)^2 + (-1575)^2 + (-1275)^2 + (-975)^2 + (-575)^2 + (-75)^2 + (925)^2 + (5425)^2$$

$$= 3515625 + 2480625 + 1625625 + 950625 + 330625 + 5625 + 855625 + 29430625$$

$$= 39345000$$

$$\sigma^2_X = \frac{39345000}{8} = 4918125$$

$$\sigma_X \approx 2217.7$$

**Step 4: Compute skewness of $X$.**

$$\gamma_1(X) = \frac{1}{n \cdot \sigma^3}\sum_{i=1}^{8}(X_i - \bar{X})^3$$

$$\sum (X_i - 3075)^3 = (-1875)^3 + (-1575)^3 + (-1275)^3 + (-975)^3 + (-575)^3 + (-75)^3 + (925)^3 + (5425)^3$$

$$= -6.59 \times 10^9 + (-3.91 \times 10^9) + (-2.07 \times 10^9) + (-9.27 \times 10^8) + (-1.90 \times 10^8) + (-4.22 \times 10^5) + 7.91 \times 10^8 + 1.60 \times 10^{11}$$

$$\approx 1.53 \times 10^{11}$$

$$\gamma_1(X) = \frac{1.53 \times 10^{11}}{8 \times (2217.7)^3} \approx \frac{1.53 \times 10^{11}}{8 \times 1.09 \times 10^{10}} \approx 1.76$$

**Step 5: Compute statistics of the log-transformed data $Y$.**

$$\bar{Y} = \frac{7.0901 + 7.3132 + 7.4955 + 7.6497 + 7.8240 + 8.0064 + 8.2941 + 9.0478}{8} = \frac{62.7208}{8} = 7.8401$$

$$\sigma^2_Y \approx 0.3785, \quad \sigma_Y \approx 0.6152$$

$$\gamma_1(Y) \approx 0.31$$

**Step 6: Comment on the effectiveness.**

> The original data $X$ had a skewness of approximately **1.76** (highly right-skewed, driven by the outlier value 8500). After the log transformation, the skewness dropped to approximately **0.31** (mildly right-skewed, much closer to zero). The log transformation is therefore **highly effective** for this dataset, dramatically reducing skewness and bringing the distribution closer to normality.

> [!NOTE]
> Marks Distribution (7 marks): 2 marks for computing original statistics + 2 marks for computing transformed statistics + 2 marks for skewness comparison + 1 mark for the comment on effectiveness.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the change-of-variables formula for probability density functions. Derive the formula and state the conditions under which it is valid. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A logistics company records the delivery times (in hours) for a sample of 6 shipments: 2, 3, 5, 8, 12, 20. Apply the Z-score standardization and the min-max normalization to this data. Compare the two transformations. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(a):

**Step 1: State the problem setup.**

Let $X$ be a continuous random variable with PDF $f_X(x)$ and CDF $F_X(x)$. Let $Y = g(X)$ be a transformation. We want to find $f_Y(y)$.

**Step 2: State the conditions for validity.**

The change-of-variables formula is valid when:

1. $g$ is a **monotonic** function (strictly increasing or strictly decreasing) on the support of $X$.
2. $g$ is **continuously differentiable** (i.e., $g'(x) \neq 0$ everywhere on the support).
3. The inverse $g^{-1}$ exists and is also differentiable.

**Step 3: Derive the formula using the CDF approach.**

$$F_Y(y) = P(Y \leq y) = P(g(X) \leq y)$$

**Case 1: $g$ is strictly increasing.**

$$F_Y(y) = P(X \leq g^{-1}(y)) = F_X(g^{-1}(y))$$

Differentiating with respect to $y$:

$$f_Y(y) = f_X(g^{-1}(y)) \cdot \frac{d}{dy}g^{-1}(y)$$

**Case 2: $g$ is strictly decreasing.**

$$F_Y(y) = P(X \geq g^{-1}(y)) = 1 - F_X(g^{-1}(y))$$

Differentiating:

$$f_Y(y) = -f_X(g^{-1}(y)) \cdot \frac{d}{dy}g^{-1}(y)$$

**Step 4: Unify both cases using the absolute value.**

$$\boxed{f_Y(y) = f_X(g^{-1}(y)) \cdot \left| \frac{d}{dy}g^{-1}(y) \right|}$$

**Step 5: Interpretation.**

> The factor $f_X(g^{-1}(y))$ represents the density of $X$ at the inverse-transformed point, while the Jacobian $\left| \frac{d}{dy}g^{-1}(y) \right|$ accounts for the "stretching" or "compressing" of probability mass caused by the transformation.

> [!NOTE]
> Marks Distribution (7 marks): 2 marks for stating conditions + 3 marks for the derivation (CDF approach with both cases) + 1 mark for the unified formula + 1 mark for interpretation.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(b):

**Step 1: Organize the data.**

Delivery times: $X = [2, 3, 5, 8, 12, 20]$, $n = 6$.

**Step 2: Z-score Standardization.**

Compute the mean:

$$\bar{X} = \frac{2 + 3 + 5 + 8 + 12 + 20}{6} = \frac{50}{6} \approx 8.333$$

Compute the variance:

$$\sigma^2 = \frac{1}{6}\left[(2-8.333)^2 + (3-8.333)^2 + (5-8.333)^2 + (8-8.333)^2 + (12-8.333)^2 + (20-8.333)^2\right]$$

$$= \frac{1}{6}[40.111 + 28.444 + 11.111 + 0.111 + 13.444 + 136.111] = \frac{229.333}{6} \approx 38.222$$

$$\sigma \approx 6.183$$

Compute $Z_i = (X_i - 8.333)/6.183$:

| $X_i$ | $Z_i$ |
|---|---|
| 2 | $-1.023$ |
| 3 | $-0.862$ |
| 5 | $-0.539$ |
| 8 | $-0.054$ |
| 12 | $0.593$ |
| 20 | $1.886$ |

**Step 3: Min-Max Normalization.**

$X_{\min} = 2$, $X_{\max} = 20$, range $= 18$.

Compute $X^*_i = (X_i - 2)/18$:

| $X_i$ | $X^*_i$ |
|---|---|
| 2 | $0.000$ |
| 3 | $0.056$ |
| 5 | $0.167$ |
| 8 | $0.333$ |
| 12 | $0.556$ |
| 20 | $1.000$ |

**Step 4: Comparison.**

| Property | Z-Score | Min-Max |
|---|---|---|
| Range | $(-\infty, +\infty)$ theoretically | Exactly $[0, 1]$ |
| Mean | Exactly $0$ | Approximately $0.351$ |
| Standard Deviation | Exactly $1$ | Approximately $0.351$ |
| Shape | Unchanged | Unchanged |
| Sensitivity to Outliers | Moderate (uses mean & SD) | **High** (uses min & max) |
| Best Use Case | ML algorithms assuming Gaussian | Neural networks, image processing |

> The Z-score standardization is **more robust to outliers** in this dataset because it uses the mean and standard deviation, whereas min-max normalization is heavily influenced by the extreme value $X = 20$, which compresses the remaining five values into a very small sub-range.

> [!NOTE]
> Marks Distribution (7 marks): 2 marks for Z-score computation + 2 marks for min-max computation + 2 marks for comparison table + 1 mark for concluding remark.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Examinations:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the absolute value in the Jacobian** — Students often write $f_Y(y) = f_X(g^{-1}(y)) \cdot \frac{dx}{dy}$ without the absolute value. This leads to negative PDF values for decreasing transformations, which is mathematically invalid. **Always use $\left| \frac{dx}{dy} \right|$.**
>
> 2. **Confusing the derivative direction** — The Jacobian is $\frac{dx}{dy}$ (derivative of the *inverse* transformation), NOT $\frac{dy}{dx}$ (derivative of the *forward* transformation). Mixing these up is a frequent error.
>
> 3. **Applying log to zero or negative values** — $\log(0) = -\infty$ and $\log(\text{negative})$ is undefined. Always use $Y = \log(X + 1)$ or $Y = \log(X + c)$ when zeros are present.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to validate after transformation** — Examiners expect you to **verify** the transformation worked (e.g., compute new skewness, plot histogram, perform normality test). Simply applying the transformation without validation loses 1–2 marks.
>
> 5. **Confusing standardization with normalization** — Z-score standardization yields mean = 0, SD = 1. Min-max normalization yields range = $[0, 1]$. These are **not the same**; examiners specifically test this distinction.
>
> 6. **In Box-Cox derivations, missing L'Hôpital's Rule** — When proving the $\lambda = 0$ case, you **must** explicitly show the L'Hôpital's Rule application step. Skipping it costs 2 marks.
>
> 7. **Misinterpreting the shape effect** — Students often incorrectly claim that standardization "normalizes" the data. It does NOT change the shape; only mathematical transformations (log, sqrt, Box-Cox) do that.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Transformation of Variables:**

- **Definition:** Transformation converts $X$ to $Y = g(X)$ for analysis purposes.
- **Linear transformation** $Y = aX + b$: preserves shape; changes mean to $a\mu + b$ and variance to $a^2\sigma^2$.
- **Z-score** $Z = (X-\mu)/\sigma$: mean = 0, variance = 1, **shape unchanged**.
- **Min-max**: rescales to $[0, 1]$, **sensitive to outliers**.
- **Robust scaling**: uses median and IQR, **outlier-resistant**.
- **Log transform**: $Y = \log(X)$ for $X > 0$; for zeros use $\log(X+1)$.
- **Square root**: $Y = \sqrt{X}$ for mildly skewed count data.
- **Box-Cox**: $Y^{(\lambda)} = (X^{\lambda}-1)/\lambda$; $\lambda = 0$ gives $\ln(X)$ via L'Hôpital.
- **Change-of-variables formula**: $f_Y(y) = f_X(g^{-1}(y)) \cdot \left| \frac{dx}{dy} \right|$.
- **Conditions**: $g$ must be monotonic, differentiable, and invertible.
- **Preserved by ALL monotonic transformations**: rank order, relative ordering, Spearman's correlation.
- **NOT preserved**: mean, variance, skewness, kurtosis, Pearson's correlation (unless linear).
- **Key exam tip**: always state the **purpose** of the transformation (normalization, variance stabilization, linearization, or scaling).
- **Always validate** post-transformation using skewness check, histogram, Q-Q plot, or Shapiro-Wilk test.
- **In regression**: $\log(Y)$ fixes heteroscedasticity; $\log(X)$ linearizes exponential relationships; $\log-\log$ models estimate elasticities.
- **In ML**: standardize features before applying PCA, KNN, SVM, or gradient descent; use min-max for neural networks.
- **In business**: common to log-transform revenue, income, and count data; Z-score customer metrics for comparison.
- **Formula to memorize cold**: $\mu_{aX+b} = a\mu_X + b$; $\sigma^2_{aX+b} = a^2\sigma^2_X$; $Z = (X-\bar{X})/s$.
- **Jacobian pitfall**: always use $\left| \frac{dx}{dy} \right|$, never $\frac{dx}{dy}$ alone.
- **Box-Cox pitfall**: $\lambda = 0$ case requires L'Hôpital's Rule to derive $\ln(X)$.

> [!TIP]
> **One-line memory aid:** *"Linear transformations shift and stretch; log transformations tame the giants; Z-scores speak a common language; and Box-Cox finds the golden ratio of normality."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Joining and Blending Data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT522 - BUSINESS ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Joining and Blending Data

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Joining Data**: A row-level operation in Business Analytics that horizontally combines two or more relational tables within a **single data source** by matching records based on one or more common key fields (join keys). The result is a single, denormalized logical table that contains all columns from both source tables.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Data Blending**: An aggregation-level operation in Business Analytics that vertically combines data from **two or more heterogeneous data sources** (e.g., Excel, SQL Server, Google Sheets, Web Connector) using a shared dimension field called the **linking field**. Blending is executed at the aggregate (summary) level rather than at the row level, and it requires the designation of a **Primary** and a **Secondary** data source.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 1 — Joining Data (The Marriage Register)**
> Imagine you have two registers in a bank branch:
> - **Register A (Customers)**: Columns — `Customer_ID`, `Customer_Name`, `City`
> - **Register B (Loans)**: Columns — `Loan_ID`, `Customer_ID`, `Loan_Amount`
>
> Both registers share a common column: **`Customer_ID`**. If you "Join" them, you physically staple the pages together so every customer's loan amount is printed right next to their name. The combined register now has every customer and every loan detail lined up row-by-row. This is **Joining**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 2 — Blending Data (The Two Department Reports)**
> Now imagine the **Sales Department** sends you a CSV file from their Excel sheet showing `Region` and `Total Sales`. The **HR Department** sends a totally separate SQL database showing `Region` and `Employee Headcount`. The systems are completely different, so you cannot physically staple the rows. Instead, you keep them as **two separate reports** on your desk, but you draw a dotted line connecting the `Region` column of one to the `Region` column of the other. Whenever a chart needs to show a metric, it first asks the primary report for its value, then looks up the linked secondary report by `Region`, and adds the headcount as an additional measure. This dotted-line, cross-report connection is **Blending**.

---

## 1.3 Core Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Join Key** | The common column(s) used to match rows between two tables during a join. |
| **Inner Join** | Returns only rows where the join key exists in **both** tables. |
| **Left Join** | Returns **all** rows from the left table; unmatched right rows appear as `NULL`. |
| **Right Join** | Returns **all** rows from the right table; unmatched left rows appear as `NULL`. |
| **Full Outer Join** | Returns **all** rows from both tables; unmatched values appear as `NULL`. |
| **Primary Data Source** | The "left" or anchor source in a blend; defines the view's granularity. |
| **Secondary Data Source** | The "right" or supplementary source queried during a blend. |
| **Linking Field** | The shared dimension used to align the primary and secondary sources. |
| **Cross-Database Join** | A join executed across two different database platforms (e.g., SQL ↔ Excel). |
| **Null Indicator** | A boolean flag (`True`/`False`) marking whether the join produced a matching row. |

---

## 1.4 Why This Topic is Critical for Business Analytics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **80%** of real-world KTU industrial case studies and analytics interviews, the dataset is **not** a single clean flat file. Analysts routinely pull from **ERP systems, CRM tools, Web APIs, and spreadsheets**. The choice between *Joining* and *Blending* directly impacts report performance, data freshness, accuracy of totals, and whether an aggregation is computed **before** or **after** the merge. Misusing one for the other is the **#1 source of incorrect KPIs** in dashboards.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Mechanics — How Joining Works

Joining follows strict relational algebra. Given two tables $A$ and $B$ with a common join key $K$:

### Step 1 — Identify Candidate Keys
Determine the column(s) that uniquely (or at minimum, non-uniquely) identify records in both tables.

### Step 2 — Select the Join Cardinality
Choose from `1:1`, `1:N`, `N:1`, or `N:M` based on the multiplicity of $K$ in each table.

### Step 3 — Apply the Predicate Filter
A join is mathematically equivalent to a **Cartesian Product followed by a selection predicate**:

$$A \bowtie B = \sigma_{(A.K = B.K)}(\,A \times B\,)$$

Where:
- $\times$ is the Cartesian Product.
- $\sigma$ is the selection operator (the join predicate).

### Step 4 — Execute Row-Level Matching
For every row in $A$, scan $B$ for matching $K$ values. Cost complexity in worst case is:

$$T(n) = \mathcal{O}(n_A \times n_B)$$

Indexed lookups reduce this to $\mathcal{O}(n_A \log n_B)$ (Hash Join / Sort-Merge Join).

### Step 5 — Output the Result Set
The resulting table has:
- **Rows** = matched or unmatched records per join type.
- **Columns** = union of all columns from $A$ and $B$ (less duplicate join keys).

---

## 2.2 Operational Mechanics — How Blending Works

Blending is fundamentally different. It is **NOT row-level**. It is a **two-pass aggregate-then-merge** operation.

### Step 1 — Designate the Primary Source
The Primary Source determines the **granularity (level of detail)** of the entire visualization.

### Step 2 — Designate the Secondary Source(s)
These are referenced lazily — only when a view requests a field from them.

### Step 3 — Identify the Linking Field(s)
A dimension present in both sources that acts as the visual "join key".

### Step 4 — Aggregate the Primary Independently
All measures from the Primary are aggregated at the Primary's grain.

### Step 5 — Aggregate the Secondary Independently
Measures from the Secondary are aggregated at the Secondary's grain (independently).

### Step 6 — Stitch via the Linking Field
The two aggregated tables are then stitched at the granularity of the Primary, using the linking field to **look up** secondary values. This introduces a known phenomenon:

> [!WARNING]
> The **Primary → Secondary relationship** controls whether secondary `NULL` values are dropped or kept. By default in Tableau, setting the relationship to a blank/asterisk (`*`) on the Primary side behaves like a **LEFT JOIN** at the aggregate level.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Mathematical Representation | Operation Type | Cardinality Impact |
|---------|-----------------------------|---------------|--------------------|
| **Inner Join** | $A \bowtie_{K} B$ | Row-level intersection | Only matching rows retained |
| **Left Join** | $(A \bowtie_{K} B) \cup (A - \pi_K(B))$ | Row-level union with left priority | All $A$ rows + matched $B$ rows |
| **Right Join** | $(A \bowtie_{K} B) \cup (B - \pi_K(A))$ | Row-level union with right priority | All $B$ rows + matched $A$ rows |
| **Full Outer Join** | $A \cup B$ (with NULL fill) | Row-level symmetric union | All rows from both sides |
| **Cross Join** | $A \times B$ | Cartesian product | $\|A\| \times \|B\|$ rows |
| **Blend (Primary → Secondary)** | $\text{Agg}(A_K) \ltimes \text{Agg}(B_K)$ | Aggregate-level stitching | Primary's grain dominates |
| **Null Card Ratio** | $\rho = \dfrac{\| \text{Unmatched} \|}{\| A \|}$ | Diagnostic metric | Higher $\rho$ → weaker join quality |
| **Join Selectivity** | $s = \dfrac{\| A \bowtie B \|}{\| A \|}$ | Efficiency metric | $s \to 1$ (efficient), $s \to 0$ (lossy) |
| **Blend Cardinality** | $\| \text{Output} \| = \| \pi_K(A) \|$ | Aggregate inheritance | Always equals Primary distinct $K$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Safety Note:** The vertical pipe symbol `|` has been intentionally replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` in formulas (e.g., $\vert A \vert$) so that markdown table syntax is **never** broken.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application of Joining | Application of Blending |
|----------|------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Retail Banking** | Joining Customer Master with Transaction Logs in a single Oracle DB. | Blending CRM (Salesforce) with legacy Mainframe AS/400 data. |
| **Healthcare Analytics** | Joining Patient records with Lab Results in a hospital data warehouse. | Blending Electronic Health Records (EHR) with external insurance claim APIs. |
| **E-Commerce** | Joining Orders with Shipment Tracking tables in Snowflake. | Blending internal Clickstream (BigQuery) with external Google Trends data. |
| **Manufacturing IoT** | Joining Sensor metadata with telemetry readings in PostgreSQL. | Blending on-premise MES data with cloud-based Power BI Streaming datasets. |
| **Telecom Churn** | Joining CDR (Call Detail Records) with Subscriber Master. | Blending billing system data with social media sentiment scores. |

---

## 2.5 Decision Framework: When to Join vs. Blend

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use JOIN when:**
> 1. Data resides in the **same database / single source**.
> 2. You require **row-level** granularity in the output.
> 3. You need **all columns** simultaneously accessible in one row.
> 4. Performance is critical — joins are SQL-optimized.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use BLEND when:**
> 1. Data resides in **physically separate sources** (different platforms).
> 2. You only need **summary-level** aggregates.
> 3. The granularity of the two sources is **fundamentally different** (e.g., Daily vs. Monthly).
> 4. The data sources have **conflicting field names** that cannot be aliased.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Joining Two Tables (Exhaustive Derivation)

**Given Tables:**

| Customer_ID (K) | Customer_Name | City |
|:--:|:--|:--|
| C01 | Arun | Kochi |
| C02 | Beena | Trivandrum |
| C03 | Chitra | Calicut |
| C04 | Deepak | Kochi |

| Customer_ID (K) | Loan_Amount | Loan_Type |
|:--:|:--:|:--|
| C01 | 500000 | Home |
| C02 | 200000 | Personal |
| C03 | 150000 | Vehicle |
| C05 | 800000 | Home |

### Derivation 1 — Inner Join Result

Apply the selection predicate $A.K = B.K$:

$$
\text{Inner Join} = \sigma_{A.K = B.K}(A \times B)
$$

For each row of $A$, we retain only those rows in $B$ where $K$ matches:

- $C01$ (Arun) matches $C01$ (Home Loan) → **Keep**
- $C02$ (Beena) matches $C02$ (Personal Loan) → **Keep**
- $C03$ (Chitra) matches $C03$ (Vehicle Loan) → **Keep**
- $C04$ (Deepak) has **no match** in $B$ → **Discard**

**Resulting Table:**

| Customer_ID | Customer_Name | City | Loan_Amount | Loan_Type |
|:--:|:--|:--|:--:|:--|
| C01 | Arun | Kochi | 500000 | Home |
| C02 | Beena | Trivandrum | 200000 | Personal |
| C03 | Chitra | Calicut | 150000 | Vehicle |

**Row Count Verification:**
$$
\vert A \bowtie_{inner} B \vert = 3
$$

### Derivation 2 — Left Join Result

Keep **all** rows of $A$; pad missing $B$ values with `NULL`:

- $C01$ → Match found → Loan_Amount = 500000
- $C02$ → Match found → Loan_Amount = 200000
- $C03$ → Match found → Loan_Amount = 150000
- $C04$ → No match → Loan_Amount = NULL, Loan_Type = NULL

**Resulting Table:**

| Customer_ID | Customer_Name | City | Loan_Amount | Loan_Type |
|:--:|:--|:--|:--:|:--|
| C01 | Arun | Kochi | 500000 | Home |
| C02 | Beena | Trivandrum | 200000 | Personal |
| C03 | Chitra | Calicut | 150000 | Vehicle |
| C04 | Deepak | Kochi | NULL | NULL |

**Row Count Verification:**
$$
\vert A \bowtie_{left} B \vert = \vert A \vert = 4
$$

### Derivation 3 — Full Outer Join Result

Retain all rows from both tables; fill gaps with `NULL`:

- All 4 rows of $A$ are retained.
- Row $C05$ from $B$ (which is absent in $A$) is also retained.

**Resulting Table:**

| Customer_ID | Customer_Name | City | Loan_Amount | Loan_Type |
|:--:|:--|:--|:--:|:--|
| C01 | Arun | Kochi | 500000 | Home |
| C02 | Beena | Trivandrum | 200000 | Personal |
| C03 | Chitra | Calicut | 150000 | Vehicle |
| C04 | Deepak | Kochi | NULL | NULL |
| C05 | NULL | NULL | 800000 | Home |

**Row Count Verification:**
$$
\vert A \bowtie_{full} B \vert = \vert A \vert + \vert B \vert - \vert A \bowtie_{inner} B \vert = 4 + 4 - 3 = 5
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Blending Data (Exhaustive Derivation)

**Scenario:** A retail analytics dashboard needs to compare **Total Sales** (from `Excel_2024.xlsx`) with **Marketing Spend** (from `Google_Sheets_Marketing`).

| Region (Linking Field K) | Total_Sales (Primary) |
|:--|:--|
| Kochi | 1200000 |
| Trivandrum | 950000 |
| Calicut | 700000 |
| Kochi | 600000 |

**Primary Source (`Excel_2024.xlsx`) at the Region level (after aggregation):**

| Region | SUM(Sales) |
|:--|:--|
| Kochi | 1800000 |
| Trivandrum | 950000 |
| Calicut | 700000 |

**Secondary Source (`Google_Sheets_Marketing`) at the Region level (after aggregation):**

| Region | SUM(Marketing_Spend) |
|:--|:--|
| Kochi | 250000 |
| Trivandrum | 150000 |
| Calicut | 180000 |
| Bengaluru | 90000 |

### Step-by-Step Blend Execution

**Step 1:** Primary aggregation performed first. Granularity = `Region`.

$$
\text{Primary}_{\text{agg}} = \text{GROUP BY Region, SUM(Sales)}
$$

**Step 2:** Secondary aggregation performed independently.

$$
\text{Secondary}_{\text{agg}} = \text{GROUP BY Region, SUM(Marketing\_Spend)}
$$

**Step 3:** Stitching via the Linking Field `Region`. The Primary drives the output schema.

**Step 4:** Relationship is set as `Region = Region` with default `*` (Primary side open).

**Final Blended Output:**

| Region | SUM(Sales) [Primary] | SUM(Marketing_Spend) [Secondary Lookup] |
|:--|:--:|:--:|
| Kochi | 1800000 | 250000 |
| Trivandrum | 950000 | 150000 |
| Calicut | 700000 | 180000 |

**Note that `Bengaluru`** (present only in Secondary) **does NOT appear** in the output because the Primary drives the granularity.

$$
\vert \text{Blend Output} \vert = \vert \pi_{Region}(\text{Primary}) \vert = 3
$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Joining | Blending |
|-----------|---------|----------|
| **Data Sources** | Single source (typically) | Two or more heterogeneous sources |
| **Granularity** | Row-level | Aggregate-level |
| **Operation Timing** | Pre-aggregation | Post-aggregation |
| **Performance** | Highly optimized (SQL engine) | Slower (separate queries per source) |
| **NULL Handling** | Inner/Left/Right/Full | Primary → Secondary relationship flags |
| **Output Cardinality** | Depends on join type | Always equals distinct Primary keys |
| **Custom SQL Support** | Yes | No |
| **Field-Level Aggregation** | Yes (any time) | Aggregated independently per source |
| **Cross-Database** | Yes (e.g., Oracle ↔ SQL Server via DB Link) | Native and platform-agnostic |
| **KPI Accuracy Risk** | Low (if join keys are correct) | High (if granularity mismatch exists) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Join Type Venn Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeTblA[Table A - Customers]
    nodeTblB[Table B - Loans]
    nodeInner[Inner Join - Only Matching]
    nodeLeft[Left Join - All A plus Matches]
    nodeRight[Right Join - All B plus Matches]
    nodeFull[Full Outer Join - All from both]
    
    nodeTblA --- nodeInner
    nodeTblB --- nodeInner
    nodeTblA --- nodeLeft
    nodeTblB --- nodeRight
    nodeTblA --- nodeFull
    nodeTblB --- nodeFull
```

## 4.2 Join Execution Flow Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeStart([Two Tables A and B]) --> nodeStep1[Step 1: Identify Join Key K]
    nodeStep1 --> nodeStep2[Step 2: Choose Join Type]
    nodeStep2 --> nodeStep3{Join Type Selected?}
    nodeStep3 -->|Inner| nodeInner[Apply A.K equals B.K]
    nodeStep3 -->|Left| nodeLeft[Keep all A rows]
    nodeStep3 -->|Right| nodeRight[Keep all B rows]
    nodeStep3 -->|Full Outer| nodeFull[Keep all rows both sides]
    nodeInner --> nodeStep4[Step 3: Row-Level Matching]
    nodeLeft --> nodeStep4
    nodeRight --> nodeStep4
    nodeFull --> nodeStep4
    nodeStep4 --> nodeStep5[Step 4: Pad NULL values]
    nodeStep5 --> nodeOutput([Unified Logical Table Output])
```

## 4.3 Data Blending Two-Pass Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodePrim[Primary Data Source Excel] --> nodeAgg1[Step A Aggregate by Region]
    nodeSec[Secondary Data Source Google Sheets] --> nodeAgg2[Step B Aggregate Independently]
    nodeAgg1 --> nodeStitch[Step C Stitch via Linking Field Region]
    nodeAgg2 --> nodeStitch
    nodeStitch --> nodeVis([Final Visualization Primary Grain])
    nodePrim --> nodePrimFlag[Designated as Primary]
    nodeSec --> nodeSecFlag[Designated as Secondary]
    nodePrimFlag --> nodeAgg1
    nodeSecFlag --> nodeAgg2
```

## 4.4 Join vs Blend Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeStart([Need to Combine Data]) --> nodeQ1{Same Data Source?}
    nodeQ1 -->|Yes| nodeJoin[Use Join]
    nodeQ1 -->|No| nodeQ2{Requires Row-Level Detail?}
    nodeQ2 -->|Yes| nodeJoin
    nodeQ2 -->|No| nodeBlend[Use Blend]
    nodeQ1 -->|Yes| nodeQ3{Different Granularities?}
    nodeQ3 -->|Yes| nodeBlend
    nodeQ3 -->|No| nodeJoin
    nodeJoin --> nodeOut([Unified Logical Table])
    nodeBlend --> nodeOut
```

## 4.5 Blending Relationship Linking Mechanism

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeP[Primary Table] --> nodeK1[Linking Field Region]
    nodeS[Secondary Table] --> nodeK2[Linking Field Region]
    nodeK1 --> nodeLink[Dotted Linking Line]
    nodeK2 --> nodeLink
    nodeLink --> nodeResult[Aggregated Stitched View]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Differentiate between Data Joining and Data Blending in Business Analytics tools.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Data Joining | Data Blending |
|-----------|--------------|---------------|
| **Source Requirement** | Works on tables within the **same** data source | Works on tables across **different** data sources |
| **Operation Level** | Performed at the **row level** | Performed at the **aggregate level** |
| **Performance** | Fast (uses native SQL engine) | Slower (issues separate queries) |
| **Use Case** | Combining normalized tables in a single DB | Combining heterogeneous files like Excel + SQL Server |

*(1 mark each for any three valid differentiating points.)*

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: List any four types of joins supported in Business Analytics tools with a one-line description of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **Inner Join** — Returns only the rows with matching join keys in both tables. *(1 mark)*
2. **Left Join** — Returns all rows from the left table and matching rows from the right; fills `NULL` for unmatched right-side records. *(1 mark)*
3. **Right Join** — Returns all rows from the right table and matching rows from the left; fills `NULL` for unmatched left-side records. *(1 mark - partially credited if 3rd listed)*
4. **Full Outer Join** — Returns all rows from both tables; fills `NULL` wherever a match is absent.

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Module Pattern: Each Part B question has internal choice. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Consider the following two tables `Orders` and `Customers` of an e-commerce company:**

**Orders Table:**

| Order_ID | Customer_ID | Amount |
|:--:|:--:|:--:|
| O101 | C01 | 4500 |
| O102 | C02 | 7200 |
| O103 | C03 | 3000 |
| O104 | C04 | 8900 |

**Customers Table:**

| Customer_ID | Name | City |
|:--:|:--|:--|
| C01 | Rahul | Kochi |
| C02 | Anjali | Calicut |
| C03 | Vivek | Trivandrum |
| C05 | Sneha | Kochi |

**Answer the following:**

### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Perform an Inner Join and a Left Outer Join on `Customer_ID`. Show the resulting tables and verify the row counts mathematically.**

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify the join key `Customer_ID`.

**Step 2:** Compute the Inner Join:

| Order_ID | Customer_ID | Amount | Name | City |
|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--|:--|
| O101 | C01 | 4500 | Rahul | Kochi |
| O102 | C02 | 7200 | Anjali | Calicut |
| O103 | C03 | 3000 | Vivek | Trivandrum |

$$
\vert \text{Orders} \bowtie_{inner} \text{Customers} \vert = 3
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the Left Outer Join (Orders as Left Table):

| Order_ID | Customer_ID | Amount | Name | City |
|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--|:--|
| O101 | C01 | 4500 | Rahul | Kochi |
| O102 | C02 | 7200 | Anjali | Calicut |
| O103 | C03 | 3000 | Vivek | Trivandrum |
| O104 | C04 | 8900 | NULL | NULL |

$$
\vert \text{Orders} \bowtie_{left} \text{Customers} \vert = \vert \text{Orders} \vert = 4
$$

### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Explain the concept of Data Blending with a suitable example. Why is the Primary data source important in blending?**

**Solution:**

**Definition (2 marks):** Data Blending is an aggregate-level technique for combining data from **two or more distinct data sources** that cannot be physically joined because they reside on different platforms (e.g., Excel + SQL Server).

**Mechanism (3 marks):**
- A **Primary Data Source** is designated first; it defines the granularity of the visualization.
- A **Secondary Data Source** is then linked via a common dimension (linking field).
- Aggregations are computed independently in each source before being stitched.
- The relationship flags control which records are displayed.

**Importance of Primary Source (2 marks):**
- It determines the **level of detail** of every chart.
- It drives the cardinality of the output (output rows = distinct values of the linking field in the Primary).
- Secondary-only records (e.g., a region present only in the Secondary source) are **dropped** unless explicitly handled.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: A retail analytics team has the following data:**

**Sales Source (Primary - Excel):**

| Region | Product | Sales |
|:--:|:--:|:--:|
| Kochi | A | 1000 |
| Kochi | B | 2000 |
| Calicut | A | 1500 |
| Trivandrum | C | 800 |

**Inventory Source (Secondary - SQL Server):**

| Region | Product | Stock |
|:--:|:--:|:--:|
| Kochi | A | 50 |
| Calicut | A | 30 |
| Calicut | B | 20 |

**Answer the following:**

### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Demonstrate the blending operation step-by-step using `Region` as the linking field. Show the final blended output table.**

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Aggregate the Primary (Sales) at Region level:**

| Region | SUM(Sales) |
|:--|:--:|
| Kochi | 3000 |
| Calicut | 1500 |
| Trivandrum | 800 |

**Step 2: Aggregate the Secondary (Inventory) at Region level:**

| Region | SUM(Stock) |
|:--|:--:|
| Kochi | 50 |
| Calicut | 50 |

**Step 3: Stitch via `Region` linking field (Primary drives granularity):**

| Region | SUM(Sales) [Primary] | SUM(Stock) [Secondary Lookup] |
|:--|:--:|:--:|
| Kochi | 3000 | 50 |
| Calicut | 1500 | 50 |
| Trivandrum | 800 | NULL |

**Step 4: Verification using cardinality formula:**

$$
\vert \text{Blend Output} \vert = \vert \pi_{Region}(\text{Primary}) \vert = 3
$$

Note that `Trivandrum` has no matching Inventory record, so its Stock is `NULL`.

### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Compare Join vs Blend across any seven parameters in a tabular form.**

**Model Answer Table (7 Marks — 1 per parameter):**

| Parameter | Join | Blend |
|-----------|------|-------|
| **Source** | Single source | Multiple sources |
| **Level** | Row-level | Aggregate-level |
| **Granularity Control** | Output can vary by join type | Always equals Primary's grain |
| **NULL Handling** | Per join type rules | Controlled by Primary → Secondary relationship |
| **Performance** | Fast (SQL engine) | Slower (multiple queries) |
| **Custom SQL** | Supported | Not supported |
| **Field Aggregation** | Pre-join or post-join | Independent per source |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Mistake #1 (Most Common):** Students often confuse **Blending** with a **Left Join**. They are NOT the same. Blending is aggregate-level; Left Join is row-level.
> - **Mistake #2:** Forgetting that the **Primary source defines the granularity** in a blend. Writing "all data appears in the blend" will cost 2 marks.
> - **Mistake #3:** Writing join types like "Inner" or "Outer" without specifying **outer** for Full Outer Join. Examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark.
> - **Mistake #4:** Mixing up which side is the Left table in a Left Join — always the first table listed.
> - **Mistake #5:** Writing `NULL` as `0` in joined tables. They are semantically different (`0` is a value; `NULL` indicates absence).

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Exam Day:**

- **Joining** = Row-level + Same source + SQL-optimized.
- **Blending** = Aggregate-level + Multiple sources + Granularity controlled by Primary.
- **Join Key** is the foundation of every join — choose it wisely (uniqueness, NULL handling).
- **Primary Source** in blending is the **boss** — it decides what appears in the visualization.
- **Secondary Source** is queried lazily — only when a Secondary field is dragged into the view.
- **Linking Field** must be present in both sources and must be a dimension (not a measure).
- **Inner Join** = strict intersection; **Left/Right Join** = preserves one side; **Full Outer Join** = preserves both.
- **Blend cardinality** always equals the distinct count of the linking field in the **Primary**.
- **Cross-Database Joins** are supported in modern BI tools via native connectors.
- **Default blend relationship** with `*` on the Primary side mimics a **Left Join** at the aggregate level.
- **Performance Tip:** Always prefer joins when both options are technically feasible — joins are 5–10× faster.
- **KTU Pattern:** Expect at least one Part A question on definition/difference and one Part B question involving table joins or blending scenarios.
- **Valuation Heuristic:** Examiners award 1 mark per correctly drawn row in join tables and 2 marks for the correct identification of granularity in blending.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tableau Worksheets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT522 - BUSINESS ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Tableau Worksheets**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

A **Tableau Worksheet** is the fundamental analytical canvas within the Tableau workspace where business users, analysts, and data scientists construct interactive data visualizations (also called *views* or *vizzes*). Each worksheet binds a data source to a visual structure defined by shelves (Columns, Rows, Pages, Filters, Marks) and produces a single, shareable chart or table.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for Business Analytics**, a worksheet acts as the *primary building block* for dashboards and stories. Every worksheet is a self-contained analytical unit that translates raw tabular data into decision-ready visual artifacts through drag-and-drop shelf operations, marks encoding, and computational fields.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** A Tableau Workbook (`.twb` or `.twbx`) can contain *multiple worksheets*, but each individual worksheet displays **only one primary visualization** at a time.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Workshop Workbench"

Imagine you walk into a carpenter's workshop.

- The **workshop room** is your **Tableau Workbook** (the entire file).
- Each **workbench** inside the room is a **Worksheet** — a dedicated, clean surface where you build *one* piece of furniture (one chart) at a time.
- The **wood (raw material)** is your **Data Source**.
- The **tools laid out on the bench** are the **Shelves and Marks cards** (Rows, Columns, Filters, Color, Size, Label, etc.).
- The **finished chair or table** sitting on the workbench is the **Visualization (View)**.

Just as a carpenter cannot glue two chairs together on the same bench (he would move to a second bench), a Tableau analyst cannot create two distinct chart types on one worksheet — he would use a **second worksheet** and later combine both into a **Dashboard**.

---

## 1.3 Why Worksheets Matter in Business Analytics

In real-world KTU-aligned business scenarios (retail, banking, healthcare, supply chain), worksheets are used to:

- Track **Key Performance Indicators (KPIs)** like sales, profit margin, churn.
- Perform **drill-down analysis** (Region → State → City → Store).
- Generate **trend reports** (Year-over-Year, Month-over-Month).
- Support **executive decision-making** with clean, self-service dashboards.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Convention in Tableau:**
> - **Dimensions** (categorical, blue pills) = *qualitative* attributes (Region, Product, Date).
> - **Measures** (quantitative, green pills) = *numerical* values (Sales, Profit, Quantity).
> - Aggregations default to **SUM**, but can be changed to **AVG, COUNT, MIN, MAX, MEDIAN**.

---

## 1.4 KTU Course Outcome Alignment

| Course Outcome (CO) | How Worksheets Address It |
|---|---|
| **CO3** – Apply visualization tools to business data | Worksheet is the core tool for building visuals |
| **CO4** – Interpret analytical outputs for decisions | Worksheets translate data into readable insight |
| **CO5** – Create dashboards for management reporting | Worksheets are the *atoms* that compose dashboards |
| **CO1** – Understand analytics fundamentals | Worksheet-based analysis demonstrates descriptive/diagnostic analytics |

---

## 1.5 Quick Glossary for KTU Exams

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Workbook** | The `.twb` file containing one or more worksheets |
| **Worksheet / Sheet** | A single analytical canvas producing one view |
| **View** | The rendered visualization on a worksheet |
| **Shelf** | A drop zone (Columns, Rows, Pages, Filters) that defines chart structure |
| **Marks Card** | Controls visual encoding (Color, Size, Shape, Label, Tooltip, Detail) |
| **Data Pane** | The left sidebar listing Dimensions and Measures |
| **Show Me** | A panel recommending chart types based on selected fields |
| **Tableau Public** | Free version of Tableau for academic/student use |
| **Tableau Desktop** | Licensed professional version |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Tableau Worksheet — The Six Pillars

Every Tableau worksheet is organized into **six functional zones**. Understanding these is *mandatory* for KTU exam questions on "describe the Tableau interface" or "explain worksheet components."

### Pillar 1: The Data Pane (Left Sidebar)
- Displays the connected **data source** as a list of fields.
- Fields are auto-classified into:
  - **Dimensions** — categorical fields (blue colour icon), shown as discrete text or date buckets.
  - **Measures** — numerical fields (green colour icon), shown as continuous aggregates.
- Right-clicking a field opens the menu for **rename, alias, create calculated field, change data type, default aggregation**.

### Pillar 2: The Shelves (Top Region)
Located just below the toolbar, shelves determine the *structure* of the view.

| Shelf | Function | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Columns** | Horizontal axis (X) | Drag `Region` here → horizontal bar chart |
| **Rows** | Vertical axis (Y) | Drag `SUM(Sales)` here → vertical bar chart |
| **Pages** | Creates a sequence of views (animation/iteration) | Drag `Year` here → animated year-by-year view |
| **Filters** | Restricts the data displayed in the view | Filter `Region = "South"` only |
| **Marks** | Dropdown shelf with Color, Size, Label, Detail, Tooltip, Shape | Drop `Category` on Color → coloured scatter |

### Pillar 3: The Marks Card (Left of Canvas)
- A square card with six mini-shelves for **visual encoding**.
- Determines *how* marks (data points) appear.
- **Detail** shelf adds granularity without changing visual form.
- **Tooltip** shelf customises hover-text shown to the user.

### Pillar 4: The Canvas / View Area (Centre)
- The white area where the visualization is rendered in real time.
- Supports drag, drop, swap, dual-axis combination, and reference line addition.

### Pillar 5: Show Me Panel (Top-Right)
- A 2×N grid of chart-type icons (bar, line, pie, scatter, map, treemap, etc.).
- Recommends valid chart types based on the fields currently selected.
- Clicking a chart icon auto-assigns fields to appropriate shelves.

### Pillar 6: Status Bar / Summary Card (Bottom)
- Shows row count, mark count, and tooltips on hover.

---

## 2.2 Marks Card — The Visual Encoding Engine

The **Marks card** is where the magic of *visual analytics* happens. The six sub-shelves each control a different visual channel:

| Sub-Shelf | Visual Channel | Typical Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Color** | Hue | Differentiate categories (e.g., Region in red/blue/green) |
| **Size** | Magnitude | Encode profit by circle area in scatter |
| **Label** | Text on marks | Show exact sales value on each bar |
| **Detail** | Granularity | Add Product Name to scatter (splits marks) |
| **Tooltip** | Hover information | Show "Profit Ratio = 12.3%" on hover |
| **Shape** | Glyph | Distinguish male/female in scatter plot |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The **mark type** dropdown (top of Marks card) defines the *primitive shape*: Bar, Line, Area, Square, Circle, Shape, Text, Pie, Gantt Bar, Polygon, Map. Changing this dropdown transforms the entire chart type instantly.

---

## 2.3 High-Yield Reference Sheet — Worksheet Operations

| Operation | Procedure | Marks Impact |
|---|---|---|
| **Create a bar chart** | Drag a Measure to Rows, a Dimension to Columns | Default mark = Bar |
| **Switch to line chart** | Click Marks card dropdown → choose "Line" | Mark type = Line |
| **Add colour** | Drag Dimension to Color shelf | Marks split by colour |
| **Sort ascending** | Click sort icon on axis (or right-click field → Sort) | Reorders marks |
| **Filter view** | Drag field to Filters shelf → set condition | Hides marks |
| **Add reference line** | Right-click axis → Add Reference Line | Adds horizontal/vertical line |
| **Create calculated field** | Right-click in Data Pane → Create Calculated Field | Adds new measure/dimension |
| **Create group** | Right-click Dimension → Create Group | Combines members |
| **Create set** | Right-click Dimension → Create Set | Defines custom subset |
| **Create parameter** | Right-click in Data Pane → Create Parameter | User-controlled input |
| **Swap axes** | Click swap arrow between Columns/Rows | Mirrors view |
| **Dual axis chart** | Drag second measure to right of canvas → "Dual Axis" | Combines two charts |
| **Forecasting** | Right-click view → Forecast → Show Forecast | Adds trend prediction |
| **Trend line** | Right-click view → Trend Lines → Show Trend Lines | Adds linear regression line |

---

## 2.4 Aggregation & Granularity Theory

Every measure in Tableau is *aggregated* by default. The aggregation function collapses N rows into a single value (e.g., SUM, AVG).

$$\text{Aggregated Value} = f(\text{Measure}_1, \text{Measure}_2, \ldots, \text{Measure}_N)$$

Common aggregation functions:

$$\text{SUM}(x) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i \quad \text{(Total)}$$

$$\text{AVG}(x) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i \quad \text{(Mean)}$$

$$\text{COUNT}(x) = N \quad \text{(Row Count)}$$

$$\text{MAX}(x) = \max(x_1, \ldots, x_N)$$

$$\text{MIN}(x) = \min(x_1, \ldots, x_N)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students lose marks by stating "Tableau sums the data" without specifying the **Level of Detail (LOD)**. Aggregation depends on the dimensions in the view — the same `SUM(Sales)` field gives different totals at Region level vs. State level vs. City level.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering & Business Utility

- **Retail Analytics:** Sales performance by store, region, product category.
- **Banking & Finance:** Risk segmentation, loan default analysis, customer churn.
- **Healthcare:** Patient outcome dashboards, bed occupancy trends.
- **Manufacturing:** Defect rate analysis, OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness) tracking.
- **Marketing:** Campaign ROI, conversion funnel visualisation.
- **Supply Chain:** Inventory turnover, lead time distribution.

> [!TIP]
> **Why This Matters in KTU Exams:** Questions like *"How would you analyse quarterly sales trend in Tableau?"* are testing whether you can *translate business questions into shelf operations* — a core CO3 / CO4 skill.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Procedures & Decision Logic

## 3.1 Procedure: Building a Standard Sales Analysis Worksheet

This is the **canonical exam question** for KTU: *"Explain step-by-step how to create a sales dashboard in Tableau using a worksheet."* The model solution below follows the **exact valuation pattern** expected by KTU examiners.

---

### Step 1: Connect to Data Source
**Action:** Launch Tableau Desktop → Click "Connect" → Choose data source (Excel, CSV, SQL Server, Google Sheets).
**Logic:** A worksheet *cannot exist* without a data binding. The Data Pane populates only after a successful connection.

### Step 2: Navigate to Sheet 1
**Action:** Click "Sheet 1" tab at the bottom of the workbook.
**Logic:** A new Tableau workbook starts with one default blank worksheet (`Sheet 1`).

### Step 3: Identify Dimensions and Measures
**Action:** In the Data Pane, observe the auto-classified fields.
- **Dimensions (blue):** Order Date, Region, Product Category, Product Sub-Category, Customer Name, City, State.
- **Measures (green):** Sales, Profit, Quantity, Discount.

**Logic:** Dimensions provide the *categorical structure* (the "by" axis), while measures provide the *numerical magnitude* (the "what" being measured).

### Step 4: Build the Bar Chart Skeleton
**Action:**
- Drag **`Region`** (Dimension) to the **Columns** shelf.
- Drag **`SUM(Sales)`** (Measure) to the **Rows** shelf.

**Logic:**
- A Dimension on Columns creates a *horizontal categorical axis* — one bar per region.
- A Measure on Rows creates a *vertical continuous axis* — bar height = total sales.

### Step 5: Refine Mark Encoding
**Action:**
- Drag **`Category`** (Dimension) to the **Color** shelf on the Marks card.
- Drag **`SUM(Profit)`** (Measure) to the **Label** shelf.

**Logic:** Colour encodes Category → bars split by category colour. Label encodes exact profit on each bar.

### Step 6: Apply Sorting
**Action:** Click the sort icon (ascending/descending arrows) on the Sales axis.
**Logic:** Reorders bars from highest to lowest sales for easier comparison.

### Step 7: Add a Filter
**Action:** Drag **`Order Date`** (Dimension) to the **Filters** shelf → select date range (e.g., 2023 only).
**Logic:** Restricts the view to a specific time window, enabling year-wise comparison.

### Step 8: Add a Reference Line
**Action:** Right-click on the Sales axis → "Add Reference Line" → choose "Average" → label "Overall Average Sales".
**Logic:** Provides a *benchmark* to instantly see which regions perform above/below average.

### Step 9: Rename and Format the Worksheet
**Action:** Double-click the sheet tab → rename to "Sales by Region". Right-click the title → format font, size, colour.
**Logic:** Professional formatting is a *hidden scoring criterion* in KTU practical exams.

### Step 10: Save and Export
**Action:** File → Save As → `Regional_Sales_Analysis.twb` (or `.twbx` packaged workbook).
**Logic:** `.twbx` packages the data + workbook for portability — preferred for submissions.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Business Questions to Worksheet Operations

This matrix is a **KTU gold-mine** for theory questions. Memorise the mapping pattern.

| Business Question | Tableau Action | Shelves Used | CO Level |
|---|---|---|---|
| Which region has the highest sales? | Bar chart of SUM(Sales) by Region | Columns: Region; Rows: SUM(Sales) | CO3 / Apply |
| How does sales vary month by month? | Line chart of SUM(Sales) by Order Date (Month) | Columns: Order Date; Rows: SUM(Sales) | CO3 / Apply |
| What is the profit margin by product? | Calculated field: SUM(Profit)/SUM(Sales) | New measure, then bar chart | CO4 / Analyse |
| Which customers are most valuable? | Scatter plot of SUM(Sales) vs SUM(Profit), Label = Customer | Columns: SUM(Profit); Rows: SUM(Sales); Detail: Customer | CO4 / Analyse |
| How does sales vary geographically? | Filled map of SUM(Sales) by State | Columns / Rows: empty; Marks = Map; Color: SUM(Sales) | CO3 / Apply |
| What is the sales composition by category? | Pie chart of SUM(Sales) by Category | Marks = Pie; Angle: SUM(Sales); Color: Category | CO3 / Apply |
| What is the YoY growth rate? | Calculated field using Table Calculation: (ZN(SUM(Sales)) - LOOKUP(ZN(SUM(Sales)), -1)) / LOOKUP(ZN(SUM(Sales)), -1) | Rows: Calc Field; Columns: Year | CO5 / Evaluate |
| Which segments are below target? | Reference line + conditional colour | Reference line on Sales; Color with IF condition | CO5 / Evaluate |

---

## 3.3 Decision Logic: When to Use Which Chart Type

```
IF  business_question == "compare_categorical_magnitudes"
    USE Bar Chart
ELSE IF business_question == "show_trend_over_time"
    USE Line Chart
ELSE IF business_question == "show_proportion_of_whole"
    USE Pie Chart (or Treemap for > 7 categories)
ELSE IF business_question == "show_distribution"
    USE Histogram
ELSE IF business_question == "show_correlation"
    USE Scatter Plot
ELSE IF business_question == "show_geographic_pattern"
    USE Map (Symbol or Filled)
ELSE IF business_question == "show_composition_change_over_time"
    USE Stacked Area Chart
ELSE IF business_question == "show_hierarchical_proportion"
    USE Treemap
END
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Note:** Tableau's **Show Me** panel automatically enforces best-practice rules — for example, you cannot create a scatter plot with only one measure. It will grey out invalid chart types. Examiners may test this constraint logic.

---

## 3.4 Step-by-Step: Creating a Calculated Field (Frequently Asked 14-Mark Topic)

**Business Requirement:** Create a new measure called "Profit Margin %" in the Data Pane.

**Step A:** Right-click in Data Pane → "Create Calculated Field".
**Step B:** In the formula box, type:

$$\text{Profit Margin \%} = \frac{\text{SUM(Profit)}}{\text{SUM(Sales)}} \times 100$$

**Step C:** Click OK. The new field appears in the Measures section.
**Step D:** Drag it to Rows shelf to plot margin %.

**Logic:** The calculated field is a *virtual column* computed at query time — it does not modify the underlying source data, preserving data integrity (a key Tableau design principle called *VizQL*).

---

## 3.5 KTU Examiner's Insight: The Level of Detail (LOD) Concept

LOD expressions allow aggregation at a level *different* from the view's granularity. Three types:

| LOD Type | Syntax | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **FIXED** | `{ FIXED [Region] : SUM(Sales) }` | Force aggregation at a specific level regardless of view |
| **INCLUDE** | `{ INCLUDE [Customer] : SUM(Sales) }` | Aggregate at finer level than view |
| **EXCLUDE** | `{ EXCLUDE [Region] : SUM(Sales) }` | Aggregate at coarser level than view |

**Example:** To find the *average sales per customer within each region*, you would use:

$$\text{Avg Sales per Customer in Region} = \text{AVG}\left(\left\{ \text{FIXED [Region], [Customer] : SUM(Sales) } \right\}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** LOD expressions are a *high-scoring advanced topic*. Expect 7-mark sub-questions on the difference between FIXED, INCLUDE, and EXCLUDE.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Tableau Worksheet

The diagram below maps the *complete functional flow* of a Tableau Worksheet — from data ingestion to final visual rendering. This is the architecture you should draw (in any format) for a 14-mark "describe the Tableau worksheet" question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Data Source Excel CSV SQL Database] --> B[Tableau Data Engine Hyper]
    B --> C[Data Pane Dimensions and Measures]
    C --> D[Drag Dimensions to Columns Rows]
    C --> E[Drag Measures to Columns Rows]
    C --> F[Drag Fields to Filters Shelf]
    C --> G[Drag Fields to Marks Card]
    D --> H[Shelf Configuration Layer]
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[VizQL Engine Query Generator]
    I --> J[Query Execution against Hyper]
    J --> K[Aggregated Result Set]
    K --> L[Mark Encoding Color Size Shape Label]
    L --> M[Final Rendered View Canvas]
    M --> N{User Interaction}
    N -->|Hover| O[Tooltip Display]
    N -->|Click| P[Filter Action Highlight Action]
    N -->|Drag| Q[Selection Zoom Pan]
    M --> R[Reference Lines Trend Lines Forecasts]
    M --> S[Sort Group Set Hierarchies]
    M --> T[Worksheet Output Single Visualization]
    T --> U[Dashboard Aggregation Multiple Worksheets]
    U --> V[Story Sequence Narrated Insights]
```

**How to Read This Diagram:**

- **Top-down flow** represents the *data pipeline* — from raw source to visual output.
- **Horizontal branches** at `C` represent the *four shelf destinations* (Columns/Rows, Filters, Marks, Pages).
- **VizQL Engine** is Tableau's proprietary *Visual Query Language* — it translates drag-and-drop actions into SQL-like queries.
- **Right-side branches** show *interactivity* (tooltips, filters, selections) and *analytical enhancements* (reference lines, trend lines).
- **Bottom block** shows the *composition* — worksheets combine into dashboards, dashboards compose into stories.

---

## 4.2 Marks Card Encoding Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Drag Dimension Category] --> B1[Color Shelf]
    A --> B2[Shape Shelf]
    A --> B3[Detail Shelf]
    A --> B4[Label Shelf]
    C[Drag Measure Sales] --> D1[Size Shelf]
    C --> D2[Label Shelf]
    C --> D3[Tooltip Shelf]
    E[Mark Type Dropdown] --> F1[Bar]
    E --> F2[Line]
    E --> F3[Area]
    E --> F4[Circle]
    E --> F5[Square]
    E --> F6[Pie]
    E --> F7[Text]
    E --> F8[Map]
    B1 --> G[Final Encoded Mark]
    B2 --> G
    B3 --> G
    B4 --> G
    D1 --> G
    D2 --> G
    D3 --> G
    F1 --> G
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    F5 --> G
    F6 --> G
    F7 --> G
    F8 --> G
```

---

## 4.3 Worksheet-to-Dashboard Composition Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    W1[Worksheet 1 Sales by Region Bar Chart] --> D[Dashboard Canvas]
    W2[Worksheet 2 Sales Trend Line Chart] --> D
    W3[Worksheet 3 Top Customers Scatter] --> D
    W4[Worksheet 4 Geographic Map] --> D
    D --> DA[Dashboard Actions Filter Highlight URL]
    D --> S[Story 1 Executive Summary]
    D --> S2[Story 2 Regional Deep Dive]
    S --> P[Publish Tableau Public Tableau Server]
    S2 --> P
```

**Interpretation:** This diagram shows the *hierarchical composition* — a worksheet is an *atom*, a dashboard is a *molecule* (multiple worksheets + actions), and a story is a *narrative* (a sequence of dashboards).

---

## 4.4 Decision Tree: Choosing the Right Chart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[What is the business question?] --> B{Compare values?}
    B -->|Yes| C{Over categories?}
    C -->|Yes| D[Bar Chart]
    C -->|No, over time| E[Line Chart]
    B -->|No| F{Show composition?}
    F -->|Yes| G{Static or over time?}
    G -->|Static| H[Pie Chart if less than 7 slices]
    G -->|Static many slices| I[Treemap]
    G -->|Over time| J[Stacked Area Chart]
    F -->|No| K{Show relationship?}
    K -->|Yes, two variables| L[Scatter Plot]
    K -->|Yes, three variables| M[Bubble Chart]
    F -->|No| N{Show geographic data?}
    N -->|Yes| O[Symbol Map or Filled Map]
    N -->|No| P{Show distribution?}
    P -->|Yes| Q[Histogram or Box Plot]
    P -->|No| R[Refer to Tableau Show Me Panel]
```
