# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

### **Question 1: Define a Tableau Worksheet and list its main components.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper 2]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Tableau Worksheet** is the primary canvas in Tableau where users build a single data visualization by binding a data source to shelf configurations and marks encoding.

**Main components (3 marks breakdown):**

1. **Data Pane** (1 mark) — Left sidebar listing Dimensions (blue) and Measures (green) from the connected data source.
2. **Shelves** (1 mark) — Drop zones including Columns, Rows, Pages, and Filters that define the chart's structure.
3. **Marks Card** (1 mark) — Visual encoding controls with sub-shelves: Color, Size, Label, Detail, Tooltip, and Shape.

---

### **Question 2: Differentiate between Dimensions and Measures in Tableau.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Supplementary]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Dimensions | Measures |
|---|---|---|
| **Colour in Data Pane** | Blue pills | Green pills |
| **Data type** | Categorical (qualitative) | Numerical (quantitative) |
| **Default behaviour** | Disaggregate, create headers | Aggregated (SUM/AVG/COUNT) |
| **Axis role** | Define chart structure (rows/columns) | Define axis values |
| **Examples** | Region, Product, Date | Sales, Profit, Quantity |

Dimensions provide the *categorical grouping*; measures provide the *numerical values* being measured. A measure without a dimension is meaningless; a dimension without a measure is just a list.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q. (a)** Explain the anatomy of a Tableau Worksheet with a neat block diagram. Describe the functions of the Data Pane, Columns/Rows shelves, and the Marks Card. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** With a suitable business scenario, explain step-by-step how to build a worksheet that compares the quarterly sales of four product categories across the last two years, using colour encoding and a reference line for the average. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

**1. Introduction to Worksheet (1 mark)**
A Tableau Worksheet is a single analytical view in a workbook where data is bound to a visual structure. It serves as the building block for dashboards and stories.

**2. Data Pane — Function (1 mark)**
The Data Pane is the left sidebar that lists all fields from the connected data source, auto-classified into **Dimensions** (categorical, blue) and **Measures** (numerical, green). It supports right-click operations like creating calculated fields, groups, sets, parameters, and hierarchies.

**3. Columns and Rows Shelves — Function (1 mark)**
- **Columns shelf** defines the horizontal (X) axis.
- **Rows shelf** defines the vertical (Y) axis.
- A dimension on Columns creates a categorical axis (e.g., Region).
- A measure on Rows creates a continuous axis (e.g., SUM(Sales)).

**4. Marks Card — Function (1 mark)**
The Marks Card controls *how each data point is visually encoded*:
- **Color** — hue (e.g., product category)
- **Size** — magnitude (e.g., profit)
- **Label** — text overlay
- **Shape** — glyph type
- **Detail** — granularity (adds without changing visual form)
- **Tooltip** — hover information

**5. Mark Type Dropdown (1 mark)**
The top of the Marks Card lets you change the *primitive mark* (Bar, Line, Area, Circle, Square, Pie, Text, Map, etc.), instantly transforming the chart type.

**6. Filters and Pages Shelves (1 mark)**
- **Filters** restrict the data displayed.
- **Pages** creates a sequence of views (e.g., animation by year).

**7. Neat Block Diagram (1 mark)**
[Student must draw the architecture from **Section 4.1** above, showing Data Source → Data Pane → Shelves → Marks Card → View → Dashboard.]

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Business Scenario:** "Create a quarterly sales comparison of four product categories (Furniture, Office Supplies, Technology, Furniture) across 2022 and 2023 for the company's executive review."

**Step 1: Connect Data (1 mark)**
Connect to the Sample-Superstore dataset. Identify fields: `Order Date`, `Category`, `Sales`, `Profit`.

**Step 2: Place Fields on Shelves (2 marks)**
- Drag `Order Date` to **Columns** → right-click → choose **"Quarter"** (continuous or discrete).
- Drag `SUM(Sales)` to **Rows**.
- This creates a bar chart of quarterly sales.

**Step 3: Add Colour Encoding (1 mark)**
- Drag `Category` to the **Color** shelf on the Marks Card.
- Result: four coloured bars per quarter, one per category.

**Step 4: Add Year Filter (1 mark)**
- Drag `Order Date` (Year) to the **Filters** shelf.
- Select only **2022** and **2023**.

**Step 5: Add Reference Line (1 mark)**
- Right-click the Sales Y-axis → "Add Reference Line".
- Choose **"Average"** as the value, **"Entire Table"** as the scope.
- Label it "Avg Sales = ₹X" and format in a contrasting colour.

**Step 6: Final Touches (1 mark)**
- Rename sheet tab to "Quarterly Category Sales".
- Add title: "Quarterly Sales by Category (2022–2023)".
- Save workbook as `Quarterly_Category_Sales.twbx`.

**Result:** A worksheet showing 8 quarters × 4 categories = 32 bars, with a horizontal average reference line, ready for executive review.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q. (a)** What are the different mark types available in the Marks Card? Explain any four with one business use case each. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** Explain the concept of aggregation in Tableau. How does the Level of Detail (LOD) affect aggregation? Differentiate between FIXED, INCLUDE, and EXCLUDE LODs with examples. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

**1. Definition of Mark Type (1 mark)**
The **Mark Type** dropdown in the Marks Card determines the *primitive visual shape* used to represent each data point in the view.

**2. Enumeration of Mark Types (2 marks)**
Tableau offers these mark types: **Bar, Line, Area, Square, Circle, Shape, Text, Pie, Gantt Bar, Polygon, Map**.

**3–6. Four Mark Types with Use Cases (4 × 1 mark = 4 marks)**

| Mark Type | Use Case |
|---|---|
| **Bar** | Comparing sales across regions. A bar chart of `SUM(Sales)` by `Region` clearly shows which region has the highest revenue. |
| **Line** | Tracking monthly revenue trend. A line chart of `SUM(Sales)` by `Order Date` (Month) shows seasonality and growth. |
| **Pie** | Showing market share composition. A pie chart of `SUM(Sales)` by `Segment` shows Consumer/Corporate/Home-Office share. |
| **Map** | Geographic sales distribution. A filled map of `SUM(Sales)` by `State` shows regional concentration of revenue. |

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**1. Definition of Aggregation (1 mark)**
Aggregation in Tableau is the process of combining multiple data values into a single summary value (e.g., SUM, AVG, COUNT, MIN, MAX) when the measure is placed on a shelf.

**2. Default Aggregation (1 mark)**
Tableau's default aggregation for measures is **SUM**. This can be changed by right-clicking the pill → "Measure" → choose AVG, COUNT, COUNTD, MIN, MAX, MEDIAN, etc.

**3. Level of Detail (LOD) Concept (1 mark)**
LOD expressions allow a measure to be aggregated at a granularity *different* from the view's structure. This solves the "wrong total" problem when a single dimension is missing from the view.

**4. FIXED LOD (1 mark)**
Syntax: `{ FIXED [Region] : SUM(Sales) }`
- Aggregates at the specified dimension level **regardless** of view's other dimensions.
- **Example:** Show the *regional* total sales on every row of a customer-level table.

**5. INCLUDE LOD (1 mark)**
Syntax: `{ INCLUDE [Customer] : SUM(Sales) }`
- Aggregates at a *finer* level than the view.
- **Example:** View is at Region level, but you want the *average sales per customer* within each region.

**6. EXCLUDE LOD (1 mark)**
Syntax: `{ EXCLUDE [Region] : SUM(Sales) }`
- Aggregates at a *coarser* level than the view.
- **Example:** View is at Region + State level, but you want the *total sales excluding* state-level detail (i.e., back to Region level only).

**7. Comparative Summary (1 mark)**
- **FIXED** = ignore view, use specified dimensions.
- **INCLUDE** = add dimensions to view's granularity.
- **EXCLUDE** = remove dimensions from view's granularity.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Tableau Worksheet Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Tableau Worksheet with Tableau Workbook** — Workbook = file; Worksheet = single canvas. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this mix-up.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention the mark type** when describing a chart — always state whether the chart is a Bar, Line, Pie, or Map.
> 3. **Stating "Tableau sums the data" without LOD context** — aggregation depends on the view's dimensions. Always specify the *level of detail*.
> 4. **Writing code/Python** — Tableau questions are about *interface operations*, not programming. Writing code gets **zero marks** for that part.
> 5. **Missing the Data Pane classification** — Dimensions are **blue**, Measures are **green**. Examiners expect this colour-coding mention.
> 6. **Not specifying shelf names exactly** — say "Columns shelf" and "Rows shelf" (capitalised), not just "horizontal axis."
> 7. **Ignoring the .twbx format** for submissions — `.twbx` packages the data, `.twb` does not. Examiners prefer `.twbx` for evaluation.
> 8. **Confusing Filters shelf with Filters dimension** — Filters is a *shelf*, but you can also right-click any field to add a quick filter.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Tableau Worksheets**

- **Worksheet** = single view canvas; **Workbook** = file containing multiple worksheets.
- **Data Pane** lists **Dimensions (blue, categorical)** and **Measures (green, numerical)**.
- **Shelves:** Columns (X), Rows (Y), Pages (animation/sequence), Filters (restrict view).
- **Marks Card** has 6 sub-shelves: **Color, Size, Label, Detail, Tooltip, Shape**.
- **Mark Type dropdown** decides primitive shape: **Bar, Line, Pie, Map, Circle, Square, Text, Area, Gantt, Polygon, Shape**.
- **Default aggregation** = **SUM**; alternative: AVG, COUNT, COUNTD, MIN, MAX, MEDIAN, STDEV, VAR.
- **Sorting:** click sort icon on axis or right-click field → Sort.
- **Reference Lines** = right-click axis → Add Reference Line → Average / Constant / Line.
- **Trend Lines** = right-click view → Trend Lines → Show Trend Lines.
- **Forecast** = right-click view → Forecast → Show Forecast (only on time-series).
- **Calculated Field** = right-click Data Pane → Create Calculated Field (formula based on existing fields).
- **Group** = right-click dimension members → Create Group (combine categories).
- **Set** = right-click dimension → Create Set (dynamic subset based on condition).
- **Parameter** = right-click Data Pane → Create Parameter (user-controlled input value).
- **Hierarchy** = drag one dimension onto another in Data Pane (e.g., Region → State → City).
- **LOD Expressions:** FIXED (specify), INCLUDE (add granularity), EXCLUDE (remove granularity).
- **Dual Axis** = drag a measure to the right of the canvas → choose "Dual Axis" to combine two charts.
- **Show Me panel** = top-right; recommends valid chart types based on selected fields.
- **Show Me Rule:** A scatter plot needs ≥ 2 measures; a map needs ≥ 1 geographic dimension.
- **Save formats:** `.twb` (workbook only) vs `.twbx` (packaged with data — preferred for exam submissions).
- **Tableau Public** = free version for students; **Tableau Desktop** = licensed professional version.
- **Worksheet workflow:** Connect Data → Build View (shelves + marks) → Enhance (sort, filter, reference) → Format → Save → Publish to Dashboard/Story.

> **Final Tip:** In the KTU exam, always *start with a one-line definition* (1 mark), then *enumerate components* (2–3 marks), then *describe with an example* (3–4 marks), then *conclude with utility* (1 mark). This 1+2+3+1 = 7-mark pattern is a guaranteed scoring template.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tableau Calculations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT522 - BUSINESS ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Tableau Calculations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework for **PEADT522 – Business Analytics**, **Tableau Calculations** are defined as a family of in-memory, formula-driven operations that allow users to create new fields, aggregate data dynamically, and perform row-level, partition-level, or view-level numeric transformations on a dataset without altering the underlying data source.

Tableau classifies calculations into **three principal families**:

1. **Calculated Fields** – row-level expressions that operate on every record of the data source.
2. **Table Calculations** – view-level transformations that operate on the *visible* result set (the table) rather than the raw rows.
3. **Level of Detail (LOD) Expressions** – aggregations computed at a *user-defined granularity*, independent of the view's dimensions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Emphasis:** Examiners frequently test the *difference* between these three families. Confusing a **Table Calculation** with an **LOD Expression** is one of the most common 3-mark pitfalls in the Business Analytics paper.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Bill

Imagine a restaurant bill.

- The **menu price** of every dish is a *raw column* (a measure from the data source).
- If you compute **"price after 5% GST"** for *every single dish individually*, that is a **Calculated Field** — it acts on every row.
- If the waiter then says **"Running total of your bill as you order"**, that is a **Table Calculation** — it is computed *only across the items you see on the bill* and changes if you reorder them.
- If the manager later asks **"What is the average bill value per table, regardless of how many items were ordered?"**, that is an **LOD Expression** — it fixes a granularity (the table) and aggregates, even if the view shows a different level (e.g., dish level).

> [!NOTE]
> **The Golden Rule of Tableau Calculations:** A *Calculated Field* uses the source data; a *Table Calculation* uses the **view's visible data**; an *LOD Expression* uses a **custom-defined granularity** that may differ from both.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants Used

- **Default aggregation direction** in Tableau: **Automatic (SUM)** for numeric measures.
- **VizQL Table Calculation scope** markers: **Table (across)**, **Table (down)**, **Pane (across)**, **Pane (down)**, **Cell**.
- **LOD keyword limit**: A workbook supports up to **65,535** unique LOD combinations in practical use.
- **Total of all partition percentages** in a `% of Total` quick table calculation: **100%**.

### 1.4 Typology at a Glance

> [!TIP]
> For 3-mark questions, memorizing the **three families** and one example of each is sufficient to secure full marks.

| Calculation Family | Operates On | Granularity Control | KTU Keyword to Remember |
|---|---|---|---|
| Calculated Field | Source row | Row-level | "Row-level" |
| Table Calculation | Visible result set | View-level | "Pivot/Partition" |
| LOD Expression | Custom dimension set | User-defined | "FIXED / INCLUDE / EXCLUDE" |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Calculated Fields — Row-Level Logic

A **Calculated Field** is created via the menu `Analysis → Create Calculated Field` (or by right-clicking a measure/dimension). It returns one value per **row** of the underlying data.

**Why it matters in KTU analytics:** Calculated fields are the foundation of derived KPIs such as **Profit Margin**, **Discount %**, **YoY Growth**, and **Customer Lifetime Value**.

**How it works — the logical flow:**

- Step 1: Tableau reads the syntax in the formula bar.
- Step 2: Tableau parses field references using **square brackets** `[FieldName]`.
- Step 3: Tableau compiles the expression into VizQL bytecode.
- Step 4: The expression is evaluated **once per row** in the data source.
- Step 5: The result becomes a virtual column in the in-memory data engine.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syntax Convention (KTU Exam):** Field references are always enclosed in **square brackets**, e.g., `[Sales]`, `[Order Date]`. Function names are always followed by parentheses, e.g., `SUM(`, `IF(`. String literals use **single quotes**, e.g., `'High'`.

**Common Calculated Field Functions (in KTU 2024 syllabus):**

- **Logical:** `IF`, `IIF`, `CASE`, `AND`, `OR`, `NOT`.
- **Numeric:** `ABS`, `ROUND`, `CEILING`, `FLOOR`, `POWER`, `SQRT`.
- **String:** `LEFT`, `RIGHT`, `MID`, `LEN`, `UPPER`, `LOWER`, `TRIM`, `CONTAINS`.
- **Date:** `DATEADD`, `DATEDIFF`, `DATENAME`, `DATEPART`, `TODAY`, `NOW`.

### 2.2 Aggregate Functions — The Aggregation Engine

Tableau provides **seven** primary aggregate functions:

- `SUM([Measure])` — additive totals.
- `AVG([Measure])` — arithmetic mean.
- `COUNT([Dimension])` — counts non-null distinct values.
- `COUNTD([Dimension])` — counts *distinct* non-null values.
- `MIN([Measure])` / `MAX([Measure])` — extremes.
- `MEDIAN([Measure])` — middle value.
- `STDEV([Measure])` / `VAR([Measure])` — dispersion.

### 2.3 Table Calculations — View-Level Logic

A **Table Calculation** is a transformation applied *after* the data has been aggregated into the view. It therefore operates on the **two-dimensional space** defined by:

- **Partitioning fields** — define *separate tables* of calculation.
- **Addressing fields** — define the *direction* across which the calculation traverses.

**Mental Model (KTU Board Standard):**

> Partitioning = "I am a separate island" → fields placed **before** the separator.
> Addressing = "I am the path I walk" → fields placed **after** the separator.

**Common Table Calculation Functions:**

- `RUNNING_SUM(expression)` — cumulative sum across addressing direction.
- `WINDOW_SUM(expression, start, end)` — sum over a sliding window.
- `WINDOW_AVG(expression, start, end)` — moving average.
- `RANK(expression, 'asc'|'desc')` — ordinal ranking.
- `INDEX()` — row index within partition.
- `FIRST()`, `LAST()` — distance from the boundary.
- `SIZE()` — count of rows in the partition.
- `LOOKUP(expression, offset)` — value `n` steps away.
- `PREVIOUS_VALUE(expression)` — value from the prior row.
- `TOTAL(expression)` — total of the entire partition (used for `% of Total`).

### 2.4 Level of Detail (LOD) Expressions — Custom Granularity

LOD Expressions, introduced in **Tableau 9.0**, allow a calculation to be computed at a *granularity chosen by the author*, independent of the dimensions in the view.

The three LOD keywords are:

- `{FIXED [Dimension] : AGG(Expression)}` — aggregates *exactly* at the specified dimensions, ignoring the view.
- `{INCLUDE [Dimension] : AGG(Expression)}` — performs the aggregation at a *finer* level than the view, then re-aggregates to the view level.
- `{EXCLUDE [Dimension] : AGG(Expression)}` — performs the aggregation at a *coarser* level than the view, omitting the specified dimension.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FIXED LODs are computed BEFORE dimension filters; INCLUDE/EXCLUDE are computed AFTER dimension filters.** This is a high-yield distinction tested in KTU Part B questions.

### 2.5 Quick Table Calculations

Tableau offers **one-click** table calculations for common analyst needs:

- **Running Total**
- **Difference**
- **Percent Difference**
- **Percent of Total**
- **Rank**
- **Percentile**
- **Moving Average**
- **Year over Year Growth**
- **Year to Date (YTD) Total**

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Construct | Generic Expression | Output Granularity | Use Case (Real-World) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Calculated Field | `[Profit] / [Sales]` | Row | Profit Margin per transaction |
| Conditional Field | `IF [Sales] > 1000 THEN 'High' ELSE 'Low' END` | Row | Customer segmentation |
| Quick Running Total | `RUNNING_SUM(SUM([Sales]))` | View | Cumulative revenue trend |
| Window Average | `WINDOW_AVG(SUM([Sales]), -2, 0)` | View | 3-period moving average |
| Rank | `RANK(SUM([Sales]), 'desc')` | View | Top-N customer list |
| FIXED LOD | `{FIXED [Region] : SUM([Sales])}` | Custom (Region) | Regional totals regardless of view |
| INCLUDE LOD | `{INCLUDE [Customer Name] : SUM([Sales])}` | Finer than view | Avg sales per customer in region view |
| EXCLUDE LOD | `{EXCLUDE [Sub-Category] : SUM([Sales])}` | Coarser than view | Category-level sales in a sub-category view |
| Percent of Total | `SUM([Sales]) / TOTAL(SUM([Sales]))` | View | Share of each region in total sales |
| YoY Growth | `(SUM([Sales]) - LOOKUP(SUM([Sales]), -1)) / LOOKUP(SUM([Sales]), -1)` | View | Annual revenue growth |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering & Business Utility

Tableau Calculations power decision intelligence across:

- **Retail:** Market basket analysis via `INCLUDE` LOD on product pairs.
- **Banking:** Customer Lifetime Value via `FIXED [Customer ID]` aggregations.
- **Healthcare:** Patient readmission rates via `WINDOW_AVG` over 30-day windows.
- **Manufacturing:** Defect-rate dashboards via running-sum cumulative SPC charts.
- **KTU Project Domains:** B.Tech final-year analytics projects in IoT, smart cities, and ERP systems routinely use these calculations for **KPI dashboarding**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Deriving Profit Margin as a Calculated Field

**Problem Statement (KTU 2024 pattern):**
A retail dataset has columns `[Sales]`, `[Cost]`, and `[Discount]`. Derive a calculated field named `Net Profit` where:
$$\text{Net Profit} = (\text{Sales} - \text{Discount}) - \text{Cost}$$

**Step 1 — Identify input fields.**
The fields `[Sales]`, `[Cost]`, and `[Discount]` are referenced using square brackets per Tableau syntax.

**Step 2 — Translate the algebraic expression to Tableau syntax.**

$$\text{Expression: } \texttt{([Sales] - [Discount]) - [Cost]}$$

**Step 3 — Validate operator precedence.**

- Parentheses are **mandatory** around `[Sales] - [Discount]` to ensure the discount is subtracted from sales *before* subtracting cost.
- Without parentheses, Tableau evaluates left-to-right: `[Sales] - [Discount] - [Cost]` yields the same result here, but **best practice in KTU valuation is to always parenthesize**.

**Step 4 — Derive the Profit Margin percentage.**

$$\text{Profit Margin \%} = \frac{\text{Net Profit}}{\text{Sales}} \times 100$$

In Tableau syntax:

$$\texttt{([Sales] - [Discount] - [Cost]) / [Sales]}$$

**Step 5 — Verify the row-level evaluation.**

For a single row with `Sales = 1000`, `Cost = 600`, `Discount = 100`:

$$\text{Net Profit} = (1000 - 100) - 600 = 300$$

$$\text{Profit Margin} = \frac{300}{1000} = 0.30 = 30\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> This calculation evaluates **once per row**. To view it aggregated, drag it onto a shelf and Tableau will default to `SUM(Profit Margin)` if the field is numeric — which is *usually* the wrong aggregation. KTU examiners prefer `AVG` or a pre-aggregated LOD for margin KPIs.

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Running Total with a Table Calculation

**Problem Statement:**
Given monthly sales `[Sales]` across 12 months, derive a running total that resets each year.

**Step 1 — Build the base view.**

Place `[Order Date]` (at Month level) on Columns and `SUM([Sales])` on Rows.

**Step 2 — Apply Quick Table Calculation.**

Right-click the `SUM([Sales])` mark → `Quick Table Calculation` → `Running Total`. Tableau internally generates:

$$\texttt{RUNNING\_SUM(SUM([Sales]))}$$

**Step 3 — Configure the addressing & partitioning.**

The default addressing is **Table (across)**. To reset annually, drag `[Year]` to the view *before* the separator line, i.e., the partition.

The conceptual evaluation for the partition *Year = 2024* is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Month 1: } & \text{RS}_1 = S_1 \\
\text{Month 2: } & \text{RS}_2 = S_1 + S_2 \\
\text{Month 3: } & \text{RS}_3 = S_1 + S_2 + S_3 \\
& \vdots \\
\text{Month } k: & \text{RS}_k = \sum_{i=1}^{k} S_i
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Closed-form summation.**

$$\text{Running Total at month } k = \sum_{i=1}^{k} S_i$$

For each partition, the running total at month $k$ equals the cumulative sum from month $1$ to month $k$. When a new partition begins (new year), the running total resets to the first month's value.

**Step 5 — Worked numeric example.**

Suppose sales for Year 2024 (Jan–Apr) are: 100, 150, 200, 250.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Jan: } & 100 \\
\text{Feb: } & 100 + 150 = 250 \\
\text{Mar: } & 250 + 200 = 450 \\
\text{Apr: } & 450 + 250 = 700
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — FIXED LOD for Regional KPI

**Problem Statement:**
Compute **Average Sales per Customer within each Region**, irrespective of whether the view shows the city, state, or country level.

**Step 1 — Identify the granularity requirement.**

We need sales aggregated at the **Region × Customer** level, but we want the result to be **FIXED** regardless of view dimensions.

**Step 2 — Write the FIXED LOD expression.**

$$\texttt{\{ FIXED [Region], [Customer Name] : SUM([Sales]) \}}$$

**Step 3 — Derive the average across the region.**

To obtain the regional average, wrap it in a windowed aggregation:

$$\texttt{\{ FIXED [Region], [Customer Name] : SUM([Sales]) \}}$$

is the per-customer sales. To average across all customers in the region:

$$
\text{Regional Avg} = \frac{\sum_{c \in \text{Region}} \text{Sales}_c}{\text{Count of Customers in Region}}
$$

In Tableau:

$$\texttt{WINDOW\_AVG( \{ FIXED [Region], [Customer Name] : SUM([Sales]) \} , -∞, +∞)}$$

Or equivalently, using a nested FIXED:

$$\texttt{\{ FIXED [Region] : AVG( \{ FIXED [Region], [Customer Name] : SUM([Sales]) \} ) \}}$$

**Step 4 — Worked numeric example.**

Region *South* has 3 customers with sales 400, 600, 1000.

$$\text{Regional Avg} = \frac{400 + 600 + 1000}{3} = \frac{2000}{3} \approx 666.67$$

This number is **invariant** to whether the user places `[City]`, `[State]`, or `[Country]` in the view — that is the essence of `FIXED`.

### 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Window Average for Smoothing

**Problem Statement:**
Compute a **3-month moving average** of monthly sales to smooth seasonal noise.

**Step 1 — Window definition.**

The window spans the **current row and the two preceding rows** (a 3-row window). The Tableau syntax is:

$$\texttt{WINDOW\_AVG(SUM([Sales]), -2, 0)}$$

**Step 2 — Mathematical formulation.**

For row $i$ with addressing direction chronological:

$$
\text{MA}_i = \frac{1}{3} \sum_{j=i-2}^{i} S_j
$$

**Step 3 — Worked numeric example.**

Suppose monthly sales are: 100, 120, 150, 200, 250.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Month 1 (incomplete window):} & \text{MA}_1 = 100 \\
\text{Month 2 (incomplete window):} & \text{MA}_2 = \frac{100 + 120}{2} = 110 \\
\text{Month 3: } & \text{MA}_3 = \frac{100 + 120 + 150}{3} = 123.33 \\
\text{Month 4: } & \text{MA}_4 = \frac{120 + 150 + 200}{3} = 156.67 \\
\text{Month 5: } & \text{MA}_5 = \frac{150 + 200 + 250}{3} = 200.00
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> Tableau automatically handles the "incomplete window" at the boundary by averaging over the available rows — examiners may ask students to explicitly state this behaviour.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What

| Scenario | Best Calculation Type | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Profit Margin per transaction | Calculated Field | Operates on each row's data |
| Cumulative sales across the chart | Table Calculation (Running Total) | Operates on visible view |
| Avg sales per customer in a region-level view | `INCLUDE` LOD | Needs a dimension *finer* than the view |
| Sales excluding the Sub-Category dimension | `EXCLUDE` LOD | Needs a dimension *removed* from the view |
| Top-5 products by sales | `RANK()` Table Calc | Requires view-level ranking |
| Year-over-year growth % | Table Calc with `LOOKUP(-1)` | Needs prior-period value |
| Cohort retention analysis | `FIXED [Cohort Month] : ...` | Needs a custom granularity |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Tableau Calculation Taxonomy (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

This block diagram maps the **three families of Tableau Calculations** and how data flows through the Tableau engine.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source Data Rows] --> B{Row-Level Expression?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Calculated Field]
    C --> C1[Evaluated Once Per Row]
    C1 --> D[Aggregated View]

    D --> E{View-Level Transformation?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Table Calculation]
    F --> F1[Partitioning Fields]
    F --> F2[Addressing Fields]
    F1 --> G[Output Table]
    F2 --> G

    A --> H{Custom Granularity Needed?}
    H -- Yes --> I[LOD Expression]
    I --> I1[FIXED keyword]
    I --> I2[INCLUDE keyword]
    I --> I3[EXCLUDE keyword]
    I1 --> J[Independent of View]
    I2 --> J
    I3 --> J
    J --> D

    D --> K[Final Visualization Marks]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**

- Every row from the source data first passes through any **Calculated Fields** (Step A → B → C).
- The aggregated result is then optionally transformed by **Table Calculations** (D → E → F).
- Independently, **LOD Expressions** operate directly on the source using custom keywords (A → H → I).
- Both pathways converge into the final **Visualization Marks** (K).

### 4.2 LOD Expression Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Need a Custom Granularity Aggregation] --> Q1{Should the view dimensions affect the LOD level?}

    Q1 -- No, ignore view dimensions --> Fixed[FIXED LOD]
    Fixed --> F1[Granularity strictly controlled by author]
    F1 --> Ex1[Example: FIXED Region, Customer Name]

    Q1 -- Yes, include additional dimension --> Q2{Add or remove a dimension?}

    Q2 -- Add finer detail --> Include[INCLUDE LOD]
    Include --> I1[Computed at finer level than view]
    I1 --> Ex2[Example: INCLUDE Customer Name]

    Q2 -- Remove coarse detail --> Exclude[EXCLUDE LOD]
    Exclude --> E1[Computed at coarser level than view]
    E1 --> Ex3[Example: EXCLUDE Sub-Category]

    Fixed --> Out[Result Aggregated in View]
    Include --> Out
    Exclude --> Out
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The decision tree helps KTU students answer "which LOD keyword should I use" — a frequent 7-mark question.
- `FIXED` is the **default** answer when the view-level dimensions must be ignored.
- `INCLUDE` is used to add a dimension *not* in the view.
- `EXCLUDE` is used to drop a dimension that *is* in the view.

### 4.3 Table Calculation — Partitioning vs Addressing Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Partition1[Partition A - Year 2023]
        A1[Q1: 100] --> A2[Q2: 150] --> A3[Q3: 200] --> A4[Q4: 250]
    end

    subgraph Partition2[Partition B - Year 2024]
        B1[Q1: 300] --> B2[Q2: 350] --> B3[Q3: 400] --> B4[Q4: 450]
    end

    A1 -.Running Sum.-> RS_A1[100]
    A2 -.Running Sum.-> RS_A2[250]
    A3 -.Running Sum.-> RS_A3[450]
    A4 -.Running Sum.-> RS_A4[700]

    B1 -.Running Sum.-> RS_B1[300]
    B2 -.Running Sum.-> RS_B2[650]
    B3 -.Running Sum.-> RS_B3[1050]
    B4 -.Running Sum.-> RS_B4[1500]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- Each **partition** (Year 2023 and Year 2024) maintains an *independent* running total.
- The running sum **resets** when the partition changes.
- Within each partition, the addressing direction walks left-to-right (Q1 → Q4).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
Differentiate between a **Calculated Field**, a **Table Calculation**, and an **LOD Expression** in Tableau. Provide one example of each.

**Model Answer:**

- **Calculated Field:** A row-level expression evaluated once per row of the source data. *Example:* `[Profit] / [Sales]` to compute profit margin per transaction.
- **Table Calculation:** A view-level transformation applied on the *visible* result set. *Example:* `RUNNING_SUM(SUM([Sales]))` to compute cumulative monthly sales.
- **LOD Expression:** A custom-granularity aggregation that may differ from the view. *Example:* `{FIXED [Region] : SUM([Sales])}` to compute total sales per region irrespective of view dimensions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The key differentiator is **where** the expression is evaluated: source row, visible table, or custom granularity.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
What is the role of **Partitioning** and **Addressing** fields in a Table Calculation? Explain with an example.

**Model Answer:**

- **Partitioning fields** divide the view into independent sections; the table calculation restarts for each partition.
- **Addressing fields** determine the direction along which the calculation moves within each partition.

**Example:** For a `Running Total` of monthly sales, if `[Year]` is the **partitioning** field and `[Month]` is the **addressing** field, the running total resets at the start of every new year and walks chronologically across months within each year.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** Explain the three types of **Level of Detail (LOD) Expressions** in Tableau with suitable syntax and one business scenario for each. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A retail dataset has the following columns:

- `[Order ID]`, `[Customer Name]`, `[Region]`, `[Sub-Category]`, `[Sales]`, `[Quantity]`, `[Discount]`, `[Order Date]`.

Construct Tableau calculations for the following requirements. Show the exact formula and explain the granularity in each case:

- **(i)** A calculated field `Effective Sales` that applies a flat 10% discount on all transactions. (2 Marks)
- **(ii)** A calculated field `Sales per Unit` showing average selling price per unit. (2 Marks)
- **(iii)** A FIXED LOD expression `Customer Total` showing the total sales per customer across the entire dataset, irrespective of the view. (3 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Three LOD Expressions:**

1. **FIXED LOD:** Computes the aggregation at a granularity strictly defined by the author, *ignoring* the view's dimensions.
   - **Syntax:** `{ FIXED [Dim1], [Dim2] : AGG([Measure]) }`
   - **Business Scenario:** Computing total sales per region for a national dashboard — the regional total stays constant whether the user drills to state, city, or store level.

2. **INCLUDE LOD:** Computes the aggregation at a *finer* level than the view, then re-aggregates to the view.
   - **Syntax:** `{ INCLUDE [Dim1] : AGG([Measure]) }`
   - **Business Scenario:** In a region-level view, the user wants to see the *average sales per customer* — `INCLUDE [Customer Name]` brings in customer-level detail that the view does not show.

3. **EXCLUDE LOD:** Computes the aggregation at a *coarser* level than the view, by *removing* a specified dimension.
   - **Syntax:** `{ EXCLUDE [Dim1] : AGG([Measure]) }`
   - **Business Scenario:** In a Sub-Category view, the user wants to see *Category-level totals* next to each Sub-Category — `EXCLUDE [Sub-Category]` aggregates at the Category level.

**(b) — Solutions:**

**(i) Effective Sales Formula:**

$$
\text{Effective Sales} = \text{Sales} \times 0.90
$$

**Tableau Syntax:**

$$\texttt{[Sales] \times 0.90}$$

**Granularity:** Row-level — the calculation evaluates once per record.

**(ii) Sales per Unit Formula:**

$$
\text{Sales per Unit} = \frac{\text{Sales}}{\text{Quantity}}
$$

**Tableau Syntax:**

$$\texttt{[Sales] / [Quantity]}$$

**Granularity:** Row-level — evaluates once per transaction.

**(iii) Customer Total FIXED LOD:**

$$
\text{Customer Total} = \sum \text{Sales} \text{ for each Customer across all rows}
$$

**Tableau Syntax:**

$$\texttt{\{ FIXED [Customer Name] : SUM([Sales]) \}}$$

**Granularity:** Customer-level — independent of `[Region]`, `[Sub-Category]`, or any view dimension. The same value is returned for every row belonging to a given customer.

**Numerical verification:** If Customer *Aman* has 3 orders with sales 500, 700, and 800, then:

$$\text{Customer Total for Aman} = 500 + 700 + 800 = 2000$$

This value of 2000 appears in the Customer Total column for *every* row associated with Aman, regardless of the view's grouping.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Define **Table Calculations** in Tableau. Explain the concepts of **Partitioning** and **Addressing** with a clear diagram-based description. List **any four** built-in Table Calculation functions. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Monthly sales data for a company is given below for two years. The data is sorted chronologically.

| Month | Year | Sales |
|---|---|---|
| Jan | 2023 | 1000 |
| Feb | 2023 | 1200 |
| Mar | 2023 | 1500 |
| Apr | 2023 | 1800 |
| Jan | 2024 | 2000 |
| Feb | 2024 | 2200 |
| Mar | 2024 | 2500 |
| Apr | 2024 | 2800 |

Compute the following Table Calculations and show the derived table:

- **(i)** `RUNNING_SUM(SUM([Sales]))` partitioned by `[Year]` and addressed by `[Month]`. (3 Marks)
- **(ii)** `WINDOW_AVG(SUM([Sales]), -1, 0)` — a 2-month trailing average within each year partition. (2 Marks)
- **(iii)** `RANK(SUM([Sales]), 'desc')` — descending rank within each year partition. (2 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Table Calculations Theory:**

**Definition:** A Table Calculation is a transformation applied to the *visible* result set of a Tableau view, after the data has been aggregated by the rows/columns in the view.

- **Partitioning Fields:** Define *separate tables* of calculation. The calculation restarts for each new partition. In the View, partitioning fields are placed **before** the dotted separator line.
- **Addressing Fields:** Define the *direction* of traversal inside each partition. In the View, addressing fields are placed **after** the dotted separator line.

**Diagram (described):**
In a Tableau view showing `[Year]` on Columns and `[Month]` on Rows, dragging the green pill of the calculated field to the left of the vertical line makes it a *partitioning* field (Year becomes a wall between partitions). Dragging it to the right makes it an *addressing* field (the calculation walks through months).

**Four Table Calculation Functions:**

1. `RUNNING_SUM(expression)` — cumulative sum across addressing direction.
2. `WINDOW_AVG(expression, start, end)` — moving average over a row window.
3. `RANK(expression, 'asc' or 'desc')` — ordinal ranking within partition.
4. `INDEX()` — row position within partition.

**(b) — Tabular Solutions:**

**(i) Running Sum (partitioned by Year):**

For Year 2023:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Jan: } & 1000 \\
\text{Feb: } & 1000 + 1200 = 2200 \\
\text{Mar: } & 2200 + 1500 = 3700 \\
\text{Apr: } & 3700 + 1800 = 5500
\end{aligned}
$$

For Year 2024:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Jan: } & 2000 \\
\text{Feb: } & 2000 + 2200 = 4200 \\
\text{Mar: } & 4200 + 2500 = 6700 \\
\text{Apr: } & 6700 + 2800 = 9500
\end{aligned}
$$

**Derived Table (Running Sum):**

| Month | Year | Sales | Running Sum |
|---|---|---|---|
| Jan | 2023 | 1000 | 1000 |
| Feb | 2023 | 1200 | 2200 |
| Mar | 2023 | 1500 | 3700 |
| Apr | 2023 | 1800 | 5500 |
| Jan | 2024 | 2000 | 2000 |
| Feb | 2024 | 2200 | 4200 |
| Mar | 2024 | 2500 | 6700 |
| Apr | 2024 | 2800 | 9500 |

**(ii) WINDOW_AVG with start=-1, end=0 (2-month trailing average):**

$$
\text{Window}_i = \frac{\text{Sales}_{i-1} + \text{Sales}_i}{2}
$$

For Year 2023:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Jan (boundary): } & \frac{0 + 1000}{2} = 500 \\
\text{Feb: } & \frac{1000 + 1200}{2} = 1100 \\
\text{Mar: } & \frac{1200 + 1500}{2} = 1350 \\
\text{Apr: } & \frac{1500 + 1800}{2} = 1650
\end{aligned}
$$

For Year 2024:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Jan (boundary): } & \frac{0 + 2000}{2} = 1000 \\
\text{Feb: } & \frac{2000 + 2200}{2} = 2100 \\
\text{Mar: } & \frac{2200 + 2500}{2} = 2350 \\
\text{Apr: } & \frac{2500 + 2800}{2} = 2650
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> At the **first row of each partition**, the `WINDOW_AVG(-1, 0)` only has one value (the current row), so the result is simply `[Sales] / 2`. This boundary behaviour is frequently tested by KTU examiners.

**(iii) RANK (descending) within Year partition:**

For Year 2023, sales sorted descending are 1800, 1500, 1200, 1000 — therefore ranks are 1, 2, 3, 4.

For Year 2024, sales sorted descending are 2800, 2500, 2200, 2000 — therefore ranks are 1, 2, 3, 4.

**Derived Table (Final Result combining all three):**

| Month | Year | Sales | Running Sum | Window Avg | Rank |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Jan | 2023 | 1000 | 1000 | 500 | 4 |
| Feb | 2023 | 1200 | 2200 | 1100 | 3 |
| Mar | 2023 | 1500 | 3700 | 1350 | 2 |
| Apr | 2023 | 1800 | 5500 | 1650 | 1 |
| Jan | 2024 | 2000 | 2000 | 1000 | 4 |
| Feb | 2024 | 2200 | 4200 | 2100 | 3 |
| Mar | 2024 | 2500 | 6700 | 2350 | 2 |
| Apr | 2024 | 2800 | 9500 | 2650 | 1 |

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Tableau Calculations (KTU 2024):**
>
> 1. **Confusing FIXED with INCLUDE/EXCLUDE:** `FIXED` ignores view dimensions entirely; `INCLUDE` and `EXCLUDE` interact with the view. Students frequently write `FIXED` when they mean `INCLUDE` and lose **2–3 marks**.
> 2. **Wrong aggregation default:** A numeric Calculated Field is dragged into the view and Tableau auto-applies `SUM`. For ratios like Profit Margin, summing ratios is meaningless. Use `AVG` or pre-aggregate with an LOD.
> 3. **Forgetting parentheses:** Operator precedence in Tableau is left-to-right. `A - B / C` ≠ `(A - B) / C`.
> 4. **Window boundary miscalculation:** For `WINDOW_AVG(-1, 0)`, students often write the formula for a 3-row window by mistake, misreading the offset.
> 5. **Misuse of `COUNT` vs `COUNTD`:** `COUNT` counts rows; `COUNTD` counts distinct values — an easy 1-mark error in Part A.
> 6. **LOD syntax errors:** Forgetting the curly braces `{ }` or the colon `:` in LOD expressions is an instant 2-mark deduction.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Three families:** Calculated Field (row-level) | Table Calculation (view-level) | LOD Expression (custom granularity).
- [x] **Calculated Fields** use **square brackets** for field references and **single quotes** for string literals.
- [x] **Table Calculations** are defined by **Partitioning** (independence) and **Addressing** (direction).
- [x] **`FIXED` LODs** are evaluated *before* dimension filters; **`INCLUDE`** and **`EXCLUDE`** are evaluated *after* dimension filters.
- [x] **`WINDOW_*(expr, start, end)`** defines a row window; `start` is negative offsets to past rows, `end=0` includes the current row.
- [x] **`RUNNING_SUM`** is a special case of `WINDOW_SUM` with `start = -∞` and `end = 0`.
- [x] **`TOTAL(expr)`** divides the current value by the entire partition's total — used for `% of Total`.
- [x] **`LOOKUP(expr, offset)`** retrieves the value `n` rows away — used for `YoY Growth`.
- [x] **Numeric constants** are written without quotes; **string constants** require single quotes.
- [x] **Aggregation functions** in Tableau: `SUM`, `AVG`, `MIN`, `MAX`, `COUNT`, `COUNTD`, `MEDIAN`, `STDEV`, `VAR`.
- [x] **Default aggregation** for a numeric measure dragged to Rows/Columns is **`SUM`** (Automatic).
- [x] **`COUNTD`** is computationally expensive; use it only when distinctness matters.
- [x] For **Year-over-Year Growth**, the standard formula is: $\frac{\text{Current} - \text{Previous}}{\text{Previous}}$, where `Previous = LOOKUP(SUM([Measure]), -1)`.
- [x] For **Top-N filtering**, combine `RANK()` with a filter on `RANK() <= N`.
- [x] **K.T.U. Answer-Writing Tip:** Always state the *granularity* and *family* of the calculation in the first line of any 7-mark answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sort and Filters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT522 - BUSINESS ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Sort and Filters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Business Analytics (PEADT522)** and KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education, **Sort** and **Filter** are two of the most fundamental **Data Preparation** (or *Data Wrangling*) operations performed on a structured dataset before any descriptive, diagnostic, predictive, or prescriptive analysis can occur.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sort (Ordering of Data):**
> A non-destructive tabular transformation operation that rearranges the rows (records) of a dataset in a **meaningful sequence** (ascending or descending) based on the values of one or more specified attribute columns (keys). The number of rows and the underlying values remain untouched — only their physical/logical order changes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Filter (Subset Selection of Data):**
> A row-extraction operation that temporarily **hides** (or excludes) records from a dataset that do not satisfy a user-defined logical condition (criterion), thereby displaying only the *relevant subset* of data. Filtering is governed by Boolean logic through the operators **AND**, **OR**, and **NOT**.

Both operations fall under the **"Get & Transform" / "Power Query"** category in modern BI tools such as Microsoft Excel, Power BI, Tableau, and SQL-based data warehouses.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a large **college library** where thousands of books are dumped in a single unsorted pile.

- **Sorting** is equivalent to the librarian arranging those books on shelves — first alphabetically by **Author**, then within the same author by **Title**, and then by **Year of Publication**. The total *number* of books does not change; only their *order* changes so that a reader can find a book effortlessly.
- **Filtering** is equivalent to a student walking into the library and saying, *"Show me only the books published after **2022**, AND belonging to the **Data Science** subject."* The other books are not destroyed; they are simply *temporarily hidden* from view because they don't match the requested criteria.

In business terms: **Sorting** answers *"What is the ranking?"* (e.g., top 10 customers by revenue), while **Filtering** answers *"What subset is relevant?"* (e.g., only customers from Kerala).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 PEADT522 – Module 3):**
> Sorting and Filtering are prerequisites for advanced operations such as **Pivot Tables**, **VLOOKUP/INDEX-MATCH**, **Data Visualization**, and **Statistical Aggregation**. Mastery of these is tested both theoretically (definition, types, operators) and practically (Excel/SQL screenshots in lab exams).

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Terminology

- **Sort Key:** The column whose values dictate the new row order.
- **Primary Key & Secondary Key (Multi-level Sort):** When sorting by more than one column.
- **Ascending Order (ASC):** Default — Smallest $\to$ Largest; A $\to$ Z; Earliest $\to$ Latest date.
- **Descending Order (DESC):** Largest $\to$ Smallest; Z $\to$ A; Latest $\to$ Earliest date.
- **Filter Criterion / Predicate:** A Boolean expression evaluating to TRUE or FALSE for each row.
- **AutoFilter vs. Advanced Filter (Excel terminology):** Single-condition vs. multi-condition filtering.
- **Slicer (Power BI/Excel):** A visual, clickable filter button.
- **Wildcards:** `*` (any number of characters), `?` (single character), `~` (escape character) — used in text filters.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Sorting Operations

1. **Single-Level (Simple) Sort**
   - Sorting the dataset using only **one** column.
   - Example: Sort the *Sales* table by `Revenue` in descending order to find the highest-grossing product.

2. **Multi-Level (Compound / Nested) Sort**
   - Sorting using **two or more** columns in a defined priority order.
   - Example: First sort by `Region` (Ascending), then within each region sort by `Profit` (Descending).

3. **Custom Sort (User-Defined Order)**
   - Sorting based on a manually defined sequence, not alphabetical/numerical.
   - Example: Days of the week as Monday, Tuesday, …, Sunday (not alphabetical).
   - Example: Sizes as Small, Medium, Large, XL (not alphabetical which would give Large, Medium, Small, XL).

4. **Color-Based / Icon-Based Sort (Excel Conditional Formatting tie-in)**
   - Sorting by the cell color or icon set applied via Conditional Formatting.

### 2.2 Taxonomy of Filtering Operations

1. **AutoFilter (Simple Filter)**
   - One-column, one-condition filtering through dropdown checkboxes.
   - Operators per data type:
     - **Text:** Equals, Begins With, Contains, Ends With.
     - **Number:** Equals, Greater Than, Less Than, Between, Top 10.
     - **Date:** Before, After, Between, Tomorrow, Today, Yesterday, Next/Last Week/Month/Quarter/Year.

2. **Advanced Filter (Criteria-Range Filter)**
   - Allows **multiple AND/OR conditions** across multiple columns.
   - A separate *Criteria Range* is defined with field headers matching the data range.
   - Rows placed in the **same line** of the criteria range imply **AND**; rows in **different lines** imply **OR**.

3. **Custom Filter (Top 10 / Dynamic)**
   - Used for ranking-based filtering: *Top 10 products by sales*, *Bottom 5% by profit*.
   - In SQL: `ORDER BY … LIMIT n OFFSET m`.

4. **Slicer / Visual Filter (BI Tools)**
   - Clickable UI elements in Power BI, Excel Pivot Tables, and Tableau.

5. **Wildcard Filter (Pattern Matching)**
   - Used to match partial text patterns.

### 2.3 KTU Formula / Logic Sheet — Boolean Filter Evaluation

For a row $R$ to be displayed after filtering, the criterion $C(R)$ must evaluate to TRUE.

$$
C(R) = \begin{cases}
\text{TRUE} & \text{Row is displayed (visible)} \\
\text{FALSE} & \text{Row is hidden (filtered out)}
\end{cases}
$$

For multiple conditions connected by Boolean operators, the composite predicate is:

$$
C_{\text{composite}}(R) = (P_1 \land P_2 \land \dots \land P_n) \quad \lor \quad (Q_1 \land Q_2 \land \dots \land Q_m)
$$

where $P_i$ are predicates in row 1 of the criteria range (joined by AND) and $Q_j$ are predicates in row 2 of the criteria range (joined by AND), with the two rows joined by OR.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wildcard Equivalence Table (Cross-Tool Reference):**

| Wildcard Symbol | Meaning | Excel | SQL | Power BI (DAX) |
| :--- | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| `*` | Zero or more characters | ✓ | `%` (in SQL) | `*` |
| `?` | Exactly one character | ✓ | `_` (in SQL) | `?` |
| `~` | Escape character | ✓ | `ESCAPE` | — |
| `[ ]` | Any one character in set | ✓ | `[ ]` | — |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Business Analytics

- **Sales Analytics:** Sort transactions by revenue, filter only those above ₹10,000.
- **HR Analytics:** Sort employees by joining date, filter those in the *Operations* department.
- **Finance:** Sort mutual funds by 1-year return, filter for low-risk (debt) category.
- **Supply Chain:** Filter SKUs with stock-out incidents in the last 30 days.
- **Marketing:** Multi-level sort by *Campaign* then *Conversion Rate* to identify top-performing channels.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Multi-Level Sort — Step-by-Step Logic

Consider a sales dataset with three columns: `Region`, `Salesperson`, and `Revenue`.

**Objective:** Sort first by `Region` (Ascending), then by `Revenue` (Descending).

**Step-by-Step Algorithm:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Step 1: Identify the primary sort key } K_1 = \text{Region} \\
&\text{Step 2: Identify the secondary sort key } K_2 = \text{Revenue} \\
&\text{Step 3: For each row } R_i, \text{ compute the sort tuple } T_i = (K_1(R_i), K_2(R_i)) \\
&\text{Step 4: Sort the rows lexicographically using } T_i \\
&\text{Step 5: Within equal } K_1 \text{ values, tie-break using } K_2 \text{ in DESC order}
\end{aligned}
$$

> Lexicographic ordering means: compare $K_1$ first; if $K_1$ values are equal, then compare $K_2$.

### 3.2 Worked Example — Multi-Level Sort

| Original Row | Region | Salesperson | Revenue (₹) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| R1 | South | Anil | 50,000 |
| R2 | North | Balu | 75,000 |
| R3 | South | Ceena | 90,000 |
| R4 | North | Divya | 60,000 |
| R5 | South | Esha | 90,000 |

After applying **Region ASC, Revenue DESC, Salesperson ASC** (three-level sort):

| Sorted Row | Region | Salesperson | Revenue (₹) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| S1 | North | Balu | 75,000 |
| S2 | North | Divya | 60,000 |
| S3 | South | Ceena | 90,000 |
| S4 | South | Esha | 90,000 |
| S5 | South | Anil | 50,000 |

**Explanation of transitions:**

- Rows R2 & R4 both belong to *North*. Among them, R2 (₹75,000) > R4 (₹60,000), so R2 comes first.
- Rows R1, R3, R5 all belong to *South*. Among them, R3 = R5 = ₹90,000. The tie-breaker is Salesperson ASC, so Ceena (C) < Esha (E) — R3 precedes R5. Then Anil (₹50,000) comes last.

### 3.3 SQL Equivalent (for KTU Lab Exam Reference)

The same multi-level sort in SQL is expressed as:

```sql
SELECT Region, Salesperson, Revenue
FROM Sales
ORDER BY Region ASC, Revenue DESC, Salesperson ASC;
```

### 3.4 Advanced Filter — Step-by-Step Logic

Suppose the criterion is: **"Region = 'South' AND Revenue > 60,000"**, OR **"Salesperson begins with 'B'"**.

**Step 1:** Build the Criteria Range with two header rows matching data column names.

| Region | Revenue | Salesperson |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| South | >60000 |  |
|  |  | B* |

**Step 2:** Apply the Boolean evaluation row-by-row.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{For each row } R: \quad \text{Display} \iff & \Big[\,(\text{Region} = \text{South}) \land (\text{Revenue} > 60000)\,\Big] \\
& \lor \Big[\,\text{Salesperson matches pattern } B*\,\Big]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Result of applying the filter on the previous sorted dataset:

| Row | Region | Salesperson | Revenue | Predicate Evaluation | Display? |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| S1 | North | Balu | 75,000 | FALSE $\lor$ TRUE (Balu begins with B) | ✓ Yes |
| S2 | North | Divya | 60,000 | FALSE $\lor$ FALSE | ✗ No |
| S3 | South | Ceena | 90,000 | TRUE $\land$ TRUE = TRUE | ✓ Yes |
| S4 | South | Esha | 90,000 | TRUE $\land$ TRUE = TRUE | ✓ Yes |
| S5 | South | Anil | 50,000 | TRUE $\land$ FALSE = FALSE | ✗ No |

**Visible output:** Rows S1, S3, S4 only — a 3-row filtered dataset.

### 3.5 Sort Stability — Important Analytical Property

> [!NOTE]
> **Stable Sort Definition:** A sort algorithm is *stable* if two rows with **equal** sort keys retain their **original relative order** after sorting. Excel's built-in sort and SQL's `ORDER BY` (in most RDBMS) are **stable sorts**. This matters when performing multi-level sorts where the secondary key is omitted — original row order is preserved for tied primary key values.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Sort Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Unstructured Dataset] --> B[Identify Sort Keys K1, K2 ... Kn]
    B --> C{Choose Sort Order}
    C -->|ASC| D1[Ascending Engine]
    C -->|DESC| D2[Descending Engine]
    C -->|Custom| D3[Custom List Engine]
    D1 --> E[Apply Lexicographic Comparison]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[Stable Reordering of Rows]
    F --> G[Sorted Output Dataset]
    G --> H[Downstream: Pivot / Chart / Aggregation]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Filter Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Dataset] --> B[Define Criteria Range]
    B --> C{Filter Type}
    C -->|AutoFilter| D1[Single Column Dropdown]
    C -->|Advanced Filter| D2[Multi-Condition Criteria]
    C -->|Custom / Top N| D3[Ranking Based Predicate]
    C -->|Slicer / Visual| D4[Interactive UI Filter]
    D1 --> E[Apply Boolean Predicate]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> F{For each row, evaluate TRUE FALSE}
    F -->|TRUE| G1[Row Visible]
    F -->|FALSE| G2[Row Hidden]
    G1 --> H[Filtered Output Subset]
    G2 --> H
    H --> I[Downstream Analysis: Reporting / KPI]
```

### 4.3 Combined Sort-Filter Workflow (Most Common Real-World Pipeline)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Step 1: Import Raw Data] --> P2[Step 2: Remove Duplicates]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Apply Filter]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: Apply Sort]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5: Conditional Formatting]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6: Aggregate / Pivot]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7: Visualize Dashboard]
```

### 4.4 Decision Matrix: Sort vs. Filter

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Need to rearrange row order?} -->|Yes| S[Use SORT]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Need to show only some rows?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| F[Use FILTER]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Need to count or sum specific values?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| A[Use AGGREGATE or SUMIF]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 1]**
*Define the term "Multi-Level Sort" in the context of business analytics data preparation. Give one example.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Multi-Level Sort** (also called *compound sort* or *nested sort*) is a data preparation operation that arranges the rows of a dataset in a meaningful sequence based on **two or more columns** used in a defined priority order. The primary key dictates the major grouping; the secondary key serves as a tie-breaker within equal primary-key values; further keys (tertiary, etc.) resolve ties progressively.

**Example:** In a sales dataset, sorting first by `Region` (ASC) and then by `Revenue` (DESC) within each region, so that for every region the top-revenue salesperson appears first.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Split (KTU Pattern):** Definition – 2 marks; Example – 1 mark.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
*Explain the difference between AutoFilter and Advanced Filter in MS Excel.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | AutoFilter | Advanced Filter |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Conditions | Single column, single condition (or simple multi-select) | Multiple columns, multiple AND/OR conditions |
| Setup | Dropdown arrows on column headers | Separate criteria range with field headers |
| Use Case | Quick filtering | Complex, multi-criteria business queries |
| Boolean Logic | Limited | Full AND, OR, NOT support |

AutoFilter is suitable for rapid, one-click filtering of a single column (e.g., show all rows where Region = "South"). Advanced Filter is used when the user needs to combine multiple criteria, such as *Region = "South" AND Revenue > 50,000* OR *Salesperson starts with "A"*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Split (KTU Pattern):** Tabular comparison – 2 marks; Example – 1 mark.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

**Question A. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** With a suitable business scenario, explain the process of performing a **Three-Level Custom Sort** in MS Excel. Mention all menu paths and the role of each sort key. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **Advanced Filter** feature of MS Excel. How does the placement of criteria in the criteria range determine the Boolean logic? Illustrate with one example. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — Three-Level Custom Sort

**Scenario:** A retail company wants to rank its 200 sales employees for a quarterly award. The dataset has columns: `Region` (North/South/East/West), `Department` (Electronics/Grocery/Apparel), and `Revenue` (numerical).

**Process (Excel 2021/365):**

1. Click anywhere inside the data range.
2. Navigate to: **Data Tab $\to$ Sort & Filter group $\to$ Sort**.
3. The **Sort dialog box** opens. Under *My data has headers*, check the box.
4. **Sort by:** `Region` — Order: **Custom List** $\to$ North, South, East, West (user-defined order, not alphabetical).
5. **Then by:** `Department` — Order: **A to Z** (alphabetical default).
6. **Then by:** `Revenue` — Order: **Largest to Smallest** (descending numerical).

**Role of Each Sort Key:**

- **Primary Key (`Region`):** Groups employees region-wise. The *Custom List* ensures North comes first irrespective of alphabetical order (which would put East first).
- **Secondary Key (`Department`):** Within each region, groups by department alphabetically.
- **Tertiary Key (`Revenue`):** Within each (Region, Department) pair, ranks employees by revenue from highest to lowest — surfacing the top performer for the award.

**Significance:** Multi-level sorting enables **granular ranking** which is impossible with single-key sorting, and is the backbone of **Top-N analysis** in dashboards.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Split (KTU Pattern):** Scenario – 1 mark; Steps (with menu paths) – 3 marks; Role of each key – 3 marks.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — Advanced Filter with Boolean Logic

**Definition (2 marks):** Advanced Filter is a powerful Excel feature under **Data $\to$ Sort & Filter $\to$ Advanced** that allows users to extract rows that satisfy **complex, multi-column conditions** using a separate criteria range.

**Boolean Logic Rule (3 marks):**

- Criteria written on the **same row** of the criteria range are joined by **AND**.
- Criteria written on **different rows** of the criteria range are joined by **OR**.

**Example (2 marks):**

Criterion: *Region = "South" AND Revenue > 60000*, OR *Salesperson begins with "B"*.

Criteria Range:

| Region | Revenue | Salesperson |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| South | >60000 |  |
|  |  | B* |

**Result:** The filter evaluates each row using:

$$
\text{Keep row} \iff \big[(\text{Region}=\text{South}) \land (\text{Revenue}>60000)\big] \lor \big[\text{Salesperson LIKE } B*\big]
$$

Rows satisfying the composite predicate are extracted (or copied to another location, if the *Copy to another location* option is used).

---

**Question B. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 3, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Compare and contrast **Sort** and **Filter** operations across nine parameters: purpose, data alteration, output row count, dependence on criteria, syntax, tools supporting it, effect on visualization, reversibility, and use in SQL. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following sales table. Perform the following tasks and show the result of each: **(7 marks)**

| OrderID | Customer | Region | Product | Quantity | UnitPrice | OrderDate |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| O101 | Ravi | North | Laptop | 2 | 50000 | 12-Jan-2024 |
| O102 | Anu | South | Mobile | 5 | 20000 | 15-Jan-2024 |
| O103 | Ravi | North | Tablet | 3 | 30000 | 18-Jan-2024 |
| O104 | Meera | South | Laptop | 1 | 50000 | 22-Jan-2024 |
| O105 | Anu | South | Mobile | 4 | 20000 | 25-Jan-2024 |
| O106 | Kiran | East | Laptop | 2 | 50000 | 28-Jan-2024 |

**Tasks:**
- (i) Sort the table by `Region` ASC, then by `OrderDate` ASC.
- (ii) Filter the original table to show only rows where `Region = South` AND `Quantity $\geq 4`.
- (iii) Write the SQL query to perform task (ii) using `WHERE` and `AND`.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — Comparison Table (7 marks)

| Parameter | Sort | Filter |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Purpose** | Rearrange rows in a meaningful order | Display only rows meeting a criterion |
| **Data Alteration** | Reorders rows; values unchanged | Hides rows; values unchanged |
| **Output Row Count** | Same as input (all rows preserved) | $\leq$ input (subset only) |
| **Dependence on Criteria** | Optional (default ASC) | Mandatory |
| **Syntax (Excel)** | `Data $\to$ Sort` | `Data $\to$ Filter` |
| **Tools** | Excel, SQL, Power BI, Tableau, Python | Excel, SQL, Power BI, Tableau, Python |
| **Effect on Visualization** | Re-orders chart categories | Limits the data points plotted |
| **Reversibility** | Reversible via Undo / re-sort | Reversible via *Clear Filter* |
| **SQL Equivalent** | `ORDER BY column ASC/DESC` | `WHERE condition` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Split (KTU Pattern):** Any 7 well-explained parameters – 7 marks (1 mark each).

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — Tasks (7 marks)

**(i) Sort by Region ASC, then by OrderDate ASC (3 marks):**

| OrderID | Customer | Region | Product | Quantity | UnitPrice | OrderDate |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| O106 | Kiran | East | Laptop | 2 | 50000 | 28-Jan-2024 |
| O101 | Ravi | North | Laptop | 2 | 50000 | 12-Jan-2024 |
| O103 | Ravi | North | Tablet | 3 | 30000 | 18-Jan-2024 |
| O102 | Anu | South | Mobile | 5 | 20000 | 15-Jan-2024 |
| O105 | Anu | South | Mobile | 4 | 20000 | 25-Jan-2024 |
| O104 | Meera | South | Laptop | 1 | 50000 | 22-Jan-2024 |

*Explanation:* East comes first (E < N < S). Within North, 12-Jan precedes 18-Jan. Within South, 15-Jan, 22-Jan, 25-Jan are already in chronological order.

**(ii) Filter Region = South AND Quantity $\geq 4$ (2 marks):**

| OrderID | Customer | Region | Product | Quantity | UnitPrice | OrderDate |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| O102 | Anu | South | Mobile | 5 | 20000 | 15-Jan-2024 |
| O105 | Anu | South | Mobile | 4 | 20000 | 25-Jan-2024 |

*O104 (Meera, South, Qty=1) is excluded because Quantity $< 4$.*

**(iii) SQL Query (2 marks):**

```sql
SELECT OrderID, Customer, Region, Product, Quantity, UnitPrice, OrderDate
FROM Sales
WHERE Region = 'South' AND Quantity >= 4
ORDER BY OrderDate ASC;
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing AND/OR placement** in the Advanced Filter criteria range is the most common mistake. *Same row = AND; Different rows = OR.* Examiners deduct up to 2 marks for this.
> 2. **Forgetting to tick "My data has headers"** in the Sort dialog causes the first data row to be used as the sort key — a costly error.
> 3. In SQL, **`ORDER BY` and `WHERE` are independent clauses** — students often mistakenly place `ORDER BY` before `WHERE`, which is a syntax error.
> 4. **Wildcard mismatch** — using `*` in SQL (which requires `%`) is a frequent lose-mark scenario.
> 5. Always **state units and specify ASC/DESC explicitly** in sort answers; vague "ascending" answers without showing the sorted table lose 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Sort** = Reorder rows; **Filter** = Hide non-matching rows. Both are **non-destructive**.
- ✅ Default sort order in every tool is **Ascending (ASC)**.
- ✅ In a **Multi-Level Sort**, the *Primary Key* has highest priority; tie-breakers follow in order.
- ✅ **Stable sort** preserves original order of tied-key rows.
- ✅ **Custom Sort** uses a user-defined list (e.g., Mon, Tue, Wed, …, Sun; or Small, Medium, Large, XL).
- ✅ In **AutoFilter**: works column-by-column with simple conditions.
- ✅ In **Advanced Filter**: criteria range with field headers — **same row = AND**, **different row = OR**.
- ✅ Wildcards: `*` (any number of characters), `?` (single character), `~` (escape).
- ✅ **SQL equivalents:** `ORDER BY` for sorting; `WHERE` for filtering; `LIMIT` for top-N.
- ✅ Always combine with **Remove Duplicates** *before* sort/filter to get accurate aggregates.
- ✅ Menu paths in Excel: `Data $\to$ Sort & Filter group $\to$ Sort / Filter / Advanced`.
- ✅ Sort and Filter are **prerequisites** for Pivot Tables, VLOOKUP, Conditional Formatting, and Dashboards.
- ✅ Performance tip: Sort large datasets only on **indexed/numeric** columns to avoid lag.
- ✅ The composite predicate for a multi-condition filter is built using Boolean algebra: $(\bigwedge P_i) \lor (\bigwedge Q_j)$.
- ✅ In KTU exams, **always present the result of sort/filter as a small table** to earn full method marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Working with Charts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT522 - BUSINESS ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Working with Charts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Business Analytics (PEADT522)**, a **chart** is defined as a *symbolic, two-dimensional graphical representation of quantitative business data* in which the data is encoded using visual primitives such as position, length, area, angle, color, and shape. Charts are the **core analytical artifacts** used in the **Data Visualization** layer of the Business Intelligence (BI) stack, sitting between raw data tables and executive decision-making.

Under the KTU 2024 Scheme Outcome-Based Education framework, working with charts satisfies the following Course Outcomes:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Course Outcome Mapping (PEADT522)**
> - **CO3** — *Apply appropriate visualization techniques to represent business data and support managerial decision-making.*
> - **CO4** — *Interpret analytical outputs to derive actionable business insights.*

The **International Organization for Standardization (ISO)** standard **ISO 9241-210** and visualization literature (Few, 2009; Tufte, 1983) classify charts into **comparative, compositional, relational, and temporal** categories based on the analytical question they answer.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box — The "Chart as a Translator" Analogy**
>
> Imagine you are at a busy Kochius international airport listening to announcements in five different languages. You cannot understand the raw audio, but a *visual flight information display* (the chart) tells you instantly: *Gate 4, Flight Emirates 502, 10:30, ON TIME*. That display is the **translator** between *raw operational data* and *your decision* (which gate to walk to).
>
> A **chart in business analytics is exactly that translator**. The Excel cell `=B2*C2` is raw audio; the bar chart that pops up is the gate-display. The CFO cannot read 10,000 rows of monthly revenue — but a single line chart tells her whether the business is climbing, falling, or flat.
>
> **Memorize this:** *If a chart cannot be understood in **5 seconds**, it has failed its job.*

---

### 1.3 Why Charts Matter in Business Analytics

| Business Need | Role of Chart |
|---|---|
| **Trend identification** | Detects seasonality, growth, decline over time |
| **Outlier detection** | Spots anomalies faster than spreadsheet scanning |
| **Composition analysis** | Shows part-to-whole contribution of categories |
| **Comparative evaluation** | Ranks regions, products, salespersons side-by-side |
| **Correlation discovery** | Reveals relationships between KPIs |
| **Storytelling to stakeholders** | Communicates findings to non-technical boards |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Students are expected to *create, customize, and interpret* charts in spreadsheet/BI tools such as **Microsoft Excel, Google Sheets, Power BI, or Tableau**, and to justify **chart-type selection** based on the **data type** and the **analytical question**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Chart — The 7 Building Blocks

Every professional chart in business analytics is composed of **seven structural elements**. Missing any one reduces analytical clarity and costs marks in the KTU valuation.

1. **Chart Title** — Answers *"What is this chart about?"* in ≤ 10 words.
2. **X-Axis (Category Axis)** — Independent variable (time, region, product).
3. **Y-Axis (Value Axis)** — Dependent variable (revenue, count, percentage).
4. **Data Series** — The plotted values (e.g., Sales 2023, Sales 2024).
5. **Legend** — Decodes colors/shapes to series names.
6. **Data Labels** — Exact values displayed on marks (use sparingly).
7. **Gridlines / Source Note** — Aids reading precision; source citation is mandatory in academic/business charts.

---

### 2.2 Taxonomy of Charts (KTU Mandatory Coverage)

#### A. Comparison Charts
| Chart Type | Best Use Case | Data Type | Example Business Question |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Column / Vertical Bar** | Compare discrete categories | Categorical vs Numerical | "Which store had highest Q1 sales?" |
| **Horizontal Bar** | Long category labels | Categorical vs Numerical | "Rank top 15 SKUs by profit" |
| **Grouped / Clustered Bar** | Compare 2–4 series across categories | Multi-series Categorical | "Sales vs Profit per region" |
| **Stacked Bar** | Show composition + total | Categorical vs Numerical | "Total revenue split by product line" |

#### B. Composition Charts
| Chart Type | Best Use Case | Data Type | Caveat |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pie Chart** | Parts of a whole (≤ 6 slices) | Categorical | Avoid if slices < 5% |
| **Doughnut** | Like pie + center KPI | Categorical | Same pie rules apply |
| **Stacked Area** | Composition changing over time | Time-series | Use 100% stacked for % |
| **Waterfall** | Running total breakdown | Numerical | Shows positive/negative contributions |
| **Treemap** | Hierarchical composition | Categorical | Replaces pie for > 6 categories |

#### C. Relationship Charts
| Chart Type | Best Use Case | Data Type | Statistical Backbone |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Scatter Plot** | Two numerical variables | Numerical vs Numerical | Pearson correlation *r* |
| **Bubble Chart** | 3 variables (x, y, size) | Numerical | Adds weighted dimension |
| **Heatmap** | Density across 2 dimensions | Matrix data | Conditional formatting |

#### D. Distribution Charts
| Chart Type | Best Use Case | Data Type | Statistical Backbone |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Histogram** | Frequency of bins | Numerical | Binning rules (Sturges, Freedman-Diaconis) |
| **Box Plot** | 5-number summary | Numerical | Median, Q1, Q3, whiskers |
| **Violin Plot** | Distribution shape | Numerical | Kernel density |

#### E. Temporal (Time-Series) Charts
| Chart Type | Best Use Case | Data Type | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Line Chart** | Continuous trend | Time vs Numerical | Default for time-series |
| **Area Chart** | Magnitude of trend | Time vs Numerical | Line + fill |
| **Candlestick** | OHLC financial data | Time + 4 prices | Stock market analysis |
| **Gantt Chart** | Project timelines | Time ranges | Project management |

#### F. Advanced / Specialized Charts
| Chart Type | Best Use Case |
|---|---|
| **Radar / Spider** | Multi-attribute performance (e.g., 5 KPIs) |
| **Funnel** | Sales pipeline stages, conversion rates |
| **Sankey** | Flow between states (e.g., customer journey) |
| **Pareto** | 80/20 analysis — combines bar + cumulative line |

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are examinable for numerical sub-parts in Part B (14-mark) questions.**

$$
\text{Percentage Change} \;=\; \frac{\text{New Value} - \text{Old Value}}{\text{Old Value}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Compound Annual Growth Rate (CAGR)} \;=\; \left(\frac{V_{\text{final}}}{V_{\text{initial}}}\right)^{\frac{1}{n}} - 1
$$

$$
\text{Cumulative Frequency}_{\text{bin } k} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{k} f_i
$$

$$
\text{Sturges' Rule (Number of Histogram Bins)} \;=\; 1 + 3.322 \cdot \log_{10}(N)
$$

$$
\text{Pearson Correlation Coefficient} \;=\; r \;=\; \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum(x_i - \bar{x})^2 \cdot \sum(y_i - \bar{y})^2}}
$$

$$
\text{Z-Score (Outlier Detection)} \;=\; Z_i \;=\; \frac{x_i - \mu}{\sigma}, \quad \vert Z \vert > 3 \Rightarrow \text{Outlier}
$$

$$
\text{Data-Ink Ratio (Tufte)} \;=\; \frac{\text{Ink used for data}}{\text{Total ink on chart}}
$$

$$
\text{Pareto Threshold} \;=\; 0.80 \cdot \text{Total Cumulative Value}
$$

### 2.4 Chart Selection Decision Rules (Engineering Logic)

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three-Question Rule for Chart Selection**
> 1. **How many variables am I plotting?** → 1 var = histogram/pie; 2 vars = bar/line/scatter; 3+ vars = bubble/radar/heatmap.
> 2. **What is my data type?** → Categorical = bar/pie; Numerical = histogram/scatter; Time = line/area.
> 3. **What is my analytical question?** → Compare = bar; Compose = pie/stacked; Relate = scatter; Trend = line; Distribute = histogram/box.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering / Business Utility

- **Retail Analytics:** Heatmaps of store layouts vs footfall.
- **Supply Chain:** Line charts of inventory turnover vs time.
- **Finance:** Candlestick charts for equity technical analysis.
- **HR Analytics:** Box plots of salary distribution across departments.
- **Healthcare Operations:** Gantt charts for surgery scheduling.
- **Manufacturing:** Pareto charts for defect-cause prioritization (Six Sigma DMAIC).
