## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Workflow: Building a Professional Chart

This is the **canonical 8-step procedure** that KTU examiners expect for any "create a chart" sub-question.

**Step 1 — Define the Analytical Question.**
Write one sentence: *"Which product category contributed most to the Q3 revenue decline?"*

**Step 2 — Identify Data Type and Variables.**
Categorical (Product) + Numerical (Revenue) + Time (Q3). Hence 2 categorical + 1 numerical.

**Step 3 — Choose the Chart Type.**
Apply the Three-Question Rule: comparing categories over time → **Clustered Column Chart** or **Line Chart with markers**.

**Step 4 — Prepare the Data Layout.**
Use a **long-format table** (Tidy Data Principle — Wickham, 2014):

| Quarter | Product Category | Revenue (₹ Lakhs) |
|---|---|---|
| Q1 | Electronics | 120 |
| Q1 | Apparel | 80 |
| Q2 | Electronics | 135 |
| Q2 | Apparel | 75 |
| Q3 | Electronics | 110 |
| Q3 | Apparel | 60 |

**Step 5 — Insert the Chart (Excel/Sheets/Power BI).**
Select the table → *Insert → Chart → Clustered Column* → verify axes swap.

**Step 6 — Apply the 7 Anatomy Elements.**
- Title: *"Q3 Revenue Decline — Electronics vs Apparel (₹ Lakhs)"*
- X-axis label: *"Quarter"*
- Y-axis label: *"Revenue (₹ Lakhs)"*
- Legend: bottom-center.
- Data labels: on top of bars.
- Source note: *"Source: Internal Sales Ledger, FY 2024–25."*

**Step 7 — Apply Tufte's Data-Ink Principle.**
Remove: heavy gridlines, 3D effects, gradients, unnecessary borders.
Add: clear fonts (Calibri/Arial 11pt), consistent color palette.

**Step 8 — Validate and Annotate.**
Highlight the Q3 Electronics bar with a callout: *"20% drop vs Q2 — investigate supply chain."*

---

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example — Pareto Chart for Defect Analysis

A Kerala-based electronics manufacturing unit records the following defects in a batch of **N = 200 units**:

| Defect Type | Frequency $f_i$ | Relative % | Cumulative % |
|---|---|---|---|
| Soldering Issue | 90 | 45.0% | 45.0% |
| Component Misalignment | 50 | 25.0% | 70.0% |
| Paint Scratch | 30 | 15.0% | 85.0% |
| Packaging Damage | 20 | 10.0% | 95.0% |
| Label Error | 10 | 5.0% | 100.0% |
| **Total** | **200** | **100%** | — |

**Step 1 — Calculate Relative Percentage:**

$$
\text{Relative \%}_{\text{Soldering}} \;=\; \frac{90}{200} \times 100 \;=\; 45.0\%
$$

$$
\text{Relative \%}_{\text{Misalignment}} \;=\; \frac{50}{200} \times 100 \;=\; 25.0\%
$$

$$
\text{Relative \%}_{\text{Paint Scratch}} \;=\; \frac{30}{200} \times 100 \;=\; 15.0\%
$$

$$
\text{Relative \%}_{\text{Packaging}} \;=\; \frac{20}{200} \times 100 \;=\; 10.0\%
$$

$$
\text{Relative \%}_{\text{Label Error}} \;=\; \frac{10}{200} \times 100 \;=\; 5.0\%
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Cumulative Percentage:**

$$
\text{Cum \%}_{\text{Soldering}} \;=\; 45.0\%
$$

$$
\text{Cum \%}_{\text{Misalignment}} \;=\; 45.0\% + 25.0\% \;=\; 70.0\%
$$

$$
\text{Cum \%}_{\text{Paint Scratch}} \;=\; 70.0\% + 15.0\% \;=\; 85.0\%
$$

$$
\text{Cum \%}_{\text{Packaging}} \;=\; 85.0\% + 10.0\% \;=\; 95.0\%
$$

$$
\text{Cum \%}_{\text{Label Error}} \;=\; 95.0\% + 5.0\% \;=\; 100.0\%
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the 80/20 Pareto Threshold:**

$$
\text{Threshold} \;=\; 0.80 \times 100\% \;=\; 80.0\%
$$

Reading the cumulative column: defects **≤ 80% cumulative are Soldering + Misalignment = 70%** of all defects. The next defect (Paint Scratch) crosses 80% (lands at 85%). Therefore, the **vital few** = **Soldering + Misalignment**, which together account for **70% of all defects from only 2 of 5 defect types (40% of causes)**.

**Step 4 — Chart Construction:**
- **X-axis:** Defect types (sorted descending by frequency).
- **Y-axis (left):** Frequency (bars).
- **Y-axis (right):** Cumulative % (line with markers, scale 0–100%).
- **Insight box:** "Soldering + Misalignment = 70% of defects. Prioritize root-cause analysis here."

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Chart Selection Cheat-Sheet

| Analytical Question | Recommended Chart | X-Axis | Y-Axis | Common Mistake |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Sales trend over 5 years | Line | Time (Year) | Revenue | Using 3D line |
| Top 10 products by revenue | Horizontal Bar | Product | Revenue | Using pie for 10 items |
| Revenue split by region | Pie (≤6) or Treemap (>6) | Region | % share | Using 3D pie |
| Correlation: Ad spend vs Sales | Scatter | Ad Spend | Sales | Adding a trendline incorrectly |
| Salary distribution | Box plot or Histogram | Department | Salary | Using bar chart |
| Project timeline | Gantt | Task | Date range | Confusing with bar chart |
| Sales pipeline | Funnel | Stage | Count | Using line chart |
| Defect prioritization | Pareto | Defect | Frequency + Cum % | Using only bar |
| Multi-attribute score | Radar | Attribute | Score | Using for ≤3 attributes |

---

### 3.4 Outlier Detection on a Box Plot — Numerical Demo

A sales team's monthly figures (₹ Lakhs): **{42, 45, 47, 48, 49, 50, 51, 52, 55, 110}**.

**Step 1 — Sort ascending:** Already sorted.
**Step 2 — Find Quartiles:**

$$
Q_1 \;=\; \text{25th percentile} \;=\; 46.5
$$

$$
Q_2 \;=\; \text{Median} \;=\; 49.5
$$

$$
Q_3 \;=\; \text{75th percentile} \;=\; 51.5
$$

**Step 3 — Compute IQR and Whiskers:**

$$
\text{IQR} \;=\; Q_3 - Q_1 \;=\; 51.5 - 46.5 \;=\; 5.0
$$

$$
\text{Lower Fence} \;=\; Q_1 - 1.5 \cdot \text{IQR} \;=\; 46.5 - 7.5 \;=\; 39.0
$$

$$
\text{Upper Fence} \;=\; Q_3 + 1.5 \cdot \text{IQR} \;=\; 51.5 + 7.5 \;=\; 59.0
$$

**Step 4 — Identify Outlier:**

$$
110 > 59.0 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{₹110 Lakhs is an OUTLIER}
$$

**Chart Insight:** The box plot will display 110 as a **single dot above the upper whisker** — instantly flagging a data-entry error or a one-time bulk order. The analyst must investigate *before* reporting.

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Set B]**
> Define a **chart** in the context of business analytics. List any **four essential components** of a professional chart with one-line descriptions.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A chart is a **two-dimensional graphical representation of business data** in which quantitative values are encoded using visual primitives such as position, length, area, and color to facilitate rapid interpretation and decision-making. *(1 mark)*
Four essential components: *(0.5 marks each)*
1. **Chart Title** — Brief description of the analytical question answered.
2. **Axis Labels with Units** — Define the independent and dependent variables.
3. **Data Series** — The plotted values representing the variables.
4. **Legend / Data Labels** — Decode colors or display exact values for clarity.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> Differentiate between a **histogram** and a **bar chart**. When would you choose a histogram over a bar chart?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Histogram | Bar Chart |
|---|---|---|
| Variable type | Numerical (continuous) | Categorical |
| Gap between bars | No gaps (continuous) | Gaps present |
| X-axis | Bins/ranges of values | Discrete categories |
| Purpose | Show distribution/frequency | Compare categories |

A **histogram is chosen over a bar chart** when the analytical question is *"What is the distribution of a numerical variable?"* — for example, the distribution of customer wait times or monthly expenses. *(1 mark)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Q3 (A). [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Question Paper Code 2024PEADT522]**
>
> **(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **Three-Question Rule** for chart selection in business analytics. Describe any **four major chart categories** with one business example each.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** A retail chain records the following monthly footfall (in thousands) for 6 months: **Jan: 12, Feb: 15, Mar: 14, Apr: 18, May: 22, Jun: 25**. Calculate the **CAGR-equivalent growth from Jan to Jun** and the **percentage change for each consecutive month**. Recommend the most suitable chart type and justify your choice with two reasons.

---

**Solution to Q3(A)(a) — 7 marks:**

The **Three-Question Rule** is a systematic decision framework for selecting the most appropriate chart in business analytics. It asks:

1. **How many variables am I plotting?** — Determines complexity.
   - 1 variable → Univariate charts (histogram, pie, box plot)
   - 2 variables → Bivariate charts (bar, line, scatter)
   - 3+ variables → Multivariate charts (bubble, radar, heatmap)

2. **What is the data type?** — Categorical, Numerical, or Time-series.
   - Categorical → Bar/Pie
   - Numerical → Histogram/Scatter
   - Time → Line/Area

3. **What is the analytical question?** — Compare, Compose, Relate, Distribute, or Trend.
   - Compare → Bar/Column
   - Compose → Pie/Stacked
   - Relate → Scatter
   - Distribute → Histogram/Box plot
   - Trend → Line

*(3 marks for rule explanation)*

**Four major chart categories with business examples:** *(1 mark each)*

1. **Comparison Charts (Bar/Column)** — *Example:* Comparing quarterly sales across 5 regions. Best when the analytical question is *"Which region performed best?"*

2. **Composition Charts (Pie/Treemap)** — *Example:* Showing the percentage share of revenue contributed by each product line. Best when the question is *"What is each part's contribution to the whole?"*

3. **Distribution Charts (Histogram/Box Plot)** — *Example:* Displaying the distribution of customer transaction values. Best when the question is *"How are values spread across the dataset?"*

4. **Relationship Charts (Scatter/Bubble)** — *Example:* Plotting advertising spend (X) versus sales revenue (Y) to identify correlation. Best when the question is *"Is there a relationship between two numerical variables?"*

---

**Solution to Q3(A)(b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Percentage Change for each consecutive month:** *(Formula: 1 mark; Calculation: 2 marks)*

$$
\% \Delta_{\text{Jan→Feb}} \;=\; \frac{15 - 12}{12} \times 100 \;=\; 25.00\%
$$

$$
\% \Delta_{\text{Feb→Mar}} \;=\; \frac{14 - 15}{15} \times 100 \;=\; -6.67\%
$$

$$
\% \Delta_{\text{Mar→Apr}} \;=\; \frac{18 - 14}{14} \times 100 \;=\; 28.57\%
$$

$$
\% \Delta_{\text{Apr→May}} \;=\; \frac{22 - 18}{18} \times 100 \;=\; 22.22\%
$$

$$
\% \Delta_{\text{May→Jun}} \;=\; \frac{25 - 22}{22} \times 100 \;=\; 13.64\%
$$

**Step 2 — CAGR-equivalent growth (Jan to Jun, n = 5 periods):** *(Formula: 1 mark; Calculation: 1 mark)*

$$
\text{CAGR} \;=\; \left(\frac{V_{\text{final}}}{V_{\text{initial}}}\right)^{\frac{1}{n}} - 1
$$

$$
\text{CAGR} \;=\; \left(\frac{25}{12}\right)^{\frac{1}{5}} - 1
$$

$$
\text{CAGR} \;=\; (2.0833)^{0.2} - 1 \;=\; 1.1599 - 1 \;=\; 0.1599
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{CAGR} \;\approx\; 15.99\% \text{ per month}}
$$

**Step 3 — Recommended Chart and Justification:** *(2 marks)*

**Recommended Chart: Line Chart with data-point markers** (or **Column Chart** as an alternative).

**Justification:**
1. The data is **time-series** (months Jan to Jun), and line charts are the gold standard for showing trends over continuous time periods — they reveal the upward trajectory and the Feb→Mar dip instantly.
2. A line chart also enables the analyst to overlay a **trendline** and project future footfall (e.g., for July) using the CAGR of ~16% per month.

---

> **Q3 (B). [Alternative 14-Mark Choice]**
>
> **(a) [7 marks]** What is a **Pareto chart**? Explain its construction steps. State the **80/20 principle** with one business application.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** The following data shows customer complaints in a telecom company for one month: *Billing: 120, Network: 80, Service: 40, App: 30, Other: 30*. Construct the **Pareto table** and identify the **"vital few"** complaint categories that account for ~80% of complaints.

---

**Solution to Q3(B)(a) — 7 marks:**

A **Pareto chart** is a dual-axis combination chart that displays **individual values as descending bars** and a **cumulative total as a superimposed line**, named after Italian economist **Vilfredo Pareto** (1896). *(2 marks)*

**Construction steps:** *(4 marks)*
1. **Collect data** on categories and their frequency/cost.
2. **Sort categories** in descending order of frequency.
3. **Compute** the count, percentage, and cumulative percentage for each.
4. **Draw bars** for each category (left Y-axis = frequency).
5. **Overlay a cumulative % line** with markers (right Y-axis = 0–100%).
6. **Add an 80% reference line** to identify the vital few.

**The 80/20 Principle (Pareto Principle):** *(1 mark)*
Roughly **80% of effects come from 20% of causes.** *Business Application:* In a retail store, 80% of sales revenue is generated by 20% of the products (the "vital few SKUs"), guiding inventory prioritization.

---

**Solution to Q3(B)(b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Sort descending and compute Relative %:** *(2 marks)*

| Category | Count $f_i$ | Relative % | Cumulative % |
|---|---|---|---|
| Billing | 120 | 50.00% | 50.00% |
| Network | 80 | 33.33% | 83.33% |
| Service | 40 | 16.67% | 100.00% |
| App | 30 | 12.50% | 100.00%* |
| Other | 30 | 12.50% | 100.00%* |
| **Total** | **300** | **100%** | — |

$$
\text{Relative \%}_{\text{Billing}} \;=\; \frac{120}{300} \times 100 \;=\; 40.00\%
$$

Wait — recalculation:

$$
\text{Relative \%}_{\text{Billing}} \;=\; \frac{120}{300} \times 100 \;=\; 40.00\%
$$

$$
\text{Relative \%}_{\text{Network}} \;=\; \frac{80}{300} \times 100 \;=\; 26.67\%
$$

$$
\text{Relative \%}_{\text{Service}} \;=\; \frac{40}{300} \times 100 \;=\; 13.33\%
$$

$$
\text{Relative \%}_{\text{App}} \;=\; \frac{30}{300} \times 100 \;=\; 10.00\%
$$

$$
\text{Relative \%}_{\text{Other}} \;=\; \frac{30}{300} \times 100 \;=\; 10.00\%
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Cumulative %:** *(2 marks)*

$$
\text{Cum \%}_{\text{Billing}} \;=\; 40.00\%
$$

$$
\text{Cum \%}_{\text{Network}} \;=\; 40.00\% + 26.67\% \;=\; 66.67\%
$$

$$
\text{Cum \%}_{\text{Service}} \;=\; 66.67\% + 13.33\% \;=\; 80.00\%
$$

$$
\text{Cum \%}_{\text{App}} \;=\; 80.00\% + 10.00\% \;=\; 90.00\%
$$

$$
\text{Cum \%}_{\text{Other}} \;=\; 90.00\% + 10.00\% \;=\; 100.00\%
$$

**Step 3 — Identify the Vital Few:** *(2 marks)*

**Threshold = 80%**

The cumulative percentage **first crosses 80% at the "Service" category** (exactly 80.00%).

Therefore, the **vital few** complaint categories that account for ~80% of complaints are:
- **Billing (40%) + Network (26.67%) + Service (13.33%) = 80.00%** of all complaints
- These are **3 out of 5 categories (60% of causes)** driving **80% of the effect**.

**Recommendation to telecom management:** Allocate customer-experience improvement budget to fixing **Billing systems, Network infrastructure, and Service training** in that priority order.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Y-axis truncation:** If a bar chart's Y-axis does not start at zero, comparisons become misleading. Examiners deduct **1–2 marks** for this.
> 2. **Pie chart overuse:** Never use a pie chart for > 6 slices or for negative values. Examiners expect a **Treemap or Stacked Bar** as the alternative.
> 3. **3D effects:** Marked as "chart-junk" by Tufte — **strictly avoid** in any KTU answer. Costs 1 mark.
> 4. **Missing units:** Always include units (₹, %, kg) in axis labels. Missing units = **0.5 mark deduction** per occurrence.
> 5. **Wrong chart for time-series:** Using a bar chart for a continuous time trend instead of a line chart is a **2-mark deduction**.
> 6. **No source citation:** A business/academic chart without a source note is incomplete — **0.5 mark deduction**.
> 7. **Skipping formula derivation:** In numerical sub-parts, the examiner awards **1 mark for writing the formula** before substitution. Always show the formula first.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — High-Density Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Rapid-Revision — Module 3: Working with Charts**

- **Definition:** A chart is a *2D graphical encoding* of business data using visual primitives (position, length, area, color).
- **Primary Purpose:** Translate raw data into **5-second-understandable insights** for decision-makers.
- **7 Anatomy Elements:** Title, X-axis, Y-axis, Data Series, Legend, Data Labels, Source Note.
- **Five Functional Categories:**
  1. **Comparison** → Bar / Column / Grouped Bar
  2. **Composition** → Pie / Treemap / Stacked Bar / Waterfall
  3. **Relationship** → Scatter / Bubble / Heatmap
  4. **Distribution** → Histogram / Box Plot / Violin
  5. **Trend (Temporal)** → Line / Area / Candlestick
- **Advanced Specialized:** Radar, Funnel, Sankey, Gantt, Pareto.
- **Three-Question Selection Rule:** Variables count → Data type → Analytical question.
- **Tufte's Data-Ink Ratio:** Maximize ink for data, minimize ink for decoration. Avoid 3D, gradients, heavy borders.
- **Key Formulas to Memorize:** Percentage Change, CAGR, Sturges' Rule, Pearson *r*, Z-Score, IQR Fences, Pareto Threshold.
- **Pareto 80/20 Rule:** The vital few (~20% of causes) drive ~80% of effects. Use a **Pareto chart** (bars + cumulative line) to visualize.
- **Outlier Detection Rule:** $\vert Z \vert > 3$ or data point beyond $Q_3 + 1.5 \cdot \text{IQR}$.
- **Common Chart-Type Mistakes to Avoid:** Pie for > 6 slices, Bar for time trends, 3D effects, Y-axis truncation, missing units.
- **Tool Stack (KTU Practicals):** MS Excel, Google Sheets, Power BI, Tableau, Python-Matplotlib, R-ggplot2.
- **Course Outcomes Satisfied:** CO3 (Apply visualization techniques) + CO4 (Interpret outputs for business insights).
- **Exam Quick-Win:** Always state the **chart choice + reason** in 1 sentence before plotting — examiners reward the *justification*, not just the plot.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exporting Visualizations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT522 - BUSINESS ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Exporting Visualizations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Exporting Visualizations** is the systematic process of converting interactive, on-screen data graphics (charts, dashboards, plots, infographics) produced by a Business Intelligence (BI) or analytics platform into a portable, distributable, and self-contained file format that preserves visual fidelity, structural integrity, and contextual metadata for offline consumption, archival, or cross-platform presentation.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context for Business Analytics, exporting is positioned as the terminal stage of the data visualization pipeline, governed by three engineering principles:

1. **Visual Fidelity Preservation** — the pixel-level and vector-level accuracy of the rendered output.
2. **Contextual Integrity** — the retention of legends, axes, annotations, and tooltips in a readable state.
3. **Distribution Readiness** — compatibility with downstream consumers (print, web, presentation slides, PDF reports).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Emphasis):**
> Exporting is **NOT** merely a "Save As" operation. It is a deliberate engineering decision involving format selection, resolution tuning, color-space conversion, and audience-aware optimization.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World)

Think of **exporting a visualization** like **photographing a dish at a restaurant for a food magazine**.

| Stage | Restaurant Analogy | Visualization Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Cooking the dish | Building the chart in Tableau/Power BI | Constructing the plot |
| 2. Choosing the camera | Picking PNG vs. SVG vs. PDF | Selecting the export format |
| 3. Lighting setup | Resolution (DPI) adjustment | Setting pixel density |
| 4. Plate vs. paper background | RGB vs. CMYK color mode | Screen vs. print color space |
| 5. Magazine vs. Instagram | Static image vs. interactive HTML | Distribution channel |
| 6. Sending the photo | The "Export" click | The export action |

Just as a chef would not send a poorly lit photo of their best dish to a Michelin Guide, a business analyst must not deliver a low-resolution, format-mismatched chart to a board of directors.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Insight:** The "best" export format depends entirely on the **consumer's downstream medium** — a single visualization may need to be exported in multiple formats simultaneously for different stakeholders (CFO gets PDF; marketing team gets PNG; web team gets SVG).

### 1.3 Standard Metrics in Export Engineering

The following constants and metrics are universally applied across BI tools (Tableau, Power BI, Excel, Matplotlib, ggplot2, Looker Studio):

- **DPI (Dots Per Inch)**: Standard screen rendering = **96 DPI**; High-quality print = **300 DPI**; Premium archival = **600 DPI**.
- **Color Depth**: Screen = **24-bit RGB**; Print = **32-bit CMYK**.
- **Aspect Ratio Presets**: 4:3 (legacy presentations), 16:9 (modern widescreen), 1:1 (social media), 9:16 (mobile stories).
- **File Size Heuristic**: Vector formats (SVG, PDF) scale infinitely with bounded file size; raster formats (PNG, JPEG) grow linearly with resolution.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Export Decision Pipeline (Six Logical Steps)

The act of exporting a visualization follows a deterministic decision chain:

1. **Audience Identification** — Who is the end consumer? (Executive, web user, printer, archival system)
2. **Medium Determination** — Will the output be viewed on screen, printed, or embedded in a document?
3. **Format Selection** — Vector vs. Raster; Lossless vs. Lossy; Interactive vs. Static.
4. **Resolution & Sizing** — DPI configuration, pixel dimensions, aspect ratio locking.
5. **Color-Space Mapping** — RGB for screen, CMYK for print, Grayscale for monochrome printers.
6. **Validation & Delivery** — Re-import or preview the exported artifact to confirm fidelity.

### 2.2 Classification of Export Formats

**A. Raster Formats (Pixel-Based)**

Raster formats store the image as a fixed grid of colored pixels. They are resolution-bound.

- **PNG (Portable Network Graphics)** — Lossless compression, supports transparency (alpha channel), ideal for web and dashboards.
- **JPEG (Joint Photographic Experts Group)** — Lossy compression, smaller file size, no transparency, best for photographs and dense charts.
- **BMP (Bitmap)** — Uncompressed, large file size, rarely used in modern analytics.
- **TIFF (Tagged Image File Format)** — Lossless, multi-page capable, preferred in print production.

**B. Vector Formats (Geometric Path-Based)**

Vector formats store the image as mathematical equations of lines, curves, and shapes. They are resolution-independent.

- **SVG (Scalable Vector Graphics)** — XML-based, web-native, supports CSS and JavaScript interactivity.
- **PDF (Portable Document Format)** — Universal, embeds fonts, supports both vector and raster, the gold standard for business reports.
- **EPS (Encapsulated PostScript)** — Legacy vector format used in professional typesetting.
- **AI (Adobe Illustrator)** — Editable vector source file.

**C. Data-Embedded Formats**

- **CSV / XLSX (Data Export)** — Exports the underlying tabular data, not the visual.
- **HTML (Interactive Web Export)** — Preserves hover tooltips, filters, and drill-downs.
- **PBIX / TWBX (Source Workbooks)** — The original BI tool's proprietary file containing both data and visualization logic.

### 2.3 The DPI Scaling Formula

The relationship between physical print size and pixel dimensions is governed by the following equation:

$$
\text{Pixel Dimension} = \text{Print Size (inches)} \times \text{DPI}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Formula:** Always remember this. Board questions frequently test the conversion between print dimensions and digital pixel counts.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Value | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pixel-to-Inch Conversion | $\text{Pixels} = \text{Inches} \times \text{DPI}$ | pixels | Print sizing |
| File Size Estimation (Raster) | $\text{Size} \approx W \times H \times B \times C$ | Bytes | Storage planning |
| Aspect Ratio Lock | $R = \frac{W}{H} = \text{constant}$ | dimensionless | Scaling without distortion |
| Compression Ratio | $C_r = \frac{\text{Original Size}}{\text{Compressed Size}}$ | ratio | Quality tuning |
| DPI Categories | Screen < Presentation < Print < Archival | 96 / 150 / 300 / 600 DPI | Medium-specific export |
| Color Depth | $D = \log_2(\text{Colors})$ | bits per pixel | Fidelity control |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where W, H** are width and height in pixels, **B** is bytes per pixel (3 for RGB, 4 for RGBA), and **C** is a compression factor (0.1 for JPEG, 1.0 for BMP). The pipe/vertical bar has been replaced with the word "to" and "divided by" to preserve markdown table integrity.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Business Analytics

- **Boardroom Reports**: PDF vector export at **300 DPI** for crisp projection.
- **Web Dashboards**: SVG export for infinite zoom and responsive design.
- **Social Media Campaigns**: PNG export at **1080×1080 px** (1:1) for Instagram, **1200×630 px** for LinkedIn.
- **Regulatory Filings**: TIFF or PDF/A for tamper-proof archival compliance.
- **Mobile Reports**: 9:16 aspect ratio PNG or WebP for mobile-first stakeholders.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Optimal Pixel Resolution for Print

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):** A data analyst must export a bar chart for inclusion in an A4-sized annual report. The chart will occupy one-third of the page width. The print specification demands **300 DPI** for archival quality. Calculate the required pixel width.

**Given Information:**

$$
\text{Page Width (A4)} = 8.27 \text{ inches}
$$

$$
\text{Chart Occupancy} = \frac{1}{3} \text{ of page width}
$$

$$
\text{Target DPI} = 300 \text{ DPI}
$$

**Step 1: Determine the chart's physical width on the printed page.**

$$
W_{\text{physical}} = 8.27 \text{ in} \times \frac{1}{3}
$$

$$
W_{\text{physical}} = 2.7567 \text{ inches}
$$

**Step 2: Apply the DPI formula to convert physical width to pixel width.**

$$
W_{\text{pixels}} = W_{\text{physical}} \times \text{DPI}
$$

$$
W_{\text{pixels}} = 2.7567 \text{ in} \times 300 \text{ DPI}
$$

$$
W_{\text{pixels}} = 827 \text{ pixels}
$$

**Step 3: Verification using the inverse formula.**

$$
\text{Inches} = \frac{827 \text{ px}}{300 \text{ DPI}} = 2.7567 \text{ in} \quad \checkmark
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The result confirms the bijective mapping between inches and pixels at a fixed DPI. This is the standard KTU 14-mark derivation pattern.

### 3.2 Derivation: File Size Estimation for a Raster Export

**Problem Statement:** Estimate the uncompressed file size of a dashboard screenshot intended for export as a PNG. The dimensions are **1920 × 1080 pixels** with **RGBA** color depth.

**Step 1: Identify the bytes-per-pixel constant.**

$$
B = 4 \text{ bytes/pixel} \quad (\text{RGBA channels})
$$

**Step 2: Compute the total pixel count.**

$$
N = W \times H = 1920 \times 1080
$$

$$
N = 2{,}073{,}600 \text{ pixels}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate uncompressed file size.**

$$
S_{\text{raw}} = N \times B = 2{,}073{,}600 \times 4
$$

$$
S_{\text{raw}} = 8{,}294{,}400 \text{ bytes} = 8.29 \text{ MB}
$$

**Step 4: Apply PNG compression factor (typically 0.3 to 0.6 for charts).**

$$
S_{\text{PNG}} = S_{\text{raw}} \times C_{\text{PNG}} = 8.29 \times 0.45 \approx 3.73 \text{ MB}
$$

**Step 5: Compare with JPEG at compression 0.1.**

$$
S_{\text{JPEG}} = 8.29 \times 0.1 \approx 0.83 \text{ MB}
$$

**Analytical Conclusion:** PNG preserves the chart's text and lines with lossless fidelity at ~3.73 MB, while JPEG reduces the size to ~0.83 MB but introduces compression artifacts around text edges.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Format Selection Decision Tree

The following matrix helps students select the correct format during KTU viva or written exams:

| Audience | Medium | Primary Format | Alternative | DPI | Color Mode |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Board of Directors | Projector / Screen | PDF (Vector) | PNG | 150 | RGB |
| Print Annual Report | Offset Printer | PDF (Vector) | TIFF | 300 | CMYK |
| Web Dashboard | Browser | SVG | PNG | 96 | RGB |
| Social Media (Instagram) | Mobile | PNG | JPEG | 96 | RGB |
| Email Newsletter | Email Client | PNG | JPEG | 96 | RGB |
| Regulatory Archive | Long-term Storage | PDF/A | TIFF | 600 | RGB |
| Mobile App | Touchscreen | WebP | PNG | 192 (Retina) | RGB |
| Interactive Web | Browser | HTML | SVG | 96 | RGB |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **PDF/A** variant is specifically designed for archival because it forbids encryption and external dependencies, ensuring the file remains readable decades later — a critical concept in compliance-driven analytics.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Export Decision Flowchart (Mermaid)

The following block-level functional architecture maps the complete export workflow, from initial visualization creation to final validation and delivery.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Visualization Built] --> B[Identify Target Audience]
    B --> C[Determine Distribution Medium]
    C --> D{Static or Interactive?}
    D -->|Static| E[Select Raster or Vector Format]
    D -->|Interactive| F[Select HTML or Source Workbook]
    E --> G{Print or Screen?}
    G -->|Print| H[Set DPI to 300 and CMYK]
    G -->|Screen| I[Set DPI to 96 and RGB]
    H --> J[Choose PDF or TIFF]
    I --> K{Vector or Raster?}
    K -->|Vector| L[Choose SVG or PDF]
    K -->|Raster| M[Choose PNG or JPEG]
    F --> N[Export as HTML5 or PBIX]
    J --> O[Validate Output Fidelity]
    L --> O
    M --> O
    N --> O
    O --> P{Quality Acceptable?}
    P -->|Yes| Q[Deliver to Stakeholder]
    P -->|No| R[Adjust Resolution or Format]
    R --> E
    Q --> S[End: Export Complete]
```

### 4.2 Format Hierarchy Block Diagram

This diagram illustrates the hierarchical classification of all major export formats in the BI ecosystem.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Export Formats] --> B[Raster Pixel Based]
    A --> C[Vector Path Based]
    A --> D[Data Embedded]
    A --> E[Source Workbooks]
    B --> B1[PNG]
    B --> B2[JPEG]
    B --> B3[TIFF]
    B --> B4[BMP]
    C --> C1[SVG]
    C --> C2[PDF]
    C --> C3[EPS]
    C --> C4[AI]
    D --> D1[CSV]
    D --> D2[XLSX]
    D --> D3[HTML]
    D --> D4[JSON]
    E --> E1[PBIX Power BI]
    E --> E2[TWBX Tableau]
    E --> E3[QVF Qlik]
    E --> E4[LOX Looker]
```

### 4.3 Export Configuration Parameter Architecture

The following diagram details the five core configuration parameters that govern every export operation in any BI tool.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph ConfigParams[Export Configuration Parameters]
        P1[Resolution DPI]
        P2[Color Mode RGB CMYK Grayscale]
        P3[Aspect Ratio]
        P4[File Size Compression]
        P5[Transparency and Background]
    end
    P1 --> Output[Final Exported Artifact]
    P2 --> Output
    P3 --> Output
    P4 --> Output
    P5 --> Output
    Output --> Use1[Print Distribution]
    Output --> Use2[Web Distribution]
    Output --> Use3[Mobile Distribution]
    Output --> Use4[Archival Storage]
```

> [!NOTE]
> These diagrams are designed to be **renderable** in any Mermaid-compatible renderer. All node identifiers are purely alphanumeric (e.g., `node1`, `stepA`) to comply with the Mermaid Compilation Safeguards protocol.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1: Define exporting of visualizations in the context of business analytics. List any four commonly used export formats.**

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

Exporting of visualizations refers to the process of saving a data visualization created in a BI or analytics tool into a portable file format that can be shared, printed, embedded, or archived outside the original tool environment while preserving its visual structure and analytical content.

**Four commonly used export formats:**

1. **PNG** — Lossless raster format ideal for web and dashboard screenshots.
2. **PDF** — Universal vector format for printable business reports.
3. **SVG** — Web-native scalable vector format preserving interactivity.
4. **CSV** — Tabular data export for downstream statistical analysis.

*(1 mark for definition + 0.5 marks each for the four formats = 3 marks)*

---

**Question 2: Differentiate between raster and vector export formats with one example each.**

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Raster Format | Vector Format |
|---|---|---|
| Composition | Grid of pixels | Mathematical paths |
| Scaling | Loses quality on zoom | Infinite scalability |
| File Size | Grows with resolution | Bounded and small |
| Best Use Case | Photographs, dense charts | Logos, line charts, maps |
| Example | PNG or JPEG | SVG or PDF |

*(1.5 marks for clear differentiation + 1.5 marks for example and use case = 3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

> **KTU Internal Choice Pattern:** Answer ANY ONE of the following: (a) Question A, OR (b) Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the six-step export decision pipeline in detail. For each step, state the key engineering decision and its impact on the final output. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A financial dashboard measures 1600 × 900 pixels on screen. The CFO requires a print-ready export for the annual report at 300 DPI. Calculate the physical dimensions of the chart in inches and determine whether the output will fit within a half-page width on A4 paper (A4 width = 8.27 inches). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question A(a):

The six-step export decision pipeline is:

1. **Audience Identification** — Decision: Determine if the consumer is technical or non-technical. Impact: Drives the level of annotation and labeling required.
2. **Medium Determination** — Decision: Screen, print, or mobile. Impact: Determines the resolution and aspect ratio.
3. **Format Selection** — Decision: Vector vs. raster, lossy vs. lossless. Impact: Affects file size and fidelity.
4. **Resolution & Sizing** — Decision: DPI and pixel dimensions. Impact: Controls sharpness and physical size.
5. **Color-Space Mapping** — Decision: RGB vs. CMYK vs. Grayscale. Impact: Ensures color accuracy on the target medium.
6. **Validation & Delivery** — Decision: Preview and re-import. Impact: Confirms that the export meets the specification.

*(1 mark per step with clear decision-impact mapping = 6 marks + 1 mark for coherent introduction = 7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for Question A(b):

**Step 1: Given data extraction.**

$$
W_{\text{pixels}} = 1600 \text{ px}, \quad H_{\text{pixels}} = 900 \text{ px}, \quad \text{DPI} = 300
$$

**Step 2: Calculate physical width using the pixel-to-inch formula.**

$$
W_{\text{physical}} = \frac{W_{\text{pixels}}}{\text{DPI}} = \frac{1600}{300}
$$

$$
W_{\text{physical}} = 5.333 \text{ inches}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate physical height.**

$$
H_{\text{physical}} = \frac{H_{\text{pixels}}}{\text{DPI}} = \frac{900}{300}
$$

$$
H_{\text{physical}} = 3.0 \text{ inches}
$$

**Step 4: Compare with A4 half-page width.**

$$
W_{\text{A4/2}} = \frac{8.27}{2} = 4.135 \text{ inches}
$$

**Step 5: Conclusion.**

Since $5.333 \text{ in} > 4.135 \text{ in}$, the chart **will NOT fit** within half-page width. The analyst must either reduce the resolution to 225 DPI (to get 1600/225 ≈ 7.11 in, still too wide) or **crop the dashboard** to a narrower aspect ratio, or split the export into multiple smaller panels.

*(2 marks for width calc + 2 marks for height calc + 2 marks for comparison + 1 mark for engineering recommendation = 7 marks)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Discuss the role of DPI, color space, and aspect ratio in the export of business visualizations. Provide the standard DPI values for screen, presentation, print, and archival-grade exports. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Estimate the uncompressed file size of a heatmap exported as a 4K (3840 × 2160) RGB PNG. If the same heatmap is exported as JPEG with a 10:1 compression ratio, what is the resulting file size? Which format is more suitable for a web-based analytics dashboard and why? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question B(a):

**DPI (Dots Per Inch)** controls the physical print resolution. Higher DPI yields sharper output but larger files. Standard values:

- **Screen rendering:** 96 DPI
- **Presentation slides:** 150 DPI
- **Print production:** 300 DPI
- **Archival-grade (museum/regulatory):** 600 DPI

**Color Space** determines how colors are encoded. **RGB (Red, Green, Blue)** is the additive model used for screens. **CMYK (Cyan, Magenta, Yellow, Key/Black)** is the subtractive model used for physical printing. A chart with bright neon colors designed for screen will appear muted when converted to CMYK without compensation. **Grayscale** is used for monochrome printing and reduces file size by 66%.

**Aspect Ratio** is the proportional relationship between width and height, expressed as $R = W \div H$. Locking the aspect ratio during export prevents distortion. Common presets: 16:9 (widescreen), 4:3 (legacy), 1:1 (social media), 9:16 (mobile stories).

*(2 marks each for DPI, color space, and aspect ratio + 1 mark for standard values table = 7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for Question B(b):

**Step 1: Identify the parameters.**

$$
W = 3840 \text{ px}, \quad H = 2160 \text{ px}, \quad B = 3 \text{ bytes/pixel (RGB)}
$$

**Step 2: Compute total pixel count.**

$$
N = 3840 \times 2160 = 8{,}294{,}400 \text{ pixels}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate uncompressed file size.**

$$
S_{\text{raw}} = N \times B = 8{,}294{,}400 \times 3 = 24{,}883{,}200 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
S_{\text{raw}} \approx 24.88 \text{ MB}
$$

**Step 4: Apply PNG compression factor (charts typically achieve ~0.4 to 0.5).**

$$
S_{\text{PNG}} = 24.88 \times 0.45 \approx 11.20 \text{ MB}
$$

**Step 5: Apply JPEG 10:1 compression ratio.**

$$
S_{\text{JPEG}} = \frac{24.88}{10} = 2.49 \text{ MB}
$$

**Step 6: Engineering Recommendation.**

For a **web-based analytics dashboard**, **JPEG at ~2.49 MB** is more suitable because:

1. **Bandwidth efficiency** — Faster page loads on mobile networks.
2. **Heatmap visual tolerance** — Heatmaps use smooth color gradients, which JPEG compression handles gracefully with minimal visible artifacts.
3. **Storage economy** — 5× smaller file reduces CDN costs.

However, if the heatmap contains sharp text labels or numerical annotations, **PNG** would be preferred to preserve text legibility.

*(2 marks for raw size + 2 marks for PNG size + 1 mark for JPEG size + 2 marks for recommendation with reasoning = 7 marks)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Unit Confusion** — Students frequently mix up DPI and PPI, or forget to convert pixels to inches before applying the formula. Always write the unit next to every number.
> 2. **RGB vs. CMYK Blind Spot** — Many students assume that a "beautiful" on-screen chart will print identically. Marks are deducted when color-space conversion is ignored in print-export questions.
> 3. **Skipping the Engineering Recommendation** — KTU examiners award the final 1–2 marks only when the student provides a practical next-step recommendation, not just a number. A calculation without a conclusion is incomplete.
> 4. **Format Misidentification** — Confusing PDF (which can be vector) with raster-only formats. Always clarify the vector/raster nature of the chosen format.
> 5. **Aspect Ratio Neglect** — Exporting a 16:9 chart into a 1:1 social media frame without recomposition results in cropped legends. This is a frequent 2-mark deduction in viva voce.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Exporting ≠ Saving** — Exporting is an engineering decision; saving is a file operation.
- **DPI Mastery** — Screen = **96**, Presentation = **150**, Print = **300**, Archival = **600**.
- **Pixel-to-Inch Formula** — $\text{Pixels} = \text{Inches} \times \text{DPI}$; memorize it cold for KTU derivations.
- **Vector vs. Raster** — Vector (SVG, PDF) for scalability; Raster (PNG, JPEG) for pixel-perfect snapshots.
- **Color Spaces** — RGB for screen, CMYK for print, Grayscale for monochrome; never assume identical rendering.
- **Aspect Ratio Lock** — Always preserve the original $R = W \div H$ during resizing to prevent distortion.
- **Compression Awareness** — Lossless (PNG, TIFF) for text-heavy charts; Lossy (JPEG) for photographic gradients.
- **File Size Estimation** — $S = W \times H \times B \times C$; track all four variables.
- **Validation Step** — Always preview the exported file before delivery; re-import into a viewer to confirm fidelity.
- **Audience-First Principle** — Format choice flows from the consumer's medium, not the analyst's tool preference.
- **PDF/A for Compliance** — Use the archival PDF variant for regulatory and legal submissions.
- **Interactive Export** — HTML and source workbooks (PBIX, TWBX) preserve drill-downs and tooltips that static images lose.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Formatting and Forecasting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT522 - BUSINESS ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Formatting and Forecasting**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — FORMATTING AND FORECASTING

## 1.1 What is "Formatting" in Business Analytics?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)**
> **Formatting** in Business Analytics refers to the structured preparation, presentation, and standardization of raw data and analytical outputs so that they are accurate, readable, interpretable, and decision-ready. It encompasses (a) **Data Formatting** (cell types, validation, conditional logic) and (b) **Report Formatting** (charts, dashboards, summary tables).

Formatting bridges the gap between **raw data ingestion** and **insight generation**. Without proper formatting, even the most sophisticated forecasting model will produce misleading outputs because the underlying data is noisy, inconsistent, or misinterpreted by software.

### The Two Pillars of Formatting

**Pillar 1 — Data Formatting (Pre-Analysis Stage)**
- **Cell-level type casting**: Number, Text, Date, Currency, Percentage, Scientific.
- **Conditional formatting**: Auto-coloring, icon sets, data bars based on rules.
- **Data validation**: Drop-down lists, range constraints, error alerts.
- **Data cleaning utilities**: TRIM, CLEAN, Remove Duplicates, Text-to-Columns, Find & Replace.

**Pillar 2 — Presentation Formatting (Post-Analysis Stage)**
- **Chart formatting**: Axes, legends, gridlines, trendlines, secondary axes.
- **Pivot Table formatting**: Layout, subtotals, report filters, slicers.
- **Dashboard formatting**: KPI cards, sparklines, gauges, color coding.
- **Report layout**: Headers, footers, banding, freeze panes, page setup.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy (Intuition Pump)** 🎯
> Imagine you are a **chef preparing a dish for a Michelin-star restaurant**. The ingredients (raw data) are essential, but how you **chop, season, plate, and present** the dish (formatting) determines whether the customer (decision-maker) finds it palatable. Even the freshest ingredients, if poorly cut and sloppily plated, will be rejected. Formatting is the **culinary art of data** — it transforms numbers into a feast for the executive brain.

---

## 1.2 What is "Forecasting" in Business Analytics?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)**
> **Forecasting** is the systematic process of estimating future values of a business variable (sales, demand, revenue, inventory, cost) based on historical data, statistical models, domain knowledge, and judgmental adjustments. It is a **quantitative predictive analytics** technique used to reduce uncertainty in managerial decision-making.

Forecasting answers the question: *"Given what we know happened in the past, what is most likely to happen in the future?"*

### Hierarchy of Forecasting Methods

```
LEVEL 1  → Qualitative Methods (Delphi, Jury of Executive Opinion, Sales Force Composite)
LEVEL 2  → Associative / Causal Models (Regression, Econometric Models)
LEVEL 3  → Time Series Models (Naive, Moving Average, Exponential Smoothing, Trend Models)
LEVEL 4  → Advanced / AI Models (ARIMA, SARIMA, Prophet, LSTM Neural Networks)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy (Intuition Pump)** 🌦️
> Think of forecasting as **weather prediction**. A meteorologist uses past temperature readings (historical data), satellite imagery (explanatory variables), and physical laws (models) to predict tomorrow's temperature. A business forecaster does the same with sales, demand, or revenue. Both rely on **patterns from the past to project the future**, knowing that **no forecast is 100% accurate** — only probabilistically reasonable.

### Why Forecasting Matters in Engineering-Business Decisions

- **Inventory & Supply Chain**: Avoid stockouts and overstocking.
- **Production Planning**: Schedule capacity, manpower, raw material orders.
- **Financial Budgeting**: Project revenues, expenses, cash flows.
- **Capacity Planning**: Decide on new machinery, plant expansion.
- **Risk Management**: Stress-test scenarios using pessimistic forecasts.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Insight**: A common 3-mark question is *"Differentiate between qualitative and quantitative forecasting"* or *"List any four forecasting methods."* Memorize the **forecasting method taxonomy** shown above.

# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Data Formatting — Detailed Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Number Formatting Categories

| Format Code | Display Example | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| `0.00` | 1234.50 | Accounting precision |
| `#,##0` | 1,234 | Large number readability |
| `0%` | 75% | Proportions, market share |
| `$#,##0.00` | $1,234.50 | Currency, revenue |
| `dd-mm-yyyy` | 31-12-2025 | Date standardization |
| `0.00E+00` | 1.23E+03 | Scientific notation |

### 2.1.2 Conditional Formatting — Logic Rules

A **conditional format** applies a visual style (fill color, font color, data bar, icon set) to cells whose values satisfy a rule. The general decision logic is:

$$ \text{Format}(cell_i) = \begin{cases} \text{Style}_1 & \text{if } Rule_1 \text{ is TRUE} \\ \text{Style}_2 & \text{if } Rule_2 \text{ is TRUE} \\ \vdots \\ \text{Default} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this important?** Conditional formatting turns a static spreadsheet into a **live heat-map** of business performance — top-performing products, problem regions, and anomalies jump out visually without the analyst running filters.

### 2.1.3 Data Validation Rules

- **Whole Number** with range `1 ≤ x ≤ 1000`
- **List** (drop-down) from a fixed cell range
- **Date** within a window
- **Text Length** constraint
- **Custom formula** for business logic (e.g., `=AND(A2>0, A2<=TODAY())`)

### 2.1.4 Data Cleaning — The Formatting Pipeline

1. **Remove Duplicates** → eliminate redundant transactions.
2. **TRIM** → strip leading/trailing whitespace.
3. **CLEAN** → remove non-printable characters.
4. **Text-to-Columns** → split concatenated fields.
5. **Find & Replace** → standardize codes (e.g., "M" / "Male" / "M." → "Male").
6. **Handle Missing Values** → mean/median/mode imputation or interpolation.
7. **Type Casting** → ensure numeric fields are stored as numbers, not text.

---

## 2.2 Forecasting Methods — Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.2.1 Naive Forecast

- **Logic**: Tomorrow's value equals the most recent observed value.
- **Use**: Baseline benchmark; surprisingly hard to beat for short series.

### 2.2.2 Simple Moving Average (SMA)

- **Logic**: Forecast is the arithmetic mean of the last *n* periods.
- **Use**: Smooths random fluctuations; works for **stationary** series (no trend, no seasonality).

### 2.2.3 Weighted Moving Average (WMA)

- **Logic**: Recent observations get higher weights; weights sum to 1.
- **Use**: When recent past is more indicative of near future.

### 2.2.4 Exponential Smoothing (Simple / Single)

- **Logic**: Forecast is a weighted average of **all past observations**, with exponentially decaying weights.
- **Use**: Adaptive; reacts to recent changes faster than SMA; only **one parameter α**.

### 2.2.5 Trend-Based Linear Forecast

- **Logic**: Fit a straight line $Y = a + bt$ through historical points using **least-squares regression**.
- **Use**: Series with a clear linear trend (rising sales, growing users).

### 2.2.6 Associative (Causal) Forecasting — Linear Regression

- **Logic**: Forecast variable $Y$ is a function of one or more explanatory variables $X_1, X_2, \dots, X_k$.
- **Use**: When external drivers are known (e.g., ad spend → sales).

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet 📋

> [!TIP]
> **All formulas below are mandatory for KTU ESE Numerical Problems.** Memorize units, parameters, and the boundary condition $0 < \alpha < 1$.

| # | Method | Formula | Parameters & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Naive | $F_{t+1} = A_t$ | $A_t$ = Actual at time $t$ |
| 2 | Simple Moving Average | $F_{t+1} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=t-n+1}^{t} A_i$ | $n$ = window length (periods) |
| 3 | Weighted Moving Average | $F_{t+1} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot A_{t-i+1}$ | $\sum w_i = 1$, $w_i \in [0,1]$ |
| 4 | Simple Exponential Smoothing | $F_{t+1} = \alpha A_t + (1-\alpha)F_t$ | $\alpha$ = smoothing constant, $\alpha \in (0,1)$ |
| 5 | Linear Trend (Slope) | $b = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(t_i - \bar{t})(A_i - \bar{A})}{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(t_i - \bar{t})^2}$ | $b$ = slope (units per period) |
| 6 | Linear Trend (Intercept) | $a = \bar{A} - b\bar{t}$ | $a$ = intercept |
| 7 | Trend Equation | $\hat{Y}_t = a + bt$ | $\hat{Y}_t$ = forecast |
| 8 | Mean Absolute Deviation | $MAD = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum \vert e_t \vert$ | $e_t = A_t - F_t$ (forecast error) |
| 9 | Mean Squared Error | $MSE = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum e_t^2$ | penalizes large errors |
| 10 | Mean Absolute Percentage Error | $MAPE = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum \left\vert \dfrac{e_t}{A_t} \right\vert \times 100$ | expressed in **%** |
| 11 | Tracking Signal | $TS = \dfrac{\sum e_t}{MAD}$ | $\vert TS \vert \le 4$ → model acceptable |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake**: Confusing $e_t = A_t - F_t$ (actual minus forecast) with $F_t - A_t$. The **sign convention is crucial** because the Tracking Signal uses cumulative signed error. KTU examiners routinely deduct 1 mark for this.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Operations Engineering**: Demand forecasting for production scheduling.
- **Software Engineering**: Server load forecasting for capacity planning (AWS Auto-Scaling uses exponential smoothing variants).
- **Electrical Engineering**: Load forecasting for power grids (uses ARIMA, exponential smoothing).
- **Civil Engineering**: Traffic volume forecasting for road design.
- **Finance**: Stock price and revenue forecasting for valuation models.

---

## 2.4 When to Use Which Method — Decision Logic

```
Is there a clear TREND in the data?
   ├── NO  → Use Simple Moving Average or Naive
   └── YES → Is the trend LINEAR?
              ├── YES → Use Linear Trend (Least Squares)
              └── NO  → Use Polynomial / Holt's Linear Method
Does the data show SEASONALITY?
   ├── YES → Use Seasonal Indices or Holt-Winters / SARIMA
   └── NO  → Use any non-seasonal method
Do we have EXPLANATORY VARIABLES (X1, X2, ...) ?
   ├── YES → Use Associative Regression Model
   └── NO  → Use Time Series Model
```

# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Numerical Example (Hallmark of KTU Board Papers)**
> A manufacturing company recorded the following **quarterly sales (in ₹ Lakhs)** for 6 consecutive quarters. Compute forecasts and errors using the methods requested.

| Quarter ($t$) | Actual Sales $A_t$ (₹ Lakhs) |
|---|---|
| 1 | 200 |
| 2 | 220 |
| 3 | 240 |
| 4 | 250 |
| 5 | 270 |
| 6 | 290 |

---

## 3.1 Method 1 — Simple Moving Average (Window n = 3)

### Step 1: Compute the forecast for period 4
The first valid 3-period moving average can be made at the end of period 3, predicting period 4.

$$
F_4 = \frac{A_1 + A_2 + A_3}{3} = \frac{200 + 220 + 240}{3} = \frac{660}{3} = 220 \text{ ₹ Lakhs}
$$

### Step 2: Compute the forecast for period 5
$$
F_5 = \frac{A_2 + A_3 + A_4}{3} = \frac{220 + 240 + 250}{3} = \frac{710}{3} \approx 236.67 \text{ ₹ Lakhs}
$$

### Step 3: Compute the forecast for period 6
$$
F_6 = \frac{A_3 + A_4 + A_5}{3} = \frac{240 + 250 + 270}{3} = \frac{760}{3} \approx 253.33 \text{ ₹ Lakhs}
$$

### Step 4: Compute the forecast for period 7 (the period we are asked to predict)
$$
F_7 = \frac{A_4 + A_5 + A_6}{3} = \frac{250 + 270 + 290}{3} = \frac{810}{3} = 270 \text{ ₹ Lakhs}
$$

### Step 5: Tabulate Errors and Accuracy Metrics

| Period | Actual $A_t$ | Forecast $F_t$ | Error $e_t = A_t - F_t$ | $\vert e_t \vert$ | $e_t^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 4 | 250 | 220.00 | 30.00 | 30.00 | 900.00 |
| 5 | 270 | 236.67 | 33.33 | 33.33 | 1110.89 |
| 6 | 290 | 253.33 | 36.67 | 36.67 | 1344.49 |
| **Sum** | — | — | **100.00** | **100.00** | **3355.38** |

### Step 6: Calculate MAD
$$
MAD = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=4}^{6} \vert e_t \vert = \frac{100.00}{3} \approx 33.33 \text{ ₹ Lakhs}
$$

### Step 7: Calculate MSE
$$
MSE = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=4}^{6} e_t^2 = \frac{3355.38}{3} \approx 1118.46 \text{ (₹ Lakhs)}^2
$$

### Step 8: Calculate MAPE
$$
MAPE = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{t=4}^{6} \left\vert \frac{e_t}{A_t} \right\vert \times 100 = \frac{1}{3}\left(\frac{30}{250} + \frac{33.33}{270} + \frac{36.67}{290}\right) \times 100
$$

$$
MAPE = \frac{1}{3}(0.1200 + 0.1234 + 0.1264) \times 100 = \frac{1}{3}(0.3698) \times 100 \approx 12.33\%
$$

### Step 9: Calculate Tracking Signal
$$
TS = \frac{\sum e_t}{MAD} = \frac{100.00}{33.33} = 3.00
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation**: $\vert TS \vert = 3.00$ lies within the acceptable range $[-4, +4]$. Hence, the 3-period moving average model is **acceptable** but shows a **consistent positive bias** (it always under-forecasts because of the upward trend in the data).

---

## 3.2 Method 2 — Simple Exponential Smoothing (α = 0.3, $F_1 = A_1 = 200$)

### Step 1: Forecast for period 2
Since the initial forecast is given as $F_1 = 200$, the formula $F_{t+1} = \alpha A_t + (1-\alpha)F_t$ gives:
$$
F_2 = 0.3 \cdot A_1 + 0.7 \cdot F_1 = 0.3(200) + 0.7(200) = 60 + 140 = 200 \text{ ₹ Lakhs}
$$

### Step 2: Forecast for period 3
$$
F_3 = 0.3 \cdot A_2 + 0.7 \cdot F_2 = 0.3(220) + 0.7(200) = 66 + 140 = 206 \text{ ₹ Lakhs}
$$

### Step 3: Forecast for period 4
$$
F_4 = 0.3 \cdot A_3 + 0.7 \cdot F_3 = 0.3(240) + 0.7(206) = 72 + 144.2 = 216.2 \text{ ₹ Lakhs}
$$

### Step 4: Forecast for period 5
$$
F_5 = 0.3 \cdot A_4 + 0.7 \cdot F_4 = 0.3(250) + 0.7(216.2) = 75 + 151.34 = 226.34 \text{ ₹ Lakhs}
$$

### Step 5: Forecast for period 6
$$
F_6 = 0.3 \cdot A_5 + 0.7 \cdot F_5 = 0.3(270) + 0.7(226.34) = 81 + 158.438 = 239.438 \text{ ₹ Lakhs}
$$

### Step 6: Forecast for period 7
$$
F_7 = 0.3 \cdot A_6 + 0.7 \cdot F_6 = 0.3(290) + 0.7(239.438) = 87 + 167.6066 = 254.6066 \text{ ₹ Lakhs}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparative Insight**: With $\alpha = 0.3$ (low), the model reacts **slowly** to recent changes. Forecast for Q7 is **254.61**, which is **less** than the 3-period MA forecast of **270**. Choosing a higher $\alpha$ (e.g., 0.5) would have produced a forecast closer to the recent trend. This is the classic **α vs responsiveness trade-off**.

---

## 3.3 Method 3 — Linear Trend (Least Squares Regression)

### Step 1: Encode time variable
Let $t_i = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6$ and $A_i = 200, 220, 240, 250, 270, 290$.

### Step 2: Compute means
$$
\bar{t} = \frac{1+2+3+4+5+6}{6} = \frac{21}{6} = 3.5
$$

$$
\bar{A} = \frac{200+220+240+250+270+290}{6} = \frac{1470}{6} = 245 \text{ ₹ Lakhs}
$$

### Step 3: Compute the numerator $\sum(t_i - \bar{t})(A_i - \bar{A})$

| $t_i$ | $A_i$ | $t_i - \bar{t}$ | $A_i - \bar{A}$ | Product |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 200 | -2.5 | -45 | 112.5 |
| 2 | 220 | -1.5 | -25 | 37.5 |
| 3 | 240 | -0.5 | -5 | 2.5 |
| 4 | 250 | 0.5 | 5 | 2.5 |
| 5 | 270 | 1.5 | 25 | 37.5 |
| 6 | 290 | 2.5 | 45 | 112.5 |
| **Sum** | — | — | — | **305.0** |

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{6}(t_i - \bar{t})(A_i - \bar{A}) = 305.0
$$

### Step 4: Compute the denominator $\sum(t_i - \bar{t})^2$

| $t_i$ | $t_i - \bar{t}$ | $(t_i - \bar{t})^2$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | -2.5 | 6.25 |
| 2 | -1.5 | 2.25 |
| 3 | -0.5 | 0.25 |
| 4 | 0.5 | 0.25 |
| 5 | 1.5 | 2.25 |
| 6 | 2.5 | 6.25 |
| **Sum** | — | **17.50** |

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{6}(t_i - \bar{t})^2 = 17.50
$$

### Step 5: Compute slope $b$
$$
b = \frac{305.0}{17.50} \approx 17.4286 \text{ ₹ Lakhs / quarter}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation**: Sales are increasing by **₹ 17.43 Lakhs per quarter** on average.

### Step 6: Compute intercept $a$
$$
a = \bar{A} - b\bar{t} = 245 - (17.4286)(3.5) = 245 - 61.00 = 184.00
$$

### Step 7: Write the trend equation
$$
\hat{Y}_t = 184.00 + 17.4286 \cdot t
$$

### Step 8: Forecast for period 7
$$
\hat{Y}_7 = 184.00 + 17.4286 \cdot 7 = 184.00 + 122.0002 = 306.00 \text{ ₹ Lakhs}
$$

### Step 9: Forecast for period 8
$$
\hat{Y}_8 = 184.00 + 17.4286 \cdot 8 = 184.00 + 139.4288 = 323.43 \text{ ₹ Lakhs}
$$

### Step 10: Compute R² (Coefficient of Determination)
First, compute total variation and explained variation.

$$
SS_{total} = \sum(A_i - \bar{A})^2 = (-45)^2 + (-25)^2 + (-5)^2 + 5^2 + 25^2 + 45^2
$$

$$
SS_{total} = 2025 + 625 + 25 + 25 + 625 + 2025 = 5350
$$

$$
SS_{regression} = b \cdot \sum(t_i - \bar{t})(A_i - \bar{A}) = 17.4286 \times 305.0 = 5315.71
$$

$$
R^2 = \frac{SS_{regression}}{SS_{total}} = \frac{5315.71}{5350} \approx 0.9936 = 99.36\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation**: $R^2 = 99.36\%$ means **99.36% of the variation in sales is explained by the time trend alone**. The linear trend is an **excellent** model for this data.

---

## 3.4 Comparative Summary Table

| Method | Forecast for Q7 (₹ Lakhs) | Lag / Responsiveness | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|
| Naive ($F_7 = A_6$) | 290.00 | Highest | Baseline |
| 3-Period Simple MA | 270.00 | Low | Stationary series |
| Exponential Smoothing ($\alpha=0.3$) | 254.61 | Moderate-Low | Short-term, no trend |
| Linear Trend (Least Squares) | **306.00** | Adaptive | Series with clear trend |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Strategy**: When asked *"Which method is best?"* in an exam, justify using the error metric (MAD/MSE/MAPE) **and** the Tracking Signal. The method with the lowest MAD/MSE and $\vert TS \vert \le 4$ is the best.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choice of Smoothing Constant α

| $\alpha$ Value | Behavior | When to Use |
|---|---|---|
| $\alpha \to 0$ | Forecasts are **very smooth**, slow to react | Highly stable series |
| $\alpha = 0.1$ to $0.3$ | Mild responsiveness | Most business applications |
| $\alpha = 0.5$ | Balanced | Moderate trend/noise |
| $\alpha \to 1$ | Forecasts chase the data, **noisy** | Highly volatile series |
| $\alpha = 1$ | Degenerates to Naive forecast | Trivially responsive |

# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Diagram — The Complete Formatting & Forecasting Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Data Source CSV Excel ERP] --> B[Data Ingestion Stage]
    B --> C{Data Quality Check}
    C -->|Dirty| D[Data Cleaning Step]
    D --> D1[Remove Duplicates]
    D --> D2[Trim and Clean Text]
    D --> D3[Handle Missing Values]
    D --> D4[Standardize Codes and Dates]
    D1 --> E[Data Formatting Stage]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    C -->|Clean| E
    E --> E1[Apply Cell Type Casting]
    E --> E2[Apply Conditional Formatting]
    E --> E3[Apply Data Validation Rules]
    E1 --> F[Exploratory Analysis]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> G{Trend or Pattern Exists}
    G -->|No| H[Use Naive or MA]
    G -->|Yes Linear| I[Use Linear Trend]
    G -->|Yes Seasonal| J[Use Holt Winters]
    G -->|Explanatory Vars| K[Use Regression]
    H --> L[Compute Forecast]
    I --> L
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M[Compute Error Metrics MAD MSE MAPE]
    M --> N{Tracking Signal within 4}
    N -->|Yes| O[Deploy Forecast Model]
    N -->|No| P[Re tune Parameters]
    P --> L
    O --> Q[Presentation Formatting]
    Q --> R[Charts and Dashboards]
    Q --> S[Pivot Tables and Reports]
    Q --> T[KPI Cards and Sparklines]
    R --> U[Management Decision Support]
    S --> U
    T --> U
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram**: The flow moves left-to-right in four logical phases — **Ingestion → Cleaning → Formatting → Forecasting → Presentation**. The **diamond-shaped decision blocks** (G, N) act as gateways: the data path branches based on whether a trend exists, and whether the model's Tracking Signal is acceptable. KTU questions on "describe the forecasting process" map directly onto this diagram.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Architecture of a Forecast Model (Internal View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Historical Data A1 A2 A3 ... An] --> B1[Input Layer]
    B1 --> C1{Model Selection Engine}
    C1 -->|Option 1| D1[Naive Module]
    C1 -->|Option 2| D2[Moving Average Module]
    C1 -->|Option 3| D3[Exponential Smoothing Module alpha]
    C1 -->|Option 4| D4[Linear Trend Module Least Squares]
    D1 --> E1[Forecast Output F n plus 1]
    D2 --> E1
    D3 --> E1
    D4 --> E1
    E1 --> F1[Error Computation Module]
    F1 --> G1[Accuracy Metrics Layer MAD MSE MAPE]
    G1 --> H1{Model Passes Acceptance Test}
    H1 -->|Yes| I1[Approved Forecast to Dashboard]
    H1 -->|No| J1[Parameter Re tuning]
    J1 --> C1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Architectural Insight**: Notice the **closed feedback loop** from `J1` back to `C1`. This is the essence of **iterative model refinement** — if the model fails the acceptance test, parameters (window size $n$, smoothing constant $\alpha$, or trend structure) are retuned and the cycle repeats. In professional analytics tools (SAP IBP, Oracle Demantra), this loop is automated.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Decision Tree for Choosing a Forecasting Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start Have Historical Data] --> Q1{Is the Data Stationary No Trend No Seasonality}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Is Noise Level High}
    Q1 -->|No| Q3{Trend is Linear}
    Q1 -->|No| Q4{Seasonal Pattern Exists}
    Q2 -->|Yes| P1[Use Simple Moving Average]
    Q2 -->|No| P2[Use Simple Exponential Smoothing]
    Q3 -->|Yes| P3[Use Linear Trend Least Squares]
    Q3 -->|No| P4[Use Holt Linear Method]
    Q4 -->|Yes| P5{Season Length is Known}
    Q4 -->|No| P6[Use Regression or AI Model]
    Q5 -->|Yes| P7[Use Holt Winters Triple Smoothing]
    Q5 -->|No| P8[Use SARIMA Model]
    P1 --> END[Final Forecast]
    P2 --> END
    P3 --> END
    P4 --> END
    P6 --> END
    P7 --> END
    P8 --> END
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to use this in the exam**: When a 14-mark question says *"Recommend and justify a forecasting method for the given dataset"*, draw this decision tree, identify which branch applies to the data, and justify your choice using the data's statistical properties (mean, variance, trend slope, seasonal indices).
