> **Recommendation:** The model is **fit for production** for non-critical monitoring. However, for **fraud or crisis detection** (where missing a Negative is costly), the recall of 0.750 should be improved to ≥ 0.85 by retraining with more negative examples, using class-weight balancing, or switching to a BERT-based model.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusion between Interval and Ratio scales:** Forgetting that the TRUE zero test is the deciding factor. (–1 mark penalty)
> 2. **NSS denominator error:** Using only `Positive + Negative` in the denominator instead of `Positive + Negative + Neutral`. This inflates the score incorrectly. (–2 marks)
> 3. **F1 confusion:** Computing F1 with Accuracy instead of Precision/Recall in the harmonic mean. (–1 mark)
> 4. **TF-IDF smoothing term:** Forgetting the `+1` in the denominator of $idf$. (–1 mark)
> 5. **Aspect-Based vs Document-Level:** Failing to specify the granularity of sentiment when describing applications. (–1 mark)
> 6. **DIKW layering:** Listing the tiers without giving a real-world business mapping — examiners want applied reasoning, not rote definitions. (–2 marks)

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Memorize Before the Exam**

### 🔹 Data Abstractions
- ✅ **DIKW** = Data → Information → Knowledge → Wisdom.
- ✅ **Four Measurement Scales** = Nominal, Ordinal, Interval, Ratio (in ascending strength).
- ✅ **True Zero** is the unique discriminator of **Ratio** data.
- ✅ **Mean** is meaningful only for **Interval and Ratio** scales.
- ✅ **Information Entropy** $H(X)$ quantifies the average information content of a dataset.
- ✅ **Abstraction layers in analytics architecture** = Source → Lake/Warehouse → Mart → Dashboard.

### 🔹 Sentiment Analysis
- ✅ **Three approaches**: Lexicon (VADER/AFINN), Machine Learning (NB/SVM/BERT), Hybrid.
- ✅ **Three granularities**: Document-level, Sentence-level, Aspect-Based (ABSA).
- ✅ **TF-IDF** = $tf(t,d) \cdot \log_2(N / (1 + df(t)))$ — the `+1` is the smoothing constant.
- ✅ **Net Sentiment Score (NSS)** ranges from $-1$ (fully negative) to $+1$ (fully positive).
- ✅ **Precision** = quality of positive predictions; **Recall** = coverage; **F1** = their harmonic mean.
- ✅ **NSS Marketing Rule**: NSS $\in [+0.5, +1]$ → Scale; NSS $\in [-0.5, -1]$ → Crisis mode.
- ✅ **Aspect-Based Analysis** is the gold standard for product reviews ("battery is great, camera is poor").

### 🔹 Examiner Hot-Spots
- ✅ Numericals on **NSS computation** appear almost every semester.
- ✅ Confusion-matrix-based **F1 calculation** for a given class is a 7-mark favorite.
- ✅ Theory questions on **DIKW with a banking/e-commerce example** are standard 7-mark theory.
- ✅ Comparison questions (**Lexicon vs ML vs Hybrid**) are common 3-mark Part A items.
- ✅ Always mention **the +1 smoothing in TF-IDF** — students who forget lose 1 easy mark.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analytics in Digital Decoding Consumer Intent
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT522 - BUSINESS ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Analytics in Digital Decoding Consumer Intent

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Digital Consumer Intent Analytics** is the systematic application of statistical, computational, and machine-learning techniques to digital behavioral traces (search queries, clickstreams, dwell times, social signals, and transactional micro-events) in order to **infer, classify, score, and predict** the underlying purchase, informational, or navigational objective of an online user.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme lexicon, consumer intent is treated as a **latent (hidden) variable** $I$ that must be estimated from **observed digital signals** $X = \{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n\}$ using probabilistic and pattern-recognition models. The discipline sits at the intersection of:

- **Web/Mobile Analytics** (quantitative behavioural data),
- **Natural Language Processing (NLP)** (semantic intent from queries and reviews),
- **Predictive Modelling** (forecasting next-best-action),
- **Customer Journey Analytics** (multi-touch attribution).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Expected):**
> *Consumer Intent Decoding* is the process of converting **observable digital actions** into a **probabilistic estimate of the consumer's next objective**, enabling real-time personalisation, recommendation, and marketing optimisation.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you walk into a large shopping mall. You do not speak. The shopkeeper must guess — purely from your *body language* — whether you are:
- **Just browsing** (informational intent),
- **Looking for a specific brand** (navigational intent),
- **Ready to buy immediately** (transactional intent).

The shopkeeper reads subtle cues: where you pause, what you touch, how long you stand, which direction you glance. **Digital Intent Analytics is exactly this — but for the web.** Search engines, e-commerce sites, and ad platforms read the *digital body language* of a user (clicks, scrolls, hovers, time-on-page, query phrasing) and infer intent within milliseconds.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Core Idea (One Line):**
> Intent cannot be observed directly — it must be **decoded** from behavioural residues left behind in digital environments.

---

## 1.3 Key Digital Intent Signals (High-Yield for KTU)

| Signal Type | Example | Typical Metric |
|---|---|---|
| Search Query Semantics | "buy iPhone 15" vs. "iPhone 15 review" | Query classification |
| Clickstream Path | Sequence of pages visited | Markov transition probability |
| Dwell Time | Seconds spent on product page | Avg. = **120s** for buyers |
| Scroll Depth | % of page scrolled | Strong buying signal if > **75%** |
| Add-to-Cart / Wishlist | Cart addition event | Binary intent flag |
| Repeat Visits | Return sessions | Conversion likelihood × **2.4** |
| Referral Source | Google Ad vs. organic | Higher intent from paid |
| Social Engagement | Likes, shares, comments | Brand affinity proxy |

---

## 1.4 The Three Classical Intent Categories (Foundational)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Broder's Taxonomy of Web Search Intent (KTU-Favoured Framework):**
> 1. **Navigational** — User wants a *specific website* (e.g., "Facebook login").
> 2. **Informational** — User seeks *knowledge* (e.g., "what is GST?").
> 3. **Transactional** — User intends to *complete an action* (e.g., "book flight to Dubai").

A modern extension adds a fourth: **Commercial Investigation Intent** (e.g., "best laptop under 50000"), which lies between informational and transactional.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework: The Intent Decoding Pipeline

The KTU 2024 Board expects students to describe consumer-intent analytics as a **5-stage sequential pipeline**. Each stage transforms raw digital noise into progressively higher-value intent intelligence.

### Stage 1 — Data Acquisition (Signal Collection)
- Clickstream logs, server logs, SDK events, CRM touchpoints.
- **Goal:** Capture every observable digital footprint.

### Stage 2 — Feature Engineering (Signal Transformation)
- Convert raw events into engineered features: recency, frequency, monetary (RFM), session depth, query embeddings.
- **Goal:** Translate behaviour into *model-ready variables*.

### Stage 3 — Intent Classification / Scoring
- Apply supervised models (Logistic Regression, Random Forest, XGBoost) or unsupervised clustering (K-Means) to assign an **intent label** or **intent probability score**.
- **Goal:** Estimate $P(I = \text{Transactional} \mid X)$.

### Stage 4 — Personalisation / Activation
- Feed the intent estimate into recommendation engines, ad-bidding algorithms, or chatbot dialogue managers.
- **Goal:** Convert inference into *business action*.

### Stage 5 — Feedback & Model Refresh
- Closed-loop learning: actual conversions retrain the model.
- **Goal:** Continuous accuracy improvement (online learning).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mnemonic — "D-C-F-P-F"**: **D**ata → **C**ode features → **F**it model → **P**ersonalise → **F**eedback.

---

## 2.2 Probabilistic Foundation: Bayes' Theorem for Intent

The mathematical heart of intent decoding is **Bayesian inference**. Given observed signals $X$, the probability of intent $I_k$ is:

$$P(I_k \mid X) = \frac{P(X \mid I_k) \cdot P(I_k)}{P(X)}$$

Where:
- $P(I_k \mid X)$ — **Posterior** probability of intent $k$ (what we want),
- $P(X \mid I_k)$ — **Likelihood** of seeing signals $X$ given intent $k$,
- $P(I_k)$ — **Prior** probability of intent $k$ (base rate),
- $P(X)$ — **Evidence** (normalising constant).

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Board-Essential)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master every formula below — these appear directly in KTU 2024 Part A and Part B papers.**

| # | Formula / Metric | Expression | Application Context |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Click-Through Rate (CTR) | $\text{CTR} = \dfrac{\text{Clicks}}{\text{Impressions}} \times 100\%$ | Ad performance |
| 2 | Conversion Rate (CVR) | $\text{CVR} = \dfrac{\text{Conversions}}{\text{Visitors}} \times 100\%$ | Funnel effectiveness |
| 3 | Bounce Rate | $\text{BR} = \dfrac{\text{Single-page Sessions}}{\text{Total Sessions}} \times 100\%$ | Engagement health |
| 4 | Average Order Value (AOV) | $\text{AOV} = \dfrac{\text{Total Revenue}}{\text{Number of Orders}}$ | Transactional intent |
| 5 | Cart Abandonment Rate | $\text{CAR} = \dfrac{\text{Carts Created} - \text{Carts Converted}}{\text{Carts Created}} \times 100\%$ | Friction analysis |
| 6 | Engagement Rate | $\text{ER} = \dfrac{\text{Interactions}}{\text{Reach}} \times 100\%$ | Social intent |
| 7 | Bayes Posterior (Intent) | $P(I_k \mid X) = \dfrac{P(X \mid I_k) \cdot P(I_k)}{P(X)}$ | Probabilistic intent decoding |
| 8 | Logistic Regression (Intent Probability) | $P(I = 1 \mid X) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-(\beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \cdots + \beta_n x_n)}}$ | Binary intent classification |
| 9 | Customer Lifetime Value (CLV) | $\text{CLV} = \text{AOV} \times \text{Purchase Frequency} \times \text{Customer Lifespan}$ | Strategic intent valuation |
| 10 | Lift in Association Rule Mining | $\text{Lift}(A \rightarrow B) = \dfrac{P(A \cap B)}{P(A) \cdot P(B)}$ | Cross-sell intent |
| 11 | RFM Score | Composite of Recency, Frequency, Monetary ranks | Customer segmentation |
| 12 | Intent Score (Composite) | $S_I = w_1 R + w_2 F + w_3 M + w_4 E$ (weights sum to 1) | Real-time intent ranking |

**Important Note on Notation:** In the table, $\vert$ has been replaced with $\mid$ for absolute-value expressions to prevent markdown table breakage.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering & Business Utility

- **Search Engines (Google, Bing):** Decode billions of queries daily into intent buckets to serve relevant ads.
- **E-Commerce (Amazon, Flipkart):** Real-time intent scoring drives product ranking and "Recommended for You" carousels.
- **FinTech (Loan Apps):** Detect *transactional* intent (e.g., "apply for personal loan") vs. casual browsing.
- **Voice Assistants (Alexa, Siri):** Speech-to-intent pipelines convert utterances into action classes.
- **Healthcare Portals:** Decode patient intent (symptom search vs. appointment booking) for triage.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** The Board tests whether you can *connect* the math to a *business outcome* (e.g., "How does Bayes' Theorem improve ad targeting ROI?"). Always close your answers with a one-line **business-impact statement**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1 — Bayesian Intent Probability

**Problem Setup (KTU-Standard):**
A digital retail platform observes two key signals from a user session:
- $x_1$ = "Added item to cart" (binary: 1 if yes, 0 if no),
- $x_2$ = "Time on product page > 90 seconds" (binary: 1 if yes, 0 if no).

Historical data (the base rates) are:
- $P(\text{Transactional Intent } I_T) = 0.20$
- $P(\text{Informational Intent } I_I) = 0.80$
- $P(x_1 = 1 \mid I_T) = 0.70$, $P(x_1 = 1 \mid I_I) = 0.15$
- $P(x_2 = 1 \mid I_T) = 0.60$, $P(x_2 = 1 \mid I_I) = 0.25$

**Assumption:** $x_1$ and $x_2$ are **conditionally independent** given the intent (Naive Bayes assumption).

**Step 1 — State Bayes' Theorem for Transactional Intent:**

$$P(I_T \mid x_1, x_2) = \frac{P(x_1, x_2 \mid I_T) \cdot P(I_T)}{P(x_1, x_2)}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Conditional Independence (Naive Bayes) Assumption:**

Since $x_1$ and $x_2$ are independent given $I_T$:

$$P(x_1, x_2 \mid I_T) = P(x_1 \mid I_T) \cdot P(x_2 \mid I_T)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the Given Numerical Likelihoods:**

$$P(x_1 = 1, x_2 = 1 \mid I_T) = 0.70 \times 0.60 = 0.42$$

$$P(x_1 = 1, x_2 = 1 \mid I_I) = 0.15 \times 0.25 = 0.0375$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Numerator for Transactional Intent:**

$$P(x_1, x_2 \mid I_T) \cdot P(I_T) = 0.42 \times 0.20 = 0.084$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Numerator for Informational Intent:**

$$P(x_1, x_2 \mid I_I) \cdot P(I_I) = 0.0375 \times 0.80 = 0.030$$

**Step 6 — Apply the Law of Total Probability for the Denominator (Evidence):**

$$P(x_1, x_2) = P(x_1, x_2 \mid I_T) \cdot P(I_T) + P(x_1, x_2 \mid I_I) \cdot P(I_I)$$

$$P(x_1, x_2) = 0.084 + 0.030 = 0.114$$

**Step 7 — Final Posterior Probability for Transactional Intent:**

$$P(I_T \mid x_1 = 1, x_2 = 1) = \frac{0.084}{0.114} \approx 0.7368$$

**Step 8 — Final Posterior Probability for Informational Intent:**

$$P(I_I \mid x_1 = 1, x_2 = 1) = \frac{0.030}{0.114} \approx 0.2632$$

**Step 9 — Interpretation & Business Decision:**

Because $0.7368 > 0.2632$, the model **classifies the user as having Transactional Intent**. The platform can therefore:
- Show a "Buy Now" CTA (Call-To-Action),
- Prioritise high-margin upsell offers,
- Allocate premium ad-budget real-estate for this session.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The user's *base rate* of being transactional was only **20%** — but the *behavioural evidence* (cart-add + long dwell) pushed the posterior to **73.68%**. This is the *exact* mechanism by which analytics *decodes* intent.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2 — Composite Intent Score (Weighted RFM)

A second KTU-favourite calculation is the **composite intent score** $S_I$ used in lead-scoring and CRM analytics.

**Formula:**

$$S_I = w_R \cdot R_{\text{score}} + w_F \cdot F_{\text{score}} + w_M \cdot M_{\text{score}} + w_E \cdot E_{\text{score}}$$

Where:
- $R_{\text{score}}$ = normalised recency rank (0–100),
- $F_{\text{score}}$ = normalised frequency rank (0–100),
- $M_{\text{score}}$ = normalised monetary rank (0–100),
- $E_{\text{score}}$ = engagement score (0–100),
- Weights: $w_R = 0.30$, $w_F = 0.25$, $w_M = 0.30$, $w_E = 0.15$ (must sum to 1).

**Step 1 — Verify Weight Sum:**

$$w_R + w_F + w_M + w_E = 0.30 + 0.25 + 0.30 + 0.15 = 1.00 \checkmark$$

**Step 2 — Plug in Sample Customer Data:**

Let $R_{\text{score}} = 85$, $F_{\text{score}} = 60$, $M_{\text{score}} = 90$, $E_{\text{score}} = 70$.

**Step 3 — Multiply Each Component by Its Weight:**

$$w_R \cdot R = 0.30 \times 85 = 25.5$$

$$w_F \cdot F = 0.25 \times 60 = 15.0$$

$$w_M \cdot M = 0.30 \times 90 = 27.0$$

$$w_E \cdot E = 0.15 \times 70 = 10.5$$

**Step 4 — Sum to Obtain Final Intent Score:**

$$S_I = 25.5 + 15.0 + 27.0 + 10.5 = 78.0$$

**Step 5 — Classification Threshold (KTU-Standard):**

| Score Range | Intent Tier | Marketing Action |
|---|---|---|
| $S_I \geq 80$ | **High Intent** | Direct sales call, premium offer |
| $50 \le S_I < 80$ | **Warm Intent** | Nurture email, retargeting ad |
| $S_I < 50$ | **Cold Intent** | Awareness campaign, top-of-funnel content |

Since $S_I = 78.0$, the customer falls in the **Warm Intent** band → assign to retargeting funnel.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Decoding Technique

| Business Question | Best Decoding Technique | Output | KTU Board Favourite? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Will user buy in next 7 days? | Logistic Regression / XGBoost | Probability (0–1) | ✅ Yes |
| Which segment does user belong to? | K-Means / DBSCAN Clustering | Cluster ID | ✅ Yes |
| What is the *type* of intent? | Naive Bayes / SVM | Class label (Nav/Info/Trans) | ✅ Yes |
| What will user do *next*? | Markov Chains / RNN | Next state prediction | ✅ Yes |
| What is user *feeling* about brand? | Sentiment Analysis (NLP) | Polarity score (-1 to +1) | ✅ Yes |
| Which products to recommend? | Association Rule Mining (Apriori) | Rule set with Lift > 1 | ⚠ Sometimes |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Intent Decoding Framework (End-to-End Architecture)

The block diagram below maps the complete pipeline of how raw digital signals are transformed into actionable consumer intent intelligence. It is a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** as mandated by the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE protocol.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[User Digital Touchpoints] --> A2[Data Ingestion Layer]
    A2 --> A3[Data Lake and Warehouse]
    A3 --> A4[Feature Engineering Module]
    A4 --> A5[Intent Classification Engine]
    A5 --> A6[Intent Score and Label Output]
    A6 --> A7[Personalisation and Activation Layer]
    A7 --> A8[Real Time Decision Engine]
    A8 --> A9[Feedback Loop and Model Retraining]
    A9 --> A5
    A8 --> A10[Business KPI Dashboard]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- **A1 → A2:** Touchpoints (web, mobile, social) feed into the ingestion layer.
- **A3:** Centralised storage (BigQuery, Snowflake, Redshift).
- **A4:** Feature engineering transforms raw events into RFM, session depth, query embeddings.
- **A5:** The classification engine (Bayes, Logistic, XGBoost) is the *brain*.
- **A6:** Output is a structured intent record.
- **A7 → A8:** Activation in real time (bidding, recommendation).
- **A9:** Closed-loop retraining ensures model freshness.
- **A10:** Business KPIs (CTR, CVR, ROAS) flow back for monitoring.

---

## 4.2 Consumer Intent Classification Hierarchy (Broder's Taxonomy + Extension)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B1[Digital Consumer Intent]
    B1 --> B2[Navigational Intent]
    B1 --> B3[Informational Intent]
    B1 --> B4[Transactional Intent]
    B1 --> B5[Commercial Investigation Intent]
    B2 --> B2a[User wants specific website]
    B2 --> B2b[Example: Facebook login]
    B3 --> B3a[User seeks knowledge]
    B3 --> B3b[Example: what is GST]
    B4 --> B4a[User wants to complete action]
    B4 --> B4b[Example: book flight Dubai]
    B5 --> B5a[User comparing options]
    B5 --> B5b[Example: best phone under 30000]
```

**Key Insight:** The four-way split is the **most-tested diagram** in KTU 2024 Part A questions. Memorise the example query for each category.

---

## 4.3 Customer Journey Funnel with Intent-Decoding Touchpoints

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C1[Awareness Stage] --> C2[Consideration Stage]
    C2 --> C3[Decision Stage]
    C3 --> C4[Purchase Stage]
    C4 --> C5[Post Purchase Loyalty]
    C1 -.-> D1[Display Ads and SEO]
    C2 -.-> D2[Retargeting and Content Marketing]
    C3 -.-> D3[Email Nurture and Reviews]
    C4 -.-> D4[Cart Recovery and Offers]
    C5 -.-> D5[CRM and Loyalty Programmes]
    D1 -. Intent Signals .-> E1[Impressions CTR Bounce]
    D2 -. Intent Signals .-> E2[Dwell Time Scroll Depth]
    D3 -. Intent Signals .-> E3[Wishlist Email Open]
    D4 -. Intent Signals .-> E4[Cart Add Coupon Use]
    D5 -. Intent Signals .-> E5[Repeat Purchase NPS]
```

**Reading the Diagram:** Each funnel stage generates a *unique family* of intent signals. The decoding model must be *stage-aware* — a strong scroll depth is highly meaningful in the *Consideration* stage but nearly meaningless in *Post-Purchase Loyalty*.

---

## 4.4 Bayes-Based Intent Decoding Flow (Computational Sequence)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F1[Observed Signal Vector X] --> F2[Compute Likelihood P of X given I equals T]
    F1 --> F3[Compute Likelihood P of X given I equals I info]
    F2 --> F4[Multiply by Prior P of I equals T]
    F3 --> F5[Multiply by Prior P of I equals I info]
    F4 --> F6[Sum Both for Evidence P of X]
    F5 --> F6
    F6 --> F7[Divide Each Numerator by Evidence]
    F7 --> F8[Posterior P of I equals T given X]
    F7 --> F9[Posterior P of I equals I info given X]
    F8 --> F10[Argmax Decision Rule]
    F9 --> F10
    F10 --> F11[Final Intent Label]
```

**Reading the Diagram:** This is the *exact computational graph* of Naive Bayes intent classification. The **argmax** operator at F10 selects the intent class with the highest posterior probability — this is the **decision rule** in the Bayes Classifier.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
**Define Digital Consumer Intent Analytics. List the three classical categories of web search intent with one example query for each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 + 2 distribution):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Digital Consumer Intent Analytics is the process of inferring a user's underlying objective from their observable digital behaviour (clicks, queries, dwell time) using statistical and machine-learning models.
- **Three Categories with Examples (2 marks — 1 for naming, 1 for examples):**

| Category | Example Query |
|---|---|
| Navigational | "Gmail login" |
| Informational | "What is blockchain?" |
| Transactional | "Buy Nike shoes online" |

---

### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Sample Paper]`
**Explain the role of Click-Through Rate (CTR) and Conversion Rate (CVR) as digital intent signals. Why is a high CTR with a low CVR a red flag?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **CTR (1 mark):** $\text{CTR} = \dfrac{\text{Clicks}}{\text{Impressions}} \times 100\%$ — measures how compelling the ad/listing is to attract a click. A high CTR signals strong *attention-capture* but not necessarily purchase intent.
- **CVR (1 mark):** $\text{CVR} = \dfrac{\text{Conversions}}{\text{Visitors}} \times 100\%$ — measures the percentage of visitors who complete the desired action. High CVR is the strongest signal of *transactional intent*.
- **Red Flag (1 mark):** High CTR with low CVR suggests **clickbait, mis-targeted ads, or landing-page friction**. The creative promises intent the page fails to deliver, leading to wasted ad spend and low ROAS (Return on Ad Spend).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** split across two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### 📌 QUESTION A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**"Consumer intent cannot be observed directly — it must be decoded from behavioural signals." In the context of this statement:**

**(a)** Explain the Naive Bayes approach to classifying consumer intent into Transactional vs. Informational. State the key assumptions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Given the following session data for 1,000 users on an e-commerce platform, compute the posterior probability of Transactional Intent for a user who **(i)** added an item to cart **and** **(ii)** spent more than 90 seconds on the product page. Comment on the business action implied. **(7 Marks)**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| $P(\text{Transactional Intent } I_T)$ | **0.20** |
| $P(\text{Informational Intent } I_I)$ | **0.80** |
| $P(\text{Cart-Add} = 1 \mid I_T)$ | **0.70** |
| $P(\text{Cart-Add} = 1 \mid I_I)$ | **0.15** |
| $P(\text{Dwell} > 90s \mid I_T)$ | **0.60** |
| $P(\text{Dwell} > 90s \mid I_I)$ | **0.25** |

---

#### 📝 Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Naive Bayes for Intent Classification (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — The Core Idea (1 mark):**
Naive Bayes is a probabilistic classifier that uses Bayes' Theorem with a *strong independence assumption* among features. It is widely used in intent classification because:
- It is fast, scalable, and works well with high-dimensional sparse data (like search queries and clickstreams).
- It provides calibrated probability estimates, not just hard labels.

**Step 2 — Bayes' Theorem (2 marks):**

$$P(I_k \mid X) = \frac{P(X \mid I_k) \cdot P(I_k)}{P(X)}$$

Where $I_k$ is the $k$-th intent class (e.g., Transactional or Informational) and $X = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)$ is the observed signal vector.

**Step 3 — The Naive Assumption (2 marks):**
The "naive" assumption is that all features are **conditionally independent given the intent class**:

$$P(X \mid I_k) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid I_k)$$

This simplifies the joint likelihood from an exponential computation to a simple product. For example, with two signals (cart-add, dwell-time):

$$P(x_1, x_2 \mid I_T) = P(x_1 \mid I_T) \cdot P(x_2 \mid I_T)$$

**Step 4 — Decision Rule (1 mark):**
The classifier selects the intent class with the **maximum posterior**:

$$\hat{I} = \arg\max_{k} P(I_k) \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid I_k)$$

**Step 5 — Limitation (1 mark):**
The independence assumption is often violated in practice (e.g., cart-add and long dwell-time are correlated). Despite this, Naive Bayes performs surprisingly well in text and behavioural intent tasks.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Posterior Computation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Write Bayes' Formula (1 mark):**

$$P(I_T \mid x_1 = 1, x_2 = 1) = \frac{P(x_1, x_2 \mid I_T) \cdot P(I_T)}{P(x_1, x_2)}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Conditional Independence (1 mark):**

$$P(x_1 = 1, x_2 = 1 \mid I_T) = 0.70 \times 0.60 = 0.42$$

$$P(x_1 = 1, x_2 = 1 \mid I_I) = 0.15 \times 0.25 = 0.0375$$

**Step 3 — Compute Numerators (1 mark):**

$$P(x_1, x_2 \mid I_T) \cdot P(I_T) = 0.42 \times 0.20 = 0.084$$

$$P(x_1, x_2 \mid I_I) \cdot P(I_I) = 0.0375 \times 0.80 = 0.030$$

**Step 4 — Total Probability for Evidence (1 mark):**

$$P(x_1, x_2) = 0.084 + 0.030 = 0.114$$

**Step 5 — Final Posterior (1 mark):**

$$P(I_T \mid x_1 = 1, x_2 = 1) = \frac{0.084}{0.114} = 0.7368 \text{ (or 73.68\%)}$$

**Step 6 — Business Action (2 marks):**
- The posterior of **73.68%** is far above the prior of **20%** → strong evidence of Transactional Intent.
- **Recommended actions:** (i) Show a "Complete Purchase" CTA, (ii) Offer a time-limited discount to reduce cart abandonment, (iii) Allocate premium ad inventory to similar lookalike users, (iv) Trigger an exit-intent popup if user attempts to leave the checkout page.

---

### 📌 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Sample Paper]`

**"Intent scoring is the bridge between raw analytics and personalised marketing." Elaborate.**

**(a)** Define an **Intent Score**. Construct a composite weighted formula using Recency (R), Frequency (F), Monetary (M), and Engagement (E) signals. State the property the weights must satisfy. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a customer with the following normalised scores and weights, calculate the Intent Score and classify the customer into Hot / Warm / Cold tiers: **(7 Marks)**

| Component | Score (0–100) | Weight |
|---|---|---|
| Recency (R) | 85 | 0.30 |
| Frequency (F) | 60 | 0.25 |
| Monetary (M) | 90 | 0.30 |
| Engagement (E) | 70 | 0.15 |

Use the thresholds: Hot ≥ 80, Warm 50–79, Cold < 50.

---

#### 📝 Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Intent Score Definition & Formula (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Definition (2 marks):**
An **Intent Score** is a *single composite numeric value* (typically 0–100) that quantifies a user's likelihood to convert (purchase, sign-up, etc.) in the near term, computed as a weighted aggregation of multiple behavioural signals. It enables marketers to **rank and prioritise** thousands of leads systematically.

**Step 2 — The Formula (3 marks):**

$$S_I = w_R \cdot R_{\text{score}} + w_F \cdot F_{\text{score}} + w_M \cdot M_{\text{score}} + w_E \cdot E_{\text{score}}$$

**Step 3 — Weight Constraint (1 mark):**

$$\sum_{i} w_i = w_R + w_F + w_M + w_E = 1.00$$

This normalisation ensures the final score lies on the same 0–100 scale as the inputs.

**Step 4 — Significance (1 mark):**
Intent scoring is the *operational layer* of analytics — it converts descriptive data (what happened) into **prescriptive action** (whom to call, what to show).

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Verify Weights Sum to 1 (1 mark):**

$$0.30 + 0.25 + 0.30 + 0.15 = 1.00 \checkmark$$

**Step 2 — Compute Each Weighted Component (3 marks):**

$$w_R \cdot R = 0.30 \times 85 = 25.50$$

$$w_F \cdot F = 0.25 \times 60 = 15.00$$

$$w_M \cdot M = 0.30 \times 90 = 27.00$$

$$w_E \cdot E = 0.15 \times 70 = 10.50$$

**Step 3 — Sum the Components (1 mark):**

$$S_I = 25.50 + 15.00 + 27.00 + 10.50 = 78.00$$

**Step 4 — Classification (1 mark):**
Since $50 \le 78 < 80$, the customer is classified as **WARM INTENT**.

**Step 5 — Business Action (1 mark):**
- Enrol the customer in a **nurture email sequence**,
- Show **retargeting ads** with mid-funnel content (case studies, testimonials),
- Do **not** yet assign a sales rep (Hot tier only) — but schedule one if score climbs above 80 in the next 30 days.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Evidence Term:** Many students compute the numerator but *omit* the denominator $P(X)$ in Bayes' Theorem. Without it, the probabilities will not sum to 1 → **lose 2 marks**.
> 2. **Skipping the Weight Sum Check:** When asked to "construct a weighted formula," you must explicitly state $\sum w_i = 1$. Examiners deduct **1 mark** for omitting this normalisation property.
> 3. **Not Linking to Business Action:** KTU 2024 Scheme uses **Outcome-Based Education (OBE)**. Any analytics question that ends *without* a business-impact sentence is considered incomplete → **lose 1–2 marks**.
> 4. **Confusing Prior with Posterior:** The **prior** is the base rate *before* observing signals; the **posterior** is *after*. Mixing them up is a **fatal error** worth up to **3 marks**.
> 5. **Mermaid Label Errors in Diagrams:** When drawing Broder's taxonomy, students often mislabel *Commercial Investigation* as a sub-type of Informational. It is a **distinct fourth category**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Consumer Intent** is a *latent variable* inferred from observable digital signals.
- **Three classical intent types** (Broder): Navigational, Informational, Transactional → plus **Commercial Investigation** as the modern fourth.
- **Key behavioural signals:** CTR, CVR, bounce rate, dwell time, scroll depth, cart-adds, repeat visits.
- **Bayes' Theorem** is the mathematical engine of intent decoding:

$$P(I_k \mid X) = \frac{P(X \mid I_k) \cdot P(I_k)}{P(X)}$$

- **Naive Bayes Assumption:** Features are conditionally independent given the intent class — simplifies joint likelihood to a product of marginals.
- **Decision Rule:** Choose the class with the **highest posterior probability** (argmax).
- **Intent Score Formula:** $S_I = w_R R + w_F F + w_M M + w_E E$ with $\sum w_i = 1$.
- **Tiers (typical):** Hot $\ge 80$, Warm $50$–$79$, Cold $< 50$.
- **Pipeline Mnemonic — D-C-F-P-F:** Data → Code features → Fit model → Personalise → Feedback.
- **Business Impact Line (mandatory in KTU answers):** Always close with "This enables [personalisation / targeting / retargeting / ROAS improvement]."
- **Logistic Regression form (for binary intent):**

$$P(I = 1 \mid X) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(\beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \cdots + \beta_n x_n)}}$$

- **Customer Journey Stages:** Awareness → Consideration → Decision → Purchase → Loyalty (each has its own dominant signal family).
- **Closed-Loop Learning:** Intent models must be *retrained* on actual conversion outcomes to remain accurate (concept drift mitigation).
- **Two KTU-Standard Diagrams to Revise:** (i) Broder's four-way taxonomy, (ii) Customer journey funnel with signal annotations.
- **Common Pitfall:** Do not confuse *click intent* (CTR) with *purchase intent* (CVR). Both are needed for a complete picture.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Decoding Customer Sentiments from Comments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT522 - BUSINESS ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Decoding Customer Sentiments from Comments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Decoding Customer Sentiments from Comments

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> **Sentiment Analysis (Opinion Mining)** is the Natural Language Processing (NLP) and computational linguistics technique used to systematically identify, extract, quantify, and study affective states and subjective information from textual customer comments, reviews, and feedback to determine the writer's attitude toward a product, service, brand, or topic — typically classified as **positive**, **negative**, or **neutral** polarity.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a restaurant owner who receives 5,000 Google reviews. Reading each one manually is impossible. Instead, the owner hires a "digital emotion reader" that scans every review and quickly labels it as a smiley (positive), a sad face (negative), or a neutral face (indifferent). This digital reader does exactly what **Sentiment Analysis** does — it decodes the *emotional fingerprint* hidden inside text.

In business terms:
- A comment like *"The delivery was lightning fast and the packaging was amazing!"* → **Positive Sentiment**
- *"Worst experience ever, the product broke in two days."* → **Negative Sentiment**
- *"The item was delivered on Tuesday."* → **Neutral Sentiment**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 Scheme specifically focuses on **decoding polarity**, **aspect-based sentiment**, and **business applications of text mining** to drive Customer Relationship Management (CRM) decisions.

### The Three Pillars of Sentiment Classification

| Polarity Class | Description | Business Implication |
|----------------|-------------|----------------------|
| **Positive** | Customer expresses satisfaction, joy, or praise | Brand advocacy, repeat purchase |
| **Negative** | Customer expresses anger, frustration, or disappointment | Churn risk, service recovery |
| **Neutral** | Customer states facts without emotional tone | Informational only, low action priority |

### Granular Levels of Sentiment Analysis

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Analytical Depths used in KTU Module 4:**

1. **Document-Level Sentiment** — The entire comment is assigned one overall polarity.
2. **Sentence-Level Sentiment** — Each sentence within a comment is scored independently.
3. **Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis (ABSA)** — Specific product/service features (e.g., "battery", "delivery", "taste") are extracted and sentiment is tied directly to them.

**Example of ABSA:**
> *"The camera is fantastic, but the battery drains too fast."*
- Aspect: **Camera** → Sentiment: Positive
- Aspect: **Battery** → Sentiment: Negative

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2. The Sentiment Analysis Pipeline (5-Stage Framework)

The end-to-end process of decoding customer sentiments follows a structured data science pipeline:

### Stage 1: Data Collection (Text Acquisition)
- **Sources:** E-commerce reviews (Amazon, Flipkart), social media (Twitter/X, Facebook), customer surveys, chatbot transcripts, support tickets, app store reviews.
- **Format:** Unstructured text — the hardest data type to analyze.

### Stage 2: Text Pre-Processing (Cleaning)
Raw text is noisy. It must be normalized:
- **Tokenization** — Splitting sentences into words/tokens.
- **Lowercasing** — "GREAT" → "great".
- **Stop Word Removal** — Eliminating "the", "is", "a", "an".
- **Stemming** — "running", "ran", "runs" → "run" (chops word ends).
- **Lemmatization** — More accurate root form using vocabulary (better than stemming).
- **Punctuation & Special Character Removal** — Strip "@", "#", emojis (or convert to text).
- **Negation Handling** — "not good" must NOT be treated as positive.

### Stage 3: Feature Extraction (Text → Numbers)
Machines cannot read words. Text is converted to numerical vectors:
- **Bag of Words (BoW)** — Frequency count of each word.
- **TF-IDF (Term Frequency–Inverse Document Frequency)** — Weights rare but important words higher.
- **Word Embeddings (Word2Vec, GloVe)** — Dense vector representations capturing semantic meaning.

### Stage 4: Sentiment Classification (The Core Engine)
Three methodological families:

| Method | Type | Example Tools | Strength |
|--------|------|---------------|----------|
| **Lexicon-Based** | Rule + Dictionary | VADER, AFINN, TextBlob | Fast, no training data needed |
| **Machine Learning** | Supervised Learning | Naive Bayes, SVM, Logistic Regression | High accuracy with labeled data |
| **Deep Learning** | Neural Networks | LSTM, BERT, RoBERTa | Handles context & sarcasm |

### Stage 5: Visualization & Business Reporting
- **Word Clouds** — Visual frequency of words.
- **Sentiment Trend Graphs** — Polarity over time.
- **Pie Charts / Bar Charts** — Distribution of sentiments.
- **Net Promoter Score (NPS) Dashboards** — Quantified loyalty metric.

---

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for KTU 2024 ESE — Memorize These:**

### 1. TF (Term Frequency)
Measures how frequently a term $t$ appears in a document $d$.

$$
TF(t,d) = \frac{\text{Number of times term } t \text{ appears in document } d}{\text{Total number of terms in document } d}
$$

### 2. IDF (Inverse Document Frequency)
Reduces the weight of common words (like "the") appearing in many documents.

$$
IDF(t,D) = \log\left(\frac{N}{df_t}\right)
$$

Where:
- $N$ = Total number of documents in the corpus $D$
- $df_t$ = Number of documents containing term $t$

### 3. TF-IDF Combined Score
$$
\text{TF-IDF}(t,d,D) = TF(t,d) \times IDF(t,D)
$$

### 4. Polarity Score (Lexicon Approach)
$$
\text{Polarity Score} = \frac{\text{Positive Word Count} - \text{Negative Word Count}}{\text{Total Word Count}}
$$

Range: $-1 \leq \text{Polarity Score} \leq +1$

### 5. Subjectivity Score
$$
\text{Subjectivity} = \frac{\text{Subjective Word Count}}{\text{Total Word Count}}
$$

Range: $0$ (purely objective) to $1$ (purely subjective).

### 6. Accuracy (Performance Metric)
$$
\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}
$$

### 7. Precision
$$
\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}
$$

### 8. Recall (Sensitivity)
$$
\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}
$$

### 9. F1-Score (Harmonic Mean)
$$
F1 = 2 \times \frac{\text{Precision} \times \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}
$$

### 10. Net Promoter Score (NPS) — Business KPI
$$
\text{NPS} = \%\text{Promoters} - \%\text{Detractors}
$$

Range: $-100$ to $+100$

### 11. Customer Satisfaction Score (CSAT)
$$
\text{CSAT} = \frac{\text{Number of Satisfied Customers (rating } \geq 4)}{\text{Total Survey Responses}} \times 100
$$

### Critical Notation Table

| Symbol | Meaning | Range $\vert$ Domain $\vert$ |
|--------|---------|-----------------------------|
| $TP$ | True Positive | $\mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}$ |
| $TN$ | True Negative | $\mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}$ |
| $FP$ | False Positive (Type I Error) | $\mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}$ |
| $FN$ | False Negative (Type II Error) | $\mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}$ |
| $N$ | Total Documents | $\mathbb{Z}_{> 0}$ |
| $df_t$ | Document Frequency of term $t$ | $\mathbb{Z}_{> 0}$ |

---

## Real-World Utility of Sentiment Analysis in Business

1. **Brand Monitoring** — Track brand reputation in real-time.
2. **Product Development** — Identify which features customers love or hate.
3. **Customer Service Prioritization** — Route negative tickets to senior agents first.
4. **Marketing Campaign Optimization** — Measure audience emotional response.
5. **Competitive Intelligence** — Compare sentiment toward competitors.
6. **Stock Market Prediction** — Financial firms use Twitter sentiment to predict stock movement.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3A. Detailed Worked Example: Computing TF-IDF Manually

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A corpus $D$ contains $N = 3$ customer reviews:
> - **D1:** *"great product fast delivery"*
> - **D2:** *"bad quality slow delivery"*
> - **D3:** *"great quality fast service"*
>
> **Task:** Compute the TF-IDF score for the word **"fast"** in document D1.

### Step 1: Build the Vocabulary
The unique terms across all documents are:
$$
V = \{\text{great, product, fast, delivery, bad, quality, slow, service}\}
$$
Total $|V| = 8$ terms.

### Step 2: Compute Term Frequency (TF) for "fast" in D1

Document D1 contains 4 terms total: "great", "product", "fast", "delivery". The word "fast" appears **1 time**.

$$
TF(\text{fast}, D1) = \frac{\text{Count of "fast" in D1}}{\text{Total words in D1}} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25
$$

### Step 3: Compute Inverse Document Frequency (IDF) for "fast"

The word "fast" appears in:
- D1: Yes
- D2: No
- D3: Yes

So $df_{\text{fast}} = 2$ documents out of $N = 3$.

$$
IDF(\text{fast}, D) = \log\left(\frac{N}{df_t}\right) = \log\left(\frac{3}{2}\right) = \log(1.5) \approx 0.4055
$$

> Using natural logarithm (base $e$).

### Step 4: Compute Final TF-IDF Score

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TF-IDF}(\text{fast}, D1, D) &= TF(\text{fast}, D1) \times IDF(\text{fast}, D) \\
&= 0.25 \times 0.4055 \\
&\approx 0.1014
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** A TF-IDF score of **0.1014** indicates that the word "fast" is moderately important in D1 — it appears in D1 but is not extremely rare across the corpus.

---

## 3B. Worked Example: Sentiment Polarity Scoring (Lexicon Method)

> **Review:** *"The food was absolutely delicious but the service was terribly slow."*
> **Lexicon provided:** Positive words: {absolutely, delicious} = +1 each; Negative words: {terribly, slow} = -1 each.

### Step 1: Count Positive and Negative Words
- Positive count: $P = 2$ (absolutely, delicious)
- Negative count: $N = 2$ (terribly, slow)
- Total meaningful words: $T = 6$

### Step 2: Apply Polarity Formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Polarity Score} &= \frac{P - N}{T} \\
&= \frac{2 - 2}{6} \\
&= \frac{0}{6} \\
&= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** The review has a **neutral overall polarity** (0.0) despite containing both positive and negative words — a perfect example of why **Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis** is more useful than document-level scoring.

### Step 3: Aspect-Based Decomposition
- **Food aspect** → Polarity = $+2/2 = +1.0$ (Positive)
- **Service aspect** → Polarity = $-2/2 = -1.0$ (Negative)

---

## 3C. Confusion Matrix Calculation Example

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Scenario:** A sentiment classifier is tested on **200** customer comments.
> Results obtained:
> - True Positives (TP) = 80
> - True Negatives (TN) = 70
> - False Positives (FP) = 20
> - False Negatives (FN) = 30
>
> **Task:** Calculate Accuracy, Precision, Recall, and F1-Score.

### Step 1: Accuracy

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Accuracy} &= \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} \\
&= \frac{80 + 70}{80 + 70 + 20 + 30} \\
&= \frac{150}{200} \\
&= 0.75 \text{ or } 75\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Precision

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Precision} &= \frac{TP}{TP + FP} \\
&= \frac{80}{80 + 20} \\
&= \frac{80}{100} \\
&= 0.80 \text{ or } 80\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Recall

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Recall} &= \frac{TP}{TP + FN} \\
&= \frac{80}{80 + 30} \\
&= \frac{80}{110} \\
&\approx 0.7273 \text{ or } 72.73\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: F1-Score

$$
\begin{aligned}
F1 &= 2 \times \frac{\text{Precision} \times \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}} \\
&= 2 \times \frac{0.80 \times 0.7273}{0.80 + 0.7273} \\
&= 2 \times \frac{0.5818}{1.5273} \\
&\approx 2 \times 0.3809 \\
&\approx 0.7618 \text{ or } 76.18\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Interpretation:** The classifier is reasonably good (76% F1), but Recall is lower than Precision — the model is missing some positive reviews. A business decision would be to **retrain with more positive examples**.

---

## 3D. Analytical Matrix: Method Comparison

| Method | Training Data Required | Handles Context | Handles Sarcasm | Speed | Best Use Case |
|--------|------------------------|-----------------|-----------------|-------|---------------|
| **Lexicon (VADER)** | No | Weak | Very Weak | Very Fast | Quick social media monitoring |
| **Naive Bayes** | Yes | No | No | Fast | Baseline classification |
| **SVM** | Yes | No | No | Fast | High-dimensional text data |
| **LSTM (Deep Learning)** | Yes (large) | Strong | Moderate | Slow | Sequential context understanding |
| **BERT (Transformers)** | Yes (very large) | Very Strong | Strong | Slowest | State-of-the-art accuracy |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4. The Sentiment Analysis Pipeline (End-to-End Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Customer Comments] --> B[Data Collection Module]
    B --> C[Text Pre-Processing Engine]
    C --> C1[Tokenization]
    C1 --> C2[Stop Word Removal]
    C2 --> C3[Stemming and Lemmatization]
    C3 --> D[Feature Extraction Layer]
    D --> D1[Bag of Words]
    D --> D2[TF-IDF Vectorizer]
    D --> D3[Word Embeddings]
    D1 --> E[Sentiment Classification Engine]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> E1[Lexicon Based]
    E --> E2[Machine Learning Models]
    E --> E3[Deep Learning Models]
    E1 --> F[Polarity Output]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    F --> F1[Positive]
    F --> F2[Negative]
    F --> F3[Neutral]
    F1 --> G[Visualization and Reporting Dashboard]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> G1[Word Cloud]
    G --> G2[Sentiment Trend Graph]
    G --> G3[Business KPI Metrics]
    G1 --> H[Business Decision Making]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
```

## 4.1 Granularity Hierarchy of Sentiment Analysis

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Document Level Sentiment] --> S2[Sentence Level Sentiment]
    S2 --> S3[Aspect Based Sentiment Analysis]
    S3 --> S4[Emotion Detection Sub Categories]
    S4 --> S5[Joy, Anger, Sadness, Fear, Surprise, Disgust]
    S1 --> E1[Example Whole Review Polarity]
    S2 --> E2[Example Per Sentence Scoring]
    S3 --> E3[Example Feature Specific Polarity]
```

## 4.2 Confusion Matrix Visualization for KTU

```mermaid
graph TD
    CM[Confusion Matrix Components] --> TP1[True Positive TP Correctly Predicted Positive]
    CM --> TN1[True Negative TN Correctly Predicted Negative]
    CM --> FP1[False Positive FP Type I Error Predicted Positive but Actually Negative]
    CM --> FN1[False Negative FN Type II Error Predicted Negative but Actually Positive]
    TP1 --> M1[Metric Accuracy]
    TN1 --> M1
    FP1 --> M1
    FN1 --> M1
    TP1 --> M2[Metric Precision]
    FP1 --> M2
    TP1 --> M3[Metric Recall]
    FN1 --> M3
    M2 --> M4[Metric F1 Score]
    M3 --> M4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note for Students:** In the KTU exam, drawing the pipeline flow diagram carries **2 to 3 marks**. Use rectangular boxes for processes, diamond shapes for decisions, and clearly label arrows. The above Mermaid block represents the industry-standard architecture taught under NEP 2020 outcome-based modules.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Sentiment Analysis. List its three polarity classes with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**
> Sentiment Analysis, also called **Opinion Mining**, is a Natural Language Processing technique used to identify, extract, and classify the emotional tone (subjective opinion) expressed in textual data such as customer reviews, social media posts, and survey responses.
>
> The three polarity classes are:
> 1. **Positive** — *"I absolutely love this phone, the camera is stunning."*
> 2. **Negative** — *"Terrible product, completely useless, total waste of money."*
> 3. **Neutral** — *"The package arrived on 14th October in a brown box."*
>
> **[3 Marks: 1 for definition + 2 for classes with examples]**

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between stemming and lemmatization in text pre-processing.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Stemming | Lemmatization |
|---------|----------|---------------|
| **Definition** | Crude chopping of word endings to obtain a root form | Sophisticated process of finding the dictionary base form (lemma) |
| **Approach** | Rule-based truncation | Vocabulary + morphological analysis |
| **Example** | "studies", "studying" → "studi" | "studies", "studying" → "study" |
| **Speed** | Faster | Slower |
| **Accuracy** | Lower (may produce non-words) | Higher (always produces real words) |

> **[3 Marks: 2 for tabular differentiation + 1 for examples]**

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the complete pipeline of sentiment analysis with a neat block diagram. Describe each stage in detail. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A corpus of 4 documents contains the word "delivery" in 2 of them. If the word appears 3 times in a specific document of 20 words, calculate the TF-IDF score. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

The sentiment analysis pipeline consists of **5 sequential stages**:

**Stage 1: Data Collection**
Customer comments are aggregated from heterogeneous sources such as e-commerce portals (Amazon, Flipkart), social media platforms (Twitter, Facebook), chatbot transcripts, email feedback, and survey forms. The data collected is **unstructured**, which is the most difficult format to analyze.

**Stage 2: Text Pre-Processing**
Raw text is cleaned using the following sub-steps:
- **Tokenization** splits text into individual words.
- **Lowercasing** ensures uniformity.
- **Stop Word Removal** eliminates common words ("the", "is", "and") that carry no sentiment.
- **Stemming and Lemmatization** reduce words to their root form.
- **Negation Handling** preserves the inverted meaning of "not good".

**Stage 3: Feature Extraction**
Cleaned text is converted into numerical vectors:
- **Bag of Words (BoW)** counts word frequencies.
- **TF-IDF** assigns weights based on importance.
- **Word Embeddings** (Word2Vec, GloVe) capture semantic relationships.

**Stage 4: Sentiment Classification**
Three method families are applied:
- **Lexicon-Based:** Uses predefined sentiment dictionaries (VADER, AFINN).
- **Machine Learning:** Naive Bayes, SVM, Logistic Regression trained on labeled data.
- **Deep Learning:** LSTM, BERT, Transformers for context-aware predictions.

**Stage 5: Visualization and Business Reporting**
Outputs are visualized using **word clouds**, **sentiment trend graphs**, **pie charts**, and **NPS dashboards** to support executive decision-making.

> **[7 Marks: 1 for diagram + 6 for stage-wise explanation — KTU marking pattern]**

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:**
- Total documents in corpus: $N = 4$
- Document Frequency of "delivery": $df_t = 2$
- Frequency of "delivery" in target document: $3$
- Total words in target document: $20$

**Step 1: Calculate Term Frequency (TF)**

$$
TF = \frac{3}{20} = 0.15
$$

**Step 2: Calculate Inverse Document Frequency (IDF)**

$$
IDF = \log\left(\frac{N}{df_t}\right) = \log\left(\frac{4}{2}\right) = \log(2) \approx 0.6931
$$

**Step 3: Calculate TF-IDF Score**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{TF-IDF} &= TF \times IDF \\
&= 0.15 \times 0.6931 \\
&\approx 0.1040
\end{aligned}
$$

> **[7 Marks: 2 for TF + 2 for IDF + 3 for final multiplication]**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Discuss any three lexicon-based approaches for sentiment analysis. Mention their advantages and limitations. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** For a sentiment classifier tested on 250 customer comments, the confusion matrix is: TP = 100, TN = 90, FP = 30, FN = 30. Calculate Accuracy, Precision, Recall, and F1-Score. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Three Lexicon-Based Approaches:**

**1. VADER (Valence Aware Dictionary and sEntiment Reasoner)**
- **Description:** A rule-based model specifically designed for social media text. It uses a curated lexicon of words rated from -4 (most negative) to +4 (most positive) and accounts for intensifiers ("very"), conjunctions ("but"), and punctuation (exclamation marks).
- **Advantage:** Works well on short, informal text like tweets.
- **Limitation:** Poor at handling sarcasm and domain-specific jargon.

**2. AFINN Lexicon**
- **Description:** Developed by Finn Årup Nielsen, this lexicon assigns an integer score between -5 and +5 to each word.
- **Advantage:** Simple to implement and computationally fast.
- **Limitation:** English-centric; limited multilingual support.

**3. TextBlob Lexicon**
- **Description:** Built upon the Pattern library, TextBlob provides both polarity and subjectivity scores.
- **Advantage:** Easy API and produces dual metrics (polarity + subjectivity).
- **Limitation:** Less accurate on noisy or highly contextual text.

> **[7 Marks: 2 marks per approach (1 description + 0.5 advantage + 0.5 limitation)]**

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:** $TP = 100$, $TN = 90$, $FP = 30$, $FN = 30$

**Step 1: Accuracy**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Accuracy} &= \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} \\
&= \frac{100 + 90}{100 + 90 + 30 + 30} \\
&= \frac{190}{250} \\
&= 0.76 \text{ or } 76\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Precision**

$$
\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{100}{130} \approx 0.7692 \text{ or } 76.92\%
$$

**Step 3: Recall**

$$
\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{100}{130} \approx 0.7692 \text{ or } 76.92\%
$$

**Step 4: F1-Score**

$$
F1 = 2 \times \frac{0.7692 \times 0.7692}{0.7692 + 0.7692} = 0.7692 \text{ or } 76.92\%
$$

> **Note:** Since Precision equals Recall in this case, F1 is identical to both values.
> **[7 Marks: 1.5 marks each for the four metrics + 1 for final interpretation]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Logarithm Base Error:** Students often use $\log_{10}$ instead of natural log or vice versa without stating the base. Always mention the base explicitly.
> 2. **Confusion Matrix Mix-up:** Students frequently swap FP and FN in formulas. Recall: **FP = False Alarm (Type I)**, **FN = Missed Detection (Type II)**.
> 3. **Stemming vs. Lemmatization Confusion:** Writing "studying → studi" for *both* methods loses marks. Lemmatization always gives a real word.
> 4. **Forgetting the Pipeline Diagram:** In 7-mark theory questions, the **diagram is mandatory** and carries 1–2 marks even if not explicitly asked.
> 5. **Not Converting Decimals to Percentage:** Final metrics should be expressed in % for business interpretation marks.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Sentiment Analysis = Opinion Mining = Polarity Detection** — all three terms are interchangeable in KTU exams.
- The **three polarity classes** are Positive, Negative, and Neutral.
- **Three granularity levels**: Document → Sentence → Aspect-Based.
- **Pipeline**: Data Collection → Pre-Processing → Feature Extraction → Classification → Visualization.
- **Pre-processing steps**: Tokenization, Stop Word Removal, Stemming, Lemmatization, Negation Handling.
- **TF** measures word frequency in a document; **IDF** penalizes common words across the corpus.
- **TF-IDF** = TF × IDF, the cornerstone feature extraction method.
- **Lexicon methods** (VADER, AFINN, TextBlob) need no training data; **ML/DL methods** require labeled data.
- **Accuracy alone is misleading** in imbalanced datasets — always report **Precision, Recall, and F1-Score**.
- **F1-Score** is the harmonic mean of Precision and Recall — the most balanced single metric.
- **NPS** is a key business KPI: NPS = %Promoters − %Detractors (range: −100 to +100).
- **ABSA (Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis)** is preferred over document-level for actionable business insights.
- **Challenges**: Sarcasm detection, multilingual data, domain-specific vocabulary, contextual ambiguity.
- **Top tools**: VADER, TextBlob, NLTK, spaCy, BERT, Hugging Face Transformers (for conceptual knowledge only — **no code required in KTU BA paper**).
- Always **draw the pipeline diagram** in 14-mark questions — it is a high-scoring component.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Text Mining from Opinion Platforms. Data Science Toolkits for Business Analytics. Clustering - K-Means
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT522 - BUSINESS ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Text Mining from Opinion Platforms. Data Science Toolkits for Business Analytics. Clustering - K-Means

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Text Mining from Opinion Platforms

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
> *Text Mining* (also called *Text Analytics*) is the process of transforming unstructured text data into structured, machine-readable information to discover meaningful patterns, trends, and insights. When applied to *opinion platforms* (e.g., Amazon reviews, Twitter, Glassdoor, TripAdvisor), it is specifically called *Opinion Mining* or *Sentiment Analysis* — the computational study of opinions, sentiments, evaluations, attitudes, and emotions expressed by users.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of text mining like a *digital librarian with a magnifying glass*. Imagine a coffee shop owner who wants to know what 10,000 customers think about his new latte. He cannot read every review manually. So he hires an "intelligent reader" (the text mining system) that:
- Reads every review (text collection),
- Tags each review as **positive / negative / neutral** (sentiment classification),
- Picks out the keywords customers mention most often ("frothy", "expensive", "bitter"),
- Groups similar complaints or praises together (clustering).

That is exactly what text mining does — it converts the "noise" of free-flowing human language into structured, business-actionable intelligence.

**Core Sub-tasks in Opinion Mining:**

| Sub-task | Formal Name | Business Value |
|---|---|---|
| Sentiment Classification | Polarity detection (Positive / Negative / Neutral) | Brand health monitoring |
| Aspect Extraction | Identifying *what* is being talked about (price, service, taste) | Product improvement |
| Opinion Summarization | Aggregating opinions into a brief report | Executive dashboards |
| Spam/Fake Review Detection | Outlier detection in text | Trust & authenticity |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metrics Used in Text Mining:**
> - **Precision** = $\frac{TP}{TP+FP}$
> - **Recall** = $\frac{TP}{TP+FN}$
> - **F1-Score** = $2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P+R}$
> - **Accuracy** = $\frac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN}$

### 1.2 Data Science Toolkits for Business Analytics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A *Data Science Toolkit* is a curated, integrated set of software libraries, frameworks, visualization engines, and statistical packages that allow analysts to ingest, clean, model, and visualize business data without building algorithms from scratch.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
A toolkit is like a *chef's kitchen*. A chef doesn't forge a knife or build an oven from iron ore — they pick the right pre-built tool for each task. Similarly, a business analyst picks:
- **Pandas** for slicing customer tables (like a data knife),
- **Matplotlib / Seaborn** for plotting sales charts (like plating the dish),
- **Scikit-learn** for predictive modeling (like the recipe book),
- **NLTK / spaCy** for processing customer reviews (like a spice grinder for text).

**Standard Toolkit Stack (KTU 2024 Reference):**

| Layer | Tool / Library | Primary Business Use |
|---|---|---|
| Data Ingestion | Pandas, NumPy | Reading Excel, CSV, SQL data |
| Statistical Modeling | SciPy, StatsModels | Hypothesis testing, regression |
| Machine Learning | Scikit-learn, XGBoost | Forecasting, classification |
| Text Mining | NLTK, spaCy, TextBlob | Sentiment, NER, tokenization |
| Visualization | Matplotlib, Seaborn, Power BI, Tableau | Executive dashboards |
| Big Data (advanced) | PySpark, Hadoop | Massive transaction logs |

### 1.3 Clustering — K-Means

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (Rigorous):**
> *K-Means* is a **centroid-based, unsupervised, partitional clustering algorithm** that partitions a dataset of $n$ observations into $K$ pre-specified, non-overlapping clusters, where each observation belongs to the cluster with the **nearest centroid (mean)**. The algorithm iteratively minimizes the **Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS)**, also called *inertia*.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Imagine a *mall with no signboards and you need to place $K$ information kiosks* so that every shopper walks the shortest distance to reach a kiosk. You:
1. Initially drop $K$ kiosks at random spots,
2. Each shopper walks to the nearest kiosk (cluster assignment),
3. You then move each kiosk to the *average position* of its shoppers (centroid update),
4. Repeat until kiosks stop moving (convergence).

The result: shoppers are naturally grouped around kiosks — these are the *K clusters*. K-Means works exactly like this with data points instead of shoppers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Characteristics of K-Means:**
> - **Unsupervised** — no labeled output required.
> - **Iterative** — alternates between assignment and update steps.
> - **Distance-sensitive** — usually uses **Euclidean distance** of **$\sqrt{2}$** as the standard $L_2$ norm metric.
> - **Sensitive to initialization** — solved by the **K-Means++** heuristic.
> - **Assumes spherical, equally-sized clusters** — a key limitation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Text Mining Pipeline (Step-by-Step)

The complete text mining workflow on opinion platforms is a six-stage funnel:

1. **Data Collection (Web Scraping / API):** Pull reviews using platform APIs (Twitter API, Amazon Product Advertising API) or scraping tools.
2. **Text Pre-processing:** Cleaning raw text — tokenization, stop-word removal, stemming/lemmatization.
3. **Feature Engineering:** Converting text to numerical vectors using **Bag-of-Words (BoW)**, **TF-IDF**, or **Word Embeddings (Word2Vec)**.
4. **Modeling:** Apply sentiment classifiers (Lexicon-based: VADER, TextBlob; or ML-based: Naive Bayes, SVM, LSTM).
5. **Evaluation:** Validate using **Precision, Recall, F1-Score, Confusion Matrix**.
6. **Deployment & Visualization:** Dashboards for managers (e.g., sentiment over time, word clouds).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Order Matters in Business Context:**
> Managers don't want raw text — they want a *sentiment score* per product per day. The pipeline converts "I hate the new packaging, it's cheap" into structured metadata: `{product: "Soap", sentiment: -0.7, aspect: "packaging", date: "2024-09-12"}`.

### 2.2 K-Means Algorithm — Theoretical Foundation

**Goal of K-Means (Mathematical Intuition):**
K-Means solves the following **optimization problem**:

$$\min_{C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_K} \; J \;=\; \sum_{j=1}^{K} \sum_{x_i \in C_j} \| x_i - \mu_j \|^2$$

Where:
- $C_j$ is the $j^{th}$ cluster,
- $x_i$ is the $i^{th}$ data point,
- $\mu_j$ is the centroid of cluster $C_j$,
- $\| \cdot \|$ is the **$L_2$ norm (Euclidean distance)**,
- $J$ is the **objective function** (also called *inertia* or *WCSS*).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind the Two-Step Iteration:**
> The objective $J$ is **not convex**, so K-Means uses an **Expectation-Maximization (EM)-style heuristic**:
> - **Step A (Assignment):** Holding centroids fixed, assign each point to the nearest centroid. This is the *optimal* assignment for the current centroids.
> - **Step B (Update):** Holding assignments fixed, recompute each centroid as the mean of its assigned points. This is the *optimal* centroid for the current assignments.
> Each step strictly *reduces* $J$, guaranteeing **monotonic convergence** to a (local) minimum.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Euclidean Distance | $d(x_i, c_j) = \sqrt{\sum_{k=1}^{n}(x_{ik} - c_{jk})^2}$ | Distance from point to centroid |
| 2 | K-Means Objective (WCSS) | $J = \sum_{j=1}^{K} \sum_{x_i \in C_j} \Vert x_i - \mu_j \Vert^{2}$ | Minimization target |
| 3 | Centroid Update Rule | $\mu_j = \frac{1}{\vert C_j \vert} \sum_{x_i \in C_j} x_i$ | Mean of cluster members |
| 4 | Manhattan Distance (alt) | $d(x_i, c_j) = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \vert x_{ik} - c_{jk} \vert$ | Used when features are categorical |
| 5 | TF (Term Frequency) | $tf(t,d) = \frac{f_{t,d}}{\sum_{t' \in d} f_{t',d}}$ | Word importance in a document |
| 6 | IDF (Inverse Doc Freq) | $idf(t) = \log \left(\frac{N}{df(t)}\right)$ | Word rarity across corpus |
| 7 | TF-IDF Combined | $tfidf(t,d) = tf(t,d) \times idf(t)$ | Standard text vectorization |
| 8 | Silhouette Score | $s(i) = \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i),\, b(i)\}}$ | Cluster quality, $s \in [-1, 1]$ |
| 9 | Elbow Method (graphical) | Plot $J$ vs $K$; pick the "elbow" | Choosing optimal $K$ |
| 10 | Cosine Similarity (text) | $\cos(\theta) = \frac{A \cdot B}{\Vert A \Vert \cdot \Vert B \Vert}$ | Document similarity |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering/Business Utility:**
> - **Retail:** Customer segmentation for targeted marketing (K-Means on RFM data).
> - **HR:** Grouping employees by performance metrics.
> - **Banking:** Fraud detection (outlier clusters).
> - **E-Commerce:** Document clustering of reviews for aspect-based summaries.
> - **Healthcare:** Patient stratification by vitals (with ethical oversight).

### 2.4 Choosing K — Two Key Methods

**A) Elbow Method:**
Plot $J$ (WCSS) on the y-axis and $K$ on the x-axis. The optimal $K$ is at the *elbow* — the point where adding more clusters yields only a marginal drop in WCSS.

**B) Silhouette Score:**
For each point $i$:
- $a(i)$ = average distance to all other points in the same cluster (cohesion).
- $b(i)$ = minimum average distance to points in the *next nearest* cluster (separation).

$$s(i) = \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i),\, b(i)\}}$$

- $s \approx 1$: well-clustered.
- $s \approx 0$: on the boundary.
- $s < 0$: probably in the wrong cluster.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Always state the **range of $s$** as **$[-1, +1]$** and explain what each extreme means. Many students lose marks for writing only "between 0 and 1" — that is **incorrect** for the *signed* silhouette formula.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the K-Means Centroid Update Rule

**Problem Statement:**
Given a cluster $C_j$ containing $m$ data points $\{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_m\}$, find the *optimal* centroid $\mu_j$ that minimizes the within-cluster sum of squared distances.

**Step 1 — Define the local objective:**
For cluster $C_j$, the local objective is:

$$J_j = \sum_{x_i \in C_j} \Vert x_i - \mu_j \Vert^2$$

**Step 2 — Expand the squared norm:**
Using the identity $\Vert a - b \Vert^2 = (a-b)^T(a-b)$:

$$J_j = \sum_{x_i \in C_j} (x_i - \mu_j)^T (x_i - \mu_j)$$

**Step 3 — Expand the quadratic term:**

$$J_j = \sum_{x_i \in C_j} \left( x_i^T x_i \;-\; 2 \mu_j^T x_i \;+\; \mu_j^T \mu_j \right)$$

*Explanation:* This is a standard vector identity: $(x - \mu)^T (x - \mu) = x^T x - 2\mu^T x + \mu^T \mu$. The cross term $\mu_j^T x_i$ appears $m$ times.

**Step 4 — Take the gradient with respect to $\mu_j$:**
Since $x_i^T x_i$ does not depend on $\mu_j$, its derivative is **0**.
The term $\mu_j^T \mu_j$ is a scalar equal to $\Vert \mu_j \Vert^2$; its gradient is $2\mu_j$.
The middle term $-2\mu_j^T x_i$ has gradient $-2 x_i$.

$$\frac{\partial J_j}{\partial \mu_j} = \sum_{x_i \in C_j} \left( 0 - 2 x_i + 2 \mu_j \right) = 2 m \mu_j - 2 \sum_{x_i \in C_j} x_i$$

**Step 5 — Set the gradient to zero (first-order optimality condition):**

$$2 m \mu_j - 2 \sum_{x_i \in C_j} x_i = 0$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $\mu_j$:**

$$\mu_j = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{x_i \in C_j} x_i$$

This is the **centroid update rule** — the new centroid is the *arithmetic mean* of all points in the cluster. $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Intuition:**
> This derivation proves *why* the centroid is the mean — it is the unique point that minimizes the sum of squared distances to all members. This is a least-squares problem disguised inside a clustering algorithm.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — K-Means on 2D Data

**Dataset (Revenue vs. Ad-Spend, normalized, 6 customers):**

| Customer | $x_1$ (Ad-Spend) | $x_2$ (Revenue) |
|---|---|---|
| A | 1.0 | 1.0 |
| B | 1.5 | 2.0 |
| C | 2.0 | 1.5 |
| D | 8.0 | 8.0 |
| E | 8.5 | 9.0 |
| F | 9.0 | 8.5 |

**Goal:** Apply K-Means with $K = 2$.

**Step 1 — Initialize centroids randomly:**
Let $\mu_1 = (1.0, 1.0) = A$ and $\mu_2 = (8.0, 8.0) = D$.

**Step 2 — Iteration 1, Assignment Step (Euclidean distance):**

For each point, compute distance to both centroids and assign to the nearest.

| Point | $d(\cdot, \mu_1)$ | $d(\cdot, \mu_2)$ | Assignment |
|---|---|---|---|
| A (1.0, 1.0) | 0.00 | 9.90 | Cluster 1 |
| B (1.5, 2.0) | 1.12 | 9.01 | Cluster 1 |
| C (2.0, 1.5) | 1.12 | 8.66 | Cluster 1 |
| D (8.0, 8.0) | 9.90 | 0.00 | Cluster 2 |
| E (8.5, 9.0) | 10.67 | 1.12 | Cluster 2 |
| F (9.0, 8.5) | 10.73 | 1.12 | Cluster 2 |

*Example calculation for B:*
$$d(B, \mu_1) = \sqrt{(1.5-1.0)^2 + (2.0-1.0)^2} = \sqrt{0.25 + 1.00} = \sqrt{1.25} \approx 1.12$$

**Step 3 — Iteration 1, Update Step (compute new centroids as means):**

$$\mu_1^{\text{new}} = \frac{1}{3}\left[(1,1) + (1.5,2) + (2,1.5)\right] = (1.50, 1.50)$$

$$\mu_2^{\text{new}} = \frac{1}{3}\left[(8,8) + (8.5,9) + (9,8.5)\right] = (8.50, 8.50)$$

**Step 4 — Compute WCSS $J$ for Iteration 1:**

$$J = \sum_{x \in C_1} \Vert x - \mu_1 \Vert^2 + \sum_{x \in C_2} \Vert x - \mu_2 \Vert^2$$

For Cluster 1:
- $\|A - \mu_1\|^2 = (1-1.5)^2 + (1-1.5)^2 = 0.50$
- $\|B - \mu_1\|^2 = (1.5-1.5)^2 + (2-1.5)^2 = 0.25$
- $\|C - \mu_1\|^2 = (2-1.5)^2 + (1.5-1.5)^2 = 0.25$
- Subtotal $C_1 = 1.00$

For Cluster 2:
- $\|D - \mu_2\|^2 = (8-8.5)^2 + (8-8.5)^2 = 0.50$
- $\|E - \mu_2\|^2 = (8.5-8.5)^2 + (9-8.5)^2 = 0.25$
- $\|F - \mu_2\|^2 = (9-8.5)^2 + (8.5-8.5)^2 = 0.25$
- Subtotal $C_2 = 1.00$

$$J_{\text{iter 1}} = 1.00 + 1.00 = 2.00$$

**Step 5 — Iteration 2, Re-assign:**
Distances are unchanged in relative ordering (centroids shifted symmetrically). Assignments remain:
- Cluster 1: A, B, C
- Cluster 2: D, E, F

Centroids do not move → **Convergence reached** in 1 iteration for this clean dataset.

**Final Result:**
- **Cluster 1 (Low-Value):** $\mu_1 = (1.5, 1.5)$ — customers with low ad-spend and low revenue.
- **Cluster 2 (High-Value):** $\mu_2 = (8.5, 8.5)$ — VIP customers.
- **Total Inertia** $J = 2.00$.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When to Use K-Means vs. Alternatives

| Criterion | K-Means | Hierarchical | DBSCAN |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pre-specify $K$? | **Yes** | No (dendrogram) | No (uses $\epsilon$, MinPts) |
| Cluster Shape | Spherical | Any | Arbitrary (density-based) |
| Handles Outliers? | Poor (forces them in) | Poor | **Excellent** (marks as noise) |
| Scalability | **High** $O(n)$ | Poor $O(n^2)$ | Medium |
| Best For | Large, clean data | Small, exploratory | Irregular, noisy data |
| Initialization Sensitive? | **Yes** | No | Slightly |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Text Mining Pipeline (Block Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Opinion Data: Reviews, Tweets, Comments] --> B[Data Ingestion Layer: APIs, Web Scrapers, SQL]
    B --> C[Text Pre-processing: Tokenization, Stop-word Removal, Stemming]
    C --> D[Feature Engineering: TF-IDF, Bag-of-Words, Word2Vec]
    D --> E[Modeling Engine: Lexicon VADER, ML Classifier, Deep Learning LSTM]
    E --> F[Sentiment Output: Polarity Score, Aspect Tags, Confidence]
    F --> G[Business Dashboard: Power BI, Tableau, Excel]
    G --> H[Decision Maker: Marketing Manager, Product Team]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style H fill:#ffe1e1
    style E fill:#fff4e1
```

### 4.2 K-Means Iterative Algorithm Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Input Dataset X of n points] --> B[Choose K, the number of clusters]
    B --> C[Initialize K centroids: Random OR K-Means Plus Plus]
    C --> D[Assignment Step: Assign each point to nearest centroid]
    D --> E[Update Step: Recompute each centroid as the mean of its cluster]
    E --> F{Centroids changed?}
    F -- Yes --> D
    F -- No --> G[Convergence Reached]
    G --> H[Output: K clusters with final centroids and WCSS]
    H --> I[Evaluate using Elbow Method or Silhouette Score]
    I --> J[Business Interpretation and Reporting]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style C fill:#fff4e1
    style G fill:#d4f4dd
    style J fill:#ffe1e1
```

### 4.3 K-Means Convergence Behavior (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Iteration 0: Random Centroids, High WCSS] --> B[Iteration 1: Re-assign Points, Lower WCSS]
    B --> C[Iteration 2: Update Centroids, Even Lower WCSS]
    C --> D[Iteration T: Converged, Minimum Local WCSS]
    D --> E[Final Cluster Map: Each point labelled C1 to CK]

    style A fill:#ffe1e1
    style D fill:#d4f4dd
    style E fill:#e1f5ff
```

### 4.4 Toolkit Layered Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Business Problem Statement] --> B[Data Layer: Pandas, NumPy, SQL Connectors]
    B --> C[Analytics Layer: SciPy, StatsModels, Scikit-learn]
    C --> D[Text Mining Layer: NLTK, spaCy, TextBlob]
    D --> E[Visualization Layer: Matplotlib, Seaborn, Power BI]
    E --> F[Insight and Decision Output]

    style A fill:#ffe1e1
    style F fill:#d4f4dd
    style D fill:#fff4e1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Tip for KTU Board Exams:**
> When drawing the K-Means flowchart in an exam, always include the **decision diamond** ("Have centroids changed?"). Examiners specifically look for evidence of the *iterative loop*. A flowchart that goes straight from "Assign" to "Output" loses 2–3 marks immediately.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Define text mining. Differentiate between text mining and opinion mining.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Text Mining (1.5 marks):** Text mining is the process of extracting meaningful patterns, insights, and structured information from unstructured textual data using NLP, statistical, and machine learning techniques.
- **Difference (1.5 marks):** *Text mining* is a broad discipline covering classification, clustering, summarization, and information retrieval on any text. *Opinion mining* is a specific sub-task of text mining that focuses on identifying **sentiments, attitudes, and evaluations** (e.g., positive/negative) expressed in text — typically from review platforms or social media.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*List any three data science toolkits used in business analytics. Mention one business use case for each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **Pandas** — Used for cleaning and merging customer transaction data from CSVs/SQL. (1 mark)
2. **Scikit-learn** — Used to build a churn prediction classifier using customer demographic data. (1 mark)
3. **NLTK / TextBlob** — Used to perform sentiment analysis on Amazon product reviews to monitor brand health. (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — K-Means & Text Mining Focus**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the K-Means clustering algorithm in detail. State the objective function and describe each step. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** For the following 2D data points, perform **one complete iteration** of K-Means with $K=2$. Initial centroids: $\mu_1 = (1, 1)$ and $\mu_2 = (5, 5)$.

| Point | $x$ | $y$ |
|---|---|---|
| P1 | 2 | 2 |
| P2 | 3 | 1 |
| P3 | 6 | 5 |
| P4 | 7 | 7 |
| P5 | 1 | 3 |

Compute cluster assignments, new centroids, and WCSS. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) K-Means Algorithm Explanation (7 marks):**

K-Means is an **unsupervised, iterative, partitional clustering algorithm** that groups $n$ data points into $K$ clusters.

**Objective Function (1.5 marks):**

$$J = \sum_{j=1}^{K} \sum_{x_i \in C_j} \Vert x_i - \mu_j \Vert^{2}$$

This is the **Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS / Inertia)** — the sum of squared Euclidean distances from each point to its assigned centroid.

**Algorithm Steps (5.5 marks):**

1. **Choose $K$** — Decide the number of clusters based on domain knowledge or methods like the elbow method.
2. **Initialize centroids** — Pick $K$ initial centroids randomly or using **K-Means++** (smart seeding that spaces centroids apart).
3. **Assignment Step** — For each data point $x_i$, compute its Euclidean distance to every centroid and assign it to the cluster of the nearest centroid:

$$C(x_i) = \arg\min_{j} \, \Vert x_i - \mu_j \Vert$$

4. **Update Step** — Recompute each centroid as the *mean* of all points currently in its cluster:

$$\mu_j = \frac{1}{\vert C_j \vert} \sum_{x_i \in C_j} x_i$$

5. **Convergence Check** — If centroids did not change (or change below a threshold $\epsilon$), stop. Otherwise, go back to step 3.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The algorithm **converges monotonically**: $J$ never increases between iterations because each step is optimal for the fixed values of the other. However, K-Means converges to a **local minimum**, not the global minimum, which is why multiple restarts are recommended.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute Euclidean distances to both centroids:**

| Point | $d(\cdot, \mu_1=(1,1))$ | $d(\cdot, \mu_2=(5,5))$ | Assignment |
|---|---|---|---|
| P1 (2,2) | $\sqrt{1+1}=\sqrt{2}\approx 1.41$ | $\sqrt{9+9}\approx 4.24$ | **C1** |
| P2 (3,1) | $\sqrt{4+0}=2.00$ | $\sqrt{4+16}\approx 4.47$ | **C1** |
| P3 (6,5) | $\sqrt{25+16}\approx 6.40$ | $\sqrt{1+0}=1.00$ | **C2** |
| P4 (7,7) | $\sqrt{36+36}\approx 8.49$ | $\sqrt{4+4}\approx 2.83$ | **C2** |
| P5 (1,3) | $\sqrt{0+4}=2.00$ | $\sqrt{16+4}\approx 4.47$ | **C1** |

**Cluster 1 (C1):** P1, P2, P5
**Cluster 2 (C2):** P3, P4

**Step 2 — Compute new centroids (mean of cluster members):**

$$\mu_1^{\text{new}} = \left(\frac{2+3+1}{3},\, \frac{2+1+3}{3}\right) = (2.00, 2.00)$$

$$\mu_2^{\text{new}} = \left(\frac{6+7}{2},\, \frac{5+7}{2}\right) = (6.50, 6.00)$$

**Step 3 — Compute WCSS $J$:**

For C1 around $\mu_1^{\text{new}} = (2, 2)$:
- $\Vert P1 - \mu_1 \Vert^2 = (2-2)^2 + (2-2)^2 = 0$
- $\Vert P2 - \mu_1 \Vert^2 = (3-2)^2 + (1-2)^2 = 1 + 1 = 2$
- $\Vert P5 - \mu_1 \Vert^2 = (1-2)^2 + (3-2)^2 = 1 + 1 = 2$
- Subtotal C1 = **4**

For C2 around $\mu_2^{\text{new}} = (6.5, 6)$:
- $\Vert P3 - \mu_2 \Vert^2 = (6-6.5)^2 + (5-6)^2 = 0.25 + 1 = 1.25$
- $\Vert P4 - \mu_2 \Vert^2 = (7-6.5)^2 + (7-6)^2 = 0.25 + 1 = 1.25$
- Subtotal C2 = **2.50**

$$\boxed{J_{\text{iter 1}} = 4.00 + 2.50 = 6.50}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - Many students compute distances to **only one** centroid. **Always show both distances** to justify cluster assignment — this is the primary marks allocation step.
> - Do **not** round off intermediate values like $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.41$ too early. Keep fractions and square roots symbolic until the final step.
