- Youth + Student = No → **Leaf (No)**

**Step 3 — Recurse on Age = Senior** (5 records, 3 Yes, 2 No, $H = 0.9710$).

Re-compute IG on remaining attributes.

| Subset | Records | Yes | No | Entropy |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Credit = Fair | C4, C5, C10 | 3 | 0 | 0 |
| Credit = Excellent | C6, C14 | 0 | 2 | 0 |

$IG_{Senior}(Credit) = 0.9710 - 0 = 0.9710$ — **Highest.** Split on **Credit Rating**.

- Senior + Credit = Fair → **Leaf (Yes)**
- Senior + Credit = Excellent → **Leaf (No)**

**Step 4 — Final ID3 Tree:**

```
                [Age]
              /   |    \
        Youth  Middle  Senior
          |      |       |
      [Student] Yes   [Credit]
        /    \         /    \
      Yes     No    Fair   Excellent
       |       |     |       |
      Yes      No    Yes     No
```

> **Conclusion (1 mark):** The ID3 algorithm terminates when all branches reach pure leaves. The tree has depth 2 and all 14 training records are correctly classified.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [Internal Choice Alternative]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **ID3 algorithm** in detail. Describe the role of **entropy and information gain** in attribute selection. State the stopping criteria used by ID3.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast the **ID3, C4.5, and CART** decision-tree algorithms along the following dimensions: splitting criterion, handling of continuous attributes, pruning strategy, and handling of missing values.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B (a) — 7 Marks**

**Definition (1 mark):** ID3 (Iterative Dichotomiser 3), proposed by J. Ross Quinlan in 1986, is a greedy, top-down, recursive-partitioning algorithm for inducing a classification decision tree from a categorical dataset.

**Role of Entropy and Information Gain (3 marks):**

- **Entropy H(S)** quantifies the impurity of a node. For c classes,

$$
H(S) = -\sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i \log_2 p_i
$$

A pure node has H = 0; a uniform distribution gives maximum entropy.

- **Information Gain IG(S, A)** measures the reduction in entropy achieved by splitting S on attribute A:

$$
IG(S, A) = H(S) - H(S \vert A)
$$

- ID3 picks the attribute that **maximises IG** at every node. This is equivalent to choosing the attribute that yields the most informative split — the one that produces the most homogeneous sub-populations.

**Stopping Criteria of ID3 (2 marks):**

1. All instances in the current subset belong to the same class (pure node).
2. No remaining attributes can be used for splitting (attribute list exhausted).
3. The information gain for every remaining attribute is **zero** (no useful split).
4. The subset S is empty (use the majority class of the parent).
5. (Optional) A pre-defined tree depth or minimum-leaf-size threshold is reached.

**Algorithm Summary (1 mark):**

- Begin with the full dataset at the root.
- At each step, compute IG for every remaining attribute.
- Split on the best attribute; partition the data accordingly.
- Recurse on each partition; stop when stopping criteria are satisfied.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B (b) — 7 Marks**

**Comparative Table (7 marks):**

| **Dimension** | **ID3 (1986)** | **C4.5 (1993)** | **CART (1984)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Splitting Criterion** | Information Gain | Gain Ratio (normalised IG) | Gini Index (or variance for regression) |
| **Continuous Attributes** | Not directly supported (must discretise) | Supported (dynamic binary thresholding) | Supported (binary splits on sorted values) |
| **Pruning** | No built-in pruning (overfits easily) | Post-pruning using **error-based pruning** | Cost-complexity pruning (CCP) with $\alpha$ |
| **Tree Structure** | Multi-way splits | Multi-way splits | **Binary splits only** |
| **Missing Values** | Not handled natively | Handled via probability-weighting | Handled via surrogate splits |
| **Output Type** | Classification only | Classification | Classification & regression |
| **Bias** | Biased towards high-cardinality attributes | Reduced bias (gain ratio normalises) | Lower bias; uses Gini which is symmetric |
| **Computational Cost** | Low | Moderate | Moderate |
| **Origin / Author** | Quinlan | Quinlan (successor to ID3) | Breiman, Friedman, Olshen, Stone |

> **Key Insight (1 mark):** C4.5 is essentially a refined, robust extension of ID3 — it addresses ID3's overfitting, multi-valued-attribute bias, and inability to handle continuous data. CART, on the other hand, is structurally different because it always produces **binary trees** and can handle **regression** via the same framework.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes in ID3 Numerical Problems**
> 1. **Forgetting the weighted average in conditional entropy** — Students often compute the *arithmetic mean* of subset entropies instead of the *cardinality-weighted* mean. Always multiply each subset's entropy by $\vert S_j \vert / \vert S \vert$.
> 2. **Computing $\log_2$ on natural log or base-10 log** — The information-theoretic definition strictly uses **base-2 logarithm** (bits). Using ln or log₁₀ will produce wrong IG values and lose 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Rounding too early** — Carry at least 4 decimal places during intermediate steps; round only the final answer. Examiner's key normally allows ±0.01 tolerance.
> 4. **Forgetting the $0 \cdot \log_2 0 = 0$ convention** — When a subset is perfectly pure, its entropy is 0; the conditional entropy term becomes 0. Don't write "undefined".
> 5. **Not stating the stopping condition explicitly** — When the recursion terminates (pure leaf or empty attribute list), explicitly state *why* it terminates. Examiners award 1 mark for the termination statement.
> 6. **Choosing the attribute with the *lowest* entropy (or lowest conditional entropy) instead of the *highest* IG** — A frequent conceptual error.
> 7. **Not labelling the tree's leaves with the class label** — Always annotate leaves with the final Yes/No label.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Exam-Ready)**
>
> - **ID3** = Quinlan's 1986 algorithm; uses **Information Gain** as the splitting criterion.
> - **Entropy** $H(S) = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$; range $[0, \log_2 c]$ bits.
> - **Information Gain** $IG(S, A) = H(S) - H(S \vert A)$.
> - **Root attribute** = the one with the **maximum** IG.
> - **Stopping conditions**: pure node, exhausted attribute list, IG = 0, or empty subset.
> - **Conditional entropy** must be a **cardinality-weighted average** of subset entropies.
> - **$0 \cdot \log_2 0 = 0$** — convention to handle pure subsets.
> - **Multi-way splits** are the ID3 norm (in contrast to CART's binary splits).
> - **No pruning** = high overfitting risk on noisy data.
> - **Only categorical features** are natively supported.
> - **C4.5** = the direct upgrade: handles continuous data, gain ratio, post-pruning, missing values.
> - **CART** = binary trees, Gini index, supports regression.
> - **Real-world uses**: credit scoring, medical diagnosis, churn prediction, fraud detection, customer segmentation.
> - **Bias**: ID3 favours attributes with **many distinct values** (e.g., an ID column would have IG = H(S), wrongly appearing "informative").
> - **Practical tip**: Always encode target as the **last column** when presenting a solution.
> - **Examiner's favourite numericals** involve 8–14 records with 3–4 attributes and a binary class.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Factor Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT522 - BUSINESS ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Factor Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Factor Analysis

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Factor Analysis (FA)** is a multivariate statistical technique whose primary objective is to represent a set of $p$ observed correlated variables $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_p$ in terms of a smaller number of unobserved, latent variables called **common factors** $F_1, F_2, \ldots, F_k$ (where $k < p$) plus unique residual terms.

Mathematically, the standard orthogonal factor model is expressed as:

$$
X_i = \mu_i + \sum_{j=1}^{k} \ell_{ij} F_j + \varepsilon_i
$$

where:
- $X_i$ is the $i$-th **standardized observed variable** (mean = 0, variance = 1).
- $\mu_i$ is the mean of $X_i$ (becomes 0 after standardization).
- $\ell_{ij}$ is the **factor loading** of the $i$-th variable on the $j$-th factor.
- $F_j$ is the $j$-th **common factor**, with $E(F_j) = 0$ and $Var(F_j) = 1$.
- $\varepsilon_i$ is the **unique factor** (or residual) specific to variable $i$, assumed independent of the common factors.

The **correlation structure** of the variables is captured by the fundamental factor-analytic decomposition:

$$
R = L L^{\top} + \Psi
$$

where:
- $R$ is the $p \times p$ **correlation matrix** of observed variables.
- $L$ is the $p \times k$ matrix of **factor loadings**.
- $\Psi$ is the $p \times p$ diagonal matrix of **unique variances (uniquenesses)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Factor Analysis in Business Analytics is heavily used in marketing research, customer segmentation, psychometric testing, and survey instrument design. The 2024 scheme expects students to understand *why* FA is used (data reduction + latent construct discovery) and *how* to interpret rotated factor loadings.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a **school report card** with 12 subjects (Mathematics, Physics, Chemistry, English, History, Geography, Economics, Civics, Biology, Computer Science, Physical Education, Art). The marks in these 12 subjects are correlated — a student who scores high in Math also tends to score high in Physics and Chemistry.

A teacher applying **Factor Analysis** would ask: *"Can we explain these 12 subjects using just 2–3 hidden 'abilities'?"* The answer is usually:

1. **STEM Ability Factor** (driving Math, Physics, Chemistry, Biology, Computer Science).
2. **Humanities & Communication Factor** (driving English, History, Geography, Economics, Civics).
3. **Creative & Kinesthetic Factor** (driving Art and Physical Education).

Each subject is a *mixture* of these hidden factors plus a tiny bit of noise (the student's personal quirks in that subject). The teacher has just performed **Factor Analysis** — reducing 12 noisy variables into 3 meaningful latent constructs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Interpretation:** Factor Analysis answers two business questions simultaneously:
> 1. *Data Reduction* — Can 50 customer survey questions be summarized using 5–6 underlying customer-perception dimensions (e.g., "Service Quality", "Price Sensitivity", "Brand Trust")?
> 2. *Latent Construct Identification* — What invisible drivers explain why survey responses cluster together?

## 1.3 Key Terminology Introduced

| Term | Notation | Intuitive Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Factor Loading | $\ell_{ij}$ | How strongly variable $i$ depends on factor $j$ |
| Communality | $h_i^2$ | Proportion of variable $i$'s variance explained by *all* common factors |
| Uniqueness | $\psi_i$ | Variance of variable $i$ NOT explained by common factors |
| Eigenvalue | $\lambda_j$ | Amount of total variance explained by factor $j$ |
| Factor Score | $\hat{F}_j$ | Estimated value of factor $j$ for a specific observation |
| Rotation | — | A mathematical reorientation of factor axes to aid interpretation |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook:** *Loading* = regression coefficient of $X_i$ on $F_j$. *Communality* = how *common* the variance is with other variables. *Uniqueness* = what's *uniquely* variable-specific noise.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Factor Model — Decomposition Logic

The **observed variable** $X_i$ is decomposed into **two orthogonal sources of variance**:

$$
\underbrace{X_i}_{\text{observed}} = \underbrace{\mu_i + \sum_{j=1}^{k} \ell_{ij} F_j}_{\text{common (systematic) part}} + \underbrace{\varepsilon_i}_{\text{unique (residual) part}}
$$

Taking variances on both sides (and noting $Cov(F_j, \varepsilon_i) = 0$):

$$
Var(X_i) = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \ell_{ij}^2 Var(F_j) + Var(\varepsilon_i)
$$

$$
1 = \underbrace{\sum_{j=1}^{k} \ell_{ij}^2}_{h_i^2 \text{ (communality)}} + \underbrace{\psi_i}_{\text{uniqueness}}
$$

This is the **fundamental variance partition identity** of Factor Analysis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why it matters:** In the KTU 2024 scheme, examiners often test whether students can articulate that $h_i^2 + \psi_i = 1$ for standardized variables. This single identity is worth 3 marks alone.

## 2.2 Assumptions of the Orthogonal Factor Model

For the mathematical theory to hold cleanly:

1. **Linearity** — The relationship between $X_i$ and the factors is linear.
2. **Standardization** — $E(X_i) = 0$, $Var(X_i) = 1$ (after z-score transformation).
3. **Common factors are independent** — $Cov(F_i, F_j) = 0$ for $i \neq j$ (orthogonal model).
4. **Unique factors are mutually independent** — $Cov(\varepsilon_i, \varepsilon_j) = 0$ for $i \neq j$.
5. **Common and unique factors are uncorrelated** — $Cov(F_j, \varepsilon_i) = 0$.
6. **Multivariate normality** of variables is required for Maximum Likelihood extraction.

## 2.3 Why Use Factor Analysis? — Practical Engineering & Business Utility

| Application Domain | Latent Factor Discovered |
|---|---|
| Marketing — Consumer Survey | Brand Loyalty, Price Consciousness, Quality Perception |
| Finance — Stock Returns | Market Risk, Sector Risk, Idiosyncratic Risk |
| HR — Employee Assessment | Cognitive Ability, Personality Traits, Leadership |
| Healthcare — Patient Records | Symptom Clusters, Disease Severity Index |
| Education — Exam Performance | Verbal Ability, Quantitative Ability, Analytical Reasoning |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Range / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Standard Factor Model | $X_i = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \ell_{ij} F_j + \varepsilon_i$ | $i = 1,\ldots,p$ |
| 2 | Correlation Decomposition | $R = LL^{\top} + \Psi$ | $R, \Psi$ are $p \times p$ |
| 3 | Communality of variable $i$ | $h_i^2 = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \ell_{ij}^2$ | $0 \leq h_i^2 \leq 1$ |
| 4 | Uniqueness of variable $i$ | $\psi_i = 1 - h_i^2$ | $0 \leq \psi_i \leq 1$ |
| 5 | Variance explained by factor $j$ | $\lambda_j = \sum_{i=1}^{p} \ell_{ij}^2$ | $\lambda_j \geq 0$ |
| 6 | Proportion of total variance | $\tau_j = \dfrac{\lambda_j}{p}$ | $\sum \tau_j \leq 1$ |
| 7 | Kaiser Criterion | Retain factors where $\lambda_j \geq 1$ | — |
| 8 | Cumulative Variance Criterion | $\sum_{j=1}^{k} \tau_j \geq 0.70$ (often 0.80) | Threshold |
| 9 | KMO Index (Sampling Adequacy) | $KMO = \dfrac{\sum_{i \neq j} r_{ij}^2}{\sum_{i \neq j} r_{ij}^2 + \sum_{i \neq j} a_{ij}^2}$ | KMO $\geq 0.6$ acceptable |
| 10 | Bartlett's Sphericity | $\chi^2 = -\left[(N-1)-\frac{2p+5}{6}\right] \ln \vert R \vert$ | $p$-value $< 0.05$ |
| 11 | Factor Score (Regression) | $\hat{F} = (L^{\top} R^{-1} L)^{-1} L^{\top} R^{-1} X$ | — |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Mark-Saver Tip:** When using the vertical-bar absolute value or determinant in your exam, **always** write $\vert R \vert$ (not $\vert R \vert$) to avoid the table-breaking pipe. Use LaTeX inline delimiters $...$ consistently.

## 2.5 Estimation Logic — What are we solving?

We **do not know** $L$ and $\Psi$ — they must be estimated from the sample correlation matrix $R$. The estimation procedure must satisfy:

$$
R \approx \hat{L}\hat{L}^{\top} + \hat{\Psi}
$$

Minimizing the residual matrix:

$$
\min_{L, \Psi} \vert\vert R - LL^{\top} - \Psi \vert\vert_F^2
$$

where $\vert\vert \cdot \vert\vert_F$ denotes the **Frobenius norm**. Different extraction methods (PCA, Principal Axis, Maximum Likelihood) differ in how they solve this optimization.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering / Business Analytics

- **Dimensionality Reduction** for high-dimensional sensor data in IoT pipelines.
- **Survey Validation** — confirm whether a designed questionnaire measures the intended latent constructs.
- **Image Compression** — early FA-based face recognition (Eigenfaces) used factor-like decompositions.
- **Portfolio Construction** — identify latent risk drivers in financial return data.
- **Recommender Systems** — discover latent user-preference dimensions from sparse rating matrices.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Variable to Factor Model

We start with the standardized observable vector $X = (X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_p)^{\top}$. We postulate that $X$ is generated by a *smaller* set of latent factors $F = (F_1, F_2, \ldots, F_k)^{\top}$ plus unique errors $\varepsilon = (\varepsilon_1, \varepsilon_2, \ldots, \varepsilon_p)^{\top}$.

**Step 1: Write the matrix form of the model.**

$$
X = \mu + L F + \varepsilon
$$

where $L$ is the $p \times k$ factor loading matrix with entries $\ell_{ij}$.

**Step 2: Compute the variance-covariance matrix of $X$.**

$$
Var(X) = E[(L F + \varepsilon)(L F + \varepsilon)^{\top}]
$$

**Step 3: Expand the product using bilinearity of expectation.**

$$
Var(X) = E[L F F^{\top} L^{\top}] + E[L F \varepsilon^{\top}] + E[\varepsilon F^{\top} L^{\top}] + E[\varepsilon \varepsilon^{\top}]
$$

**Step 4: Apply the orthogonality assumptions.**

- $E[F F^{\top}] = I_k$ (factors are uncorrelated, unit variance).
- $E[F \varepsilon^{\top}] = 0_{k \times p}$ (factors and uniqueness are independent).
- $E[\varepsilon \varepsilon^{\top}] = \Psi$ (diagonal uniqueness matrix).

Therefore:

$$
Var(X) = L \cdot I_k \cdot L^{\top} + 0 + 0 + \Psi
$$

**Step 5: Final decomposition identity.**

$$
\boxed{\Sigma = LL^{\top} + \Psi}
$$

For standardized variables $\Sigma = R$, the correlation matrix.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Communality from Squared Loadings

For standardized variable $X_i$, we have $Var(X_i) = 1$. From the model:

$$
X_i = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \ell_{ij} F_j + \varepsilon_i
$$

**Step 1: Compute the variance of both sides.**

$$
Var(X_i) = Var\left(\sum_{j=1}^{k} \ell_{ij} F_j + \varepsilon_i\right)
$$

**Step 2: Split the variance into common and unique parts.**

$$
Var(X_i) = Var\left(\sum_{j=1}^{k} \ell_{ij} F_j\right) + Var(\varepsilon_i)
$$

The cross-term vanishes because $F_j \perp \varepsilon_i$.

**Step 3: Expand the common variance using orthogonality of factors.**

$$
Var\left(\sum_{j=1}^{k} \ell_{ij} F_j\right) = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \ell_{ij}^2 Var(F_j) = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \ell_{ij}^2
$$

since $Var(F_j) = 1$.

**Step 4: Identify communality and uniqueness.**

$$
1 = \underbrace{\sum_{j=1}^{k} \ell_{ij}^2}_{h_i^2 \text{ communality}} + \underbrace{Var(\varepsilon_i)}_{\psi_i \text{ uniqueness}}
$$

**Step 5: Conclude the fundamental identity.**

$$
\boxed{h_i^2 = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \ell_{ij}^2 \quad ; \quad \psi_i = 1 - h_i^2}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Principal Component Extraction of Factors

The **Principal Component Method** approximates the correlation matrix $R$ using its first $k$ eigenvalues and eigenvectors.

**Step 1: Eigen-decompose the correlation matrix.**

$$
R = V \Lambda V^{\top}
$$

where $V = [v_1 \mid v_2 \mid \cdots \mid v_p]$ contains eigenvectors as columns, and $\Lambda = diag(\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \ldots, \lambda_p)$ contains eigenvalues sorted in descending order: $\lambda_1 \geq \lambda_2 \geq \cdots \geq \lambda_p$.

**Step 2: Truncate to the top $k$ components.**

$$
R \approx V_k \Lambda_k V_k^{\top}
$$

**Step 3: Identify the factor loading matrix.**

The principal component decomposition is structured such that $LL^{\top}$ matches the leading eigenvalues. Therefore:

$$
\hat{L} = V_k \sqrt{\Lambda_k}
$$

**Step 4: Verify by reconstruction.**

$$
\hat{L}\hat{L}^{\top} = V_k \sqrt{\Lambda_k} \sqrt{\Lambda_k} V_k^{\top} = V_k \Lambda_k V_k^{\top} \approx R
$$

**Step 5: Compute the communality for each variable.**

$$
\hat{h}_i^2 = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \hat{\ell}_{ij}^2 = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \lambda_j v_{ij}^2
$$

**Step 6: Compute the unique variance estimate.**

$$
\hat{\psi}_i = 1 - \hat{h}_i^2 = 1 - \sum_{j=1}^{k} \lambda_j v_{ij}^2
$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Variance Explained by Each Factor

The total variance of all $p$ standardized variables is $p$ (since each has variance 1). The variance attributable to factor $j$ is the sum of squared loadings in the $j$-th column of $L$:

$$
\lambda_j^{FA} = \sum_{i=1}^{p} \ell_{ij}^2
$$

**Proportion of total variance:**

$$
\text{Prop. variance}_j = \frac{\lambda_j^{FA}}{p}
$$

**Cumulative proportion after $k$ factors:**

$$
\text{Cumulative}_k = \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{k} \lambda_j^{FA}}{p}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Rule Integration (for KTU 14-mark questions):**
> 1. **Kaiser Criterion:** Retain factors with $\lambda_j \geq 1$ (since each factor should explain at least one variable's worth of variance in standardized data).
> 2. **Scree Plot Rule:** Drop factors after the "elbow" in the eigenvalue plot.
> 3. **Cumulative Variance Rule:** Retain enough factors such that cumulative proportion $\geq 0.70$–$0.80$.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Rotation: Varimax Criterion (Sketch)

After initial extraction, factors may be hard to interpret. **Varimax rotation** maximizes the sum of variances of squared loadings within each factor to achieve a "simple structure".

**Objective Function (Varimax):**

$$
V = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \left[ \frac{1}{p} \sum_{i=1}^{p} \ell_{ij}^{*4} - \left(\frac{1}{p} \sum_{i=1}^{p} \ell_{ij}^{*2}\right)^2 \right]
$$

where $\ell_{ij}^{*}$ are the **rotated loadings**. The rotation matrix $T$ is applied as:

$$
L^{*} = L \cdot T \quad \text{where} \quad T^{\top} T = I_k
$$

This preserves the orthogonality of factors and keeps the communalities unchanged.

## 3.6 Summary of Computable Quantities

After completing the analysis, the following must be reported (this is what KTU examiners expect in 14-mark answers):

| Output Quantity | How Computed | Business Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| Eigenvalues $\lambda_j$ | From $R$ decomposition | Total variance per factor |
| Factor Loadings $\ell_{ij}$ | From eigenvectors | Item-to-factor relationship |
| Communalities $h_i^2$ | Sum of squared loadings (per row) | How well each variable is explained |
| Variance Explained % | $(\lambda_j / p) \times 100$ | Practical importance of factor |
| Rotated Loadings | After Varimax / Promax | Cleaner interpretive pattern |
| Factor Scores | Regression of $X$ on $L$ | Position of each case on each factor |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Factor Analysis Workflow

The following Mermaid block diagram presents the complete Factor Analysis pipeline that the KTU 2024 scheme expects a student to articulate in a 14-mark descriptive question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Step 1: Problem Formulation and Variable Selection] --> nodeB[Step 2: Data Collection and Standardization]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Step 3: Compute Correlation Matrix R]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Step 4: Test Sampling Adequacy]
    nodeD --> nodeE{KMO greater than 0.6 and Bartlett p less than 0.05}
    nodeE -- Yes --> nodeF[Step 5: Choose Extraction Method]
    nodeE -- No --> nodeZ[STOP: Data Not Suitable for FA]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Step 6: Extract Initial Factors via Eigen Decomposition]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Step 7: Determine Number of Factors]
    nodeH --> nodeI{Kaiser Criterion plus Scree Plot plus Cumulative Variance}
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Step 8: Rotation using Varimax or Promax]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Step 9: Interpret Rotated Factor Loadings]
    nodeK --> nodeL[Step 10: Compute Factor Scores]
    nodeL --> nodeM[Step 11: Report and Validate Factor Structure]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Each block represents an obligatory stage. In KTU 14-mark answers, the examiner awards 1–2 marks for correctly stating the *sequence of stages*. The "STOP: Data Not Suitable" branch is a frequently-tested trap: if the correlation matrix is near-identity (variables uncorrelated), Factor Analysis is meaningless.

## 4.2 Conceptual Block Diagram — The Factor Model Decomposition

This block-level schematic illustrates the core decomposition at the heart of Factor Analysis. Each observed variable is built as a linear combination of latent factors plus a unique noise term.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeF1[Common Factor F1] --> nodeMix[Weighted Linear Combination L times F]
    nodeF2[Common Factor F2] --> nodeMix
    nodeFk[Common Factor Fk] --> nodeMix
    nodeMix --> nodeObs[Observed Variable Xi]
    nodeU[Unique Factor epsilon i] --> nodeObs
    nodeObs --> nodeDecomp[Variance Decomposition]
    nodeDecomp --> nodeCom[Communality hi squared]
    nodeDecomp --> nodeUn[Uniqueness psi i]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Architectural Insight:** The "block-level" abstraction here mirrors the KTU-emphasized distinction between the **systematic part** (driven by common factors, captured by $LL^{\top}$) and the **idiosyncratic part** (unique variance, captured by $\Psi$). This is the architectural foundation of every FA computation.

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Choosing the Number of Factors

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart[Compute All Eigenvalues of R] --> nodeSort[Sort Eigenvalues Descending]
    nodeSort --> nodeKaiser[Apply Kaiser Criterion: Retain lambda greater than 1]
    nodeKaiser --> nodeScree[Plot Scree and Locate Elbow]
    nodeScree --> nodeCumul[Check Cumulative Variance greater than 0.75]
    nodeCumul --> nodeParallel[Apply Horn Parallel Analysis if available]
    nodeParallel --> nodeFinal[Final Factor Count k]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick-Scoring Heuristic:** In a 14-mark answer, mention **at least two** of the four criteria (Kaiser, Scree, Cumulative Variance, Parallel Analysis). Awarding 2–3 marks for the *criterion selection* step is a common KTU valuation pattern.

## 4.4 Pre-FA Adequacy Testing Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeR[Input Correlation Matrix R] --> nodeBart[Bartlett Test of Sphericity]
    nodeR --> nodeKMOA[Compute KMO Index per Variable]
    nodeKMOA --> nodeKMOagg[Aggregate KMO Index]
    nodeBart --> nodeDecide{Overall Adequacy Decision}
    nodeKMOagg --> nodeDecide
    nodeDecide --> nodeGo[Proceed with Factor Extraction]
    nodeDecide --> nodeStop[Reject FA: Use PCA only or Redesign]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: Define Factor Analysis. [3 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (2 marks):** Factor Analysis is a multivariate statistical technique used to model the interrelationships among a large set of observed variables in terms of a smaller number of unobserved latent variables called **common factors**, plus unique error terms for each observation.
>
> **Objective (1 mark):** Its two main objectives are (a) **data reduction** — replacing $p$ correlated variables with $k < p$ factors, and (b) **latent construct identification** — discovering the hidden dimensions that explain observed variable correlations.

### Question A2: Differentiate between Communality and Uniqueness. [3 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Communality ($h_i^2$) | Uniqueness ($\psi_i$) |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Proportion of variable's variance explained by all common factors | Proportion of variable's variance NOT explained by common factors |
| **Formula** | $h_i^2 = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \ell_{ij}^2$ | $\psi_i = 1 - h_i^2$ |
| **Range** | $0 \leq h_i^2 \leq 1$ | $0 \leq \psi_i \leq 1$ |
| **Interpretation** | Higher $h_i^2$ = variable is well-represented by factors | Higher $\psi_i$ = variable is unique/noisy |

(Each row is awarded ~1 mark; the differentiating conclusion is awarded the final mark.)

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question B-A: Principal Component Method of Factor Extraction** [14 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the Principal Component Method of Factor Extraction. Derive the factor loading matrix and the communality estimates using eigenvalue decomposition of the correlation matrix $R$. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A marketing analyst collects data on 5 customer attributes. The correlation matrix is given below. Perform a Principal Component Factor Analysis and report: (i) the eigenvalues, (ii) the factor loadings for the first two principal components, (iii) the communalities, and (iv) the total variance explained. **[7 Marks]**

$$
R = \begin{pmatrix}
1.000 & 0.620 & 0.580 & 0.400 & 0.300 \\
0.620 & 1.000 & 0.550 & 0.380 & 0.280 \\
0.580 & 0.550 & 1.000 & 0.450 & 0.330 \\
0.400 & 0.380 & 0.450 & 1.000 & 0.500 \\
0.300 & 0.280 & 0.330 & 0.500 & 1.000
\end{pmatrix}
$$

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Theory (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Eigen-decompose the correlation matrix $R$.

$$
R = V \Lambda V^{\top}
$$

where $V$ contains eigenvectors and $\Lambda = diag(\lambda_1, \ldots, \lambda_p)$ contains eigenvalues.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Retain the top $k$ eigenvalues. The principal component approximation is:

$$
R \approx V_k \Lambda_k V_k^{\top}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Define the factor loading matrix.

$$
\hat{L} = V_k \sqrt{\Lambda_k}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Derive communalities from the loadings.

$$
\hat{h}_i^2 = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \hat{\ell}_{ij}^2
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Conclude the variance explained by factor $j$:

$$
\lambda_j^{FA} = \sum_{i=1}^{p} \ell_{ij}^2
$$

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks):** The eigenvalues of $R$ (solved via standard eigen-routines) are:

$$
\lambda_1 = 2.741, \quad \lambda_2 = 1.062, \quad \lambda_3 = 0.583, \quad \lambda_4 = 0.354, \quad \lambda_5 = 0.260
$$

**Check:** $\sum \lambda_j = 2.741 + 1.062 + 0.583 + 0.354 + 0.260 = 5.000 = p$. ✔

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Apply the Kaiser criterion — retain factors with $\lambda_j \geq 1$. Two factors qualify: $\lambda_1$ and $\lambda_2$.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute the first two eigenvectors (normalized to unit length):

$$
v_1 = \begin{pmatrix} 0.510 \\ 0.491 \\ 0.510 \\ 0.382 \\ 0.301 \end{pmatrix}, \quad
v_2 = \begin{pmatrix} -0.330 \\ -0.262 \\ -0.221 \\ 0.495 \\ 0.726 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Compute factor loadings:

$$
\hat{L} = V_k \sqrt{\Lambda_k} = \begin{pmatrix}
0.510 \sqrt{2.741} & -0.330 \sqrt{1.062} \\
0.491 \sqrt{2.741} & -0.262 \sqrt{1.062} \\
0.510 \sqrt{2.741} & -0.221 \sqrt{1.062} \\
0.382 \sqrt{2.741} & 0.495 \sqrt{1.062} \\
0.301 \sqrt{2.741} & 0.726 \sqrt{1.062}
\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}
0.845 & -0.340 \\
0.813 & -0.270 \\
0.845 & -0.228 \\
0.633 & 0.510 \\
0.498 & 0.748
\end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Compute communalities:

$$
\hat{h}_1^2 = (0.845)^2 + (-0.340)^2 = 0.714 + 0.116 = 0.830
$$

$$
\hat{h}_2^2 = (0.813)^2 + (-0.270)^2 = 0.661 + 0.073 = 0.734
$$

$$
\hat{h}_3^2 = (0.845)^2 + (-0.228)^2 = 0.714 + 0.052 = 0.766
$$

$$
\hat{h}_4^2 = (0.633)^2 + (0.510)^2 = 0.401 + 0.260 = 0.661
$$

$$
\hat{h}_5^2 = (0.498)^2 + (0.748)^2 = 0.248 + 0.560 = 0.808
$$

**Step 6 (bonus / verification):** Total variance explained = $(2.741 + 1.062) / 5 = 0.761 = 76.1\%$. Since $\geq 75\%$, the two-factor solution is acceptable.

---

### **Question B-B: Factor Rotation and Interpretation** [14 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** What is Factor Rotation? Explain the Varimax rotation method and discuss why rotation is needed. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A study on employee satisfaction yields the following unrotated factor loadings for 4 variables on 2 factors. Apply Varimax rotation and interpret the resulting factor structure. **[7 Marks]**

$$
L_{unrotated} = \begin{pmatrix}
0.70 & 0.50 \\
0.65 & 0.55 \\
0.55 & 0.60 \\
0.60 & 0.45
\end{pmatrix}
$$

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Theory (7 marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks):** **Definition.** Factor rotation is a linear orthogonal transformation applied to the loading matrix $L$ to obtain a new loading matrix $L^* = L T$, where $T^{\top} T = I_k$. The transformation preserves communality and orthogonality of factors.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** **Why rotation is needed.** Initial extracted factors are mathematical solutions with no inherent interpretive meaning. Rotation reorients the factor axes to achieve **simple structure** — where each variable loads highly on exactly one factor and low on all others — making the factors substantively meaningful.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** **Varimax Criterion.** Varimax is an *orthogonal* rotation that maximizes the variance of squared loadings within each factor:

$$
V = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \left[ \frac{1}{p}\sum_{i=1}^{p} \ell_{ij}^{*4} - \left(\frac{1}{p}\sum_{i=1}^{p} \ell_{ij}^{*2}\right)^2 \right]
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** **Distinguish from oblique rotation.** Varimax is orthogonal; Promax and Oblimin are oblique (factors may correlate).

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Compute the unrotated communalities and the rotation angle.

The factor correlation matrix after centering:

$$
\text{Loadings}^2: \begin{pmatrix} 0.49 & 0.25 \\ 0.4225 & 0.3025 \\ 0.3025 & 0.36 \\ 0.36 & 0.2025 \end{pmatrix}
$$

Apply the standard Varimax angle formula:

$$
\tan(4\theta) = \frac{D - 2AB / p}{C - (A^2 - B^2)/p}
$$

where $A = \sum \ell_{i1}^2$, $B = \sum \ell_{i2}^2$, $C = \sum (\ell_{i1}^4 - \ell_{i2}^4)$, $D = 2 \sum \ell_{i1}^2 \ell_{i2}^2$.

**Numerical calculation:**

- $A = 0.49 + 0.4225 + 0.3025 + 0.36 = 1.575$
- $B = 0.25 + 0.3025 + 0.36 + 0.2025 = 1.115$
- $C = (0.49^2 - 0.25^2) + (0.4225^2 - 0.3025^2) + (0.3025^2 - 0.36^2) + (0.36^2 - 0.2025^2)$
- $C = 0.1776 + 0.0870 - 0.0203 + 0.0887 = 0.3330$
- $D = 2(0.70 \cdot 0.50 + 0.65 \cdot 0.55 + 0.55 \cdot 0.60 + 0.60 \cdot 0.45) = 2(0.35 + 0.3575 + 0.33 + 0.27) = 2(1.3075) = 2.615$

With $p = 4$:

$$
\tan(4\theta) = \frac{0.3330 - 2(1.575)(1.115)/4}{2.615 - (1.575^2 - 1.115^2)/4} = \frac{0.3330 - 0.8781}{2.615 - 0.3093} = \frac{-0.5451}{2.3057} = -0.2364
$$

$$
4\theta = -13.30^\circ \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta \approx -3.32^\circ
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Construct the rotation matrix:

$$
T = \begin{pmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.9983 & 0.0580 \\ -0.0580 & 0.9983 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute rotated loadings:

$$
L^* = L \cdot T = \begin{pmatrix}
0.70 & 0.50 \\
0.65 & 0.55 \\
0.55 & 0.60 \\
0.60 & 0.45
\end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 0.9983 & 0.0580 \\ -0.0580 & 0.9983 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}
0.670 & 0.540 \\
0.617 & 0.587 \\
0.514 & 0.631 \\
0.573 & 0.484
\end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** **Interpretation.** Variables 1 and 2 load more on Factor 1; Variables 3 and 4 load more on Factor 2. Compared to the unrotated structure, the loadings are now more differentiated, supporting a clear **simple structure interpretation**. In business terms, Factor 1 could represent "Job-Satisfaction Drivers" and Factor 2 could represent "Workplace-Engagement Drivers".

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Factor Analysis with PCA (2–3 marks lost):** FA explicitly models a *unique error* term $\psi_i$ for each variable. PCA does not. KTU examiners frequently award a "trick question" mark for this distinction.
> 2. **Forgetting to verify $h_i^2 + \psi_i = 1$ (1 mark lost):** Always end the numerical problem with a verification row.
> 3. **Misreporting variance explained:** Report *proportion* = $\lambda_j / p$, not the raw eigenvalue, in the "total variance explained" interpretation.
> 4. **Skipping rotation interpretation (2 marks lost):** In a 14-mark question on Factor Analysis, simply computing rotated loadings without business-context interpretation forfeits the "analysis" component marks.
> 5. **Confusing orthogonal vs. oblique rotation:** Varimax is orthogonal (factors remain independent). Promax is oblique (factors allowed to correlate). Mixing these loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Factor Analysis**

- **Definition:** FA models $p$ observed variables using $k < p$ latent common factors + unique errors.
- **Core equation:** $X = LF + \varepsilon$ → Variance decomposition: $R = LL^{\top} + \Psi$.
- **Communality:** $h_i^2 = \sum_{j=1}^{k} \ell_{ij}^2$ — proportion of variable $i$'s variance explained by common factors.
- **Uniqueness:** $\psi_i = 1 - h_i^2$ — the residual, variable-specific variance.
- **Identity check:** $h_i^2 + \psi_i = 1$ (for standardized variables).
- **Eigenvalue criterion:** Retain factors with $\lambda_j \geq 1$ (Kaiser rule).
- **Scree plot:** Locate the "elbow" in the eigenvalue plot.
- **Cumulative variance:** Retain factors such that cumulative proportion $\geq 0.75$–$0.80$.
- **Adequacy tests:** KMO $\geq 0.6$ AND Bartlett's test $p < 0.05$ are required before extraction.
- **Extraction methods:** Principal Component (default), Principal Axis Factoring, Maximum Likelihood.
- **Rotation:** Varimax (orthogonal, default) vs. Promax/Oblimin (oblique).
- **Simple structure:** Each variable should load high on exactly one factor and low on others.
- **Factor Score formula:** $\hat{F} = (L^{\top}R^{-1}L)^{-1}L^{\top}R^{-1}X$ (Bartlett's regression method).
- **Practical uses:** Marketing segmentation, psychometric test design, portfolio risk decomposition, dimensionality reduction in high-dimensional datasets.
- **Common trap:** Factor Analysis is *not* PCA — PCA is a special case where $\Psi = 0$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: And Segmentation Analysis. Build Spread Sheet Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT522 - BUSINESS ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** And Segmentation Analysis. Build Spread Sheet Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Segmentation Analysis — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Segmentation Analysis** is a strategic analytical process that divides a heterogeneous market, customer base, or population into relatively homogeneous sub-groups (*segments*) based on shared characteristics, needs, behaviours, or demographics, so that each sub-group can be targeted, served, or analysed with a tailored strategy.

In the KTU 2024 scheme context, segmentation analysis is treated as a **descriptive–diagnostic analytics** technique that precedes prescriptive modelling. It allows decision-makers to move from *one-size-fits-all* decisions to *segment-specific* decisions, increasing conversion, retention, and ROI.

The four classical bases of segmentation (often remembered as the **4 Ds of STP-D segmentation**):

1. **Demographic** — Age, gender, income, education, occupation, family size.
2. **Geographic** — Region, climate, urban/rural, population density.
3. **Psychographic** — Lifestyle, values, personality, attitudes (VALS framework).
4. **Behavioural** — Usage rate, loyalty, benefits sought, occasion, readiness stage.

A fifth, modern base widely accepted in digital analytics:
5. **Technographic / Need-based** — Device usage, platform affinity, jobs-to-be-done.

## 1.2 Build Spreadsheet Models — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> A **Spreadsheet Model** is a structured, cell-based computational representation of a real-world business problem in which *inputs* (assumptions/parameters), *calculations* (formulas/relationships) and *outputs* (decisions/key metrics) are clearly separated so that the model behaves predictably under *what-if* and *sensitivity* analysis.

In KTU Business Analytics (PEADT522), spreadsheet models are positioned as the **primary vehicle for managerial decision modelling** — they are the bridge between analytical theory (statistics, optimisation, finance) and managerial action.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Restaurant Menu & Recipe Card"

> [!TIP]
> **Think of segmentation like a restaurant menu.** A single restaurant cannot serve *everyone* the same dish — vegetarians, spice-lovers, children, and diabetic customers each need a different offering. The chef (marketer/manager) first **segments** the customers by taste, then **targets** a profitable group, and finally **positions** a tailored dish. The **spreadsheet model** is the *recipe card* on the kitchen wall: it lists ingredients (inputs), the cooking procedure (formulas), and the final plating size (outputs). Change an ingredient (e.g., oil from 10 ml to 15 ml) and the entire dish changes — just like a *what-if* analysis changes the entire decision.

This three-layer mental model — **Segment → Model → Decide** — is the heart of Module 4.

## 1.4 The STP–Segmentation–Spreadsheet Linkage

The textbook flow that every KTU answer must follow:

$$\text{Segment} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Profile} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Model} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Decide}$$

Where:
- **Segment** = grouping step
- **Profile** = statistical description of each segment
- **Model** = spreadsheet (or analytical) representation
- **Decide** = what-if / sensitivity / optimisation output

## 1.5 Standard Metrics a KTU Examiner Will Test

| Metric | Symbol | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| Segment Size | $N_i$ | Number of customers in segment $i$ |
| Segment Profit | $\Pi_i$ | Revenue minus cost for segment $i$ |
| Segment Value | $SV_i$ | Lifetime value of segment $i$ |
| Penetration Rate | $PR$ | % of segment reached |
| Response Rate | $RR_i$ | % of segment that responds |
| Break-Even Volume | $Q^*$ | Volume where $\Pi = 0$ |
| Profit Margin | $m$ | (Price $-$ Cost) / Price |

> [!WARNING]
> KTU examiners *frequently* test the difference between **segmentation variables** (the attributes used to split) and **segmentation descriptors** (attributes used to describe segments *after* splitting). Mixing them up is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Segmentation Process — Step-by-Step

1. **Define the Market** — Identify the total addressable market (TAM) boundary.
2. **Choose Segmentation Base** — Demographic, geographic, psychographic, behavioural, or need-based.
3. **Identify Segmentation Variables** — Convert base into measurable variables (e.g., income is *demographic*; attitude toward risk is *psychographic*).
4. **Develop Segment Profiles** — Build a profile table for each resulting group.
5. **Evaluate Segment Attractiveness** — Use the **SEGMENT** criteria.
6. **Select Target Segments** — Apply targeting strategies (undifferentiated, concentrated, differentiated, micromarketing).
7. **Position & Launch** — Develop segment-specific value proposition.

## 2.2 The SEGMENT Attractiveness Criteria (High-Yield)

For KTU exams, a segment is attractive if it satisfies:

| Letter | Criterion | Operational Question |
|---|---|---|
| **S** | Size | Is the segment large enough to be profitable? |
| **E** | Expected growth | Will it grow in the future? |
| **G** | Gap (Accessibility) | Can we reach it with our channels? |
| **M** | Measurability | Can we measure its size and reactions? |
| **E** | Earnings | Does it offer profit potential? |
| **N** | Non-divisibility / Compatibility | Does it fit our resources? |
| **T** | Timeliness / Sustainability | Is it stable and defensible? |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic:** *Some Excellent Gems Make Earnings Non-divisible Today.* A KTU 12-mark question can be fully answered by applying this framework to a case study.

## 2.3 Statistical Distance Between Segments

Once we have quantitative variables, the distance between two customer records $x = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_p)$ and $y = (y_1, y_2, \dots, y_p)$ is measured by:

**Euclidean Distance:**

$$d(x, y) = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{p} (x_j - y_j)^2}$$

**Manhattan Distance (preferred for mixed / categorical data):**

$$d(x, y) = \sum_{j=1}^{p} \vert x_j - y_j \vert$$

**Standardised Euclidean (Z-score normalised):**

$$d_Z(x, y) = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{p} \left(\frac{x_j - y_j}{s_j}\right)^2}$$

where $s_j$ is the standard deviation of variable $j$.

## 2.4 RFM Segmentation (Recency – Frequency – Monetary)

> [!NOTE]
> **RFM** is the most exam-relevant *behavioural segmentation* model in B.Tech Business Analytics. It assigns each customer three scores (commonly 1–5) and then forms segments such as **Champions**, **Loyal Customers**, **At Risk**, **Hibernating**.

| Component | Symbol | Formula (operational) |
|---|---|---|
| Recency | $R$ | Days since last purchase (lower = higher score) |
| Frequency | $F$ | Number of purchases in lookback window |
| Monetary | $M$ | Total spend in lookback window |
| RFM Score | $S$ | $S = R + F + M$ (range 3–15) |

## 2.5 Spreadsheet Model — The Three-Layer Architecture

A well-built spreadsheet model has three logically separated layers:

| Layer | Cell Convention | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Inputs (Assumptions)** | Blue font, named ranges | All changeable parameters live here |
| **Calculations (Engine)** | Black font, formulas | Derived values; never hard-coded |
| **Outputs (Decision)** | Bold/boxed, top-of-sheet | Key metrics visible at a glance |

> [!TIP]
> KTU answer scripts that *describe* this three-layer separation always score 1–2 bonus marks in 14-mark model-building questions.

## 2.6 High-Yield Formula Sheet for Spreadsheet Models

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-day cheat sheet** for Module 4. Memorise the units and the boundary conditions.

| # | Model / Concept | Formula | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total Revenue | $TR = P \times Q$ | Currency |
| 2 | Total Cost | $TC = FC + vQ$ | Currency, $FC$ = fixed, $v$ = variable/unit |
| 3 | Profit | $\Pi = TR - TC = (P - v)Q - FC$ | Currency |
| 4 | Break-Even Quantity | $Q^* = \dfrac{FC}{P - v}$ | Units; condition $P > v$ |
| 5 | Break-Even Revenue | $R^* = P \cdot Q^* = \dfrac{FC \cdot P}{P - v}$ | Currency |
| 6 | Contribution Margin | $CM = P - v$ | Currency per unit |
| 7 | Margin of Safety (units) | $MS = Q_{actual} - Q^*$ | Units |
| 8 | Margin of Safety (%) | $MS\% = \dfrac{Q_{actual} - Q^*}{Q_{actual}} \times 100$ | % |
| 9 | Operating Leverage | $DOL = \dfrac{Contribution}{\Pi}$ | Dimensionless |
| 10 | Payback Period | $PP = \dfrac{Initial\ Investment}{Annual\ Cash\ Inflow}$ | Years |
| 11 | NPV | $NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}$ | Currency |
| 12 | Linear Programming (LP) Objective | $\max/\min\ Z = c_1 x_1 + c_2 x_2 + \dots + c_n x_n$ | — |
| 13 | LP Constraint (typical) | $a_{i1}x_1 + a_{i2}x_2 \le b_i$ | — |
| 14 | Sensitivity (1-Variable Output) | $\Delta O = \dfrac{\partial O}{\partial x} \cdot \Delta x$ | Output units |
| 15 | Tornado Sensitivity | $S_k = \dfrac{O_{max,k} - O_{min,k}}{\vert O_{base} \vert}$ | Ratio |
| 16 | RFM Recency Score | $R_i = 5 - \lfloor 4 \cdot (d_i / d_{max}) \rfloor$ | Integer 1–5 |
| 17 | Customer Lifetime Value | $CLV = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \dfrac{M_t \cdot r_t}{(1+d)^t} - CAC$ | Currency |
| 18 | Segment Profitability Index | $SPI_i = \dfrac{\Pi_i / N_i}{\max_j (\Pi_j / N_j)}$ | 0–1 |
| 19 | Penetration Index | $PI_i = \dfrac{N_i^{buyers}}{N_i^{total}}$ | 0–1 |
| 20 | Distance (Euclidean) | $d(x,y) = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{p}(x_j - y_j)^2}$ | Variable units |

## 2.7 Why This Matters in Real Business Analytics

- **Marketing Mix Modelling** — Each segment is fed into a separate *what-if* model for price, promotion, place, product.
- **Customer Relationship Management (CRM)** — RFM segmentation feeds retention campaigns.
- **Financial Planning** — Break-even and NPV models in spreadsheets determine go/no-go.
- **Operations** — LP-based resource allocation in spreadsheets solves product-mix and blending problems.
- **Risk Analysis** — Tornado and spider sensitivity in Excel ranks which assumptions matter most.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry reality:** Over **80 % of mid-market firms** still use Excel as the primary modelling tool for tactical decisions, even when they have advanced BI tools. The KTU syllabus reflects this industry reality.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Break-Even Quantity

We start with the standard profit identity.

$$\Pi = TR - TC$$

Substituting $TR = P \cdot Q$ and $TC = FC + v \cdot Q$:

$$\Pi = P \cdot Q - (FC + v \cdot Q)$$

Group the $Q$ terms:

$$\Pi = (P - v)Q - FC$$

At the break-even point, by definition $\Pi = 0$:

$$(P - v) Q^* - FC = 0$$

Solve for $Q^*$:

$$(P - v) Q^* = FC$$

$$Q^* = \frac{FC}{P - v}$$

This is the **minimum volume** the firm must sell to cover all costs.

**Boundary condition:** The model is *only* meaningful when $P > v$. If $P \le v$, the firm loses money on every additional unit and the break-even quantity is undefined (or negative — interpret as *never*).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Operating Leverage from First Principles

Operating leverage measures how a percentage change in sales translates to a percentage change in profit.

$$DOL = \frac{\%\Delta \Pi}{\%\Delta Q}$$

Since $\Pi = (P - v)Q - FC$, differentiate with respect to $Q$:

$$\frac{d\Pi}{dQ} = P - v$$

Divide by $\Pi$:

$$DOL = \frac{(P - v)Q}{(P - v)Q - FC} = \frac{Contribution}{Profit}$$

The denominator $\Pi$ can be rewritten as $Contribution - FC$, which exposes the *fixed-cost trap*: as $FC \to Contribution$, the $DOL \to \infty$, meaning a 1 % change in sales produces a near-infinite change in profit. This is the **operational risk** of high fixed-cost businesses (airlines, telecoms, manufacturing).

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — NPV and the Internal Rate of Return (IRR)

The Net Present Value of a project with cash flows $CF_t$ over $n$ periods at discount rate $r$:

$$NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1 + r)^t}$$

For a typical 3-year project with initial investment $I_0$ (a negative cash flow at $t=0$) and uniform annual cash inflow $A$:

$$NPV = -I_0 + \frac{A}{(1+r)} + \frac{A}{(1+r)^2} + \frac{A}{(1+r)^3}$$

Apply the geometric-series formula $\sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{1}{(1+r)^t} = \frac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r}$:

$$NPV = -I_0 + A \cdot \frac{1 - (1+r)^{-3}}{r}$$

The **IRR** is the value of $r$ that makes $NPV = 0$:

$$0 = -I_0 + A \cdot \frac{1 - (1+r)^{-n}}{r}$$

In a spreadsheet, the IRR is solved numerically via the `=IRR()` function using a bisection / Newton-style algorithm (Excel's goal-seek).

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — RFM Score Construction

Suppose customer $i$ has, over a 12-month window:

- $d_i$ = days since last purchase (recency raw)
- $f_i$ = number of transactions (frequency raw)
- $m_i$ = total spend (monetary raw)

Define the lookback maximums: $d_{max}, f_{max}, m_{max}$.

Convert each to a quintile score 1–5:

$$R_i = 1 + \left\lfloor 4 \cdot \frac{d_{max} - d_i}{d_{max} - d_{min}} \right\rfloor$$

$$F_i = 1 + \left\lfloor 4 \cdot \frac{f_i - f_{min}}{f_{max} - f_{min}} \right\rfloor$$

$$M_i = 1 + \left\lfloor 4 \cdot \frac{m_i - m_{min}}{m_{max} - m_{min}} \right\rfloor$$

> Note: $R_i$ uses an *inverted* transformation — *less* recency = *higher* score. The floor function $\lfloor \cdot \rfloor$ is the *greatest integer less than or equal to* operator.

Total RFM score:

$$S_i = R_i + F_i + M_i \in [3, 15]$$

Segmentation rule of thumb:

| $S_i$ Range | Segment Name | Action |
|---|---|---|
| 13–15 | **Champions** | Reward, early access |
| 11–12 | **Loyal Customers** | Upsell, cross-sell |
| 8–10 | **Potential Loyalists** | Onboarding offers |
| 6–7 | **At Risk** | Win-back campaigns |
| 4–5 | **Hibernating** | Reactivation or drop |
| 3 | **Lost** | Remove from active campaigns |

## 3.5 Worked Example — Spreadsheet Model for a Two-Product Mix

A firm sells two products A and B with the following data:

| Parameter | Product A | Product B |
|---|---|---|
| Price (₹) | 50 | 80 |
| Variable cost (₹) | 30 | 50 |
| Machine hours / unit | 2 | 4 |
| Maximum demand (units) | 1000 | 600 |
| Fixed cost (₹) | 20,000 (shared) | — |

Total machine hours available per period: **3,000 hours**.

**Step 1 — Build the LP.**

Decision variables: $x_1$ = units of A, $x_2$ = units of B.

Objective (maximise contribution):

$$\max\ Z = (50-30)x_1 + (80-50)x_2 = 20x_1 + 30x_2$$

Subject to:

$$2x_1 + 4x_2 \le 3000$$

$$x_1 \le 1000$$

$$x_2 \le 600$$

$$x_1, x_2 \ge 0$$

**Step 2 — Solve graphically (KTU frequently tests this).**

Corner points of the feasible region:

| Corner | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $Z = 20x_1 + 30x_2$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| (0, 0) | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| (1000, 0) | 1000 | 0 | 20,000 |
| (1000, 250) | 1000 | 250 | 20,000 + 7,500 = 27,500 |
| (300, 600) | 300 | 600 | 6,000 + 18,000 = 24,000 |
| (0, 600) | 0 | 600 | 18,000 |

Maximum at $(x_1, x_2) = (1000, 250)$:

$$Z^* = ₹27,500\ \text{contribution}$$

Subtract fixed cost:

$$\Pi^* = 27,500 - 20,000 = ₹7,500$$

**Step 3 — Sensitivity.**

- If price of A increases by ₹1, contribution/unit of A becomes ₹21.
- New objective: $Z = 21x_1 + 30x_2$.
- Re-solving gives the same corner point (binding constraints do not change) → solution is **stable** in the range of A's price from ₹45 to ₹75.
- This is exactly the kind of insight a *what-if* analysis in Excel delivers.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Connecting Segmentation to Spreadsheet

> [!NOTE]
> This table is the **central exam-ready mapping** of the entire Module 4. Use it as a one-page revision.

| Analytical Step | Segmentation Output | Spreadsheet Model Input | Decision Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Define market | TAM = ₹10 Cr | `Input!TAM` | — |
| 2. Choose variables | Income, Age, Recency | `Input!IncomeRange`, `Input!AgeRange` | — |
| 3. Cluster | 4 segments: A, B, C, D | `Input!SegID` | Segment sizes $N_A, N_B, N_C, N_D$ |
| 4. Profile | Mean spend, mean frequency per segment | `Input!MeanSpend_i` | — |
| 5. Build model | — | `Calc!Revenue_i = N_i × MeanSpend_i$ | Revenue by segment |
| 6. Cost allocation | Fixed cost split by usage | `Calc!FC_i = FC × UsageShare_i$ | Cost by segment |
| 7. Profit | — | `Calc!Profit_i = Revenue_i - Cost_i$ | Segment profit |
| 8. Sensitivity | What if price changes ±10 % | `Calc!Price_Change` | Tornado chart |
| 9. Optimise | Best segment mix | Solver / LP | Optimal allocation |
| 10. Decide | Go / no-go per segment | — | Strategic action |

## 3.7 Common Pitfall — Confusing *Cumulative* and *Incremental* Sensitivity

When running a tornado analysis, the *range* of the output for input variable $k$ is:

$$\Delta O_k = O(\text{Input}_k = High) - O(\text{Input}_k = Low)$$

while holding **all other inputs at base values**. A common KTU error is to vary *all* inputs simultaneously, which produces a *Monte-Carlo* range, not a tornado. The two are different:

- **Tornado (deterministic, one-at-a-time):** $\Delta O_k^{Tornado} = O_k^{max} - O_k^{min}$
- **Monte-Carlo (probabilistic):** $P(O < O_{threshold}) = \int f(O)\, dO$

> [!WARNING]
> A KTU answer that mixes these two methods in a 14-mark question loses 2–3 marks for *methodological imprecision*.
