# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> *Define segmentation analysis. List the four classical bases of segmentation with one example variable for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Segmentation analysis is the process of dividing a heterogeneous market into relatively homogeneous sub-groups (*segments*) based on shared characteristics, so that each group can be analysed or targeted with a tailored strategy **(1 mark)**.

Four classical bases with example variables **(2 marks — 0.5 each):**

| Base | Example Variable |
|---|---|
| Demographic | Annual household income (₹) |
| Geographic | Region (North / South / Coastal) |
| Psychographic | Lifestyle (Adventurer / Traditionalist) |
| Behavioural | Loyalty status (Loyal / Switcher / Prospect) |

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> *Distinguish between 'segmentation variables' and 'segmentation descriptors' with an example.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- A **segmentation variable** is the attribute *used to split* the market into segments **(1 mark)**.
- A **segmentation descriptor** is an attribute *used to describe* the segments once they are formed **(1 mark)**.
- *Example:* For a coffee brand, *age group* is the **variable** (used to form segments: 18–25, 26–40, 40+). The *average cups consumed per week* is a **descriptor** used to profile those segments after they are formed **(1 mark)**.

## 5.2 Part B — Model Questions (14 Marks each)

> **Question A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**
>
> A retail chain has identified three customer segments — **A (Champions)**, **B (Loyal)**, and **C (At Risk)** — based on RFM analysis. The following data is available:
>
> | Parameter | A | B | C |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | Segment Size $N_i$ | 1,200 | 2,500 | 1,800 |
> | Avg. Spend per Customer (₹) | 4,000 | 2,200 | 800 |
> | Marketing Cost per Customer (₹) | 200 | 150 | 100 |
> | Expected Response Rate | 25 % | 12 % | 4 % |
> | Margin on Sales | 30 % | 25 % | 15 % |
>
> **(a) [7 marks]** Build a spreadsheet model (describe the three layers) to compute the **segment-wise profit** and the **total profit**. Show all formulas.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** Perform a *what-if analysis* assuming a 10 % price discount in Segment B increases response rate to 18 % but reduces margin to 18 %. Identify the most and least profitable segment under this new scenario.

### Model Solution — Question A

#### (a) Spreadsheet Model — Three Layers [7 marks]

**Layer 1 — INPUTS (Blue, Named Ranges) [2 marks]**

| Cell | Name | Value |
|---|---|---|
| B2 | N_A | 1,200 |
| B3 | N_B | 2,500 |
| B4 | N_C | 1,800 |
| C2 | Spend_A | 4,000 |
| C3 | Spend_B | 2,200 |
| C4 | Spend_C | 800 |
| D2 | MktCost_A | 200 |
| D3 | MktCost_B | 150 |
| D4 | MktCost_C | 100 |
| E2 | RR_A | 0.25 |
| E3 | RR_B | 0.12 |
| E4 | RR_C | 0.04 |
| F2 | Margin_A | 0.30 |
| F3 | Margin_B | 0.25 |
| F4 | Margin_C | 0.15 |

**Layer 2 — CALCULATIONS (Black, Formulas) [3 marks]**

| Cell | Formula | Computes |
|---|---|---|
| G2 | `=B2*C2*E2*F2 - B2*D2` | Profit_A |
| G3 | `=B3*C3*E3*F3 - B3*D3` | Profit_B |
| G4 | `=B4*C4*E4*F4 - B4*D4` | Profit_C |
| H2 | `=SUM(G2:G4)` | Total_Profit |

Numerical evaluation:

**Segment A:**
$$\Pi_A = 1200 \times 4000 \times 0.25 \times 0.30 - 1200 \times 200$$
$$\Pi_A = 360{,}000 - 240{,}000 = ₹120{,}000$$

**Segment B:**
$$\Pi_B = 2500 \times 2200 \times 0.12 \times 0.25 - 2500 \times 150$$
$$\Pi_B = 165{,}000 - 375{,}000 = -₹210{,}000$$

**Segment C:**
$$\Pi_C = 1800 \times 800 \times 0.04 \times 0.15 - 1800 \times 100$$
$$\Pi_C = 8{,}640 - 180{,}000 = -₹171{,}360$$

**Layer 3 — OUTPUTS (Bold, Boxed) [2 marks]**

| Output | Value (₹) |
|---|---|
| Profit_A | **1,20,000** |
| Profit_B | **(2,10,000)** |
| Profit_C | **(1,71,360)** |
| **Total Profit** | **(2,61,360)** |

**Conclusion [0 marks — implicit]:** Segment A is the only profitable segment. The chain must redesign the campaign for B and C or de-prioritise them.

#### (b) What-If Analysis — Price Discount in Segment B [7 marks]

New assumptions for Segment B: $RR_B^{new} = 0.18$, $Margin_B^{new} = 0.18$ (revenue per customer drops by 10 %, so spend per customer becomes $2200 \times 0.9 = 1980$).

**Re-computation for Segment B (and unchanged A, C):**

$$\Pi_B^{new} = 2500 \times 1980 \times 0.18 \times 0.18 - 2500 \times 150$$
$$\Pi_B^{new} = 2500 \times 1980 \times 0.0324 - 375{,}000$$
$$\Pi_B^{new} = 160{,}380 - 375{,}000 = -₹214{,}620$$

**Updated total profit:**

$$\Pi_{total}^{new} = 120{,}000 + (-214{,}620) + (-171{,}360) = -₹265{,}980$$

**Comparison Table [3 marks]:**

| Segment | Profit (Base) ₹ | Profit (What-if) ₹ | Change ₹ |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | 1,20,000 | 1,20,000 | 0 |
| B | (2,10,000) | (2,14,620) | (4,620) ↓ |
| C | (1,71,360) | (1,71,360) | 0 |
| **Total** | **(2,61,360)** | **(2,65,980)** | **(4,620)** ↓ |

**Conclusions [4 marks]:**

- **Most profitable segment (in both scenarios):** Segment **A** (Champions) at ₹1,20,000 — the only positive contributor. **(1 mark)**
- **Least profitable segment:** Segment **C** (At Risk) at ₹(1,71,360) — even a small reallocation away from C can be considered. **(1 mark)**
- **Insight on the discount:** The 10 % discount in B worsens loss by ₹4,620 because the marketing cost (₹3,75,000) overwhelms the small revenue boost. **(1 mark)**
- **Decision recommendation:** Halt B-discount experiment; concentrate spend on Segment A; consider de-prioritising C or shifting it to a low-cost digital channel. **(1 mark)**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall #1:** Students often *forget to subtract marketing cost* in segment profit, computing only gross margin. Loss of 1–2 marks.
>
> **Examiner Pitfall #2:** Students apply the new response rate of 18 % to the *original* spend of ₹2,200 instead of the discounted ₹1,980. Loss of 1 mark.
>
> **Examiner Pitfall #3:** Students do not present the three layers of the spreadsheet model *separately* and lose the 2 marks allotted for *modelling structure* rather than *computation*.

---

> **Question B [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
>
> A company manufactures two products P and Q. The selling price, variable cost, and per-unit machine hours are:
>
> | | P | Q |
> |---|---|---|
> | Price (₹) | 60 | 90 |
> | Variable cost (₹) | 40 | 60 |
> | Machine hours | 3 | 5 |
>
> Total machine hours available: **1,500**. Maximum demand: P ≤ 400, Q ≤ 250. Fixed cost: ₹5,000.
>
> **(a) [7 marks]** Formulate a Linear Programming problem to maximise profit, solve it, and show the spreadsheet model layout (inputs, calculations, outputs).
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** Compute the break-even quantity for each product individually. Then identify which product is *more sensitive* to a 10 % rise in fixed cost using the Degree of Operating Leverage (DOL).

### Model Solution — Question B

#### (a) LP Formulation and Solution [7 marks]

**Formulation [2 marks]:**

Decision variables: $x_1$ = units of P, $x_2$ = units of Q.

Objective:

$$\max\ Z = (60 - 40)x_1 + (90 - 60)x_2 = 20x_1 + 30x_2$$

Subject to:

$$3x_1 + 5x_2 \le 1{,}500 \quad \text{(machine hours)}$$

$$x_1 \le 400 \quad \text{(demand P)}$$

$$x_2 \le 250 \quad \text{(demand Q)}$$

$$x_1, x_2 \ge 0$$

**Corner-Point Solution [2 marks]:**

| Corner | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $Z = 20x_1 + 30x_2$ | Feasible? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| (0, 0) | 0 | 0 | 0 | ✓ |
| (400, 0) | 400 | 0 | 8,000 | ✓ |
| (400, 60) | 400 | 60 | 8,000 + 1,800 = 9,800 | ✓ |
| (83.33, 250) | 83.33 | 250 | 1,666.67 + 7,500 = 9,166.67 | ✓ |
| (0, 250) | 0 | 250 | 7,500 | ✓ |

(Where the (400, 60) point comes from $3(400) + 5x_2 = 1500 \Rightarrow x_2 = 60$; and the (83.33, 250) point from $3x_1 + 5(250) = 1500 \Rightarrow x_1 = 83.33$.)

**Optimal solution:** $x_1^* = 400,\ x_2^* = 60,\ Z^* = ₹9,800$ **contribution**. **(1 mark)**

**Spreadsheet Model Layout [2 marks]:**

| Layer | Cell | Content |
|---|---|---|
| Input | B2:B5 | Price, VC, MC_hr, FC |
| Input | B6:B7 | Max demand P, Max demand Q |
| Input | B8 | Machine hours available |
| Calc | D2 | `=B2-B3` Contribution P |
| Calc | E2 | `=C2-C3` Contribution Q |
| Calc | D3 | `=D2*B10` Total contribution P |
| Calc | E3 | `=E2*C10` Total contribution Q |
| Calc | F2 | `=D3+E3-B5` Total profit |
| Output | G2 | `=MAX over feasible` Solver output |

#### (b) Break-Even and DOL Analysis [7 marks]

**Break-even for P alone [1 mark]:**

$$Q_P^* = \frac{FC_P}{P - v_P} = \frac{5{,}000}{60 - 40} = \frac{5{,}000}{20} = 250\ \text{units}$$

**Break-even for Q alone [1 mark]:**

$$Q_Q^* = \frac{FC_Q}{P - v_Q} = \frac{5{,}000}{90 - 60} = \frac{5{,}000}{30} = 166.67\ \text{units} \approx 167\ \text{units}$$

**DOL for P at optimal production [1.5 marks]:**

$$DOL_P = \frac{Contribution_P}{\Pi_P} = \frac{20 \times 400}{20 \times 400 - 5{,}000} = \frac{8{,}000}{3{,}000} = 2.667$$

**DOL for Q at optimal production [1.5 marks]:**

$$DOL_Q = \frac{Contribution_Q}{\Pi_Q} = \frac{30 \times 60}{30 \times 60 - 5{,}000} = \frac{1{,}800}{-3{,}200} = -0.5625$$

> Note: $\Pi_Q$ is negative in isolation because $Q$ alone produces only ₹1,800 contribution against ₹5,000 fixed cost.

**Sensitivity interpretation [2 marks]:**

- Product P has a **positive DOL of 2.667** → a 1 % rise in sales of P produces a 2.667 % rise in its standalone profit. P is **more sensitive** in the profitable direction.
- Product Q has a **negative DOL of −0.5625** → it is currently loss-making; a 1 % rise in its sales *reduces* the absolute loss magnitude (improves the bottom line), so its sensitivity is *corrective* rather than *amplifying*.
- For a **10 % rise in fixed cost** (from ₹5,000 to ₹5,500), the new $DOL_P$ becomes $8000 / (8000 - 5500) = 8000/2500 = 3.2$, a ~20 % rise in operating leverage. This is the *risk* of high fixed costs.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall #1:** In DOL calculation, students often forget to subtract fixed cost in the denominator. Loss of 1 mark.
>
> **Examiner Pitfall #2:** Students confuse **per-product break-even** with **joint break-even**. Per-product break-even is calculated *as if* that product alone bears the entire fixed cost — a useful *what-if* but not the firm's true break-even. KTU expects both interpretations.
>
> **Examiner Pitfall #3:** Students do not *verify feasibility* at each corner point (e.g., the corner (0, 300) is infeasible because demand Q ≤ 250). Loss of 1 mark.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 4 — Rapid Revision Checklist (KTU 2024 Scheme)**

- **Segmentation** is *descriptive* analytics; **Spreadsheet Modelling** is *prescriptive* analytics. The two combine to form a complete decision loop.
- The **four classical bases** are *Demographic, Geographic, Psychographic, Behavioural* — memorise one example variable per base.
- **SEGMENT criteria** (S-E-G-M-E-N-T) are the standard attractiveness test; always apply in 14-mark case-study answers.
- Distinguish **segmentation variable** (input) from **segmentation descriptor** (output).
- **RFM** = Recency + Frequency + Monetary; quintile scoring 1–5; total range 3–15.
- A **spreadsheet model** has three layers: **Inputs (blue) → Calculations (black) → Outputs (bold/boxed)**.
- **Break-even quantity** formula: $Q^* = FC / (P - v)$, with strict condition $P > v$.
- **Margin of Safety** = actual − break-even; expressed in units *and* percentage.
- **DOL** = Contribution / Profit. Higher DOL ⇒ more operating risk.
- **NPV** uses discount rate $r$; **IRR** is the rate that makes NPV = 0.
- **Tornado analysis** = deterministic one-at-a-time sensitivity; **Monte-Carlo** = probabilistic joint sensitivity. Do *not* mix the two.
- For **LP-based spreadsheet models**, always verify corner-point feasibility, not just objective value.
- **Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)** is the unifying metric across segmentation and modelling.
- Use **named ranges** in Excel to make formulas auditable; examiners expect this practice.
- In a 14-mark answer, **always show the spreadsheet structure (layers)** before plugging in numbers — 2 marks are reserved for *modelling discipline*.
- **STP** (Segment–Target–Position) is the strategic wrapper; **RFM / SEGMENT** are the analytical tools; **Spreadsheet Models** are the decision engine.

> [!TIP]
> **One-line summary a KTU topper would write:**
> *"Segmentation answers WHO to serve; spreadsheet models answer HOW MUCH to invest and WHAT to expect. Together, they convert data into defensible managerial decisions."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analysis Using Spread Sheets - What-if Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT522 - BUSINESS ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Analysis Using Spread Sheets - What-if Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 4 — ANALYSIS USING SPREAD SHEETS: WHAT-IF ANALYSIS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **What-If Analysis** is a systematic decision-support process in spreadsheet computing wherein one or more **input variables (assumptions / parameters)** are deliberately altered to observe and quantify the resulting change in one or more **output variables (dependent values, such as profit, NPV, breakeven point, or revenue)**. It allows the analyst to test multiple business scenarios without rewriting formulas, thereby enabling **sensitivity analysis**, **forecasting**, and **risk evaluation** directly within a worksheet environment such as **Microsoft Excel** or **Google Sheets**.

In formal KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, What-If Analysis is treated as a **sub-domain of descriptive and predictive analytics** that operates on the dependency graph of a spreadsheet model. Every cell in Excel is either an **input cell (assumption)**, a **formula cell (calculation)**, or an **output cell (result)**. What-if analysis perturbs the input cells and tracks the ripple effect on the output cells.

The three principal techniques taught under the KTU 2024 syllabus for the course **PEADT522 — Business Analytics** are:

1. **Scenario Manager** — for storing and comparing multiple discrete sets of input values.
2. **Goal Seek** — for back-solving a single input value when a target output is known.
3. **Data Tables** — for sensitivity analysis across one or two input variables simultaneously.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Restaurant Owner and the Recipe"**
>
> Imagine you run a small café. Your monthly **Profit** depends on three assumptions: the **Selling Price** of a cup of coffee, the **Cost of Raw Materials** (milk, beans, sugar), and the **Daily Footfall** (number of customers). You obviously cannot build 50 real cafés to test 50 different price points. So instead, you sit at a desk, open Excel, and ask:
> - *"What if I raise the price from ₹150 to ₹180?"* → Profit changes from X to Y.
> - *"What if footfall drops by 20% during monsoon?"* → Profit changes from X to Z.
> - *"What monthly footfall do I need to hit a target profit of ₹1,00,000?"* → Answer: 750 customers/day.
>
> This act of *changing assumptions to observe outcomes* is exactly **What-If Analysis**. The spreadsheet is your **digital laboratory**, and the assumptions are your **dials and knobs**.

### 1.3 Why It Matters in Business Analytics

| Term | Meaning in Spreadsheet Context |
|---|---|
| **Input Cell** | A cell that holds an assumption or raw data (e.g., a price, rate, or volume). |
| **Formula Cell** | A cell that performs arithmetic or logical operations on other cells. |
| **Output Cell** | A cell whose final value the analyst cares about (e.g., Net Profit, ROI). |
| **Sensitivity** | The degree to which the output changes per unit change in input. |
| **Break-Even Point** | The input value at which output = 0 (revenue equals cost). |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Takeaway:** What-if analysis is the spreadsheet-based equivalent of a *flight simulator* for business decisions — it lets you crash-test strategies without real-world consequences.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of What-If Analysis

### 🔹 Pillar 1: Goal Seek (Reverse / Backward Calculation)

**Operational Logic:**

Goal Seek works in the **reverse direction** of normal spreadsheet flow. Instead of *Input → Formula → Output*, it executes *Target Output → Reverse-Solve Input*.

**Step-by-step internal logic:**

1. The user identifies a **single output cell** that must reach a **specific target value**.
2. The user identifies a **single input cell** that Excel is allowed to modify.
3. Excel uses an **iterative numerical technique** (a variant of the *bisection method* and *Newton-Raphson method*) to find the input value that drives the output to the target.
4. The input cell is updated in-place with the discovered value.

**Mathematical Underpinning:**

For a one-variable target equation of the form:
$$f(x) = T$$
Goal Seek finds the value of $x$ such that the function $f(x)$ converges to the target $T$. It uses **iterative substitution**:
$$x_{n+1} = x_n - \frac{f(x_n) - T}{f'(x_n)}$$
This is essentially a bounded root-finding routine that Excel performs transparently to the user.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constraint:** Goal Seek works on **only one input cell** and **only one output cell** at a time. It cannot handle multi-variable constraints.

---

### 🔹 Pillar 2: Scenario Manager (Comparative Scenario Storage)

**Operational Logic:**

A **Scenario** is a named snapshot of a group of input cells under a specific set of values. The Scenario Manager allows the user to:

1. Define **multiple named scenarios** (e.g., "Best Case", "Worst Case", "Most Likely").
2. **Switch between** scenarios instantly, with Excel automatically replacing the input cell values.
3. **Generate a Scenario Summary Report** that shows the output cell value for each scenario side-by-side.

**Why it differs from Goal Seek:**

| Feature | Goal Seek | Scenario Manager |
|---|---|---|
| Direction | Reverse (output → input) | Forward (input → output) |
| Variables | 1 input | Up to 32 inputs |
| Use Case | "Find the value of X" | "Compare multiple what-if situations" |

---

### 🔹 Pillar 3: Data Tables (Sensitivity Grid)

**Operational Logic:**

A **Data Table** is a rectangular array of cells that displays how one or two input variables affect an output. There are two flavors:

- **One-Variable Data Table:** A single column (or row) of input values is replaced one at a time, and the corresponding output is recorded. Useful for **sensitivity to a single assumption**.
- **Two-Variable Data Table:** A two-dimensional grid where one input varies down the column and another varies across the row, with the output shown at the intersection. Useful for **sensitivity to two assumptions simultaneously**.

**Mathematical View:**

If the output is $O = f(x, y)$, then:
- A **one-variable table** with input $x$ taking values $\{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n\}$ produces outputs $\{O_1, O_2, \dots, O_n\}$ where $O_i = f(x_i, y^*)$ and $y^*$ is held constant.
- A **two-variable table** produces a full matrix $O_{i,j} = f(x_i, y_j)$ for $i = 1 \dots m$ and $j = 1 \dots n$.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every formula and concept the KTU 2024 examiner expects you to know for this topic. **Memorize the structure, not just the formula.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Variables | Where Used |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Profit | $\pi = R - C$ | $\pi$ = profit, $R$ = revenue, $C$ = cost | All what-if models |
| 2 | Revenue | $R = P \times Q$ | $P$ = price, $Q$ = quantity | Sales what-if |
| 3 | Total Cost | $C = F + (V \times Q)$ | $F$ = fixed cost, $V$ = variable cost/unit | Breakeven models |
| 4 | Break-Even Quantity | $Q_{BE} = \dfrac{F}{P - V}$ | $P - V$ = contribution margin | Goal Seek target |
| 5 | ROI | $ROI = \dfrac{\text{Gain} - \text{Cost}}{\text{Cost}} \times 100$ | Percentage return | Investment analysis |
| 6 | Compound Growth | $A = P(1 + r)^n$ | $P$ = principal, $r$ = rate, $n$ = periods | Data table sensitivity |
| 7 | NPV | $NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} - I_0$ | $CF_t$ = cash flow, $I_0$ = initial investment | Scenario comparison |
| 8 | Linear Depreciation | $D_t = \dfrac{C - S}{n}$ | $C$ = cost, $S$ = salvage, $n$ = life | Asset what-if |
| 9 | Goal Seek Equation | $f(x) = T$ | $f$ = output formula, $x$ = input, $T$ = target | Reverse solving |
| 10 | Sensitivity Slope | $\Delta O / \Delta I$ | Change in output per unit change in input | Risk analysis |

> [!WARNING]
> In any KTU numerical problem, **always state the cell references** (e.g., `B2`, `C5`) clearly when explaining the steps. Examiners award marks for *demonstrating spreadsheet methodology*, not just the final number.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Business

| Application Domain | What-If Use Case |
|---|---|
| **Finance** | Stress-testing loan portfolios under different interest rate scenarios. |
| **Operations Management** | Simulating inventory reorder points under variable demand. |
| **Marketing** | Projecting revenue under different pricing strategies and ad-spend levels. |
| **HR Analytics** | Estimating payroll impact of headcount changes. |
| **Manufacturing** | Computing production cost under different raw-material price shocks. |
| **Project Management** | Evaluating NPV under optimistic, pessimistic, and baseline timelines. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Examples

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following derivations are exhaustive. Every algebraic step is shown. **No shortcut placeholders.** Read top-to-bottom in a single pass to build complete intuition.

---

## 📐 EXAMPLE 1 — Goal Seek to Find Break-Even Sales Volume

### Problem Statement

A small-scale manufacturing firm sells a product at **₹500 per unit**. The **fixed cost per month** is **₹80,000** and the **variable cost per unit** is **₹300**. Using Goal Seek methodology, determine the **monthly sales volume** at which **profit = ₹0** (the break-even point). Then, determine the volume required to achieve a **target profit of ₹1,20,000**.

### Step 1 — Construct the Spreadsheet Model

| Cell | Label | Value / Formula |
|---|---|---|
| B1 | Selling Price (₹) | 500 |
| B2 | Variable Cost/unit (₹) | 300 |
| B3 | Fixed Cost (₹) | 80000 |
| B4 | Units Sold | 0 *(this is the input cell Goal Seek will modify)* |
| B5 | Total Revenue | `= B1 * B4` |
| B6 | Total Variable Cost | `= B2 * B4` |
| B7 | Total Cost | `= B3 + B6` |
| B8 | Profit | `= B5 - B7` |

### Step 2 — Derive the Algebraic Equation

Total revenue is:
$$R = P \times Q = 500 \cdot Q$$

Total cost is:
$$C = F + (V \times Q) = 80{,}000 + 300 \cdot Q$$

Profit is:
$$\pi = R - C = 500Q - (80{,}000 + 300Q) = 200Q - 80{,}000$$

### Step 3 — Apply Goal Seek for Break-Even ($\pi = 0$)

We require:
$$200Q - 80{,}000 = 0$$
$$200Q = 80{,}000$$
$$Q = \frac{80{,}000}{200} = 400 \text{ units}$$

> ✅ **Goal Seek Result:** Set cell **B4** (Units Sold) such that cell **B8** (Profit) equals **0** → Excel returns **B4 = 400**.

### Step 4 — Apply Goal Seek for Target Profit ($\pi = 1{,}20{,}000$)

We require:
$$200Q - 80{,}000 = 1{,}20{,}000$$
$$200Q = 2{,}00{,}000$$
$$Q = \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{200} = 1000 \text{ units}$$

> ✅ **Goal Seek Result:** Set cell **B4** such that cell **B8** equals **1,20,000** → Excel returns **B4 = 1000**.

### Step 5 — Interpretation

A sales volume of **400 units/month** is the **break-even point**; below this, the firm makes a loss, and above this, it makes a profit. To earn a monthly profit of ₹1,20,000, the firm must sell **1000 units/month**.

---

## 📐 EXAMPLE 2 — Two-Variable Data Table for Profit Sensitivity

### Problem Statement

Using the same firm (Price = ₹500, Variable Cost = ₹300, Fixed Cost = ₹80,000), construct a **two-variable data table** that displays the **profit** for combinations of:
- **Selling Price** ranging from ₹450 to ₹600 in steps of ₹50.
- **Units Sold** ranging from 300 to 700 in steps of 100.

### Step 1 — Identify the Profit Formula

$$\pi = (P - V) \cdot Q - F = (P - 300) \cdot Q - 80{,}000$$

### Step 2 — Construct the Data Table Grid

| Price ↓ \ Units → | 300 | 400 | 500 | 600 | 700 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **₹450** | $\pi_{1,1}$ | $\pi_{1,2}$ | $\pi_{1,3}$ | $\pi_{1,4}$ | $\pi_{1,5}$ |
| **₹500** | $\pi_{2,1}$ | $\pi_{2,2}$ | $\pi_{2,3}$ | $\pi_{2,4}$ | $\pi_{2,5}$ |
| **₹550** | $\pi_{3,1}$ | $\pi_{3,2}$ | $\pi_{3,3}$ | $\pi_{3,4}$ | $\pi_{3,5}$ |
| **₹600** | $\pi_{4,1}$ | $\pi_{4,2}$ | $\pi_{4,3}$ | $\pi_{4,4}$ | $\pi_{4,5}$ |

The top-left corner of the grid contains a **reference to the profit formula** (linked to cell B8 in the model).

### Step 3 — Calculate Each Cell Explicitly

**For Price = ₹450, Units = 300:**
$$\pi = (450 - 300) \cdot 300 - 80{,}000 = 150 \cdot 300 - 80{,}000 = 45{,}000 - 80{,}000 = -35{,}000$$

**For Price = ₹450, Units = 400:**
$$\pi = 150 \cdot 400 - 80{,}000 = 60{,}000 - 80{,}000 = -20{,}000$$

**For Price = ₹450, Units = 500:**
$$\pi = 150 \cdot 500 - 80{,}000 = 75{,}000 - 80{,}000 = -5{,}000$$

**For Price = ₹450, Units = 600:**
$$\pi = 150 \cdot 600 - 80{,}000 = 90{,}000 - 80{,}000 = 10{,}000$$

**For Price = ₹450, Units = 700:**
$$\pi = 150 \cdot 700 - 80{,}000 = 1{,}05{,}000 - 80{,}000 = 25{,}000$$

**For Price = ₹500, Units = 300:**
$$\pi = (500 - 300) \cdot 300 - 80{,}000 = 200 \cdot 300 - 80{,}000 = 60{,}000 - 80{,}000 = -20{,}000$$

**For Price = ₹500, Units = 400:**
$$\pi = 200 \cdot 400 - 80{,}000 = 80{,}000 - 80{,}000 = 0$$

**For Price = ₹500, Units = 500:**
$$\pi = 200 \cdot 500 - 80{,}000 = 1{,}00{,}000 - 80{,}000 = 20{,}000$$

**For Price = ₹500, Units = 600:**
$$\pi = 200 \cdot 600 - 80{,}000 = 1{,}20{,}000 - 80{,}000 = 40{,}000$$

**For Price = ₹500, Units = 700:**
$$\pi = 200 \cdot 700 - 80{,}000 = 1{,}40{,}000 - 80{,}000 = 60{,}000$$

**For Price = ₹550, Units = 300:**
$$\pi = 250 \cdot 300 - 80{,}000 = 75{,}000 - 80{,}000 = -5{,}000$$

**For Price = ₹550, Units = 400:**
$$\pi = 250 \cdot 400 - 80{,}000 = 1{,}00{,}000 - 80{,}000 = 20{,}000$$

**For Price = ₹550, Units = 500:**
$$\pi = 250 \cdot 500 - 80{,}000 = 1{,}25{,}000 - 80{,}000 = 45{,}000$$

**For Price = ₹550, Units = 600:**
$$\pi = 250 \cdot 600 - 80{,}000 = 1{,}50{,}000 - 80{,}000 = 70{,}000$$

**For Price = ₹550, Units = 700:**
$$\pi = 250 \cdot 700 - 80{,}000 = 1{,}75{,}000 - 80{,}000 = 95{,}000$$

**For Price = ₹600, Units = 300:**
$$\pi = 300 \cdot 300 - 80{,}000 = 90{,}000 - 80{,}000 = 10{,}000$$

**For Price = ₹600, Units = 400:**
$$\pi = 300 \cdot 400 - 80{,}000 = 1{,}20{,}000 - 80{,}000 = 40{,}000$$

**For Price = ₹600, Units = 500:**
$$\pi = 300 \cdot 500 - 80{,}000 = 1{,}50{,}000 - 80{,}000 = 70{,}000$$

**For Price = ₹600, Units = 600:**
$$\pi = 300 \cdot 600 - 80{,}000 = 1{,}80{,}000 - 80{,}000 = 1{,}00{,}000$$

**For Price = ₹600, Units = 700:**
$$\pi = 300 \cdot 700 - 80{,}000 = 2{,}10{,}000 - 80{,}000 = 1{,}30{,}000$$

### Step 4 — Final Completed Data Table

| Price ↓ \ Units → | 300 | 400 | 500 | 600 | 700 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **₹450** | $-35{,}000$ | $-20{,}000$ | $-5{,}000$ | $10{,}000$ | $25{,}000$ |
| **₹500** | $-20{,}000$ | $0$ | $20{,}000$ | $40{,}000$ | $60{,}000$ |
| **₹550** | $-5{,}000$ | $20{,}000$ | $45{,}000$ | $70{,}000$ | $95{,}000$ |
| **₹600** | $10{,}000$ | $40{,}000$ | $70{,}000$ | $1{,}00{,}000$ | $1{,}30{,}000$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the Table:** The cell at (₹500, 400 units) shows ₹0, which confirms our Goal Seek result. The table also reveals that *raising price has a stronger effect on profit than increasing volume* in this model — a critical managerial insight.

---

## 📐 EXAMPLE 3 — Scenario Manager Comparison (Loan Amortization)

### Problem Statement

A bank offers three loan scenarios to a borrower seeking a **₹10,00,000** loan:

| Scenario | Interest Rate | Tenure (years) |
|---|---|---|
| Conservative | 8.0% | 7 |
| Moderate | 9.5% | 5 |
| Aggressive | 11.0% | 3 |

Compute the **monthly EMI** and **total interest paid** for each scenario and compare using Scenario Manager methodology.

### Step 1 — EMI Formula

The standard EMI formula used in Indian banking is:
$$EMI = \frac{P \cdot r \cdot (1 + r)^n}{(1 + r)^n - 1}$$
where $P$ = principal, $r$ = monthly interest rate, $n$ = number of monthly installments.

### Step 2 — Scenario 1: Conservative (8%, 7 years)

Monthly rate: $r = 0.08 / 12 = 0.00667$
Number of months: $n = 7 \times 12 = 84$

$(1 + r)^n = (1.00667)^{84}$

Computing step by step:
$$(1.00667)^2 \approx 1.01338$$
$$(1.00667)^4 \approx 1.02693$$
$$(1.00667)^8 \approx 1.05470$$
$$(1.00667)^{16} \approx 1.11240$$
$$(1.00667)^{32} \approx 1.23743$$
$$(1.00667)^{64} \approx 1.53127$$
$$(1.00667)^{84} = (1.00667)^{64} \cdot (1.00667)^{16} \cdot (1.00667)^4$$
$$\approx 1.53127 \times 1.11240 \times 1.02693 \approx 1.74950$$

Substituting:
$$EMI = \frac{10{,}00{,}000 \times 0.00667 \times 1.74950}{1.74950 - 1}$$
$$= \frac{10{,}00{,}000 \times 0.01166}{0.74950}$$
$$= \frac{11{,}660}{0.74950} \approx 15{,}557$$

Total amount paid: $15{,}557 \times 84 = 13{,}06{,}788$
Total interest: $13{,}06{,}788 - 10{,}00{,}000 = 3{,}06{,}788$

### Step 3 — Scenario 2: Moderate (9.5%, 5 years)

Monthly rate: $r = 0.095 / 12 = 0.00792$
Number of months: $n = 5 \times 12 = 60$

$$(1.00792)^{60}$$
Using $\ln(1.00792) \approx 0.00789$, so $60 \times 0.00789 = 0.4734$, and $e^{0.4734} \approx 1.6054$.

$$EMI = \frac{10{,}00{,}000 \times 0.00792 \times 1.6054}{1.6054 - 1}$$
$$= \frac{10{,}00{,}000 \times 0.01271}{0.6054}$$
$$= \frac{12{,}710}{0.6054} \approx 21{,}000$$

Total amount paid: $21{,}000 \times 60 = 12{,}60{,}000$
Total interest: $12{,}60{,}000 - 10{,}00{,}000 = 2{,}60{,}000$

### Step 4 — Scenario 3: Aggressive (11%, 3 years)

Monthly rate: $r = 0.11 / 12 = 0.00917$
Number of months: $n = 3 \times 12 = 36$

$$(1.00917)^{36}$$
Using $\ln(1.00917) \approx 0.00913$, so $36 \times 0.00913 = 0.3287$, and $e^{0.3287} \approx 1.3891$.

$$EMI = \frac{10{,}00{,}000 \times 0.00917 \times 1.3891}{1.3891 - 1}$$
$$= \frac{10{,}00{,}000 \times 0.01274}{0.3891}$$
$$= \frac{12{,}740}{0.3891} \approx 32{,}743$$

Total amount paid: $32{,}743 \times 36 = 11{,}78{,}748$
Total interest: $11{,}78{,}748 - 10{,}00{,}000 = 1{,}78{,}748$

### Step 5 — Scenario Summary Report

| Scenario | Rate | Tenure | Monthly EMI | Total Interest |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Conservative | 8.0% | 7 yrs | ₹15,557 | ₹3,06,788 |
| Moderate | 9.5% | 5 yrs | ₹21,000 | ₹2,60,000 |
| Aggressive | 11.0% | 3 yrs | ₹32,743 | ₹1,78,748 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** A shorter tenure reduces **total interest** but increases **monthly cash outflow**. The Scenario Manager allows the borrower to instantly visualize this trade-off.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Conceptual Architecture of What-If Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Business Problem Identified] --> B[Identify Input Variables Assumptions]
    B --> C[Build Spreadsheet Model with Formulas]
    C --> D{Choose What-If Technique}
    D --> E1[Goal Seek]
    D --> E2[Scenario Manager]
    D --> E3[Data Table]
    E1 --> F1[Specify Target Output and Single Input Cell]
    F1 --> G1[Excel Iteratively Solves for Input Value]
    G1 --> H1[Display Solved Input]
    E2 --> F2[Define Multiple Named Scenarios Best Worst Likely]
    F2 --> G2[Switch Between Scenarios]
    G2 --> H2[Generate Scenario Summary Report]
    E3 --> F3[Define Range of Input Values for One or Two Variables]
    F3 --> G3[Excel Recomputes Output for Each Combination]
    G3 --> H3[Display Sensitivity Grid]
    H1 --> I[Decision Maker Interprets Results]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    I --> J[Choose Optimal Strategy]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Spreadsheet Cell Dependency Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN1[Input Cell Selling Price B1] --> F1[Formula Cell Revenue B5 equals B1 times B4]
    IN2[Input Cell Variable Cost B2] --> F2[Formula Cell Variable Cost Total B6 equals B2 times B4]
    IN3[Input Cell Fixed Cost B3] --> F3[Formula Cell Total Cost B7 equals B3 plus B6]
    IN4[Input Cell Units Sold B4] --> F1
    IN4 --> F2
    F1 --> OUT1[Output Cell Profit B8 equals B5 minus B7]
    F2 --> F3
    F3 --> OUT1
    OUT1 --> DEC[Strategic Decision: Adjust Pricing or Volume]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Comparison of Three Techniques

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT[What-If Analysis in Spreadsheets] --> GS[Goal Seek]
    ROOT --> SM[Scenario Manager]
    ROOT --> DT[Data Tables]
    GS --> GS1[Direction: Reverse]
    GS --> GS2[Inputs: 1 Variable]
    GS --> GS3[Use: Find X to hit Target Y]
    SM --> SM1[Direction: Forward]
    SM --> SM2[Inputs: Up to 32 Variables]
    SM --> SM3[Use: Compare Discrete Scenarios]
    DT --> DT1[Direction: Forward Grid]
    DT --> DT2[Inputs: 1 or 2 Variables]
    DT --> DT3[Use: Sensitivity Mapping]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define What-If Analysis. List its three main types as per the KTU 2024 syllabus.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **What-If Analysis** is a spreadsheet-based analytical technique that involves changing one or more input values (assumptions) in a worksheet to observe how those changes affect the calculated outcomes. It is widely used in business analytics for **sensitivity analysis, scenario planning, and decision-making** without altering the underlying formulas.
>
> The **three main types** of What-If Analysis taught in the KTU 2024 scheme are:
>
> 1. **Scenario Manager** — for storing and comparing multiple discrete sets of input values.
> 2. **Goal Seek** — for back-solving a single input value to achieve a desired output target.
> 3. **Data Tables** — for performing sensitivity analysis using one-variable or two-variable input ranges.

---

### **Q2. Differentiate between Goal Seek and Scenario Manager.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Goal Seek | Scenario Manager |
|---|---|---|
| **Direction of Computation** | Reverse (output → input) | Forward (input → output) |
| **Number of Input Variables** | Only **one** input cell at a time | Up to **32** input cells simultaneously |
| **Purpose** | To find a specific input value that produces a target output | To compare results across multiple pre-defined scenarios |
| **Use Case Example** | Finding units to sell to hit a profit of ₹1,00,000 | Comparing profit under Best, Worst, and Most-Likely cases |

> **Conclusion:** Goal Seek is a *point-solver*; Scenario Manager is a *comparative dashboard*.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Rule (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

A company manufactures a product with the following data:

| Item | Value |
|---|---|
| Selling Price per unit | ₹400 |
| Variable Cost per unit | ₹250 |
| Fixed Cost per month | ₹1,50,000 |
| Current Monthly Sales | 800 units |

#### **(a) Using Goal Seek, determine the number of units that must be sold monthly to achieve a target profit of ₹60,000. Show the formula and cell layout.** (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Construct the spreadsheet model:**

| Cell | Label | Value / Formula |
|---|---|---|
| B1 | Selling Price | 400 |
| B2 | Variable Cost | 250 |
| B3 | Fixed Cost | 1,50,000 |
| B4 | Units Sold | 800 *(Goal Seek input cell)* |
| B5 | Revenue | `= B1 * B4` |
| B6 | Variable Cost Total | `= B2 * B4` |
| B7 | Total Cost | `= B3 + B6` |
| B8 | Profit | `= B5 - B7` |

**Step 2 — Derive the profit equation:**

$$\pi = (P - V) \cdot Q - F = (400 - 250) \cdot Q - 1{,}50{,}000 = 150Q - 1{,}50{,}000$$

**Step 3 — Set the Goal Seek target:**

We require:
$$150Q - 1{,}50{,}000 = 60{,}000$$
$$150Q = 2{,}10{,}000$$
$$Q = \frac{2{,}10{,}000}{150} = 1400 \text{ units}$$

**Step 4 — Goal Seek operation:**

In Excel: `Data` tab → `What-If Analysis` → `Goal Seek` → Set cell **B8** to value **60000** by changing cell **B4** → Click OK.

> ✅ **Result:** Goal Seek sets **B4 = 1400**.

**Step 5 — Verification:**

$$\pi = 150 \times 1400 - 1{,}50{,}000 = 2{,}10{,}000 - 1{,}50{,}000 = 60{,}000 \checkmark$$

---

#### **(b) Construct a One-Variable Data Table showing the profit for Selling Prices ranging from ₹350 to ₹500 in steps of ₹25, holding Units Sold at 1400.** (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Profit formula with P as variable (Q = 1400, V = 250, F = 1,50,000):**

$$\pi = (P - 250) \cdot 1400 - 1{,}50{,}000 = 1400P - 350{,}000 - 1{,}50{,}000 = 1400P - 5{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 2 — Calculate profit for each price point:**

| Selling Price (₹) | Calculation | Profit (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| 350 | $1400 \times 350 - 5{,}00{,}000$ | $-10{,}000$ |
| 375 | $1400 \times 375 - 5{,}00{,}000$ | $25{,}000$ |
| 400 | $1400 \times 400 - 5{,}00{,}000$ | $60{,}000$ |
| 425 | $1400 \times 425 - 5{,}00{,}000$ | $95{,}000$ |
| 450 | $1400 \times 450 - 5{,}00{,}000$ | $1{,}30{,}000$ |
| 475 | $1400 \times 475 - 5{,}00{,}000$ | $1{,}65{,}000$ |
| 500 | $1400 \times 500 - 5{,}00{,}000$ | $2{,}00{,}000$ |

**Step 3 — One-Variable Data Table layout in Excel:**

| | A | B |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Price (₹) | Profit (₹) |
| 2 | 350 | -10,000 |
| 3 | 375 | 25,000 |
| 4 | 400 | 60,000 |
| 5 | 425 | 95,000 |
| 6 | 450 | 1,30,000 |
| 7 | 475 | 1,65,000 |
| 8 | 500 | 2,00,000 |

**Step 4 — Excel procedure:**

Place the profit formula reference at the top of the column, list the prices in column A, select the table range, then go to `Data` → `What-If Analysis` → `Data Table` → set the **Column Input Cell** to the price reference.

> **Interpretation:** Every ₹25 increase in price yields ₹35,000 in additional profit (slope = $1400 \times 25 = 35{,}000$).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

A retail business wants to evaluate the impact of advertising spend and store size on monthly revenue. The base model is:

$$R = 5000 + 12 \cdot A + 0.8 \cdot S$$

where $A$ = monthly advertising spend (₹), $S$ = store size (sq ft), and $R$ = monthly revenue (₹).

#### **(a) Using a Two-Variable Data Table, compute the monthly revenue for combinations of: Advertising Spend = {₹10,000, ₹20,000, ₹30,000} and Store Size = {1000, 2000, 3000} sq ft. Present the grid and interpret.** (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Construct the 2D Data Table:**

| Ad Spend ↓ \ Store Size → | 1000 sq ft | 2000 sq ft | 3000 sq ft |
|---|---|---|---|
| **₹10,000** | $R_{1,1}$ | $R_{1,2}$ | $R_{1,3}$ |
| **₹20,000** | $R_{2,1}$ | $R_{2,2}$ | $R_{2,3}$ |
| **₹30,000** | $R_{3,1}$ | $R_{3,2}$ | $R_{3,3}$ |

**Step 2 — Calculate each cell:**

For **Ad = ₹10,000, Size = 1000**:
$$R = 5000 + 12 \times 10{,}000 + 0.8 \times 1000 = 5000 + 1{,}20{,}000 + 800 = 1{,}25{,}800$$

For **Ad = ₹10,000, Size = 2000**:
$$R = 5000 + 1{,}20{,}000 + 0.8 \times 2000 = 5000 + 1{,}20{,}000 + 1600 = 1{,}26{,}600$$

For **Ad = ₹10,000, Size = 3000**:
$$R = 5000 + 1{,}20{,}000 + 0.8 \times 3000 = 5000 + 1{,}20{,}000 + 2400 = 1{,}27{,}400$$

For **Ad = ₹20,000, Size = 1000**:
$$R = 5000 + 12 \times 20{,}000 + 800 = 5000 + 2{,}40{,}000 + 800 = 2{,}45{,}800$$

For **Ad = ₹20,000, Size = 2000**:
$$R = 5000 + 2{,}40{,}000 + 1600 = 2{,}46{,}600$$

For **Ad = ₹20,000, Size = 3000**:
$$R = 5000 + 2{,}40{,}000 + 2400 = 2{,}47{,}400$$

For **Ad = ₹30,000, Size = 1000**:
$$R = 5000 + 12 \times 30{,}000 + 800 = 5000 + 3{,}60{,}000 + 800 = 3{,}65{,}800$$

For **Ad = ₹30,000, Size = 2000**:
$$R = 5000 + 3{,}60{,}000 + 1600 = 3{,}66{,}600$$

For **Ad = ₹30,000, Size = 3000**:
$$R = 5000 + 3{,}60{,}000 + 2400 = 3{,}67{,}400$$

**Step 3 — Completed Data Table:**

| Ad Spend ↓ \ Store Size → | 1000 | 2000 | 3000 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **₹10,000** | 1,25,800 | 1,26,600 | 1,27,400 |
| **₹20,000** | 2,45,800 | 2,46,600 | 2,47,400 |
| **₹30,000** | 3,65,800 | 3,66,600 | 3,67,400 |

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**

- **Advertising Spend has a much larger impact on revenue** than store size. Increasing ad spend by ₹10,000 raises revenue by **₹1,20,000**, while increasing store size by 1000 sq ft raises revenue by only **₹800**.
- The **marginal revenue per rupee of ad spend** is ₹12, while the **marginal revenue per sq ft** is only ₹0.80.
- **Managerial Insight:** The business should prioritize **advertising investment** over physical expansion for short-term revenue growth.

---

#### **(b) Explain how the Scenario Manager would be used to compare a "Pessimistic", "Baseline", and "Optimistic" scenario for this revenue model. List the parameter values and expected outcomes.** (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define the three scenarios:**

| Parameter | Pessimistic | Baseline | Optimistic |
|---|---|---|---|
| Intercept (₹) | 3000 | 5000 | 7000 |
| Ad Coefficient | 10 | 12 | 14 |
| Size Coefficient | 0.6 | 0.8 | 1.0 |

**Step 2 — Expected revenue for each scenario at base values (A = ₹20,000, S = 2000):**

**Pessimistic:**
$$R = 3000 + 10 \times 20{,}000 + 0.6 \times 2000 = 3000 + 2{,}00{,}000 + 1200 = 2{,}04{,}200$$

**Baseline:**
$$R = 5000 + 12 \times 20{,}000 + 0.8 \times 2000 = 5000 + 2{,}40{,}000 + 1600 = 2{,}46{,}600$$

**Optimistic:**
$$R = 7000 + 14 \times 20{,}000 + 1.0 \times 2000 = 7000 + 2{,}80{,}000 + 2000 = 2{,}89{,}000$$

**Step 3 — Excel procedure for Scenario Manager:**

1. Set up the formula `R = intercept + ad_coeff * A + size_coeff * S` in a cell.
2. Go to `Data` → `What-If Analysis` → `Scenario Manager` → `Add`.
3. Name each scenario and assign the input cell values.
4. Click `Summary` to generate a comparison report.

**Step 4 — Scenario Summary Report:**

| Scenario | Intercept | Ad Coefficient | Size Coefficient | Revenue (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Pessimistic | 3000 | 10 | 0.6 | 2,04,200 |
| Baseline | 5000 | 12 | 0.8 | 2,46,600 |
| Optimistic | 7000 | 14 | 1.0 | 2,89,000 |

> **Interpretation:** The business faces a revenue range of ₹2,04,200 to ₹2,89,000, a **spread of ₹84,800** around the baseline — useful for **risk quantification**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> ⚠️ **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully**
>
> 1. **Confusing Goal Seek with Solver:** Goal Seek handles **one input → one output** only. If you write about "multiple inputs" in a Goal Seek answer, you will lose 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting cell references:** KTU examiners expect you to label cells (B1, B2, etc.) when describing the spreadsheet model. Omitting these costs at least 1 mark per sub-part.
> 3. **Mixing up one-variable and two-variable data tables:** In a one-variable table, the input varies down a **column** (or across a **row**) with the output in the adjacent column/row. In a two-variable table, one input varies down a column and another across a row. Drawing them identically is a common error.
> 4. **Not showing the formula derivation:** Numerical answers alone are insufficient. The KTU 2024 scheme rewards the *process* (formula → substitution → answer). Always show intermediate steps.
> 5. **Ignoring the Scenario Summary Report:** When asked about Scenario Manager, many students only describe how to *create* scenarios but forget to describe the **summary report** output — examiners allocate 2–3 marks for this.

---

## 🔁 TOPIC RECAP — Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **What-If Analysis** = changing inputs to observe outputs in a spreadsheet.
- ✅ The **three main types** are: **Goal Seek**, **Scenario Manager**, and **Data Tables**.
- ✅ **Goal Seek** works **backwards**: it finds the input that produces a desired output. It is **one-input, one-output only**.
- ✅ **Scenario Manager** stores **multiple named input sets** and produces a **summary comparison report**. It can handle up to **32 changing cells**.
- ✅ **One-Variable Data Table** examines how one input affects one output across a range of values.
- ✅ **Two-Variable Data Table** examines how two inputs jointly affect one output in a grid.
- ✅ Always include the **algebraic formula** before plugging in numerical values — this shows the analytical thinking KTU examiners reward.
- ✅ In Excel, the What-If Analysis tools are located under: **`Data` tab → `What-If Analysis` dropdown**.
- ✅ Sensitivity slope = $\Delta \text{Output} / \Delta \text{Input}$ — a key measure of risk exposure.
- ✅ The **break-even formula** $Q_{BE} = F / (P - V)$ is the most frequently tested application of Goal Seek.
- ✅ Scenario Manager is best for **discrete** comparisons (Best/Worst/Likely); Data Tables are best for **continuous** sensitivity sweeps.
- ✅ Always **interpret the managerial insight** after presenting numerical results — KTU awards marks for business reasoning, not just arithmetic.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Break Even Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT522 - BUSINESS ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Break Even Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Break Even Analysis (BEA)** is a fundamental managerial accounting and financial modeling technique used within the domain of **Cost-Volume-Profit (CVP) Analysis**. It identifies the point at which **Total Revenue (TR)** exactly equals **Total Cost (TC)**, resulting in **zero profit** and **zero loss**. This specific point is known as the **Break Even Point (BEP)**.

Mathematically, the condition for Break Even is:

$$\text{Profit} = \text{Total Revenue} - \text{Total Cost} = 0$$

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, BEP is the inflection point where the contribution margin generated from sales completely absorbs the total fixed costs of the organization, leaving neither surplus (profit) nor deficit (loss).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Break Even Analysis is a cornerstone of short-term managerial decision-making. It is frequently examined as a Part B (14-mark) numerical problem and as a Part A (3-mark) conceptual question in Business Analytics ESE papers.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Cup of Chai" Analogy

Imagine you are a small tea-stall owner in Kerala. Every day, you must pay a fixed rent of **₹500** for the stall space, regardless of whether you sell one cup of tea or one hundred. This is your **Fixed Cost (FC)**.

Each cup of tea you brew costs you **₹10** in milk, sugar, tea powder, and gas — this is your **Variable Cost (VC)** per unit. You sell each cup for **₹40** — this is your **Selling Price (SP)** per unit.

Now, on a rainy day when sales are terrible, you keep asking yourself: *"How many cups do I need to sell JUST to cover my rent?"* The answer to that question is your **Break Even Point**.

The cups you sell beyond the BEP are **pure profit**, because your fixed cost is already "paid off." Until you cross BEP, every cup sold is simply recovering the rent.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The BEP is essentially the "survival line" of a business. It tells management the minimum performance threshold required to avoid financial losses.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology

The following metrics are **standardized across all KTU Board Examinations**:

- **Total Cost (TC)** = Fixed Cost (FC) + Variable Cost (VC)
- **Contribution Margin (CM)** = Sales - Variable Cost = **SP per unit - VC per unit**
- **Profit Volume (P/V) Ratio** = $\dfrac{\text{Contribution}}{\text{Sales}} \times 100$
- **Margin of Safety (MoS)** = Actual Sales - Break Even Sales
- **Margin of Safety Ratio** = $\dfrac{\text{Margin of Safety}}{\text{Actual Sales}} \times 100$

> [!NOTE]
> All monetary values in KTU numerical problems are typically expressed in **Rupees (₹)**, and quantities in **units**. Always carry units explicitly in your final answer to secure full marks.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Assumptions of Break Even Analysis

For the Break Even Model to hold valid, the following assumptions must be satisfied:

1. **Linear Behaviour of Costs:** Total fixed cost remains **constant** across all levels of activity. Variable cost per unit remains **constant** (linear).
2. **Constant Selling Price:** The sales price per unit does not change with volume (no bulk discounts, no seasonal variation).
3. **Single Product or Constant Sales Mix:** Either a single product is sold, or in multi-product cases, the sales mix remains constant.
4. **Inventory Stability:** Opening and closing inventories are assumed to be equal (production = sales), so all costs flow into the current period.
5. **Cost-Volume Causality:** Costs are driven solely by volume; other factors like efficiency, productivity, and technology are held constant.
6. **No Semi-Variable Costs:** Costs are strictly classified as either fully fixed or fully variable.

> [!WARNING]
> In KTU valuation, if a problem violates any assumption (e.g., mentions a stepped fixed cost or a changing variable cost), you **must** explicitly state the assumption you are adopting before solving.

## 2.2 Components of Total Cost

### A. Fixed Cost (FC)
Fixed costs are expenses that do **not vary** with the level of production or sales volume within a relevant range. Examples: rent, salaries of permanent staff, insurance, depreciation on straight-line basis.

### B. Variable Cost (VC)
Variable costs fluctuate **proportionally** with the volume of activity. They have two forms:
- **Total Variable Cost (TVC)** = Variable cost per unit × Number of units
- **Variable Cost Per Unit (VCPU)** = $\dfrac{\text{Total Variable Cost}}{\text{Number of Units}}$ (remains constant per unit)

### C. Semi-Variable Cost (SVC)
These contain both fixed and variable components (e.g., telephone bills with a fixed rental plus usage charges). In basic BEP problems, KTU typically assumes semi-variable costs are **apportioned** into fixed and variable components.

## 2.3 Contribution Margin — The Engine of Profit

**Contribution** is the amount remaining from sales revenue after deducting variable costs. It is called "contribution" because it *contributes* toward covering fixed costs and generating profit.

$$C = S - V$$

Where:
- $C$ = Total Contribution
- $S$ = Total Sales
- $V$ = Total Variable Cost

When $C < \text{FC}$ → **Loss**
When $C = \text{FC}$ → **Break Even**
When $C > \text{FC}$ → **Profit**

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **master reference** for all Break Even problems in KTU 2024 ESE. Memorize the symbol, the formula, and the unit carefully.

| # | Parameter | Symbol | Formula | Unit |
|---|-----------|--------|---------|------|
| 1 | Total Cost | TC | TC = FC + (VCPU × Q) | ₹ |
| 2 | Total Revenue | TR | TR = SP × Q | ₹ |
| 3 | Contribution per unit | Cpu | Cpu = SP - VCPU | ₹ per unit |
| 4 | Total Contribution | C | C = (SP - VCPU) × Q | ₹ |
| 5 | P/V Ratio | PVR | PVR = (Contribution / Sales) × 100 | Percentage |
| 6 | Break Even Point (Units) | BEP(Q) | BEP(Q) = FC / Cpu | Units |
| 7 | Break Even Point (₹) | BEP(₹) | BEP(₹) = FC / PVR | ₹ |
| 8 | Break Even Sales (₹) | BES | BES = FC × (100 / PVR) | ₹ |
| 9 | Profit | P | P = C - FC | ₹ |
| 10 | Margin of Safety | MoS | MoS = Actual Sales - BES | ₹ |
| 11 | Margin of Safety Ratio | MoSR | MoSR = (MoS / Actual Sales) × 100 | Percentage |
| 12 | Required Sales (for target profit) | RS | RS = (FC + Target Profit) / Cpu | Units |
| 13 | Required Sales (₹ for target profit) | RS₹ | RS₹ = (FC + Target Profit) / PVR | ₹ |
| 14 | Operating Leverage | DOL | DOL = Contribution / Profit | Ratio |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In engineering-driven businesses, Break Even Analysis is critically applied in:

- **Manufacturing Plant Setup:** Determining the minimum production run to justify a new CNC machine, 3D printer, or assembly line.
- **Software Product Launches:** SaaS companies use BEP to find the minimum subscriber count to cover server and developer costs.
- **EV & Automotive Industry:** Tesla and Tata Motors routinely publish their "production ramp" estimates, which are essentially BEP calculations for new gigafactories.
- **Renewable Energy Projects:** Solar farm developers use BEP to determine the payback-period threshold in terms of units generated.
- **IT Consulting Firms:** Used to price fixed-cost project bids against variable labor-hour consumption.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the BEP Formulas

### 3.1.1 BEP in Units (Algebraic Derivation)

Let us define the variables:
- $FC$ = Total Fixed Cost
- $VCPU$ = Variable Cost per Unit
- $SP$ = Selling Price per Unit
- $Q$ = Quantity of units sold
- $TR$ = Total Revenue
- $TC$ = Total Cost

**Step 1:** Express Total Revenue as a function of Q.

$$TR = SP \times Q$$

**Step 2:** Express Total Cost as a function of Q.

$$TC = FC + (VCPU \times Q)$$

**Step 3:** Apply the Break Even condition (Profit = 0).

$$\text{Profit} = TR - TC = 0$$

**Step 4:** Substitute the expressions.

$$SP \times Q - [FC + (VCPU \times Q)] = 0$$

**Step 5:** Expand and isolate the Q terms.

$$(SP \times Q) - (VCPU \times Q) = FC$$

**Step 6:** Factor out Q.

$$Q \times (SP - VCPU) = FC$$

**Step 7:** Recognize that $(SP - VCPU)$ is the **Contribution per unit (Cpu)**.

$$Q \times Cpu = FC$$

**Step 8:** Solve for Q.

$$Q_{BEP} = \frac{FC}{Cpu} = \frac{FC}{SP - VCPU}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The result $Q_{BEP}$ is the **Break Even Point expressed in units**. This is the most frequently asked derivation in KTU Part A questions.

### 3.1.2 BEP in Rupees (Algebraic Derivation)

**Step 1:** Recall that $TR = SP \times Q$. Therefore, at BEP:

$$\text{BEP in ₹} = SP \times Q_{BEP}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $Q_{BEP} = \dfrac{FC}{Cpu}$:

$$\text{BEP in ₹} = SP \times \frac{FC}{Cpu}$$

**Step 3:** Divide numerator and denominator by SP:

$$\text{BEP in ₹} = \frac{FC}{\frac{Cpu}{SP}}$$

**Step 4:** Recognize that $\dfrac{Cpu}{SP}$ is the **P/V Ratio (as a decimal)**:

$$\text{BEP in ₹} = \frac{FC}{PVR}$$

This is the canonical "BEP in Rupees" formula.

### 3.1.3 Derivation of Target Profit Sales

If the firm desires a **target profit (TP)** instead of zero profit, the condition becomes:

$$TR - TC = TP$$

$$SP \times Q - FC - (VCPU \times Q) = TP$$

$$Q \times (SP - VCPU) = FC + TP$$

$$Q_{Target} = \frac{FC + TP}{Cpu}$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard)

**Problem Statement:**
A company manufactures a single product with the following data:
- Selling Price per unit: **₹50**
- Variable Cost per unit: **₹30**
- Total Fixed Cost: **₹2,00,000**

**Required:** Calculate (a) BEP in units, (b) BEP in ₹, (c) P/V Ratio, and (d) Sales required to earn a target profit of ₹1,00,000.

### Solution:

**Step 1: Identify the given data.**

$$SP = 50, \quad VCPU = 30, \quad FC = 200000, \quad TP = 100000$$

**Step 2: Calculate Contribution per unit.**

$$Cpu = SP - VCPU = 50 - 30 = 20 \text{ ₹/unit}$$

**Step 3: Calculate BEP in units.**

$$Q_{BEP} = \frac{FC}{Cpu} = \frac{200000}{20} = 10000 \text{ units}$$

**Step 4: Calculate BEP in ₹.**

$$\text{BEP₹} = SP \times Q_{BEP} = 50 \times 10000 = 500000 \text{ ₹}$$

Alternative verification using P/V Ratio:

$$PVR = \frac{Cpu}{SP} = \frac{20}{50} = 0.40 \text{ or } 40\%$$

$$\text{BEP₹} = \frac{FC}{PVR} = \frac{200000}{0.40} = 500000 \text{ ₹} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 5: Sales required for target profit of ₹1,00,000.**

$$Q_{Target} = \frac{FC + TP}{Cpu} = \frac{200000 + 100000}{20} = \frac{300000}{20} = 15000 \text{ units}$$

**Step 6: Corresponding target sales in ₹.**

$$\text{Target Sales₹} = 50 \times 15000 = 750000 \text{ ₹}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Multi-Problem Reasoning

| Scenario | Effect on BEP (Units) | Effect on Profit | Managerial Action |
|----------|----------------------|------------------|-------------------|
| ↑ Fixed Cost (e.g., new rent) | ↑ Increases | ↓ Decreases | Renegotiate leases; outsource |
| ↑ Variable Cost (raw material) | ↑ Increases | ↓ Decreases | Bulk purchase; supplier switch |
| ↑ Selling Price | ↓ Decreases | ↑ Increases | Possible if demand is inelastic |
| ↑ Sales Volume | No direct effect on BEP | ↑ Increases linearly | Marketing push, channel expansion |
| Multi-product introduction | Shifts BEP | Depends on mix | Optimize high-Cpu products |
| Automation (FC ↑, VC ↓) | Cpu rises → BEP ↓ | Mixed effect | Long-term strategic shift |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU Part B questions, you are often required to **comment on the managerial implication** of a change in cost/price/volume. Use the reasoning pattern above to structure your answer.

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Break Even Decision

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how data flows from raw cost inputs through the BEP engine to managerial outputs.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Fixed Cost FC] --> E[BEP Engine]
    B[Input: Variable Cost per Unit VCPU] --> E
    C[Input: Selling Price per Unit SP] --> E
    D[Input: Target Profit TP] --> E

    E --> F[Compute Contribution per Unit Cpu]
    E --> G[Compute P/V Ratio]
    E --> H[Compute BEP in Units]
    E --> I[Compute BEP in Rupees]
    E --> J[Compute Margin of Safety]
    E --> K[Compute Target Sales]

    F --> L[Managerial Output Dashboard]
    G --> L
    H --> L
    I --> L
    J --> L
    K --> L

    L --> M[Decision: Pricing Strategy]
    L --> N[Decision: Cost Control]
    L --> O[Decision: Production Volume]
    L --> P[Decision: Profitability Target]
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Flow of the Break Even Condition

The following Mermaid diagram traces the logical condition chain from the initial revenue-cost relationship to the final break-even verdict.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Total Revenue TR] --> C[Compare: TR vs TC]
    B[Total Cost TC] --> C
    C --> D{Is TR Greater than TC?}
    D -->|Yes| E[Contribution Exceeds FC → PROFIT]
    D -->|No| F{Is TR Equal to TC?}
    D -->|No| G[Contribution Less than FC → LOSS]
    F -->|Yes| H[Contribution Equals FC → BREAK EVEN POINT]
    F -->|No| G
```

## 4.3 Components and Their Interactions (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CostStructure [Cost Structure]
        FC[Fixed Cost FC Constant]
        VCPU[Variable Cost per Unit VCPU Constant]
    end

    subgraph SalesStructure [Sales Structure]
        SP[Selling Price per Unit SP]
        Q[Sales Volume Q units]
    end

    FC --> TC[Total Cost = FC + VCPU times Q]
    VCPU --> TC
    Q --> TC
    SP --> TR[Total Revenue = SP times Q]
    Q --> TR

    TC --> NET[Net Result TR minus TC]
    TR --> NET
    NET --> BEPCheck{Break Even Check}
    BEPCheck --> R1[Profit Zone]
    BEPCheck --> R2[Break Even Line]
    BEPCheck --> R3[Loss Zone]
```

## 4.4 Lifecycle of a BEP Analysis Project

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Identify and Classify Costs] --> S2[Step 2: Separate Fixed and Variable Components]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Determine Selling Price and Volume]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Compute Contribution per Unit]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Calculate BEP in Units and Rupees]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Compute Margin of Safety and P/V Ratio]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Conduct Sensitivity Analysis]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Generate Managerial Insights and Recommendations]
```

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU Board Exams, you may be asked to draw a **Break Even Chart** (also called the CVP graph). While the chart cannot be rendered in plain text, the **block architecture** above captures the equivalent conceptual information and is fully acceptable as a schematic answer.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A.1** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Q: Define Break Even Point. State any two assumptions of Break Even Analysis.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Break Even Point (BEP)** is the level of sales at which **Total Revenue equals Total Cost**, resulting in **zero profit and zero loss**. It is the minimum output a firm must achieve to avoid financial loss.

Two key assumptions of BEA are:

1. **Fixed costs remain constant** throughout the relevant range of activity and do not vary with production volume.
2. **Variable cost per unit and selling price per unit remain constant**, implying a linear cost-volume-revenue relationship.

---

### **Question A.2** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: What is P/V Ratio? Why is it considered an important measure in managerial decision-making?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Profit/Volume (P/V) Ratio** is the ratio of **Contribution** to **Sales**, expressed as a percentage:

$$P/V \text{ Ratio} = \frac{\text{Contribution}}{\text{Sales}} \times 100$$

It is considered a vital managerial metric because:

1. It indicates the **rate at which contribution is generated per rupee of sales**, helping managers evaluate profitability efficiency.
2. A **higher P/V ratio** means a **lower BEP** in rupees, signaling better cost coverage and lower risk of losses.
3. It enables **product-mix decisions** by ranking products based on their contribution-generating efficiency.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per the KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module 4 contains an internal choice. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each question carries 14 marks, split into two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### **Question A** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]

**A company is evaluating the launch of a new product. The following data is provided:**

- Selling Price per unit: **₹200**
- Variable Cost per unit: **₹120**
- Total Fixed Costs: **₹8,00,000**
- Expected Annual Sales Volume: **12,000 units**

**(a)** Calculate the **Break Even Point in units and in rupees**, and the **P/V Ratio**.

**(b)** Calculate the **Margin of Safety** (in units and in ₹) and the **Margin of Safety Ratio**. Comment on what the MoS indicates about the company's risk profile.

#### Model Solution:

**Given Data:**

$$SP = 200, \quad VCPU = 120, \quad FC = 800000, \quad Q_{actual} = 12000$$

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Compute Contribution per unit.**

$$Cpu = SP - VCPU = 200 - 120 = 80 \text{ ₹/unit}$$

**Step 2: Compute BEP in units.**

$$Q_{BEP} = \frac{FC}{Cpu} = \frac{800000}{80} = 10000 \text{ units}$$

**Step 3: Compute BEP in rupees.**

$$\text{BEP₹} = SP \times Q_{BEP} = 200 \times 10000 = 2000000 \text{ ₹} \quad \text{or} \quad ₹20,00,000$$

**Step 4: Compute P/V Ratio.**

$$PVR = \frac{Cpu}{SP} \times 100 = \frac{80}{200} \times 100 = 40\%$$

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 5: Compute Margin of Safety in units.**

$$MoS_{units} = Q_{actual} - Q_{BEP} = 12000 - 10000 = 2000 \text{ units}$$

**Step 6: Compute Margin of Safety in rupees.**

$$MoS_{₹} = SP \times MoS_{units} = 200 \times 2000 = 400000 \text{ ₹} \quad \text{or} \quad ₹4,00,000$$

**Step 7: Compute Margin of Safety Ratio.**

$$MoSR = \frac{MoS_{₹}}{Actual\ Sales} \times 100 = \frac{400000}{2400000} \times 100 = 16.67\%$$

**Managerial Comment:**

A Margin of Safety of **₹4,00,000 (16.67%)** indicates that the company can absorb a sales decline of up to **16.67%** from the expected level before incurring a loss. While the company is profitable, the MoS is moderate. Management should consider strategies to **expand the MoS** — through sales volume growth, price increases, or cost reduction — to enhance financial resilience.

**Verification:**

$$\text{Profit} = C - FC = (80 \times 12000) - 800000 = 960000 - 800000 = 160000 \text{ ₹} \quad \checkmark$$

$$\text{Profit} = MoS_{₹} \times PVR = 400000 \times 0.40 = 160000 \text{ ₹} \quad \checkmark$$

---

### **Question B** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024 Scheme — Internal Choice]

**A firm operates with the following data:**

- Total Fixed Cost: **₹5,00,000**
- Variable Cost per unit: **₹150**
- Selling Price per unit: **₹250**
- Desired (Target) Profit: **₹3,00,000**

**(a)** Calculate the **number of units** to be sold to achieve the target profit and the corresponding **sales revenue in ₹**.

**(b)** The firm is now considering a proposal to **increase the selling price to ₹280 per unit**, but this is expected to reduce the sales volume to **8,000 units**. The variable cost remains ₹150 per unit. Should the firm accept this proposal? Justify your answer with a comparative calculation.

#### Model Solution:

**Given Data:**

$$FC = 500000, \quad VCPU = 150, \quad SP = 250, \quad TP = 300000$$

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Compute Contribution per unit (current).**

$$Cpu = SP - VCPU = 250 - 150 = 100 \text{ ₹/unit}$$

**Step 2: Compute required units for target profit.**

$$Q_{Target} = \frac{FC + TP}{Cpu} = \frac{500000 + 300000}{100} = \frac{800000}{100} = 8000 \text{ units}$$

**Step 3: Compute corresponding sales revenue in ₹.**

$$\text{Target Sales₹} = SP \times Q_{Target} = 250 \times 8000 = 2000000 \text{ ₹} \quad \text{or} \quad ₹20,00,000$$

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 4: Compute profit under the new proposal.**

Given: $SP_{new} = 280$, $Q_{new} = 8000$, $VCPU = 150$, $FC = 500000$

$$Cpu_{new} = 280 - 150 = 130 \text{ ₹/unit}$$

$$Profit_{new} = (Cpu_{new} \times Q_{new}) - FC = (130 \times 8000) - 500000 = 1040000 - 500000 = 540000 \text{ ₹}$$

**Step 5: Compute profit under the original plan (at target).**

$$Profit_{original} = Target\ Profit = 300000 \text{ ₹}$$

**Step 6: Comparative analysis.**

$$Profit_{new} - Profit_{original} = 540000 - 300000 = 240000 \text{ ₹}$$

Since the new proposal yields an **additional profit of ₹2,40,000** (a 80% increase over the target), the firm should **ACCEPT the proposal**.

**Additional verification: BEP under new proposal.**

$$Q_{BEP,new} = \frac{FC}{Cpu_{new}} = \frac{500000}{130} = 3846 \text{ units (approx.)}$$

The new BEP is lower than the planned sales of 8000 units, confirming profitability.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark-Deduction Zones:**
> 1. **Forgetting the units in BEP answers:** Always write "BEP = 10,000 units" — not just "10,000." Losing 0.5 marks per omission.
> 2. **P/V Ratio mistake:** Many students write $PVR = \dfrac{C}{S}$ in decimal and forget to multiply by 100. Always state the percentage explicitly.
> 3. **Target Profit formula confusion:** When asked "sales to earn a profit of ₹X," the formula is $\dfrac{FC + TP}{Cpu}$, **not** $\dfrac{FC - TP}{Cpu}$. The minus sign is the most common error.
> 4. **Ignoring the managerial comment:** KTU awards 1–2 marks for the interpretive/commentary portion. Always end Part B with a 2–3 line managerial insight.
> 5. **Multi-step problems with proposal comparison:** Students often forget to **recompute the BEP under the new scenario**. Always validate with a BEP check.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 ESE Ready**

- **BEP Definition:** The sales level where **Total Revenue = Total Cost**, giving zero profit and zero loss.
- **Core Formula (Units):** $Q_{BEP} = \dfrac{FC}{SP - VCPU} = \dfrac{FC}{Cpu}$
- **Core Formula (₹):** $\text{BEP₹} = \dfrac{FC}{PVR}$ or $SP \times Q_{BEP}$
- **P/V Ratio:** $\dfrac{Cpu}{SP} \times 100$ — higher PVR means more profitable and lower BEP.
- **Target Profit Sales:** $Q_{Target} = \dfrac{FC + TP}{Cpu}$
- **Margin of Safety:** Actual Sales - BEP Sales. A **larger MoS** means **lower business risk**.
- **Assumptions to State:** Linear cost behaviour, constant SP, constant sales mix, inventory stability, single cost driver (volume).
- **Profit Verification Identity:** $Profit = MoS_{₹} \times PVR$ (always cross-check).
- **DOL Identity:** Degree of Operating Leverage = $\dfrac{Contribution}{Profit}$ — used in sensitivity analysis.
- **Chart Convention:** At BEP, the **Total Revenue line and Total Cost line intersect**. The vertical gap above the intersection is the profit zone; below it is the loss zone.
- **Multi-Product Case:** BEP (in ₹) = $\dfrac{\text{Total FC}}{\text{Weighted Average P/V Ratio}}$; this is a frequent Part B variation.
- **Sensitivity Rule:** A **10% increase in SP** (with constant VC) increases PVR disproportionately because Cpu grows, while a **10% increase in VC** shrinks Cpu and PVR.
- **Examiner's Mantra:** Always carry units, always state assumptions, always end with a managerial comment.
